The  Ethics  of  Pockets 

By  HELEN  CAMPBELL 

-"  She  was  a  simple  farmer  lass." 


SO  sang  the  leader  of  the  village  choir, 
his  clear  baritone  ringing  through  the 
lecture  room  of  the  little  church, 
where  the  social  supper  and  whatever  en- 
tertainment could  be  provided  for  was  the 
monthly  feature  from  November  to  May. 
"Most  a  pot  o'  baked  beans  and  five  kinds 
o'  cake  in  him  an'  it  don' t  seem  to  hinder 
him  one  mite,  with  that  voice  of  his  clear 
as  a  bell, ' '  Aunt  Patty  Reynolds  said  to  her 
nearest  neighbor,  "but  as  for  'simple 
farmer  lassies,'  there  ain't  none  such. 
They  all  wear  white  waists  in  the  afternoon 
and  sit  on  the  porch  and  rock  for  all  as  if 
they  was  summer  boarders,  and  there  ain't 
one  the  length  and  breadth  of  the  whole 
land  that '  s  got  a  pocket. ' ' 

The  minister,  just  behind  her,  a  fun-lov- 
ing man  with  a  solemn  face  that  belied  his 
tendencies,  bent  forward  to  listen. 

"It's  so;' '  Aunt  Patty  continued,  ' '  and 
there  ain't  one  can  deny  it.  The  dress- 
maker is  at  the  bottom  of  it,  but  the  fool 
that '  s  in  all  women  is  down  there  along 
side.  'Taint  fashionable  to  have  a  pocket 
they  say,  but  who  said  it  first,  and  why 
ain't  it?  That's  what  I  want  to  know. 
Now  here  we  are,  seven  women  in  a  row, 


and  every  one  of  us  old  enough  to  have 
sense.  How  many  of  you  has  got  a  pocket? 
Not  one  but  me  and  I  knew  it  ! "  she 
added  triumphantly,  after  a  pause  in  which 
defiance  had  come  into  every  face.  "I 
thought  so.  Your  handkerchiefs  are  tucked 
up  your  sleeves,  and  your  money,  gracious 
knows  where,  and  not  even  a  grandmother 
that  could  hand  out  a  peppermint  to  a 
wriggling  child  of  a  Sunday,  or  smelling  salts 
to  some  one  took  sudden  with  faintness  und 
such.  It '  s  time  to  find  out  what  it  means 
and  why  ain'  t  this  the  time  ?  Who  '  s  got 
such  authority  that  there  ain' t  a  woman  left 
in  the  land  that  dares  to  go  agi'n  it? 
What '  s  your  opinion  Dr.  Green  ?  ' '  and  she 
turned  so  suddenly  that  Mrs.  Green  started 
and  gave  a  little  cry. 

The  minister's  eyes  twinkled  but  he  sat 
silent. 

"You  'd  a  told  fast  enough  if  Mis  Green 
hadn't  given  you  a  twitch,"  Aunt  Patty 
continued  undaunted.  "I  seen  her  and 
it '  s  all  right  enough,  for  she  '  s  all  for  peace, 
and  who  knows  how  they'd  all  take  it, 
she  thinks.  Maybe  you  never  found  out. 
Dr.  Green,  but  if  a  woman  sets  out  to  be  a 
fool,  she's  bound    to  prove    that    folly  is 


26o 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


wisdom.  I've  been  there  and  I  know. 
There  ain'  t  one  of  you  now  with  a  word  to 
say.  There  was  one  boarder  here  this 
summer  —  Enghsh  at  that,  and  they '  re  a 
kind  that  do  what  they  like  no  matter  what 
folks  say.  She  had  two,  and  man-fashion 
at  that,  set  in  with  stitched  flaps  each  side, 
her  front  breadth  to  '  em  never  showing  a 
mite,  unless  she  picked  up  apples  that  will 
bulge  you  know.  But  she  carried  things 
she  wanted  in  'em  just  like  a  man  and  had 
the  comfort  of 'em, —  knife  and  pencil  and  a 
book  most  of  the  time  and  no  going  up  her 
own  arm  for  handkerchiefs  or  anything  else, 
not  she.  But  here  right  before  us  is  a 
generation  coming  up  with  no  knowledge 
that  they  can  have  pockets.'  The  dress- 
makers say,  'Oh,  you  wouldn't  be  out  of 
fashion  would  you  ?  '  and  the  fool  mothers 
just  the  same,  and  never  using  their  wits 
enough  to  see  that  if  little  boys  need  'em 
for  taffy  and  empty  spools  and  fish  bait,  or 
anything  else  that's  valuable  to  them,  little 
girls  has  just  as  many  uses  for  them.  But 
there  !  You  can' t  make  one  of  them  think 
so,  and  bymbye,'no  pockets  for  the  female 
sect  will  settle  down  into  hard  and  fast  law, 
same  as  veils  for  Turks'  wives  and  families, 
'till  some  sense  comes  suddenly  to  some- 
body, may  be  a  hundred  years  from  now, 
aod^  there '  11  he  an  uprising  all  of  a  sudden 
and  forming  a  society  for  the  Propagation 
of  Pockets. ' ' 

''Go  it.  Aunt  Patty,"  said  the  president 
of  the  Young  Peoples'  Union,  a  young 
woman  whose  tightly  fitting  sheath-skirt 
gave  no  evidence  of  even  the  possibility  of 
a  pocket.  "I  do  like  to  hear  you  talk. 
We  all  do,  whether  we  agree  with  you  or 
not.  But  if  we  don't  mind,  why  should 
you  ?  " 

Aunt  Patty' s  eyes  grew  large  and  very 
serious. 

"  Child,  you  '11  live  to  know  that  for  the 
fool  things  that  folks  do  there's  always 
somebody  that  must  mind,  and  I  suppose 
I  'm  one,  since  it  kind  of  hurts  to  see  sense 
growing  in  such  lots  of  ways,  and  then  some- 
thing coming  sudden  like  to  upset  the  good. 
Up  in  Boston,  the  last  time  I  went  up  with 
Mirandy  for  a  little  change  after  summer 


boarders,  first  thing  I  saw  was  every  woman 
with  her  purse  in  her  hand,  laying  it  down 
most  anywheres,  or  else  she  had  a  little  bag 
that  every  pickpocket  knew  had  her  money 
in  it,  and  that  she  held  handy  on  her  wrist 
for  anyone  of '  em  to  snatch  and  run.  I  said 
then  somebody  ought  to  interfere  and  put  a 
stop  to  it  and  the  temptation  their  ways  was, 
making  thieving  easy,  besides  giving  up  the 
common  sense  that  any  woman  needs, 
same's  a  man;  more'n  a  man  I  say,  for 
many  a  time  she  has  to  have  it  for  him,  too, 
in  everything  but  pockets.  Here's  your 
Young  Folks'  Social  Union,  and  sending 
for  lecturers  and  things.  Why  ain' t  there 
somebody  that  might  come  along  and  just 
settle  this  question." 

Dr.  Green  had  laughed  more  than  once, 
his  mellow,  comfortable  laugh,  and  now  he 
took  from  his  pocketbook  a  printed  slip 
and  rose  in  his  place.  "  It  is  a  little  singu- 
lar, ' '  he  said,  ' '  that  while  you  are  laughing 
at  Aunt  Patty  and  her  argument,  I  hap- 
pened to  have  cut  from  a  western  paper  for 
my  wife's  benefit  —  you  see  she  also  is  in 
fashion  —  a  paragraph  or  so  holding  the 
conclusion  of  a  wise  man,  the  principal  of  a 
high  school  in  Denver,  Colorado,  one  of 
the  most  progressive  of  western  cities.  He 
has  ordered  that  all  the  girls  attending 
school  shall  have  pockets  in  their  dresses, 
and  the  reason  he  gives  is  that  he  cannot 
have  pupils  of  the  school  subjected  to  un- 
necessary temptation.  Every  day,  he  says, 
eight  or  ten  purses  are  found  about  the 
school  building  and  brought  to  the  princi- 
pal's  office.  He  considers  it  rem.arkable 
that  the  pupils  are  so  honest,  but  thinks  it 
unfair  to  put  their  virtue  to  this  constant 
test.  If  the  local  school  board  agree  with 
him,  hereafter  every  girl  attending  school 
will  be  expected  to  have  in  her  clothing  a 
safe  receptacle  for  the  money  she  carries 
about  with  her.^  Today' s  paper  gives  two 
cases.  A  shopper  left  her  purse  on  the 
counter,  and  the  woman  who  picked  it  up 
and  returned  it  was  arrested  as  a  thief. 
Another  woman  had  her  wrist  badly  torn 
by  a  thief  who  snatched  her  wrist  bag 
through  an  open  window  as  the  train  started, 
he  on   the   outside   platform.     -Argument 


OPPORTUNITY 


261 


is  n'  t  much  use  of  course;  it  never  has  been, 
from  the  day  even  when  old  John  Cotton 
and  his  successor  fulminated  against  '  the 
creature  called  starch,'  which  appears  to 
have  been  pecuharly  obnoxious  to  the  Pu- 
ritan mind.  But  you  don' t  want  tempta- 
tion before  any  weak  sister  or  brother,  and 
I  submit  that  you  take  the  question  for  your 
next  debate,  and  see  what  conclusion  you 
reach,  and,  having  reached  it,  are  willing 
to  act  upon. ' ' 

Aunt    Patty   shook   her    head    as     Dr. 


Green    sat    down. 

"It's  easy  enough  to  conclude,"  she 
said,  '^I  've  done  it  myself,  and  then  gone 
right  on  the  same  old  way.  But  I  '11  be 
examining  committee  for  the  next  Social, 
with  a  list  of  all  the  women  and  girls  here, 
and  just  check  off  them  that  really  has  had 
conscience  enough  to  put  in  pockets.  But 
law  me.  Dr.  Green  !  You  know  women 
and  sp  do  I.  We'll  see  if  they're  any 
different  now  from  what  John  Cotton  found 
'  em;  good  enough  for  him  too,  I  say. ' ' 


Opportunity 


By  MRS.  CHARLES  NORMAN 


ONCE,  in  a  small  town,  where  peo- 
ple dwelled  without  the  formalities 
of  life,  there  lived  a  lovely  young 
girl,  who  had  a  passion  for  cooking.  Her 
ability  first  showed  itself  in  the  manufacture 
of  candies,  then  in  fancy  cakes,  then  in 
salads.  Finally  she  reached  the  summum- 
bonum  —  the  broiling  of  a  beefsteak  and 
the  making  of  a  fine  loaf  of  bread,  which 
''tasted  like  food,"  to  her  three  hearty 
brothers. 

She  was  a  high-school  girl,  but  while  she 
was  mastering,  her  Latin  participles  and 
endeavoring  to  obtain  some  comprehension 
of  the  heavenly  bodies,  her  chief  interest 
was  in  household  affairs,  and  especially  in 
the  culinary  line.  Saturday  morning  in 
the  kitchen  was  the  happiest  part  of  her 
week. 

At  length  the  time  came  for  graduation, 
and  troubles  new  and  strange  began  to 
weigh  upon  her  mind.  The  final  examin- 
ations were,  one  by  one,  eliminated  from 
her  list  of  worries.  Her  own  fingers  were 
skillful  enough  to  assist  in  the  making  of  a 
dainty  Swiss  commencement  gown;  but 
something  else  besides  examinations  and 
costume  must  be  provided  for. 


She  must  write  an  essay  and  read  the 
same  ' '  in  public  on  the  stage. ' '  It  was 
hard  to  say  which  ordeal  seemed  worse  to 
her.  She  had  not  been  trained  to  write. 
English  composition  had,  by  some  strange 
neglect,  been  given  only  the  briefest  and 
most  bookish  consideration.  Moreover,  it 
had  never  been  intimated  to  her  that  her 
own  ideas  were  worth  recording,  and  she 
was  too  honest  to  sham.  ■  But  to  read  to 
the  public  a  production  of  her  own,  to 
seem  to  be  wise  on  great  topics,  wherein 
she  was  ignorant  —  how  could  she  do  it? 
Her  seatmate  could  blandly  discourse  upon 
The  River  of  Life,  though  entirely  un- 
aware of  its  windings — its  beauties  or  its 
perils.  The  boys  could  grow  eloquent 
upon  the  Dangers  of  Republican  Govern- 
ment—  but  upon  what  subject  could  she 
venture  to  speak? 

She  meditated.  She  suffered.  At  the 
very  crisis  of  fear  and  discouragement,  one 
of  her  brothers  said: 

"  Well,  Sis,  you  may  not  write  the  best 
essays,  but  I  '11  bet  you  're  the  best  cook 
who  was  ever  graduated  from  our  school. ' ' 

That  remark  offered  a  suggestion.  She 
went    straight    to    her    room,    and    under 


262 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


an  enthusiasm  she  could  not  account  for, 
wrote  an  essay  of  twice  the  required  length. 
Her  subject  was  "Cooking."  Her  pen 
moved  as  fast  as  possible,  so  there  was  no 
doubt  of  the  spontaneity  of  the  article. 
The  next  day  it  was  approved  by  the 
teacher,  and,  in  due  season,  read. 

There  was  ''nothing  so  wonderful" 
about  the  production,  every  one  said;  yet 
it  seemed  to  have  made  an  impression. 
No  one  knew  that  the  highest  art  was  ex- 
hibited in  the  girl's  artlessness,  and  no 
one  could  explain  the  captivating  power  of 
her  simplicity  and  enthusiasm.  The  men 
who  listened  to  her  were  delighted;  the 
women  were,  for  the  most  part,  pleased 
and  inspired;  her  classmates  were  almost 
aghast,  that  one  of  their  number  had 
dared  be  so  ' '  commonplace  ' '  and  ' '  nat- 
ural" on  the  important  occasion  of 
graduation.  It  was  like  an  appeal  to  arms, 
as  she  told  of  the  need  of  cooks  that  were 
good  cooks,  and  it  was  a  spur  to  the  en- 
thusiasm of  the  very  young  girls,  when  the 
writer  mentioned  the  fun  of  putting  her 
hands  into  real  dough,  when  she  got  too 
big  for  mud  pies. 

Now  it  chanced,  that,  among  her  hear- 
ers, was  a  young  woman,  who  was  acting 
cook  in  one  of  the  best  homes  in  the 
town.  She  was  a  literary  cook,  who  went 
about  her  work  with  her  pencil  in  her  hair 
and  a  book  in  her  pocket;  and  who  talked 
sometimes  of  a  club  she  used  to  belong  to. 

She,  as  a  matter  of  course,  had  no 
thought  of  missing  the  commencement." 
''She  had  treated  many  of  the  same  topics 


that  the  graduates  had  chosen,"  as  she 
afterward  told  her  long-suffering  mistress. 

"There  was  one  paper  on  cooking," 
shyly  suggested  her  benefactor.  "Have 
you  ever  treated  that  theme  ?  " 

' '  Oh  no  ! "  observed  the  maid,  quite  un- 
conscious of  the  humor  of  the  query.  "  I 
did  not  care  for  that  essay.  Such  a  sub- 
ject is  not  worth  talking  about.  " 

Now  the  above  is  a  sketch  quite  faith- 
ful to  fact.  Shall  we  draw  the  moral  or 
relate  the  sequence  ? 

The  young  graduate  did  not  rise  to  glory 
or  eminence,  but,  continuing  in  her  nat- 
ural, feminine  tastes  —  altogether  superior 
to  some  who  thought  she  should  have  had 
"a  career  "  — she 

"  Studied  households  well 

And  good  works  in  her  husband  did  promote." 

Perhaps  the  other  girl  was  not  to  be 
blamed  for  not  having  domestic  tastes;  but 
the  fact  remains,  that  she  wasted  her 
days  in  sighing  for  opportunities,  neglect- 
ing the  only  one  that  fortune  had  given  • 
her  —  which  was  in  reality,  a  very  rare  op- 
portunity. She  had  a  good  home.  Her 
mistress  was  extremely  generous,  patient 
and  anxious  to  befriend.  The  girl  had 
abundant  material,  a  clean,  well-furnished 
work  shop,  instruction  free,  and  kindness 
and  encouragement  in  abundance,  but  she 
was  totally  bUnd  to  the  present.  Oppor- 
tunity was  to  her,  what  it  is  to  many  peo- 
ple, something  way  off  there.  Ignoring 
her  present,  she  was  stretching  out  her 
hand  for  what  she  thought  was  better. 
What  she  actually  grasped,  you  can  guess! 


"  Oh  !  had  we  some  bright  little  isle  of  our  own, 
In  the  blue  summer  ocean  far  off  and  alone, 
Where  a  leaf  never  dies  in  the  still-blooming  bowers. 
And  the  bee  banquets  on  through  a  whole  year  of  flowers  ; 
Where  the  sun  loves  to  pause 

With  so  fond  a  delay, 
That  the  night  only  draws 
A  fine  veil  o'er  the  day, 
Where  simply  to  feel  that  we  breathe,  that  we  live, 
Is  worth  the  best  joy  that  life  elsewhere  can  give." 

Thomas  Moore. 


Specialties 


By  KATE  GANNETT  WELLS 


A  BOY  of  nine  years  old  gave  as  his 
criticism  of  a  wise,  old-fashioned 
story,  ' '  The  Swiss  Family  Robin- 
son," that  "it  makes  us  learn  how  to  be 
patient  without  so  much  strain. ' '  And  a 
little  blue- eyed  Irish  girl,  who  was  winner 
of  the  first  prize  for  sewing  offered  in  a 
city  summer  playground,  also  reseated  a 
cane  chair  for  her  mother,  finished  one 
that  another  child  had  abandoned,  made  a 
hammock,  and  took  entire  care  of  two 
babies  for  the  whole  summer.  These  two 
stories  always  come  to  my  mind,  when  I 
hear  of  the  great  stress  which  is  now  being 
laid  on  the  cultivation  of  specialties.  That 
boy  had  discovered  how  self-control  makes 
home  life  successful,  and  that  girl  how 
home  usefulness  dominates  character. 

Yet  specialization  is  now  to  the  front  in 
all  education,  especially  in  that  of  the  in- 
dustrial arts,  and  the  question  presses 
whether  or  not  such  specialization  is  men- 
acing the  all  round  education  which  con- 
servative, genuine  lovers  of  knowledge, 
for  its  own  sake,  have  always  believed  was 
essential  to  character.  Only  a  few  dare  to 
doubt  openly,  for,  when  once  a  notion 
takes  hold  of  the  public,  doubt  of  its 
excellence  is  considered  bad  form. 

Professor  William  James,  of  Harvard 
University,  is  said  to  have  given  as  a  reason 
for  the  higher  education  of  women,  that 
''a  college  education,  dealing  as  it  does 
with  the  work  of  great  men,  gives  women 
the  power  of  judging  men,  .  .  .  a  female 
college  person  should  be  able  to  judge 
human  nature. ' '  Is  not  success  in  such  a 
specialty  rare  ?  President  Eliot  of  Harvard 
declares,  that  "it  is  not  the  chief  happi- 
ness and  end  of  women  as  a  whole  to  keep 
in  the  professions  —  as  the  most  intellect- 
ual occupation  that  there  is  in  the  world  is 
that  of  women's  taking  their  place  in  the 
home."  So  home  is  a  specialty.  But 
what  a  travesty  on  a  truism,  that  training 


for  home  life,  an  immemorial  belief,  at  least, 
must  now  be  considered  as  a  specialty,  in 
order  to  enforce  respect  for  home  duties! 
It  is  well  to  have  such  public  declaration 
of  what  constitutes  feminine  duty,  since  it 
but  emphasizes  what  many  women  them- 
selves have  been  saying,  as  others  of  them 
found  specialties  outside  of  the  home. 

The  real  conflict  Kes  not  between  spe- 
cialties and  the  home,  but  betwixt  special- 
ties and  an  all  round  education  —  and  it 
can  be  decided  only  by  personality.  For 
a  specialty  is  but  a  trained  hobby  and, 
ever  since  mankind  was  invented,  it  has 
been  addicted  to  hobbies,  as  a  relief  from 
the  stupidity  of  self.  We  all  have  our 
hobbies,  little  or  great.  A  child's  collec- 
tion of  stamps,  postal  cards,  is  but  the  out- 
come of  his  hobby.  Only  in  these  latter 
days,  education  has  gotten  itself  on  to  ped- 
agogic lines,  till  ever  so  much  of  its  happy 
naturalness  is  lost  and  our  hobbies  have 
become  trained  specialties.  One  must  be 
an  expert  in  something,  if  one  wants  to  be 
famous  or  successfully  self-supporthig.  Yet 
as  it  is  so  much  easier  to  go  to  extremes 
than  to  keep  a  safe,  medium  course,  spe- 
cialties have  multiplied  until  an  all  round 
education  is  now  looked  upon  as  a  mild 
degradation  of  personal  possibilities. 

It  is  specialization  in  domestic  service 
that  bothers  easy  housekeeping.  In  one 
Boston  office,  within  the  last  year,  there 
were  672  applicants  for  general  housework 
against  3,075  employers,  who  desired  such 
service.  It  is  considered  more  stylish  and 
is  more  lucrative  to  be  a  waitress,  or  a 
woman-chef  than  to  be  a  general  house- 
worker.  Still  it  is  infinitely  more  useful  to 
know  how  to  do  everything  in  a  home 
pretty  well  than  only  to  do  one  thing 
supremely  well.  Who  would  not  rather 
have  good  bread  than  pastry?  Who 
would  not  prefer  to  have  her  kitchen  kept 
fairly   clean    than    herself  waited    upon  ? 


263 


264 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


The  intelligent  all  round  girl  of  all  work  is 
a  blessing,  which  only  those  of  us  can  ap- 
preciate who  have  not  gone  into  special- 
ties,—  because  we  did  not  want  them.  It 
is  not  our  ignorance  that  bids  us  beware, 
but  our  well-reasoned  convictions,  and,  for- 
tunately, there  is  many  a  school  of  domes- 
tic science  and  cookery  where  we  can  learn 
to  be  good  all  round  workers. 

Specialties  are  now  urged  as  modes  of 
industrial  education.  The  country  must 
have  skilled  workmen  and  industrial  intel- 
ligence. Yet  well  is  it  that  the  latter  phrase 
includes  wide  insight  into  the  bearing  of 
any  trade,  as  well  as  skill.  It  is  as  absurd 
a  division  of  specialty  to  make  merely  the 
sleeves  of  dresses,  year  in  and  year  out, 
as  it  is  profitable  to  know  how  to  make  a 
whole  garment;  and  wiser  still  is  it  to  know 
how  to  do  tolerably  well  for  one's  self  what 
one  cannot  afford  to  have  perfectly  done  by 
somebody  else. 

Therefore,  it  is,  that  we  are  having  indus- 
trial schools  to  supplement  and  to  fit  in  with 
the  present  public  school  teaching,  for  ap- 
prenticeships are  out  of  vogue,  and  some 
kind  of  education  is  needed  to  make  up  for 
the  all  round  education,  which  boys  and 
girls  used  to  get  by  working  at  home  and 
on  the  farm.  Granted  that  to  argue  against 
specialties  is  proving  one's  self  behind  the 
times,  still  the  times  are  apt  to  be  fad- 
dish and,  if  industrial  training  in  the  pubHc 
schools  should  lessen  the  latent  love  and 
enjoyment  of  beauty  in  art  and  literature, 
it  will  be  because  children  too  early  are  in- 
troduced to  specialties. 

After  all,  what  most  women  ask  of  them- 
selves is, — are  we  likely  to  be  married  and 
so  need  an  all  round  education,  or  are  we 
to  support  ourselves  outside  of  home  life 
and  so  should  become  specialists,  and  if  so. 


what  kind?  Here  is  the  puzzle  of  choice. 
First,  don't  depend  on  being  married  and, 
second,  don't  be  married  unless  you  are 
sure  you  love  a  man  so  much  that  you  just 
can't  help  yourself  from  loving  him.  Only 
while  you  are  waiting  (?) — in  an  indefinite 
state  of  mind,  choose  as  specialty  some  one 
thing  which  you  like  best.  Learn  it  thor- 
oughly, but  keep  heart  and  mind  open  all 
the  time  to  the  influences  of  an  all  round 
education.  Don' t  lose  yourself,  as  a  woman, 
in  being  a  specialist.  And  watch  the  mar- 
ket so  that,  when  the  craze  for  one  kind 
of  specialty  is  lessening,  you  can  lay  hold 
on  the  beginning  of  another.  If  you  don't 
do  this,  you  '11  try  to  manufacture  mission 
furniture  when  people  are  tired  of  its  stern- 
ness. You  will  decorate  china,  make  col- 
lars, bows,  do  raffia  and  enamel  work  for 
necklaces  and  buckles,  string  beads,  only 
to  find  out  that  the  fashion  for  such  things 
has  changed.  Useless  specialties  soon  wear 
out,  and  many  a  one,  who  found  them  ex- 
ceeding profitable  one  year,  sells  them  at  a 
loss  the  next. 

Beware  of  specialties  in  cookery,  educa- 
tion and  industries  —  and  of  mannerisms 
in  behavior,  which  are  but  uncomfortable 
variations  on  the  usual  good  manners  of 
one' s  comrades,  and  are  assumed  for  the 
purpose  of  appearing  remarkable  or,  at 
least,  different  from  others.  Do  a  great 
many  things  well  and  one  thing  a  little 
better.  Don't  try  to  be  original  at  the 
expense  of  being  all  round.  A  noted 
speaker  once  said,  "A  man  should  know 
something  of  everything  and  everything 
of  something;"  and,  as  a  woman's  life  is 
specially  related  to  housekeeping,  it  is 
twice  as  important  that  she  should  have 
the  same  relative  and  positive  knowl- 
edge. 


Conversation 


Two  egotists  conversed  one  day, 
Each  in  a  quite  contented  way. 
And  each  — the  vain  and  happy  elf — 
Soliloquized  about  himself. 


Speech  is  a  bridge,  from  mind  to  mind, 
For  gainful  interchange  designed  ; 
But  when  you  meet  a  selfish  man, 
The  bridge  has  lost  its  central  span! 


A 


Labor   of   Love   in   the  Bethel-Zion 
Baptist  Church 


By  LEE  McCRAE 


I  ST  AH  Ruby  Johnsing  done  call  a 
meetin'  uv  de  chu'ch  soci'ty  to 
come  to  her  house  dis  aftahnoon. 
She  say  she  hab  a  'portant  projec'. " 

This  was  the  word  spread  one  day 
through  the  little  negro  town,  Ishkooda, 
on  the  other  side  of  the  mountain  from  the 
busy  southern  city.  The  mystery  of  the 
*  projec'  "  brought  out  the  entire  mem- 
bership of  the  society,  although  it  was  iron- 
ing day  and  all  the  washings  had  to  be 
ready  for  toting  over  the  hill  in  the  morn- 
ing. At  the  last  moment  even  Pinkie 
Jackson  left  her  work  and  came,  pausing 
in  the  doorway  before  the  assembled  soci- 
ety to  spread  out  her  hands  and  exclaim, 

'^Ain'  I  jes  de  othah  side  uv  filthy! 
But  you  mus'  scuse  mah  'pearance,  ladies, 
'cause  I  done  built  nine  hen-coops  sense  I 
folded  down  de  does." 

Now  the  '^sistahs"  had  all  taken  time 
to  put  on  clean  aprons  before  coming,  but 
Pinkie  was  graciously  received  nevertheless, 
and,  after  greetings  were  over,  the  meet- 
ing opened  with  all  the  usual  formality. 
Then  with  several  hems  and  haws  to  give 
due  prominence  to  her  speech,  Ruby  John- 
son arose  and  told  her  latest  ' ' '  sperience. ' ' 

In  an  emergency  she  had  been  called  on 
by  *^her  white  family"  in  town  to  act  as 
nurse  and  carry  the  baby  to  the  church  for 
its  christening.  There,  sitting  in  the  back 
seat,  she  had  witnessed  the  communion 
service.  The  beautiful  building,  and  its 
furnishings,  the  orderliness  and  solemnity 
of  the  service  had  thrilled  this  earnest- 
hearted  colored  woman,  and  she  had 
trudged  back  over  the  mountain  pondering 
the  needs  of  her  own  httle  clap-boarded 
church  in  Ishkooda. 

"  Ef  we  jes'  had  silvah  communion  fix- 
in' s,  I  could  Stan'  de  res' — 'deed  I  could  ! 
—  deed  I  could  !  ' '  she  said   in  conclu- 


sion. ''But  when  I  see  Bruddah  Burges 
po'ing  de  wine  out'naoP  beer  bottle  into 
de  jelly  glasses,  to  pass  'roun',  it  doan' 
seem  consequentious  !     Hit  sho'  doan'  ! ' ' 

''Dat's  right!"  ''Sho'  '  nough !  "  "  Dat' s 
de  truf  !  ' '  cried  various  sisters,  evidently 
in  full  sympathy. 

"So  all  week  I'se  been  wrastlin'  wid  de 
ideed  at  we  alls  got  to  raise  some  money  an 
buy  us  a  silvah  pitcher  an'  silvah  glasses 
befo'  de  nex'  communion.  Can't  we  tek 
up  a  'scription?" 

With  that  she  sat  down  and  a  "miserable 
silence  settled  over  the  Httle  room  full  of 
' '  sistahs. ' '  At  last  old  Ceeley  Suttles  spoke 
up  bravely: 

"  De  chu'ch  sho'  needs  communion 
things,  an'  I  says  Amen  to  de  prescrip- 
tion \  but  you  can't  'spect  me  to  gib  no

an'  lef  me  'er  five  chil'en  to  keep 'long  uv 
mah  nine.  Dat' s  mos'  mo'  chil'  en  dan  we 
can  say  grace  ovah  ! ' ' 

"An  I'se  got  seben  chil'en  an'  six  head 
uvgran'  chil'en  for  to  brung  up,"  quickly 
put  in  Riar  Milsap,  rocking  mournfully  to 
and  fro,  twirling  her  thumbs  cog-wheel 
fashion. 

' '  I  can  gib  a  lee-tle  towards  hit,  but  you 
alls  know  mah  man  can' t  wuck  much  '  cause 
uv  havin'  dat  air  spine  in  his  back,  an'  I'se 
only  got  three  washin's,"  added  Lize  Mas- 
sey. 

As  sheep  jump  over  a  fence  after  a 
thoughtless  leader,  so  went  the  excuses  one 
after  another.  The  "  'portant  projec'  " 
seemed  doomed  to  ignominious  defeat.  As 
a  last  straw  some  one  reminded  the  society 
that  the  preacher's  salary  was  far  from  paid 
and  not  even  all  "  prescribed." 

Suddenly  Car' line  Kitt  rose  laboriously 
to  her  feet  (she  weighed  210  pounds), 
swayed  back  and  forth,   waving  her  arms

Panting  for  breath  she  sat  down,  while 
loud  rose  the  din  of  applause.  When  she 
could  be  heard.  Ruby  Johnson  finished

''We'll  gaddah  at  Sis'  Todd's  an'  go

So  the  tide  was  turned,  and  by  and  by, 
almost  hysterical  with  enthusiasm,  the  so- 
ciety adjourned  to  finish  the  ironings  and 
get  ready  for  the  charity  work  in  town  the 
next  day. 

Early  in  the  morning,  over  the  mountain, 
went  a  most  comical  procession;  fat  aunties, 
tall,  sHm  aunties,  old  ones  and  young  ones, 
all  carrying  upon  their  heads  great  baskets 
heaped  up  with  beautifully  ironed  clothes. 
It  was  no  uncommon  sight,  for  twice  a  week 
each  carried  similar  loads  to  and  fro  over 
the  road,  and  they  often  came  and  went  in 
groups;  but  never  did  they  make  quite  so 
hvely  a  noise  or  separate  upon  the  street 
corners  with  so  much  laughter  and  well 
wishing. 

But  a  still  noisier  scene  took  place  on  the 


bridge  at  sundown  as  they  gathered  one 
by  one,  triumphantly  spread  out  their 
''pieces  ob  money"  on  the  flat  railing, 
and  related  the  day's  experiences.  They 
hadscrubbed  kitchens  and  porches,  cleaned 
windows,  cellars  and  attics,  scoured  grates, 
shaken  carpets,  swept  whole  houses  from 
top  to  bottom,  dressed  chickens,  nursed 
babies,  and  blackened  stoves.  Ceeleyhad 
even  helped  pack  household  goods  for  mov- 
ing, and  Lize  Massey  had  dug  potatoes. 

At  many  houses  they  had  pleaded  in 
vain  for  work,  but,  undaunted,  had  gone 
on  until  each  had  managed  to  earn  a  re- 
spectable day's  wages. 

Nor  had  they  come  back  empty  handed 
for  themselves.  Southern  women  are  pro- 
verbially generous  to  their  help,  especially 
such  help  as  comes  from  Ishkooda.  Queenie 
Todd  bore  a  flatiron  on  her  head,  and  in- 
verted over  it  a  huge  old  armchair.  Lize 
Massey  had  three  pairs  of  slightly  worn  shoes 
dangling  from  a  string  over  her  shoulder. 
Ceeley  Suttles  had  a  big  ham  bone  for  "a 
stew. ' '  Many  had  bundles  of  old  clothing; 
Ruby  Johnson  proudly  exhibited  two  pairs 
of  lace  curtains,  and  Car' line  Litt,  proud- 
est of  all,  displayed  a  cast-off  fur  cape  that 
would  not  begin  to  meet  around  her  ample 
shoulders.    Ah,  but  it  was  a  happy  crowd  ! 

When  all  the  returns  were  in.  Ruby  an- 
nounced that  the  collection  amounted  to 
nine  dollars  and  forty-five  cents.  She  was 
unanimously  appointed  to  "speechify  at  ■ 
chu'ch  in  de  mawin',  an'  tell  dem  lazy 
niggah-men  dat  we  alls  gwine  hab  things 
tight  r' 

' '  Speechify ' '  she  surely  did  !  Even  the 
preacher  hung  his  head  under  her  rapid  fire 
of  hot  words.  After  exhibiting  the  precious 
$9.45,  and  telling  how  they  had  earned  it, 
she  cried  at  the  top  of  her  voice  : 

"  We's  jes'  'shamed  uv  dis  yere  Bethel- 
Zion  Baptis'  Chu'  ch  wid  hits  plastrun  faUin'

Some  Secrets  in  French  Cookery 

By  the  Author  of  '■'■  Modei-n  Housecraft^ ^ 
[Fro7n  The  Epictire) 


FRENCH  cookery  has  enjoyed  the 
reputation  for  being  the  best  cook- 
ery in  the  world,  but  whether  it  has 
really  deserved  this  high  praise  is  a  matter 
of  doubt.  It  does  not  suit  the  average 
EngUsh  taste  and  digestion  nearly  so  well 
as  EngHsh  methods  of  cooking  do,  when 
these  are  really  what  they  ought  to  be,  but 
whenever  we  want  to  be  smart  we  have  re- 
course to  French  combinations  and  French 
names,  thinking  to  improve  our  table 
thereby,  and  all  because  of  this  high  repu- 
tation. In  doing  so,  the  average  Enghsh 
cook  often  makes  the  most  absurd  mistakes 
in  little  things,  as  well  as  the  one  great  mis- 
take of  attempting  to  express  herself  in  a 
language  of  which  she  does  not  know  the 
commonest  phrases. 

I  am  not  going  to  argue  the  point  of 
whether  or  no  French  cookery  is  the  best 
or  not,  per  se;  what  I  want  to  show  here 
is  that  its  excellence  depends  on  a  few  cer- 
tain special  items  receiving  due  atten- 
tion. 

There  are  several  reasons  why  French 
cookery  is,  as  a  rule,  superior  to  ours,  the 
chief  one  being  that  a  great  interest  is 
taken  in  the  subject  by  all  people.  The 
French  mind  takes  pleasure  in  thinking 
about  and  discussing  the  composition  and 
preparation  of  a  meal,  and  as  a  result  of 
this  previous  care,  the  meal  is  carefully  en- 
joyed when  it  is  on  the  table.  And  this 
is  not  at  all  because  they  are  a  gluttonous 
people  —  far  from  it.  It  arises  from  a  just 
appreciation  of  the  value  of  food  materials, 
and  an  epicurean  appreciation  of  the  time, 


trouble  and  thought  involved  in  the  prep- 
aration of  a  meal. 

At  the  table  you  will  hear  Monsieur 
gravely  discussing  the  relative  merits  of  the 
fish  being  served  au  beurre  noir,  or  a  la 
sauce  blanche,  while  in  the  kitchen  Madame 
and  her  daughters  have  exercised  their 
minds  over  the  suitabiHty  of  serving  this 
meat  with  that  vegetable  in  such  and  such 
a  manner.  They  have  been  to  the  market 
to  buy  and  make  their  good  bargains,  and 
they  have  met  a  friend  who  has  imparted 
the  discovery  of  a  new  method  of  serving 
some  choice  morsel  just  in  season.  After 
all  this  concentration  of  interest,  is  it  sur- 
prising that  the  dinner  is  eaten  with  satis- 
faction and  enjoyed  by  all  concerned  with 
it?  Do  you  hint  at  a  waste  of  time? 
Not  at  all  .  .  .  all  this  took  up  very  little 
more  time  than  you  would  give  to  the 
careless,  unthinking  putting  together  of 
your  roast  and  pudding,  but  while  it  was 
being  done  they  gave  to  its  doing  all  the 
attention  and  respect  which  it  rightly 
deserved  —  they  did  not  slight  it  by  indif- 
ference and  affected  dislike.  When  they 
come  to  the  table  they  are  quite  as  intel- 
lectual, quite  as  vivacious  and  full  of  talk 
about  every  other  subject  in  the  world  as 
you  could  wish.  Then,  in  addition  to  this 
taking  a  vital  interest  in  the  food  subject, 
they  allow  time  for  slower  and  more  careful 
methods  of  cooking  which  their  favorite 
casseroles  and  terrlues  recpiire.  The  stove 
and  its  pots  and  pans  are  treated  respect- 
fully and  kept  daintily,  so  that  they  are  a 
pleasure   to   contemplate    and   use.     The 


268 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


secret  of  those  dainty  stews  and  soups  de- 
pends on  long,  slow  cooking,  not  upon 
the  clever  mixing  of  superior  ingredients. 
In  nine  cases  out  of  ten,  the  meat  which 
they  use  is  much  inferior  to  English  meat, 
and  the  vegetables,  though  pleasanter  to 


look  at  and  handle,  because  so  clean,  are 
not  really  finer  or  better  grown  of  their 
kind.  Nor  is  butter  so  cheap,  nor  is 
variety  so  great  as  with  us.  It  is  the  care 
that  is  given  to  the  dish,  which  makes  it 
what  it  is,  not  the  quality  of  its  materials. 


Diet  in 'Childhood,  Sickness,  and  Old  Age 


By  MAR  V  D.   CHAMBERS 

Seventh  Paper 
Sickness  and  Health 
I.     Health 


BEFORE  beginning  our  study  of  the 
effects  of  sickness  on  the  human  or- 
ganism it  will  be  well  to  consider 
briefly  what  are  the  marks  and  signs  of 
that  ideal  health  which  is,  or  should  be,  our 
birthright  —  so  that  we  may  earnestly  strive 
and  work  toward  it,  as  toward  any  other 
ideal.  And,  speaking  of  ideals,  how 
wonderful  is  the  development  in  physical 
ideal,  from  that  of  the  savage,  who  prized 
health  and  vigor  in  order  that  he  might 
benefit  himself  and  overcome  others,  to 
that  of  the  present  day,  which  prizes  health 
and  vigor  as  a  means  to  the  overcoming  of 
self  and  the  helping  of  others.  Surely,  this 
is  the  mark  of  a  spiritual  evolution. 

But  let  us  return  to  the  signs  of  health. 
-Not  only  should  there  be  absence  of 
pain,  but  in  a  condition  ol  ideal  health  there 
will  be  a  consciousness  of  well-being,  a  pos- 
itive delight  in  the  goodness  of  the  physi- 
cal life.  That  this  is  not  dependent  on 
youth  is  proved  by  the  testimony  of  Car- 
naro,  who  said  that  life  can  be  an  earthly 
paradise  after  the  age  of  eighty-five. 

Sleep  should  be  sound,  refreshing,  and 
dreamless.  It  should  come  immediately 
on  lying  down  for  the  night,  and  should 


last  unbroken  until  morning  —  when  wak- 
ing should  be  accompanied  by  a  sensation 
of  restored  vitality. 

The  vegetative  functions  of  the  body 
should  be  unconsciously  performed. 

There  should  be  a  good  appetite  for 
simple,  and  slightly  seasoned  lood. 

Work  should  be  undertaken  with  zest, 
with  a  positive  enjoyment  in  the  mental  or 
muscular  activity;  a  joy  in  the  overcoming 
even  of  one's  own  resistance. 

There  should  be  abihty  for  sustained 
effort.  This,  the  great,  one  might  almost 
say  the  sole,  requisite  of  success,  is  depen- 
dent more  commonly  on  physical  well-being 
than  on  mental  equipment. 

There  should  be  ability  to  control  emo- 
tion, not  only  the  expression  of  emotions, 
such  as  laughter  (at  the  wrong  time  and 
place),  sorrow,  anger  and  impatience,  — 
but  there  should  be  abihty  to  control  the 
emotions  themselves.  It  is  well  known 
that  certain  mental  states,  notably  grief  and 
fear,  actually  cause  an  unhealthy  action  in 
the  secretions  of  the  body,  a  change  in  the 
very  nature  of  the  secretions  themselves. 

Toward  attaining  this  enviable  condition 
of  ideal  health,   food  is  the  prime  factor, 


DIET  IN  CHILDHOOD,  SICKNESS  AND  OLD  AGE 


food,  which  is  of  the  right  kind,  taken  at 
the  right  time,  eaten  with  reHsh,  thorough- 
ly masticated.  And  after  all,  when  we 
teachers  of  dietetics  have  preached  and  ex- 
horted according  to  our  several  ability,  per- 
haps the  individual,  who  is -intelligent  on 
the  subject,  is  the  best  judge  of  what  is 
best  for  him.  There  are  a  few  important 
**Thou  shalt  nots,"  and  after  that  the 
best  diet  is  largely  a  matter  of  personal 
idiosyncrasy. 

Pure  drinking  water  naturally  has  its 
place  under  the  heading  of  food,  and  so 
has  pure  air.  This  should  be  enjoyed  out 
of  doors,  and  for  a  rninimum  period  of  three 
hours  each  day,  at  least  one  of  which  should 
be  spent  in  exercise. 

Nervous  persons  should  eat  freely  of  the 
easily  digested  fats,  cream,  butter,  olive  oil, 
etc. 

Not  less,  but  rather  more  important  is 
the  cultivation  of  a  healthy  optimism. 
There  is  always  a  bright  side,  —  look  deter- 
minedly on  that  if  you  would  preserve  the 
alkalinity  of  the  blood  and  the  healthfulness 
of  all  the  secretions.  A  friend,  who  at- 
tributed her  vigorous  health,  at  the  age  of 
ninety,  to  what  she  called  her  ' '  incorrigible 
optimism,"  said  once,  ''If  you  lose  your 
arm  in  a  railroad  accident,  just  think  how 
fortunate  you  are  not  to  have  lost  your 
light  arm."  ''But  if  it  should  be  the 
right  arm  ?  "  we  questioned,  skeptically. 
"Then  think  how  fortunate  you  are  not 
to  have  lost  both  !  " 

II.    Sickness 

Sickness  may  be  defined  as  an  effort  of 
nature  to  restore  equilibrium.  The  energy 
which  should  be  expended  in  carrying  on 
the  normal  functions  of  the  body  is  diverted 
to  combat  disease,  with  the  result  that  there 
is  general  disturbance  and  malaise.  Hence, 
■sickness  is  really  an  effort  to  get  well. 

The  effects  of  sickness  in  general,  and  of 
acute  disease,  vary  in  degree,  rather  than 
in  kind.  They  are  chiefly:  Fever,  re- 
sulting in  loss  of  weight,  and  increased  ex- 
cretion of  urea,  carbon  dioxide,  and  water; 
lessened  efficiency  or  scantier  secretion  of 
the  digestive  fluids,  i.    e.,   saliva,   gastric 


juice,  pancreatic  fluid,  bile,  producing  de- 
fective assimilation,  loss  of  appetite,  and 
sensitiveness  of  stomach;  and  lack  of  ner- 
vous "  tone.  "  It  will  be  easily  seen  that 
food  is  often  the  one  determining  factor  in 
the  question  of  recovery;  hence,  appropri- 
ate dieting  is  fully  as  necessary  as  medica- 
tion. 

The  problems  in  diet  for  the  sick  are 
two  : 

1.  To  keep  up  the  bodily  strength  by 
sufficient  nourishment. 

2.  To  administer  this  nourishment  in 
an  easily  assimilated  form,  so  as  to  tax  the 
energies  of  the  patient  as  little  as  possible. 

These  problems  will  be  considered  in  de- 
tail. 

' '  Sufficient  Nourishment ' '  means  at 
least  a  ' '  life  ration, ' '  which  is  estimated  to 
be  about  75  gms.  of  protein  and  2000  cal- 
ories of  energy. 

The  "work  ration"  in  this  country  is 
supposed  to  be  125  gms.  of  protein  and 
3000  to  4000  calories  of  energy,  from 
which  it  will  be  seen  that  very  much  the 
larger  part,  indeed  about  two-thirds  of  the 
diet  for  work,  is  needed  for  the  running  of 
the  body  itself  as  a  machine,  maintaining 
the  action  of  the  heart,  lungs,  etc. ,  as  well 
as  the  large  amount  of  heat  necessary  to 
life.  Hence,  unless  the  illness  should  be  a 
very  brief  one,  a  life  ration  must  be  fur- 
nished to  the  organism. 

The  apparently  contradictory  statements 
that  over-feeding  has  been  the  death  of 
many  invahds  and  sick  people,  and  that 
under-feeding  is  a  common  error  in  the 
treatment  of  patients,  depleting  the  strength 
to  a  dangerous  degree,  are  both  true. 
A  few  grams  of  unsuitable  or  indigestible 
food  may  be  over-feeding,  taxing  the 
strength  of  the  patient  to  eliminate  the  re- 
sulting evils;  while  on  the  other  hand  the 
customary  diet  of  liquids  and  "slops  "  is 
apt  to  be  a  process  of  slow  starvation,  con- 
taining as  they  generally  do,  an  insuffi- 
ciency of  nutrients,  and  faihng  even  to  ap- 
proximate the  "life  ration." 

The  consideration  of  the  second  prob- 
lem and  its  implications  must  form  the 
topic  of  another  paper.

gether with  the  Post-office,  County,  State,  Post-

A  GREAT    SOCIAL    MOVEMENT 

ACCORDING  to  Professsor  Harvey 
A.  Wiley,  "the  next  great  social 
movement  in  America  will  be  toward 
an  improvement  in  American  Cooking." 
Professor  Wiley  found,  he  says,  "The 
sanitary  conditions  better  in  France  than  at 
home,  the  cooking  better,  and  the  people 
healthier  in  appearance. ' '  And  their  supe- 
riority he  attributes  to  the  better  cookery 
of  the  French  people. 

The  superior  excellence  of  French  cook- 
ery has  long  been  well  known.  Compared 
with  it  that  of  all  other  civilized  lands  is 
largely  an  imitation.  We  read  French 
books  on  the  culinary  art.  We  make  use 
of  their  terms  and  copy  their  menus  and 


recipes.  And  why  should  this  be  the  case  ? 
Certainly  it  is  not  because  the  French  have 
greater  natural  resources.  The  Food  Sup- 
plies of  America  are  greater  than  those  of 
any  other  land.  Nor  is  it  because  of  greater 
pecuniary  means  that  the  French  excel  in 
their  cookery.  The  French  housewife  is 
proverbially  the  most  frugal  in  the  world, 
and  yet  France  is  thought  to  be  the  home 
of  gourmets. 

The  truth  is,  it  would  seem,  the  French 
people  have  from  time  immemorial,  culti- 
vated the  fine  art  of  cooking  and  eating. 
Of  the  Frenchman,  it  is  said,  he  has 
learned  how  to  dine.  He  partakes  of  his 
savory  dishes,  and  sips  his  light  wine  at 
leisure,  and  with  evident  reUsh,  whereas 
the  average  American  not  infrequently 
eats  his  heavy  meal  in  haste,  often  stand- 
ing or  sitting  on  a  stool  at  a  lunch  counter. 
As  a  people,  the  art  of  cooking  and  eating 
is  not  yet  wide-spread  among  us.  We  fail 
to  realize  fully  the  ill  effects  that  surely  fol- 
low from  poor  cooking,  and  consequent 
malnutrition,  to  say  nothing  of  the  ex- 
travagance and  waste  that  are  to  be  traced 
to  the  same  source. 

Home  economy  is  a  much  neglected 
science.  People  are  just  beginning  to 
grasp  its  full  significance.  A  movement 
toward  an  improvement  in  American  cook- 
ing bears  with  it  an  advancement  in  the 
physical,  mental  and  moral  welfare  of  the 
nation. 


FOOD  AND  CLOTHING 

A  DISTINGUISHED  Professor  and 
teacher  said  recently  that  young 
women,  especially,  did  not  dres  sap- 
propriately  to  withstand  the  rigor  of  our 
climate  in  winter.  He  advised  a  class  of 
art  students  to  don  flannels  as  a  protection 
against  the  inclemencies  of  the  season.  The 
reason  he  gave  for  this  advice  was  that, 
in  contending  with  a  low  temperature,  the 
physical  strength  and  vitality  were  exhausted 
long  before  the  most  trying  months  of  win- 
ter and  spring  had  come. 

This  is  an  old  yet  an  undoubtedly  truth- 
ful story.      People  look  forward  with  dread 


EDITORIALS 


271 


to  the  days  of  late  winter  and  early  spring. 
At  this  season  those  who  can  are  wont  to 
migrate  to  a  milder  clime.  But  for  the  most 
of  us  it  follows  that  it  is  the  part  of  wisdom 
to  keep  up  our  physical  strength  and  vitality 
by  means  of  suitable  clothing  and  whole- 
some, nourishing  food.  We  must  learn  as 
far  as  possible  to  adapt  our  mode  of  life  to 
the  ever  changing  conditions  of  the  seasons. 
We  do  this  in  part,  naturally;  it  were  better 
that  it  be  done  intelligently  and  scientifi- 
cally. Most  people  will  give  attention 
enough  to  dress,  there  is  little  doubt  of 
that,  regardless  of  the  cost;  the  use  of  fit- 
ting, hygienic  clothing  is  another  matter. 
But  the  chief  tactor  in  maintaining  health 
is  a  proper  diet,  and  how  few  people  are  will- 
ing to  make  the  question  of  food  even  a 
subject  of  thought,  much  less  of  study  ! 

That  we  eat  to  live  is  only  silently  ad- 
mitted, by  many.  Would  that  it  were  not 
so  !  We  all  want  to  live  well,  but  let  others 
take  thought  of  the  means  of  so  doing.  And 
yet  people  are  coming  more  and  more  to 
regard  the  physical  well  being  of  the  race 
as  an  indispensable  condition  of  individual 
welfare  and  social  betterment.  The  wise 
and  thoughtful  not  only  wish  to  live  well, 
here  and  now,  but  that  all  others  may  do 
the  same. 

Ruskinsaid,  and  the  saying  will  bear  re- 
peating: ''Sure  good  is  first  in  feeding 
people,  then  in  dressing  people,  then  in 
lodging  people,  and  lastly  in  rightly  pleasing 
people,  with  arts  or  science,  or  any  other 
subject  of  thought. ' '  And  the  question  still 
is  to-day  how  to  feed  and  clothe  and  lodge 
and  please  people  rightly.  Right  living  is  of 
far  higher  importance  than  the  abihty  merely 
to  live  at  all.  Certainly  at  this  season  it  were 
wise  for  all  to  look  well  to  their  feeding, 
clothing  and  lodging,  that  they  may  the 
better  withstand  the  ill  effects  of  the  pro- 
longed cold  and  changeable  cHmate  to  which 
our  northern  latitudes  are  subjected. 


SCHOOL  FEEDING  AND  HYGIENE 

HE  Second  International  Congress 
on  School  Hygiene,  held  at  the 
London  University,  South  Kensing- 


T 


ton,  proved  a  most  interesting  and  impor- 
tant event.  In  many  ways  it  afforded  evi- 
dence that  in  order  to  reform  nations  we 
must  begin  with  the  children.  Of  course 
questions  of  sanitation  were  the  order  of 
the  day:  the  well  planned,  ventilated, 
hghted  and  drained  building;  means  for 
personal  cleanliness  and  physical  develop- 
ment, medical  and  dental  inspection.  But 
we  are  glad  to  say  that  the  feeding  ques- 
tion was  by  no  means  neglected.  The 
necessity  for  taking  measures  to  insure  that 
youthful  scholars,  kept  hard  at  work  at  their 
lessons,  should  not  be  starved. 

Connected  with  the  Congress  was  an 
instructive  Exhibition,  including  a  compre- 
hensive loan  collection,  diagrams  and  sta- 
tistical tables  and  reports  from  various  ed- 
ucational authorities,  both  home  and  for- 
eign. It  could  be  seen  from  these  exhibits 
that  the  question  of  feeding  school  chil- 
dren is  better  understood  on  the  Continent. 
To  begin  with,  the  means  for  feeding  chil- 
dren in  the  elementary  schools  are  far 
more  systematised.  In  view  of  the  strong 
remarks  made  in  and  out  of  Parliament  as 
regards  our  own  permissive  Act,  this  was 
particularly  useful. 

Another  interesting  matter  was  to  com- 
pare the  albums  giving  particulars,  (often 
illustrated  by  photographs),  of  the  teaching 
of  domestic  economy  and  cookery,  in 
schools,  in  London  County  Council  schools, 
schools  under  the  Committee  for  Scotland, 
and  public  schools  abroad.  The  Scandi- 
navians, Germans  and  French  stand  very 
well  in  these  matters.  In  the  Swedish  sec- 
tion there  was  a  model  of  a  kitchen  at- 
tached to  the  Adolf  Fredrick  School  at 
Stockholm.  There  were  four  large  cook- 
ing stoves,  with  ample  space  all  round 
each  of  them.  Some  distance  from  them 
was  a  row  of  tables,  and  at  the  end  of  the 
long  room,  a  demonstration  platform,  with 
a  large  blackboard  near  it,  while  a  sink 
was  at  one  corner.  It  appeared  to  be  ad- 
mirably adapted  for  teaching  a  large  num- 
ber of  children.  Another  good  feature  of 
the  Swedish  schools  was  the  collection  of 
large  colored  diagrams  of  food  animals, 
joints,  and  also  pictures  showing  how  di-

JAILS,  hospitals,  police  courts,  officers 
of  the  law,  physicians,  and  all  the  ap- 
pliances of  healing  and  regeneration 
represent,  after  all,  not  the  great  tides 
of  life  out  of  which  a  nation  grows.  Di- 
sease, degeneracy,  delinquency,  are  evils 
that  call  for  processes,  but  no  community 
can  thrive  where  they  come  first  and  en- 
gage the  attention  of  the  majority.  If  in  a 
tree,  a  garden  or  a  field  of  wheat  the  pests 
are  too  numerous,  they  will  overpower  the 
growing  vegetation  and  spoil  the  crop.  It 
is  only  when  these  things  are  incidental 
and  are  easily  controlled  that  the  earth  re- 
wards the  cultivator  with  its  bounty.  Hos- 
pitals are  needed,  but  one  should  be 
enough  for  ten  thousand  homes.  Jails  are 
still  necessary,  but  one  ought  to  hold  the 
criminals  of  a  city.  Reformers  are  among 
the  elect  saints  and  heroes;  but,  when  a 
nation  needs  many  of  them,  it  is  a  sign  not 
of  health, .  but  of  disease  and  decay.  — 
Christian  Register. 


The  death  of  M.  Gouffe,  the  venerable 
authority  on  the  culinary  art,  occurred  at 
his  residence  at  St.  Leonards  on  Sea  re- 
cently. By  his  death,  says  Food  and 
Cookery,  the  country's  oldest  confectioner 
is  removed,  for  he  attained  the  ripe  age  of 
94.  M.  Gouffe  was  head  pastry  cook  to 
her  late  Majesty,  Queen  Victoria,  an  hon- 
orable and  responsible  position,  which  he 
lield  for  a  period  of  41  years.  He  retired 
from  active  service  in  1881,  and  enjoyed 
wonderful  health  and  vigor  until  quite  re- 
cently. An  attack  of  influenza,  followed 
by  double  pneumoni,  was  the  cause  of 
his  death. 


Bad  cooking  is  the  cause  of  innumerable 
evils.  Few  are  taught  to  consider  the  re- 
sults either  of  good  or  ill  feeding.  Happily 
for  us  all,  appetite  in  this  matter  is  not  an 
entirely  mischievous  guide.  A  memorial 
has  been  presented  to  Congress  to  estabHsh 
a  Bureau  of  Domestic  Science.  The  me- 
morial calls  attention  to  the  vast  outlays  of 


the  Government  to  investigate  the  source 
of  food  supolies  and  methods  of  produc- 
tion, and  a  ,ks  of  what  value  is  this,  if  the 
food  product  \:  ruined  in  cooking,  through 
ignorance. 

The  object  of  cooking  is  to  render  nat- 
ural food  products,  "which  by  themselves 
and  in  their  original  state  would  be  either 
indigestibh  and  unwholesome,  or  unat- 
tractive ana  injurious  to  man,"  nutritious 
and  palatable.  —    anitary  Home. 


Man  thrive3  on  almost  any  kind  of  diet 
common  to  a  nadon,  the  general  conditions 
being  favorable.  Both  races  and  indi- 
viduals living  entirely,  or  chiefly,  on  plant 
food,  or  on  plant  food  with  the  addition  of 
eggs  and  milk,  are  stronger  and  happier, 
other  thing  being  equal,  than  races  and 
individuals  living  chiefly  on  the  flesh  of 
beasts.  Frugal  persons  and  nations  live 
better  and  longer  than  others,  and  are  more 
free  from  disorders  of  body  and  mind.  — 
Hon.  R.  Russell. 


"  No  one  can  rise  to  the  higher  Devel- 
opments of  the  soul  who  does  not  regulate 
his  Hfe  in  acccordance  with  the  strictest 
hygienic  rules  and  correct  educational 
principles.  Among  the  first  factors  in  the 
practice  of  such  rehgion  stands  a  well  reg- 
ulated diet."  — F>r.  Reich,  of  Zurich. 


''It  is  one  of  the  axioms  of  physiology 
that  the  majority  of  the  diseases  of  man- 
kind are  due  to,  or  connected  with,  per- 
versions of  nutrition. "  —  Frof.  Chittenden. 


' '  I  have  come  to  the  conclusion  that 
more  than  half  the  disease  that  embitters 
life  is  due  to  avoidable  errors  in  diet."  — 
Sir  Henry  Thompson,  M.  D. 


''Food  is  the  point  about  which  turns 
the  whole  problem  of  degeneracy. ' '  —  Al- 
fred Eichholz,  M.  D. 


"  One  half  of  the  cost  of  living  among 
the  middle  lower  classes  is  the  cost  of  food, 
but  a  large  part  of  the  other  part  is  spent 
in  clothing." 


Canned  Pineapple  Fritters     (Page  277) 


Seasonable  Recipes 


Bv  JANET  M.  HILL 


IN  all  recipes  where  flour  is  used,  unless  otherwise  stated,  the  flour  is  measured  after  sifting  once. 
When  flour  is  measured  by  cups,  the  cup  is  filled  with  a  spoon,  and  a  level  cupful  is  meant.     A 
tablespoonful  or  a  teaspoonful  of  any  designated  material  is  a  level  spoonful  of  such  material. 


Cream  of  Corn-and-Tomato  Soup 

Put  a  can  of  com,  a  sliced  onion,  two 
stalks  or  branches  of  celery,  a  sprig  of  pars- 
ley and  three  pints  of  chicken  or  veal  broth 
(broth  made  from  the  bones  and  trimmings 
of  a  roast  of  veal  or  poultry  is  good  for  this 
purpose),  over  the  fire  to  simmer.  Stir  fre- 
quently,.  that  the  corn  may  not  ''stick" 
to  the  saucepan,  and  let  cook  half  an  hour; 
add  a  pint  of  canned  tomatoes  and  let  cook 
about  fifteen  minutes,  then  press  the  mix- 
ture through  a  puree  sieve;  add  two  tea- 
spoonfuls  of  salt  and  half  a  teaspoonful  of 
paprika  and  let  heat  to  the  boiling  point. 
In  the  meantime  melt  one-fourth  a  cup  of 
butter;  in  it  cook  one-fourth  a  cup  of  flour, 
then  add  one  pint  of  rich  milk  or  i)art  milk 
and  part  cream,  and  stir  until  boiling;  then 
stir  into  the  corn  and  tomato  mixture  and 
serve  at  once. 

Turbans  of  Fish   Fried  in   Batter 
This  dish  may  be  made  of  any  fish  from 


which  fillets,  or  thin  pieces  of  flesh,  free 
from  skin  and  bone,  may  be  taken.  For  the 
illustration  shown  here,  halibut  was  used. 
Have  the  shces  of  hahbut  cut  from  a  small 
fish  and  below  the  opening  in  the  body. 
The  shces  should  not  be  more  than  half  an 
inch  thick.  Remove  the  skin  and  bones, 
thus  securing  four  pieces  from  each  slice. 
Put  the  pieces  in  a  dish,  pour  over  them  a 
tablespoonful  of  oil,  half  a  tablespoonful  of 
vinegar  and  a  few  drops  of  onion  juice,  or 
lay  over  them  a  few  slices  of  onion.  Cover 
and  set  aside  for  half  an  hour  or  longer. 
Have  ready  the  yolk  of  a  hard-cooked  Qgg) 
press  this  through  a  sieve,  and,  also,  a  sweet 
red  pepper  boiled  soft;  add  the  pepper  pulp 
to  the  egg  and,  also,  two  tablespoonfuls  of 
grated  cheese  and  one-fourth  a  teaspoonful 
of  salt.  Mix  the  whole  together  thoroughly, 
then  use  to  spread  on  the  pieces  of  fish. 
Roll  each  piece  and  pass  through  it  a  wooden 
toothpick  dipped  in  melted  butter.  Dip  the 
rolled  fillets  in  batter  and  fry  about  six  min- 
utes in  deep  fat.      Serve  with  Spanish  sauce

274

and  mashed  potato,  in  which  tomato  puree 
takes  the  place  of  milk  or  cream. 


Turbans  of    Fish  Fried  in  Batter     (Page  273) 


Mashed   Potato    for  Fried    Fish 

Pare  and  boil  the  potatoes  as  usual,  in 
salted  water,  then  drain  and  press  them 
through  a  ricer  or  vegetable  press.  Add  a 
generous  piece  of  butter,  plenty  of  salt  and 
cooked  tomatoes,  pressed  through  a  sieve,  to 
moisten  as  needed.  Serve  in  a  separate  dish 
or  on  the  plate  with  the  fish. 

Spanish  Sauce  for  Fried  Fish 

Cook  two  tablespoonfuls  of  butter  until 
it  is  a  nut  brown  color;  add  three  table- 
spoonfuls  of  flour  and  cook  and  stir  until 
browned,  then  add  a  cup  of  stock,  made  of 
the  fish  trimmings,  two  tablespoonfuls  of 
lean  ham,  cut  in  bits,  half  a  celery  stalk, 
cut  in  bits,  an  onion  chopped,  two  cloves, 
four  slices  of  carrot  and  five  or  six  pepper 


and  stir  until  boiling;  add  salt  as  needed 
and  serve. 

Crab   Mousse 

Pound  one  cup  (generous 
measure)  of  crab  meat  and 
the  unbeaten  whites  of  two 
eggs  in  a  mortar  until  the 
whole  is  reduced  to  a  pulp; 
continue  pounding  while 
gradually  adding  half  a  cup 
of  cold,  white  or  Bechamel 
sauce.  After  all  the  sauce 
has  been  added  and  the  mix- 
ture is  perfectly  smooth, 
press  it  through  a  puree 
sieve  and  set  aside  to  become  chilled.  Beat 
the  whites  of  two  eggs  until  dry,  and  one 
cup  of  double  cream  until  firm  to  the  bot- 
tom of  the  bowl;  add  half  a  teaspoonful  or 
more  of  salt  and  a  generous  dash  of  pep- 
per to  the  crab  mixture,  then  fold  in  the 
beaten  egg  and  cream.  Turn  into  a  well- 
buttered  mould,  set  the  mould,  on  many 
folds  of  paper,  into  a  pan,  and  pour  in  boil- 
ing water  to  half  the  height  of  the  mould. 
Let  cook  until  the  mixture  is  firm  without 
allowing  the  water  to  boil.  Serve  turned 
from  the  mould,  with  peas  and  fish  Becha- 
mel or  Hollandaise  sauce.

Mignons    with 
Anna 

Have  as  many  English  mufiin  rings  as  in- 
dividuals to  serve,  and  the  same  number  of 
rounds,  cut  from  a  fillet 
of  beef.    Let  the  rounds 
of  beef  be  of  the  same 
size  as  the  mufiin  rings. 
Butter  the  inside  of  the 
rings  and  set  them  on  a 
baking  sheet,  also  but- 
tered.     Fill    the    rings 
compactly  with  pared, 
raw      potatoes,      sliced 
very  thin  and  dipped  in 
melted   butter.      Also 
season    the    potatoes 
with  salt  and  pepper  as  they   are    set   in 
place.     Put  half  a  teaspoonful  of  butter  on 
the  top  of  each  ring  of  potatoes.    Let  cook

on  the  floor  of  a  hot  oven  about  ten  minutes, 
then  with  a  spatula  turn  ring  and  pota- 
toes together,  and  let  cook  ten  minutes 
longer.  With  the  oven  at  a  proper  tem- 
perature the  potatoes  will 
be  browned  on  both  sides 
and  well  cooked  throughout 
in  twenty  minutes.  Remove 
the  potatoes  and  rings  (with 
a  spatula  or  broad  knife)  to 
the  serving  dish,  then  dis- 
card the  rings.  Have  the 
small  fillets  of  beef  nicely 
broiled;  set  one  above 
each  round  of  potato  and 
put  a   little    Bernaise    sauce    on    each. 

Bernaise  Sauce 

Chop  fine  a  peeled  shallot  (or  slice  of 
onion);  crush  five  pepper  corns  ;  chop 
parsley  fine  to  make  half  a  tablespoonful; 
to  these  add  three  tablespoonfuls  of  vine- 
gar and  let  stand  on  the  back  of  the  range 
until  the  vinegar  is  reduced  one-half,  then 
strain  through  a  cheese  cloth  and  press  out 
all  the  liquid.  To  this  hquid  add  the 
beaten  yolks  of  two  eggs  and  one-fourth  a 
cup  of  soft  butter;  set  the  dish  over  hot 
water  and  stir  and  cook  until  the  mixture 
thickens.  The  sauce  will  thicken  in  a 
gentle  heat  and  care  must  be  taken  to 
avoid  curdling.  This  sauce  is  used  either 
hot  or  cold. 


rounds  of  toast  in  boiling,  salted  water  and 
set  them  on  a  hot  serving  dish;  spread 
lightly  with  butter  and  then  with  the 
ham  mixture   and  finish  by  placing   care-

Moulded  Chicken 

Wash  carefully  a  disjointed  chicken,  put 
in  a  saucepan,  and  pour  over  boihng  water 
to  cover;  heat  quickly  to  the  boiling  point 
and  let  boil  five  minutes,  then  draw  to  a 
cooler  part  of  the  range  to  simmer  until 
tender.  Shake  or  pick  the  meat  from  the 
bones  and  return  the  bones  to  the  liquid 
in  which  the  chicken  was  cooked.  Add  a 
stalk  of  celery,  a  sprig  of  parsley,  half  an 
onion,  into  which  two  cloves  have  been 
pressed,  and  about  half  a  teaspoonful  of 
salt.  Let  simmer  until  reduced  to  consid- 
erably less  than  a  pint.  Put  the  meat, 
light  and  dark,  alternately,  into  a  mould 
lined    with    paper,   and   decorated  with  a 


Creamed  Ham   with 

Poached   Eq^o-s 

Put  three-fourths  a  cup 
of  milk  in  a  double  boiler 
with  half  a  stalk  of  celery 
and  a  slice  of  onion;  let 
cook  fifteen  minutes,  then 
pick  out  the  celery  and 
onion  and  stir  in  a  level 
tablespoonful  and  a  half, 
each,  of  butter  and  flour, 
creamed  together;  stir  until  the  mixture 
thickens,  then  cover  and  let  cook  ten  min- 
utes; stir  in  three-fourths  a  cup  of  fine- 
chopped  ham  (cold,  boiled)  and  let  stand 
to  become  very  hot.      Dip  the   edges    of 


Fillet  Mignons  with  Potatoes  Anna     (Page  274) 


hard-cooked  egg,  cut  in  slices,  then  strain 
the  liquid  over  the  meat;  let  stand  in  a 
cool  place  about  half  an  hour,  then  set  a 
closely-fitting  board  upon  the  meat  and 
dispose  a  weight  upon  it.      Let  stand  until

the  next  day,  when,  if  the  meat  has  been 
cooked  tender,  it  may  be  shced  with  a 
sharp  knife. 


Ham  and  Macaroni  Timbales  (For  Two) 


Ham  and    Macaroni    Timbales 
(For  Two) 

Have  a  scant  half  a  cup  of  macaroni  broken 
into  short  pieces.  Set  them  to  cook  in  a 
saucepan  of  rapidly -boihng  water,  to  which 
a  teaspoonful  of  salt  has  been  added.  When 
tender  drain  in  a  colander  and  let  cold  water 
run  through  it  for  a  few  minutes,  then  turn 
on  to  a  cloth.  Cut  part  of  the  macaroni  into 
one-fourth  an  inch  slices;  take  these,  one 
by  one,  on  a  skewer,  dip  in  melted  butter 
and  use  to  hne  well-buttered  moulds.  The 
number  of  moulds  needed  will  depend  on 
the  size.  To  half  a  cup  of  boiled  ham, 
chopped  fine,  add  one-fourth  a  cup  of  the 
macaroni  rings,  one  beaten  egg,  one-fourth 
a  teaspoonful,  each,  of  salt  and  paprika,  and 


folds  of  paper,  in  a  baking  dish,  and  pour  in 
boiling  water  to  come  half  way  to  the  top 
of  the  moulds.  Let  cook  in  the  oven  or 
on  top  of  the  range  until 
the  centers  feel  firm.  The 
water  should  not  boil  around 
the  moulds. 

Tomato    Sauce    for 

Ham    Timbales 

Melt  two  tablespoonfuls 
of  butter;  in  it  cook  a  slice 
of  onion  till  softened  and 
yellowed;  add  a  level  table- 
spoonful  of  flour  with  a  few  grains  of  salt, 
and  cook  until  frothy,  then  add  half  a  cup 
of  tomato  puree  and  cook  and  stir  until 
boiling;  then  strain  over  the  rest  of  the 
macaroni  and  let  stand  over  hot  water  to 
reheat  the  macaroni.  Cheese  may  be 
added  at  pleasure. 

Rechaufee  of   Ham  with   Broiled 
Apples 

Melt  about  half  a  tumbler  of  currant  jelly 
in  a  saucepan  set  over  boiling  water;  lay  in 
it  some  thin  slices  of  cold,  boiled  ham  and 
cover  closely  to  become  hot.  In  the  mean- 
time core  three  or  more  apples,  cut  them 
into  shoes  or  rings  half  an  inch  thick  and 
remove  the  skin,  also  any  of  the  seed  cav- 
ities that  may  remain.      Have  ready  a  hot

pared moulds.      Set  the  moulds,  on  many 


broiler;  rub  it  over  with  a  bit  of  fat  ham  or 
with  a  piece  of  butter  in  a  cloth.  Set  the 
apples  in  the  broiler  and  let  cook  over  the

Cold    Baked    Bean 
Sandwich,  Club  Style 

Butter  two  slices  of  Bos- 
ton   brown   bread;   on    one 
of  these  dispose  a  heart-leaf 
of  lettuce    holding   a     tea- 
spoonful  of  cold  Bernaise  sauce,  above  the 
sauce  set  a  generous  tablespoonful  of  cold, 
baked  beans,  then  another  lettuce  leaf  and 
sauce;  then  finish  with  a  second  slice   of 
bread,  a  tablespoonful  of  beans,  a  floweret 
of  cauliflower,   and  a  teaspoonful  of  the 
sauce  over  the  cauliflower. 

Boston    Baked    Beans,    Reheated 

with  Bacon 

Have  ready  as  many  rounds  of  toast  as 
individuals  to  serve.      The  toast  may  be 
made  of  any  kind  of  bread  preferred,  with 
all  crust  removed.     Put    the  beans  in  an 
agate  dish;  add  two  or  three  tablespoon- 
fuls  of  boiling  water,  cover  the  dish  closely 
and  set  into  the  oven  to  become  very  hot. 
In  the  meantime,  roll  strips  of  thin  bacon 
(one  for  each  service;  into  a  compact  shape, 
pass  a  wooden  toothpick  through  each   to 
hold  it  in  shape,  put  all  in  a 
frying  basket,  and  let  cook 
in  deep  fat  about  two  min- 
utes   or   until    crisp;    drain 
carefully.      Dip  the  edges  of 
the  slices  of  toast  in  boiling, 
salted  water  and  dispose  on 
a  hot  dish;  spread  each  slice 
with    butter,    dispose   the 
beans  on  the  slices,   and  a 
roll  of  bacon  on  each. 


Rechaufee  of  Ham  with  Broiled  Apples 

and  dot  here  and  there  with  bits  of  butter, 
using  in  all  two  or  three  tablespoonfuls; 
pour  in  three  or  four  tablespoonfuls  of  boil- 
ing water,  and  let  cook  in  the  oven  until  hot 
throughout  and  a  bit  of  potato,  here  and 
there,  is  lightly  browned.  For  a  change  a 
Httle  onion  juice  or  grated  onion  may  be 
mixed  with  the  potato.  Fat  taken  from 
the  top  of  the  water  in  which  corned  beef 
was  cooked  may  be  used  in  place  of  the 
butter. 

Canned  Pineapple  Fritters 

Drain  the  slices  of  pineapple  from  the 
syrup  in  the  can.  Dip  the  slices,  one 
at  a  time,  in  fritter  batter  and  fry  in 
deep  fat  to  a  delicate  amber  tint.  Drain 
on  soft  paper.  Serve  at  once  with  a 
hot  sauce  made  of  the  syrup  from  the 
can.

Boston  Baked  Beans  on  Toast     Bacon  Rolls

Batter  for  Pineapple   Fritters 

Beat  one  egg;  add  one-half  a  cup  of 
milk  and  gradually  stir  into  one  cup  of  sift- 
ed flour,  sifted  again  with  a  level  teaspoon- 
ful and  a  half,  each,  of  baking  powder  and 
sugar,  and  one-fourth  a  teaspoonful  of  salt. 


Rasped  Rolls 

Scald  two  cups  of  milk  and,  when  cooled 
to  a  lukewarm  temperature, 
add  half  a  cup  of  water  in 
which  a  yeast  cake  has  been 
dissolved,  and  stir  in  about 
three  cups  of  sifted  flour. 
Beat  the  mixture  until  very 
smooth,  then  cover  and  let 
stand  in  a  temperature  of 
about  70°  F.  until  fight  and 
puffy;  now  add  one  tea- 
spoonful of  salt  and  one- 
third  a  cup  of  melted  butter 
or  other  shortening,  and 
three  or  four  cups  of  sifted  flour  and  stir 
these  to  a  dough.  Knead  the  dough  about 
fifteen  minutes  or  until  smooth  and  elastic, 
then  cover  and  set  aside  to  become 
doubled  in  bulk.  Shape  into  small  ovals 
of  about  two  ounces  each,  and  set  in  a 
baking  pan  some  distance  apart,  that  they 
may  not  touch  each  other  in  baking.  When 
light  bake  about  twenty-five  minutes. 
When  baked,  with  a  lemon  grater  remove 
the  thin  glossy  crust  that  completely  covers 
the  roll,  and  so  leaves  a  light  brown, 
rasped  exterior.  Where  large  quantities 
of  these  rolls  are  prepared,   a  machine  is 


Rasped    Rolls 


Beat  the  mixture  thoroughly;  cover  and 
let  stand  a  short  time  before  using.  To 
use  this  batter  for  fish  omit  the  sugar. 


used  for  this  purpose.  A  fittle  more  time 
is  required  in  using  a  lemon  grater,  but  the 
result  is  the  same.

Stuffed  Dates 

Work  into  some  well-ripened  (by  stand- 
ing over  night)  fondant 
some  fine-chopped  pecan 
nut  meats  or  meats  of  shag- 
bark  walnuts,  and  enough 
sherry  wine  to  flavor.  Have 
ready  dates,  from  which  the 
stones  have  been  taken;  fill 
the  open  space  in  the  center 
of  each  with  the  prepared 
fondant,  press  the  pulp  to- 
gether, to  enclose  the  fill- 
ing and  then  roll  in  fine 
granulated  sugar. 

Individual  Apple  Dumplings 

Butter  six  English  muflin  rings,  and  set 
these  on  a  shallow,  buttered,  agate  baking 
pan.  Fill  the  rings  with  pared  apples, 
sHced  very  thin.  Pack  the  slices  in  closely 
and  pile  them  up  well.  Sift  together,  sev- 
eral times,  one  cup  and  one-half  of  sifted 
pastry  flour,  a  scant  half  a  teaspoonful  of 
salt  and  three  level  teaspoonfuls  of  baking 
powder.  With  the  tips  of  the  fingers 
work  one-fourth  a  cup  of  shortening  into 
the  flour  mixture,  and  then  gradually  mix 
in  a  generous  "half-cup  of  milk  or  water. 
Drop  the  dough  on  to  the  apples  in  the 
rings,  giving  it  a  smooth 
round  shape.  Let 
bake  about  twenty  min- 
utes. With  a  spatula 
or  broad  knife  remove 
each  dumpling  to  the 
serving  dish,  turning  it 
crust  side  down  and 
discarding  the  ring. 
Serve  with  cream  and 
sugar,  hard  sauce,  or 
with  a  fruit  hard  sauce.

Sauce 


and  half  a  cup  of  canned  raspberries, 
pressed  through  a  sieve  to  exclude  the 
seeds. 


Caramel  Sponge      (See  Page  x) 

Raspberry  Charlotte  Russe 

Line  tall,  handsome  glasses,  sherbet  cups 
or  paper  cases,  with  lady  fingers,  letting  the 
cake  come  up  about  an  inch  above  the  re- 
ceptacles. Have  at  hand  a  cup  of  red  rasp- 
berry pulp  and  juice  (canned  raspberries 
pressed  through  a  sieve),  and  the  same 
measure  of  heavy  cream.  Beat  the  cream 
until  firm  to  the  bottom  of  the  bowl,  then 
gradually  fold  the  raspberry  puree  into  it. 
Let  stand  to  become  thoroughly  chilled, 
then  dispose  in  the  cake-lined  receptacles. 

Bell's  Prize  Recipe  of  Pound  Cake 


Date  Shape  with  Whipped  Cream      (See  page  x) 


Beat  half  a  cup  of  butter  to  a  cream; 
then  gradually  beat  in  a  cup  of  pow- 
dered or  fine  granulated  sugar;  then  fold 
in   the   white    of    an    egg,    beaten    dry, 


Beat  two-thirds  a  cup  of  butter  to  a  cream; 
gradually  beat  in  one  cup  of  sugar,  then  the 
well-beaten  yolks  of  four  eggs.  Sift  together 
several  times,  one  cup  and  0  half  of  flour 


2«0 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


and  a  level  teaspoonful  of  baking  powder. 
Add  a  little  of  the  flour  to  the  cake  mixture, 
then  add  one  tablespoonful  of  milk.  Then 
beat  in,  alternately,  the  whites  of  four  eggs, 
beaten  dry,  and  the  rest  of  the  flour.  Pour 
into  a  cake  tin,  lined  with  paper,  and 
bake  in  a  moderate  oven  thirty-five  or 
forty  minutes.  Frost  with  Bell's  chocolate 
frosting. 

One  Egg  Chocolate  Cake 

Beat  half  a  cup  of  butter  to  a  cream; 
gradually  beat  in  one  cup  of  sugar,  then  one 
egg,  beaten  light.  Sift  together  two  cups 
of  sifted  flour,  half  a  teaspoonful  of  soda, 
one  teaspoonful  of  cream  of  tartar,  two  level 
teaspoonfuls  of  cinnamon,  a  level  teaspoon- 
ful, each,  of  clove  and  allspice,  and  one- 
fourth  a  nutmeg,  grated;  add  this  mixture 
to  the  first,  alternately,  with  one  cup  of 
sweet  milk.  Bake  in  a  sheet  and  cover  ox 
not  with  frosting. 

Frosting  for  One  Egg  Cake 

Stir  one  cup  of  sugar,  two  squares  of 
chocolate,  half  a  cup  of  water  and  a  table- 


of  cinnamon  and  a  few  grains  of  salt.  Beat 
the  yolk  of  an  egg;  gradually  beat  in  one- 
third  a  cup  of  sugar,  three  tablespoonfuls 
of  melted  butter  and  one-fourth  a  cup  ot 
milk;  stir  these  into  the  flour  mixture,  then 
beat  in  two  ounces  of  chocolate,  melted  over 
hot  water,  and  the  white  of  one  egg,  beaten 
dry.  Turn  into  two  or  three  buttered 
cups  and  steam  about  twenty-five  minutes. 
Serve  with 

Sultana  Sauce 

Pick  the  stems  from  one-fourth  a  cup  of 
sultana  raisins;  add  a  cup  of  boiling 
water  and  let  simmer  half  an  hour,  adding 
water  if  needed,  then  add  half  a  cup  of 
sugar  and  let  boil  to  a  syrup;  flavor  to 
taste. 

A    Winter    Dessert 

Break  one  dozen  macaroons  into  pieces; 
cut  candied  cherries  into  quarters  or,  in- 
stead of  the  cherries  use  quince  or  other 
preserves,  or  white  grapes.  The  grapes 
should  be  skinned,  cut  into  halves  and 
seeded,  the  preserves,  cut  into  small  pieces. 


A  Winter  Dessert 


spoonful  of  butter  over  a  slow  fire  until  the 
sugar  and  chocolate  are  melted;  let  boil 
twenty  minutes;  beat  until  cool;  add  a  tea- 
spoonful of  vanilla,  beat  again  and  spread 
on  the  cake. 

Steamed  Chocolate  Pudding 

Sift  together,  several  times,  one  cup  of 
sifted  flour,  one  level  teaspoonful  and  a  half 
of  baking  powder,  nearly  half  a  teaspoonful 


To  half  a  pint  of  heavy  cream,  add  from 
three  tablespoonfuls  to  one-fourth  a  cup  of 
sugar  and  flavor  to  the  taste.  Beat  the 
cream  solid  to  the  bottom  of  the  bowl  and 
add  the  prepared  macaroons  and  fruit  and 
mix  thoroughly  but  lightly  together.  Set 
paper  doihes  on  six  small  plates;  on  these 
dispose  a  slice  of  canned  pineapple,  with 
the  cream  mixture  above.  Serve  thor- 
oughly chilled.

"  A  little  Stelton  cheese  brought  up  the  rear  with  a  nice  salad.     This  made  way  for  a  foolish,  trifling  dessert  of  muscatel 
grapes,  guava  jelly,  and  divers  kickshaws  diluted  with  agreeable  wines."  —  Charles  Read.
