Cookery  for  Young  Housekeepers 


By  JANET  M.  HILL 

LESSON  VII 

Cooking  of  Fish 


FISH  is  neither  palatable  nor  whole- 
some unless  it  be  well  cooked;  it  is 
cooked  enough  when  the  flesh  will 
separate  easily  from  the  bones.  When 
this  condition  is  reached,  the  coagulation 
of  the  nitrogenous  juices  has  been  carried 
far  enough  and  the  fibres  —  under  proper 
conditions  of  moisture  —  are  reduced  to  a 
gelatinous  consistency.  The  fibres  of  all 
varieties  of  fish  being  short,  the  flesh  is 
always  tender.  The  varieties  that  are  de- 
ficient in  fat,  halibut,  cod,  haddock,  bass, 
pickerel,  etc.,  (we  do  not  refer  to  shell 
fish),  are  easily  digested.  The  main  thing 
that  claims  our  attention  in  the  cooking  of 
fish  is  the  coagulation  of  the  nitrogenous 
juices  with  as  Httle  loss  of  these  juices  as 
possible.  When  fish  has  been  cooked  in 
water,  the  water  should  be  used  or  the 
process  is  extremely  wasteful. 

Broiled  Fish 

Any  fish  that  presents,  when  dressed,  a 
thin  flat  appearance,  as  mackerel,  bass,  or 
bluefish,  or  a  fish  that  may  be  cut  in  slices, 
as  halibut,  salmon  or  sword  fish,  may  be 
broiled.  While  all  the  fish  mentioned 
may  be  broiled,  preference  would  be  given 
to  an  oily  fish,  as  it  is  not  easy  to  baste 
a  fish  while  broiling,  and  a  dry  fish  can 
ill  aff"ord  to  lose  any  of  its  juices.      Mack- 


erel,  salmon,  and  bluefish  are  especially 
good  for  broiling.  A  whole  mackerel  and 
slices  of  salmon  and  bluefish  should  be 
purchased.  Sometimes  it  is  possible  to  buy 
half  a  bluefish,  cut  lengthwise  of  the  fish. 
Half  a  four-pound  fish  may  not  be  too 
much  for  two,  as  the  portion  left  over 
makes  a  most  palatable  salad. 

Broiled  Bluefish 

Heat  the  broiler  and  rub  over  the  wires 
on  the  inside  with  a  piece  of  fat  salt  pork. 
Set  the  fish  in  place,  and  the  broiler  over 
the  coals  or  under  the  gas  flame,  with  the 
flesh  side  towards  the  heat  and  rather 
close  to  it.  After  a  few  seconds,  less  than 
a  minute,  draw  the  brofler  farther  from  the 
fire,  to  finish  the  cooking.  Over  the 
coals  turn  the  broiler,  occasionally,  to  cook 
the  skin  side  of  the  fish.  As  the  skin 
burns  easily  the  cooking  must  be  done, 
largely,  on  the  flesh  side.  Whenever  the 
flesh  side  is  to  be  turned  to  the  coals, 
brush  it  over  with  a  little  melted  butter. 
Cook  from  fifteen  to  twenty  minutes. 
When  the  fish  is  cooked,  remove  the  broiler 
to  a  zinc-covered  table  or  board  (a  labor- 
saving  article  the  value  of  which  in  a 
kitchen  can  scarcely  be  estimated)  or  to  a 
large  agate  or  tin  dish,  then  press  the  back 
of  a  four-pronged  fork  down  upon  the  fish 


284 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


285 


in  such  a  manner  that  two  prongs  of  the 
fork  will  be  on  either  side  of  a  wire  of  the 
broiler,  now  gently  draw  the  fork  down  the 
wire  the  full  width  of  the  fish,  thus  sep- 
arating the  flesh  from  the  wire  of  the 
broiler  ;  repeat  this  with  each  wire  on  one 
side,  then  turn  the  broiler  and  repeat  on 
the  other  side,  when  the  fish  may  be  slipped 
—  skin  side  down  —  from  the  broiler  to  a 
platter  made  hot  to  receive  it.  Spread  the 
fish  with  mEitre  d  'h6tel  butter  and  serve 
at  once. 

Broiled  Halibut  or  other  Sliced  Fish 

Heat  and  oil  the  broiler  as  above  j  brush 
over  both  sides  of  the  halibut  with  butter 
or  salt  pork  fat,  then  set  in  place  in  the 
broiler ;  cook  ten  seconds  and  turn;  repeat 
this  for  three  or  four  minutes,  then  baste 
with  fat  and  draw  farther  from  the  fire  and 
let  cook  fifteen  to  twenty  minutes,  turning 
each  three  or  four  minutes.  Use  a  four- 
tined  fork  to  separate  the  fish  from  the 
broiler  (see  preceding  recipe)  and  slide  to 
a  hot  platter.  Spread  with  maitre  d  'hotel 
butter  (page  32,  June-July,  1907)  and 
serve  at  once. 

Fried  Fillets  of  Fish 

Have  thin  pieces  of  fish  freed  from  fat 
and  bone;  season  with  salt  and  pepper  if 
approved  and,  if  the  flavor  be  agreeable, 
rub  each  fillet  with  the  cut  side  of  an 
onion.  Have  ready  sifted  bread  crumbs 
fi-om  the  center  of  a  loaf  of  bread,  and  an 
egg  beaten  and  diluted  with  two  table- 
spoonfuls  of  water;  dip  the  pieces  of  fish 
in  the  egg,  then  in  the  crumbs,  to  cover 
them  completely,  then  shake  off  superflu- 
ous crumbs.  Dip  a  frying  basket  into  a 
kettle  of  hot  fat,  set  it  on  a  tin  plate  and  in 
it  dispose  two  or  four  pieces  of  fish;  lower 
the  basket  into  the  hot  fat,  of  which  there 
should  be  enough  to  cover  the  fish,  and 
let  cook  from  three  to  six  minutes.  If  the 
fish  be  not  rolled,  three  or  four  minutes  of 
cooking  will  be  enough.  Rolled  fillets 
will  take  from  four  to  six  minutes. 

Testing  Fat  for  Frying 
When  the  bowl  (a  Scotch  bowl  costing 


about  35  c.  is  the  best  shaped  and  most 
durable  utensil  that  can  be  had  for  frying) 
of  fat  has  been  over  the  fire  for  a  Uttle  time 
after  it  has  melted,  drop  in  a  crumb  of 
bread;  if  the  bread  browns  on  one  side 
while  you  count  thirty  as  the  clock  ticks,  it 
is  of  the  right  temperature  for  the  fish. 
When  the  fish  is  cooked,  let  it  drain  in  the 
basket,  then  remove  it  to  a  tin  plate  cov- 
ered with  tissue  or  blotting  paper.  Let 
stand  at  the  door  of  the  oven  a  moment, 
then  serve.  Do  not  let  the  pieces  of  fish 
touch  each  other,  either  in  the  basket  or 
while  draining  on  the  paper,  or  they  will 
lose  their  crispness.  Fish  may  be  fried  in 
batter  after  the  same  manner  as  in  egg 
and  crumbs.  The  fish  may  be  more  easily 
covered  with  batter  than  with  crumbs. 
Small  fish,  as  brook  trout  and  smelt,  may  be 
fried  in  the  same  manner.  They  will  be 
cooked  through  when  of  a  good  golden 
color. 

Fish  Fried  in  Batter 

Season  the  pieces  of  fish  as  above;  have 
them  flat  or  roll  them,  turban  shape,  and 
run  through  each  a  wooden  tooth  pick, 
dipped  in  melted  butter,  that  it  may  be 
easily  removed.  Immerse  the  fish  in  bat- 
ter, drain  by  holding  it  on  a  fork,  and 
lower  it  into  the  fat,  with  or  without  a  bas- 
ket. Let  cook  from  three  to  six  minutes, 
or  until  nicely  browned,  then  drain  and 
serve.  With  fat  at  the  proper  temperature 
no  appreciable  fat  will  be  absorbed. 

Sauted  Fish.       Halibut. 

A  small  slice  of  halibut  or  fillets  of  a 
white  fish,  bass  or  pickerel,  or  small  whole 
fish,  as  brook  trout  and  smelts,  may  be 
sauted.  Wash  a  slice  of  halibut  and  wipe 
■it  carefully.  Dredge  a  board  with  Indian 
meal  or  white  flour  and  shake  on  a  Httle 
salt,  then  lay  the  slice  of  fish  in  the  mixture; 
pat  it  a  little  that  it  may  take  up  the  flour 
or  meal  and  salt,  then  turn  it  over  that  the 
other  side  may  be  covered  in  the  same 
manner.  Have  ready  a  frying  pan  of  such 
width  that  it  will  take  the  full  length  of  the 
slice  of  fish  ;  in  this  cook  a  slice  of  fat  salt 
pork  until  the  fat  is  well  tried  out;  put  in 


286 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


the  fish  and  let  cook  over  a  steady  fire  un- 
til well  browned  on  one  side,  then  turn  the 
fish  and  brown  the  other  side.  The  fire 
must  not  be  too  hot,  or  the  fish  will  be 
burned.  If  it  is  not  hot  enough,  the  ni- 
trogenous juices  will  run  from  the  fish, 
and  the  sHce  will  not  hold  together.  Prop- 
erly cooked  the  fat  will  not  be  absorbed,  and 
the  fish  may  be  easily  turned  and  lifted 
from  the  frying  pan  in  perfect  shape. 

Sauted  Brook   Trout 

Clean  the  fish  by  slitting  them  open  in 
front,  after  the  heads  have  been  removed. 
After  all  have  been  cleaned,  dip  the  fingers 
in  salt  and  take  hold  of  the  top  of  the  back 
bone  with  the  left  hand,  then  with  a  knife 
in  the  right  hand  separate  the  backbone 
and  small  bones  attached  to  it  from  the 
flesh,  by  scraping  or  pushing  the  flesh  from 
the  bones.  Wash  in  salted  water,  dry  on 
a  cloth,  then  roll  in  Indian  meal,  to  which 
a  little  salt  has  been  added;  lay  side  by  side 
in  a  frying  pan,  containing  hot,  salt  pork  fat 
■to  cover  the  bottom  of  the  dish;  let  brown 


on  one  side  and  then  turn  to  brown  the 
other  side.  Properly  cooked  these  will 
absorb  no  appreciable  fat.  Smelts  maybe 
cooked  in  the  same  way,  though  these  are 
quite  as  often  egged  and  crumbed  and 
cooked  in  deep  fat.  The  bones  are  not 
always  removed,  as  they  can  be  easily  taken 
out  after  cooking. 

Slice  of  Salmon,  Boiled 

Butter  a  piece  of  cloth  large  enough  to 
take  a  slice  of  salmon;  tie  the  cloth  secure- 
ly at  the  corners  and  lower  it  into  a  sauce- 
pan containing  enough  lukewarm  water  to 
cover  the  fish;  add  a  teaspoonful  of  salt, 
cover  the  saucepan,  and  heat  quickly  to 
the  boihng  point,  then  let  cook  six  or 
eight  minutes.  Lift  up  the  cloth  with  a 
fork  under  the  knot;  let  drain  well,  then 
remove  to  a  tin  plate,  untie  the  knot  and 
turn  the  fish  on  to  a  hot,  folded  napkin,  set 
on  a  hot  plate.  Cook  other  fish  in  the 
same  manner,  the  time  of  cooking  depend- 
ing upon  the  thickness  of  the  fish.  Serve 
with  boiled  potatoes  and  egg  or  caper  sauce. 


Katrina's  Portrait 


By   GRACE  STONE  EIELD 


Her  eyes  are  bluest  of  the  blue. 
Twin  gentians,  born  each  day  anew. 
Of  flaxen  pigtails  she  has  two 
And  little  foot  in  wooden  shoe. 
And  O,  her  kerchief,  white  as  snow, 
Sets  off  a  cheek  where  roses  blow; 
And  redder  than  the  roses,  glow 
The  sweet  lips  of  Katrina. 


And  just  to  think,  —  a  lass  like  that 
May  wed  some  burgher,  stolid,  fat;  — 
A  rotund  wooer,  fond  of  schnapps, 
Will  win  that  dimpled  hand,  perhaps; 
Which  then  must  knit  his  stockings  gray. 
With  wifely  patience,  day  by  day, 
As  smile  upon  him.  Withe  and  gay, 
The  sweet  lips  of  Katrina. 


If  you  could  speak,  what  would  you  say  ? 
Dear,  dimpled  ''Dutch  maid  of  today. " 
Would,  your  soft  gutterals  of  speech 
Grant  me  the  story  I  beseech  ? 
Ah  well;  —  contentment  be  your  lot, 
Whether  in  mansion  or  in  cot. 
They  smile  at  me  and  answer  not  ! 
The  sweet  lips  of  Katrina.

Contributions  to  this  department  will  be  gladly  received.      Accepted  items  will  be  paid  for  at 
reasonable  rates. 


By  Way  of  Economy 

A  YOUNG  woman  who  lives  within  easy- 
driving  distance  of  a  large  college  and 
two  preparatory  schools  for  girls,  has  built 
a  small  greenhouse  as  an  extension  to  her 
own  home.  Here  she  raises  such  flowers 
as  pansies,  violets,  mignonette,  carnations, 
Toses,  etc.,  and  these  she  has  gained  per- 
mission to  sell  in  some  of  the  dormitories 
of  the  neighboring  institutions.  She  has 
Tegular  days  and  hours  for  each  building, 
and  has  been  able  to  support  herself  by  the 
sales  thus  made. 

A  FRIEND  has  for  many  years  been  the 
possessor  of  a  flat- topped  reed  organ. 
The  black  walnut  case,  though  plain,  is 
really  good,  and  therefore  it  has  been 
,given  house  room,  though  the  works  within 
long  since  ceased  to  be  of  any  value.  Re- 
cently the  owner,  without  the  outlay  of  a 
penny,  changed  this  old  organ  into  a  useful 
desk.  With  her  own  hammer  and  screw 
driver  she  removed  the  keyboard  and  other 
apparatus,  till  she  reached  a  plain  wooden 
surface  about  three  feet  above  the  floor. 
This  made  the  bottom  of  a  box  of  which  the 
organ  Hd  was  the  cover.  The  box  is  about 
one  foot  deep.  Neatly  lined  with  green 
blotting  paper,  it  makes  an  admirable  place 
in  which  to  keep  writing  materials.  The 
pedal  straps  were  cut,  letting  the  pedals 
•drop  flat  upon  the  floor.  Over  these  a  small 
-doormat  (soft  rug)  was  spread,  lengthwise, 
through  the  opening  in  the  case  intended 
for  the  pedals,  and  these,  thus  covered, 
made  a  warm  and  comfortable  foot  rest. 
IVhen  the  organ  case  is  closed,  the  flat  top 


furnishes  a  broad,   smooth    surface    upon 
which  to  write.  L.  S. 

A  Cook  Book  Protector 

IT  cannot  fail  to  be  a  source  of  annoy- 
ance to  any  neat  housekeeper  to  have 
her  favorite  cook  book  or  magazine  disfig- 
ured by  a  spatter  of  egg  or  milk  or  an  equal- 
ly unsightly  smudge  of  butter,  even  if  the 
said  cook  book  is  only  one  of  those  really  re- 
liable little  pamphlets  that  are  sent  for  the 
asking.  They  are  all  worth  taking  care  of. 
I  use  the  following  device  and  find  it  fully 
answers  the  purpose. 

Procure  a  nice,  clean  sheet  of  ordinary 
window  glass,  and  be  sure  that  it  is  clean,  of 
course;  put  it  over  the  page  where  the  recipe 
is  you  wish  to  use,  and  you  will  find  you  can 
read  the  type  quite  as  well  or  even  better,  and 
the  glass  will  also  keep  the  book  open,  with 
perhaps  a  weight  of  some  kind,  if  the  book 
be  large,  and  there  is  no  possibility  of  any- 
thing injuring  the  best  of  your  books. 

Mrs.  C.  C.  H. 

*  *  # 

When  There  Is  an  Invalid  in 
the  House 

IF  any  of  you  have  an  invalid  in  the 
house,  as  I  hope  you  do  not,  just  pause 
and  think  what  life  must  be  to  that  one, 
how  irksome  the  time  often  becomes,  and 
be  as  patient  and  considerate  as  you  can. 

First  of  all,  if  possible,  place  a  comfort- 
able chair  without  cushions  (wicker  is  best) 
near  a  window  where  the  sun  shines  in,  and 
where  the  light  will  faU  on  the  left  side; 


288 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


under  the  chair,  to  rest  the  feet,  put  a  small 
hassock;  in  it  have  three  tiny  hair  pillows, 
one  to  be  put  at  the  small  of  the  back,  one 
for  the  neck,  and  one  for  the  elbow,  these 
to  be  covered  with  cretonne;  over  the  back 
throw  a  light  weight  shoulder  shawl  and 
knee  cover  for  cool  times,  so  as  to  be  in 
readiness  when  needed,  and  thus  avoid  dis- 
turbing any  one  to  get  them.  Let  the  in- 
vahd  feel  that  she  is  part  of  the  household, 
and  has  some  share  in  its  workings,  and  a 
share  of  personal  work  —  by  work  I  mean 
stocking  darning,  hemming  towels,  napkins 
or  curtains,  but  of  course  never  giving  her 
anything  heavy  to  handle. 

Near  the  chair  should  be  a  small  table, 
the  kind  with  a  shelf,  with  a  cover  over  it; 
on  the  shelf  have  a  scrap  book  and  tube  of 
photographers'  paste,  a  magazine  or  two, 
they  are  often  more  interesting  than  books, 
a  pretty  wooden  box  containing  manicure 
articles.  On  top  of  the  table  let  there  be  a 
well-equipped  writing  case,  a  completely 
furnished  work  bag,  a  box  containing  knit- 
ting and  crocheting  materials,  a  bottle  of 
''eau  de  cologne"  and  one  of  laven- 
der salts.  On  the  window  seat  have  a 
fern,  a  finger  bowl  half  filled  with  water, 
and  a  doiley.  These  are  means  of  comfort, 
easily  supplied,  and  an  aid  to  a  pleasant, 
independent  feeling,  simply  because  the 
patient  is  not  obliged  to  ask  for  everything 
she  needs. 

Encourage  an  invalid  to  do  dainty  things, 
and  let  her  know  she  is  really  helping  you; 
many  times  she  can  write  letters,  or  orders 
for  the  stores,  and  even  take  care  of  the 
housekeeping  accounts,  plan  the  meals,  and 
save  mother  much  time  and  brain  work,  too. 

Books  are  always  prized  as  entertainers 
for  invalids,  but  they  are  not  so  good  as 
magazines,  especially  those  devoted  to 
women' s  interests ;  a  magazine  can  be  picked 
up,  and  read  at  intervals,  and  anything  par- 
ticular may  be  cut  out  and  pasted  in  the 
scrap  book  for  future  reference.  Among 
the  useful  things  an  invalid  can  make,  beside 
those  mentioned,  are  bags  of  all  kinds  (bags 
are  indispensable),  dusters,  holders  for  the 
cook  to  use,  crocheted  or  knitted  reins  for 
children,  etc. 


In  one  of  our  exchanges,  recently,  was  a 
call  for  knitted  white  garters  for  an  old  lady, 
also  knitted  stomach  warmers  for  children, 
with  a  tab  in  front  to  put  a  safety  pin 
through,  and  they  were  unobtainable. 

Some  of  these  articles  could  be  made  for 
profit  as  well  as  pastime,  if  need  be,  and  I 
know  from  long  experience,  that  an  invalid 
is  better  off  busy  than  idle;  besides  she  is 
happier  when  she  feels  she  is  useful  to  those 
around  her.  Henry  Van  Dyke  says  "  Blessed 
be  drudgery. ' '  Show  your  invalid  that  her 
efforts  are  valuable,  and  you  would  not 
know  how  to  manage  without  her  help. 
Give  an  encouraging  word  of  praise,  when- 
ever you  can,  coupled  with  a  pleased  look 
and  smile;  these  are  wonderfully  exhilarat- 
ing and  inspiring  in  her  future  efforts. 

Lucia  Noble. 

Household  Hints 

IN  washing  clothes,  if  you  desire  to  do 
away  with  rubbing  them  on  a  wash- 
board, with  its  attendant  wear  and  tear, 
not  only  on  the  clothes,  but  also  on  the 
hands,  arms  and  back,  the  following  method 
is  excellent :  —  To  the  amount  of  cold 
water  you  would  use  in  boiling  an  ordinary 
wash  boiler  full  of  clothes,  add  two  table- 
spoonfuls  of  turpentine,  and  the  same 
quantity  of  kerosene,  and  one  bar  of 
laundry  soap  (shaved).  Put  in  the  clothes 
and  let  them  come  to  a  boil,  but  do  not  let 
them  boil.  The  clothes  will  be  whiter 
when  rinsed  than  when  washed  in  the 
ordinary  way.  This  method  will  be  found 
particularly  beneficial,  in  washing  fine  lace 
curtains,  which  are  easily  damaged  if  rubbed 
on  a  board. 

A  few  drops  of  turpentine  on  a  woollen 
cloth  will  clean  tan  shoes,  and  a  drop  or 
two  of  lemon  juice  will  give  them  a  brilliant 
pohsh. 

To  remove  mildew  from  hnen,  soak  in  but- 
termilk and  spread  on  the  grass  in  the  sun. 

Mortar  and  paint  may  be  removed  with 
ease  by  the  use  of  hot  vinegar. 

To  remove  stains  from  silver,  which 
have  been  caused  by  eggs,  apply  dry  salt 
with  a  cloth. 


HOME  IDEAS  AND  ECONOMIES 


289 


Never  keep  vinegar  in  stone  jugs.  The 
acid  attacks  the  glazing,  which  is  said  to 
be  poisonous. 

Whiting  dampened  with  aqua  ammonia 
will  clean  brass  beds  beautifully. 

To  make  tough  meat  tender,  lay  it  a  few 
minutes  in  strong  vinegar  water. 

Equal  parts  of  skimmed  milk  and  water, 
warmed  together,  will  remove  fly  specks 
from  varnished  wood  work,  or  furni- 
ture. 

Something  that  is  new  to  me,  and  ''oh! 
so  good  "  is  a  Fruit  Frosting.  It  may  be 
served  on  various  kinds  of  puddings  or 
cake.  Take  one  large  apple,  or  two  small 
ones  (grated)  and  mix  with  a  cup  of  sugar 
and  the  white  of  one  egg.  Beat  for  about 
twenty  minutes,  or  till  stiff.  Other  fruits, 
as  bananas,  berries  and  cooked  apricots 
may  be  used  in  the  same  way. 

When  plaster  of  paris  ornaments  get  so 
dirty  that  they  are  apparently  ready  for  the 
rubbish  heap,  they  can  be  tinted  a  nice 
brown,  by  mixing  burnt  umber  with  regular 
shellac  and  applying  with  a  brush.  A 
variety  of  shades  can  be  produced  by  sub- 
stituting other  brown  paint  powders  for 
the  umber,  sienna  for  instance.  After  the 
application  of  this  stain,  the  ornaments 
can  be  washed  without  harming  the  color 
in  any  way. 

A  whisk  broom  is  a  very  good  thing  with 
which  to  sprinkle  clothes. 

A  teaspoonful  of  powdered  sugar  stirred 
into  a  bottle  of  cream  will  put  off  the  sour- 
ing process  at  least  twelve  hours,  provided 
the  cream  so  treated  be  kept  on  ice. 

If  powdered  sugar  is  used  instead  of 
granulated,  in  making  hard  sauce,  the 
sauce  will  be  very  much  smoother. 

To  people  living  in  places  where  the 
water  is  muddy  or  sandy,  it  may  not  be 
generally  known  that  alum  will  settle  the 
water.  The  alum  will  make  the  water 
hard,  but  borax  will  soften  it. 

A  very  good  floor  stain  can  be  made 
with  a  dry  pigment  (such  as  raw  umber 
for  a  brown  stain)  mixed  with  vinegar,  the 
mixture  to  be  as  thin  as  water.  It  is  a 
great  deal  cheaper  than  an  oil  stain.  It 
has  to  be  varnished  before  being  walked  on. 


Powdered  borax,  dampened  and  pressed 
under  the  finger  nails,  and  allowed  to 
remain  for  a  short  time,  is  an  excellent 
bleach,  and  is  a  great  deal  better  for  the 
nails  than  most  of  the  preparations  sold 
for  that  purpose. 

Hydrogen  Peroxide  is  a  wonderful  thing 
for  the  teeth.  It  not  only  whitens  them, 
but  also  purifies  and  disinfects  the  mouth. 
It  will  also  remove  ink  stains  from  the 
hands. 

I  never  throw  away  stockings  that  are 
beyond  mending.  By  folding  them  in  a 
square,  and  sewing  over  and  over  around 
the  edge  they  make  excellent  holders,  for 
use  around  the  stove  and  kitchen. 

Mrs.  R.  S. 


Making  Sundaes  at  Home 

SOME  novelties  in  the  way  of  nut  sun- 
daes may  be  made  at  home  for  dessert, 
or  for  an  evening  entertainment,  with  little 
trouble. 

Vanilla  ice  cream  is  the  preferred  foun- 
dation. Still  a  brick  of  Neapohtan  may  be 
used  if  preferred.  Choose  flaring  topped 
glasses.  In  the  country  hickory  nuts  may 
be  used;  in  the  city  choose  English  walnuts 
and  pecans,  filberts  and  almonds,  mixed 
and  chopped.  Sprinkle  these  over  the  ice 
cream,  pour  over  some  syrup  from  a  jar  of 
preserved  peaches,  and  top  off  with  a  spoon- 
ful of  whipped  cream.  In  some  places 
whipped  cream,  not  whipping  cream,  may 
be  bought;  it  is  so  stiff  that  it  will  keep 
well  in  the  refrigerator  until  wanted  for 
serving. 

Very  tender  figs,  with  raisins,  currants, 
and  citron,  with  a  little  of  the  best  and 
most  fragrant  cinnamon  are  desirable  for 
sundaes. 

Another  change  may  be  a  foundation  of 
vanilla  ice  cream,  with  banana  on  it,  cut 
in  slices,  and  some  apricot  syrup  and  a 
scattering  of  nuts.  If  you  have  not  time 
to  fix  the  nuts,  crumble  some  macaroons 
in  their  place.  If  preserves  are  not  ac- 
cessible, make  a  chocolate  sauce  to  use 
with  vanilla  ice  cream.  J.  D.  C. 


Ways  of  Entertainin; 


A   Mysterious  Evening 

THE  decorations  should  be  of  ever- 
green, with  flowers  appropriate  to  the 
season.  The  arrangements  should  be  a 
profound  secret  and  the  participants  all  dis- 
guised. Invitations  should  be  sealed  with 
wax,  bearing  the  word  ' '  secrecy. ' '  On  en- 
tering, the  guest  is  given  a  half  mask,  and 
is  then  conducted  by  four  masked  women, 
representing  the  seasons  and  bearing  fancy 
lamps,  to  another  room,  where  the  Seven 
Wise  Men  examine  the  card;  the  seventh, 
"Father  Time,"  interprets  the  cipher. 
The  guest  is  then  conducted  into  the  re- 
ception hall. 

A  curtain  should  be  here  drawn  back  by 
**  Father  Time,"  since  "only  Time  may 
reveal  what  lies  before  us. ' '  At  the  end  of 
the  entertainment  a  Herald  appears  and 
calls,  "Hear  ye,  hear  ye  !  Be  it  known 
unto  all  ye  merry  men  and  maidens  that  the 
Three  Fates  are  spinning  the  threads  of  your 
lives.  Ye  are  desired  to  pay  them  a  visit, 
that  you  may  learn  what  is  in  store  for  you. 
Hear  ye,  hear  ye  !  " 

The  Three  Fates  may  be  arranged  in 
another  room.  As  the  guests  pass  out  two 
pages  hand  each  guest  a  card  bearing  some 
prophetic  quotation.  The  hostess  can  then 
clear  the  room  for  a  social  hour. 

The  table  should  be  artistically  deco- 
rated, the  contents  of  every  dish  covered 
with  pretty  tissue  paper. 

The  program  should  he  something  as 
follows: 

1.  Piano  Duet 

2.  Tableau,  Father  Time 

3.  Vocal  Solo.     (In  Italian  Costume) 

4.  Tableau,  The  Seasons 

5.  Mandolin,  Flute  and  Guitar  Trio, 

Meditation  by  Moyer 

6.  Appropriate  Reading 

7.  Violin  Solo,  The  Crusader  by  Pryor 

S.     Prophecy.     (An  original  article,  should  be 

humorous) 
9.     Tableau,  The  Three  Fates. 

Father  Time 

Man  dressed  as  "  Father  Time ' '  stooping 


to  unlock  the  door.  Boy  dressed  as  "New 
Year" — Gieek  costume,  chaplet  on  head 
—  waiting  to  enter. 

The  Seasons 

Spring,  demi-blonde,  pale  green  dress 
with  short  sleeves,  low  neck,  wreath  and 
dress  decorations  of  spring  blossoms. 
Summer,  a  blonde,  white  dress,  short 
sleeves,  low  neck,  flowers  of  summer.  Au- 
tumn, demi-brunette,  corn-color  dress, 
wheat  and  autumn  leaves  on  hair  and 
dress,  basket  of  fruit  in  her  hand.  Winter, 
brunette,  pale  blue  evening  dress  and  a 
long  crimson  cape,  with  hood  thrown  over 
her  shoulders.  Holly  and  mistletoe  on 
hair  and  dress. 

The  Three  Fates 

Framed  in  by  draperies,  and  lighted  with 
quaint  colored  lamps,  the  Three  Fates  are 
seen  at  work.  Clotho  sits  at  the  wheel, 
Lachesis,  holding  the  thread,  turns  her 
sad,  imploring  eyes  toward  Atropos,  in 
whose  hand  are  seen  immense  shears. 
High  above  their  heads,  in  the  foreground, 
is  perched  an  owl,  and  below  is  this  in- 
scription : 

"  Spin,  spin,  Clotho  spin, 

Lachesis  twist  and  Atropos  sever 

Sorrow  is  strong  and  so  is  sin. 
But  only  God  endures  forever." 

Appropriate  favors  would  be  papier 
mache  figures  of  "Old  Father  Time," 
filled  with  assorted  candied  fruits,  including 
candied  rose  petals  and  violets.  "Snow- 
time  ' '  snapping  mottos  at  each  place  also 
cause  much  merriment. 


A    Figure  Seven  Social 

Bj>   CARRIE  MAY  ASHTON 

THE  Ladies'   Aid  Society  of  a  certain 
church  decided  to  give  a  Figure  Seven 
Social,  in  order  to  replenish  their  treasury. 
Seven  has  long  been  looked  upon  as  a 
symbol  of  luck  and  good  fortune. 

The     general     admission     was    placed 


290 


WAYS  OF  ENTERTAINING 


291 


at   the   low   price    of  seven    cents. 

In  one  corner  of  the  church  parlors  was 
a  fish  pond,  where  any  and  every  one  could 
fish  by  paying  seven  cents. 

Farther  along  was  a  table  containing  all 
manner  of  packages,  large  and  small,  prac- 
tical and  impractical,  amusing  and  other- 
wise. These  were  offered  for  sale  at  seven 
cents  each. 

At  the  rear  of  the  room  was  a  veritable 
gypsy  tent  presided  over  by  the  seventh 
daughter  of  the  seventh  daughter,  who  told 
fortunes  for  the  low  price  of  seven  cents,  but 
of  course,  if  you  paid  her  two  or  three,  or 
even  four  times  seven  cents,  you  obtained  a 
better  fortune. 

There  was  a  candy  booth  where  every- 
thing was  seven  cents  a  package.  Popcorn 
and  peanuts  and  gum  were  also  found  here. 

Ice  cream  and  a  variety  of  wafers,  maca- 
roons, kisses,  etc. ,  were  found  at  a  booth 
just  beyond. 

Supper  was  served  to  a  large  crowd. 

The  menu  is  given  below: 

Baked  Beans,  7  Cents 

Coffee  and  Rolls,  7  Cents 

Baked  Hash,  7  Cents 

Tea  and  Sandwiches,  7  Cents 

Coffee  and  Doughnuts,  7  Cents 

Fruit  Salad  and  Wafers,  7  Cents 

Bread  and  Butter  and  Pickles,  7  Cents 

Pumpkin  Pie  and  Cheese,  7  Cents 

Ice  Cream,  7  Cents 

A  good  program  was  rendered  in  the 
evening.  Jean  Ingelow's  poem,  ''Songs 
of  Seven,"  being  read  and  illustrated  with 
tableaux. 

It  was  an  entertainment  out  of  the  or- 
dinary and  took  well. 

A  number  of  other  novel  features  might 
be  added,  by  which  more  money  could  be 
made.  The  mass  of  people  are  always  look- 
ing for  something  novel  and  unique  in  the 
way  of  entertainments,  and  the  cleverest 
and  most  original  committees  are  the  ones 
who  succeed  best  in  giving  church  sociables 
and  other  amusements.

A   Children's   Party 

CHILDREN'S  party  was  given  re- 
cently, by  a  girl. of  twenty,  to  nine 


other  maidens  of  her  own  age.      The  menu 
was  as  follows  : 

Bouillon 

Bread  Sticks 

Chicken     Boiled  Rice     Jelly 

Lettuce-and-Fresh  Tomato  Salad 

Animal  Crackers 

Ice  Cream 

Cake 

Macaroni  in  the  form  of  letters  was 
found  in  the  bouillon.  The  ice  cream 
was  moulded  in  the  form  of  birds,  auto- 
mobiles, etc.  The  cake  was  baked  in 
patty  pans,  and  decorated  with  white 
frosting,  in  which  were  nuts  and  candied 
cherries. 

The  table  was  decorated  with  a  large 
centerpiece  of  scarlet  carnations  and  ferns. 
At  each  plate  was  a  stick  of  red  and  white, 
striped  candy,  tied  with  a  bow  of  white  rib- 
bon, on  one  end  of  which  was  printed  the 
pet  name  of  one  of  the  guests.  Over  the 
back  of  each  chair  was  a  white  linen  bib  of 
generous  size,  on  the  front  of  which  was 
outlined  in  red  silk  the  same  pet  name, 
(Dolly  or  Mamie)  as  that  on  the  corre- 
sponding white  ribbon  just  mentioned. 
These  bibs  served  as  place  cards,  and 
were  worn  by  the  guests  during  the  meal. 
At  each  place  was  a  small  braided  basket 
of  red  and  white  peppermint  candy.  These 
baskets  were  filled  with  salted  almonds. 
The  guests  came  dressed  as  very  young 
children,  each  one  bringing  with  her  one 
or  more  photographs  taken  in  her  baby- 
hood. These  were  collected  by  the  host- 
ess as  her  friends  arrived,  and  were  passed 
about  during  the  meal,  that  the  ' '  children  ' ' 
might  guess  the  originals. 

A  Japanese  Party 

A  Japanese  party  was  recently  given  in 
a  girls'  college.  Some  of  the  students 
dressed  as  Japanese  men,  and  some  as 
women,  the  latter  looking  very  pretty  in 
charming  kimonas,  held  in  place  by  sashes. 
The  elaborate  hair  ornaments  and  slanting, 
penciled  eye-brows  greatly  transformed  the 
ladies. 

The  hall  was  a  bower  of  apple  blossoms. 
Bare  branches  of  trees  had  previously  been

decorated  with  thousands  of  small,  pink, 
paper  flowers,  made  by  the  girls  themselves. 
Dancing  was  the  entertainment  of  the 
evening,  and  an  orchestra  furnished  music. 


No  attempt  was  made  to  serve  Japanese 
refreshments,  as  ordinary  frappe  was  pre- 
ferred. 

L.  S. 


How  a  Five-Cent  Lunch  Is  Cooked  and 
Served  at  the  Honolulu  Normal  School 


By  MARION  BELL 


IT  may  be  interesting  to  many  to  know 
how,  in  the  Hawaiian  Islands,  a  pop- 
ular department  of  cookery  has  been 
established  on  a  self-paying  basis,  to  the 
material  benefit  of  the  school  children  and 
the  financial  rehef  of  the  school  depart- 
ment. 

At  the  Honolulu  Normal  School,  the 
work  is  carried  on  by  the  sale  of  a  five-cent 
lunch  daily,  the  receipts  from  which  more 
than  balance  the  expense  of  materials,  and 
fuel,  the  latter  item  amounting  to  six  or 
seven  dollars  a  month.  The  books  for  the 
first  year  showed  a  surplus  of  over  five 
dollars,  and  this  without  any  allowance  from 
the  school  board. 

Provisions  are  somewhat  more  expensive 
on  the  Hawaiian  Islands,  but  this  does  not 
detract  from  either  the  quality  or  quantity  of 
the  lunches.  Three  or  four  sandwiches,  with 
a  bowl  holding  a  little  more  than  half  a  pint 
of  soup,  stew,  or  whatever  there  may  be  on 
the  menu,  is  sold  for  five  cents.  Besides 
this  an  ^'  Extra ' '  is  prepared  in  the  form  of 
a  salad,  a  dessert  or  some  fancy  side  dish, 
which  is  sold  for  an  additional  nickel,  and 
purchased  mostly  by  the  teachers  and  older 
pupils.  Waitresses  from  the  kitchen  serve 
the  teachers  in  their  rooms,  while  the 
students  buy  at  the  door. 

The  lunch  varies  as  much  as  can  be  de- 
sired, including  aU  kinds  of  meat  and  cream 
soups,  macaroni,  cooked  various  ways,  Bos- 
ton baked  beans,  with  stews  and  chowders 
of  different  kinds.     A  menu  for  a  week  will 


give  some  idea  of  what  is  provided: 
Monday  — 

{a)     Curried  Stew  with  Rice,  Bread  and  Butter 

{b)      Tomato  Salad  —  Mayonnaise  Dressing 
Tuesday  — 

{a)     Boston  Baked  Beans.     Bread  and  Butter 

{b)     Curried  Eggs  on  Toast 
Wednesday  — 

(yd)     Rice  Tomato  Soup.  Bread  and  Butter 

\b)      Italian  Cream  Pudding 
Thursday  — 

{a)      Macaroni  and  White  Sauce.     Bread  and 
Butter 

{b)     Strawberry  Shortcake 
Friday  — 

{a)  ■  Cod-fish  Chowder.     Bread  and  Butter 

{b)     Steamed  Bread  Pudding.     Lemon  Sauce 

All  the  above  costing  but  five  cents  a  dish! 

The  pupils  are  mostly  Hawaiian,  Chinese, 
Japanese  and  Portuguese,  with  a  small  per- 
centage of  whites.  There  is  no  distinction 
shown  between  the  girls  and  the  boys  — 
and  the  latter,  big  fellows  of  eighteen  or 
twenty,  knead  bread  and  wash  dishes,  with 
as  much  readiness  as  they  would  handle  a 
saw.  The  Hawaiian  girls,  of  a  soft,  pliable 
nature,  take  naturally  to  womanly  work; 
the  Japanese  are  quick  imitators,  and  the 
Chinese  are  always  bright. 

The  interest  of  the  pupils  is  kept  alive 
by  the  object  in  view,  and  there  can  be  no 
such  word  as  fail,  with  a  variety  of  fastidi- 
ous tastes  to  satisfy,  —  and  fifty  hungry 
children  clamoring  to  be  fed  at  the  tap  of 
the  bell. 

Nor  is  the  scientific  side  neglected.  A 
class  of  preparatory  cooks,   in  addition  to 


TUNA  CHEESE 


293 


bread-making  and  some  simple  dishes, 
study  the  digestibility  of  food,  its  effects  on 
the  system,  and  a  certain  amount  of  physi- 
ology and  chemistry  in  connection  with  it. 
A  year  in  this  class  equips  them  well  for 
practical  work  and  they  enter  into  it  with 
zest  and  pleasure. 

In  connection  with  the  kitchen  is  a  veg- 
etable garden,  — a  large  plot  one  hundred 
by  seventy  feet,  where  vegetables  of  all 
kinds  are  cultivated.  This  work  is  done 
by  the  boys,  and  they  are  repaid  for  their 
labor  by  receiving  all  returns  over  and 
above  the  price  of  seed,  etc.,  by  selling 
their  produce  to  the  Domestic  Science 
Department. 

In  May  last,  a  five-course  luncheon  was 
served  to  sixty  of  the  Congressional  Party, 
visiting  the  Islands,  and  on  Commence- 
ment Day,  a  five  or  ten-cent  lunch,  as  de- 
sired, was  provided  for  all  guests  coming  to 


the  exercises.  Over  two  hundred  people 
were  present,  and  these  were  served  on 
the  lawn,  all  the  food  having  been  pre- 
pared by  the  cooking  classes,  with  abso- 
lutely no  outside  help.  This  represented 
no  small  amount  of  labor  on  the  part  of 
teacher  and  students,  but  the  provisions 
were  pronounced  excellent  by  all,  and  the 
price  was  a  matter  of  wonder. 

What  this  lunch  means  to  a  growing 
boy  or  girl  can  easily  be  imagined  by  any 
one  who  stops  to  think  of  the  hours  of  con- 
finement within  the  four  walls  of  a  school- 
room,—  many  of  the  children  slipping 
away  from  a  hurried  breakfast,  with  scarce- 
ly enough  nourishment  to  carry  them  half 
through  the  morning.  To  the  students  of 
the  Honolulu  Normal  it  means  a  whole- 
some meal  at  cost,  instead  of  indiges- 
tible pastry  bought  at  a  nearby  Chinese 
store. 


Tuna  Cheese 


By  JULIA  DA  VIS  CHANDLER 


IF  any  friends  traveling  in  the  south- 
west send  you  a  stiff  marmalade  or  fruit 
cheese,  colored  red,  and  wrapped  in  tin 
foil,  you  may  be  quite  sure  it  is  made  from 
the  cactus  fruit,  or  prickly  pear,  or  opuntia. 
It  is  even  newer  here  in  the  United 
States  than  guava  cheese,  and  that  is  not 
as  well  known  as  it  should  be.  It  will 
doubtless  be  labeled  '^Quesa  de  tuna,"  or 
Cheese  of  the  Tuna. 

We  Americans  know  this  plant  as  the 
prickly  pear,  and  so  do  the  Australians, 
while  the  English,  in  India,  call  it  the  In- 
dian fig;  Frenchmen  term  it  the  Barbary 
fig,  and  in  Spanish  speaking  countries  it  is 
known  as  the  tuna  nopal,  higos  chumbros, 
etc.  Country  folk  in  the  United  States 
say,  *' only  them  wild  cactus  apples." 
Cattlemen  in  the  plains  of  the  Southwest 
use  the  plant  for  feeding  their  stock,  after 


the  spines  have  been  burned  off.  To 
show  how  much  may  be  learned  about  one 
plant,  when  it  be  seriously  studied,  is 
proved  by  a  recent  pamphlet;  and  a  pre- 
ceding one,  both  devoted  to  the  opuntia, 
issued  by  the  Agricultural  Experiment 
Station  in  New  Mexico.  These  bulletins, 
illustrated  with  colored  pictures  and  pho- 
tographs of  many  scenes  connected  with 
the  tuna,  growing,  gathering,  and  cooking, 
represent  a  long  series  of  experiments  and 
research  on  the  part  of  Mr.  R.  F.  Hare, 
Chemist  of  the  New  Mexico  College  of 
Agriculture  and  Mechanic  Arts,  and  Mr. 
David  Griffith,  Assistant  Agrostologist, 
U.  S.  Department  of  Agriculture. 

We  often  have  the  opuntia  on  our  fruit 
stands.  The  fruits  are  about  the  size  of  a 
lemon,  and  are  oval,  with  a  soft,  smooth 
skin  of  a  dehcate  yellow  color,  shading  to 


294 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


rose,  with  spiny  dots  of  black,  and  dark 
ends.  Other  varieties,  not  shipped  here, 
bear  greenish-white  or  orange  fruits,  and 
some  have  rosy-red  and  even  mulberry 
hues. 

Before  they  reach  our  fruit  stores,  in 
eastern  cities,  the  spines  have  been  re- 
moved, but  the  natives  of  all  warm  climates, 
from  Sicily  to  Mexico,  have  to  learn  how 
to  be  very  skillful  in  gathering  and  hand- 
ling the  tuna.  To  eat  it  they  usually 
hold  it  on  a  little  stick,  or  mesquite  thorn, 
or  sharp  tip  of  a  maguey  leaf,  and  then  pro- 
ceed to  pick  oul  the  edible  pulp. 

The  process  of  making  the  tuna  fruit  into 
a  sweet  that  will  keep  may  be  said  to  be 
divided  into  three  stages,  a  syrup  that  re- 
places our  molasses  or  honey  for  general 
use,  the  melconcha  which  is  a  paste,  and  the 
above  mentioned  product,  a  firm  marma- 
lade, the  quesa  de  tuna.  The  name  cheese 
is  given  to  it,  because,  at  first,  it  looks  like 
soft  cheese,  and  in  England  that  name  is 
commonly  applied  to  many  fruits  when 
made  into  a  firm  paste  that  will  slice,  the 
pulp  separated  from  the  juice,  just  as  curd 
is  taken  from  whey. 

Laborers  in  the  tuna  regions  do  not  hes- 


itate to  indulge  freely  in  the  fresh  fruits, 
eating  twenty-five  and  fifty  without  stop^ 
ping,  and  often  from  a  hundred  to  two 
hundred  daily.  When  one  is  surrounded 
with  the  fruit,  it  is  easy  to  continue  eating 
without  reckoning,  just  as  it  is  when  one 
is  up  in  a  cherry  tree,  or  walking  about  in 
a  vineyard. 

Everything  of  a  fruit  nature  that  is  sac- 
charine is  always  experimented  on  for  al- 
coholic results.  Colonche,  says  Mr.  Hare, 
is  a  fermented  drink  made  from  tuna  pulp 
and  seeds;  after  three  weeks  time  it  is  sel- 
dom used,  because  it  is  intoxicating,  with 
some  very  harmful  after  results. 

The  dried  tunas  form  quite  an  article 
of  sale.  There  is  so  much  difficulty  in 
drying  the  skins  that  they  remain  about 
like  good  dates.  The  wild  kinds  are  too 
small  and  spiny  for  this  use,  and  so,  for 
all  purposes,  the  cultivated  ones  are  to  be 
chosen  when  possible. 

A  lady  in  Florida  writes  that  when  fruit 
is  scarce,  in  some  sections,  the  cactus  fruits 
may  be  eaten  in  place  of  peaches,  or  apri- 
cots, by  shcing  them  for  use  in  the  fresh 
state,  and  that  they  may  be  stewed  and 
canned  or  preserved. 


A   Few  Thoughts  Thought   on   Hearin 
Folks  Find  Fault  With  the  Weather 


By  NIXON  WATERMAN 

I  love  cold  winter  weather  with  the  snow 
A-driftin'  on  the  walks  I  hast  to  clear. 
And  frost  a-bitin'  nose  and  cheek  and  ear, 

With  the  thermometer  ''  away  below." 

(I  also  love  the  summer  when  it 's  so 

Red  hot  that  clothes  next  to  you  all  "adhere" 

And  everybody' s  frantic,  pretty  near. 

And  sayin'  things  that  hot  folks  do,  you  know  ? 

I  love  both  seasons,  but  I  wish  I  could 

Enjoy  them  whilst  they're  with  us,  for,  you  see, 
It 's  winter  when  the  summer  seems  so  good. 

And  summer  when  the  winter  pleases  me. 
But,  somehow,  I  have  never  understood 

Why  either  of  them  whilst  it's  here's  ''n.  g."

Query  1310.  —  Mrs.  F.  W.  M.,  Canton,^ 
Maine  :  "  Kindly  publish  directions  for  Roasting, 
Whole,  a  Sucking  Pig.  Also  recipe  for  French 
Bread." 


Sucking   Pig,  Roasted 

The  pig  may  be  from  four  to  six  weeks 
old. '  One  six  weeks  old,  and  weighing 
sixteen  pounds,  though  rather  long  to 
handle  easily,  will  be  found  most  delicate 
eating.  Wash,  and  wipe  inside  and  out  with 
care,  rub  over  the  inside  with  salt,  and,  if 
desired,  black  pepper.  Fill  the  cavity  with 
a  bread  or  other  dressing,  and  sew  up  the 
slit  made  in  dressing  the  pig.  Wrap  the 
ears  and  tail  curled  over  the  back  in  but- 
tered papers,  fastening  each  securely  with  a 
stitch.  Put  a  cork  in  the  mouth,  to  hold 
it  open.  Tie  two  or  three  strips  of  cotton 
round  the  pig  to  hold  it  in  an  upright  posi- 
tion, resting  on  the  legs  pressed  forward. 
Rub  the  outside  with  salt  and  pepper,  and 
dredge  with  flour.  Set  to  cook  in  a  mod- 
erate oven.  Baste  every  fifteen  minutes 
with  the  fat  in  the  pan  or  with  butter  melted 
in  hot  water.  Cook  from  three  to  four 
hours.  Turn  the  pig  occasionally,  that  all 
sides  and  the  under  part  may  be  evenly 
cooked.  Serve  with  apple  sauce  and  cab- 
bage, celery,  apple,  or  orange  salad. 

Bread  Stuffing  for  Roast  Pig 

Cut  the  liver  in  slices.  Over  these  pour 
boiling  water,  and  let  stand  ten  or  fifteen 
minutes,  then  drain.  Add  a  dozen  sage 
leaves,  scalded  in  boiling  water  and  drained. 


and  three  small,  mild  onions,  parboiled 
until  nearly  tender  and  drained.  Chop  the 
whole  very  fine,  then  add  ten  or  twelve 
ounces  of  stale  bread,  pressed  through  a 
colander,  a  teaspoonful  or  more  of  salt,  and 
a  generous  measure  of  black  pepper  (half  a 
teaspoonful  will  be  none  too  much  for  some 
tastes).  Mix  thoroughly,  then  mix  again 
with  one- third  a  cup  of  melted  butter.  A 
pig  a  month  old  will  take  more  dressing  than 
is  here  given,  but  possibly  this  quantity  may 
suffice,  as  none  is  needed  to  keep  the  pig 
in  shape. 

French  Bread 

Soften  a  cake  of  compressed  yeast  in  one- 
fourth  a  cup  of  boiled  water,  cooled  to  a 
lukewarm  temperature,  and  stir  in  about 
three-fourths  a  cup  of  flour,  enough  to 
make  a  dough  that  may  be  kneaded.  Knead 
until  the  Httle  ball  of  dough  is  smooth 
and  elastic.  Then  make  a  cut  across  the 
top  in  both  directions.  Have  ready  a  pint 
of  boiled  water,  cooled  to  a  lukewarm  tem- 
perature, and  into  this  put  the  ball  of 
dough.  It  will  sink  to  the  bottom  of  the  ' 
dish.  In  about  fifteen  minutes  it  will  float 
upon  the  water,  a  hght  puffy  ''sponge." 
Into  this  water  and  sponge  stir  a  teaspoon- 
ful of  salt,  and  between  five  and  six  cups  of 
flour,  enough  to  make  a  dough  stiff  enough 
to  knead.  Knead  or  pound  the  dough 
until  it  is  smooth  and  elastic,  and  does  not 
stick  to  the  hands  or  board.  It  will  take 
fifteen  or  twenty  minutes.  Part  of  the 
time  the  dough,  may  be  lifted  up  high  and 


295 


296 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


dropped  on  to  the  board  with  force.  When 
properly  kneaded,  cover  the  dough,  and  set 
it  aside  in  a  temperature  of  about  70®  F. , 
until  it  has  doubled  in  bulk.  There  will 
be  about  two  and  three-fourths  pounds  of 
dough.  This  may  be  baked  in  any  kind  of 
a  pan;  but,  to  secure  the  crusty  French  loaf, 
a  Russia  iron  pan,  giving  long,  narrow 
loaves,  is  desirable.  For  one  of  these  pans, 
divide  the  dough  into  two  equal  pieces. 
Roll,  and  stretch  these  under  the  hands,  on 
the  board,  until  they  are  as  long  as  the  pan. 
Have  a  round  stick  (like  a  curtain  roller) 
lightly  floured.  Press  this  down  through 
the  centre,  lengthwise  of  the  loaf,  and  roll 
it  back  and  forth,  to  make  a  furrow.  French 
bread  is  concave  rather  than  convex  on  the 
top,  but  this  shape  may  be  omitted.  Cover, 
and  let  stand  to  become  light.  Cut  three 
or  four  slantwise  cuts  in  the  top  of  the 
bread,  five  or  six  inches  apart.  Bake  about 
forty  minutes.  When  nearly  baked,  brush 
over  the  surface  with  the  white  of  an  egg, 
beaten  with  a  tablespoonful  of  cold  water, 
and  return  to  the  oven.  Repeat  the  glazing, 
if  desired. 


Query  131  i. —  A.  M.  P.,  Troy,  N.  Y. :  "Recipe 
for  genuine  Boston  Baked  Beans." 

Boston  Baked  Beans 

Let  one  pint  of  pea  beans  soak  in  cold 
water  overnight.  In  the  morning  wash  and 
rinse  in  several  waters.  Then  parboil  un- 
til they  may  be  pierced  with  a  pin.  Change 
the  water  during  the  parboiling,  adding  a 
teaspoonful  of  soda  with  the  last  water. 
Rinse  thoroughly  in  hot  water.  Put  one- 
half  of  the  beans  into  the  bean-pot.  Pour 
scalding  water  over  one-fourth  a  pound  of 
salt  pork  and,  after  scraping  the  rind  thor- 
oughly, score  it  in  half-inch  strips.  Lay 
the  pork  on  the  beans  in  the  pot,  and  turn 
in  the  rest  of  the  beans.  Mix  two  table- 
spoonfuls  of  molasses  and  one  teaspoonful, 
each,  of  mustard  and  salt,  with  hot  water  to 
pour,  and  turn  over  the  beans.  Then  add 
boiling  water  to  cover.  Bake  about  eight 
hours  in  a  moderate  oven.  Keep  the  beans 
covered  with  water  and  the  cover  on  the 
pot  until  the  last  hour.     Then  remove  the 


cover,  and  bring  the  pork  to  the  top,  to 
brown  the  rind.  Beans  are  better  baked 
in  large  quantities,  and  the  size  of  the  pot 
should  correspond  to  the  quantity  baked. 

Less  than  a  pint  of  beans  can  not  be 
baked  very  successfully.  When  properly 
baked  the  beans  are  neither  dry  nor  sloppy, 
and  each  bean  is  whole  yet  tender. 


Query  1312. —  Mrs.  J.  T.,  Cincinnati,  O. : 
"  Recipes  for  Tomato  Rabbit,  Gnocchi  a  la  Ro- 
main  and  Baked  Bean  Rabbit." 

Tomato  Rabbit 

Melt  one  tablespoonful  of  butter  in  a 
saucepan;  add  half  a  pound  of  cheese, 
grated  or  cut  fine,  one-eighth  a  teaspoonful 
of  soda,  and  one-fourth  a  teaspoonful,  each, 
of  salt  and  paprika  and  stir  until  the  cheese 
is  melted.  The  cooking  may  be  done  di- 
rectly over  the  fire,  but  it  is  a  little  safer  to 
set  the  dish  over  hot  water.  The  blazer 
of  a  chafing  dish  is  a  good  utensil  for  this 
purpose,  as  it  presents  considerable  surface 
to  the  heat.  When  the  cheese  is  melted, 
stir  into  it  the  yolks  of  two  eggs,  beaten 
and  mixed  with  half  a  cup  of  tomato  puree 
(cooked  tomato  pressed  through  a  sieve  to 
exclude  seeds).  Serve  on  the  untoasted 
side  of  bread  toasted  upon  but  one  side. 

Baked  Bean  Rabbit 

Have  ready  a  cup  of  cooked  beans, 
stewed  Lima,  Boston  or  New  York  baked 
beans,  pressed  through  a  sieve  or  gravy 
strainer,  and  one-fourth  a  pound  of  common 
factory  cheese,  grated  or  cut  in  thin  bits. 
Melt  two  level  tablespoonfuls  of  butter;  to 
it  add  the  prepared  beans,  half  a  teaspoon- 
ful of  salt,  one-fourth  a  teaspoonful  of 
paprika,  the  same  of  mustard  and  half  a 
cup  or  either  cream,  milk  or  tomato  puree; 
with  the  latter  use  a  tablespoonful  of  tomato 
catsup;  stir  until  the  whole  is  thoroughly 
heated,  then  add  the  cheese  and  continue 
stirring  until  the  cheese  is  melted.  Serve 
on  the  untoasted  side  of  bread  toasted  on 
but  one  side. 

Gnocchi  a  la  Romain 
Mix  one-fourth  a  cup,  each,  of  cornstarch 


ADVERTISEMENTS 


SIMPLE  METHODS  of  SOUP  MAKING 

By  MARY  JANE  McCLURE 

IT  is  usually  a  person's  first  impression  of  anything  that  lasts  the  longest  and  is  the  most  diffi- 
cult to  efface  from  one' s  mind.     How  all  important  it  is,  then,  to  the  housewife  who  would 
be  recognized  as  an  excellent  hostess  to  have  her  dinner,  be  the  company  large  or  small, 
start  off  right — with  a  soup  that  is  right.     Because  you  have  never  had  much  success  with  your 
soups  don't  give  up  and  acknowledge  that  it  is  the  one  thing  you  can't  make.     You  can  make 
soup — and'  as  good  a  soup  as  the  best  of  chefs  ever  produce  if  you  will  only  use  a  small  quantity  of  Ar- 
mour's  Extract  of  Beef.     To  weak  soups,  insipid  soups,  flat  soups,  a  little  of  Armour's  Extract  of  Beef 
works  a  magic  you  would  never  believe  possible ;  it  adds  the  taste  where  before  it  was  lacking. 


C  The  housewife  of  yesterday  was  content  with 
her  lot  in  life — she  knew  of  nothing  different.  Soup 
to  her  simply  meant  the  boiling  and  watching  and 
watching  and  boiling  of  her  raw  material.  When 
it  was  done,  so  was  she.  With  the  modern  house- 
wife this  is  all  different. 

CTo  a  stock  easily  prepared  with  Armour's  Ex- 
tract of  Beef  she  adds  her  ingredients  and  in  a 
short  time  she  has  produced  a  soup  fit  for  a  king, 
or  more  important  still — a  soup  she  can  rest  assured 
will  please  her  husband,   family  or  guests,    even 
though  connoisseurs  they  may  be.     A  point  not  to 
be  overlooked  in  using  Armour's  Extract  of  Beef  is 
its  high  degree  of  concentration.    It  contains  prac- 
tically quadruple  the  strength  of  any  other  brand 
and  is  therefore  four  times 
as    economical.      If   too 
much    is  used  the   result 
is  likely  to  be  a  slightly 
burnt  flavor.    A  little  will 
prove  delicious,   but  too 
much  spoils  the  broth,  like 
too  many  cooks. 
CThe  following  directions 
will  prove  a  help  to  the 
user  of  Armour's  Extract 
of  Beef : 

C  For  a  clear  soup,  such 
as  bouillon,  rice  or  spa- 
ghetti, use  one  teaspoon- 
ful  of  Armour's  Extract 
of  Beef  to  every  quart  of 
water. 

<I.  For  a  puree,  bisque  or 
a  soup  which  has  a  sub- 
stance or  body,  use  one-

CFor  soups  which  have  a  stock  of  their  own,  use

to  every  quart  of  water. 

CA  puree  or  cream  soup  must  be  bound  together 
to  keep  it  from  separating.  Melt  butter  and  when

well  mixed,  add  to  the  boiling  soup,

mixed.  This  will  clarify  them.

and  flour  and  half  a  teaspoonful,  each,  of 
salt  and  paprika  with  milk  to  a  thin  con- 
sistency, then  pour  the  mixture  into  the  rest 
of  a  pint  of  milk,  scalded  over  hot  water; 
stir  until  the  mixture  thickens,  then,  occa- 
sionally, for  fifteen  minutes.  Beat  one- 
fourth  a  cup  of  butter  to  a  cream,  then  beat 
in,  one  at  a  time,  the  yolks  of  two  eggs,  and, 
lastly,  half  a  cup  of  grated  cheese;  stir  this 
mixture  into  the  hot  mixture;  continue  the 
stirring  until  the  cheese  is  melted  and  the 
egg  is  cooked,  then  turn  the  mixture  into  a 
shallow  dish,  to  form  a  paste  half  an  inch 
thick.  When  cold  cut  in  squares,  rounds 
or  diamonds  and  set  these  in  a  buttered 
dish  suitable  to  send  to  the  table;  sprinkle 
with  grated  cheese,  add  a  second  layer  of 
pieces,  if  there  be  such,  and  sprinkle  them 
with  grated  cheese.  Set  the  dish  in  the 
oven  until  the  whole  is  very  hot. 


Query  1313. —  G.  H.  K. :  "Recipes for  Caramel 
Sponge,  dark  and  rich  like  chocolate  in  color,  only 
strongly  flavored  with  caramel,  and  Date  Shape, 
moulded  and  served  cold,  with  whipped  cream." 

Caramel  Sponge 

Stir  and  cook  three-fourths  a  cup  of  sugar 
until  it  melts  and  becomes  caramel;  add 
three-fourths  a  cup  of  water  and  let  cook 
until  the  caramel  is  melted;  add  one-fourth 
a  cup  of  sugar  and  stir,  occasionally,  until 
it  is  melted.  In  the  meantime  let  one- 
fourth  a  package  of  gelatine  soften  in  one- 
fourth  a  cup  of  cold  water  and,  when  the 
sugar  is  melted,  turn  the  softened  gelatine 
into  the  caramel  mixture  and  stir  until  dis- 
solved. Cool  the  caramel-gelatine  mixture 
by  setting  it  in  a  dish  of  cold  water  and  add 
the  juice  of  a  lemon.  Beat  the  whites  of 
three  eggs  until  dry,  then  gradually  beat 
into  them  the  gelatine  mixture;  continue 
beating  until  the  mixture  will  hold  its  shape, 
then  turn  it  into  a  mould  or  with  pastry  bag 
aud  tube  dispose  it  in  serving  dishes.  Serve 
with  a  cold,  boiled  custard  made  of  a  pint 
of  milk,  one-third  a  cup  of  sugar  and  the 
yolks  of  three  eggs.  The  caramel  sponge 
so  made  will  not  be  a  dark,  rich  chocolate 
color.  We  know  of  no  way  to  secure  this 
except  by  adding  an  ounce  or  two  ol  melted 


chocolate  to  the  hot  caramel  mixture,  but 
in  this  case  the  sponge  would  seem  to  be  a 
chocolate  sponge. 

Date  Shape 

Pour  a  generous  pint  of  boiling  water  over 
half  a  pound  of  dates;  stir  and  separate  the 
dates  with  a  silver  fork,  then  skim  them 
from  the  water  to  an  agate  pan.      Set  the 


Both   Gained 

Man  and  Wife  Fatten  on  Grape-Nuts 

The  notion  that  meat  is  necessary  for 
real  strength  and  the  foundation  of  solid 
flesh  is  now  no  longer  as  prevalent  as 
formerly. 

Excessive  meat  eaters  are  usually  slug- 
gish a  part  of  the  time  because  they  are- 
not  able  to  fully  digest  their  food,  and  the 
undigested  portion  is  changed  into  what  is- 
practially  a  kind  of  poison  that  acts  upon 
the  blood  and  nerves,  thus  getting  all 
through  the  system. 

''I  was  a  heavy  meat  eater,"  writes 
an  Illinois  man,  ' '  and,  up  to  two  years 
ago,  was  in  very  poor  health.  I  suffered 
with  indigestion  so  that  I  only  weighed  95 
pounds. 

"Then  I  heard  about  Grape-Nuts  and 
decided  to  try  it.  My  wife  laughed  at  me 
at  first  but,  when  I  gained  to  125  pounds 
and  felt  so  fine,  she  thought  she  would 
eat  Grape-Nuts  too. 

"  Now  she  is  fat  and  well  and  has 
gained  40  pounds.  We  never  have 
indigestion  any  more  and  seldom  feel  the 
desire  for  meat.  A  neighbor  of  ours,  68 
years  old,  was  troubled  with  indigestion 
for  years  ;  was  a  heavy  meat  eater,  and 
now,  since  he  has  been  eating  Grape-Nuts 
regularly,  he  says  he  is  well  and  never  has 
indigestion.  I  could  name  a  lot  of  persons 
who  have  really  been  cured  of  indigestion 
by  changing  from  a  heavy  meat  diet  to 
Grape-Nuts."  "There's  a  Reason." 
Name  given  by  Postum  Co. ,  Battle  Creek, 
Mich. 

Read  the  little  book,  "The  Road  to 
Wellville, ' '  in  pkgs.

THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


pan  in  the  oven  for  about  five  minutes,  to 
dry  off  the  dates,  then  remove  the  stones 
and  cut  the  flesh  of  each  into  three  or  four 
pieces;  to  theseadd  thejuice  of  half  alemon, 
one-fourth  a  cup  of  sugar,  half  a  cup  of 
orange  or  other  fruit  juice,  or  sherry  wine, 
and  one-fourth  a  package  of  gelatine,  soft- 
ened in  one-fourth  a  cup  of  cold  water  and 
dissolved  in  one-fourth  a  cup  of  boiling 
water.  Have  ready  about  a  dozen  blanched- 
and-halved  almonds  and  half  a  dozen  dates, 
reserved  whole  when  the  dates  were  stoned. 
Put  half  an  almond  in  each  date  and  use  to 
decorate  a  mould.  Mix  the  rest  of  the 
almonds  with  the  date  and  gelatine  mixture 
and  when  slightly  chilled  mix  thoroughly  and 
turn  into  the  decorated  mould.  Serve  with 
cream   sweetened  and  flavored  to  taste. 


Query  1314. — Miss  M.  K.,  Petrolea,  Canada: 
"Recipe  for  Devil's  Cake  that  will  be  light  and 
easier  to  make  than  the  usual  Devil's  Cake." 

Devil's  Cake 

Beat  two  thirds  a  cup  of  butter  to  a 
cream;  beat  in  one  cup  of  sugar;  beat  the 
yolks  of  four  eggs  very  light;  beat  into 
them  one  cup  of  sugar,  then  beat  the  two 
mixtures  together;  add  one  cup  of  hot, 
mashed  potato,  two  squares  of  melted 
chocolate,  and,  alternately,  half  a  cup  of 
sweet  milk  and  two  cups  of  sifted  flour, 
sifted  again  with  three  and  one-half  level 
teaspoonfuls  of  baking  powder,  ateaspoon- 
ful,  each,  of  cinnamon  and  nutmeg,  and 
half  a  teaspoonful  of  clove.  Lastly,  add  a 
cup  of  walnut  meats,  chopped  fine,  and 
the  whites  of  four  eggs,  beaten  dry.  Bake 
in  a  loaf,  a  sheet,  or  in  layers.  Frost  with 
chocolate,  or  plain  white  icing,  as  desired. 


Query  1315.  — Miss  H.  C.  C,  Troy,  N.  Y. : 
"  Recipe  for  Candied  Orange  or  Grapefruit  Peel." 

Candied    Orange    or    Grapefruit 
Peel 

Cut  the  peel  into  quarter  sections,  then 
remove  from  the  fruit  in  perfect  pieces. 
Weigh  and  let  stand  overnight  in  cold, 
salted  water  to  cover.  Use  a  teaspoonful 
of  salt  to  a  quart  of  water.      In  the  morn- 


ing drain  the  peel  and  set  to  cook  in  fresh 
water;  when  the  water  boils  drain  and  add 
a  fresh  supply,  and  let  cook  until  tender. 
Take  the  weight  of  the  peel  in  sugar,  and 
half  the  weight  in  water;  let  these  boil, 
skimming  as  needed,  to  a  syrup;  add  the 
peel  and  let  simmer  until  the  peel  takes  up 
the  syrup.  Spread  on  agate  plates  to 
dry. 


Memory    Improved 

Since  Leaving  Off  Coffee 

Many  persons  suffer  from  poor  memory 
who  never  suspect  coffee  has  anything  to 
do  with  it. 

The  drug — caffeine — in  coffee,  acts 
njuriously  on  the  nerves  and  heart,  caus- 
ng  imperfect  circulation,  too  much  in  the 
brain  at  one  time,  too  little  in  another 
part.  This  often  causes  a  dullness  which 
makes  a  good  memory  nearly  impossible. 

"  I  am  nearly  70  years  old  and  did  not 
know  that  coffee  was  the  cause  of  the 
stomach  and  heart  trouble  I  suffered  from 
for  many  years,  until  about  four  years 
ago,"  writes  a  Kansas  woman. 

"  A  kind  neighbor  induced  me  to  quit 
coffee  and  try  Postum.  I  had  been  suffer- 
ing severely  and  was  greatly  reduced  in 
flesh.  After  using  Postum  a  little  while  I 
found  myself  improving  ;  my  heart  beats 
became  regular  and  now  I  seldom  ever 
notice  any  symptons  of  my  old  stomach 
trouble  at  all.  My  nerves  are  steady  and 
my  memory  decidedly  better  than  while  I 
was  using  coffee. 

"  I  like  the  taste  of  Postum  fully  as  well 
as  coffee.  My  sister  told  me  two  years 
ago  that  she  did  not  like  it,  but  when  I 
showed  her  how  to  make  it  according  to 
directions,  she  thought  it  was  delicious. 

"It  is  best  to  pour  cold  water  over  your 
Postum,  let  ii  come  to  a  boil,  then  boil  1 5 
minutes.  That  brings  out  the  flavour  and 
full  food  value." 

' '  There  '  s  a  Reason. ' ' 

Name    given    by    Postum    Co.,    Battle 
Creek,    Mich.       Get    the    booklet    "The' 
Road  to  Wellville,"  in  pkgs.

Book  Reviews 


Many   Ways  for    Cooking    Eggs.     By 
Mrs.  S.  T.  Rorer.    12  mo,  cloth  bound. 
Price,  35c.,  net;  by  mail  38  cents.  Phila- 
delphia: Arnold  and  Company. 
It  seems  a  pity  that  there  is  not  a  wider 
knowledge  of  the  possibilities  that  lie  in  an 
egg.      It  is  such  a  standby  in  every  family 
that  it  is  wrong  for  any  one  to  remain  in 
ignorance  of  the  great  variety  of  ways  in 
which  it  can  be  made  to  minister  to  the  ap- 
petite.    The  skill  of  the  housewife  or  cook 
is  mostly  confined    to   boiling,    frying   or 
poaching,   with  now  and  then  an  attempt 
at  a  plain  omelette.      This  is  simply  cheat- 
ing the  family  out  of  its  proper  share  of  the 
good  things  of  life. 

Here  in  this  book  Mrs.  Rorer  gives  her 
readers  a  knowledge  of  very  many  ways  of 
preparing  egg  dishes  for  any  and  all  meals. 
Then,  too,  there  's  a  chapter  on  the  mak- 
ing of  sauces  to  use  with  many  of  the 
dishes.  All  told  it  is  a  valuable  little  book, 
and  will  add  much  to  the  variety  of  the 
daily  bill  of  fare,  and  be  a  source  of  enjoy- 
ment,  health  and  profit. 


conducted  to  the  sources  for  the  study  of 
their  subject. 


Thanksgiving.  By  Robert-Haven  Schauf- 

fler.      Price,   ^i.oo,   net.      New    York: 

Moffat,  Yard  &  Co. 

Every  autumn  in  school  and  home  re- 
curs the  need  for  a  collection  of  literature 
on  Thanksgiving,  —  an  anthology  com- 
prehensive enough  to  include  all  the  best 
that  has  been  written  about  this  intensely 
American  holiday.  For  years  the  impera- 
tive ungratified  demand  for  such  a  book 
has  almost  suggested  a  dark  conspiracy 
among  literary  folk,  —  a  conspiracy  which 
the  present  volume  is  intended  to  thwart. 
It  brings  to  children  of  all  ages  all  the  best 
poems,  essays,  plays  and  stories  of  Thanks- 
giving.     And  its  scope  is  yet  wider. 

The  introduction  gives  a  rapid  view  of 
the  holiday's  origin,  its  derivation  from 
ancient  festivals;  its  development,  its  spirit 
and  its  significance.  This  part  of  the  book 
endeavors  to  be  as  suggestive  as  possible 
to  parents  and  teachers,  who  are  personally 


Christmas.  Its  Origin,  Celebration  and 
Significance  as  related  in  Prose  and 
Verse.  By  Robert-Haven  Schauffler. 
Price,  ^i.oo,  net.  New  York:  Moffat, 
Yard  &  Company. 

This  volume  contains  the  best  prose  and 
verse  selections  in  our  language,  relating 
to  the  home  festival  of  Christmas.  The 
material  has  been  selected  from  many 
sources.  Historical,  Poetical,  Religious, 
Patriotic,  Dramatic,  etc.  Christmas  cus- 
toms and  the  Christmas  spirit  in  our  own 
and  other  lands  are  treated  here  and  ex- 
emplified fully  in  the  selections.  It  pre- 
sents in  condensed  form  the  literature  of 
Christmas  and  is  admirably  adapted  to 
home  and  school  reading. 

It  will  be  noted  that  these  volumes  on 
our  American  Holidays  are  handy  and 
helpful  in  both  school  and  home.  They 
are  admirably  adapted  to  the  purposes  for 
which  they  were  compiled.  In  many  a 
collection  of  working  books  these  are  fitted 
to  occupy  a  useful  niche. 


Ease  in  Conversation.      By  Emma  C. 
Hewitt.    Cloth.     Price,  50  cents.    Phil- 
adelphia: George  W.  Jacobs  &  Co. 
This  book  is  not  a  profound  treatise  of 

any  kind.     It  is  designed  to  awaken  an  in-

THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


terest  in  a  most  important  study,  and  to 
show  how  unsuspected  faults  in  speech 
may  be  overcome.  No  one  can  read  the 
book  without  being  brought  face  to  face 
with  the  inaccuracies  and  inelegancies  of 
speech  that  occur  everywhere  in  common, 
everyday  parlance;  he  may  also  be  led  to 
see  the  way  to  correct  and  avoid  his  own 
faults  in  matter  and  manner  of  conversa- 
tion. The  mission  of  the  book  is  excellent, 
and,  as  far  as  it  goes,  the  subject  is  well 
presented.  It  may  serve  to  incite  the 
careless,  yet  earnest,  ambitious  young  per- 
son, who  desires  to  cultivate  the  ways  and 
manners  of  the  well-bred,  to  further  study. 


Books  and  My  Food.  By  Elisabeth 
Luther  Cary  and  Annie  M.  Jones.  i6 
mo.  Boards,  ^i.oo,  net.  New  York: 
Moffat,  Yard  &  Co. 

''Books  and  ]\Iy  Food  "  is  a  compilation 
of  365  quotations  referring  to  food;  each 
quotation  is  accompanied  by  a  recipe  sug- 
gested by  the  sentiment. 

The  quotations  are  largely  from  English 
novelists;  thus  the  recipes  are  English. 
Shakespeare,  Swift,  Goldsmith,  Dryden, 
Dickens,  Thackeray,  Lamb,  Charles  Reade, 
Anthony  Trollope,  Mrs.  Gaskell,  Jane 
Austin,  Charlotte  Bronte  and  George 
Eliot,  are  the  authors  most  largely  drawn 
upon. 

But  with  all  this  array  of  authors,  the 
subject  is  not  worn  threadbare  or  even 
frayed  on  the  edges  (if  we  maybe  forgiven 
the  figure  of  speech) ;  for  many  of  the  best 
sayings  from  even  these  authors  have  been 
omitted,  doubtless  for  lack  of  space,  and 


the  dishes  referred  to  in  the  works  of  a 
host  of  other  eminent  writers  are  not  so 
much  as  glanced  at. 

Until  one  makes  a  study  of  the  matter, 
he  has  no  idea  of  the  extent  to  which  not 
only  old-time  authors  but  those  of  more 
recent  date  allude  in  their  works  to  the 
homely  duties  of  food  and  its  service.  The 
idea  of  the  book  is  interesting  and  the 
recipes  as  a  whole  are  fairly  good.  Occa- 
sionally the  authors  fail  to  catch  the  recipe 
intended  by  the  author  and  wander  far 
afield  in  search  of  a  recipe. 


Tuberculosis.      By  S.  A.  Knopf.    Price, 

Paper  25c.;  Cloth  50c.  New  York:  F.  P 
Flori. 

This  is  a  prize  essay,  and  contains  the 
information  with  which  every  one  should 
be  familiar.  It  is  an  essay  for  the  people, 
and,  if  read  and  appreciated,  it  might  save 
many  lives. 

"The  chapter  on  the  installation  for  the 
sanatorium  treatment  at  home  contains 
much  of  value  to  the  physician,  who  is  con- 
fronted with  the  problem  of  how  best  to 
care  for  his  patients  who  cannot  avail  them- 
selves of  the  advantages  to  be  gained  by  a 
sojourn  in  a  well-regulated  sanatorium. 
The  essay  is  one  which  is  valuable  and

The  Nose  as  a  Germ-Filter 

The  researches  of  modern  science  have 
revealed  many  wonderful  things  in  regard 
to  the  matters  of  every -day  life,  and  par- 
ticularly in  regard  to  the  manner  of  dissem- 
ination and  prevention  of  disease. 

None  of  these  observations  is  perhaps 
more  striking  or  of  greater  practical  impor- 
tance than  are  those  bearing  on  the  preva- 
lence in  the  dust- laden  air  of  cities  and 
towns  of  the  germs  of  many  of  the  most 
serious  diseases,  such  as  tuberculosis, 
grippe,  diphtheria,  pneumonia  and  others. 
But  it  appears  that,  as  usual,  nature  has 
provided  suitable  defenses  against  the  dan- 
ger. 

If  the  act  of  breathing  is  performed  nor- 
mally, through  the  nose  and  not  the  mouth, 
the  inspired  air  must  pass  through  a  skill- 
fully designed  filtering-machine  which 
greatly  reduces  the  perils  from  this  source. 
The  simple  exterior  of  the  human  nose 
gives  no  hint  of  the  complication  of  its  in- 
ner construction.  Its  interior  is,  however, 
broken  up  into  many  devious  passages  and 
channels,  so  arranged  that  a  very  large  sur- 
face of  mucous  membrane  is  exposed  to 
each  breath  of  incoming  air  on  its  way  to 
the  larynx  and  lungs.  The  mucous  mem- 
brane is  moist  and  warm,   and  the  air  is

UffiTHE 


AS  a  substitute  for  genuine  coffee  I  think  the  Old 
Grist  Mill  Wheat  Coffee  comes  nearer  to  it  than 
any  other,  and  we  have  tried  all  brands.  My 
brother,  a  traveling  salesman  for  tea  and  coffee,  who  is 
quite  an  expert  as  to  its  quality,  would  not  believe  when 
drinking  a  cup  of  the  beverage  at  my  house  that  it  could 
be  made  of  Old  Grist  Mill  Wheat  Coffee,  so  nearly  like 
the  real  article  did  it  taste. — From  Good  Housekeeping 
Testimonial. 


forced  to  pass  over  it  in  thin  layers,  so  that 
it  is  not  only  charged  with  moisture,  but  is 
also  warmed  to  the  body  heat  before  it 
enters  the  lower  air  passages. 

But  in  addition  to  these  important  mod- 
ifications an  opportunity  is  afforded  for  all 
suspended  dust  particles,  floating  germs, 
and  so  forth,  to  be  deposited  on  these 
damp  surfaces,  from  which  the  natural  flow 
of  the  secretions  promptly  washes  them  to 
the  exterior. 

But  this  beneficent  action  of  the  nose  as 
a  germ-filter  can  take  place  only  if  the  in- 
spired air  passes  through  it,  and  not  the 
mouth.  Through  the  mouth,  on  account 
of  its  comparatively  large  size,  the  incom- 
ing air  passes  with  a  rush,  and  immediately 
strikes  the  tonsils,  which  have  been  shown 
to  be  the  most  vunerable  portion  of  the 
throat,  and  form  veritable  portals  of  entry 
for  germs  of  many  diseases. 

It  is,  therefore,  a  matter  of  great  impor- 
tance that  children  be  taught  early  to 
breathe  through  the  nose,  so  that  dry,  un- 
warmed,  dust-and-germ-laden  air  shall  not 
be  taken  into  the  throat  and  lungs.  If 
this  were  consistently  done,  not  only  more 
serious  ailments,  but  also  ordinary  colds 
would  be  much  rarer. 

Children  or  adults  who  persistently 
breathe  through  the  mouth,  especially 
when  asleep,  are  usually  sufferers  from 
some  obstruction  of  the  nasal  passages,  and 
urgently  require  medical  care.  —  Youth's 
Companion. 


Avoidance  of  Dirt  on  Kitchen 

Walls 

The  accumulation  of  dirt  on  the  kitchen 
walls  is  due  in  part  to  the  stirring  activity 
of  this  busy  department,  the  large  amount 
of  dust  which  comes  from  the  coal  range, 
and  still  more  it  is  due  to  the  vapors 
and  steam  which  arise  from  the  cook- 
ing, and  it  is  this  steam  on  rising  to  the 
top  of  the  room,  which  attaches  to  the  par- 
ticles of  dirt,  and  glues  them  there.  This 
circumstance  is  one  more  argument  for 
ventilating  facihties  which  will  serve  to 
emit     the    vapor  while    it    is   yet   steam.

How  to  Clean  Waxed  Floors 

A  hardwood  floor,  which  has  been  fin- 
ished, after  the  French  manner,  with  wax 
only,  is  easily  cleaned  and  refinished.  In 
this  country,  such  floors,  instead  of  being 
polished  with  a  brush  every  morning,  as  is 
done  in  good  houses  in  France,  are  left  to 
themselves  for  weeks  or  months.  The 
consequence  is  that  the  surface  of  the  wax 
grows  dim  and  sticky,  and  dust  clings  to 
it,  accumulating  until  it  forms  blackish 
streaks  of  spots,  especially  under  rugs  or 
near  windows  or  in  other  places  exposed  to 
dust.  Washing  with  water  or  ammonia 
will  not  remove  these  spots,  and  will  in- 
jure the  floor,  raising  and  roughening  the 
grain.  Instead  of  this  they  should  be 
rubbed  with  fine  sandpaper.  No.  i  or  No  o, 
dipped  in  turpentine.  The  turpentine  will 
soften  the  wax  without  injuring  the  floor, 
and  the  sandpaper  will  remove  the  dust. 
If  the  floor  is  too  thickly  coated  with  wax, 
the  sandpaper  clogs  quickly,  and  steel 
wool,  dipped  in  turpentine,  is  more  con- 
venient. When  the  floor  has  been  suffi- 
ciently cleaned,  a  fresh  dressing  of  wax 
may  be  put  on,  and  rubbed  in  the  usual 
manner.  This  process  can  be  repeated  in- 
definitely until  the  floor  is  worn  through. 


Walking 

Much  interest  has  lately  manifested  it- 
self in  the  attempt  of  a  man  sixty-nine  years 
old  to  walk  from  Portland,  Maine,  to  Chi-

garded as  wonderfully  quick  time.  Now, 
when  he  is  within  one  year  of  the  span 
of  fife  allotted  by  the  Psalmist,  he 
has  succeded  in  cutting  down  the  time  by

Crushing  ice  by  hand  is  a  very  nasty  and 
disagreeable  job,  besides  it  is  hard  work  and 
takes  lots  of  time,  and  you  cannot  crush  the 
ice  to  a  uniform  and  proper  size. 

With  this  little  machine  all  you  have  to  do 
is  to  put  a  piece  of  ice  in  the  hopper  and  turn 
the  crank.  Either  a  small  piece  or  a  piece 
that  fills  the  hopper  will  go  straight  through. 

It  does  the  work  very  easily  and  quickly 
and  is  built  very  substantially,  good  for  years 
of  hard  usage. 

We  send  these  machines  on  trial,  to  be  re- 
turned if  not  satisfactory.  Let  us  send  you 
'one. 

We  also  manufacture  larger  machines  from 
hand  power  capacity  to  power  machines  hav- 
ing capacity  of  forty-five  tons  per  hour. 

Davenport  Ice  Chopping  Machine  Co., 

1378  West  Third  St.,  Davenport,  Iowa 


several  hours,  and  finished  the  twelve- 
hundred-mile  journey  in  good  liealth  and 
with  an  abundance  of  reserve  strength. 

To  many  persons  the  undertaking  will 
suggest  only  a  desire  for  notoriety  and  a 
willingness  to  subject  the  body  to  injudi- 
cious strain.  Something  of  both  objec- 
tions is  valid;  and  yet  the  writer  saw  him 
as  he  entered  Boston,  and,  leaving  out  the 
rabble  which  dogged  his  steps,  and  the 
cheap  "sports"  who  sought  a  reflected 
glory  by  walking  by  his  side,  there  was 
something  wholesome  in  the  sight.  Al- 
ready Mr.  Weston  had  covered  more  than 
fifty  miles  that  day,  yet  his  head  was  up, 
his  chest  out,  and  his  stride  long  and  full 
of  vigor. 

The  picture  was 
seemed  so  natural, 
thing  primeval — man 
tended  he  should  be: 
from  one  place  to  another  by  his  own  un- 
aided powers,  dependent  upon  no  artificial 
means  of  locomotion.  It  is  a  pity  that 
walking,  the  most  natural  and  wholesome 
and  easily  practised  of  all  athletic  exercises 
should  be  so  much  neglected.  Unlike 
most  other  diversions,  it  can  be  followed 
either  alone  or  in  company,  and  made  as 
gentle  or  as  strenuous  as  one  may  wish; 
and  more  than  any  other  exercise,  it  con- 
fers a  sense  of  freedom,  of  individual 
physical  power,  which  makes  it  as  refresh- 
ing to  the  mind  as  to  the  body. 

It  is  not  too  much  to  say  that  it  is  a  de- 
veloper of  character.  The  man  who  made 
the  walk  to  Chicago  has  preserved  his 
powers  by  abstention  from  liquor  and  to- 
bacco, and  he  rests  on  Sundays,  out  of 
respect  to  his  mother's  wishes. 


pleasing  because  it

Some  Exercises  for  Women  to 
Take 

There's  one  doctor  in  Boston  who  is 
very  much  distressed  about  the  antics  which 
women  are  going  through  under  the  name 
of  physical  culture.  He  says  they  are  sure 
to  strain  themselves  and  ruin  their  consti- 
tutions unless  they  stop  these  contortions. 
So  he  suggests  one  or  two  sane  exercises.

Heart  Throbs:     A   Book  Compiled 
by  50,000  People 

A  book  that  like  Topsy — "just  growed." 

Born  of  the  deep  sentiment  associated  with  home  and  mother. 
Not  ours,  but  theirs — by  the  people  for  the  people. 
Out   of   50,000    contributions,    just   840    unduplicated,    showing 
the   kinship   of   human   nature. 

President  Roosevelt  and  hundreds  of  Public  men  are  numbered  among  those  who  fur- 
nished their  favorite  selections. 

Heart  Throbs — the  Old  Scrap  Book — is  the  only  book  ever  compiled  by  50,000  people. 
Affording  a  wonderful  insight  into  the  aims  and  purposes  of  "  the  plain  people  of  America  " 
as  Lincoln  loved  to  call  us.  In  the  avalanche  of  choice  bits  of  prose  and  verse  that  came 
from  every  part  of  the  country  only  840  selections  were  found  to  be  unduplicated,  think  of 
that!  and  these  have  been  preserved  in  the  handsomely  bound  Gift  book^ — Heart  Throbs — 
making  the  most  notable  book  of  the  times.  What  are  these  840  selections  ?  Is  one  your 
favorite  ?  Probably,  for  at  heart  we  are  all  pretty  much  alike.  Heart  Throbs  is  an  ideal 
book  to  read  aloud  at  the  family  gathering  during  the  long  winter  evenings  with  the  children 

"  hanging  on  the  arms  of  the  chair," 
drinking  in  the  enduring  life  stories. 
Every  teacher  should  have  a  copy  for 
the  school  room ;  every  preacher  for  the 
pulpit;  every  "good  old  soul  "  for  the 
memories  it  brings ;  every  youth  and 
maiden  for  the  nobleness  and  optimism  it 
teaches.  A  universal  book  with  a  range 
of  emotion  greater  than  Shakespeare ; 
in  comfort,  second  only  to  the  Bible.
