"Breathing  exercises  come  first,"  said 
he.  "  Do  them  before  breakfast,  with  the 
windows  open.  Raise  the  hands  slowly  until 
tliey  extend  straight  above  the  head,  in- 
terlock the  thumbs.  While  raising  fill  the 
lungs  slowly  with  air.  Now,  holding  the 
breath  and  keeping  the  arms  stiff  and 
straight,  slowly  lower  them  and  gradually 
force  them  down  as  near  the  floor  as  possi- 
ble. This  curves  the  back  and  gives  the 
body  the  appearance  of  an  inverted  U. 
When  the  arms  can  reach  no  further  slowly 
expel  the  air  in  the  lungs.  After  a  second' s 
pause  repeat  the  exercise.  •  This  may  be 
done  10  or  20  times. 

"■  Next:  Hold  the  hands  on  each  side 
of  the  waist,  keeping  the  shoulders  as  flat 
and  as  stiff  as  possible,  and  the  legs  stiff 
and  straiglit;  bend  the  upper  part  of  the 
body  far  to  one  side,  and  then  at  that 
angle  move  it  round  in  a  circle,  the  centre 
of  which  will  be  the  waist.  The.  object  is  to 
move  the  body  from  the  hips,  the  circular 
movement  being  particularly  good  for  the 
abdominal  muscles  and  organs. 

"To  increase  the  breadth  of  the  should- 
ers and  develop  the  bust  and  bury  the  collar- 
bone: Stand  upright  with  head  erect  in 
such  a  way  tliat  when  one  arm  is  extended 
parallel  with  the  floor  it  will  just  touch  the 
wall.  Now,  move  the  feet  an  inch  at  a  time 
away  from  the  wall,  and  while  keeping  the 
legs  and  lower  spine  erect,  stretch  the  arm 
and  shoulder  so  as  to  keep  the  tips  of  the 
fingers  continually  in  touch  with  the  wall. 
When  you  have  moved  as  far  as  you  can 
from  the  wall  without  having  lost  your 
^  touch,'  resume  the  original  position  and 
repeat  with  the  other  arm. ' ' 


Trouble  in  Fudge 

Many  girls  have  trouble  in  making  fudge 
that  is  creamy,  rich  and  smooth.  The 
trouble  lies  in  over-boiling.  The  candy 
should  always  be  taken  from  the  stove  the 
minute  it  makes  a  soft  ball  that  can  just 
hold  together  when  dropped  in  ice  water. 
If  allowed  to  cook  longer,  the  fudge  will  be 
stiff;  if  a  less  time  is  given,  it  will  be  gritty 
and  sticky.

Beat  hard  with  a  wooden  spoon.  It  is 
better  than  the  wire  beaters,  as  it  permits 
more  vigorous  whipping.  Do  not  add  the 
vanilla  until  the  chocolatehas cooled  slightly.

is  still  thin  enough  to  smooth  or  the  surface 
will  not  be  glazed -when  cold. 


For  Chafinor  Dish 

o 

The  girl  who  has  put  away  her  chaiing 
dish  because  she  is  tired  of  fudge  and  rabbit, 
should  try  this  recipe  for  eggs  and  mush- 
rooms. It  came  from  an  old  Enghsh  cook 
book,  printed  ages  before  anybody  ever 
heard  of  a  chafing  dish,  but  would  be  a 
novelty  in  parlor  cookery.  SHce  a  dozen 
button  mushrooms  and  two  onions  and  fry 
them  together  in  butter.  Turn  into  the 
pan  a  cupful  of  beef  gravy  or  of  sauce  made 
of  meat  stock,  add  half  a  dozen  hard-boiled 
eggs,  cut  in  slices,  and  simmer  a  few  minutes; 
serve  very  hot.  The  small  canned  mush- 
rooms may  be  used. 


An  agent  in  the  Midland  Revision  Court 
objected  to  a  person  whose  name  was  on 
the  register,  on  the  ground  that  he  was 
dead.  The  revision  attorney  demanded 
conclusive  testimony.  ' '  But,  sir,  how  do 
you  know  the  man's  dead?"  demanded 
he.  "Well,"  was  the  reply,  ''I  don't 
know.  It's  very  difficult  to  prove." 
"As  I  suspected,"  returned  the  barrister. 
"You  don't  know  whether  he's  dead  or 
not. ' '  Whereupon  the  witness  coolly  con- 
tinued: "I  was  saying,  sir,  that  I  don't 
know  whether  he  is  dead  or  not;  but  I  do 
know  this.  They  buried  him  about  a 
month  ago  on  suspicion."  — Harper's 
Weekly.

"The    'Vvenue,"  Hobbema 


What  Pictures  Shall  We  Hang  in  Our 

Dining  Room 

\By  MARGARET  L.    SEARS 

IT  would  not  do  to  lay  down  hard  and  would  be  entirely  inappropriate  in  another, 

fast  rules  as  to  what  should  hang  in  our  The  style  and  size  of  a  house,  its  situa- 

dining  room  or  in  any  other  apartment,  tion  and  environment,  as  well  as  the  taste 

for  what    would  look  well   in    one  house  of  its    occupants    sliould    be    considered. 

.299  . 


300 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


«« Going  to  Work,"  Millet 


' '  Should  be  "  is  used  advisedly.  Perhaps 
it  would  be  well  to  revise  the  sentence  and 
say:  ''Should  be  considered,  if  possible.^  ^ 
There  are  many  reasons  why  we  cannot 
always  do  as  we  would  like  in  such  matters. 
Some  have  inherited  pictures  which  they 
cannot  cast  aside.  Many  of  them  are  gifts 
and  represent  the  giver.      When  he  calls, 


he  always  notes  their  position  and  condition, 
and  one  must  not  offend  one's  friends  ! 

The  sad  moral  of  the  well-known  story 
by  Edward  Everett  Hale,  ' '  Aunt  CaroHne'  s 
Present,"  is  readily  felt  by  many  house- 
holders with  a  similar  experience. 

We  have  all  been  torn  by  conflicting 
emotions  when  contemplating  a  present 
given  by  some  ' '  Aunt  Caroline. ' '  Fear  of 
offending  some  relative,  whom  we  feel  we 
must  not  offend,  in  view  of  the  fact  that  we 
may  receive  some  legacy  in  the  future,  leads 
us  to  hang  the  atrocity  in  the  best  place  in 
the  room,  where  its  pervading  ugliness 
seems  to  dominate  everything. 

While,  however,  there  are  so  many  hin- 
drances in  the  way  of  reaching  our  ideal, 
still,  it  is  well  to  have  an  ideal  to  strive  for, 
and,  perhaps,  we  may  get  nearer  to  it  than 
we  realize. 

There  is  place  for  individuality,  as  well 
as  originahty,  in  choosing  what  shall  hang 
in  the  room  where  the  family  meet  three 
times  a  day,  and  if  the  two  qualities  are 
governed  by  good  taste,  the  result  cannot 
fail  to  be  satisfactory.  Unless  so  governed 
the  result  is  sometimes  eccentric,  which  is 
an  unfortunate  condition  that  should  be 
most  decidedly  avoided. 

A  recent  lecturer  tells  a  story  of  going 
on  an  errand  to  a  house  in  the  country. 
The  woman  of  the  house  ushered  him  into 
an  apartment,  which  had  rows  of  photo- 
graphs of  different  people  arranged  symetri- 


Le  Grie,"  Troyon 


WHAT  PICTURES  SHALL  WE  HANG  IN   OUR  DINING  ROOM     301 


cally  on  the  walls.  The  woman  noticed  her 
visitor's  interest  in  the  collection,  and  with 
evident  pleasure  at  his  attention  said:  "  My 
husband  is  an  undertaker,  and  those  are 
pictures  of  the  people  he  has  buried  ! ' ' 
This  scheme  of  decoration  has,  at  least,  the 
merit  of  originality  ! 

The  trinity  of  individuality,   originaUty 
and  good  taste  should  not  be  separated, 


dining  room.  The  place  for  fruit  and  game 
is  on  the  table,  which  should  be  the  centre 
of  interest.  Scenes  of  battle,  confuVion,  or, 
in  fact,  anything  that  is  disturbing  should 
by  no  means  be  found  in  the  dining  room, 
or  any  other  place  in  a  moderate  home. 
Cheerful  subjects  are  best  in  the  room 
where  the  family  gather  for  refreshment  and 
social   enjoymenl. 


The   Mill,"   Ruysdael 


if  an    harmonious   result   is    desired. 

In  considering  what  to  hang  in.  one's 
dining  room,  it  may  be  well  to  decide  just 
what  not  to  put  upon  the  walls.  As  was 
said  in  the  beginning  of  this  article,  many 
things  must  be  thought  of  in  looking  at  this 
question,  especially  the  situation,  style  and 
environment  of  the  house,  and  the  taste  of 
the  occupants. 

Take  a  dining  room  that  is  found  in  the 
homes  of  the  majority  of  people  of  moder- 
ate means.  The  type  is  familiar;  usually  a 
square,  comfortable  room,  with  papered 
walls  and  the  usual  furniture,  consisting  of 
chairs,  table,  sideboard,  and,  perhaps,  a 
sofa. 

In  such  a  room  do  not  hang  pictures  of 
fruit  or  dead  animals,  unless  you  have  a  line 
piece  painted  by  some  well-known  artist. 
In  that  case  you  will  have  to  hang  it  in  the 


There  are  so  many  charming  prints  from 
the  best  artists  that  every  one  of  moderate 
means  can  possess  a  few. 

In  the  dining  room  some  landscapes  are 
very  effective,  but  one  must  use  great  dis- 
crimination in  choosing  these  bits.  The 
engravings  of  Turner,  which  are  of  great 
fineness  of  texture  and,  thus  produced,  are 
not  suitable  for  this  place.  Something  more 
sharp  in  outline  is  far  better. 

''The  Avenue,"  by  Hobbema,  makes  a 
good  dining  room  picture.  ''The  Mill," 
by  Ruysdael,  is  another  effective  bit.  The 
Dutch  landscape,  where  the  windmill  is  in 
evidence,  is  another  excellent  tyi)C  for  this 
purpose. 

Troyon's  pictures  of  cattle  are  very  good, 
especially  "  Le  Grie,"  which  is  i)eaceful 
and  picturesque. 

*'The  Rlessini^s,"  bv  Chardin,  is  one  of 


302 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


the  finest  pieces  of  sentiment  to  be  found, 
and  admirably  suited  to  the  dining  room. 

Millet's  pictures  are  splendid  examples, 
especially  ' '  Feeding  H er  Birds. "  '■'  Going 
to  Work"  and  '*The  Milkmaid"  are  also 
good. 

"  Haymaking  in  Auvergne,"  by  Rosa 
Bonheur,  and  her  ''Horse  Fair"  make 
good  scenes  for  this  room,  the  first  of  the 
two  being  especially  good. 

''  The  Buttery,"  by  Pieter  de  Hooch,  is 
an  ideal  subject  for  the  purpose,  and,  in 
fact,  most  of  this  artist' s  pictures  are  suit- 
able for  the  dining  room. 

Gerard  Don's  "Woman  Watering  a 
Plant,"  "The  Poulterer's  Shop,"  "The 
Grocer's  Shop"  and  "Girl  with  a  Fowl," 
are  excellent  subjects. 

In  the  "Woman  PeeUng  Apples,"  by 
Ter  Borch,  there  is  much  expression,  and  it 
makes  a  good  dining  room  piece.  The  face 
of  the  little  girl  in  this  picture  is  very  ex- 
pressive and  naive. 

"The  Shrimp  Girl,"  by  Hogarth,  is 
rather  a  good  subject. 


A  picture  of  entirely  different  character 
from  those  mentioned  is  "Lavinia, "  by 
Titian.  This  is  a  very  beautiful  composi- 
tion and  seems  especially  adapted  to  the 
purpose.  The  figure  of  the  girl  holding 
high  the  salver  of  fruit  is  extremely  grace- 
ful. 

If  one  has  a  large  engraving  of  "The 
Fire  in  the  Borgo,"  by  Raphael,  no  better 
place  could  be  found  for  it  than  the  dining 
room.  Although  scenes  of  confusion  are 
not  adapted  to  this  room,  in  the  "  Fire  in 
the  Borgo"  the  composition  is  so  har- 
monious and  the  figures  seem  to  have  a 
definite  aim  so  that  there  is  no  confusion. 
Only  controlled  activity  is  portrayed, 
and  in  a  masterly  manner  by  the  great 
artist. 

These  few  examples  given  are  only  types 
that  one  may  develop  along  any  lines  that 
one' s  personal  taste  may  direct.  The  field 
from  which  to  choose  is  great,  and  it  re- 
mains with  the  individual  to  see  that  her 
selection  is  harmonious  and  appropriate  to 
the  surroundings. 


"The  Fire  in  the  Borgo,"  Raphael 


The  House  That  Was  to  Make  People 

Happy 

By  FRANCES    CAMPBELL  SPARHA  WK 

Part  hi 

Conclusion 


MIRIAM  moved  through  the  house 
restlessly,  glancing  every  now  and 
then  at  the  great  old  clock  in  the 
hall,  which  ticked  its  moments  away  as 
calmly  as  if  its  owner  were  not  trembling 
on  the  verge  of  an  interview  that  in  her 
very  soul  she  hated.  Her  home  had 
grown  so  dear  to  her  that  the  very  ques- 
tion of  parting  with  it  was  full  of  sorrow  to 
her.  However  matters  turned  there  would 
be  a  hard  struggle  ;  she  was  passing  through 
it  now. 

As  she  had  said  to  her  guest,  Miriam 
had  never  seen  her  cousin;  she  dreaded 
meeting  the  kind  of  man  she  knew  he  must 
be,  dreaded  discussing  this  matter  with 
him,  and  the  inevitable  result.  But  she 
had  asked  him  to  come;  for  she  must  talk 
of  this  thing  to  him  face  to  face. 

Now  he  was  due.  The  minutes  dragged; 
she  wanted  it  all  over  —  since  it  must 
be.  Half  an  hour  before  she  had  seen 
Mrs.  Stone  and  Helen  with  book  and  work 
quietly  leave  the  house  for  some  sheltered 
spot  out  of  sight  and  hearing  of  herself 
and  cousin.  Would  he  be  late?  He 
would  probably  walk  from  the  station;  he 
must  be  poor  enough  to  need  badly  the 
property  he  had  come  to  wrest  from  her  if 
he  could. 

A  cloud  of  swiftly  moving  dust  far  down 
on  the  road  below!  As  it  drew  nearer 
she  saw  that  it  was  an  automobile.  Had 
the  Ashmonts  taken  that  morning  of  all 
days  to  pay  her  a  visit  ? 

The  automobile  turned  in  at  the  drive- 
way. As  it  now  bowled  slowly  along  she 
saw  that  it  was  very  handsome  and  richly 
appointed — no    public    conveyance,     al- 


though not  Mr.  Ashmont's;  nor  was  that 
he  sitting  beside  the  chauifeur,  a  tall  man, 
and  distinguished  looking;  even  before  he 
threw  off  his  dust  coat  and  ran  up  the 
steps  she  could  see  that.  Not  her  lawyer  ! 
His  lawyer  ? 

Her  maid  brought  in  the  card:  "  Mr. 
Frederic  Stone."  It  had  not  struck  her 
until  that  moment  that  the  man  who  in- 
tended to  deprive  her  of  her  home  was  of 
the  same  name  as  the  guests  who  had 
helped  to  make  that  home  dearer  to  her. 
Fate  was  full  of  irony,  and  among  the 
thousands  of  Stones  in  the  world  she  had 
come  to  perceive  that  some  were  stones  of 
stumbling.  But  of  course  she  was  glad 
this  man  was  alive.  And  yet  —  why,  he 
must  be  a  multi-millionaire  I  He  was 
faultlessly  groomed  and  had  the  air  of 
wealth  and  power.  What  did  he  want  of 
her  home,  her  own  precious  home,  which 
to  him  would  make  but  another  drop  of 
wealth  in  the  bucket  of  his  possessions  ? 

At  first  glance  he  looked  to  her  very 
young  for  his  almost  fifty  years;  but  study- 
ing his  face,  she  read  there  signs  of  mental 
strain  and  suffering,  controlled,  however, 
by  a  strong  will.  Because  he  looked  like 
his  mother,  Miriam  greeted  him  with  a 
kindness  he  had  not  expected.  '  •  If  only 
you  could  have  come  six  months  earlier, 
what  happiness  you  would  have  brought, ' ' 
ran  her  thoughts  as  she  o-azed  at  the  man 
of  whom  his  mother  would  have  been  so 
proud. 

Her  cousin's  manners  were  polished. 
But  no  charm  of  these  diminished  his 
intention  of  having  his  rights.  Yet  the 
interview  was,  evidentlv,  difficult  for  him. 


304 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


He  had  looked  for  anger,  for  bitter  remon- 
strance, for  pleading,  perhaps.  He  found 
a  woman  who  looked  at  him  with  calm 
eyes  and  spoke  in  even  tones. 

''I  asked  you  to  come  to  see  me,  Mr. 
Stone,"  she  said, after  they  had  exchanged 
a  few  courteous  remarks,  ' '  because  this  is 
a  matter  which  requires  no  intervention  of 
lawyers;  you  and  I  can  settle  it  ourselves 
in  five  minutes  —  or  so,  I  believe.  You 
are  perfectly  right  in  saying  that,  if  your 
mother  had  known  of  your  being  ahve,  she 
would  have  Vv-illed  this  place  and  at  least 
the  greater  part  of  the  money  to  you. 
That,  you  see,  lifts  this  out  of  a  mere 
question  of  legal  rights;  it  is  a  matter  of 
carrying  out  your  mother' s  wishes. ' ' 

Her  listener  gazed  at  her  in  a  surprise 
mingled  with  embarassment.  Undoubted- 
ly, it  is  hard  to  kick  against  the  pricks; 
but  to  kick  against  nothing  sometimes  has 
the  effect  of  upsetting  the  kicker.  "  Ah, 
yes,  Miss  Leslie,  yes,"  he  answered;  ''I 
am  rejoiced  that  you  see  the  matter  in  this 
light.      But  —  " 

''Pardon  me  one  moment,"  returned 
Miriam.      "  I  have  not  finished." 

''Ah  I"  exclaimed  the  other  suddenly, 
as  if  he  were  now  on  firmer  ground. 

' '  But  whatever  the  moral  right  may  be,' ' 
she  continued,  "  I  hold  the  legal  right 
to  this  jjlace;  and  I  shall  continue  to 
hold  it,  unless  you  will  pledge  yourself  to 
be  as  careful  to  carry  out  your  mother's 
wishes  in  willing  it  to  me,  as  I  shall  be  if  I 
resign  it  to  you.      Will  you  do  this?  " 

He  sat  looking  at  her  intently.  Some- 
what of  the  restfulness  of  Miriam's  pres- 
ence and  of  the  beauty  of  the  summer 
morning  were  stealing  into  his  heart. 
"What  were  my  mother's  wishes?"  he 
asked,  incredulous  that  they  could  have 
anything  in  harmony  with  the  woman 
before  him. 

She  told  him. 

He  sat  still  watching  her  as  he  listened. 
"When  you  draw  up  a  deed  of  gift, 
Miss  Leslie,"  he  said,  "as  I  suppose  you 
will  do,  if  we  settle  the  matter  in  this  way 
out  of  the  courts,  you  will  put  into  it  this 
condition;  and   I  will  promise  to  carry  it 


out  to  the  best  of  my  ability.  Will  you  do 
this  ?  Will  you  make  me  out  a  deed  of 
gift  of  the  place  and  the  money  in  this 
way?" 

''Yes,"  she  answered  him,  "I  will." 
Then  she  waited  for  him  to  rise  and  be 
gone;  she  felt  she  had  borne  all  that  she 
could  endure  that  day. 

But  he  sat  looking  about  him,  apparently 
in  no  haste.  "  It  is  thirty-five  years  since 
I  left  here,"  he  said.  "Then  I  never 
intended  to  come  back,  the  place  was  too 
full  of  hateful  memories  to  me.  I  suppose 
it  has  pleasant  ones  to  you  ?  " 

"Yes,  indeed,"  she  answered,  choking 
back  a  sob. 

"To  be  sure,"  he  said  coldly,  "your 
experience  and  mine  must  have  been  quite 
different."  He  rose  and  stood  silent, 
looking  out  of  the  library  window.  Sud- 
denly he  said:  "I  long  to  have  a  turn 
around  that  old  garden.  Would  you  mind 
taking  me  ?  I  used  to  pull  flowers  there 
when  I  was  a  baby  —  and  be  whipped  for 
it,  I  have  n't  a  doubt." 

"  Ah,  if  I  could  only  tell  you  how,  in  the 
later  years,  every  blow  and  every  hard 
word  fell  back  with  tenfold  force  upon  the 
giver!  "  she  cried.  "  If  I  could  only  make 
you  believe  that  love,  although  it  came 
late,  came  in  full  flood  at  last,  Mr.  Stone." 
And  Miriam  turned  to  him  a  face  full  of 
eager  entreaty,  as  she  told  of  the  long 
years  of  sorrow  and  remorse  and  the  hope- 
less search  for  the  son  whom  the  mother 
had  driven  from.  her.  She  perceived  his 
rapt  attention  and  hoped  that  he  would  do* 
justice  to  the  memory  of  her  aunt. 

But  when  she  had  finished  he  thanked 
her  without  comment.  As  they  went  on 
together  through  the  old  garden  paths  be- 
side which  the  old-fashioned  flowers  still 
bloomed,  he  seemed  rather  to  be  struggling 
with  a  question  that  embarassed  him  than 
to  be  taking  notice  of  these.  "  If  I  take 
my  own.  Miss  Leslie,"  he  said,  at  last,  ab- 
ruptly, "as  you  agree  that  my  mother 
would  have  wished,  I  don't  leave  —  that 
does  n'  t  leave  you  without  anything  to  Hve 
upon  ?  " 

"  I  shall  do  very  well,"  answered  Mir-

iam  haughtily.  At  the  moment  it  did  not 
seem  of  consequence  how  Uttle  she  would 
have  if  this  were  withdrawn.  For  at  every 
perfume  of  the  flowers,  at  every  breath  of 
the  pine  trees,  at  every  gliinpse  of  the 
grassy  slopes  stretching  out  to  the  hills 
beyond,  her  love  for  them  all  grew  still 
stronger,  and  it  seemed  as  if  her  heart 
would  break  to  part  with  them. 

''Because,  you  know,"  he  went  on, 
* '  T  —  I  don' t  want  you  to  be  —  to  be  —  I 
can  see  how  much  you  have  been  to  my 
mother  —  and  — ' ' 

''We  have  arranged  the  matter^  I 
think,"  returned  Miss  Leslie  still  more 
haughtily.  "I  have  told  you  my  condi- 
tions.    Then  I  shall  abdicate. " 

"But,  pray,  don't  hurry  away,"  he 
said.  "I  am  a  lonely  man.  I  have  lost 
all  that  was  dearest  to  me  —  wife  and  two 
beautiful  children —  all  in  an  instant.  In 
making  the  best  of  what  is  left  to  me  I 
travel  a  good  deal;  as  you  see,  I  am  now 
on  a  trip.  Don't  think  of  going  away 
from  here  before  —  before  winter.  Ah  ! ' ' 
and  he  hurried  forward,  "there  is  the  old 
summer-house.  Before  I  ran  away,  when 
I  was  a  boy,  I  used  to  hide  here  things 
that  I  planned  to  take  with  me.  I  had  to 
leave  most  of  them.  I  wonder  if  they  are 
there  now  under  the  seats?  " 

Miriam  did  not  answer.  He  was  a  few 
steps  in  advance  of  her.  He  turned.  She 
had  not  expected  that.    She  was  crying. 

Suddenly,  two  hands  fell  lightly  upon 
her  shoulders  and  dark  eyes  full  of  kind- 
ness (looked  full  into  her  own.  "I'm  a 
brute,"  said  the  man,  "to  keep  up  this 
farce  and  tease  you  any  longer.  It  is  true 
I  came  here  intending  to  take  back  my 
own.  But  Miriam  Leslie,  my  cousin, 
daughter  of  the  only  near  relative  who  was 
ever  really  kind  to  me  in  my  desolate  boy- 
hood, no  honor  requires  you  to  give  up 
this  house;  to  you  it  is  home  and  you  love 
it.  Tome  —  well  !  One  thing  I  ain  going 
to  ask,  Miriam,  let  me  come  here  one  day 
in  the  year  and  learn  from  you  to  think  of 
my  mother  as  you  have  told  me  I  may 
think;  take  the  bitterness  out  of  my  heart; 
the  sorrow  can  never  go.      Don' t  you  see, 


you  unselfish  child,"  he  went  on,  "  that  it 
is  you,  and  not  I,  who  can  carry  out  my 
mother's  wishes,  you  and  not  I  who  can 
make  people  happy  in  this  house;  there- 
fore, even  by  her  wishes,  it  belongs  to  you ! 
Why  —  even  now  you  have  told  me  you 
have  friends  here  —  friends,  at  first,  be- 
cause they  needed  you,  I  am  sure,  what- 
ever more  there  may  be  in  it  now.  All  my 
mother  left  you  is  your  own  by  her  wish,  if 
she  were  here  now.  All  my  financial 
affairs  have  prospered;  it  is  only  my  home 
that  is  desolate.  But  come,"  he  added, 
changing  his  tone,  —  "t.le  summer-house! 
I  want  to  see  it."  And  the  two  turned 
into  the  box-bordered  walk  that  led  up  to 
it. 

Helen  dropped  her  book  at  the  sound 
of  voices.  "They're  coming  straight 
here,  mamma,"  she  said,  peeping  out  be- 
tween the  vines.  "  We're  caught  unless 
we  fairly  run  away,  and  we  can' t  do  that, 
because  they  would  see  us.  We  shall 
have  to  face  the  music,  make  our  bow  and 
then  slip  away.  Poor  Miss  LesHe  !  How 
I  hope  he  wasn't  cruel  to  her.  Why, 
mamma,  she  's  smiling  and  he  is  laughing. 
I  think  he  has  a  good,  voice,  a  true  voice. 
Why,  mamma,  are  you  ill?  What  is  the 
matter  with  you  ?  Mamma  !  mamma  ! 
What  shall  I  do?     What  is  it?" 

For  her  mother  had  risen  and  stood 
with  her  hand  pressed  on  her  heart,  all 
color  gone  from  her  face,  her  lips  parted, 
her  eyes  wide-opened,  her  breath  coming 
pantingly.  As  if  drawn  irresistibly,  she 
moved  forward  until  she  stood  in  the 
doorway  of  the  summer-house.  Her  lips 
moved,  but  Helen  could  scarcely  catch  her 
words:  "The dead  !  the  dead  !  "  she  whis- 
pered. "But  that  voice  !  Hush,  Helen  I 
Let  me  hear  it.  If  it  is  only  a  dream,  I 
would  dream  forever  !  " 

"Mamma!  maninia  !  "  cried  the  girl. 
"  Oh,  what  is  it  ?  " 

But  the  other  clitl  not  hear  her.  She 
threw  back  with  one  liand  the  tlrooping 
branch  of  the  vine  in  the  doorway,  and, 
cHnging  to  this  ami  to  the  doorpost,  she 
bent  still  further  forward,  her  eyes  fastened 
upon  the  face  of  the  stranger.

He  had  reached  the  foot  of  the  arbor 
steps,  and  looked  up. 

The  face  above  him  was  radiant.  The 
eyes  sought  his  with  ever  new  assur- 
ance. 

"  My  wife  !  "  he  cried,  ''  My  wife  !  Is 
it  a  vision  ?  It  must  be  !  Oh,  Helen  ! 
Helen!" 

The  next  moment  she  was  in  his  arms. 

Mrs.  Stone  had  indeed  seen  her  husband 
go  down.  But  he  had  come  up  again,  not, 
however,  until  the  raging  sea  had  swept 
the  two  far  apart.  A  whaler's  boat  had 
picked  him  up  and  carried  him  delirious  to 


Labrador.  He  was  a  year  finding  himself 
and  getting  able  to  leave  there.  He  found 
that  he  himself,  his  wife  and  little  ones 
had  all  been  recorded  dead.  But,  know- 
ing the  record  false  in  one  instance,  he 
had  long  searched;  but  years  ago  had 
given  up  hope. 

''If  I  hadn't  made  it  right  with  you, 
Miriam,  before  this."  he  said,  when  he 
had  heard  the  story  of  her  guests,  ''draw- 
ing and  quartering  would  have  been  too 
good  for  me.  I  must  say  that  your  house 
to  make  people  happy  in  has  been  a  perfect 
success." 


Miss  Hetty's  Valentine 


By  ALIX  THORN 


MISS  SIMPSON  Hved  just  opposite 
the  Library,  in  a  great,  solemn, 
stone  house,  with  many  windows, 
and  so  closely  were  the  heavy  lace  curtains 
drawn,  that  one  could  not  get  so  much  as 
a  peep  inside.  The  front  door  with  its 
big  brass  knocker  looked  as  if  it  were  never 
opened. 

Miss  Hetty  Simpson  lived  on  the  cor- 
ner, below  the  school,  in  a  cheerful  white 
house  that  had  tall,  fluted  columns.  Gay 
flowers  bloomed  in  Miss  Hetty's  sunny 
windows,  and  Miss  Hetty  always  had  a 
smile  and  a  pleasant  ' '  good  morning, 
dears,"  for  the  children  on  their  way  to 
school.  Indeed,  she  looked,  with  her 
bobbing  white  curls  and  pink  cheeks,  not 
unlike  one  of  her  own  fragrant  roses. 

Now  every  child  in  the  town  knew  and 
loved  Miss  Hetty,  but  few,  very  few,  knew 
Miss  Simpson.  In  fact,  I  think  they 
were  rather  afraid  of  the  stern  looking  old 
lady,  who  was  no  relation  of  merry  Miss 
Hetty.  Sometimes  Miss  Simpson  might 
be  seen  walking  slowly  around  her  garden, 
between  prim  rows  of  box,  her  skirts  held 
up  daintily,  peering  curiously  through  her 
big  gold  spectacles. 


Little  eight-year-old  Cicely  Thomp- 
son was  one  of  the  children  who  passed 
Miss  Hetty  Simpson's  every  day,  and  she 
never  failed  to  look  up  and  send  a  quiet 
little  smile  to  the  window  where  her  old 
friend  sat  knitting.  It  was  February  and 
only  a  week  before  St.  Valentine's  Day. 
and  Cicely,  who  had  been  long  saving  her 
ten  cent  pieces,  five  cent  pieces,  as  well  as 
all  her  pennies,  started  out,  one  Saturday 
morning,  to  buy  her  valentines.  As  every 
one  knew,  the  best  place  for  valentines  was 
the  post-oflice.  The  post-mistress,  her- 
self, always  went  to  the  city,  early  in  the 
month,  to  select  her  stock,  and  her  win- 
dow was  well  worth  seeing,  I  can  tell  you. 

Cicely  had  chosen  a  lovely  valentine,  all 
lace  paper,  for  her  best  friend,  Patty 
Phipps,  a  fine  silvery  heart  for  Myra,  her 
next  door  neighbor,  a  pink  little  cherub 
for  brother  William,  and  a  red  arrow  held 
by  a  fat  cupid  for  Cousin  Amanda.  Then 
Cicely's  cheeks  grew  pinker  as  she  whis- 
pered to  herself:  "I  wonder  if  Miss 
Hetty  won't  be  surprised  when  she  gets  a 
valentine,  for  I'm  going  to  buy  her  just 
the  beau  tifulest  one  here!"  The  valentine 
she    at   last    decided    upon  seemed   fairly 


MISS  HETTY'S  VALENTINE 


307 


made  for  Miss  Hetty.  Around  a  little 
gold-latticed  window  climbed  the  reddest 
of  red  roses,  and  between  the  roses  could 
be  seen  a  smiling  lady,  as  sweet  as  Miss 
Hetty,  and  just  below  the  window,  in 
shining  letters,  one  might  read,  ''I  love 
you,  Lady  Mine."  Cicely  could  not  re- 
sist, though,  to  be  sure,  it  was  not  quite 
the  usual  way,  putting  on  the  back  of  the 
valentine,  ''from  Cicely  Thompson,"  for 
she  thought,  "I'd  like  Miss  Hetty  to 
know  I  sent  it.  And  oh,  oh,"  giggled 
Cicely  delightedly  surveying  the  purchase, 
"oh,  oh,  she  '11  be  a  happy  lady  when 
she  sees  it."  Then  in  her  precise  litde 
hand  writing,  she  carefully  addressed 
the  embossed  envelope: 

"For  Miss  Simpson,  Elmville. " 
She    decided    that    Miss  Simpson   looked 
more  elegant  than  Miss   Hetty  Simpson, 
though,  as  Miss  Hetty,  people  always  spoke 
and  thought  of  her. 

St.  Valentine's  Day  fiad  come  and 
gone,  and  Cicely  had  been  very  gener- 
ously remembered.  It  made  a  httle  girl 
feel  pretty  rich  to  find  how  many  friends 
she  had,  it  gave  one  quite  a  Christmasy 
feeling. 

It  was  the  afternoon  of  the  fifteenth  that 
Cicely,  who  had  been  sent  on  an  errand, 
went  skipping  past  the  stone  house,  where 
Miss  Simpson  lived.  She  looked  straight 
ahead,  for  the  somber  mansion  always  made 
her  feel  like  walking  quietly,  almost  like  a 
grown-up  person,  and  to-day  she  was  too 
happy  to  go  slowly.  Suddenly  she  heard 
a  smart  tap,  tap,  tap,  on  a  window,  and, 
turning,  she  saw  old  Miss  Simpson  stand- 
ing at  the  door,  beckoning  to  her. 

"Come  here,  a  moment,  child,"  she 
called  shrilly,  "I  want  to  speak  to  you," 
and  frightened  Cicely  obeyed,  though,  as 
she  went  up  the  steep  steps  her  heart  beat 
surprisingly  loud.  Into  a  long,  shadowy 
parlor  the  child  followed  the  old  lady,  and 


then  Cicely  almost  cried  aloud  as,  to  her 
great  astonishment,  she  saw  lying  upon  the 
polished  center  table,  the  lovely  valentine, 
with  its  tender  message,  that  she  had 
chosen  for  Miss  Hetty. 

"Little  girl,"  said  Miss  Simpson,  and 
her  voice  was  not  shrill  at  all,  now,  but 
gentle,  and  with  an  odd  sort  of  quiver  in 
it,  "  you  made  an  old  woman,  very  happy, 
yesterday;  I  didn't  know  that  any  child 
thought  enough  about  me  to  send  me  a 
valentine,  and  such  a  charming  one,  too. 
My  maid,  Jane,  told  me  who  you  were,  to- 
day. Thank  you  Cicely  Thompson,  I 
shall  keep  the  valentine  on  this  table,  and 
when  I'm  not  feeling  very  well,  or  when 
I'm  lonely,  as  old  ladies  are,  sometimes, 
I'm  going  to  look  at  it,  and  say,  "Cicely 
sent  me  this.  Cicely  remembered  me." 

And  I  am  glad  to  say  that  Cicely  didn't 
tell  Miss  Simpson  that  it  was  all  a  sad 
mistake,  and  that  the  valentine  intended 
for  Miss  Hetty  was  now  lying  upon  that 
polished  table.  She  started  to  say,  "Oh, 
you  see  the  envelope,"  — but  closed  her 
lips  firmly,  and,  instead,  held  out  a  friendly 
little  hand,  as  she  said  softly,  "I,  I'm 
glad  you  like  it ;  it  is  a  pretty  nice  one,  I 
think.  I  had  some  valentines,  too,  —  I 
guess  I  must  go  now,  good-bye." 

"Good-bye,  dear  child,"  replied  Miss 
Simpson,  "come  and  see  me;  I  rather 
think  I  have  several  things  that  might  in- 
terest a  Httle  maid.  There  's  a  China  doll, 
in  a  silk  gown,  up  stairs,  and  a  cabinet  of 
stuffed  birds  in  the  library,  and  Jane 
makes  good  seed  cakes ;  come  soon. 
Cicely." 

"Oh,  I'd  like  to  ! "  breathed  Cicely, 
and  come  she  did,  again  and  again,  to  the- 
solemn  stone  house.  And  such  firm 
friends  did  she  and  Miss  Simpson  become 
that,  before  the  next  St.  Valentine's  Day, 
Cicely  could  truthfully  repeat  the  words  of 
the  valentine,  "  I  love  you.  Lady  Mine." 


Who  Was  George  Washington? 

{^Being  the  Chronicle  of  an  Unbeliever) 
By  GRACE  STONE  FIELD 


IT  was  an  old-fashioned  and  inartisti- 
cally  shiny  print;  and  it  depicted  a 
very  small  boy,  arrayed  in  a  bygone 
style  and  clasping  a  hatchet. 

There  was  also  a  large  pompous  man, 
attired  even  to  powdered  hair  and  shoe 
buckles,  like  his  small  son,  and  holding 
his  hands  suggestively  behind  him.  And 
a  tiny  cherry  tree,  showing  unmistakable 
signs  of  unfortunate  relations  with  the 
aforesaid  hatchet,  completed  the  picture. 

Sylvia  cocked  her  curly  head  on  one 
side  and  surveyed  the  print.  Marked  dis- 
approval shone  in  her  luminous  eyes. 
"  Who  did  you  say  it  was  ?  "   she  asked. 

Her  aunt  replied  with  somewhat  la- 
bored cheerfulness.  ''George  Washing- 
ton, Sylvia,  and,"  impressively,  ''he  never 
told  a  lie. ' ' 

' '  Well  ?  ' '  interrogated  Sylvia,  with  a 
painful  show  of  animosity,  ' '  Well  ?  ' ' 

' '  Why,  don' t  you  think  that  was  very 
noble  of  him,  Sylvia  ?  You  see  he  had 
chopped  his  father' s  cherry  tree  and  when 
his  father  asked  him:  'George,  who  did 
that  ? '  he  said  :  '  Father  I  cannot  tell  a  he, 
I  did  it  with  my  little  hatchet. '  ' ' 

There  was  silence,  punctuated  by  loud 
approving  ticks  from  the  old  hall  clock. 
Sylvia  shifted  her  hmpid  gaze  from  the 
print  to  her  aunt's  face.  She  looked  puz- 
zled, and  she  sighed  a  little.  "I  have 
never  told  a  he  either,"  she  said  simply. 

Her  aunt  looked  disappointed.  This 
was  not  the  answer  she  had  hoped  for, 
but  she  was  used  to  defeat  at  Sylvia's 
hands,  and  she  took  it  like  a  veteran.  It 
seemed  time  to  change  the  subject. 

"Sylvia  dear,"  she  said,  "would  you 
like  some  bread  and  jam  ?  " 

The  child's  sober  face  dimpled  instantly. 
"  Yes,  please,"  she  said,  "  bread  and  but- 
ter and  jam,  please. ' ' 

Left  alone  with  this  toothsome  morsel, 


Sylvia  again  surveyed  the  print,  and  then 
allowed  her  glance  to  wander,  furtively, 
to  a  framed  portrait  of  her  aunt's  hero, 
hanging  on  the  wall  of  the  large,  old- 
timey  living  room.  The  eyes  above  the 
thin,  unsmihng  lips,  followed  her  about 
the  room.  She  tried  the  low  footstool, 
the  window  seat,  the  couch,  all  to  no  pur- 
pose. To  her  childish  mind  the  eyes 
were  distinctly  disapproving.  They  rested 
on  her  tangled  curls,  her  sticky  hands, 
and  a  stain  of  jam  on  her  white  pinafore, 
alike  with  distaste. 

"Why  does  he  always  look  at  me?" 
moaned  Sylvia.  ' '  Why  does  he  never  look 
at  any  one  else  ?  I  don' t  like  you,  so 
there  now !  I  don' t  care  if  you  never  told 
a  he,  you  'd  be  horrid  if  you  had.  That 's 
nothing  !  Nothing  !  " 

The  eyes  looked  at  her  with  a  sort  of 
pitying  contempt  in  their  serene  depths. 
A  sudden  impulse  seized  her.  Could 
she  ?  She  would.  She  dragged  a  chair 
across  the  room  and  climbed  upon  it.  She 
could  just  reach,  and  her  sticky  fingers  did 
their  worst. 

The  father  of  his  country  retired,  per- 
force, behind  a  veil  of  jam,  and  Sylvia  de- 
scended with  a  long  breath  of  satisfaction. 

Her  victory  was  complete  but  short- 
lived. Full  well  she  knew  her  aunt  might 
return  at  any  moment.  Detection  would 
be  instant;  punishment,  inevitable.  So, 
waiting  as  long  as  she  dared,  at  last  she 
surrendered,  and  climbing  the  chair  again, 
erased,  with  a  damp  cloth,  at  once  the  jam 
and  her  peace  of  mind,  for  the  eyes  were 
on  her  in  a  second.  Avoiding  them  care- 
fully, she  brought  her  own  httle  rocker, 
and,  planting  it  with  its  back  to  the  pic- 
ture, sat  down  and  began  her  "work," 
said  work  consisting  of  some  crinkled 
braid  known  as  rick  rack,  twisted  by  Syl- 
via's small,  unskilful  fingers  into  geomet- 


308 


WHO  WAS  GEORGE  WASHINGTON 


309 


rically  impossible  circles,  amalgamated  by 
discs  of  twisted  thread.  When  Sylvia  had 
laboriously  built  a  yard  of  this  trimming, 
her  mother  bought  it  for  ten  cents,  and 
when  enough  for  a  pinafore  was  finished, 
the  colossal  sum  of  thirty  cents  was  hers 
to  n-we  and  to  hold.  All  this  was  sup- 
posed to  foster  a  spirit  of  industry  in  the 
small  Sylvia,  and  a  green  metal  bank,  with 
a  tall  red  chimney,  conspicuously  dis- 
played on  the  living-room  mantelpiece, 
suggested  a  wise  disposal  of  her  funds. 

However,  a  saving  habit  was  not  bred 
in  Sylvia's  bones,  and  so,  alas  !  it  refused 
to  come  out  in  her  flesh. 

When  brought  to  account,  dilligent 
questioning  usually  elicited  the  enlighten- 
ing remark  that  she  had  "  spent  it, "  how 
she  did  not  divulge. 

The  fact  is  that  Sylvia  was  quite  per- 
suaded in  her  own  mind  that  General 
George  Washington  knew  all  about  it, 
and  also  that  he  disapproved  entirely,  and 
that  was  quite  enough  without  her  aunt's 
disapproval,  and  her  aunt  would  never  un- 
derstand. 

Had  the  genial  gentleman  who  dis- 
pensed soda  water  at  the  corner  drug 
store,  near  Sylvia's  city  home,  been  con- 
sulted, he  would  have  told  where  her 
pennies  went.  For  three  cents  she  could 
purchase  a  glass  half  full  of  "  any  desired 
flavor,"  and  it  was  Sylvia's  dearest  wish  to 
taste  each  in  turn.  She  had  almost  reached 
that  tempting  faucet  which  was  labelled 
"cofl"ee  "  and  which  she  longed  yet  hard- 
ly dared  to  sample  ;  for  coffee  was  natu- 
rally a  forbidden  beverage,  and  to  Sylvia, 
even  at  nine,  forbidden  waters  were 
sweet.  However,  she  had  skirted  all  round 
this  faucet;  had  tried  the  fizzy  joys  of 
sarsaparilla,  of  birch  beer,  of  chocolate  and 
lemon,  and  was  really  bravely  trying  to 
make  up  her  mind  to  begin  all  over  again, 
and  wear  the  blue  ribbon  as  far  as  coffee 
soda  was  concerned,  when  temptation  was 
providentially  removed  from  her  path. 
The  family  moved  to  the  country,  and 
Sylvia  was  saved. 

Packing  had  been  going  on  for  weeks, 
and  early  in  the  proceedings  Sylvia's  bete 


noir,  her  embodied  conscience,  George 
Washington,  of  the  stern  eyes  and  con- 
demning expression,  had  been  consigned 
to  the  depths  of  a  large  packing  box. 
Sylvia  hailed  this  reprieve  with  a  glad 
heart,  although  she  knew  it  was  not  for 
long.  But  who  shall  deny  that  hidden 
forces,  whether  for  good  or  evil,  were  at 
work  ?  When  unpacking  was  in  progress 
at  their  country  home,  a  sudden  exclama- 
tion of  horror  and  dismay,  proceeding  from 
the  house,  caused  Sylvia  to  drop  her 
skipping  rope,  and  rush  in  from  the  broad 
veranda. 

Mary,  the  maid  of  all  work,  was  on  her 
knees  before  the  box  of  pictures.  Dismay 
was  written  on  her  face,  her  hands  were 
raised  to  heaven.  "  For  the  land  sakes! 
Miss  Sylvia,"  she  gasped,  "what  will  your 
aunt  say,  here  's  General  George  Wash- 
ington's picture  all  smashed  in  ?  " 

Sylvia  drew  near  and  gazed  aghast.  Not 
thus  would  she  have  compassed  her  ene- 
my's downlali.  A  cataclysm  of  splintered 
glass  was  uppermost,  and  underneath, 
what  wreck  !  what  ruin  ! 

And  yet  a  load  was  lifted  from  her 
childish  mind.  No  more  the  disapprov- 
ing eyes  followed  her,  and  only  a  chas- 
tened memory  restrained  her  some-time 
frivohty.  For  Sylvia  grew  a  little  frivo- 
lous. She  was  pretty  and  she  wanted  to 
be  prettier.  So  to  this  end  she  adorned 
her  small  person  somewhat  lavishly  with 
frills  and  ribbons,  and  whatnot. 

At  fourteen  she  narrowly  missed  conver- 
sion to  an  admiration  of  George  Washing- 
ton of  hateful  memory.  She  happened  on 
Thackeray' s  ' '  Virginians  ' '  and  gained  from 
its  pages  a  different  view  of  him.  But 
when  she  confided  her  delight  to  her 
aunt,  that  prosaic  person  said:  ''  Dear  me, 
Sylvia,  you  don' t  believe  all  that,  do  you  ? 
Why,  all  you  read  in  books,  is  n'  t  true, 
child." 

Sylvia  had  felt  like  crying.  Perhaps  she 
did  cry.  7/"  she  did,  it  was  far  away  from 
her  aunt,  that  shatterer  of  illusions,  that 
destroyer  of  dreams. 

When  Sylvia  was  seventeen,  her  family 
moved  back  again  to  the  city,  and  strange-

THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


ly  enough,  she  chose  for  her  bosom 
friend  a  girl  of  twenty,  as  well  poised  and 
thoughtful  as  Sylvia  was  flighty  and  hare- 
brained. At  twenty,  Emma  Hall  had 
already  chosen  her  ' '  career. "  Dowered 
with  beauty  and  wealth,  she  might  have 
had  all  the  things  for  which  Sylvia' s  frivo- 
lous soul  vainly  longed.  Instead,  she 
elected  to  teach  daily,  in  the  public  schools, 
as  cosmopolitan  a  lot  of  children  as  ever 
assembled  in  one  room. 

Sylvia  marveled  much,  and  could  not 
understand.  One  day,  at  Emma's  urgent 
invitation,  she  went  to  visit  the  school. 
The  spirit  moving  her  she  made  a  most 
elaborate  toilet,  quite  out  of  keeping  with 
the  simplicity  of  the  occasion.  She 
reached  the  schoolhouse  a  Uttle  late,  and 
as  she  neared  the  big  door,  marked  grade 
four,  she  heard  her  friend's  voice.  "  Now 
children,  all  together,"  it  said.  Her 
knock  was  not  heard,  and  turning  the 
knob,  she  paused  on  the  threshold. 


There  on  the  wall,  draped  with  his 
country's  majestic  colors,  hung  a  picture 
of  Washington.  The  same,  the  very 
same,  that  had  hung  in  the  living  room, 
long  ago  ! 

As  of  old  he  fixed  her  with  his  eye;  as 
of  old  she  read  all  her  shortcomings  in 
his  wide  gaze.  She  knew  that  her  sash 
was  too  tight,  her  small  shoes  even  smaller 
than  they  need  be,  and  yes,  there  was  too 
much  powder  on  her  nose.  And  as  she 
stood  transfixed,  the  children,  all  together, 
as  they  had  been  bidden,  Jew  and  Gen- 
tile, Irish  and  American,  Swede  and 
Italian,  and  one  little  nigger,  lifted  up 
their  voices,  as  if  in  answer  to  her  child- 
hood's question,  and  chanted,  with  ab- 
surd inflection,  but  unmistakable  enthu- 
siasm: "Who  was  George  Washington  ? 
He  was  the  father  of  his  country  !  Ftrsf 
in  peace,  Jirs^  in  war,  and  yfr^Y  in  the 
hearts  of  his  country-men  ! ' ' 


Varied  Philanthropy 

By  KATE   GANNETT  WELLS 


IT  is  comical  and  aggravating  to  work 
with,  you  can't  work  against  it,  the 
kindly  conservatism  of  caution.  Peo- 
ple are  so  afraid  that  some  co-worker  may 
not  be  socially  what  might  be  desired,  that 
philanthropy  gets  all  broken  up  into  cliques. 
It  is  respectability  rather  than  fitness  that 
often  decides  admission  to  inner  circles. 

Any  one,  who  is  old  enough  to  remem- 
ber the  homogeneousness  of  philanthropy 
in  Boston  fifty  years  ago,  when  everybody 
worked  together  as  far  as  social  lines  were 
concerned  (because  there  really  were  not 
any  other  people  for  workers  than  one's 
selves),  must  rejoice  that  now  philan- 
thropy has  grown  hybrid,  and  that  there 
are  many  kinds  of  people  to  do  divers  so- 
cial works.     All   the   same,   the  study  of 


Who  is  Who  in  church  teas,  mission  work, 
fairs,  etc. ,  is  amusing. 

Still  it  is  to  be  feared  that  the  subdivi- 
sions of  philanthropy  are  becoming  so 
minute  that  broad  outlooks  will  be  lost  in 
social  research  and  statistics.  For  in- 
stance, in  Rhode  Island,  there  are  1,251 
organizations,  not  counting  co-partnerships 
in  business  and  churches.  Think  of  thirty- 
two  temperance  organizations  in  one  small 
city, —  and  yet  temperance  work  must  be 
so  largely  individual,  in  order  to  be  suc- 
cessful, that  thirty-two  societies  do  not 
seem  enough. 

But  how  are  these,  and  hundreds  of 
thousands  of  organizations  all  over  the 
country,  going  to  be  supported?  This  is 
the  crux  of  the  matter.     Which,  the  State 


VARIED  PHILANTHROPY 


or  philanthropy,  is  to  support  them,  one 
or  both  ?  Shall  government  become  minu  te- 
ly  paternalistic  ?  As  illustration,  look  at 
our  public  schools,  and  find  in  them  the 
multiplicity  of  inferences  that  can  be  drawn 
in  regard  to  the  proper  scope  of  their  sup- 
port. Do  free  text-books  'involve  free 
ocuhsts  and  free  spectacles  ?  Shall  any 
worthy  person  introduce  by  free  gift  any 
reform  which  seems  advisable  to  him  ? 
Take  the  great  playground  movement, 
started  first,  in  this  country,  in  Boston  as 
a  private  philanthropy,  but  which  now  has 
become  a  national  organization,  its  work 
supported  by  civic  authorities.  Since 
playgrounds  are  so  essentially  educational, 
perhaps  they  should  not  remain  on  a  level 
with  charity,  but  shall  they  include  golf 
links,  shall  there  be  field  houses  as  well  as 
school  houses?  An  enthusiast  in  the 
value  of  playgrounds  does  not  want  to 
have  his  zeal  lessened  by  a  mere  question 
of  money. 

Then  see  how  the  ideas  of  leaders 
change,  how  the  "best"  people  trans- 
form their  emphases  !  When  the  Asso- 
ciated Charities  sprang,  or  grew  into  be- 
ing, investigation,  registration,  co-opera- 
tion and  adequate  reUef  were  its  separate 
fundamentals.  Now  they  are  not  so  set 
off  from  each  other,  and  an  all-round 
knowledge  is  demanded  on  behalf  of  for - 
mal  registration.  If  all  churches  had 
taught  the  art  of  human  sympathy,  socio- 
logical names  might  not  have  been  invented. 
But  shall  the  art  be  under  civic  control, 
or  a  private  concern?  Again,  where  is 
the  money  to  come  from  ? 

Hospital  work,  shall  it  be  constantly 
enlarged  by  State  and  City,  provided 
sectarianism  does  not  creep  in?  This  is 
almost  an  annual  legislative  question,  sad- 
dened by  personal  entreaties.  Certainly 
the  better  the  hospital,  the  more  need  for 
it  and  its  training  schools,  and,  conse- 
quently, more  expense  is  entailed.  Yet, 
at  present,  there  is  a  dearth  of  student 
nurses,  for  other  as  respectable  and  less 
arduous  ways  of  training  for  self-support 
have  opened.  Shall  nurses  on  probation 
give  their  time  and  also  pay  a  tuition  fee, 


or  shall  the  State,  through  the  hospital, 
pay  them  to  learn  that  which  is  useful,  not 
only  to  the  community,  but  also  is  their 
very  self-support?  A  great  deal  of  cant 
is  talked  about  the  nobleness  of  that  ser- 
vice for  others,  which  after  all  has  to  look 
out  first  for  its  own  self-support,  else  it, 
in  turn,  will  have  to  be  supported  by  the 
State  as,  — an  indigent. 

But  enough  of  illustrations  now,  for  they 
multiply  day  by  day.  Let  us,  instead, 
be  glad  that  there  are  large  general  rules, 
governing  the  expenditures  of  State  and 
City,  which  are  accepted,  and  that  the 
pressure  of  private  philanthropies  makes  it- 
self felt  only  in  the  by-corners  of  legislation. 
Friendliness,  not  graft,  it  might  be  called, 
yet  its  cash  value  is  the  same.  The 
shrewder  the  lady  manager  is,  the  more 
likely  is  she  to  see  some  legally  winding 
road,  along  which  her  pet  Society  can  re- 
ceive aid. 

Supposing,  however,  that  what  is  now 
private  philanthropy  were  carried  on  by 
State  or  City  aid,  then  there  would  be  no 
opportunity  left  for  the  display  of  that  air 
of  injured  dignity  with  which  individuals 
so  often  decline  to  help  State  or  City 
work.  Take  playgrounds,  for  instance. 
Just  as  fast  as  their  matrons  or  guardians 
become  salaried  employees  of  the  City, 
does  private  aid  cease,  and  rightly.  ''I 
am  already  taxed  as  a  citizen  for  the  sup- 
port of  playgrounds.  Why  should  I  do 
more?" — is  constantly  offered  in  justifi- 
cation of  money  withheld  as  a  personal 
gift.  It  is  not  selfishness  on  the  part  of 
those  refusing.  They  are  standing  up  for  a 
principle.  They  decline  to  accept  double 
taxation,  by  the  State  and  by  friends.  Yet 
these  very  persons  may  ask  for  State  aid 
for  some  project  of  their  own,  the  last  one 
appearing  the  best.  Such  petitions  come 
to  the  State  more  than  to  the  City,  for  the 
former  is  recognized  as  the  supreme  arbiter 
in  personahties;  hence  the  Resolves  on 
behalf  of  one  or  another  philanthropy  have 
to  be  gravely  considered  in  Committee 
hearings. 

And  so  it  comes  about  that,  underlying 
varied  philanthropy,  is   the  unchangeable 


312 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


principle  that,  if  you  want  a  thing,  you 
have  got  to  pay  for  it,  and  that  taxation, 
no  matter  how  subdivided,  is  personal 
payment. 

And  then  comes  the  next  puzzle,  what 
philanthropies  can  legitimately  appeal  for 
State  aid?  —  and  the  answer  too  often  is: 
That  for  which  I  ask. 

Among  the  many  departments  of  phil- 
anthropy, one  that  has  escaped  tbe  per- 
niciousness  of  appeal  to  the  State  is  the 
vast  net- work  of  private  cooking  schools. 
As  a  science,  domestic  cookery  is  taught 
in  public  schools  —  but,  as  a  personal 
household  remedy  and  joy,  these  private 
schools  have  gone  their  own  way,  their 
text  books  and  receipts  winnowing  paths 
to  success  faster  than  ever  could  be  done 
by  means  of  State  aid.  And  I  like  to 
think  this  is  so,  because  their  work,  being 


a  personal  one  for  home  ends,  rests  on  its 
own  base  of  personahty,  and  has  never 
alleged  on  altruistic  grounds,  that  the 
State  ought  to  support  it.  If  other  organ- 
izations would  only  remain  as  firm  in  re- 
jection of  insidious  approaches  to  State 
aid,  an  additional  one-quarter  of  one  per 
cent,  might  not  have  to  be  levied  so  often, 
to  meet  the  expenses  of  government. 

And  just  because  we  are  so  heavily 
taxed,  are  we  right  in  being  reluctant 
about  appealing  for  State  aid  on  behalf  of 
an  organization  that  cannot  prove  its  in- 
fallibihty  more  than  any  or  all  other  or- 
ganizations. What  does  it  all  amount 
to? 

That  each  one  of  us  should  be  slow  in 
starting  up  any  scheme  of  education  or 
beneficence  that  is  going  to  involve  an  in- 
crease in  taxation. 


His  Valentine 

By   GRACE  STONE   FIELD 

"The  rose  is  red,  the  violet  blue," 
Quoth  Polly,  sweetly  smiling; 

"So  Billy,  here's  a  rose  for  you," 
The  witch,  my  heart  beguiling  ! 

Of  course  I  know  that  ancient  rhyme, 

Of  love,  defying  cleaver. 
And  yet,  I  '11  bet  a  silver  dime 

That  Polly's  a  deceiver  — 


If  she  's  in  love,  and  that  may  be,. 

It '  s  with  some  other  fellow; 
You  see,  the  rose  she  gave  to  me. 

Instead  of  red,  was  yellow  ! 


For  the  Amateur  House- Builder 


By  LEE  McCRAE 


EVERY  one  who  has  never  attempted 
it  is  absokitely  certain  that  he  or  she 
could  build  a  house  that  would  be  a 
'  t  thing  of  beauty  and  a  joy  forever. ' ' 

But  a  single  experience  convinces  the 
most  confident  that  it  is  not  at  all  easy, 
that  the  best  laid  plans  '"o  mice  and 
men" — even  women's  —  appear  better 
upon  paper  than  they  materialize  in  wood 
and  stone.  Even  the  samples  of  paint  on 
a  color  card  look  different  when  the  shade 
is  spread  over  walls  and  roofs. 

Barring  the  too  common  trial  of  having 
dishonest  contractors  and  unskilled  work- 
men to  deal  with,  the  amateur  house- 
builder's  troubles  are  due  ordinarily  to  a 
few  simple  things.      Hence  this  paper. 

A  stroll  about  town  in  a  study  of  archi- 
tecture will  prove  that  the  roof  is  the  chief 
feature  of  a  building.  Its  plan  and  pitch 
make  or  mar  the  whole.  And  since  an 
architect  alone  can  calculate  it,  the  owner 
must  content  himself  with  pointing  out  some 
neighboring  roof  which  he  admires  or  in 
showing  a  pictured  roof  which  he  wants 
copied.  Let  me  advise  him  to  stand  by 
and  watch  this  part,  however  he  may  neg- 
lect other  parts  of  the  work,  for  workmen, 
left  to  themselves,  will  put  up  the  kind  that 
is  the  least  troublesome,  and,  in  nine  cases 
cases  out  of  ten,  the  result  is  the  hideous 
''sugar  loaf"  effect.  A  mistake  in  paint 
may  be  remedied,  but  a  roof  is  as  unaltera- 
ble as  a  Medio-Persian  decree. 

In  drawing  their  plans  amateurs  see  things 
just  as  they  want  them,  making  arbitrary 
proportions,  not  calculating  the  width  of 
doors  and  windows,  not  allowing  for  the 
necessary  thickness  of  walls,  and,  above  all, 
not  leaving  sufficient  space  fo.  staircases 
and  chimneys. 

In  order  to  proportion  a  plan  so  that  it 
will  work  out  as  fondly  expected,  one  must 
calculate  according  to  a  scale,  using  inches 
or  fractions  of  inches  to  stand  for  feet.    This 


is  easily  done,  if  the  eighihs  on  the  yard 
stick  are  counted  as  feet.  One  wise  woman 
I  knew  was  so  determined  to  be  accurate 
that  she  used  an  inch  measure  for  every 
foot,  thus  making  her  drawing  30-inches 
square  for  a  thirty-foot  house.  She  had 
measured  her  doors,  windows,  even  her 
beds  and  large  pieces  of  furniture  so  that 
she  knew  exactly  what  space  each  would 
require.  She  had  figured  out  the  depth  of 
the  closets  and  which  way  each  door  should 
swing.  The  contractor  was  surprised  and 
delighted  with  her  drawings,  and,  best  of  all, 
she  had  a  correct  picture  of  the  completed 
house  in  mind  so  that  there  was  no  disap- 
pointment in  store. 

As  the  roof  baffles  the  amateur  builder, 
on  the  outside,  so  does  the  spacing  of  the 
stairway,  within.  Only  a  skilled  workman 
can  tell  what  room  it  will  take,  as  the  height 
of  the  ceihng,  the  width  and  height  of  the 
steps,  and  also  the  landings  must  be  taken 
into  account.  One  double  stairway,  a  marvel 
of  convenience  and  the  housewife's  long- 
cherished  plan,  was  hopelessly  wrecked, 
when  it  came  to  putting  it  into  wood. 
Doubtless  many  others,  beautiful  upon 
paper,  ' '  gang  agley ' '  for  lack  of  space. 

The  strolls  about  town  for  a  lesson  in  up- 
to-date  architecture  will  also  show  the  pro- 
spective builder  that  wide  eaves  and  wide, 
home-like  verandas  are  not  simply  styHsh, 
but  greatly  to  be  desired.  He  will  learn 
that  there  are  fashions  in  house-painting 
as  well  as  fashions  in  chimneys  and  porch 
railings. 

The  experienced  housekeeper  will  plead 
for  a  swinging  door  between  the  kitchen 
and  dining  room,  and  for  slightly  sloping 
floors  upon  all  porches  so  that  they  may  be 
easily  mopped  or  washed  clean  simply  by 
using  the  hose.  She  will  beg  for  all  window 
casings,  door  frames  and  baseboards  to  have 
rounded  edges,  and  for  light  oak  staining 
upon  floors  instead  of  the  dark  finish  that 


313 


314 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


shows  dust  and  damage  so  quickly.  Many 
of  these  things  do  not  add  a  penny  to  the 
cost,  and  it  is  just  such  little  things  as  these 
that  help  or  hinder  the  daily  round  of 
housework. 

Workmen  tell  me  that  an  economy  in 
lumber  may  be  had  by  planning  for  a  ten 
and  a  half  foot  ceiling  down  stairs  and  a 
nine  foot  ceiling  above,  as  the  timbers  ordi- 
narily used  in  the  framework  are  just  twenty 
feet  in  length  and  therefore  neither  splicing 
nor  cutting  is  required. 

Before  making  any  plan  whatsoever,  the 
lot,  its  size,  shape,  position,  elevation  and 
surroundings  must  be  studied.  The  house 
that  "  fits  "  one  spot  will  not  be  at  all  suit- 
able upon  another.  ' '  What  rooms  need  the 
sun  most  ?  What  views  are  most  desirable  ? 
What  rooms  will  be  darkened  by  trees  or 


neighboring  houses  ? ' '  asks  the  wise  lot- 
owner.  The  comfort  of  living  in  a  house 
depends  upon  these  very  things,  which  too 
often  are  neglected.  It  is  only  after  one 
has  built  that  he  fully  realizes  the  import- 
ance of  details  and  the  necessity  of  a  care- 
fully and  correctly  proportioned  plan.  Our 
towns  and  cities  are  full  of  "eye-sores," 
because  of  a  lack  of  these,,  and  because  so 
many  houses  are  built  to  be  rented,  not  to 
be  lived  in. 

To  put  the  whole  responsibility  upon  an 
architect  may  and  probably  will  result  in  a 
well-built  house,  yet  by  so  doing  one  loses 
one  of  the  greatest  joys  that  ever  comes  to 
us.  Planning  a  home  is  every  bit  as  good 
as  the  actual  moving  into  it.  Words  can- 
not tell  about  it,  but  every  one  who  has 
done  this  knows  the  thrill  that  goes  with  it. 


Diet  in  Childhood,  Sickness,  and  Old  Age 

By  MAR  Y  D.   CHAMBERS 

Eighth  Paper 
Conditions  Favoring  the  Easy  Assimilation  of  Food 


THE  second  great  problem,  in  diet 
for  the  sick,  is  that  it  be  easily  as- 
similated. It  used  to  be  frequently 
said  that  sick  people  were  overfed;  we  are 
now  inclined  to  say  they  are  underfed,  and, 
curiously  enough,  both  of  these  contradic- 
tory statements  may  be  true.  As  discussed 
in  the  last  paper,  a  patient  may  be  underfed 

—  so  as  to  deplete  dangerously  the  resisting 
power  of  the  organism,  thus  inviting  collapse 

—  who  does  not  receive  food  enough  to 
yield  two  thousand  calories  of  energy  daily 
during  a  protracted  illness  or  convalescence. 
On  the  other  hand,  a  diet  of  the  required 
proteid  or  calorific  value  may  be  adminis- 
tered in  such  a  form  as  to  be  not  only  un- 
available, but  positively  injurious,  taxing 
the  enfeebled  body  to  get  rid  of  something 


it  cannot  use,  or  developing  injurious  or 
poisonous  products  of  fermentation  from  too 
long  retention  in  the  system  unable  to  utilize 
it.  Thus,  at  one  and  the  same  time,  a  pa- 
tient may  be  overfed,  while  being  starved 
to  death,  and  the  problem  of  administering 
food  that  shall  be  assimilated  is  much  more 
complex  than  that  of  administering  food 
that  shall  merely  fulfill  the  requisite  of  being 
sufficient  in' amount. 

To  be  easily  assimilated,  then,  a  food 
must  fulfill  the  following  requirements: 

7.  The  food  must  be  digestible.  This 
word,  "digestible,"  covers  three  condi- 
tions: the  time  consumed  in  the  process 
of  digestion;  the  ease  with  which  the  pro- 
cess is  accomplished;  and  the  completeness 
of  the  change,  or  transformation  of  the  food 


DIET  IN  CHILDHOOD,   SICKNESS  AND  OLD  AGE 


315 


into  a  fluid  condition  and  one  that  is  easily- 
dealt  with  by  the  absorbent  vessels.  Thus, 
milk,  for  the  average  adult,  demands  three 
or  four  hours  for  complete  digestion,  but 
gives  no  trouble  during  the  process,  and 
leaves  comparatively  little  residue.  Hard- 
boiled  white  of  egg  demands  a'  long  time, 
often  gives  trouble  during  the  process,  but, 
in  health,  at  least,  leaves  little  or  no  resi- 
due. Sweet  corn  has  its  pulp,  rather  easily 
and  quickly  digested,  but  leaves  a  large  and 
often  troublesome  residue  of  cellulose,  often 
intolerable  in  health,  unquestionably  so  in 
illness.  Rice,  properly  cooked,  is  quickly 
and  easily  digested  and  leaves  no  residue  to 
speak  of.  Raw  cabbage,  well  masticated  by 
a  healthy  adult,  is  rather  quickly  digested, 
quite  easily  dealt  with  by  the  internal  ma- 
chinery, and  is  guiltless  of  any  troublesome 
residue.  Cooked  cabbage  takes  a  long  time 
to  be  digested,  frequently  gives  trouble  dur- 
ing the  process,  and  its  residue  of  cellulose 
seems  to  produce  excessively  unwholesome 
fermentation  products.  This  is  one  of  the 
mysteries  of  dieietics  —  unnoticed,  and  un- 
solved, by  those  who  had  scientific  training 
and  abiUty  to  deal  with  it,  but  who,  like 
children,  have  been  charmed  by  the  foreign 
and  far-off — and  have  studied  the  solar 
spectrum  rather  than  the  ' '  narrow  things  of 
the  house. "  It  may  be  that  cooked  cab- 
bage escapes  mastication, —  it  may  be  that 
the  nitrogenous  extractives,  which  it  is 
known  in  some  obscure  way  stimulate  the 
trophic  centres,  are  dissolved  out  in  the 
process  of  cooking, —  it  may  be  that  both 
factors  aid  in  decreasing  its  digestibility 
when  cooked,  — the  fact  remains. 

To  return,  however,  to  food  that  is  ' '  di- 
gestible, ' '  when  the  term  is  applied  to  food 
for  the  sick,  it  will  be  easily  seen  that  such 
food  must  fulfill  all  three  conditions;  it  must 
not  only  be  quickly  and  easily  digested,  but 
the  10  percent,  or  15  percent,  of  indi- 
gestible material,  cellulose,  etc.,  which  is 
present,  and  with  benefit,  in  the  diet  of  the 
well  person,  must  be  absent  from  the  diet  of 
the  sick,  whose  powers  of  digestion  are 
enfeebled,  diverged  from  normal  use,  and 
should  therefore  be  taxed  as  little  as  pos- 
sible. 


2.  The  food  must  be  palatable.  ' '  Food 
that  is  relished  is  half  digested, ' '  said  the 
Father  of  Medicine.  The  result  of  later 
experiments,  notably  those  described  in 
Bulletin  2 1  of  the  Department  of  Agricul- 
ture, ''  Methods  and  Results  of  Investiga- 
tions on  the  Chemistry  and  Economy  of 
Food,"  p.  77,  appear  to  indicate  that 
wholesome  food,  although  quite  unappetiz- 
ing, is  apparently  as  completely  digested  as 
food  that  is  relished,  provided  the  will  be 
strong  enough  for  perseverance  in  eating  it. 
Then  came  Pawlow'  s  famous  experiments 
resulting  in  the  discovery  of  ''appetite- 
juice,"  and  appearing  to  indicate  that  the 
nerves  of  taste,  when  pleasurably  excited, 
convey  stimulus  to  the  stomach  through  the 
sympathetic  system.  In  summarizing,  it 
may  be  said  that  the  nerves  are  largely  de- 
pendent on  the  will,  and  the  will  is  a  very 
curious  thing,  whose  powers  most  of  us  have 
never  measured  —  and  the  period  of  sick- 
ness is  not  the  time  to  put  them  to  the  test. 
Let  us  then  try  to  administer  appetizing 
food,  such  as  will  be  relished  and  enjoyed. 
The  likes  and  dislikes  of  the  patient  should 
be  carefully  studied,  whims  are  often  better 
humored  than  combated,  and  *  •  cravings  ' ' 
are  frequently  an  indication  that  certain 
foods  are  needed  by  the  organism. 

J.  The  food  must  be  administered  i?t 
proper  quantity.  Assurance  of  what  is  the 
"proper  quantity"  is  the  result  only  of 
painstaking  observation  and  trained  judg- 
ment on  the  part  of  the  nurse.  ''  Proper 
quantity ' '  means  not  less  than  the  organism 
can  assimilate,  yet  not  in  excess  of  the 
amount  that  can  be  acted  upon  by  the  en- 
feebled digestive  secretions.  Excess  of  even 
a  highly  digestible  food  results  not  only  in 
overtaxing  the  organs,  but  in  fermentation 
of  the  undigested  and  unassimilable  residue, 
which  irritates  the  stomach  and  intestines, 
and,  if  prolonged,  may  give  rise  to  the  form- 
ation of  toxic  products,  which  are  more  or 
less  absorbed  into  the  blood. 

4..  The  food  must  be  administered  at 
proper  times.  All  natural  processes  are 
rhythmic  —  not  in  sickness  or  health,  not 
in  childhood,  maturity,  nor  old  age,  do  we 
ever  progress  in  straight  lines,  —  each  day. 


3i6 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


even,  has  its  ebb  and  flow,  its  periods  of 
exaltation  and  depression  of  vitality,  its 
times  to  work  and  its  times  to  rest.  Even 
in  health  many  persons  are  sensible  of  a 
period  of  low  vitality  about  four  o'clock  in 
the  afternoon,  and  anyone  who  has  done 
much  night- watching  of  the  sick  has  learned 
to  dread  the  hour,  generally  about  two  in 
the  morning,  when  the  hold  on  life  seems 
feeblest.  These  hours,  and  others,  of  al- 
ternate ebb  and  flow  of  vital  force,  are  more 
evident  in  sickness  than  in  health,  and 
should  be  carefully  watched  for  by  the  nurse, 
so  that  food,  or  the  more  nourishing  por- 
tion of  the  food,  may  be  given  at  the  times 
when  the  ability  to  digest  and  assimilate  is 
greatest.  The  same  care  should  be  observed 
in  taking  advantage  of  remissions  in  the 
sickness,  for  example,  those  in  "remitting 
fevers,"  when  by  proper  nourishment  the 
patient  may  be  armed  against  the  next  at- 
tack. "Proper  times"  also  mean  more 
frequently  than  in  health,  because  the 
systemi,  enfeebled  by  disease,  cannot  as- 
similate so  much  at  a  time. 

J".      The  food  should  be  administered  in 


suitable  form.  In  acute  disease,  "suitable 
form"  usually  means  in  liquid  or  fluid 
form,  both  to  favor  speedy  absorption  and 
to  reheve  the  patient  from  the  expenditure 
of  energy  necessary  for  mastication.  More- 
over, starchy  food  in  fluid  form,  such  as 
gruels,  must  be  sufticiently  cooked  to  trans- 
form, or  hydrolize  the  starch,  to  an  extent 
equivalent  to  that  which  would  be  produced 
by  thorough  insalivation.  This  is  merely 
compensation  for  the  lack  of  insalivation 
attendant  on  sipping  or  swallowing  a  gruel 
—  or  for  the  enfeebled  power  of  the  saliva 
itself,  usually  to  be  observed  in  sickness. 
The  "suitable  form"  of  food  in  various 
conditions  of  illness  —  such  for  example,  as 
typhoid  fever,  where  the  food  must  be  of 
such  a  nature  as  to  leave  no  irritating  resi- 
due in  the  intestine;  or  in  illnesses  where 
rectal  feeding  is  indicated,  and  where  it 
must  be  remembered  that  the  rectum  is 
better  quaHfied  to  absorb  than  to  digest  — 
will  be  dwelt  upon  in  some  detail  in  the 
next  paper,  where  the  diseases  which  are 
amenable  to  diet  rather  than  to  medication 
will  be  considered. 


A  Valentine 

By  A.   T.  FROST 

A  white,  white  rose  in  a  garden  close, 

With  shy  sweet  petals  curled. 
From  the  crowd  apart,  with  a  fragrant  heart, 

The  rarest  in  the  world. 


Through  Winter's  snow,  and  Summer's  glow, 

Still  blooms  this  rose  of  mine, 
And  I  say  she  knows,  does  this  white,  white  rose, 

That  she  is  my  valentine. 


Housekeeping  in  Egypt 


By  EMILY  WILLIAMS 


WHEN  one  speaks  of  the  native 
Egyptians,  one  means  only  the 
common  people.  The  ruling 
classes,  the  aristocrats,  the  millionaires  are 
all  foreigners,  and  their  customs  are  as 
varied  as  their  nationalities. 

The  life  of  the  peasants  is  interesting  to 
us  first,  because  of  its  resemblance  to  ours, 
and  secondly,  because  of  its  difference  from 
ours.  The  houses  are  built  of  mud  bricks, 
the  floors  are  of  stone,  the  ceilings  of 
beams,  the  walls  of  plaster.  The  windows 
on  the  first  floor  are  so  high  that  no  one 
can  see  in,  on  the  other  hand  neither  can 
anyone  see  out.  These  windows  are  un- 
softened  by  screens,  blinds,  shades  or  cur- 
tains. The  walls  are  bare.  There  is  no 
furniture.  The  floor  is  strewn  with  the 
straw  mats  that  serve  as  chairs.  There 
are  neither  bedsteads  nor  tables.  Some- 
times the  people  sleep  on  a  mattress  which 
is  spread  on  the  floor  by  night  and  rolled 
up  in  a  corner  by  day,  sometimes  in  a  sort 
of  cradle  made  of  palm  boughs,  which 
raises  them  a  little  out  of  the  direct  path  of 
the  scorpions. 

At  one  end  of  the  kitchen  is  a  brick 
platform  in  which  are  set  iron  grates,  about 
six  inches  square,  and  of  the  same  depth, 
the  number  varying  with  the  size  and 
wealth  of  the  family.  In  these  the  fire  of 
twigs  and  charcoal  is  kindled,  and  over 
them  the  kettles  are  placed.  The  food 
has  to  be  boiled  or  stewed,  never  roasted, 
for  there  are  no  ovens. 

There  is  no  set  time  for  meals  in  an 
Egyptian  home.  When  the  housewife  has 
the  food  ready  and  the  majority  of  the 
family  are  hungry,  they  fall  to  without  any 
ceremony,  the  men  eating  first,  and  the 
women  contenting  themselves  with  what  is 
left.  As  soon  as  an  Egyptian  awakes  in 
the  morning,  he  wishes  his  cup  of  black 
cofl'ee.  They  use  there  pure  Arabic  coffee, 
roasted  from  day  to    day,   and    pounded 


very  fine  in  a  mortar.  The  coffee  pot  is 
fifled  three  fourths  full  of  hot  water,  the 
powdered  coffee  and  sugar  are  added,  and 
the  mixture  is  aUowed  to  boil  up  three 
times  and  is  served  at  once.  Coffee  is 
drunk  after  every  meal. 

The  first  real  meal  of  the  day  is  about 
ten  o'  clock,  and  usually  consists  of ' '  fool ' ' 
beans,   onions  and  bread.      The  origin  of 
the  name  for  these  beans  is  explained  by 
a   most  amusing  legend.     Years   ago  the 
Egyptians  ate  almonds  instead  of  beans, 
and  in  those  days  they  were  a  clever  and 
brilliant  nation.    A  certain  Kedive,  finding 
them  too  wise  to  be  easily  controlled  by 
him,   changed   their  diet    to    the   present 
beans,  and  they  have  been  dull  and  stupid 
ever  since.     Still  another  ancient  tale  sets 
forth  the  evil  results  of  eating  these  beans. 
One  day  a  man  was  driving  ten  donkeys 
to  market.      Growing  tired  of  walking,  he 
mounted  one  and  rode  comfortably  along 
the  dusty  road.      In  his  idleness  he  began 
to  count  his  drove.       i,  2,  3,  4,  5,  6,  7, 
8,  9,  he  reckoned,  but  what  had  become  of 
the  tenth  ?     He  quite  forgot  the  one  he 
was  riding  on.      Much  worried  he  counted 
again  and  again  and  found  only  nine.      In 
great  excitement  he  sprang  from  his  don- 
key and  counted,   there  were  surely  ten. 
With  a  sigh  of  rehef  he    mounted    once 
more,    and  then   counted,    only  nine .  .  , 
He  never   discovered    where  the  missing 
animal    had    gone.       In    spite    of    these 
warnings    the    average  citizen   eats  beans 
every  day,   and,   strange  to  say,  they  are 
c5oked  as  we  cook  ours.      Onions  are  al- 
ways eaten  with  them.      Not  only  are  the 
Egyptians  exceedingly  fond  of  onions,  but 
they  consider  them  most  healthful.     They 
dip  them  in  salt,   and  munch  them  as  we 
would  an  apple. 

The     Egyptians  have    excellent    teeth, 
which  is  said  to   be   due  to  the   bran  in
