EDUCATIONAL   REFORM 

MISS  FRANCES  LOW,  writing  in 
The  Daily  Chronicle,  says:  "Un- 
til the  whole  education  of  girls  — 
I  care  not  what  their  social  rank  —  be  re- 
formed, so  that  the  crafts  of  the  home  be- 
come part  of  every  girl's  education,  we 
shall  have  not  only  our  servant  problem, 
but  inefficient  homes,  inefficient  women- 
citizens,  failing  in  the  one  great  national 
service  which  they,  and  they  alone,  can 
contribute,  and  an  inferior  environment  for 
the  coming  race.  If  we  ever  attain  to  any 
sort  of  full,  complete  education  for  girls, 
how  infinitely  absurd  our  present  system 
will  seem,  not  only  of  one  class,  but  of 
all  classes. 


THOUGHT  AND  HEALTH 


;HE  bishop  of  the  Reformed  Epis- 
copal Church  of  Chicago,  the  Rev. 
Mr.  Fallows,  announces  that  here- 
after he  will  add  to  former  conventional 
methods  of  dealing  with  the  weak,  ill  and 
sorrowing,those  ways  and  means  which  seem 
to  have  been  common  in  the  earliest  days  of 
the  Christian  faith,  and  which  Dr.  Elwood 
Worcester  of  the  Emmanuel  Church  of  this 
city,  and  physicians  co-operating  with  him, 
have  been  using  with  benefit  to  many  who 
have  sought  aid.  Both  the  multiplication 
of  books  on  this  subject  and  the  frequency 
with  which  clergymen  are  announcing  their 
willingness  to  experiment  with  the  method 
indicate  that  the  movement  has  passed  be- 
yond the  stage  where  it  can  be  viewed  with 
cynical  scepticism. ' ' 

The  foregoing  is  one  of  many  instances 
that  indicate  the  rise  and  growth  of  a  new 
movement  in  matters  of  belief  and  practice. 
Whatever  may  be  said,  pro  or  con,  of 
Christian  Science,  Modern  Psychology  and 
the  New  Thought,  certainly  the  tenets  of  all 
these  are  modifying  the  teaching  and  the 
preaching  of  all  sects.  That  cheerfulness 
is  a  potent  remedy  in  bodily  disorders  is  not, 
perhaps,  a  new  idea,  but  the  systematic  cul- 
tivation of  a  spirit  of  cheerfulness  seems  to 
be  rapidly  gaining  prominence  in  the  atten- 
tion of  large  numbers. 

The  influence  of  thought  —  suggestive 
thought  —  on  the  physical  affections  is  be- 
coming a  matter  of  far-reaching  significance. 
Healing  by  thought  is  no  longer  uncommon 
in  practice.  Many  a  sufferer  seems  a  fit 
subject  rather  foj-  the  mental  healer  than  for 
the  dispenser  of  drugs  and  tonics.  The 
books  on  this  subject,  produced  by  trained 
psychologists,  are  increasing.  By  these  not 
only  the  effects  of  thought  on  health  are 
admitted,  but  the  solution  between  cause 
and  effect  is  scientifically  explained. 

Health  is  the  thing  of  first  import.  The 
intimate  relation  that  exists  between  our 
states  of  health  and  our  ways  of  thinking 
is  worthy  of  a  better  understanding.  A 
germ  of  truth  lies  in  the  old  saying,  ''We 
are  able,  because  we  think  we  are  able. ' ' 


EDITORIALS 


319 


FLETCHERISM 

OF  the  many  books  on  dietetics  that 
have  appeared  of  late,  by  far  the 
most  important  is  the  A.  B.-Z.  of 
our  own  nutrition  by  Horace  Fletcher. 
The  author  is  not  a  vegetarian,'  though  he 
may  disclose  a  tendency  in  that  direction. 
He  does  not  recommend  any  particular 
form  or  kind  of  diet.  In  the  selection  of 
food,  appetite  is  made  sole  guide  and  in- 
structor. The  manner  in  which  our  food 
is  partaken  and  -ingested,  we  are  taught;  is 
the  sine  qua  non  of  proper  or  improper 
feeding. 

Over  eating  is  the  source  of  most  of  our 
bodily  disorders.  The  ill  effects  of  undi- 
gested, unassimilated  waste  products  in  the 
system  are  untold.  The  inference  is  rea- 
sonable, and  experience  and  facts  substan- 
tiate the  same.  Now,  to  avoid  over  eating, 
here  is  the  simple  remedy.  Our  food 
must  be  finely  divided,  thoroughly  insah- 
vated  and  masticated,  before  it  is  taken 
into  the  stomach.  <■ '  Sip  and  taste  all  hquids 
as  the  wine-tasters  do;  and  in  a  very  short 
time,  sipping  and  tasting  liquids  and  mas- 
ticating and  tasting  food  for  all  they  are 
worth  will  become  an  agreeable  and  profit- 
able fixed  habit.  Eat  all  you  crave,  but 
do  not  eat  more  than  this  simply  because 
you  have  been  in  the  habit  of  doing  so.  See 
to  it  that  each  morsel  put  into  your  mouth 
is  thoroughly  masticnted  and  mixed  with 
the  saliva  before  going  down  into  the 
stomach,  which  is  not  equipped  to  per- 
form the  work  which  the  teeth  and  salivary 
g-ands  were  given  you  for.  The  stomach 
will  struggle  bravely  to  overcome  the  abuse 
which  you  heap  upon  it,  but  in  spite  of  all 
it  can  do  to  manage  hastily  chewed  food, 
undigested  portions  remain,  which  clog  the 
intestines  and  interfere  with  the  healthy 
conditions  which  nature  intended. 

The  appetite  is  given  as  an  indicator  of 
what  the  body  requires.  The  appetite  will 
close  the  valve  when  you  have  eaten  enough, 
if  you  will  give  it  a  fair  chance.  Food  is  in- 
tended to  be  eaten  that  nourishment  may 
be  gained  from  it,  and  when  you  only  gain 
a  part  of  the  nourishment,  you  prostitute 


your  stomach,  and  take  tremendous  risks 
of  germ  diseases  in  your  body.  P^conomic 
nutrition  is  not  a  joke,  is  not  a  fad;  it  is 
solely  an  appeal  to  self-examination  and 
self-instruction  in  the  most  vital  question 
of  all  the  world,  since  upon  perfect  nutri- 
tion depends  not  only  health,  but  strength, 
mental  acuteness,  moral  tendencies,  at- 
tractability  to  others,  happiness,  and,  in 
fact,  life  itself ' ' 


KIPLING  AS   A    PREACHER 

[Frorn  the  Boston  Herald) 

WHEN  Rudyard  KipHng  passed 
through  Canada,  recently,  he 
was  impressed  into  addressing 
the  students  of  McGill  College,  and  it  is 
interesting  to  note  the  sort  of  advice  he 
gave.  Evidently  he  saw  signs  in  the  Do- 
minion that  accumulation  of  wealth  is  com- 
ing to  figure  too  much  in  the  thought  of 
its  residents,  for  his  first  exhortation  was 
against  overvaluation  of  wealth.  His  sec- 
ond was  to  avoid,  if  possible,  the  pessi- 
mism of  youth  and  excessive  subjectiveness; 
and  his  last  appeal  was,  above  all  things, 
to  avoid  being  known  as  ''smart."  In- 
deed, his  advice  was  that,  whenever  the 
McGill  men  found  any  of  their  "dear  lit- 
tle playmates  showing  signs  of  smartness  in 
their  work,  talk  or  play,"  they  should  take 
them  "tenderly  by  the  hand,  by  both 
hands,  by  the  back  of  the  neck,  if  neces- 
.sary,  and  lovingly,  playfully,  but  firmly  lead 
them  to  a  knowledge  of  higher  and  more 
interesting  things. "  "  The  sins  of  youth, ' ' 
he  said,  "are  due  largely  to  its  virtues.  Its 
arrogance  is  very  often  the  result  of  its  in- 
nate shyness.  Its  brutahty  is  the  outcome 
of  its  natural  virginity  of  spirit. ' '  By  such 
touches  as  these  the  artist  in  English  was 
revealed,  but  the  address,  on  the  whole, 
was  a  sensible  warning  against  materialism 
and  was  an  exaltation  of  the  man  of  ideals 
and  ideas  above  the  man  of  skill  in  amass- 
ing wealth.  Kiphng  expected  his  hearers, 
at  first,  to  be  dazzled  by  the  man  of  money, 
but  he  assured  them  they  would  presently 
find  out,  "  that  money  dominates  every- 
body except  the  man  who  does  not  want 


320 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


money.  You  may  meet  that  man  on 
your  farm,  in  your  village,  or  in  )'-ou:  Leg- 
islature. But  be  sure  that,  whenever  or 
wherever  you  meet  him,  as  soon  as  it  comes 
to  a  direct  issue  between  you,  his  little  fin- 
ger will  be  thicker  than  your  loins.  You 
will  go  in  fear  of  him;  he  will  not  go  in 
fear  of  you.  You  will  do  what  he  wants; 
he  will  not  do  what  you  want.  You  will 
find  that  you  have  no  weapon  in  your  ar- 
mory with  which  you  can  attack  him,  no 
argument  with  which  you  can  appeal  to 
him.  Whatever  you  gain,  he  will  gain 
more." 

It  is  "wealth  for  wealth's  sake"  that 
Kipling  warns  against.  Acquire  it  with  the 
left  hand,  ' '  but  keep  your  right  for  your 
proper  work  in  life.  If  you  employ  both 
arms  in  that  game,  you  will  be  in  danger 
of  stooping,  in  danger,  also,  of  losing  your 
soul. ' '      Wise  words  well  spoken. 


IT  is  now  proposed  by  the  scientists,  as 
the  next  great  possibility,  to  estabhsh  a 
new  department  of  government,  a  Na- 
tional Bureau  of  Health.  The  plan  is  to  place 
in  control  of  this  bureau  such  problems  as 
quarantine,  food  supply  and  the  dissemin- 
ation of  educational  instruction  on  health. 
"  Experts  have  shown  that  the  death  rate 
in  the  United  States  could  be  reduced  one- 
half,  if  living  conditions  were  what  they 
should  be. ' '  Could  any  department  of  gov- 
ernment be  more  reasonable,  humane,  or 
beneficial  to  the  masses  than  a  Bureau  of 
Health  ? 

WHILE  the  State  Department  is 
actively  engaged  in  many  ways 
and  many  directions  in  striving 
to  pry  open  and  hold  open  the  doors  for 
the  entrance  of  American  commerce  and  to 
make  its  progress  and  extension  easy,  I  do 
not  believe  in  too  much  government.  The 
limitations  on  what  government  effort  can 
do  and  ought  to  do  are  very  clear.  The 
real  work  must  be  done  by  the  business 
men  of  the  United  States  themselves.  The 
great  things  in  this  country  have  been  ac- 
complished by  the  association  of  individ- 
ual private  enterprise.  —  Elihu  Root. 


THERE  is  a  democratic  soul  of  truth 
in  every  stubborn  aristocratic  preju- 
dice. ''The  hireling  fleeth  be- 
cause he  is  an  hireling  and  careth  not;  but 
the  Good  Shepherd  lays  down  his  life  for 
his  sheep. ' '  And  the  cogency  of  this  prin- 
ciple loses  nothing  by  lapse  of  time;  the 
man  that  wjrks  because  he  wants  to  is 
bound  to  have  a  better  earth-grip  than  the 
fellow  who  takes  hold  because  he  wants  to 
let  go.  There  is  n'  t  a  ghost  of  a  show  for 
the  Hired  Man,  when  once  the  Master  of 
the  House  shall  have  decided  to  make  an 
issue.  —  Charles  Fergusofi. 


"  It  is  a  greater  disgrace  to  be  sick  than 
to  be  in  the  penitentiary.  If  you  are  in 
the  pen,  it  is  a  sign  you  have  broken  some 
man-made  statute,  but  if  you  are  sick,  it  is 
a  sign  that  you  have  broken  some  God- 
made  law. ' '

Nut,  Fig-and-Date  Pudding,  Decorated  with  Whipped  Cream  and  wSlices  of  Fig 


Seasonable  Recipes 

By  JAXET  J/.  HILL 

IN  all  recipes  where  flour  is  used,  unless  otherwise  stated,  the  flour  is  measured  after  sifting  once. 
^Yhen  flour  is  measured  by  cups,  the  cup  is  filled  with  a  spoon,  and  a  level  cupful  is  meant.     A 
tablespoonful  or  a  teaspoonful  of  any  designated  material  is  a  level  spoonful  of  such  material. 


Scallop   Cocktail 

Buy  small  scallops,  allowing  six  for  each 
service.  Pour  a  cup  of  cold  water  over  the 
scallops,  rinse  thoroughly  and  drain  on  a 
soft  cloth.  For  forty-eight  scallops  mix  a 
cup  and  a  fourth  of  tomato  catsup,  the  juice 
of  three  lemons,  three  or  four  tablespoonfuls 
of  horse-radish,  a  scant  half  a  teaspoonful  of 
salt  and  four  tablespoonfuls  of  AVorcester- 
shire  sauce^ 

Scallops  and   Bacon 

Pour  cold  water  over  the  scallops,  rinse 
and  remove  from  the  water;  set  over  the 
fire  and  let  heat  quickly  to  the  boiling  point, 
then  drain  and  roll  in  fine  cracker  crumbs. 
Cut  thin  slices  of  bacon  into  pieces  about 
twice  the  length  of  the  scallops.  Dispose 
the  bacon  and  scallops,  alternately,  on 
wire  skewers,  and  set  to  cook  in  a  hot 
frying  pan.  When  the  bacon  is  cooked, 
the  scallops  will  be  cooked.  Let  drain  on 
soft  paper.  Serve  the  skewers  —  one  to 
each  at  table  —  on  slices  of  hot  toast. 


Cream-of-Celery  Soup  for  Ten 

Plates 

Cut  in  small  pieces  the  leaves  and  outer, 
green  stalks  of  celery  enough  to  make  three 
pints;  cover  with  boiling  water,  heat  the 
whole  to  the  boiling  point  and  let  cook  ten 
minutes;  drain  oif  the  water,  add  two  quarts 
of  fresh  water,  half  an  onion  and  a  few  slices 
of  carrot,  if  at  hand,  and  cook  until  the 
celery  is  tender.  Press  as  much  of  the  pulp 
as  possible  througti  the  sieve,  the  fibrous 
portions  will  be  left  in  the  sieve.  Scald 
five  cups  of  milk  with  the  other  half  of  the 
onion.  Melt  one-fourth  a  cup  of  butter; 
in  it  cook  half  a  cup  of  flour,  two  teaspoon- 
fuls  of  salt  and  half  a  teaspoonful  of  pepper; 
stir  and  cook  until  frothy,  then  add  a  quart 
or  more  of  the  puree  (celery)  cooled  for 
the  purpose;  cook  and  stir  vigorously  until 
smooth,  then  add  the  rest  of  the  puree, 
(made  hot  beforehand^  and  stir  until  boil- 
ing; strain  in  the  hot  milk;  add  also  a  cuj) 
of  cream  if  convenient  and  more  salt  and 
pepper  if  needed.     This  soup  is  particularly 


322 


THE  BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


good,  when  less  water  is  used  in  cooking  the 
celery,  and  the  equivalent  ot  chicken  broth 


Bouillon  Cup,  with  Cover 

is  added  to  the  puree  after  it  is  strained. 

Cream-of-Chicken  Soup  for    Ten 

Plates 

Let  two  quarts  of  chicken  broth  (the 
better  and  richer  the  broth  the  better  the 
soup)  with  two  or  three  stalks  of  celery,  a 
few  slices  of  carrot,  and  half  an  onion  sim- 
mer twenty  minutes.  If  the  soup  is  to  be 
made  from  the  framework  and  trimmings  of 
roast  fowls,  discard  all  stuffing,  cover  the 
whole  with  cold  water  and  let  simmer  an 
hour,  then  add  the  vegetables,  simmer  fifteen 
minutes  and  strain.  Melt  one- third  a  cup 
of  butter;  cook  in  this  half  a  cup  of  flour, 
a  teaspoonful  of  salt  and  a  dash  of  pepper; 
add  five  cups  of  milk  and  stir  until  smooth 
and  boiling  (to  save  time  scald  three  cups 
of  the  milk  and  add,  after  the  cooked  flour 
and  butter  have  been  smoothly  blended  and 
brought  to  the  boihng  point  with  two  cups 
of  cold  milkj.      When  all  the  milk  has  been 


Corned  Beef- Hash,  with  Poached  Eggs  for  Two 


added  and  the  w^hole  is  smooth  and  boiling, 
add  the  hot  broth  and  strain  if  needed. 
More  salt   will   be  needed.      The  beaten 


yolks  of  two  or  three  eggs  mixed  with  a  cup 
of  cream  improve  the  soup  wonderfully. 
Do  not  let  the  soup  boil,  after  the  egg 
mixture  has  been  stirred  into  it. 

Finnan    Haddle  a  la  .  Newburgh 

Cover  a  choice  finnan  haddie  with  luke- 
warm water;  heat  the  water  very  gradually 
to  the  boiling  point,  then  let  simmer  fifteen 
minutes,  remove  from  the  water  and  separ- 
ate the  flesh  into  flakes.  There  should 
be  one  pint  of  the  fish.  Melt  four  level  table- 
spoonfuls  of  butter;  in  this  cook  half  a  table- 
spoonful  of  cornstarch,  half  a  teaspoonful 
of  salt  and  one-fourth  a  teaspoonful  of 
paprika,  or  a  smaller  measure  of  cayenne; 
add  one  cup  of  cream,  and  stir  until  the 
sauce  boils,  then  add  the  fish;  stir  and  cook 
until  the  fish  is  hot,  then  add  the  yolks  of 
two  eggs,  beaten  slightly,  and  when  the  egg 
thickens  (do  not  allow  the  sauce  to  boil  or 
it  will  curdle),  stir  in  one-fourth  a  cup  of 
sherry  wine. 

Corned   Forequarter  of  Lamb 

Have  your  dealer  put  a  fresh  shoulder  of 
yearhng  lamb  into  the  corn  beef  brine  to 
remain  over  night,  or  twenty-four  hours. 
When  brought  from  the  market  wash  thor- 
oughly, then  set  to  cook  covered  with  cold 
water;  let  the  water  heat  gradually  to  the 
boiling  point,  then  skim  and  continue  the 
cooking  at  a  gentle  simmer.  Cook  five  or 
six  hours  or  until  tender.  Cook  in  another 
saucepan  some  nicely  pared  turnips.  Cut 
in  slices,  the  turnips  will  require  from  one 
to  two  hours  to  cook. 

Corned  Beef  or  Lamb

Chop,  fine,  cold  meat  and 
cold,  boiled  potatoes.     The

which  the  meat  was  cooked  in  case  of  beef

—  then  put  in  the  chopped  material  and 
a  few  spoonfuls  of  hot  water;  stir  and  mix

and  let  cook  until  hghtly  set.  Turn  the

eggs  above  the  hash. 

Hashed   Lamb,  with   Rice  and 
Peas 

Carefully  remove  all  unedible  portions 
from  a  quantity  of  cold  meat,  then  chop 
the  meat  fine.  To  the  meat  add  a  little 
broth  or  boiling  water,  salt  and  pepper,  as 
needed,  and  let  become  very  hot.  In  the 
meantime  have  from  half  to  three-fourths  a 
cup  of  rice  nicely  boiled  and,  when  fresh 


water  enough  to  cover  the  whole.  Let 
simmer  two  or  three  hours,  then  strain  off 
the  broth.  If  the  meat  is  not  to  be  cooked 
until  the  next  day,  the  bones  may  be 
cooked  an  hour  or  more  before  the  vege- 
tables are  added,  or  the  vegetables  may 
not  be  used  until  the  next  day,  a  broth 
being  made  simply  from  the  bones  and 
trimmings  of  the  meat.  Dredge  the  meat 
(spread  skin  side  down  on  a  meat  boaraj 
lightly  with  salt  and  pepper;  lay  over  it 
two  or  three  thin  slices  of  cooked  bacon,  if 
such  be  at  hand,  or  pound  three  or  four 
slices  of  crisp,  fresh-cooked  bacon  to  a 
powder  (in  a  wooden  bowl)  and  sprinkle 
this  over  the  meat;  spread  on  a  thin  layer 
of  bread  dressing;  roll  the  meat  into  a 
compact  shape  and  tie  it  with  narrow  strips 


Hashed  Lamb,   ^vith  Rice  and  Peas 


peas  are  not  available,  a  can  of  peas  made 
hot.  Season  the  peas  with  a  level  tea- 
spoonful  of  sugar,  salt,  and  a  httle  black 
pepper;  add  a  tablespoonful  of  butter,  in 
little  bits,  to  the  meat.  Turn  the  meat 
into  the  center  of  a  hot  platter,  surround 
with  the  rice,  and,  in  turn,  surround  the 
rice  with  the  peas. 

Stuffed  Shoulder  of  Lamb 

Have  the  bones  removed  from  a  shoul- 
der of  yearling  lamb  and  sent  home  with 
the  meat.  Wash  the  meat,  wipe  it  dry 
and  spread  it  upon  a  meat  board;  trim  on 
the  edges  if  needed.  Put  the  bones  and 
trimmings  with  two  onions,  sliced,  half  a 
carrot,  sliced,  a  stalk  of  celery,  cut  in 
small  pieces,  half  a  dozen  peppercorns  and 
a  sprig  of  parsley  over  the  fire  with  cold 


of  cloth,  to  hold  it  in  shape.  Have  half  a 
cup  (or  a  little  less)  of  bacon  or  salt-pork 
fat  hot,  in  a  stewpan  of  a  size  to  take  the 
roll  of  meat.  Put  in  the  roll  and  let  cook 
until  delicately  browned  on  all  sides.  The 
meat  will  brown  quite  easily,  if  it  be  first 
rolled  in  flour.  Pour  in  a  pint  of  the  broth 
previously  made,  half  a  dozen  onions,  half 
a  dozen  stalks  of  celery  neatly  trimmed, 
half  a  tablespoonful  of  salt,  a  chilh  pepper 
and  half  a  tumbler  of  cider,  or  one-fourth 
this  quantity  of  vinegar.  Heat  the  whole 
to  the  boiling  point,  then  cover  and  set  on 
the  back  of  the  range  or  in  the  oven,  to 
cook  slowly  until  the  meat  is  tender.  Re- 
move the  meat,  celery  and  onions  to  the 
serving  dish.  Strain  off  the  broth,  remove 
the  fat  (with  a  si)Oon  and  tissue  paper) 
and  add  a  tablespoonful   of  currant  jelly.

and,  if  vinegar  be*  not  used,  the  juice  of 
half  a  lemon.  Let  the  sauce  boil,  to  dis- 
solve the  jelly;   add  salt  if  needed,    and

Bread  Dressing  for  Lamb 
Press  enough  crumbs  from  the  center  of 
a  loaf  of  bread  through  a  colander,  to  make 
one  cup  and  a  half;  add  a  grating  of  lemon 
rind,  a  tablespoonful  of  fine-chopped 
thyme,  two  tablespoonfuls  of  fine-chopped 
parsley,  half  a  teaspoonful  of  ground  pep- 
per, half  a  teaspoonful,  scant  measure,  of 
salt,  and  one-third  a  cup  of  melted  butter. 
Mix  thoroughly  and  use  to  spread  over  the 
meat.

"'  ^^^^^U 

small  pieces.  Beat  two  eggs  and  the  yolks 
of  two  more,  until  a  full  spoonful  can  be 
taken  up;  add  one-fourth  a  teaspoonful  of 
salt,  three  tablespoonfuls  of  hot  or  cold 
water,  and  a  dash  of  pepper,  if  desired. 
Mix  thoroughly,  then  strain  over  the  bits 
of  sausage.  Have  a  tablespoonful  of  butter 
melted  in  the  omelet  pan.  Bring  this  for- 
ward to  a  hot  part  of  the  range,  and  turn 
in  the  egg  mixture.  Shake  the  pan  back 
and  forth  with  one  hand,  and,  with  a  spat- 
ula or  thin  knife  in  the  other,  separate  the 
cooked  egg  from  the  pan,  at  the  edge,  so 
that  in  shaking  the  uncooked  egg  may  at 
this  point  run  down  on  to  the  hot  pan. 
Raise  the  side  of  the  pan  next  the  handle, 
when  shaking  forward,  and  lower  it,  when 
the  pan  is  brought  back.  When  nearly 
creamy  throughout,  roll  the  omelet,  let 
stand  a  moment  to  color  a  little,  then  turn 
on  to  a  hot  serving  dish. 

Curried  Potatoes 

Peel  a  small  onion  and  cut  it  into  very 
thin  rings;  melt  three  tablespoonfuls  of 
butter  in  a  frying  pan,  put  in  the  onion 
and  stir  constantly,  until  it  is  softened  and 
yellowed;  add  six  or  eight  cold,  boiled  po- 
tatoes, cut  hi  sHces;  sprinkle  these  with  salt 
and  a  level  tablespoonful  of  curry  powder 
stirred  smooth  in  a  cup  of 
broth  or  water;  shake  the 
pan  a  moment,  then  cover 
and  let  simmer  about  ten 
minutes.  Squeeze  in  the 
juice  of  half  a  lemon,  shake, 
or  turn  with  a  knife  and 
fork,  and  serve  very  hot.

Melting    Potatoes 

Pare  potatoes  of  the  same 

size  and  cook  them  in  boil- 

ing,     salted    water;   when 

done,   drain,   sprinkle   with 

salt,  and  cover  with  a  towel, 

to    take   up    the    moisture; 

then  take  the  potatoes,  one 

by  one,  and  press  each  firmly  in  a  towel, 

to  give  it  a  round  shape,  and  set  them, 

side  by  side,  in  a  well-buttered  baking  dish; 

turn  in  consomme  or  milk  and  cream,  half

325 


and  half,  to  surround  the  potatoes  to  one- 
third  their  height.  Set  a  piece  of  butter 
on  each  potato,  or  brush  over  the  tops  gen- 
erously with  melted  butter,  and  let  bake 
from  fifteen  to  twenty  minutes  or  until  the 
liquid  is  absorbed  and  the  potatoes  are 
browned  a  little  on  top. 

Macaroni  for  Luncheon  or  Supper 

Cook  three-fourths  a  cup  of  macaroni, 
broken  into  inchjengths,  in  boiling,  salted 
water  until  tender;  drain,  rinse  in  cold 
water  and  drain  again.  Melt  three  table- 
spoonfuls  of  butter;  cook  in  this  a  slice, 
each,  of  onion  and  carrot  until  softened 


tablespoon fuls  of  butter;  cook  in  it  one 
level  tablespoonful  and  a  half  of  flour  and 
one-fourth  a  teaspoonful,  each,  of  salt  and 
paprika;  when  frothy  stir  in  three-fourths  a 
cup  of  rich  milk;  stir  until  boiling,  then 
stir  in  half  or  three-fourths  a  cup  of  grated 
cheese;  continue  stirring  until  the  cheese 
is  melted,  than  pour  over  the  toast.  A 
slice  of  crisp  bacon  is  a  good  addition  to 
each  slice  of  toast.  For  bacon  rolls,  roll 
the  bacon,  pass  a  wooden  toothpick  through 
it,  then  fry  in  deep  fat. 

Rich  EngHsh  Muffins 

Soften  a  yeast  cake  in  a  httle  scalded- 


Cheese  Toast,  with  Bacon 


somewhat,  then  pick  out  the  vegetables; 
add  two  tablespoonfuls  of  flour,  one-fourth 
a  teaspoonful  of  salt,  and  a  dash  of  pep- 
per, and  cook  until  frothy,  then  add  half  a 
cup,  each,  of  beef  broth  and  tomato  puree 
(or  a  full  cup  of  either,  not  both)  and 
stir  and  cook  until  boiling;  add  the  maca- 
roni and  half  a  cup  of  grated  cheese,  then 
with  fork  and  spoon  lift  the  macaroni  to 
mix  the  sauce  and  cheese  evenly  through  it. 
Cover  and  let  stand  over  hot  water  a  few 
moments.  Serve  very  hot.  Haifa  cup 
of  chopped  ham  or  other  cooked  meat  may 
be  used  either  with  or  without  the  cheese. 

Cheese  Toast,  with  Bacon 

This  dish  may  be  made  of  any  variety  of 
bread,  but  it  is  particularly  good  when 
made  of  Boston  brown  bread.  While  the 
bread  is  being   toasted,   melt   three   level 


and-cooled  milk,  then  stir  into  the  rest  of 
two  cups  of  scalded-and-cooled  milk;  then 
stirin  two  cups  and  a  half  of  flour;  beat  thor- 
oughly, cover  and  set  aside,  to  become  light 
and  puffy.  Add  one-third  a  cup  of  melted 
butter,  two  eggs,  a  teaspoonful  of  salt, 
a  tablespoonful  of  sugar,  and  about 
two  cups  and  a  half  of  flour.  Beat  the 
mixture  very  thoroughly.  Add  more 
flour  if  needed,  but  leave  the  dough 
too  soft  to  be  handled  on  a  board. 
Cover  and  let  become  light.  Cut  the 
dough  into  pieces  that  will  about  half  fill 
the  rings.  Knead  the  pieces  with  floured 
hands  or  on  a  well-floured  board  and  pat 
them  to  fit  into  the  rings.  Have  the 
board  well-floured  and  the  rings  well- 
buttered,  standing  on  the  board;  set  the 
dough  in  the  rings,  cover  closely,  and, 
when  the  dough  about  half  fills  the  rings, 


326 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


remove  the  ring  and  dough,  with  a  spatula, 
to  a  well-heated  griddle,  that  has  been 
thoroughly  rubbed  over  with  a  slice  of  fat 


of  paper  in  a  dish  of  boihng  water  until  firm. 
It  will  take  about  an  hour.  Serve,  hot  or 
cold,   turned  from  the  mould.       To  a  cup

salt  pork.  When  the  muffins  are  baked 
on  one  side,  turn  muffin  and  ring  and  bake 
the  other  side.  When  the  muffins  are 
baked,  tear  them  apart,  and  toast  over  the 
coals;  spread  with  butter  and  serve  at  once. 

Nut,  Fig-and-Date  Pudding 

Cut  six  ounces,  each,  of  dates  and  figs, 
into  thin  slices.  Butter  a  mould,  holding 
a  pint  and  a  half  (three  cups),  then  dredge 
thickly  with  sugar;  line  the  mould  with 
lady-fingers,  standing  upright,  cutting  them 
off  even  with  the  top  of  the  mould.  Fill 
the  mould  with  the  prepared  fruit,  mixing 
in  with  it  six  ounces  of  pecan  or  English 
walnut  meats.  Beat  three  eggs;  add  one- 
fourth  a  cup   of  sugar,  wine  or  other  fla- 


of  thin  cream,  add  two  tablespoonfuls  of 
sugar,  and  such  flavoring  as  is  desired, 
then  beat  with  a  whip  churn  and  skim  off 
the  froth  as  it  rises.  Use  this  as  a  sauce 
for  the  pudding.  If  the  pudding  be 
served  cold,  it  may  be  decorated  with  the 
cream;  if  the  pudding  be  hot,  serve  the 
sauce    in    a    bowl. 

Salted  Almonds 

Cover  the  almonds  with  lukewarm  water 
and  heat  quickly  to  the  boihng  point;  drain 
and  cover  with  cold  water,  then  press  each 
nut,  one  by  one,  between  the  thumb  and 
finger,  to  slip  off  the  skin;  now^  dry  the 
nuts  on  a  cloth.  Dip  the  tips  of -the  fin- 
gers of  the  right  hand  into  unbeaten  white

voring,  and  one  cup  and  a  half  of  milk; 
mix  thoroughly  and  pour  over  the  fruit  in 
the  mould.    Let  cook,  set  on  several  folds 


of  egg,  and  repeatedly  take  up  and  drop 
a  few  nuts,  until  they  are  well  coated  with 
the  egg.      Continue  until  all  the  nuts  are

Orange,  Chestnut-and-Raisin 
Salad 

Peel  and  blanche  the  nuts  at 'the  same 
time,  then  boil  until  tender.  Select 
oranges  with  but  little  membrane,,  or  else 
slice  in  a  manner  to  avoid  all  membrane. 
Cook  the  raisins  in  boiling  water  until  well 
plumped  out;  drain,  remove  seeds  and  set 
aside  to  become  chilled,  ^vlix  the  sliced 
nuts  and  raisins  lightly  with  French  dress- 
ing, then  add  the  prepared  orange  and  dis- 
pose on  well-washed  and  dried  lettuce 
leaves.      Serve  with  roast  meats  or  game, 


without  the  tough  membrane.  Peel  and 
scrape  the  bananas,  then  cut  in  even 
slices.  Retain  all  the  juice  of  the  grape- 
fruit. Dispose  the  prepared  fruit  in  glass 
saucers,  dividing  the  juice  among  them. 
Dispose  a  few  slices  of  banana  and  halves 
of  dates  on  the  top  of  the  fruit  in  a  sym- 
metrical manner;  sprinkle  with  powdered 
sugar  and  serve  when  thoroughly  chilled, 
either  as  an  appetizer  before  luncheon  or 
as  a  dessert  dish  at  dinner  or  luncheon. 

Apple  Fritters,  English  Style 

Beat  an  egg,  add  a  few  grains  of  salt  and 
one-fourth  a  cup  of  milk;  cut  out  four  or 
five  rounds  of  bread  from  half-inch  slices 
of  stale  bread.      Set   the   bread   into  the 


Macedoine  of  Tvlidwinter  Fruit 


or  with  bread  and  butter,  as  a  luncheon 
salad,  when  mayonnaise  dressing  may  be 
used. 

Macedoine   of  Midwinter  Fruit 

For  each  service  and  a  generous  dish, 
take  five  or  six  dates,  half  a  small  banana, 
and  half  a  grape  fruit.  Cover  the  dates 
with  boiling  water;  stir  and  separate  them 
in  the  water  with  a  silver  fork;  skim  them 
from  the  water  to  an  agate  pan,  and  set 
them  into  the  oven  for  three  or  four  min- 
utes to  dry  off.  Cut  each  date  in  halves, 
removing  the  stones.  Cut  around  the  pulp 
in  each  section  of  the  halves  of  grape  fruit, 
to  remove  the  pulp  in  whole  pieces  and 


milk  and  egg  mixture,  and,  when  it  is  ab- 
sorbed, cook  the  bread  in  deep  fat,  or  in  a 
little  butter  made  hot  in  a  frying  pan.  In 
the  latter  case,  cook  the  bread  until  brown 
on  one  side,  then  turn  to  brown  the  other 
side.  Have  ready  some  apples,  pared, 
cored,  stewed  in  a  few  spoonfuls  of  water, 
strained  and  sweetened  with  sugar.  The 
apple  should  be  quite  consistent.  Put  a 
spoonful  of  the  hot  apple  sauce  on  each 
round  of  bread,  grate  a  little  nutmeg  over 
the  top  and  serve  at  once,  and  very  hot. 

Canned  Pineapple  Toast 

Add  a  little  sugar  to  the  syrup  in  a  can 
of  pineapple,    (use  half  the  can  of  pine-

apple  for  this  dish,  and  the  other  half  for

lemon  juice,   and  let  boil  a  few  minutes, 


Flower  Holder 

then  add  the  slices  of  pineapple  and  keep 
the  whole  hot.  From  sHces  of  stale  sponge 
cake  cut  as  many  rounds  as  there  are 
slices  of  pineapple  to  be  used,  and  of  the 
same  size.  Butter  the  rounds  of  cake, 
dredge  the  butter  with  sugar,  and  set 
them  into  the  oven  to  brown  and  glaze. 
When  ready  to  serve,  set  a  round  of  pine- 
apple above  a  round  of  cake,  pour  over  the 
sauce  and  serve,  at  once.  A  little  nice 
jam  or  marmalade  may  be  spread  upon 
the  cake  before  the  pineapple  is  set 
above  it. 


fire  until  smooth ;  the  juice  of  half  a  lemon 
may  be  added,  if  at  hand.  Have  ready  a 
tablespoonful  of  granulated  gelatine,  soft- 
ened in  one-fourth  a  cup  of  cold  water; 
dissolve  this  in  the  hot  marmalade.  Set 
the  dish  of  marmalade  into  ice  water  and 
stir,  occasionally,  until  it  begins  to  set, 
then  turn  into  a  mould.  Serve  turned  from 
the  mould  with  cream.  Strawberry  and 
raspberry  jam  may  be  prepared  in  the  same 
way,  and  provide  choice  dessert  dishes. 

Oranges  for  Breakfast 

Cut  the  oranges  crosswise  into  halves; 
take  out  such  seeds  as  are  in  sight,  then, 
with  a  thin-bladed,  sharp-pointed  knife,  cut 
around  the  pulp  in  each  section  close  to 
the  membrane,  that  the  pulp  maybe  easily 
removed  and  in  a  whole  piece.  Half  an 
orange  is  one  service.  These  may  be  served 
on  small  plates,  but  a  deep  bowl-shaped 
plate  comes  specially  for  this  use. 


The  magic  cup  for  fortune  telling,  the 
bouillon  cup  with  cover,  the  baked  apple 
set,  the  orange  bowl,  the  large  dish  with 
individual  dishes  for  salted  almonds  and 
the  flower  holder,  are  shown  through  the 
courtesy  of  Jones,  McDuffey  &  Stratton, 
Boston.  The  flower  holder  is  dc^^igned  to 
hold  small  bouquets  of  flowers;  these  hold- 
ers come  in  various  sizes,  with  from  seven 
to  twenty  or  more  vases.     It  is  a  pretty

an  equal  measure  of  boihng  water  over  the 


conceit  that  the  number  of  bouquets  corre- 
spond to  the  number  of  guests  and  that 
each  guest  be  given  a  bouquet  on  taking 
her  departure.

Menus  for  Children  Five  or  Six  Years  of  Age 

If  starch  be  administered  in  a  form  which  obliges  the  child  to  chew  it  properly,  not  only  will  the  jaws,  the  teeth,  and  the 
gums  obtain  the  exercise  which  they  crave,  and  without  which  they  cannot  develop  normally,  but  the  starch  will  be  so  thor- 
oughly insalivated  that  much  of  it  will  be  converted  within  the  mouth  into  maltose. — Horace  Fletcher.

Cookery  for  Young  Housekeepers 

By  JANET  M.  HILL 

LESSON  VIII 

Gelatinous  Soups  and  Jellies.     Proteid  Sparers 


IN  the  first  chapter  we  spoke  of  ''the 
gelatin oids,"  a  group  of  proteids  that 
were  not  as  important  as  the  group 
containing  albumin,  gluten,  etc. ,  inasmuch 
as  they  cannot  alone  supply  the  nitrogen 
needed  by  the  body.  Though  they  be  not 
the  equivalent  of  true  proteids,  they  may 
replace  some  of  the  proteid  in  the  diet. 
They  are  easily  digested  and  absorbed  and 
tend  to  promote  nutrition.  They  also  fur- 
nish variety  in  diet.  The  gelatinoids,  with 
which  we  have  most  to  do  in  cooking,  are 
found  largely  in  the  connective  tissues  of 
meat  and  in  the  ligaments  and  bones  of 
veal  and  chickens.  This  gelatinous  prin- 
ciple is  dissolved  by  heat  when  moisture  is 
present.  The  main  point  to  keep  in  mind 
is  that  connective  tissue  and  bone  are  both 
practically  flavorless  and,  if  this  principle  is 
to  be  made  use  of  in  cookery,  flavor  must 
be  added  to  it.  If  the  juices  of  the  meat 
be  used,  then  rich  flavor  is  assured.  When 
these  can  be  had  only  in  small  quantity, 
then  flavor  must  be  secured  from  vegeta- 
bles, herbs  and  the  like.  In  making  sweet 
dishes,  we  use  the  gelatine  of  commerce, 
and  for  flavor  coffee,  chocolate,  wine,  fruit 
juices,  etc.  Such  dishes  do  not  possess  high 
nutritive  value  of  themselves,  but  nutriment 
may  be  added,  when  advisable,  by  the  use 
of  eggs  or  cream. 


Soup  Stock  from  Bones  of  a  Roast 
Fowl  or  Roast  of  Veal 

Break  up  the  bones,  carefully,  discarding 
any  dressing  that  may  be  attached  to  them. 
If  a  few  bits  of  raw  lamb  (ends  of  chops) 
or  veal  be  at  hand,  these  will  give  flavor  to 
the  soup.  Also  if  uncooked  giblets,  heart, 
Hver  and  gizzard,  or  the  neck  of  the  fowl 
be  available,  the  soup  will  be  much  im- 
proved. Cover  the  materials  to  be  used 
with  cold  water,  and  let  heat  slowly  to  the 
boiling  point,  then  let  simmer  an  hour,  or 
longer,  if  uncooked  material  were  added. 
Add  now  half  an  onion,  cut  in  bits,  half  a 
small  carrot,  cut  in  bits,  a  stalk  of  celery  or 
half  a  tablespoonful  of  celery  seed,  a  sprig 
of  parsley,  six  peppercorns  and  a  scant  tea- 
spoonful  of  salt,  and  let  cook  nearly  an 
hour,  then  strain  off  the  broth.  Set  this 
aside  in  a  cool  place  and  when  cold  remove 
the  fat.  This  fat  may  be  used  in  making 
the  thickening,  if  a  thickened  soup  be 
desired. 

Soup  i 

Have  ready  two  or  three  tablespoonfuls 
of  cooked  rice  and  one-fourth  a  cup  of  bits 
of  cooked  chicken;  reheat  the  soup,  add  the 
rice  and  chicken  with  salt  and  pepper  as 
needed. 


332 


COOKERY  FOR  YOUNG   HOUSEKEEPERS 


333 


Soup  2 
Reheat  the  soup  stock  in  a  double  boiler; 
beat  the  yolk  of  an  egg  —  two  are  better  — 
add  half  a  cup  of  thin  cream  and  stir  into 
the  soup.  Season  as  needed  and  stir  until 
the  egg  is  set,  then  serve  at  once. 

Soup  3 

To  the  stock  add  half  to  a  full  cup  of 
tomato  puree;  serve  in  this  way  or  finish  as 
soup  I  or  2. 

Soup  4 

Melt  two  tablespoonfuls  of  butter,  or  fat 
taken  from  the  top  of  the  stock;  cook  in  it 
two  tablespoonfuls  of  flour;  add  half  a  cup 
of  cooked  spinach,  pressed  through  a  sieve, 
and  half  a  cup  of  the  stock;  stir  until  boiling, 
then  stir  into  the  rest  of  the  stock.  Season 
to  taste.  The  yolk  of  an  egg  and  cream 
may  be  added  at  the  last  moment.  This 
is  a  most  delicious  soup. 

Soup  5 

Cooked  celery,  onions,  peas,  or  aspara- 
gus, may  be  pressed  through  a  sieve,  and 
from  half  to  a  full  cup  be  used  in  place  of 
the  spinach  in  soup  4. 

Chicken  Stock  or  Broth  from 
Uncooked  Fqwl 

Have  the  fowl  separated  into  pieces  at 
the  joints;  wipe  the  pieces  with  a  small  piece 
of  cheese  cloth  wrung  out  of  cold  water; 
put  into  a  saucepan,  and  pour  on  cold  water 
to  cover  well  the  pieces  of  fowl,  heat  slowly 
to  the  boiling  point,  then  let  simmer  until 
the  meat  is  tender.  Pour  off  the  liquid  and 
set  it  aside  to  become  cold,  then  remove 
all  of  the  fat.  Season  with  salt  and  reheat, 
or  use  in  any  of  the  soups  given  above. 
There  should  be  a  pint  of  broth  for  each 
pound  of  chicken  taken.  If  less  broth  be 
secured,  water  may  be  added.  Use  the 
pieces  of  fowl  in  any  of  the  dishes  given  for 
stewed  fowl.  If  the  broth  jellies  when  cold, 
scrape  off  the  fat,  then  wipe  the  surface 
with  tissue  paper  or  a  piece  of  cheese  cloth 
wrung  out  of  hot  water.  A  stalk  of  celery 
and  half  an  onion  may  be  cooked  with  the 
fowl  for  flavor. 


Soup  from  Remnants  of  Beef  or  Lamb 
Roasts 

Prepare  the  stock  in  the  same  manner 
as  when  the  bones  and  remnants  of  roast 
fowl  are  used.  Tomato  puree  is  a  good 
addition  to  this  stock.  Cooked  macaroni, 
cut  in  rings,  makes  a  good  garnish  for  the 
soup. 

Chicken  Breast  in  Jelly 

Use  broth  from  either  cooked  or  un- 
cooked fowl.  If  the  broth  makes  a  firm 
jelly  when  cold,  gelatine  is  unnecessary. 
If  the  broth  does  not  make  a  very  firm 
jelly,  add  gelatine,  about  a  level  table- 
spoonful  to  a  pint  of  broth.  Soften  the 
gelatine  in  one-fourth  a  cup  of  cold  water, 
and  dissolve  in  the  broth,  freed  of  fat  and 
made  hot.  If  cooked  bones  have  been 
used  freely  in  making  the  broth,  the  jelly 
will  not  be  very  clear.  It  may  be  clarified 
a  litde  by  the  use  of  the  white  of  an  egg. 
Crush  the  shell  —  nicely  washed  before 
the  egg  is  broken,  and  beat  the  white 
slightly,  mix  these  through  the  broth  and 
heat  the  whole  (broth,  gelatine  and  egg) 
slowly  to  the  boiling  point,  stirring  con- 
stantly meanwhile.  Let  boil  five  minutes, 
then  keep  hot  ten  minutes  (without  boil- 
ing) while  the  mixture  settles.  Skim  care- 
fully, then  strain  through  two  folds  of 
cheese  cloth,  laid  over  a  colander  or  strainer. 
Add  seasoning  as  needed.  Put  a  Httle 
of  the  chilled  broth  into  a  mould,  set  in  a 
pan  of  ice  and  water;  when  nearly  firm 
put  in  some  thin  slices  of  cooked  chicken, 
add  more  of  the  chilled-and-partly  "set" 
broth  and  continue  with  chicken  and  broth, 
until  the  mould  or  moulds  are  filled. 
Serve  when  firm,  with  lettuce,  cress  or 
celery,  and  salad  dressing.  The  chicken 
may  also  be  chopped  and  moulded  in  lay- 
ers with  the  jelly.  Use  first  a  layer  of 
plain  jelly,  then  a  layer  of  jelly  into  which 
all  the  chopped  chicken  the  jelly  would 
take  up  has  been  stirred.  Continue  the 
layers  in  the  same  manner.  Slices  of  hard- 
cooked  egg  or  cooked  asparagus  tips  may 
be  moulded  with  the  other  ingredients  at 
pleasure. 


334 


THE  BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


Coffee  Jelly 


Lemon  Jelly 


In  making  the  coffee  for  breakfast  make 
one  or  two  extra  cups;  strain  all  the  coffee 
from  the  grounds  when  pouring  the  coffee 
at  table.  Let  it  settle,  then  pour  off  and 
use  the  top  of  the  coffee.  For  two  small 
cups  (half  a  pint)  of  jelly,  put  half  a  level 
tablespoonful  of  granulated  gelatine  into 
four  or  five  tablespoonfuls  of  cold  water  to 
stand  about  fifteen  minutes;  add  a  scant 
quarter  of  a  cup  of  sugar  and  the  cup  of 
hot  coffee;  stir  over  the  fire  until  the  gel- 
atine and  sugar  are  dissolved,  then  strain 
into  the  cups.  When  ready  to  serve  set  a 
cup  of  the  jelly  into  warm  (not  hot)  water 
that  comes  up  as  high  on  the  outside  of 
the  cup  as  the  jelly  comes  on  the  inside, 
remove  at  once,  turn  the  cup  from  side  to 
side,  to  see  that  the  jelly  loosens  from  it, 
and  turn  the  jelly  on  to  a  chilled  plate. 
Serve  whipped  cream  or  boiled  custard 
around  it. 


Let  one  level  tablespoonful  of  granulated 
gelatine  stand  in  one-fourth  a  cup  of  cold 
water  about  fifteen  minutes,  or  until  it  has 
absorbed  all  the  water;  add  one  cup  of 
boiling  water  and  a  scant  half  a  cup  of  sugar; 
stir  until  the  sugar  and  the  gelatine  are  dis- 
solved and  then  let  become  cool;  add  half  a 
cup  of  lemon  juice  and  turn  into  cups.  Set 
aside  to  become  cold  and  firm.  Serve  with 
boiled  custard  or  cream.  Preserved  peaches 
or  pears,  cooked  prunes  or  figs,  or  nut 
meats,  also  sections  of  orange,  from  which 
the  membrane  has  been  removed,  or  slices 
of  banana,  may  be  moulded  in  the  jelly. 

Orange  Jelly 

Make  as  lemon  jelly,  cutting  down  the 
quantity  of  boiling  water  to  three-fourths  a 
cup  and  increasing  the  fruit  juice  to  three- 
fourths  a  cup.  Add  also  a  tablespoonful 
of  lemon  juice. 


Some  Secrets  in  French  Cookery 

By  the  Authoi-  of  ''Modern  Housecraft^' 

[From  t/ie  Epicttre) 
( Concluded) 


I  DO  not  think  I  am  wrong  in  saying 
that  want  of  interest  and  hasty  prep- 
aration are  together  responsible  for 
the  terribly  commonplace  character  of  so 
much  English  cookery.  We  may  pride  our- 
selves on  being  as  simple  and  plain  as  we 
like  in  our  tastes,  but  there  is  no  virtue  in 
waste,  and  indigestible,  ill-cooked  food  is 
always  wasted,  even  where  every  scrap  of 
it  is  .eaten.  The  day's  food  is  the  day's 
thought  and  work;  this  cannot  be  too  often 
said,  and  ill-tempers,  irritability,  deranged 
livers  and  lack  of  appetite,  are  the  results  of 
undigested,  mal-assimilated  food,  and  this 
is  due  to  the  lack  of  attention  given  to  the 


subject.  We  have  the  cheapest  and  best 
food  materials  of  the  world  at  command  — 
there  is  always  a  market  in  England  for 
whatever  is  grown  elsewhere  —  yet  we  are, 
as  a  people,  ill- fed,  and  to  judge  by  the 
faces  of  the  middle-class  majority  taken  in 
the  bulk,  we  are  an  ill-nourished  people. 

Our  plain  food,  even  when  good  and  well 
served,  is  often  lacking  in  those  little  ad- 
ditional touches  which  give  it  variety  and 
make  it  appetizing.  We  tacitly  acknowl- 
edge this  when  we  seek,  on  occasion,  to 
present  it  a  la  mode  Francaise.  But  with 
the  French  cook  it  is  invariably  a  question 
of  whether  this  shall  be  a  la  Frovencale,  a 


SOME  SECRETS  IN  FRENCH  COOKERY 


335 


la  Parisiemie,  a  la  Jardiniere,  etc.  Some- 
times it  is  decided  to  present  it  a  VAnglaise, 
but  this,  be  it  known,  is  when  is  it  to  be 
roast  or  boiled  in  the  plainest  possible  man- 
ner. 

Let  me  now  try  to  unveil  a  few  of  those 
secrets  of  variety,  giving  at  the  same  time 
the  correct  definition,  so  far  as  I  myself 
have  learned  them,  of  the  terms  most  com- 
monly used  in  cookery,  used  in  France  to 
designate  types  of  dishes,  in  England  to 
glorify  and  often  make  ridiculous  that  which 
does  not  belong  to  any  type.  French 
people  themselves  have  often  been  guilty 
of  giving  names  to  dishes  that  were  quite 
inappropriate  to  them,  because  they  liked 
to  make  innovations  and  were  ambitious  of 
inventing  new  modes,  but  when  this  form  of 
nomenclature  becomes  abused,  our  safety 
lies  in  reverting  to  the  broad  and  original 
type  and  making  sure  of  that.  To  do  this 
gives  us  the  additional  advantage  of  help- 
ing us  to  classify  recipes  as  we  find  them 
in  the  cookery  books. 

A  r  Anglaise,  as  we  have  said,  implies 
that  a  dish  is  plain  roast  or  boiled,  but  it 
may  also  denominate  a  specially  English 
dish  like  roast  sirloin  of  beef,  or  plum 
pudding. 

A  r  Italienne  generally  implies  that  the 
dish  is  made  of  or  garnished  with  Italian 
pastes  —  macaroni,  vermicelli,  etc.,  and  that 
cheese  or  tomato  sauce  are  served  with  it. 

A  la  Fraficaise  is,  strangely  enough, 
often  appUed  to  the  least  typically  French 
dishes,  but  to  be  correct  it  should  be  kept 
for  those  very  special  dishes  like  omelettes, 
pates,  and  cotelettes,  the  preparation  of 
which  is  peculiar  to  France. 

A  la  Provencale  generally  means  a  dish 
prepared  with  olive  oil,  and  one  in  which 
garlic  and  herbs  enter  into  the  composition. 

A  la  Ferigord dJ^-^XxQ-a  to  dishes  flavoured 
with  truffles,  because  these  abound  in  the 
district  of  Perigord. 

A  la  Normande  indicates  that  apples  are 
used  in  some  form  or  other  in  the  compo- 
sition of  the  dish  ;  to  this  rule  the  Matelote 
Normande  makes  exception,  for  that  is  a 
fish  ragout  wherein  white  sauce,  mush- 
rooms,  etc. ,   figure,   and  Sauce  Normande 


is  a  sauce  made  with  milk  or  white  stock 
enriched  with  cream. 

A  la  Bordelaise  signifies  a  dish  enriched 
with  the  red  Burgundy  or  wine  of  Bor- 
deaux. 

A  la  Parisienne  has  really  no  special 
significance,  but  appHes  to  dishes  rather 
elaborately  and  expensively  garnished,  and 
fancifully  prepared. 

A  la  Jardiniere  means  a  collection  of 
cooked  vegetables  used  daintily  as  a  gar- 
nish, or  in  combination  with  meat  in  stews, 
or  cut  small  and  freely  introduced  into 
soups. 

A  la  Macedoine  means  also  a  collection 
of  vegetables  cut  small,  but  set  in  white 
sauce,  and  generally  is  confined  to  green 
vegetables  garnished  with  others.  It  also 
means  a  collection  of  fruits  embedded  in 

jelly. 

A  la  Maitre-d' hotel  usually  signifies  a 
substantial  dish  with  which  melted  butter 
or  parsley  butter  sauce  is  served,  or  it  may 
be  a  homely  grill  served  with  butter  and 
chopped  parsley. 

A  la  Soubise  implies  that  onions  have 
largely  entered  into  the  composition  of  the 
dish  or  its  sauce. 

A  la  Crecy  also  indicates  that  carrots 
have  been  made  the  distinguishing  feature 
of  the  dish. 

A  r  Irlandaise  applies  to  dishes  which 
contain  potatoes  in  some  form,  or  is  gar- 
nished with  potatoes. 

A  r  Espagnole  means  not  a  typically 
Spanish  dish,  but  one  of  a  dark,  savoury 
nature  in  which  brown  Espag7iole  sauce 
figures. 

A  la  Flamande  usually  indicates  that  the 
ingredients  are  stewed  with  butter  and 
herbs,  or  that  cabbage  enters  into  their 
combination,  especially  a  garnish  of  Brus- 
sels sprouts. 

A  la  Chipolata  means  that  a  certain 
strongly  flavoured  Italian  sausage  is  added 
to  the  compound,  and  used  as  a  garnish. 

A  r  A  Hem  ande  is  applied  to  dishes  distinc- 
tively German  in  character,  as  sauer-kraut 
with  pork,  dishes  of  meat  accompanied  by 
prunes  and  fruit,  fruit  and  sweet  soups, 
and  such-hke.  •

Recreation 

THE  secret  of  one  woman's  youthful 
appearance  and  steady  nerves  is  at- 
tributable to  a  nap  every  day,  no  matter 
how  short  it  may  be. 

She  has  always  led  a  busy,  active  life  and 
many  marvel  at  her  fresh,  unwrinkled  face; 
no  matter  what  interruptions  there  are,  she 
takes  a  bath  and  nap  —  of  perhaps  not  more 
than  twenty  minutes  or  half  an  hour  each 
day. 

ONE  wise  woman  gave   the    following 
advice  to  a  friend  recently,  which  will 
bear  passing  on: 

"Take  every  good  thing  that  comes 
your  way.  There  are  so  many  pleasant  little 
outings  that  would  send  you  home  rested 
and  with  a  different  view-point,  but  you 
think  up  some  excuse  and  do  not  go. 

"  Don't  you  ever  stop  to  realize  that  no 
one  is  wholly  indispensable  in  this  world. 
Some  one  can  take  our  places  if  we  drop 
out.  Why  not  accept  all  that  is  offered  to 
us  and  get  the  most  out  of  Ufe  we  can? 
You  were  just  ready  to  decline  that  lovely 
invitation  for  a  week  in  the  woods.  Go, 
and  you  will  come  home  so  much  bene- 
fitted that  it  will  react  on  the  whole  family, 
and  they  will  be  the  gainers  as  well  as 
yourself."  C.  M.  A. 

*  *  * 

Aunt  Sally's  Lemon   Pie 

AUNT  Sally  is  a  colored  cook  in  one  of 
the  smaller  cities  of  Pennsylvania. 
When  asked  how  she  made  her  lemon  pies, 
which  are  quite  different  from  the  usual  kind 
with  a  meringue,  she  brought  her  bowl  of 


batter,  the  next  time  she  made  pies,  for  the 
guest  to  see,  and  gave  this  explanation  of 
the  ingredients: 

Take  two  grated  lemon  rinds,  and  the 
juice  of  the  two  lemons,  two  cups  of  brown 
sugar,  and  two  tablespoonfuls  of  New  Or- 
leans or  other  molasses.  The  sugar  should 
be  the  light-brown  coffee  sugar. 

Use  six  eggs,  two  tablespoonfuls  of  flour, 
even  full.  Mix  the  lemon  and  sugar  thor- 
oughly, and  add  the  eggs,  one  at  a  time, 
beating  constantly  between  the  adding  of 
each.  Add  the  remaining  ingredients  to 
make  a  nice,  light  batter.  It  will  be  thick 
b<i|pw,  of  course,  but  foamy  on  top  and  yet 
well  incorporated. 

Use  ifwa  crusts,  making  the  pastry  of  half 
lard  and  half  butter.  They  should  be  thick, 
but  flaky. 

Try  this  and  credit  Aunt  Sally  with  some- 
thing out  of  the  ordinary. 

Grated  Citron  Melon  Preserve 

THE  citron  melon  preserve  is  usually 
made  with  the  melon  cut  in  dice,  but 
it  is  far  more  delicate  and  enjoyable  when 
grated,  and  the  syrup  and  flavoring  pene- 
trate far  quicker.  It  will  keep  a  pale  green 
color. 

Cook  the  grated  melon  tender  in  a  rich 
syrup  flavored  with  lemon  rind  and  juice. 
Half  a  lemon  to  one  pound  of  sugar  is  a 
good  allowance.  J.  D.  C. 


About  Fat 

NOTICE  that  Mrs.  W.  W.  of  New 
York  City,  asks  in  the  Department  for 
Subscribers  about  the  use  of  fats.  Per- 
haps my  experience   will  help    her.      My 


I 


338 


HOME  IDEAS  AND  ECONOMIES 


339 


husband  has  bacon  every  morning  for 
breakfast,  so  we  have  plenty  of  bacon  fat. 
I  have  all  fats  saved,  turkey,  chicken, 
duck,  bacon,  sausage,  lamb,  mutton,  and 
top  of  the  pot.  Put  all  in  a  kettle  together 
while  fresh.  To  every  four  po-unds  of  fat 
put  in  a  quart  or  two  of  cold  water  and  a 
heaping  tablespoonful  of  powdered  borax. 
Let  boil  five  minutes,  stirring  frequently, 
at  first,  and  watching  lest  it  boil  over. 
Set  away  in  the  kettle  to  cool,  then  place 
in  ice  chest  overnight.  In  the  morning 
slide  a  knife  around  the  fat  to  free  it  from 
the  kettle  and  lift  the  cake  out  on  a  platter, 
bottom  side  up.  Scrape  off  the  black  top 
for  soap  grease.  Throw  away  the  water 
in  the  kettle,  wash  and  wipe  the  same  and 
put  in  the  cake  of  fat  and  cook  the  remain- 
ing water  out  of  it.  Now  it  is  ready  for 
fried  cakes,  potato  chips  or  anything. 

Get  a  can  of  lye  and  put  some  of  it  with 
the  soap  grease  for  soft  soap.  It  is  fine 
for  scrubbing  floors  and  for  making  suds  for 
soaking  the  clothes  overnight  before  wash- 
ing. I  never  waste  one  bit  of  fat.  I  make 
pie  crust  of  butter,  because  it  is  more 
tasteful  and  much  more  healthful,  but  my 
pie  crust  is  only  a  little  richer  than  bis- 
cuit. One  can  make  pies  this  way  that  are 
as  healthy  to  eat  as  bread  and  butter.  All 
the  children  can  have  a  piece.  A  little 
baking  powder  can  be  put  into  the  pie 
crust. 

By  the  way,  I  make  my  own  baking 
powder,  too. 

For  those  who  cook  by  a  coal  or  a  wood 
range,  I  will  say  when  cooking  pancakes 
close  all  the  drafts  in  the  stove  and  draw 
the  griddle  close  up  to  a  partially  opened 
lid,  all  the  smoke  from  the  griddle  will 
rush  over  the  side  into  the  aperture  and 
on  up  the  chimney.  I  have  used  this 
method,  when  people  were  eating  in  the 
same  room,  and  no  one  could  detect  any 
smoke.      Bacon  can  be  fried  without  smoke 

the  same  way.  A.  C.  C. 

*  *   * 

ONE  of  the  handiest  articles  in  my 
kitchen  is  a  little  board  of  hard  wood, 
which  hangs  on  a  convenient  nail  over  my 
table.      I    use    it   when    cutting  bread  or 


meat,  thus  saving  my    table    oil-cloth. 

MAKE  small  puffs  from  cream-puff 
mixture,  but  instead  of  filling  them 
with  the  usual  custard  or  cream,  fill  with 
rich  vanilla  ice  cream,  put  on  the  cover, 
place  each  one  in  a  glass  and  pour  over  it 
a  maple-and-nut  sauce,  or  a  hot  chocolate 
sauce.  The  hot  sauce  is  delicious  with 
the  ice  cream  and  its  cream-puff  casing  is 
a  novelty. 

IN  making  the  ''island"  for  floating  is- 
land, if  you  will  put  the  beaten  white 
of  egg  in  a  small  wire  strainer,  a  table- 
spoonful  at  a  time,  and  place  over  a  kettle 
of  boiling  water  and  so  steam  the  island, 
they  will  not  be  tough,  as  they  frequently 
are  when  cooked  on  top  of  boiling  milk. 

B.  A.  B. 


Especially  For  Men  Folk 

HERE  is  a  dessert  more  especially  for 
the  men  folk  of  the  family,  and  I 
am  quite  sure  it  will  satisfy  the  most  fas- 
tidious. It  is  "coffee  jelly."  There  are 
numerous  recipes,  but  this  is  made  from 
my  own  composition,  and  is  perfectly  relia- 
ble and  delicious.  Soak,  for  two  hours, 
half  a  box  of  gelatine  in  one  gill  of  cold 
water,  add  to  it  a  good  pinch  of  salt;  at 
the  end  of  two  hours,  put  it  in  a  bowl  with 
one  gill  of  granulated  sugar.  Now  make 
one  quart,  and  one  gill  of  very  strong 
coffee,  strain  and  have  it  boilbig  hot; 
pour  this  over  the  gelatine  and  stir  until 
dissolved.  Put  in  a  cool  place,  and 
stir  several  times  (to  prevent  any  set- 
tlings) before  it  "sets.  "  When  cold,  put 
it  in  the  refrigerator,  and  serve  ice-cold 
with  the  following  sauce:  Sauce — Dis- 
solve one  teaspoonful  of  gelatine  in  one 
tablespoonful  of  cold  water;  add  to  this 
(when  dissolved)  halfa  pint  of  fresh  cream, 
also  two  tablespoonfuls  of  granulated  sugar, 
and  one  teaspoonful  of  extract  of  vanilla; 
put  this  in  a  bowl,  and  when  thoroughly 
cold  it  will  be  slightly  thick;  beat  it  with  an 
egg  beater  or  cream  whip  until  frothy, 
and  resembling  whipped  cream,  then  put

Especially  For  Men  Folk 

HERE  is  a  dessert  more  especially  for 
the  men  folk  of  the  family,  and  I 
am  quite  sure  it  will  satisfy  the  most  fas- 
tidious. It  is  '' coffee  jelly. "  There  are 
numerous  recipes,  but  this  is  made  from 
my  own  composition,  and  is  perfectly  relia- 
ble and  delicious.  Soak,  for  two  hours, 
half  a  box  of  gelatine  in  one  gill  of  cold 
water,  add  to  it  a  good  pinch  of  salt;  at 
the  end  of  two  hours,  put  it  in  a  bowl  with 
one  gill  of  granulated  sugar.  Now  make 
one  quart,  and  one  gill  of  very  strong 
coffee,  strain  and  have  it  boiU?ig  hot; 
pour  this  over  the  gelatine  and  stir  until 
dissolved.  Put  in  a  cool  place,  and 
stir  several  times  (to  prevent  any  set- 
thngs)  before  it  ' '  sets.  ' '  AVhen  cold,  put 
it  in  the  refrigerator,  and  serve  ice-cold 
with  the  following  sauce:  Sauce — Dis- 
solve one  teaspoonful  of  gelatine  in  one 
tablespoonful  of  cold  water;  add  to  this 
(when  dissolved)  halfa  pint  of  fresh  cream, 
also  two  tablespoonfuls  of  granulated  sugar, 
and  one  teaspoonful  of  extract  of  vanilla; 
put  this  in  a  bowl,  and  when  thoroughly 
cold  it  will  be  slightly  thick;  beat  it  with  an 
^gg  beater  or  cream  whip  until  frothy, 
and  resembling  whipj^ed  cream,  then  put 


340 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


it  in  a  pretty  glass  pitcher,  and  at  serving 
time  pour  it  over  the  jelly;  the  jelly  will  be 
a  nice  golden  brown.  Lady  fingers,  or  any 
sweet  wafer  make  a  good  accompaniment. 
It  is  best  to  make  any  dish  where  gelatine 
is  used  the  day  before  it  is  required,  thus 
giving  it  plenty  of  time  to  harden  properly. 
The  vanilla  is  my  own  idea,  and  is  a  de- 
cidedly pleasant  addition  in  connection 
with  the  coffee;  just  try  it  and  see. 

L.  N. 


From   Experience 

FROM  experience  these  little  helps  have 
been  gleaned,and  with  the  Editor's  kind 
permission  I  would  like  to  pass  them  on. 
After  using  the  grater  we  all  know  too 
well  how  hard  it  is  to  clean  it;  hereafter, 
simply  scrub  it  with  a  small  brush,  and  vic- 
tory is  yours.  After  using  a  frying  pan, 
and  the  grease  or  other  substances  persist 
in  adhering  to  the  sides,  empty  the  con- 
tents of  the  tea  pot  into  it,  allow  it  to  stand 
while  the  dishes  are  being  washed,  and  you 
will  find  every  particle  can  be  removed 
with  ease.  If  only  leaves  are  left,  put 
them  in  the  pan,  and  fill  it  with  water 
(hot)  and  let  it  stand  a  few  minutes. 

AFTER  lighting  a  candle,  if  it  is  too 
small  for  the  socket,  and  insists  upon 
going  sidewisejust  hold  the  candle(lighted) 
in  the  hand,  and  let  a  little  grease  fall  into 
the  opening,  then  quickly  replace  the  candle, 
in  a  few  minutes  the  grease  hardens,  and 
the  candle  stands  up  as  straight  as  a 
major. 

AFTER  underwear  is  worn  out,  es- 
pecially vests,  they  make  the  best  kind 
of  scrub  cloths;  being  soft,  they  got  into 
the  cracks  and  remove  dust  and  dirt 
readily. 

AFTER  the  handles  of  knives  become 
soiled,  they  may  be  cleaned  by  dipping 
half  a  lemon  in  salt,  and  scouring  the 
pieces  with  it,  then  wash  and  dry  in  the 
usual  manner,  using  hot  soap  suds  for  the 
purpose. 


To  Exterminate  Mosquitoes  and 
Fleas 

WHILE  on  a  vacation  one  summer, 
mosquitoes  were  beyond  description, 
so  bad  as  to  be  like  an  epidemic,  or  plague; 
they  fortunately  disappeared  during  the 
day,  to  return  in  regular  swarms  at  dusk; 
sometimes  they  would  get  into  the  bed- 
rooms and  we  could  not  sleep;  we  had  ex- 
hausted all  the  usual  remedies,  such  as 
citronella,  banana  oil,  Chinese  punk,  and 
pennyroyal,  when  one  night,  out  of  sheer 
desperation,  I  fastened  across  the  rooms  and 
across  the  head  boards,  too,  tiny  lines  of 
twine,  wet  rags  with  kerosene  oil,  and  hung 
them  on  the  lines;  the  odor  for  a  while  was 
most  unpleasant,  but  the  result  was  fine, 
and  we  were  not  bothered  by  a  mosquito  in 
the  house  again. 

If  you  are  troubled  with  fleas,  try  sprink- 
ling the  floors  plentifully  with  dry  table  salt, 
they  cannot  exist  where  salt  is  plentiful. 
All  may  not  know  that  pulverized  cloves  will 
drive  away  red  or  black  ants;  simply  sprinkle 
it  dry  around  their  haunts;  have  never 
known  it  to  fail.  S.  H.  R. 

#  *  # 

Potato  Dumplings 

PARE  and  boil,  six  good-sized  potatoes 
and  put  through  the  ricer;  then  add  one 
pint  of  bread  crumbs,  browned  in  butter, 
also  two  well-beaten  eggs,  enough  salt  to 
season,  and  flour  enough  to  make  them  hold 
together.  Form  into  twelve  balls  and  lay 
them  on  a  buttered  pie  plate  and  steam  about 
twenty  minutes;  place  on  a  warm  platter 
and  pour  over  them  one-half  a  cup  of  hot 
butter.  These  are  very  nice  to  eat  in  a 
course  with  lettuce,  or  any  kind  of  salad; 
or  a  special  sauce  for  them  is  made  as 
follows : 

Sauce  for  Potato    Dumpling 

BROWN  one  teaspoonful  of  minced 
onion,  and  one  tablespoonful  of  flour, 
in  two  tablespoonfuls  of  butter,  and  then 
add  one  cup  of  good  cider  vinegar  and  stir 
until  it  comes  to  a  boil;  salt  to  season. 

P.  C.  H.
