THIS  department  is  for  the  benefit  and  free  use  of  our  subscribf^rs.  Questions  relating  to  menus 
and  recipes,  and  those  pertaining  to  culinary  science  and  domestic  economics  in  general,  will 
be  cheerfully  answered  by  the  editor.  Communications  for  this  department  must  reach  us  before  the 
first  of  the  month  preceding  that  in  which  the  answers  are  expected  to  appear.  In  letters  requesting 
answer  by  mail,  please  enclose  postage  stamps;  for  menus,  $i.  Address  queries  to  Janet  M.  Hill 
Editor  Boston  Cooking-School  Magazine^  372  Boylston  Street,  Boston,  Mass. 


Dr.  C.  F.  Langworthy,  an  expert  in 
nutrition  connected  witli  the  office  of  the 
experiment  stations,  U.  S.  Department  of 
Agriculture,  having  noticed  the  inquiry  re- 
garding the  whipping  of  tliin  cream  that 
appeared  in  our  December  issue,  has  very 
kindly  sent  us  a  copy  of  an  article  on  restor- 
ing the  consistency  of  pasteurized  cream. 
This  matter  may  be  found  on  page  23  of 
Experiment  Station  Work,  No.  3.  Possi- 
bly the  main  points  of  the  article  may  be  of 
interest  to  many  of  our  readers. 

'' Within  the  past  few  years  the  use  of 
pasteurized  cream  for  domestic  purposes  has 
spread  quite  extensively.  A  quite  serious 
practical  objection  that  has  been  made  to 
pasteurized  cream  is  its  lack  of  consistency. 
The  cream  does  not  appear  to  be  as  rich  as 
normal  cream,  because  it  is  thinner  and  less 
viscous  and  does  not  whip  as  readily.  So 
strong  has  this  objection  become,  on  the 
part  of  the  consumers,  that  it  has  materially 
retarded  the  further  introduction  of  this 
method  of  supplying  cream.  The  Wiscon- 
sin station  has  been  studying  the  problem 
for  some  time,  and  has  recently  suggested  a 
remedy.  It  has  found  that  the  consistency 
of  pasteurized  cream  may  be  completely  re- 
stored by  the  addition  of  lime  in  solution. 
Limewater,  while  it  materially  increases  the 
consistency  of  pasteurized  cream,  dilutes 
the  cream  to  an  undesirable  degree.  Thus 
it  is  proposed  to  use  lime  dissolved  in  a  solu- 
tion of  granulated  sugar  (sucrate  of  lime). 
Such  a  lime  solution  contains  more  than  a 
hundred  times  as  much  lime  as  does  lime- 


water.  Consequently  it  may  be  added  to 
cream  in  sufficient  quantity  to  produce  the 
desired  result  without  perceptibly  changing 
the  cream  otherwise.  This  solution  of  lime 
in  sugar  is  called  viscogen  on  account  of  its 
viscous-producing  properties  and  the  treated 
products  are  called  visco  cream,  etc. 

"  Viscogen  is  not  a  commercial  article,  but 
it  may  be  easily  prepared.  The  method 
described  by  the  station  for  its  preparation 
is  as  follows: 

''Two  and  a  half  parts  by  weight  of  cone 
sugar  (granulated)  are  dissolved  in  five  parts 
of  water  and  one  part  of  quicklime  gradually 
slaked  in  three  parts  of  water.  This  milk 
of  lime  should  be  poured  through  a  wire 
strainer,  to  remove  coarse,  unslaked  parti- 
cles and  then  added  to  the  sugar  solution. 
The  mixture  should  be  agitated  at  frequent 
intervals,  and  after  two  or  three  hours  al- 
lowed to  settle  until  the  clear  supernatant 
fluid  can  be  siphoned  off.  The  clear  liquid 
(viscogen)  should  be  kept  in  well-stoppered 
bottles,  which  are  filled  full.  (The  liqtiid 
absorbs  carbonic  acid  from  the  air,  thus 
reducing  its  strength  and  darkening  the 
color).  The  quantity  of  viscogen,  to  be 
added  to  the  cream,  is  usually  about  one 
part  to  150  parts  of  cream.  Enough  is 
added  to  neutrahze  the  acid  in  the  cream. 
A  practical  means  of  adding  the  right  quan- 
tity is  to  determine  the  quantity  of  viscogen 
recpiired  to  neutralize  a  small,  measured 
quantity  of  cream,  and  then  calculate  the 
quantity  of  viscogen  rc(|uired  for  the  whole 
amount  of  cream.      The  neutral  point  of  the 

341 


342 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


cream  is  shown  by  a  little  phenolphthalein 
solution;  a  drop  of  it  mixed  with  a  drop  of 
the  cream  should  give  a  pink  color,  which 
quickly  disappears;  if  the  pink  color  is  per- 
manent, add  cream,  by  measure,  till  the  pro- 
portions are  correct. 

"In  adding  the  viscogen  to  the  cream,  it 
should  be  poured  into  the  cream  slowly, 
and  the  mixture  should  be  stirred  constantly 
meanwhile.  The  pasteurized  cream  should 
be  cooled  to  a  point  below  60**  F.  before 
adding  the  viscogen.  It  is  believed  that 
any  physiological  effect  from  this  small 
quantity  of  lime  would  be  beneficial  rather 
than  injurious.  In  its  practical  application 
viscogen  may  be  used  for  the  following 
purposes: 

*'(i)  To  restore  the  consistency  of 
pasteurized  cream,  for  which  use  it  stands 
pre-eminent. 

"  (2)  To  increase  the  body  of  separa- 
tor cream,  and  so  overcome  the  objection 
urged  against  this  in  comparison  with  gravity 
cream. 

*'  (3)  To  increase  the  viscosity  of  cream 
designed  for  whipping,  for  which  it  is  highly 
recommended,  inasmuch  as  the  kind  of 
consistency  imparted  enables  one  to  whip 
cream  at  temperatures  that  would  otherwise 
be  xmpossible. 

''  (4)  To  give  greater  body  to  con- 
densed milk,  where  the  method  of  prepara- 
tion does  not  interfere  with  its  use." 


into  a  serving  dish.  While  the  pudding  is 
cooking,  stir  and  cook  a  cup  of  sugar  until 
it  becomes  caramel;  do  not  get  it  too  brown. 
Add  a  cup  of  water  and  let  cook  to  a  thick 
syrup.    Use  hot  as  a  sauce  for  the  pudding. 


Query  1316. —  M.  F.  A.,  Minneapolis,  Minn.: 
"  Recipe  for  a  Banana-Caramel  Pudding.  The 
pudding  was  served  hot,  g,nd  was  evidently  custard 
with  some  caramel  feature  that  made  it  delicious." 

Banana-Caramel  Pudding 
Mix  one-third  a  cup  of  cornstarch  with  a 
little  cold  milk;  put  the  rest  of  a  quart  of 
milk  over  hot  water  to  scald.  When  the 
milk  is  scalded,  stir  the  cornstarch  into  it, 
and  continue  stirring  till  the  mixture  thick- 
ens, then  cover  and  let  cook  twenty  minutes, 
stirring  occasionally.  Beat  the  yolks  of  four 
eggs;  add  one-fourth  a  cup  of  sugar  and  half 
a  teaspoonful  of  salt  and  beat  again,  then 
stir  into  the  hot  mixture;  let  cook  until  the 
egg  is  set,  then  lightly  stir  in  four  or  five 
peeled  bananas,  cut  in  even  shces,  and  turn 


Query  1317.— Mrs.  R.  P.  R.,  Davenport, 
Iowa:  "  May  I  submit  to  you  this  recipe  for  a 
Caramel  Cake  for  correction.?  A  friend  of  mine 
makes  a  delicious  Caramel  Cake,  and,  when  I 
asked  for  the  recipe,  this  is  what  I  received.  Per- 
haps she  does  not  use  accurate  measurements, 
but  I  cannot  make  a  good  cake  with  these  direc- 
tions. Will  you  please  tell  through  the  Cooking- 
School  Magazine  what  the  trouble  is?  " 

Caramel  Cake 

One  cup  of  granulated  sugar,  caramelized. 
Remove  from  fire  and  add  one-half  a  cup  of 
boiling  water;  this  makes  the  syrup. 

Cake 

One-half  a  cup  ot  butter;  one-half  a  cup 
of  sugar;  yolks  of  two  eggs;  one  cup  of  water; 
two  cups  of  flour.  Beat  for  five  minutes; 
add  three  teaspoonfuls  of  caramel,  one  tea- 
spoonful  of  vanilla,  and  another  one-half  a 
cup  of  flour.  Beat  again  and  add  two  tea- 
spoonfuls  of  baking  powder  and  the  well- 
beaten  whites  of  the  eggs.  Bake  in  two 
layers. 

Filling 

A  boiled  icing,  to  which  add  two  tea- 
spoonfuls  of  caramel  and  one  teaspoonful  of 
vanilla  when  ready  to  spread. 

Trouble  with  Caramel  Cake 

There  does  not  seem  to  be  sweetening 
enough  in  the  cake.  Twice  the  measure 
of  butter  in  sugar  does  not  seem  too  much. 
The  following  recipe,  sent  to  us  from  Kan- 
sas City,  seems  to  be  for  a  similar  cake  and 
the  proportions  are  probably  more  accurate. 

Burnt  Sugar  Cake 

Put  a  cup  of  sugar  in  a  frying-pan,  add- 
ing nothing  to  it;  cook  the  sugar  stirring  con- 
stantly until  it  melts  and  becomes  a  brown 
color.  Do  not  let  the  sugar  blacken  in  the 
least.  Stop  the  cooking,  when  it  is  a  little 
less  than  a  chestnut  color.      Add  one  cup 


QUERIES  AND  ANSWERS 


343 


of  water  and  let  the  mixture  boil  to  a  syrup, 
then  store  in  a  bottle  for  use  as  needed. 

Beat  half  a  cup  of  butter  to  a  cream; 
gradually  beat  into  it  one  cup  of  sugar.  Beat 
the  yolks  of  three  eggs;  gradually  beat  in 
half  a  cup  of  sugar  and  beat  into  the  sugar 
and  butter.  Sift  together  two  cups  of  flour 
and  two  level  teaspoonfuls  of  baking  powder; 
beat  the  flour  mixture  into  the  first  mixture, 
alternately,  with  one  cup  of  cold  water  and 
three  teaspoonfuls  of  the  burnt  sugar  (from 
the  bottle).  Lastly,  beat  in  the  whites  of 
two  eggs,  beaten  dry. 

Frosting  for  Burnt  Sugar  Cake 

Melt  one  cup  of  sugar  in  one-third  a  cup 
of  water;  add  two  teaspoonfuls  of  burnt 
sugar  and  let  boil  until  the  syrup  spins  a 
thread  two  or  three  inches  long,  then  grad- 
ually beat  it  into  the  white  of  an  egg,  beaten 
dry.  Flavor  with  one  teaspoonful  of  vanilla 
extract.  When  cool  and  firm  enough  to 
hold  its  shaoe  spread  upon  the  cake. 


Query    1318. —  Mrs.  Wm.  B.,  Coshocton,  0.: 
'Recipe  for  Chess  Pie." 

Chess  Pie  (Mrs.  Henderson) 

For  two  pies  take  five  eggs,  three-fourths 
a  cup  of  butter,  one  cup  of  sugar  and  flavor- 
ing. Beat  the  yolks  of  the  eggs  until  very 
light,  then  gradually  beat  in  the  sugar. 
Beat  the  butter  to  a  cream,  and  then  grad- 
ually beat  into  it  the  yolks  and  sugar. 
Flavor  with  vanilla.  Bake  it  in  a  crust;  it 
will  rise  very  light.  Have  ready  the  whites 
of  the  eggs,  beaten  dry;  gradually  beat  in 
five  level  tablespoonfuls  of  sugar,  and  then 
cut  and  fold  in  five  level  tablespoonfuls  of 
sugar.  Flavor  with  a  few  drops  of  the 
vanilla.  Spread  the  meringue  over  the 
pies  and  set  them  into  the  oven  to  cook  the 
meringue.  It  should  take  about  ten  min- 
utes. It  is  said  that  the  best  results  are 
secured,  if  the  pies  be  cut  while  hot. 

Chess  Custard  (Aunt  Maggie) 

Cream  two  level  tablespoonfuls  of  butter; 
gradually  beat  into  it  half  a  cup  of  sugar, 
then  the  beaten  yolks  of  two  eggs,  a  tea- 
spoonful  of  vanilla  and  a  generous  half-cup 


of  milk.  Turn  the  mixture  into  a  baking 
dish  of  suitable  size  and  bake,  with  the 
dish  set  in  water  just  below  the  boiling 
point,  until  firm  in  the  center.  Beat  the 
whites  of  two  eggs  to  a  stiff  froth,  then 
gradually  beat  in  two  level  tablespoonfuls 
of  sugar;  then  cut  and  fold  in  two  level 
tablespoonfuls  of  sugar  and  spread  the  mix- 
ture over  the  custard;  set  the  custard  into 
the  oven  to  remain  eight  or  ten  minutes, 
when  the  meringue  should  be  cooked  and 
delicately  browned.  Serve  either  hot  or 
cold,  preferably  cold.  This  will  serve  four 
persons  as  it  is  very  rich.  As  the  meringue 
above  the  custard  is  sweet,  the  quantity  of 
sugar  in  the  custard  might  be  cut  down  to 
advantage. 


Query  13 19.—  Mrs.  J.  S.  B.,  Boston  :  "  Recipes 
for  Brownies  and  Golden  Rod  Cake  for'  Waldorf 
Triangles." 

Brownies 

Beat  one-third  a  cup  of  butter  to  a  cream; 
gradually  beat  in  one-third  a  cup  of  sugar, 
one  egg  beaten  without  separating  the  white 
and  yolk,  then  one-third  a  cup  of  molasses. 
Lastly,  beat  in  one  cup  of  sifted  flour  and 
one  cup  of  nut  meats  (pecans  or  walnuts) 
broken  in  pieces.  Bake  in  small  cake  tins. 
Press  half  a  nut  meat  into  the  top  of  the 
mixture  in  each  tin,  before  baking.  Small 
tins  are  sold  specially  for  these  little  cakes. 
Eighteen  tins  are  needed. 

Golden  Rod  Cake  for  Waldorf 
Triangles 
Beat  the  yolks  of  six  eggs  very  light; 
gradually  beat  into  these  half  a  cup  of  sugar, 
then  two  tables-poonfuls  of  milk  or  orange 
juice,  and,  lastly,  half  a  cup  of  sifted  flour, 
sifted  again  with  a  level  teaspoonful  of  bak- 
ing powder  and  a  few  grains  of  salt.  Flavor 
with  a  grating  of  orange  rind  or  half  a  tea- 
spoonful of  vanilla.  This  will  make  one 
dozen  triangles.  The  oven  should  be  hotter 
than  for  the  usual  loaf  of  sponge  cake.  The 
cakes  are  finer-grained  and  more  tender 
than  sponge  cake.  Turn  the  cakes  from 
the  tins  as  soon  as  they  are  removed  from 
the  oven.      When  cold   cover  the  sloping 


344 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


sides  with  boiled  frosting  and  then  sprinkle 
the  frosting  with  fine-chopped  pistachio 
nuts.  A  dozen  nuts  are  needed.  The 
recipes  for  both  Brownies  and  Waldorf  Tri- 
angles may  be  found  among  the  cake  recipes 
in  '^Practical  Cooking  and  Serving." 


Query  1320. —  M.  S.,  Roe,  Arkansas  :"  Recipes 
for  making  Graham  Bread  and  Whole  Wheat 
Bread." 

Graham   Bread 

Soften  one-third  a  cake  of  compressed 
yeast  in  half  a  cup  of  water;  add  a  cup 
and  one-fourth  of  scalded-and-cooled  milk, 
two  tablespoonfuls  of  butter,  a  teaspoonful 
of  salt  and  one-fourth  a  cup  of  molasses. 
Stir  in  two  cups  and  one-half  of  graham 
flour  and  one  cup  and  a  half  of  white  flour. 
Mix  very  thoroughly.  The  dough  should 
not  be  quite  firm  enough  to  knead.  Cover 
and  let  stand  overnight.  In  the  morning 
cut  down  and  turn  over  and  over  with  a 
knife  and  turn  into  butttered  bread  pans. 
Make  smooth  with  a  knife  and  let  become 
light.      Bake  about  an  hour. 

Entire- Wheat  Bread 

In  making  any  kind  of  bread,  the  pro- 
portions of  flour  and  liquid  are  as  two  and 
a  half  or  three  to  one.  It  makes  no  differ- 
ence whether  the  bread  be  mixed  by  hand 
or  in  one  of  the  patented  bread-mixers, 
the  proportions  are  the  same.  Whether 
two  and  one-half  parts  or  more  of  flour  be 
used  depends  entirely  upon  the  brand  of 
flour  and  individual  taste.  Equally  good 
results  may  be  obtained  with  brewer' s  liquid 
yeast,  compressed  or  dry  yeast.  Any  yeast 
bread,  to  be  healthful,  needs  be  light  and 
sufficiently  baked  to  kill  the  yeast  plants 
that  have  been  the  occasion  of  making  it 
light.  The  formula  for  either  whole-wheat 
or  white  bread  is:  two  cups  of  lukewarm 
liquid  (scalded-and-cooled  milk,  or  milk 
and  water,  half  and  half) ,  two  level  table- 
spoonfuls  of  sugar,  two  level  tablespoonfuls 
of  shortening,  if  desired,  one  teaspoonful 
of  salt,  one  or  more  cakes  of  compressed 
yeast,  softened  in  half  a  cup  of  lukewarm 
water,  or  half  a  cup  of  brewer's  yeast,  and 


from  two  and  one-half  to  three  times  the 
measure  of  liquid  in  flour,  /.  e. ,  two  and 
one-half  cups  of  liquid,  and  from  six  and 
one-fourth  to  seven  and  one-half  cups  of 
flour.  Melt  the  butter  in  the  liquid.  Add 
sugar  and  salt,  and,  when  lukewarm,  the 
yeast;  mix  thoroughly,  then  add  all  the  flour 
and  mix  to  a  dough.  Knead  until  elastic, 
then  set  to  rise  until  nearly  doubled  in  bulk; 
cut  down  and  shape  into  two  loaves.  Bake, 
when  again  light,  about  one  hour. 

To  make  the  bread  overnight  use  one- 
third  a  cake  of  compressed  yeast.  If  the 
dough  be  mixed  about  seven  o'clock  in  the 
morning,  and  a  whole  yeast  cake  be  used, 
the  bread  should  be  baked  in  the  early 
afternoon.  Whole  wheat  bread  is  of  the 
best  texture,  when  one-third  to  one-half  of 
the  flour  used  be  white  flour. 


Query  1321. —  Mrs.  M.  H.,  Detroit,  Michigan: 
"Recipe  for  Chicken  Marengo.  What  kind  of 
salad  is  best  to  serve  with  roast  turkey?  In 
making  Mayonnaise  Dressing,  which  is  added  first, 
the  vinegar  or  the  oil  ?  " 

Regarding  Mayonnaise  Dressing 

In  making  mayonnaise  dressing  select 
fresh  eggs;  this  is  important;  beat  in  the 
condiments,  then  beat  in,  gradually,  all  the 
vinegar  that  is  to  be  used;  put  an  tgg  beater 
into  the  mixture  and  beat  in  the  oil  by  the 
teaspoonful.  After  a  Httle  time  the  oil 
may  be  added  by  the  tablespoonful.  The 
egg  beater  must  be  used  vigorously. 

Salad  with  Roast  Turkey 

With  roast  turkey,  cress,  endive,  ro- 
maine  or  other  variety  of  lettuce,  or  celery, 
with  French  dressing,  are  appropriate. 
Tomatoes  with  French  dressing  are  also 
particularly  good,  though  not  often  seen, 
as  the  two  are  not  in  season  at  the  same 
time.  Apple  or  pineapple  with  celery  and 
French  dressing  are  also  good  with  roast 
turkey.  As  cranberry  sauce  usually  ac- 
companies roast  turkey,  the  fruit  in  the 
salad  would  seem  superfluous  unless  pre- 
sented to  give  a  choice  of  dishes.

Mother's  Best  Helper 


By  MARY  JANE  McCLURE. 

^  "Children  and  chickens  must  always  he  picking"— thus  runs  a 
wise  old  Danish  saying.  The  plump,  pinky  fists  of  the  "Darling 
of  the  Gods"  never  feel  easy  unless  they  are  delving  into  the 
depths  of  dishes  in  search  of  novel  experiences.  Her  busy, 
budding  brain  evolves  fanciful  fairy  lore  out  of  kitchen  common- 
places. Intent  upon  being  "Mother's  best  helper,"  the  wee- 
woman  swings,  pendulum-like,  between  a  help  and  a  hindrance. 
Pretty,  coaxing  infant  wiles,  however,  cover  a  multitude  of  sins. 
The  infatuated  mother  prizes  the  child's  co-operation.  To  her 
glamored  vision  a  rosy  radiance  hovers  around  the  tiny,  restless 
fingers. 


^  Children  are  angels  whose  wings  decrease  in  size 
as  their  legs  increase  in  length  and  strength.  There 
comes  a  time  when  the  kitchen  holds  no  lure  for  the 
growing  girl— when  the  mother  finds  that  she  must 
depend  upon  other  help  to  lighten  and  brighten  her 
culinary  labors.  She  rebels  against  spending  all  the 
time  "from  dawn  to  sunset's  marge,"  extravagantly 
consuming  the  plus  of  her  vitality  in  planning  and 
cooking  meals.  That  is  a  fetich 
of  the  past  before  which  the 
Twentieth  Century  housewife  re- 
fuses to  bow  and  cringe. 
^  Then  commences  a  ceaseless 
quest  for  time  and  labor  econo- 
mizers. After  many  fruitless  ex- 
periments the  panacea  for  all 
kitchen  ills  —  "Mother's  Best 
Helper" — is  found  in  the  little  jar 
of  Beef  Extract  —  provided  it  is 
Armour's. 

^  There  may  appear  to  be  beef 
extract  and  beef  extract— but  in 
the  last  analysis  the  only  one 
that  stands  the  test  is  Armour's. 
I  speak  from  experience. 
Armour's  Extract  of  Beef  has  so 
many  points  of  vantage  that  a 
conscientious  cook  quickly  aban- 
dons all  other  brands  in  its  favor. 
Only  the  finest  quality  of  fresh 
beef  is  used  in  making  it.  This 
renders  Armour's  more  delicious- 
ly  tempting  and  stronger  than 
any  other  beef  extract — and  more 
economical  because  less  of  it  is 
required  to  produce  desired  re- 
sults. 

^  Soup-making,  sauce-cooking,  flavoring  and  season- 
ing all  evolve  out  of  the  experimental  stage  into 
scientific  safety  when  Armour's  Extract  of  Beef  is 
used  as  a  basic  constituent.  It  provides  a  precision, 
an  absolute  certainty  as  to  the  outcome,  which  is 
gratifying  to  a  hurried,  flurried  housewife.  It  is 
invaluable    as    a  seasoning   for    vegetable,   egg  and 


cheese  dishes  and  for  restoring  the  pristine  prime- 
beef  flavor  to  cooked-over  meats.  It  gives  the 
French  and  Italian  piquancy  and  zest  to  the  simplest 
dishes,  which  so  many  American  cooks  desire  to 
accomplish  and  so  few  attain. 

^  Some  people  labor  under  an  impression  that  the 
range  of  soup  dishes  covered  by  Extract  of  Beef  is 
extremely  limited.  Armour's  Extract  of  Beef  may 
be  substituted  in  any  recipe  in 
place  of  stock.  It  also  is  well 
to  bear  in  mind"  that,  in  this 
case,  the  Extract  contains  all 
the  fine  flavor  of  the  meat  with 
none  of  the  waste  ordinarily  at- 
tendant upon  the  manufacture  of 
stock. 

^  The  following  is  a  dainty  soup 
conception.      Try    it    for    dinner 
_  tonight.     It  will  prove  a  pleasant 
surprise: 

Royal  Bisque  a  la  Creme 

1  can  of  tomatoes, 
1  stalk  of  ceiery, 

1  bay  leaf, 
H  medium  sized  onion,  cut  up  fine, 

2  tablespoonfuls  of  butter, 
2  tablespoonfuls  of  browned  flour, 
1  teaspoonful  of  allspice, 

1  teaspoonful  of  sugar, 
pepper  and  salt  to  taste, 

2  cupfuls  of  water, 
14  teaspoonful    of    Armour's    Extract   of 

Beef. 

^  Fry  the  onion  in  the  butter  and 
add  to  it  the  celery,  bay  leaf,  and 
water.  When  thoroughly  cookec^ 
strain  through  a  sieve  and  add  the 
other  ingredients.  Let  it  boil  a 
few  minutes,  then  strain  it  again  through  a  sieve. 
Return  to  the  fire  to  heat.  Serve  it  with  whipped 
cream  heaped  on  top  of  each  bouillon  cup.  If 
whipped  cream  is  not  available,  pass  around  rich 
cream  along  with  it,  which  may  be  poured  into  the 
soup,  just  as  cream  is  used  in  coffee  or  tea.  to  suit 
the  taste  of  those  who  partake  of  it.

THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOT  MAGAZINE 


pieces  at  the  joints.  Roll  the  pieces  in 
flour  into  which  a  little  salt  and  pepper 
have  been  added.  Have  ready  in  a  frying 
pan  some  fat  tried  out  of  salt  pork.  There 
should  be  fat  enough  to  cover  over  the 
bottom  of  the  pan.  Put  the  pieces  of 
chicken  into  the  hot  fat  and  cook  until 
browned  on  one  side,  then  turn  them  to 
brown  the  other  side.  While  the  chicken 
is  browning  cook  a  tablespoonful,  each,  of 
fine-chopped  onion,  carrot  and  celery  in 
three  or  four  tablespoonfuls  of  butter  or 
salt  pork  fat;  when  the  vegetables  are 
browned  somewhat,  add  one-fourth  a  cup 
of  flour  and  stir  until  it  is  browned,  then 
add  a  cup  of  cooked  tomato,  strained,  and 
nearly  two  cups  of  water  with  a  teaspoon- 
ful  of  salt  and  half  a  teaspoonful  of  paprika; 
stir  and  cook  until  the  sauce  boils,  then 
strain  it  over  the  pieces  of  chicken  disposed 
in  a  saucepan;  cover  closely  and  let  simmer 
until  the  chicken  is  tender.  Add  half  a 
can  of  mushrooms,  cut  in  halves.  Let 
stand  five  minutes  (without  boiling)  to 
heat  the  mushrooms,  then  dispose  the 
whole  on  a  serving  dish. 


Query  1322. —  A.  W.  G.,  Lewiston,  Me.: 
"  Recipe  for  preparing  Bar-le-duc  currants  and 
gooseberries." 

Take  selected  gooseberries  or  currants 
of  large  size,  one  by  one,  and  with  a  pair 
of  tiny  embroidery  scissors  carefully  cut 
the  skin  on  one  side,  making  a  slit  of  per- 
haps one-fourth  an  inch.  Through  this, 
with  a  sharp  needle,  remove  the  seeds, 
one  at  a  time,  to  preserve  the  shape  of  the 
fruit.  Take  the  weight  of  the  fruit  in 
strained  honey,  and,  when  hot,  add  the 
prepared  fruit.  Let  simmer  three  or  four 
minutes.  Carefully  skim  out  the  fruit. 
Reduce  the  syrup,  at  a  gentle  simmer,  to  the 
desired  consistency.      Then  store  as  jelly. 


Query  1323.  —  Mrs.  C.  D.  L.:     "Recipes  for 
Onion  Soup  au  Gratin  and  Mayonnaise  Dressing." 

Onion  Soup  au  Gratin  or  Onion 
Soup  in  Petites  Marmites 

Melt  half  a  cup  of  butter;  in  it  cook  four 
large  white  onions,  sliced  fine;  stir  and  cook 


the  onions  until  they  are  softened  and  yel- 
lowed, then  add  three  or  four  sprigs  of 
parsley,  two  quarts  of  rich  beef  broth  and 
a  pint  of  water  and  let  simmer  twenty  min- 
utes. Have  ready  eight  petites  marmites; 
into  each  of  these  put  three  round  slices  of 
French  bread  freed  from  crust,  browned  in 
the  oven,  or  toasted,  and  then  sprinkled 
with  grated  Parmesan  cheese.  Strain  the 
soup  over  the  toast,  which  will  rise  to  the 
top  of  the  marmites;  again  sprinkle  the  toast 
with  grated  cheese  and  set  the  marmites 
into  the  oven,  to  melt  the  cheese  and  brown 
it  slightly.  The  soup  is  now  ready  to  send 
to  the  table  in  the  little  vessels.  If  pre- 
ferred, pick  out  the  parsley  and  leave  the 
onion  in  the  soup. 


"  Coffee  Grunters  " 

Ever  See  One  ? 

Thoughtful  people  have  a  laugh  on  coffee 
cranks  now  and  then. 

'^I  had  used  coffee  ever  since  I  was  a 
small  child,"  writes  an  Ind.  lady,  ''and 
have  always  had  bad  spells  with  my 
stomach. 

''  Last  spring,  just  after  I  began  house- 
keeping, I  had  a  terrible  time  with  my 
stomach  and  head.  My  husband  bought 
a  package  of  Postum  and  asked  me  to  try 
it. 

"I  laughed  at  it  because  none  of  my 
folks  would  ever  try  it.  But  I  made  some 
the  following  morning,  following  directions 
on  the  package,  about  boiling  it  well. 

' '  I  was  greatly  pleased  with  the  results 
and  kept  right  on  using  it.  Now  I 
would  n'  t  drink  anything  else.  I  tell  every 
old  coffee  "grunter,"  I  see,  about  Postum, 
and  all  my  folks  and  my  husband' s  people, 
except  a  few  cranks,  use  Postum  instead  of 
Cofl'ee. 

' '  When  put  to  soak  in  cold  water  over 
night  and  then  boiled  15  minutes  in  the 
morning  while  getting  breakfast  it  makes  a 
delicious  drink." 

Name  given  by  Postum  Co.,  Battle 
Creek,  Mich.  Read  ''The  Road  to  Well- 
ville,"  in  pkgs.      "There's  a  Reason."

Mayonnaise   Dressing 

Mix  half  a  teaspoonful  of  salt  and  a  few 
grains  of  cayenne  or  paprika;  add  the  yolks 
of  two  fresh  eggs  and  beat  thoroughly;  beat 
until  the  yolks  are  well-thickened,  then 
gradually  beat  in  four  tablespoonfuls  of  vine- 
gar or  lemon  juice,  or  half  of  each.  Use  a 
Dover  egg  beater  and  beat  in  one  pint  of 
olive  oil.  At  first  add  the  oil  a  teaspoon- 
ful at  a  time,  beating  in  each  teaspoonful 
thoroughly  before  the  next  is  added.  After 
a  time  the  oil  may  be  added  by  the  table- 
spoonful.  A  small  quantity,  from  one- 
fourth  to  one-half  a  teaspoonful  of  sugar  or 
mustard,  may  be  added  to  the  salt  and 
pepper. 


The  Professor —  ''Some  of  you  gentle- 
men are  not  giving  me  your  closest  atten- 
tion. Mr.  Briggs,  what  do  we  find  under 
the  kidneys  ?  ' ' 

Future  M.  D.  —  "Toast,  sir." 


Query  1324.  A.  M.  B.,  Berlin,Ontario,  Canada, 
"Recipes  for  Chili  con  Carne  and  for  cooking 
hominy." 

Chili  con  Carne 

To  prepare  the  chih  used  in  this  dish: 
from  two  pods  of  dried,  red,  chili  peppers, 
take  out  all  the  seeds  and  discard  them. 
Soak  the  pods  in  warm  water  until  they  are 
soft,  then  scrape  the  pulp  from  the  skins 
into  the  water,  discarding  the  skins  and 
saving  the  pulp  and  water.  Cut  two  pounds 
of  round  steak  into  small  pieces  and  cook 
them  in  a  hot  frying  pan,  in  melted  butter, 
or  dripping,  until  well  browned;  add  three 
or  four  tablespoonfuls  of  flour  and  stir  until 
browned,  then  add  a  clove  of  garlic,  in  which 
two  gashes  have  been  cut,  and  the  chih 
water,  of  which  there  should  be  about  a 
pint;  let  simmer  until  the  meat  is  tender, 
(about  two  hours)  adding  hot  water  if 
needed.  When  done  the  sauce  should  be 
of  a  good  consistency;  add  salt  to  season. 

Hominy 

Put  three  cups  of  boiling  water  and  a 
teaspoonful  of  salt  over  the  fire  in  the  upper 
part  of  a  double  boiler;  when  the  water 
boils  again,  stir  vigorously  while  slowly 
shaking  in  one  cup  of  hominy.  Let  the 
hominy  boil  eight  or  ten  minutes,  stirring 
occasionally,  then  set  into  boiling  water, 
cover  and  let  cook  about  one  hour.

Cubs'  Food 

They  Thrive  on  Grape-Nuts 

Healthy  babies  don't  cry  and  the  weU- 
nourished  baby  that  is  fed  on  Grape- Nuts 
is  never  a  crying  baby.  Many  babies  who 
cannot  take  any  other  food  relish  the  per- 
fect food.  Grape- Nuts,  and  get  well. 

"  My  httle  baby  was  given  up  by  three 
doctors  who  said  that  the  condensed  milk 
on  which  I  had  fed  her  had  ruined  the 
child's  stomach.  One  of  the  doctors  told 
me  that  the  only  thing  to  do  would  be  to 
try  Grape- Nuts,  so  I  got  some  and  pre- 
pared it  as  follows:  I  soaked  i  1-2  table- 
spoonfuls  in  one  pint  of  cold  water  for  half 
an  hour,  then  I  strained  off  the  liquid  and 
mixed  12  teaspoonfuls  of  this  strained 
Grape- Nuts  juice  with  six  teaspoonfuls  of 
rich  milk,  put  in  a  pinch  of  salt  and  a  little 
sugar,  warmed  it  and  gave  it  to  baby  every 
two  hours. 

"In  this  simple,  easy  way  I  saved  baby's 
life  and  have  built  her  up  to  a  strong, 
healthy  child,  rosy  and  laughing.  The 
food  must  certainly  be  perfect  to  have  such 
a  wonderful  effect  as  this.  I  can  truthfully 
say  I  think  it  is  the  best  food  in  the  world 
to  raise  delicate  babies  on  and  is  also  a  de- 
licious healthful  food  for  grown-ups  as  we 
have  discovered  in  our  family. ' ' 

Grape-Nuts  is  equally  valuable  to  the 
strong,  healthy  man  or  woman.  It  stands 
for  the  true  theory  of  health.  "There's 
a  Reason. ' '  Read  ' '  The  Road  to  Well- 
ville, ' '  in  pkgs.

Book  Reviews 


The  Nutrition  of  Man.  By  Russell 
H.  Chittenden,  Ph.D.,  LL.D.,  Sc.D. 
Price  ^3. 00,  net;  postpaid,  ^3.22.  New 
York:  Frederick  A.  Stokes  Company. 
This  is  the  latest  word  of  science  on  the 
subject  of  nutrition.  It  has  immediate 
practical  value  for  the  layman,  in  showing 
the  truths,  half-truths,  and  errors  of  the 
many  dietetic  doctrines  now  in  vogue. 
Never  have  such  thorough  and  convincing 
experiments  been  made  in  this  field  as 
those  of  Prof.  Chittenden  and  his  colleagues 
at  Yale.  They  have  been  undertaken 
without  bias,  and  remarkable  as  are  the 
conclusions  drawn  by  Prof.  Chittenden, 
further  research  is  not  likely  to  shake  them. 
The  processes  of  the  digestive  tract,  and 
the  chemistry  of  assimilation;  the  combus- 
tion of  food  in  the  body,  and  its  transform- 
ation into  energy;  the  balance  of  nutrition, 
and  the  prevalence  and  results  of  over- 
feeding; accounts  of  experiments  upon 
men  and  animals,  showing  the  results  of 
vegetable,  meat,  and  other  diets,  are  some 
of  the  principal  subjects  of  this  work. 

A  notable  feature  of  the  book  is  16  half- 
tone illustrations  showing  athletes  and 
United  States  soldiers  who  were  the  sub- 
jects of  these  experiments  and  should, 
according  to  ordinary  theories,  have  been 
half  starved.  A  wonderful  revelation  of 
the  real  dietary  needs  of  man,  the  product 
of  scientific  research  and  experiment  ii_  a 
scientific  age,  it  marks  the  tendency  of  the 
day  towards  right  methods  of  living. 


Little  Dinners  with  the  Sphinx.      By 

Richard  LeGalliene.     Price,  ^1.25,  net. 

New  York:  Moffat,  Yard  &  Co. 

Ten   papers,  well-styled   prose    fancies, 

make  up  the  contents  of  this  book.      The 

Butterfly  of  Dreams,  My  Castle  in  Spain, 

What '  s  in  a  Name,  The  Dream  Documents, 

are    suggestive    of    the    subjects    treated. 

For  the  quiet,  restful  hour,  the  reader  will 

find  in  these  chapters  light  and  pleasing 

entertainment,  entirely  free  from  excitement 

or  mental  strain. 


Of  new  books,  Hamilton  W.  Mabie  writes 
in  the  Ladies'  Home  Journal : 

' '  Several  lives  of  Tennyson  have  appeared 
during  the  past  few  years,  none  probably 
more  satisfactory  to  the  general  reader  than 
the  study  which  has  recently  come  from  the 
hand  of  Mr.  A.  C.  Bensoij,  the  well-known 
essayist.  In  this  book  Mr.  Benson  gives 
a  simple  narrative  of  the  life  of  Tennyson, 
with  a  study  of  his  temperament,  character, 
ideals  and  beliefs.  So  far  as  possible  he 
presents  from  the  poet's  own  words  his  view 
of  the  poetic  life  and  character,  and  char- 
acterizes the  qualities  of  his  art  from  the 
technical  point  of  view.  There  have  been 
books  more  definitely  critical,  and  there 
are  several  much  more  complete  biographies, 
especially  that  by  the  present  Lord  Ten- 
nyson; but  no  more  readable  and  con- 
venient than  this. 

Everything  relating  to  Abraham  Lincoln 
is  intensely  interesting,  and  Mr.  D.  H. 
Bates'  volume  of  incidents  and  anecdotes, 
connected  with  the  President's  daily  visit 
to  the  War  Department  telegraphic  office 
during  the  most  trying  days  of  the  Civil 
War,  under  the  title,  ''Lincoln  in  the  Tel- 
egraph Office, ' '  will  take  its  place  among 
the  books  that  throw  light  upon  the  man. 
The  book  is  necessarily  sketchy,  and  is  not 
in  any  way  a  continuous  history;  but  it  is 
a  valuable  footnote  to  the  history  of  the 
time,  and  it  helps  the  reader  to  gain  a 
clearer  idea  of  a  unique  character  and  per- 
sonality. 

Very  few  readers  of  Mr.  Owen  Wister's 
' '  Seven  Ages  of  Washington ' '  will  realize 
how  much  work  hes  behind  it;  what  a 
great  amount  of  material  has  been  sifted  in 
order  that  a  group  of  authoritative  histor- 
ical impressions  could  be  gathered;  and 
how  much  has  been  rejected  that  the  por- 
trait of  a  great  man  might  stand  out  with 
perfect  clearness.  Nothing  written  about 
Washington  has  been  more  interesting  and 
illuminating  than  this  book  of  very  moder- 
ate size,  in  which  the  man  descends  from 
the  pedestal  so  laboriously  raised  for  him,

The  New  Patriotism 

{A.J.  Uaynes,  in  Social  and  Religious  Ideas.) 
The  old  patriotism  was  much  concerned 
with  guns  arid  flags  and  all  the  parapher- 
nalia of  war.  The  new  patriotism  is  to  be  a 
thing  of  schools,  hospitals  and  churches  and 
mission  halls  for  all  who  feel  the  extraor- 
dinary burdens  of  life.  The  new  patri- 
otism will  concern  itself  with  clean  streets 
and  weU-built  houses;  it  will  demand  that 
the  rich  be  satisfied  with  less  and  that  the 
poor  have  more;  it  wih  not  be  so  spectac- 
ular, but  it  will  be  more  real,  more  vitally 
related  with  the  raw  needs  of  human  life. 
The  day  of  the  hero  on  horseback  is  past; 
the  day  of  the  hero  in  the  bonds  of  civio 
self-sacrifice  has  come. 


Evolution 

I  ate  me  a  Welsh  rabbit 
In  the  night  last  past; 

I  ate  me  a  Welsh  rabbit 
Whereby  to  stay  my  fast; 
Simply  a  Welsh  rabbit, 
A  harmless,  armless  thing, 
With  not  a  leg  to  stand  on, 
Nor  voice  to  speak  or  sing. 

I  ate  me  a  Welsh  rabbit. 
Then  hied  myself  away 
To  bed  and  dreams  and  wishing 
'Twere  longer  yet  till  day; 
Simply  a  Welsh  rabbit, 
A  wileless,  guileless  beast 
That  hath  no  other  mission 
Than  serving  for  a  feast. 

I  ate  me  a  Welsh  rabbit, 

Gadzooks  !     I  thought  it  so; 

But  after  I  had  gone  to  sleep, 
How  quickly  did  it  grow. 

Into  the  strangest  creatures  — 
Into  the  mares  of  night, 
Into  the  gibberish  monkeys, 


Into  the  shapes  that  fright. 

Into  the  ring-tailed  roosters, 

Into  the  jabberwocks. 

Into  the  jangling  jaguars. 

Into  the  six-horned  ox. 

Into  the  horse  with  flippers. 

Into  the  hog  with  wings. 

Into  the  cat  with  feathers. 

Into  the  cow  that  sings  — 

Into  all  manner  of  creatures 

Of  the  earth  and  the  air  and  the  sea, 

And  all  of  them  promenading 

Or  sitting  around  on  me. 

• 

I  ate  me  a  Welsh  rabbit 
In  the  night  last  past; 
I  ate  me  a  Welsh  rabbit 
Whereby  to  stay  my  fast; 
Simply  a  Welsh  rabbit  — 
How  could  there  possibly  be 
In  a  little  thing  hke  that 
A  whole  menagerie  ? 

William  J.  Lampton

Congress  of  Mothers 

Besides  opening  the  White  House  to  the 
first  International  Congress  of  Mothers,  to 
be  held  in  Washington  on  March  lo, 
President  Roosevelt  has  placed  the  ma- 
chinery of  the  office  of  secretary  of  state  at 
the  disposal  of  the  National  Congress  of 
Mothers,  so  that  there  shall  be  representa- 
tion from  every  country  to  which  we  send 
a  diplomat. 

Mrs.  Frederick  Schoff  of  Philadelphia, 
president  of  the  National  Congress  of 
Mothers,  said: 

''The  President  wants  this,  the  first  in- 
ternational congress  of  mothers  held  in 
America,  to  be  a  success.  He  has  not  only 
invited  the  delegates  to  come  to  the  White- 
House,  the  first  time  he  has  ever  thus 
honored  any  body  of  women,  but  he  wants 
it  to  be  a  success,  and  to  that  end  will  ad- 
dress the  women  on  some  topic  relating  to 
the  home  and  motherhood. 


Where  Animals  Beat  Men 

''Nature  faking  aside,"  said  John 
Lover,  the  Zoo  keeper,  "mice  won't 
eat  oleo.  It  is  a  fact.  Lay  a  pat  of 
oleo  and  a  pat  of  butter  side  by  side,  and 
in  the  morning  the  butter  will  be  gone, 
but  the  oleo  will  remain  untouched. 

"  Oh,  yes,  some  animals  are  incredibly 
nice  about  their  food.  The  otter,  when 
living  wild,  will  only  eat  one  piece,  one 
mouthful,  out  of  each  fish,  he  catches.  He 
will  land  a  beautiful  trout,  but  only  one 
bite  of  it,  from  the  back  just  behind  the 
neck,  is  good  enough  for  him.  The  rest 
he  tosses  aside.  This  epicure  often  kills  a 
dozen  fine  trout,  to  make  one  meal. 

"Chimpanzees  have  delicate  tastes.  A 
banana  or  a  pineapple  that  to  you  seems 
delicious,  to  a  chimpanzee,  may  be  revolt- 
ing. His  taste  is  keener.  Grapes  grown 
in  hothouses  where  sulphur  fumes  are 
used  as  an  insecticide  taste  all  right  to  'c 
man,  but  a  chimpanzee  will  have  none  of 
them. 

"The  ichneumon  loves  eggs.  He  can 
tell  a  fresh  from  a  stale  one  simply  by  tap  - 
ping  the  shell."

Whilst  You  're   Keeping    up   the 
Fire 

{By  Frank  L.  Stanton,  in  Uncle  Remus'  Magazine) 
I 

I  can, hear  the  old  man  sayin' — long  be- 
fore he  went  away 

To  the  calm,  untroubled  country  where 
the  wisest  of '  em  stay, 

That  plain,  ol' -fashioned  lesson  that  we 
took  to  heart  for  good: 

"Whilst  you're  keepin'  up  the  fire  you're 
a-burnin'  up  the  wood  ! ' ' 

II 

Sorter  set  us    all    to    thinkin'  —  as,   when 

harvest  came  along. 
An'  we  saw  the  prospect  brighten  like  a 

sunbeam  in  a  song; 
An'   the  silver  jingled  careless-like  —  that 

word  we  understood: 
"While  you  're  keepin'  up  the  fire  you  're 

a-burnin'  up  the  wood  !  " 

III 

"Dream    your    dreams  —  there   must   be 

dreamers  in  a  world  the  Lord  made 

bright  — 
The   dreamers    of  the    darkness    an'    the 

dreamers  of  the  hght; 
Of  the  sowin'   an'   the  reapin'  dreams  of 

glory  an'  of  good  — 
You  must  keep  the  fire  blazin'  but  you  're 

burnin'  up  the  wood  !  " 

IV 

Jest  that  ol'  -fashioned  lesson;  an'  I  'm  glad 

we  read  it  right, 
An'  felt  it's  deeper  meanin'  fore  he  said 

his  last  "Good-night;" 
For  where  the  heart  beats  truest   still   is 

where  it 's  understood: 
"Whilst  you  're  keepin'  up  the  fire  you  're 

a-burnin'  up  the  wood  ! ' '

tomb  is  especially  reserved  for  luncheons. "

Cooking  Bacon 

A  noted  hotel  chef  has  at  last  invented 
a  way  of  cooking  bacon,  that  most  delicious 
of  winter  breakfast  dishes,  so  that  it  is 
crisp,  without  losing  any  of  its  flavor:  SHce 
the  bacon  thin  —  thinner  than  ordinary,  if 
you  can  do  it.  Remove  the  rind,  of  course, 
and  lay  the  meat  upon  a  bed  of  cracked 
ice.  A  cold  platter,  or  even  a  dish  of 
snow  will  do  as  well,  if  you  do  not  take  ice 
in  the  winter  time.  When  it  is  thoroughly 
chilled  —  place  it  in  the  broiler,  and  brown 
it  nicely,  but  on  one  side  only.  Then  re- 
turn it  to  the  ice,  and  leave  it  until  it  has 
had  time  to  become  thoroughly  chilled 
once  more,  after  which  you  may  broil  the 
other  side  of  the  meat.      Serve  quickly. 


A  Scotch  minister  had  been  away  on  a 
vacation,  says  a  writer  in  Punch,  and  on 
his  return  asked  the  sexton  how  all  had 
gone  in  his  absence.  ' '  Very  well  indeed, ' ' 
was  the  cheering  response.  ''They  do 
say  that  most  meenisters  leave  some  one 
worse  than  themselves  to  fill  the  pulpit 
when  they  go  away  —  but  you  never  do 
that,  sir." 


The  founder  of  a  servantless  housekeep- 
ing plan  is  at  present  in  New  York  city  to 
see  if  Americans  will  take  kindly  to  his 
method  of  insuring  an  earthly  Paradise. 
Well,  if  New  York  does  not  approve,  Boston 
will  welcome  this  Mr.  Orto  Fick  with  open 
arms.  Let  him  come  here,  and  tell  us  all 
about  the  Copenhagen  flat-house  with  its 
co-operative  kitchen. 


Baked  Sardines 

REMOVE  the  skin  from  twelve  large 
sardines.  Put  them  in  a  dish  and  heat 
them  through.  Put  the  oil  from  the  sar- 
dines in  a  saucepan,  and  when  it  comes  to  a 
boil,  stir  in  one  cup  of  water,  then  add  one 
teaspoonful  of  Lea  &  Perrins'  Sauce,  salt 
and  pepper  to  taste.  Remove  from  the  fire 
and  stir  in  the  beaten  yolk  of  one  ^gg,  one 
teaspoonful  of  vinegar,  and  one  of  mustard. 
Serve  the  fish  very  hot,  with  this  sauce 
n^nr-"d  over  the^n  or  in  a  separate  dish.

Little  Giant 
Ice  Crusher 


Did  you  ever  stop  to  consider  the 
value  of  table  napkins  or  other  linen 
that  your  sei"vants  were  destroying 
by  crushing  ice  by  hand  ?  Have  you 
ever  thought  of  the  valuable  pieces 
of  china  and  glassware  that  are 
broken  ?  Have  you  ever  had  to  re- 
place the  zinc  lining  in  your  ice  box 
or  chest  on  account  of  your  servants 
having  chopped  it  to  pieces  ?  Have 
you  ever  stopped  to  consider  the 
amount  of  dirt  and  dust  that  can  col- 
lect in  a  box  in  a  month  ?  Certainly 
you  could  not  expect  clean  ice  when  broken  in  such  a  box, 
in  fact,  cleanliness  is  out  of  the  question- when  ice  is 
crushed  by  hand. 

Crushing  ice  by  hand  is  a  very  hard  and  disagreeable  job, 
besides  it  takes  lots  of  time.  With  a  LITTLE  GIANT 
M  ACH I N  E  all  you  have  to  do  is  to  drop  a  piece  of  ice  into 
the  hopper  and  turn  the  crank.  It  is  only  a  moment's 
work  and  you  have  the  ice  crushed  to  just  the  size  you  wanf 
it.  You  avoid  muss  and  slop  and  the  ice  is  as  clean  as 
when  in  the  cake. 

The  LITTLE  GIANT  MACHINE  is  substantially 
built,  and  is  capable  of  giving  years  of  service.  Certainly 
the  saving  and  convenience  of  this  little  machine  is  worth 
more  than  50c.  per  year.  Size,  hopper  4  in.  x  6  in.,  height 
18  in.,  weight  30  lbs. 

Let  us  send  you  one  now. 

Price,  $7.50,  cash  to  accompany  the  order  and  to  be 
refunded  if  machine  is  not  satisfactory. 

Davenport   Ice  Chipping  Machine  Co., 


1378  West  Third  St. 


Davenport,  Iowa 


BYStAL  DLUt  CO, 


Six   O'clock 

(/.    M^.  Foley,  in  the  New  York  Ti?nes) 

Six  o'clock  in  the  tower  and  street, 
The  tune  and  tramp  of  hurrying  feet, 
The  feeble  flicker  of  smoke- dimmed  stars, 
The  clatter  and  clang  of  cabs  and  cars; 
A  splash  of  blood  from  the  dying  sun, 
Life  with  its  duties  shirked  or  done, 
A  battle  lost  or  a  battle  won, 

And  people  hurrying  home. 

Six  o'clock  by  the  mists  and  marts. 

By  the  murky  wave  where  the  ferry  darts. 

By  the  lights,   like    stars,   that   glow    and 

gleam, 
By  the  ebbing  tides  of  the  human  stream; 
By  the  clicking  flags  and  the  hearthstones 

far 
Where  the  joys  of  waiting  households  are. 
By  the  tasks  laid  by  that  make  or  mar. 
And  people  hurrying  home. 

Six  o'clock  by  the  forge  grown  cold, 

By  the  steel  come  chill  and    the    hollow 

mould, 
By    the    halted    column    and    half-reared 

spire. 
The  dying  blaze  of  the  dampened  fire; 
Six  o'clock  by  the  lines  grown  deep 
In  the  adder's  brow;  by  the  shades  that 

creep 
To  the  crest  of  day  from  the  caves  of  sleep. 
And  people  hurrying  home. 

Six  o'clock  and  a  mantle  spread 

By  the  snowy  flakes  for  the  day  now  dead; 

Six  o'clock  by  the  dreams  and  deeds. 

By  the  laugh  that  leaps  and  the  heart  that 

bleeds; 
Six  o'clock  by  the  lights  that  shine 
In  a  distant  window  —  yours  or  mine, 
By  the  emptied  glass  or  the  bubbling  wine, 
And  people  hurrying  home. 

Six  o'clock  and  the  tangled  webs 

Of  the  day  lay  snarled,  but  the  folk  tide 

ebbs. 
From  the  pebbled   shore    to   the  sea  out 

there. 
And  the  beach  of  the  street  lies  bleak  and 

bare:

Heart  Throbs:     A   Book  Compiled 
by  50,000  People 

A  book  that  like  Topsy — "just  growed." 

Born  of  the  deep  sentiment  associated  with  home  and  mother. 
.    Not  ours,  but  theirs — by  the  people  for  the  people. 

Out   of    50,000     contributions,    just   840    unduplicated,    showing 
the   kinship   of   human   nature. 

President  Roosevelt  and  hundreds  of  Public  men  are  numbered  among  those  who  fur- 
nished their  favorite  selections. 

Heart  Throbs — the  Old  Scrap  Book — is  the  only  book  ever  compiled  by  50,000  people. 
Affording  a  wonderful  insight  into  the  aims  and  purposes  of  "  the  plain  people  of  America  " 
as  Lincoln  loved  to  call  us.  In  the  avalanche  of  choice  bits  of  prose  and  verse  that  came 
from  every  part  of  the  country  only  840  selections  were  found  to  be  unduplicated,  think  of 
that !  and  these  have  been  preserved  in  the  handsomely  bound  Gift  book — Heart  Throbs — 
making  the  most  notable  book  of  the  times.  What  are  these  840  selections  ?  Is  one  your 
favorite  ?  Probably,  for  at  heart  we  are  all  pretty  much  alike.  Heart  Throbs  is  an  ideal 
book  to  read  aloud  at  the  family  gathering  during  the  long  winter  evenings  with  the  children 

"  hanging  on  the  arms  of  the  chair," 
drinking  in  the  enduring  life  stories. 
Every  teacher  should  have  a  copy  for 
the  school  room ;  every  preacher  for  the 
pulpit;  every  "good  old  soul  "  for  the 
memories  it  brings ;  every  youth  and 
maiden  for  the  nobleness  and  optimism  it 
teaches.  A  universal  book  with  a  range 
of  emotion  greater  than  Shakespeare; 
in  comfort,  second  only  to  the  Bible.

Six  o'clock  and  the  great  piles  there 
Close  shut,  like  prison  cells  for  Care, 
Whose  hollow  eyes  through  gratings  stare, 
And  people  hurrying  home. 


Two  dishes  that  attract  northern  guests 
in  the  ' '  Land  of  Dixie  ' '  are  ' '  nigger  hoe 
cake ' '  and  candied  yams,  and  if  the  hotel 
man  is  shrewd  he  will  keep  an  old  nigger 
man  or  ''mammy"  around  who  can  make 
a  hoe  cake  and  candy  yams.  Hoe  cake 
gets  its  name  from  once  being  baked  on  an 
old  cotton  hoe  in  the  negro  cabin  in  front 
of  the  fire  place,  and  is  composed  of  salt, 
water  and  yellow  corn  meal.  When  made 
in  the  old  original  way,  it  was  simple,  with 
a  great  sufficiency  of  beating  to  make  it 
light,  but  ' '  mammy  ' '  says,  ' '  no  count  nig- 
gers put  in  aigs,  buttah,  milk  and  powdas  " 
(meaning  baking  powder).  It  is  baked  in 
the  oven. 

Candied  yams  in  Alabama  are  much  dif- 
ferent from  those  of  northern  climes,  where 
any  kind  of  sweet  potato  is  boiled,  skinned 
and  then  sprinkled  with  sugar  and  browned 
in  the  oven.  Down  here  they  are  really 
and  truly  ' '  yellow  yams ' '  baked  in  the 
ashes,  peeled  and  placed  in  a  buttered  pan 
where  a  sauce  is  poured  over  them,  made 
from  New  Orleans  sugar  and  hog  fryings. 
Then  they  are  set  before  the  fire  until  the 
sugar  and  drippings  crystalize  and  permeate 
the  potatoes  or  tubers  or  yams,  and  I  tell 
you  they  are  the  real  thing.  Jersey  sweets 
are  not  in  it.  —  Hotel  World. 


Perfectly   Evident 

''Now,"  said  the  physician,  "you  will 
have  to  eat  plain  food  and  not  stay  out  late 
at  night." 

"Yes,"  replied  the  patient,  "that  is 
what  I  have  been  thinking  ever  since  you 
sent  in  your  bill." —  The  Catholic  News. 


He  Counted  for  Two 

Once  at  a  dinner  at  which  Liszt  was 
present  the  hostess  suddenly  exclaimed  in 
alarm  that  there  were  thirteen  at  table. 
' '  Don' t  let  that  distress  you,  madam, ' ' 
said  Liszt  with  a  reassuring  smile.  "I'll 
eat  for  two  !  "  —  Democj'atic  Telegram.

The  Torch-bearer 

A  voice  came  ringing  down  the  way, 
"  Room!  room  for  the  Torch -bearer!  room 
for  the  keeper  of  the  gates  of  To-morrow! 
room!  " 

' '  Ah !  yes, ' '  I  said.  ' '  It  is  h  e,  the  great 
sage,  who  has  Hghtened  the  world-shadows 
this  many  a  year.  Who  should  bear  the 
torch  but  he?" 

I  looked,  and  the  sage  passed,  his  arms 
folded  on  his  breast,  his  calm  eyes  bent 
forward,  seeing  many  things;  but  no  torch 
was  in  his  hand. 

And  still  the  cry  came  ringing  down  the 
world' s  way.  ' '  Room  for  the  Torch-bearer ! 
make  way !  make  way  for  the  keeper  of  the 
gates  of  To-morrow! " 

' '  Ah !  "  I  said.  <  ^t  wiU  be  the  mighty 
leader,  then;  he  who  so  long  has  marshalled 
our  hearts,  and  led  us  whithersoever  he 
would  with  a  wave  of  his  hand.  Hail  to 
him,  hail  to  the  Master  of  Armies ! '  * 

But  as  I  looked,  the  master  passed,  and 
his  truncheon  hung  low  by  his  side,  and  his 
eyes  looked  downward,  remembering;  and 
no  torch  was  in  his  hand. 
^  Yet  still,  as  I  marvelled,  came  that  great 
cry  ringing  down  the  world's  way,  and  now 
it  sounded  loud  in  my  ears:  — 

' '  Room !  room !  make  way !  give  place ! 
the  Torch-bearer  comes.  Make  way  for 
the  keeper  of  the  gates  of  God!  '* 

And  once  more  I  looked. 

Ah!  bare  and  dusty  were  her  feet,  the 
little  woman;  and  she  went  bowed,  and 
stumbled  on  the  rough  stones,  for  the  great 
torch  hung  heavy  in  her  hand,  and  heavy 
the  babe  on  her  arm;  but  he  sat  there  as 
on  a  throne,  and  laughed  and  leaped  as  he 
sat,  and  clutched  the  living  torch,  and  shook 
it,  flinging  the  blaze  abroad,  and  the  world- 
way  lightened  before  him. —  Laura  E, 
Richards,  in  the  Silver  Crown.

Hallways  and  Staircases 

By  Lilian  Harrod 


IN  the  early  feudal  dwellings  of 
our  English  ancestors,  there  was 
slight  connection  between  hall  and 
staircase.  The  latter  was  spiral  in 
form,  small  in  size,  and  stowed  away 
inconspicuously  within  the  thickness 
of  a  castle  wall.  Later  on,  turrets 
made  an  excellent  cover  for  stairways. 
Such  was  the  unfriendly,  not  to  say 
belligerent,  spirit  of  the  times,  that  it 
was  deemed  well  to  have  several  flights 
of  stairs,  some  of  them  secret,  and  con- 
nected with  hidden  passageways  lead- 
ing to  various  rooms.  Here  originated 
the  secret  staircase  of  the  good  old- 
fashioned  novel.  This  architectural 
phenomenon  was  invariably  reached 
by  pressing  a  concealed  spring,  which 
moved  a  sliding  panel.  It  was  cus- 
tomary for  heroes  and  heroines  to 
make  their  exits  and  entrances  in  this 
somewhat  unconventional  fashion ; 
sometimes,  however,  the  villain  had 
basely  acquired  knowledge  of  the  ex- 
istence of  this  stairway,  and  used  it 
with  baleful  effect. 

During  the  whole  Gothic  period,  and 
far  into  the  Renaissance,  the  staircase 
was  treated  as  a  mere  necessary  evil, 
to  be  bundled  unceremoniously  into 
hidden    corners.     From    the    Italians, 


through  the  Renaissance,  came  our 
final  realization  of  the  possibilities  and 
the  importance  of  hall  and  stairway 
combined. 

The  growth  of  the  hall  in  our  Ameri- 
can houses  is  a  curious  development. 
In  the  poor  and  makeshift  dwellings, 
first  constructed,  the  outer  door  opened 
directly  into  the  living-room,  with  a 
little  closed  porch  built  on  the  outside, 
and  fitted  sometimes  with  a  second 
door,  for  warmth,  and  for  protection 
from  driving  storms  of  rain  or  snow. 

When  hard  work  had  made  living  a 
little  easier,  and  the  people  found 
themselves  supplied  with  money  and 
leisure  to  build  more  desirable  homes, 
there  arose,  both  North  and  South, 
those  lovely  old  colonial  mansions  that 
were  the  fit  abode  of  a  brave  people. 
However  their  simplicity  may  have 
been  criticized  by  later  generations, 
no  other  style  of  architecture  has  ever 
appeared  in  this  country  that  so 
fully  satisfied  all  the  conditions  in 
comfort,  in  dignity,  and  in  nobility  of 
outline. 

This  distinctive  type  insisted  on 
the  great  hall,  which  extended  quite 
through  the  house,  from  its  pillared 
portico    and   stately    front    door,    with 


347 


348 


THE   BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


Hallway  in  Couch  House,  Danvers,  Mass. 

its  fan  lights  and  brazen  knocker,  to 
another  door  at  the  rear,  through 
whose  glazed  upper  panels  could  be 
caught   tantalizing   glimpses   of   holly- 


hocks and  prince's  feather,  in  Grand- 
ma's garden,  with  box-bordered  paths. 
When  both  doors  were  set  wide  open, 
in  the  summer  season,  no  place  in  the 
whole  house  was  so  cool  as  this  hall, 
especially  if  the  house  faced  the  south- 
west, as  our  ancestors  loved  to  place 
their  dwellings. 

In  houses  built  toward  the  middle 
of  the  nineteenth  century,  the  hall 
was,  in  nearly  every  case,  an  entry 
exclusively,  being  wider  or  narrower, 
as  the  dignity  of  the  house  demanded. 
It  usually  held  a  mirror  and  a  hatrack, 
the  two  being  sometimes  combined  in 
one  piece  of  furniture.  It  was  spacious, 
if  large  enough  to  hold,  besides  these, 
a  chair  and  a  table.  If  it  could  con- 
tain a  lounge,  the  owner  felt  obliged 
to  apologize  for  it,  by  saying  that  the 
house  was  old-fashioned,  having  been 
built  a  long  time  ago,  and  that  he  had 
run  the  partition  of  the  kitchen  across 
the  back  part  of  the  hall,  which  used 
to  run  quite  through  the  house. 

In  modern  times  our  tendency  seems 
to  be  to  return  to  the  great  hall  and 
secluded  stairway  of  feudal  times.  If 
the    hall   becomes   the    general    living- 


Hall  in  Edward  B.  George's  House,  Rowley,  Mass.

room,  the  stairway  must  be  shut  off. 
It  must  be  separated  from  the  hall  by 
a  screen  or  by  a  partition,  containing  an 
opening  of  such  width  as  may  suit 
the  taste  of  the  owner.  If  desired, 
this  opening  may  be  closed  by  portieres 
instead  of  by  a  door,  but  the  arrange- 
ment is  more   satisfactory   if  the  two 


to  a  summer  home,  in  the  mountains 
or  at  the  beach,  than  to  a  permanent 
residence,  which  mmst  be  occupied 
during  the  winter  season.  In  the 
former  case,  entertainments  are  ex- 
pected to  be  less  elaborate  and  more 
informal,  so  that  slight  discomforts  are 
more    readily    overlooked.     Moreover, 


Hall  and  Livixg-Koom  ix  W.  B.  Walker's  House,  Manchester,  Mass. 


apartments  are  kept  quite  distinct. 
There  are  two  objections  to  a  hallway 
of  this  kind.  One  is  the  absolute 
loss  of  architectural  beauty,  afforded 
by  a  dignified  entrance  hall,  with  a 
graceful  stairway  ascending  from  it. 
In  order  to  economize  space,  we  may 
have  to  overlook  this,  but  we  must 
still  face  the  second  objection,  which 
is  the  discomfort  of  guests,  who,  when- 
ever the  hostess  entertp-ins,  are  forced 
to  come  into  her  presence  attired  in 
outer  wraps,  with  no  chance  to  slip 
away,  up  some  convenient  stairway, 
to  make  themselves  presentable  in 
a  dressing-room  before  making  their 
formal  appearance. 

In  general,  I  should  say  that  this 
plan  of  having  the  hall  take  the  place 
of   a   living-room   is   far   better   suited 


outer  garments,  during  the  summer 
season,  are  a  very  inconsiderable  feature. 

Our  frontispiece  gives  suggestions 
for  the  treatment  of  a  reception  hall. 
It  is  found  in  the  home  of  Mrs.  Otis 
H.  Luke,  at  Beverly  Farms,  Mass.,  a 
house  once  occupied  by  Attorney- 
General  Knox,  and  lately,  for  one 
summer  only,  by  J.  D.  Rockefeller,  Jr. 

A  very  handsome  hall  of  similar 
type,  from  which  the  staircase  ascends 
most  gracefully,  is  in  the  home  of 
Mr.  Ezra  C.  Fitch,  at  Manchester-by- 
the-Sea,  Mass.  It  is  unusually  large 
and  remarkably  well-lighted.  It  con- 
nects with  the  dining-room  upon  the 
right  of  the  entrance  doors,  and  with 
the  living-room  upon  the  left,  where 
the  partition  may  be  seen  to  consist 
of  a  mere   grill-work,  which  increases

the  apparent  size  of  both  rooms,  and 
forms  an  effective  decoration.  At  one 
side,  is  a  large  stone  fireplace;  at  an- 
other, glazed  doors  open  upon  a  ve- 
randa, fitted  up  with  Turkish  hangings, 
and  overlooking  the  water.  This  house 
is  situated  at  the  extreme  end  of 
Norton's  Head,  near  where  Vice- 
President  Fairbanks  resided  last  sum- 
mer. It  is  called  "River  House," 
from  its  closeness  to  the  harbor,  and 
commands  one  of  the  finest  views 
along  the  coast. 

In  our  next  illustration  we  see  a  taste- 
ful treatment  of  a  spacious  entrance 
hall  and  ascending  stairs,  as  it  occurs 
in  the  home  of  Mr.  Couch,  in  Danvers, 


a  fine  example  of  a  very  fine  type, 
running  the  whole  width  of  the  house, 
from  back  to  front,  and  being  itself 
sixteen  feet  wide.  The  finish  is  a 
neat  white  enamel,  and  it  has  a  fire- 
place in  one  corner,  near  the  entrance, 
which  is  built  of  concrete,  set  with 
smoothly-worn  stones  from  the  neigh- 
boring hills.  In  the  hearth  are  set 
three  polished  brass  disks,  and  across 
the  front  is  a  narrow  band  of  clear 
glass  mosaic.  Perfect  taste  is  shown 
in  polished  floor,  Persian  pattern  rug, 
and  plain  wooden  settle. 

Another  fine  example  of  hall  and 
living-room  is  taken  from  the  house  of 
Mr.    W.    B.    Walker,    at    Manchester, 


Hall  and  Stairway  in  Stark  House,  Dunbarton,  N.H. 


Mass.  Note  the  alcoved  recess,  op- 
posite the  entrance  door,  as  well  as 
the  smaller  one,  of  similar  design, 
which  holds  the  tall  clock.
