B.  George's  house,  in  Rowley,  Mass.,  is 


Mass.  All  the  finish  is  in  dark  oak, 
and  the  effect  of  the  grand  sweep  of 
stairway  is  exceptionally  clever. 

The  staircase  and  hallway  that  fol- 
low are  found  in  a  no  less  famed  lo- 
cality than  the  Stark  House,  in  Dun- 


HALLWAYS  AND  STAIRCASES 


351 


'"Hall  in  House  of  Grafton  St.  Lee  Abbott,  Concord,  Mass. 


barton,  N.H.  Only  the  corner  from 
which  the  stairs  ascend  is  shown  here; 
but  the  hallway  is  of  the  true  colonial 
type,  extending  through  the  house, 
from  entrance  portal  to  the  rear  door, 
which  opens  upon  an  old-fashioned 
garden,  where  bloom  the  flowers 
planted  by  dead-and-gone  Starks,  long 
before  the  Revolution.  The  picture  of 
General  John  Stark  hangs  in  this  hall- 
way, with  other  paintings,  miniatures, 
and  relics  of  various  kinds;  the  furni- 
ture has  come  down  from  the  four 
famous  families  of  Stark,  Morris,  Pierce, 
and  Wentworth;  every  piece  has  a 
history. 

The  reception  hall  shown  here  is 
found  in  the  remodeled  home  of  "Sir. 
Grafton  St.  Lee  Abbott,  at  Concord, 
Mass.  Close  inspection  will  reveal  the 
almost  perfect  taste  of  all  the  appoint- 
ments in  this  room.  Across  the  ceil- 
ing stretch  oaken  beams,  dark  with 
age;  the  floor  is  brick-tiled,  with  one 
rug,  of  suitable  dimensions,  laid  in 
the  center.  The  walls  are  wholly  of 
paneled  woodwork,  done  in  white 
enamel,  which  relieves  the  sombreness 
of  tiled  floor,  oaken  beams,  and  carved 


oaken  furniture.  It  is  still  an  open 
question  whether  there  should  be  a 
fireplace  in  a  hall;  but  nobody  could 
doubt  for  one  moment  that  the  fire- 
place in  this  picture  is  the  right  thing 
in  the  right  place.  While  the  opening 
for  the  grate  is  not  especially  large, 
its  setting  of  light-colored  marble  in- 
creases its  apparent  size,  as  does  the 
high  mantel,  with  its  suitable  orna- 
ments. Before  the  fireplace  lies  a  fox- 
skin  rug;  while,  upon  each  side,  a 
quaint  old-time  mirror,  in  a  bronze 
gilt  frame,  surmounted  by  a  bronze 
gilt  eagle  with  outspread  wings,  is  set 
into  a  small  alcoved  recess.  Even  the 
sconces  on  the  wall  and  the  andirons 
on  the  hearth  have  an  individual 
touch  that  makes  for  interest,  as  Avell 
as  beauty.  The  closed-in  staircase 
ascends  through  the  doorway  at  the 
right,  between  antique  vases,  on  their 
slender  carven  stands. 

For  purposes  of  general  utility,  it  is 
better  to  have  the  halls,  on  both  upper 
and  lower  floors,  communicate  directly 
with  every  room,  so  that  no  room  must 
be  entered  from  another,  no  matter 
how  many   connecting  doors  we  may 


352 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


choose  to  put  in.  This  type  of  haUway 
should  have  only  the  severest  treat- 
ment. It  should  haA^e  no  fireplace  and 
no  lounging-chairs.  The  darkly  pol- 
ished floor,  and  the  rush-bottomed 
and  splint-bottomed  "slat-back"  chairs 
are  true  to  the  ideal.  The  hall  is  a 
mere  passageway,  and  should  not  in- 
vite us  to  linger.  The  stairs  ascend 
from  the  left-hand  corner,  at  the  ex- 
treme front  of  the  hall. 

This  reminds  me  that  the  subject  of 
staircases  has  not  yet  been  considered 
in  this  article  as  fully  as  I  would  like. 
Stairs  that  are  in  constant  use  should 
be  of  the  easiest  possible  ascent,  if  we 
would  spare  the  health  of  the  users. 
The  tread  should  be  broad,  and  the 
rise  should  be  low.  One  of  these 
requisites  will  produce  the  other,  as 
the  product  of  rise  by  tread  is  always 
supposed  to  be  seventy.  Hence,  with 
the  ordinary  ten-inch  tread,  the  rise 
is  seven  inches,  which  is  too  exhaust- 
ing for  common  use.     Try  to  spare  for 


your  staircase  enough  room  to  allow 
for  twelve-inch  treads,  with  a  rise  of 
less  than  six  inches,  and  the  improve- 
ment will  be  marked. 

If  your  stud  is  A^ery  high,  do  not  try 
to  carry  the  stairs  up  in  a  straight  run 
without  a  landing,  to  ease  the  strain. 
A  landing  should  always  occur  at  a 
turn,  and  be  sure  that  all  landings  are 
ample,  whether  at  the  foot  or  half-way 
up.  Think  how  much  large  and  heavy 
furniture  must  go  up  and  down  those 
stairs,  and  consider  the  expressman, 
who  bangs  the  walls  with  great  iron- 
bound  trunks.  Again,  if  the  landing 
half  way  up  be  spacious,  it  forms  the 
prettiest  possible  location  for  a  win- 
dow-seat, a  tall  clock,  or  a  statue  set 
within  a  niche.  A  niche  is  prefer- 
able to  a  pedestal,  from  considerations 
of  beauty  as  well  as  of  safety;  and 
halls  and  staircases  are  the  place 
of  all  others  to  exhibit  statuary, 
rather  than  pictures,  to  their  very  best 
advantage  and  effect. 


Handsome  Hall  in  House  of  Ezra  C.  Fitch,  Manchester,  Mass. 


Children's    Perspective 

By  Kate  Gannett  Wells 


A  CERTAIN  college  president  says 
another  college  president  is 
responsible  for  the  elective 
system,  and  he  replies  it  was  atmos- 
pheric, or  a  kind  of  spontaneous  genera- 
tion, something  that  had  got  to  come, 
willy-nilly.  Yet  if  both  men  could 
divest  themselves  of  their  official  dig- 
nity, would  they  not  see  that  the 
elective  system  is  the  outcome  of  our 
loose  way  of  letting  children  get  along 
anyhow?  They  seldom  realize  that  the 
perspective,  drawn  from  their  limited 
point  of  view,  is  not  necessarily  correct, 
since  any  perspective  varies  according 
to  the  given  distance.  With  children 
everything  is  so  immediate  that  their 
foreshortening  is  ludicrous,  as  they 
jump  at  conclusions,  which  they  con- 
sider final.  It  is  only  when  doing 
as  they  should  be  made  to  do,  that 
they  get  an  idea  of  distance,  of 
time,  of  value,  and  see  things  in 
relation,  that  their  perspective  grows 
correct. 

We  are  so  afraid  of  OA^erburdening 
childhood  with  responsibility,  of  mak- 
ing it  old  before  its  time,  that  we 
shrink  from  giving  anything  but  im- 
mediate results  to  a  child  as  reason  for 
doing  or  not  doing  this  or  that.  Of 
course,  we  do  not  want  to  overload 
him  with  an  endless  chain  of  sequences, 
so  that  his  poor,  little,  moral  self, 
from  being  overweighted,  becomes  puny 
and  finical.  But  why  should  we  not 
let  him  have  glimpses  along  lines  that 
suggest  a  leading  up  to  and  into  some- 
thing, one  beyond  opening  into  another 
beyond,  till  his  childlike  notions  get 
adjusted  to  such  simple  perspective 
as  he  can  appreciate  ? 

To  do  this,  he  need  not  feel  that  the 
burden  of  the  world  is  going  to  rest 
on  him,  but  merely  that  he  has  got  to 


learn  to  see  aright.  ^lany  of  us  are 
so  maudlin  about  the  beauty  and  sim- 
plicity of  childhood  that  it  never  learns 
how  to  be  resolute.  So  much  early 
teaching  is  weak  in  its  methods,  a 
kind  of  mildewed  gentleness  pervading 
it,  with  namby-pamby  games  as  a 
stimulant,  that  a  child  is  never  kept 
long  on  any  thing,  and  is  subjected  to 
a  constant  and  feverish  change,  to  the 
accompaniment  of  a  soft  allegretto 
music,  which  just  weakens  him. 

He  originates  and  perpetuates  the 
elective  system  in  his  little  self.  Doing 
as  he  likes,  not  as  he  must,  he  is  often 
sedulously  guarded  from  any"  kind  of 
persistence,  lest  he  get  tired,  and  thus 
he  loses  the  power  to  "stick  at  any- 
thing. ' '  So  the  bane  of  choosing  what 
he  wants  follows  him  down  the  years, 
when,  being  only  an  average  child,  he 
never  gets  anywhere.  If  he  had  early 
been  taught  to  see  things  in  perspec- 
tive and  not  as  foreground  values,  he 
would  not  always  have  elected  to  have 
his  own  w^ay,  for  he  would  have  seen 
that  it  seldom  amounts  to  much  or 
leads  anywhere. 

Then  in  order  to  keep  the  child 
what  we  call  original,  so  much  stress 
has  been  laid  upon  development  of  his 
imagination  that  it  has  become  a  bug- 
bear to  him  and  he  himself  to  others. 
The  Kindergarten  training  is  partly 
accountable  for  this,  also  the  still  more 
unwise  practice  of  asking  children  to 
write  out  what  they  want  to  be.  If 
their  elders  and  teachers  only  knew 
how  many  times  they  get  "fool"  re- 
plies and  "put  up  jobs"  from  their 
pupils,  instead  of  honest  answers,  such 
questions  would  not  be  sent  forth. 
Imagination,  cultivated  on  personal 
lines,  often  leads  to  building  dream- 
castles,  to  baseless  hopes  and  delirious 


353 


354 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


vanities,  which  began  way  back  in  the 
early  years  of  childish  election. 

Illustrative  drawing  and  cutting  is 
another  of  the  pleasing  devices  by 
which  we  endeavor  to  fathom  a  child's 
mind,  as  we  urge  his  freedom  with 
pencil  and  scissors,  the  results  being 
wonderful,  when  our  own  little  boy 
or  girl  produces  them,  and  merely  or- 
dinary, when  done  by  other  people's 
children. 

If  a  child  is  to  gain  a  sense  of  per- 
spective in  relation  to  truth,  do  not 
let  him  imagine  too  much,  but  give 
him  facts  in  the  easy  lessons  of  nature. 
The  little  girl  who  plays  at  housekeep- 
ing as  a  school  lesson,  sweeping  clean 
floors  with  a  toy  broom,  dusting  a 
smooth  chintz  covered  couch  (a  tufted 
one  would  offer  a  real  lesson),  and 
wiping  toy  dishes,  which  have  not  been 
used,  could  learn  a  great  deal  more  of 
the  ever-enlarging  perspective  in  house- 
keeping by  making  mud  pies.  Mean- 
while, we  elders,  complacently  believ- 
ing that  our  dear  young  children  are 
content  with  shams  and  with  electing 
what  they  want  to  do,  may  find  that 
we  are  being  humbugged  by  another 
"put  up  job." 

We  teach  orthographic  projection  as 
a  matter  of  drawing,  the  two  views  of 
a  model.  But  any  such  moral  per- 
spective in  the  teaching  of  truth  to  a 
child  is  utterly  misleading.  To  him 
truth  has  but  one  face.  His  mother 
cannot  say  she  is  at  home  and  she  is 
not  to  two  different  persons,  at  the 
same  time,  and  she  cannot  welcome  one 
whom  her  child  has  just  heard  her  say 
she  wishes  had  not  called,  without  get- 
ting his  perspective  about  truth  all 
awry  and  obscured,  which  no  philan- 
thropic explanation  on  his  mother's 
part  makes  right. 

If  we  want  our  children  to  elect 
wisely,  to  gain  true  perspective  views 
of  their  myriad  electives,  that  is, 
choices,  we  should  surround  them  with 
real  things,  real  objects,  real  duties. 
We  weaken  our  boys  and  girls  by  our 


sentiment  and  our  desire  to  avoid 
being  disagreeable  to  them  or  to  have 
them  find  out  that  much  of  life  is  just 
plain  uncomfortable.  For  instance, 
there  is  too  much  sentimentalism 
talked  about  the  art  of  housekeeping, 
when  there  is  lots  of  drudgery  in  it, 
though,  of  course,  much,  both  of  the 
art  and  the  drudgery,  lie  in  our  mental 
attitude  about  them.  Yet  to  pretend 
that  the  child's  perspective  as  to  house- 
keeping is  the  same  as  the  adult's  is 
a  pleasing  fiction,  which  any  girl  soon 
outgrows.  Need  there  have  been  any 
illusion,  if  we  had  not  allowed  her  to 
elect  her  own  juvenile  courses  instead 
of  coming  down  square  with  the  cate- 
gorical imperative  of  Ought.  Children 
are  not  a  whit  happier  because  they 
have  no  idea  of  Ought.  Only  again, 
it  is  we  elders,  who  are  so  tired  or  given 
to  platitudes,  that  we  make  ought 
something  disagreeable  instead  of  some- 
thing joyous,  because  of  its  perspective, 
which  the  child  can  follow  automatically 
until  he  learns  in  later  years  to  elect 
it.  Then  he  recognizes  that  his  per- 
spective depends  upon  the  distance 
from  his  eye,  not  anybody's  else,  to 
some  given  distance  or  situation. 

This  learning  how  to  obtain  the  true 
perspective  is  most  delightful,  as  vistas 
constantly  open  into  unsuspected  en- 
joyments and  we  put  ourselves  into 
relation  to  all  that  is  around  us,  instead 
of  trying  to  force  it  into  relation  to  us, 
where  it  won't  stay  put. 

The  elective  system  begins  at  home, 
when  a  child  does  not  know  what  it 
wants  except  for  the  moment.  It  de- 
stroys robustness  of  purpose,  gets  per- 
spective values  topsy-turvy,  amuses 
itself  with  scatter-brained  illustrative 
drawing  of  nursery  tales  and  harms 
itself  by  the  focussing  of  minor  morals 
as  major  ones. 

The  elective  system  at  home  and 
perspective  in  morals  were  never  more 
satisfactorily  and  comically  set  forth 
than  in  the  reply  of  the  great  East 
Indian  administrator,  Sir  Henry  Law- 


THE  SODA  FOUNTAIN  AND  THE  HEN 


355 


rence,  when  a  small  boy,  to  his  little 
sister.  She,  girl-like,  was  bemoaning 
that  she  and  her  brother  had  not  been 


better.  Boy-like,  he  answered,  "Well, 
that's  past.  We  can  now  teach  our- 
selves." 


The  Soda  Fountain  and  the  Hen 

By  Helen  Campbell 


W 


'HAT  we've  got  to  have  is 
a  Burbank.  I  mean  a  man 
who  will  study  hens  as  Bur- 
bank  has  his  transformation  business 
in  fruits  and  flowers  and  all  that,  or 
that  Loeb  with  his  secret  of  life.  For 
you  see  we  can't  go  on  this  way. 
There's  got  to  be  a  three  or  four 
hundred  eggs  a  year  hen  or  even  a 
three  hundred  and  sixty-five  one,  but 
when  you  make  it  statistics  that  a 
hen  fed  to  the  top  notch  is  good  for 
only  two  hundred  and  twenty-five  at 
the  outside,  and  an  average  hen, 
treated  most  anyhow,  won't  give  over 
a  hundred,  if  she  does  that,  it  won't 
do,  you  know.  1  scour  everywhere  for 
fresh  eggs;  must  have  'em  fresh  for  this 
fad  of  egg-phosphates  and  egg-lemon- 
ade, and  I  can't  get  'em  half  the  time." 

It  was  a  melancholy  voice  that  made 
the  plaint  and  the  serious-faced  young 
druggist  had  shaken  his  head  pensively 
at  the  request  of  three  in  the  party 
for  egg-phosphates,  for  which  another 
group,  just  off  their  line  of  trolley, 
were  also  asking.  The  beautifully  ap- 
pointed store  stood  on  one  side  of  the 
square,  the  center  of  a  busy  little  city, 
where  half  a  dozen  trolley  lines  came 
in,  and  to  which  not  only  all  the  sur- 
rounding cotintry  all  the  year,  and 
the  siunmer  people  along  the  shore 
came  to  shop  and  also,  it  seemed,  to 
imbibe  egg-phosphate,  if  they  could 
get  it. 

"Get  them  at  the  groceries?  Why 
of  course,  madam,  if  you  want  that 
kind  of  an  egg;  but  you  see  I  started 


out  with  the  announcement  that  new- 
laid  eggs  would  always  be  used  in  my 
drug  store,  and  I've  stuck  to  it  till 
today.  I've  used  the  last  one  to  be 
had  from  the  people  that  supply  me, 
and  the  trade  is  growing  so,  I  wish 
more  folks  would  go  into  the  hen 
business.  They  swear  it  doesn't  pay, 
or  some  of  them  do,  but  I  can't  see 
why  not." 

"My  friend  is  an  invalid  and  very 
tired  and  depends  upon  an  egg- 
phosphate  for  strength  to  finish  the 
ride,"  said  an  urgent  voice  in  the 
group.  "The  best  groceries,  of  course, 
must  have  them." 

"Good  eggs,  of  course,  madam. 
My  point  was  new-laid  ones,  but  I 
can't  keep  it  up  if  the  hens  go  back 
on  me.  George,  you  may  run  round 
to  Payne's  for  two  dozen  of  the  best, 
in  fact,  he  doesn't  keep  any  other," 
and  now  with  a  melancholy  counte- 
nance he  took  down  the  printed  card 
above  the  fountain,  "New-laid  eggs  in 
all  Egg  Drinks,"  shook  his  head  as  he 
received  the  box  of  grocery  eggs,  and 
piled  them  in  the  dish  on  the  counter, 
breaking  one  to  test  the  quality. 

"Certainly  its  looks  are  in  its  favor," 
the  waiting  customer  said  with  a 
smile,  and  drank  her  phosphate  hastily, 
for  the  car  was  coming  and  there  was 
a  sudden  exodus.  It  was  our  turn 
now,  and  even  then  half  an  hour  to 
wait  for  a  shore -line  car,  and  the 
young  proprietor  seated  himself  on  a 
high  stool  and  gave*  further  views  on 
the  egg  question. 


356 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


"I  was  up  in  Boston  most  of  yester- 
day," he  said,  "and  I  got  a  lot  of 
fresh  Hght  on  this  egg  business.  Had 
a  talk  with  a  man  in  one  of  the  swell 
drug  stores  —  crowds  round  the  soda 
counter  all  the  time  and  most  of  them 
for  these  egg  drinks.  He  told  me  it 's 
winter  that  they  call  fpr  them,  just  as 
much  as  summer.  Business  men  have 
found  they  can  take  an  egg-phosphate 
in  the  morning,  say  about  eleven 
o'clock,  and  work  right  on  at  the 
hardest  without  being  tired.  A  good 
many  of  them  make  a  lunch  of  it,  and 
lots  of  women  do,  too.  There  is  n't  a 
temperance  drink  of  any  sort  that  has 
as  much  good  and  as  little  harm  in  it 
as  an  egg-phosphate,  and  the  doctors 
say  so.  This  town  is  n't  a  city,  so  to 
speak,  but  this  very  summer  I've 
used  twenty  dozen  eggs  a  day,  for  that 
is  what  a  good  half  of  the  customers 
wanted.  But  up  in  Boston  this  man 
said  it 's  getting  to  be  a  question  where 
a  full  supply  will  come  from.  He'd 
been  looking  up  the  ways  eggs  have 
to  go.  In  1890  we  had,  the  census 
says,  820,000,000  dozen,  and  in  1900 
experts  figured  it  out  that  in  a  single 
year  the  country  had  1,280,810,180 
dozen!  The  thing  is,  people  eat  them 
more  and  more  in  every  kind  of  a  way. 
Cooking  schools,  hundreds  of  them, 
they  say,  now  all  over  the  country, 
take  thousands  every  day,  in  their 
lessons.  When  the  standard  of  living 
goes  up,  eggs  come  in,  and  folks  that 
never  thought  of  them  as  possible, 
take  to  them  just  as  naturally  as  if 
they  'd  been  born  to  them.  Cake,  too! 
Look  at  the  bakers'  windows,  and  even 
if  it 's  desiccated  eggs  in  some  of  the 
stuff,  it  gets  eaten  just  the  same,  for 
there  is  n't  a  nation  on  the  face  of  the 
earth  that  puts  away  sweet  stuff  the 
way  we  do.  Candies,  too,  low  grade 
and  high  grade,  they  take  more  millions 
of  eggs,  though  how  I  don't  know. 
They're  even  sending  them  to  the 
army  since  doctors  say  they're  good 
for    folk;    in    reason,  of    course,    and 


they're  part  of  the  ration,  they  say. 
But  the  soda  fountains  are  ahead,  and 
there'll  have  to  be  a  census  for  them 
and  the  eggs  they  take.  The  best  way 
anyhow  to  eat  an  egg  is  raw,  they  say, 
and  we're  doing  it  by  the  millions, 
nicely  disguised  with  phosphates  and 
syrups,  so  there's  none  of  the  awful 
slipperiness  of  eggs  taken  straight. 

"  That  settles  most  of  them,  but  then 
there  are  the  manufactories  that  call 
for  whites  in  enormous  amount,  and 
for  a  while  threw  away  the  yolks,  but 
not  long,  for  now  there's  egg  powder, 
and  desiccated  eggs,  about  the  meanest 
parody  on  an  egg  there  is  in  existence. 
Then  we  send  them  out  of  the  country 
to  Europe,  a  thing  that  I  do  think 
ought  to  be  stopped,  when  we  can't 
get  enough  for  our  own  purposes. 
England  had  twenty  million  dollars' 
worth  of  eggs  in  1902,  over  what  she 
raised  at  home.  In  fact,  she  talks 
about  poultry  raising,  but  never  seems 
to  really  manage  it,  and  Denmark 
leads  the  world  in  getting  the  most 
out  of  a  hen  —  real  scientific  handling, 
you  know.  Germany  is  as  bad  as 
England,  and  had  twenty-seven  mil- 
lion dollars'  worth  of  eggs  from  other 
countries  last  year.  We're  improving 
though  here  in  America.  In  1890,  we 
had  to  import  15,000,000  dozen,  from 
Canada  mostly,  but  in  1901  we  sent 
to  foreign  breakfast  tables,  5,920,728 
dozen,  almost  a  million  dollars'  worth, 
and  had  to  buy  in  outside  markets 
only  126,520  dozen.  You  see  I've  got 
it  all  here  on  a  slip,  for  I'm  bound  to 
find  out  why  there  are  n't  more  people 
at  it  right  round  here.  Good  business 
for  women  you'd  say,  but  they'd 
rather  come  into  town,  in  the  stores, 
most  of  them.  The  farmers  are  too 
busy  with  all  the  rest  of  the  work,  and 
the  hens  don't  get  the  right  kind  of  at- 
tention. I  've  been  reading  hen  books, 
a  lot  of  them,  some  of  them  promising 
a  fortune,  but  some  telling  the  plain 
truth.  A  hen  wants  lots  of  attention; 
clean  place  to  live,  right  kind  of  food, 


LOVE'S  FRAGRANCE 


357 


and  as  little  incubator  business  as 
possible.  I  'm  no  friend  to  incubators. 
If  it  goes  on,  by  and  by  a  hen  won't 
know  enough  to  brood  her  chickens  if 
they  let  her  have  any,  and  it's  almost 
against  Scripture  to  have  things  that 
way.  'As  a  hen  gathereth  her  chick- 
ens under  her  wings,'  could  n't  have 
been  said,  if  incubators  had  been 
running  in  Palestine.  But  to  get  uni- 
form, or  pretty  nearly  so,  laying  through- 
out the  year,  would  settle  a  lot  of 
difficulties,  and  I  don't  see  why  women 
should  n't  take  to  it  and  make  inde- 
pendent, first-rate  livings.  Some  of 
them  do.  Most  of  them  don't.  There 's 
your  car,  madam,  on  the  switch  below, 
and,  really,  I  never  meant  to  talk  so 
much  about  a  thing  that  does  n't 
interest  anybody  but  those  that  have 
to  be  interested." 


"It's  about  hens  or  poultry  raising 
in  general  as  it  is  about  fishing,"  said 
the  man  of  our  party.  "Nobody 
seems  able  to  tell  the  truth  who  holds 
a  rod  or  runs  a  poultry  farm,  yet  with 
a  never-ending,  always-increasing  de- 
mand there  must  be  money  in  it,  if 
one  could  only  find  out  just  how  to  do 
it.  I've  even  thought  of  it  myself," 
and  the  speaker,  a  bookkeeper  in  a 
a  city  office,  off  for  his  summer  vaca- 
tion, looked  wistfully  at  the  farm- 
steads by  which  we  were  now  whisking. 
"  It  looks  so  fair  on  a  day  like  this  and 
as  if  everything  might  be  easy,  but 
zero  weather  would  tell  a  different 
story,  I  suppose.  On  the  whole,  I 
think  with  the  young  fellow,  there 
will  have  to  be  a  Burbank  set  to  work, 
to  give  us  presently  the  easiest  sure 
way  with  the  American  hen." 


Love's   Fragrance 

By  Mary  Emery  Smith 


Can  you  remember,  dear,  those  pauses,  calm  and  sweet. 
When  the  new  moon  shone  before  us  above  the  grassy  street  ? 
The  hills  in  mist  were  shrouded  and  from  violets  down  below 
Came  an  eerie  sort  of  fragrance,  melting  in  the  evening's  glow. 

Then  we  took  the  time  for  loving,  but  the  day  came  all  too  soon 
When  that  tender,  slender,  moonlet  had  become  a  round-eyed  moon : 
And  we  left  those  moon-swept  meadows,  for  the  hurly-burly  called 
The  city,  whose  mad  business  holds  all  one's  mind  enthralled. 


That  dear  old  idle  loving,  in  this  seething  rush  lies  dead. 

But  ever  with  my  spirit,  like  a  fragrance  faintly  shed, 

Is  Love's  spirit  all  pervading,  is  Love's  fragrance  stealing  sweet, 

Like  the  woodsy  smell  of  violets  along  the  village  street. 


A  War-Time  Dinner  Party  on  a  Southern  Prairie 

From  the  Reminiscences  of  a  Refugee 
By  Pauline  Carrington  Bouve 


DE  QUINCEY  once  said  that  a 
nation  was  civilized  only  when 
its  people  give  dinners  at  which 
they  converse. 

If  he  had  conceived  the  possibility 
of  a  well-ordered,  well-cooked  dinner 
served  beneath  the  canvas  roof  of  an 
army  tent,  eaten  from  a  miscellaneous 
collection  of  several  different  dinner 
sets,  placed  upon  a  table  devised  of 
planks  laid  across  empty  barrels,  and 
made  appetizing  by  the  best  of  all 
digestants,  good  humor  and  better  wit, 
he  would  surely  have  granted  the  palm 
of  civilization  to  Americans. 

Now  a  successful  dinner  party  is 
per  se  a  difficult  thing  to  accomplish, 
but  a  dinner  party  on  a  wide  waste  of 
prairie  is  something  out  of  the  ordi- 
nary altogether. 

Once  upon  a  time  far  back  in  the 
sixties,  a  long  caravan  of  refugee 
wagons  made  its  tortuous,  serpentine 
trail  through  the  sea  of  high,  waving 
Musquit  grass  that  grew  so  high  in 
some  places  that  it  reached  almost  to 
the  top  of  the  tent  wagons,  rendering 
the  journey  slower  than  a  snail's  pace, 
towards  Dallas.  Here  and  there  a 
chaparral  of  stunted  growth  broke  the 
monotonous  stretch  of  gray-green  ver- 
dure. In  this  region,  where  there  is, 
in  the  summer  season,  absolutely  no 
water,  it  was  necessary  in  those  days 
to  build  tanks  between  the  different 
settlements  for  the  benefit  of  ranchers, 
cattle  dealers,  and  chance  travelers. 
It  was  at  one  of  these  tanks  that  a 
party  of  Southern  refugees  had  stopped 
for  a  week's  rest.  It  was  about  two 
o'clock  of  a  Texas  afternoon. 

The  travelers  gazed  with  some  per- 
turbation upon  the  limitless  expanse  of 

358 


sun-burned  grass  as  the  negroes  dug 
a  circular  trench  about  a  suitable  spot, 
and  burned  a  clearing  for  the  tents. 
The  spot  selected  for  the  week's  rest- 
ing place  was  near  a  locality  known  as 
Pleasant  Run,  though  there  was  not  a 
drop  of  water  within  a  radius  of  ten 
miles. 

A  great  fire  soon  blazed  before  the 
kitchen  tent,  which  was  the  first  one  to 
be  raised,  and  over  which  Aunt  Mimie 
presided  with  conscious  authority. 
Since  the  beginning  of  the  journey 
she  had  insisted  on  having  the  pro- 
vision wagon  stop  at  the  same  hour 
each  day,  in  order  that  she  might  set 
the  bread  to  rise  with  her  ante-bellum 
regularity.  To  have  neglected  this 
habit  of  her  life  would  have  been  to 
the  old  woman  an  enormity  not  to 
be  borne,  and  so  it  befell  that  the 
travelers  always  had  a  hot  loaf  and  a 
savory  supper  when  tents  were  pitched 
each  night. 

At  a  short  distance  from  the  cook's 
fire  a  large  bed  of  red  coals  sent  up 
clouds  of  smoke  that  protected  the 
strips  of  beef,  that  were  being  sun- 
dried  or  cured  according  to  prairie 
fashion,  from  the  swarms  of  insects 
attracted  by  the  odor  of  the  meat. 
Stakes  were  driven  into  the  ground  and 
a  lattice-platform  was  hastily  con- 
structed upon  which  the  fresh  beef 
was  exposed  to  the  fierce  heat  of  the 
sun.  After  two  or  three  days  of  this 
curing  the  beef  is  ready  for  the  table, 
and  has  attained  a  sweetness  not  to 
be  acquired  by  more  conventional 
methods.  When  cured  it  forms  the 
greater  part  of  the  cow-boy's  or  cattle 
driver's  food.  With  a  canteen  of  water 
slung    over    his    shoulder,    a    bag    of 


A  WAR-TIME  DINNER  PARTY 


359 


parched  corn  in  his  pocket,  and  a 
string  of  dried  beef  around  his  neck, 
he  is  equipped  for  a  day's  journey 
over  the  prairie. 

Squads  of  young  negroes  were 
grouped  around  the  scaffolding,  osten- 
sibly to  replenish  the  fire  from  time 
to  time,  but  in  reality  stealing  op- 
portunities to  toast  slices  of  raw  meat, 
after  the  primitive  manner  of  our  first 
ancestors,  by  running  them  through 
with  sharp  sticks  and  holding  them  in 
the  flames,  or  to  roast  sweet  potatoes 
in  the  hot  ashes,  to  the  detriment  of 
the  dinner  of  the  "white  folks." 

"Whar  you  reckin  dat  smoke  comin' 
from  over  dar?"  asked  one  of  them, 
pointing  across  the  prairie  where  a 
thin,  spiral  column  of  smoke  was 
faintly  visible. 

"It  mought  be  Injins,"  replied  a 
very  black  youngster,  who  was  noted 
for  his  cowardice. 

"It  more  likely  ter  turn  out  ter  be 
some  folks  like  we-all,  who  done  got 
dey  house  bun't  up,  an'  iz  runnin' 
from  de  Yankees,  same  as  we  iz," 
was  the  reply. 

At  this  moment  a  man  on  horse- 
back came  galloping  up  towards  the 
camp.  He  wore  a  Confederate  uni- 
form, and  was  greeted  by  a  shout  of 
welcome  as  he  drew  rein  and  alighted 
at  the  fire. 

"Lor'  sakes,"  cried  Aunt  Mimie  in 
great  excitement,  "ef  it  ain'  Mars 
Cap'n  Jack  Rector!  I  reckin  Master 
an'  Mistis  '11  mek  a  mighty  miration 
uv  glorifyin'  now,  sho'!"  And  Aunt 
Mimie  turned  her  oven  around,  and 
stirred  the  hominy  in  a  black  pot  hung 
from  an  improvised  crane,  keeping  one 
eye  on  another  vessel  in  which  a  pair 
of  mule-eared  rabbits  were  stewing. 

The  children  of  the  party  were  over- 
joyed at  this  meeting  with  their  old 
friend,  which  was,  indeed,  a  surprise  all 
round. 

Captain  Rector  had,  as  Jim  had  sur- 
mised, suffered  the  same  losses  as  the 
other  refugees,  and  was  traveling  with 


his  family  and  servants  toward  the 
same  goal.  He  had  been  informed  by 
some  cowboys  that  some  "refugees" 
were  on  the  road,  and  had  at  once  set 
out  to  find  them,  gleaning  from  some 
chance  words  who  the  sojourners  might 
be. 

Now  the  Texan  had  an  unreasonable 
and  unreasoning  hatred  for  Southerners 
from  the  other  states  who  were  seeking 
a  refuge  within  their  borders,  and 
began  a  system  of  petty  persecutions 
toward  their  hapless  neighbors.  This 
antagonism,  indeed,  was  so  strong, 
that  Captain  Rector  had  ridden  in 
great  haste  to  warn  the  travelers  that 
vague  threats  and  menacing  intentions 
had  been  expressed  against  them. 

The  two  friends  then  separated. 
Captain  Rector  having  to  go  back  to 
his  encampment,  to  superintend  the  un- 
packing of  some  commissary  wagons. 
No  sooner  had  he  gone,  than  the 
Colonel's  hospitable  instincts  mani- 
fested themselves. 

"My  dear,"  he  said  to  his  wife,  "we 
must  get  up  a  dinner  for  Captain 
Rector  and  his  party.  Don't  you 
think  we  could  manage  it?" 

"A  dinner?"  cried  Mrs.  Ridgely  in 
dismay,  "why  there's  nothing  to  eat 
except  what  we  brought  in  the  wagon! " 

"  I  '11  engage  to  furnish  the  meat,  and 
I  will  leave  the  rest  to  your  ingenuity 
and  Aunt  Mimic's  skill." 

"Well,"  exclaimed  Mrs.  Ridgely  with 
a  sigh,  "it  will  be  just  about  as  easy 
as  giving  a  dinner  on  the  Sahara,  but 
we  will  do  what  we  can." 

Now  the  point  of  giving  a  dinner 
having  been  settled,  the  next  thing  to 
be  considered  was,  what  in  the  way 
of  eatables  could  be  secured. 

On  the  prairies  of  Texas  there  is  no 
vegetation  except  the  grass,  so  that, 
with  only*  those  necessaries  of  daily 
life  that  the  commissary  wagons  could 
offer,  together  with  the  beef  which 
was  abundant  in  the  vicinity,  little 
could  be  expected  in  the  way  of  a 
feast. 


360 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


Women  are  naturally  inventive 
creatures,  however,  and  after  a  long 
and  grave  consultation  between  Mrs. 
Ridgely  and  Aunt  Mimie,  it  was  decided 
that  a  united  effort  should  be  made, 
and  that  every  member  of  the  family 
should  be  taxed  for  a  contribution. 

"Lord  knows  what  we  all  a  comin' 
to,"  ejaculated  Aunt  Mimie.  "Here  I 
got  ter  scrimmage  aroun'  ter  git  dinner, 
an'  nothin'  ter  cook !  It  are  scan'lous ! ' ' 
and  the  old  negress  poked  the  fire 
vigorously,  and  then  sat  down  on  a 
reversed  water-cask  and  began  to 
devise  ways  and  means. 

"Beef  an'  hominy!  Lor'  sakes,  I 
wonder  how  Mis'  Polly  willin'  ter  dis- 
grace de  fambly  dis  way!  Who  ever 
heern  tell  uv  qual'ty  folks  sett'n'  down 
ter  sech!  Dis  ole  hominy  ain'  wuth 
noth'n  nohow,"  she  continued,  "when 
dey  grin'  it  up  in  de  mills  it  ain'  fit  ter 
give  ter  pigs,  much  less'n  people,  but 
I  don'  spec'  dese  yer  wil'  cattle  folks 
ever  heern  uv  a  hominy  mortar." 
And  she  sighed  heavily. 

"What  you  call  a  hominy  mortar?" 
inquired  a  young  Texas  negro,  who  was 
a  recent  addition  to  the  household  of 
the  refugees. 

Aunt  Mimie  sniffed  contemptuously. 
"  I  reck'n  nobody  aroun'  here  ever  seen 
one,"  she  said  loftily,  "but  we  all  useter 
hev  'em  at  home  in  Virginny.  Why, 
de  mortar,  honey,  iz  de  hardes'  gum 
tree-stump  burnt  out  in  de  middle, 
whar  you  beats  de  husks  off'n  de  flint 
corn,  twel  de  grains  looks  az  white  az 
purls  what  you  see  roun'  white  ladies' 
bres'-pins.  An'  hominy  you  make  dis 
way,  iz  sho'  nuff  hominy,  chil' — not 
like  dis  yer  yeller  stuff  you  has  'bout 
here!" 

Aunt  Mimie  went  to  the  fire  as  she 
spoke  and  peered  in  at  the  contents  of 
the  hominy  pot  with  a  disgusted  and 
disdainful  expression. 

"Well,"  she  said  as  she  came  back 
to  her  seat,  "I  wish  ter  de  Lord  I  could 
git  up  some  kin'  uv  dizurt,  ef  it  was  n' 
nothin'  more'n  er  apple  pie,  but  you 


mought  jes'  as  well  talk  about  mekin* 
Charlotte  Roos  out  here  ez  ter  talk 
about  apple  pie!" 

"I  dunno,  now  come,"  replied  her 
companion,  who  did  not  like  this  as- 
persion. "I  reck'n  you  kin  git  pie 
melon  dat'U  mek  az  good  apple  pie 
az  any  apples  '11  mek." 

Aunt  Mimie  sprang  up  electrified. 

"Whar  it  grow?  How  you  git  it? 
How  you  cook  it?  Quick,  gal,  I'll 
give  you  er  pa'r  uv  hoop  ear-bobs  ef 
you  bring  me  some  uv  dat  kin'  uv 
melon  you  braggin'  so  'bout." 

The  little  darky  grinned  delightedly. 
"I'll  go,  but  yer  gotter  ax  Mist'is  fer 
ter  give  me  a  li'l  holiday  fust." 

"You  go  long,"  said  Aunt  Mimie,. 
"I  reck'n  I  kin  answer  fer  you." 

The  gloom  was  now  dissipated  from 
Aunt  Mimic's  brow,  and  she  began  ta 
chant  a  favorite  hymn  as  she  dug  inta 
a  certain  chest  that  she  had  herself 
packed  before  moving,  and  from  which 
she  now  produced  various  bottles  and 
vials  of  "extracts,"  and  "seesinin's." 

The  gentlemen  had  volunteered  to  go 
out  and  shoot  prairie  chickens  —  a 
species  of  very  delicious  grouse  —  and 
mule-eared  rabbits,  which  are  very 
palatable  when  properly  cooked. 

As  it  was  necessary  to  kill  a  beef 
every  few  days  to  provide  sufficient 
food  for  the  camp,  it  was  agreed  ta 
sacrifice  an  animal  for  the  present 
occasion,  in  order  that  the  best  of 
steaks  and  roasts  should  be  forth- 
coming on  the  morrow. 

All  the  afternoon  there  was  great 
excitement  and  much  going  to  and  fro. 
A  settlement  some  miles  distant  was. 
visited,  and  a  supply  of  pecans,  brown 
sugar,  and  dried  figs  were  obtained; 
while  the  reports  of  the  fowling  pieces 
announced  that  hunters  were  success- 
ful in  their  quest. 

At  nightfall  the  several  foraging: 
parties  returned  to  the  camp,  laden 
with  their  spoils,  and  by  daybreak  the 
next  morning  Aunt  Mimie  was  up  and 
bristling  with  activity  and  importance^ 


A  WAR-TIME  DINNER  PARTY 


361 


for  she  was  to  win  renown,  this  day. 
The  pie  melon  was  religiously  guarded 
as  a  surprise  for  the  Mistress  as  well 
as  her  guests.  This  melon  was,  in 
size  and  appearance,  very  much  like  a 
small  green  watermelon.  When  opened, 
and  the  seeds  have  been  carefully  re- 
moved, the  fruit  is  stewed  until  tender, 
strained,  and  seasoned  with  a  little 
vinegar  and  essence  of  lemon  (if,  as  in 
this  case,  lemon  is  not  to  be  had),  it  is 
difficult  for  even  an  epicure  to  discover 
the  difference  between  it  and  the  fruit 
of  a  genuine,  old-fashioned  apple  pie. 

Aunt  Mimie  followed  these  directions 
to  the  letter,  and  was  full  of  pride  over 
the  result.  The  prairie  chickens,  which 
were  prepared  like  small  turkeys,  were 
filled  with  a  dressing  of  bread  crumbs 
and  pecans,  while  the  rabbits  were 
barbecued  very  much  in  the  same  way 
that  little  pigs  are.  Small,  rounded 
pats  of  beef  marrow  formed  a  pala- 
table substitute  for  butter,  while  the 
same  article  was  used  for  making  pound 
cake  instead  of  butter. 

Captain  Rector  and  his  party  arrived 
before  noon,  and  the  ladies  of  both 
camps  exchanged  their  experiences  of 
the  painful  episodes  that  had  rendered 
them  fugitives,  their  mutual  fears,  and 
their  hopes  of  a  "better  time,"  when 
the  * '  war  was  over. ' ' 

Meantime  Aunt  Mimie  and  the  old 
butler.  Bob,  were  busy  with  the  mys- 
terious preparations.  The  latter  had 
bewailed  the  absence  of  wine,  feeling 
it  beneath  his  dignity  to  serve  a  dinner 
"  'th  out  anything  fer  de  gen'lemans." 
But  at  last  a  happy  idea  had  struck 
him.  He  had  found  several  flasks  of 
com  whiskey,  which  he  determined  to 


make  do  duty  as  something  better. 
With  this  laudable  intention  he  secured 
a  quantity  of  brown  sugar,  which  he 
burned  and  dissolved  in  the  whiskey, 
turning  it  with  a  magician's  skill  to  a 
clear,  rich  beverage,  closely  resembling 
cognac  in  appearance. 

As  the  Colonel's  family  and  his 
guests  sat  around  the  improvised  table 
there  was  much  bright  talk,  in  spite 
of  the  unfortunate  circumstances  that 
had  drawn  them  together  in  such  a 
singular  fashion.  Aunt  Mimic's  skill 
was  loudly  praised  on  all  sides,  but  the 
enthusiasm  reached  its  height  when 
two  decanters  of  something  that  cer- 
tainly looked  like  cognac  were  solemnly 
placed  upon  the  table  by  old  Bob, 
while  Aunt  Mimie  herself  set  before 
Mrs.  Ridgely  two  closely  covered 
platters. 

With  a  surprised  expression  the 
hostess  raised  the  coverings.  There 
before  her  astonished  eyes  lay  two 
tempting  apple  pies!  A  chorus  of 
exclamations  and  questions  arose  im- 
mediately. 

"Where  did  you  get  the  apples?" 
was  the  question.  But  Aunt  Mimie 
was  discreet. 

"Dem  pies,"  she  said  at  last,  "wuz 
an  inspensation  uv  Provydence  Mis' 
Polly,  an'  dat  all  I  got  ter  say." 

"Well,"  said  Captain  Rector,  "I 
suppose  you  can  tell  us  what  kind  of 
apples  they  were  made  of." 

The  old  negress  gave  a  cunning 
smile.  "Yassah,"  she  said  respect- 
fully, "dey  wuz  refugee  apples,"  and 
not  until  long  after  the  war  was  over 
was  the  secret  of  the  "refugee"  apple 
pies  ever  discovered. 


The  Pickle  Garden 

By  Evelyn  Prince  Cahoon 


FOR  your  pickling  next  fall  you 
are  going  to  need  dill,  caraway, 
horse-radish,  cucumbers,  large 
tomatoes,  small  pear-shaped  (to  pickle 
whole),  gherkins,  celery,  common  cab- 
bage, red  cabbage,  beets,  cauliflower, 
carrots,  brussels  sprouts,  pickle  onions, 
string  beans,  mixed  peppers  and  bull- 
nose  peppers. 

Many  people,  of  course,  do  not  use 
all  of  these.  Some  even  confine  them- 
selves to  a  mere  packing  away  of  a 
few  cucumbers,  but  for  the  real  artist 
in  pickle  making,  who  loves  to  set 
away  tempting  shelves  full  of  fancy 
looking,  as  well  as  good  tasting  condi- 
ments, a  garden  containing  all  we  have 
named  will  be  none  too  well  supplied. 

Start  as  much  as  possible  in  the 
house.  After  your  garden  has  pro- 
gressed far  enough,  so  that  you  are 
picking  cucumbers  and  tomatoes  from 
day  to  day  (as  you  must  lest  they 
grow  too  large),  have  a  large  jar  and 
a  good- sized  keg  of  salt,  both  under 
cover,  to  protect  from  heat  and  rain, 
but  near  your  garden,  and  as  you  pick 
from  the  vines  wash  at  once  and  toss 
into  the  jar,  covering  with  a  layer  of 
salt.  This  keeps  them  properly  until 
you  have  leisure  to  make  up. 

Make  your  bed  sloping,  if  possible, 
toward  the  south,  and  make  a  very 
rich  earth. 

Start  cucumbers,  common  tomatoes, 
small  pear-shaped  tomatoes,  gherkins, 
celery,  cauliflower,  brussels  sprouts, 
peppers  (small),  peppers  (large  bull- 
nose),  red  cabbage  and  martynia  in 
the  house. 

Do'  not  think  of  planting  a  whole 
package  of  each.  A  dozen  seeds  of 
each  will  be  plenty  and  plenty. 

Plant  your  cucumbers  and  gherkins 
in  little  wads  of  grass  sod,  turned  roots 
upward,  in  a  box  in  the  house.     They 


can  grow  six  weeks  this  way,  and  then, 
when  the  warm  weather  comes,  sod 
and  all  can  be  set  out  with  no  dis- 
turbance to  the  roots. 

Plant  three  or  four  red  cabbage  seed. 
The  red  cabbage  makes  a  fine  variety 
for  pickles;  but  don't  bother  to  start 
the  white  for  yourself,  for  you  can 
easily  get  three  or  four  plants  from 
some  one  who  raises  them  for  the  field. 

Of  course,  you  can  buy  the  grown 
cabbage  so  far  as  that  is  concerned, 
but  we  are  making  our  pickle  garden 
for  the  pleasure  of  it  —  for  the  fun 
of  going  out  a  step  from  the  back  door 
and  picking  what  we  want  at  the  time 
we  want  it,  and  for  the  pleasure  of 
occasionally  walking  around  the  bed 
and  planning  our  pickles. 

Place  your  cucumbers  in  three  hills 
across  the  north  end;  put  plenty  of 
fertilizer  in  each  hill  and  always  keep 
cucumbers  especially  well  watered. 

Place  your  tomato  vines  next,  but 
be  sure  to  allow  four  feet  between  your 
row  of  tomatoes  and  row  of  cucumbers. 
Let  half  your  tomatoes  be  the  small 
round  or  small  pear-shaped  —  it 
does  n't  matter  whether  red  or  yellow 
as  they  are  to  be  used  when  green. 

Gherkins  are  similar  to  cucumbers, 
you  will  need  only  one  plant;  place 
them  next,  and  here,  too,  is  a  good  row 
in  which  to  place  two  red  cabbage 
plants.  Your  rows  are  now  two  feet 
and  a  half  apart,  you  understand. 

Better  put  a  row  of  white  cabbage 
next;  there  will  be  room  for  seven. 

You  will  need  a  full  row  of  cauli- 
flower (about  nine),  also,  as  they  may 
head  small,  and  cauliflower  is  so  very 
well  liked  and  useful  in  so  many 
varieties  of  pickles. 

Of  brussels  sprouts  you  will  need 
only  about  half  a  row,  as  they  are 
nothing  more  or  less  than  a  form  of 


362 


THE  PICKLE  GARDEN 


363 


cabbage  and  only  especially  useful, 
because  the  miniature  roselike  cab- 
bages look  very  pretty  used  whole  in 
mixed  pickles.  For  all  chopped  picca- 
lilli, chow-chow,  etc.,  ordinary  cab- 
bage is  just  as  good.  Better  fill  out 
this  row  by  sowing  dill  and  caraway. 

In  the  next  row  plant  your  pepper 
plants,  but  withhold  them  until  late. 
They  are  very  sensitive  to  cold,  indeed. 
Remember  half  the  large  sweet  peppers 
are  for  mangoes,  and  half  the  small 
fancy  mixed  red  and  yellow  are  to  use 
whole  as  spice. 

Now,  plant  pickle  onion  seed,  but 
don't  misunderstand  me  as  to  the 
**now."  It  refers  to  place  rather  than 
time,   for   the   onions   must   reallv   be 


planted  long,  long  before  anything 
else.  Plant  plenty  of  them  —  possibly 
three  rows. 

Then  come  string  beans.  Be  care- 
ful to  have  them  of  some  small,  round, 
green  variety,  and  dwarf.  One  row 
will  be  plenty,  as  they  are  prolific, 
and  the  picking  from  them  when  only 
barely  three  inches  long  tends  to  keep 
them  bearing  persistently. 

Last  set  out  your  celery.  Probably 
one  row  will  be  enough,  with  room 
taken  from  that  for  a  root  of  horse- 
radish, from  which  to  pick  leaves  to 
lay  over  your  pickles  to  keep  away  the 
the  mold. 

Your  celery  may  be  of  any  variety, 
as  it  is  going  to  be  chopped  any  wav. 


Diet  in  Childhood,  Sickness  and  Old  Age 

By  Mary  D.  Chambers 

Ninth  Paper 

Diseases  Especially  Influenced  by  Diet 


THE  limits  of  this  paper  will  allow 
brief  discussion  of  only  four  of 
the  many  diseases  in  which,  to 
quote  a  physician,  "a  suitable  dietary 
and  an  intelligent  cook  are  more  valu- 
able to  patients  than  drugs  or  doctors." 
These  four  are:  Diabetes,  B right's  Dis- 
ease, Gout  and  Dyspepsia. 

Diabetes 

Nature  of  the  Disease.  —  So  varied 
are  the  opinions  of  pathologists  on  this 
subject,  some  classifying  it  under  kid- 
ney diseases,  some  under  liver,  some 
under  nerves,  etc.,  that  we  laity  can 
hardly  presume  to  an  opinion,  but  may 
take  refuge  under  the  non-commital 
statement  of  a  late  United  States  cen- 
sus, which  ranks  it  under  "General 
Diseases."     Its  peculiar   character   is. 


however,  the  presence  of  a  large  amount 
of  sugar  in  the  urine, — though  again 
the  cause  of  this  is  variously  stated  to 
be  either  an  inability  of  the  organism 
to  assimilate  starch  and  sugar,  or  a 
morbid  production  of  sugar  from  other 
foods,  or  even  from  the  tissues  of  the 
patient's  body.  But,  since  most  path- 
ologists are  of  the  opinion  that  the 
liver  is  the  organ  mainly  at  fault,  it 
may  be  well  to  review  briefly  what  is 
known  as  its  glycogenic  function.  The 
sugar  carried  to  the  liver  as  the  result 
of  the  digestion  of  carbohydrates,  is 
transformed  in  the  liver  to  glycogen, 
a  kind  of  starch.  This,  again,  is  re- 
transformed  into  a  sugar,  and  paid 
out,  as  it  were,  in  small  amounts  from 
time  to  time  into  the  blood  which 
leaves  the  liver,  thus  equalizing,  dur- 


364 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


ing  the  time  between  meals,  the  supply 
of  energy-yielding  material.  The  nor- 
mal amount  of  sugar  present  in  the 
blood  is  from  0.2  to  0.3  per  cent.  In 
excess  of  this,  it  will  appear  in  the 
urine,  and  in  diabetes  the  blood  has 
been  found  to  contain  from  20  to  40 
per  cent  of  sugar. 

Varieties  of  the  Disease.  —  Probably 
everyone  who  has  suffered  malaise  from 
over-eating  candy  or  sweets  has  had 
a  mild  and  transient  form  of  diabetes. 
Of  the  more  serious  and  permanent 
forms  some  pathologists  recognize  two 
varieties,  one  largely  influenced  by 
diet,  the  other  not.  Others  think  the 
disease  essentially  the  same,  only  that 
in  the  milder  form  it  runs  a  longer 
course.  Some  difference,  however, 
seems  probable,  since  in  some  cases 
the  patient  is  intolerant  of  the  depriva- 
tion of  carbohydrates,  especially  bread, 
and  the  majority  of  these  cases  termi- 
nate fatally  in  three  or  four  years,  fre- 
quently much  sooner;  while  in  the 
milder  form  the  permanent  deprivation 
of  carbohydrates  is  well  borne,  the 
disease,  if  taken  in  time,  can  be  con- 
trolled by  this  treatment,  and  life  pro- 
longed ten  or  twenty  years. 

Dietetic  Treatment.  —  Reduce,  mini- 
mize, or  cut  off,  the  starch  and  sugar- 
containing  foods  in  the  diet. 

Replace  these  carbohydrates  by  fats 
of  easy  digestion,  cream,  butter,  olive 
oil,  toasted  bacon,  etc. 

Give  as  much  animal  food  as  can  be 
assimilated,  and  abundance,  though  not 
excess,  of  drinking  water,  to  wash  out 
accumulation  of  sugar  from  blood  and 
tissues. 

Keep  the  body  warm  and  the  mind 
tranquil. 

Substitutes  for  Sugar  in  the  Diet.  — 
Glycerine,  little  used  at  present,  causes 
a  continuous  sweet  taste  in  the  mouth, 
and  may  promote  an  over-laxative  con- 
dition, or,  if  used  to  the  extent  of  one 
or  two  ounces  per  day,  may  occasion 
intestinal  disorder. 

Saccharin  is  the  sweetest  thing  in  the 


world.  Three-quarters  of  an  ounce 
equals  twenty-five  pounds  of  sugar. 
Two  or  three  grains  a  day  may  be 
safely  used,  but  in  excess  may  cause 
indigestion.  Porcelain-lined  or  earth- 
enware dishes  must  be  used  to  cook  in, 
as  poisons  are  sometimes  developed 
from  contact  of  saccharin  with  iron, 
copper,  or  tin  utensils. 

Levalose  is  sold  under  the  name  "  Dia- 
betin."  This  is  a  fruit  sugar  in  crys- 
talline form,  which  is  said  to  be  safe  to 
use  in  fairly  considerable  quantity.  It 
is  sweeter  than  cane  sugar,  and  has  an 
agreeable,  fruity  taste. 

Bright's  Disease 

Nature.  —  A  disease  of  the  kidneys, 
which  allows  the  albumen  to  be  carried 
off  from  the  blood  into  the  urine,  and 
the  poisonous  ■  waste  to  be  retained  in 
the  circulation.  This  is  a  perversion 
of  the  normal  function  of  the  kidneys, 
which  is  to  purify  the  blood  by  filtra- 
tion under  pressure  of  its  waste  pro- 
ducts, while  retaining  the  albumen,  to 
be  employed  in  reconstructing  the 
tissues. 

Varieties.  —  1.  Acute,  an  inflamma- 
tion of  the  kidneys  caused  by  exposure 
to  cold  and  wet,  or  by  great  fatigue,  or 
by  excessive  use  of  proteid  foods. 
Remedies:  Bed  and  perspiration.  Diet. 
2.  Chronic,  a  very  slow  process,  pro- 
voked by  continued  errors  in  diet,  or 
chronic  alcoholism,  or  business  cares 
and  lack  of  sufficient  exercise.  Rem- 
edies: Diet.  Attention  to  bowels.  Cheer- 
fulness. Milk  diet  for  six  weeks  to  three 
months. 

Dietetic  Treatment.  —  Of  all  the  ni- 
trogenous foods,  milk  is  the  one  that 
gives  rise  to  the  least  amount  of  poison- 
ous by-products  in  the  body,  hence  its 
use  is  indicated  in  a  disease  where  the 
chief  problem  is  to  give  such  nutritious 
food  as  will  make  good  the  waste  of 
albumen  without  increasing  the  toxic 
refuse  in  the  blood.  From  two  to  four 
quarts  of  milk  may  be  taken  in  the 
day,  a  glass  or  two  on  rising  and  on 


DIET  IN  CHILDHOOD,  SICKNESS  AND  OLD  AGE 


365 


retiring,  and  six  or  eight  ounces  e very- 
hour.  An  antiseptic  mouth  wash  must 
be  used,  constipation  must  be  corrected, 
and  the  return  to  a  mixed  diet  must  be 
slowly  and  carefully  made.  When 
symptoms  have  disappeared,  the  patient 
may  be  allowed  all  kinds  -of  starchy 
foods,  all  vegetables,  except  in  serious 
cases,  the  legumes,  cooked  fruit,  kou- 
miss, junket,  milk  soups  and  thin 
stock  soups,  and  fish  and  white  meats 
in  small  quantity. 

Gout  and  the  Uric  Acid  Treatment 

Nature.  —  This  large  group  of  dis- 
eases, varying  from  a  sensation  of 
dulness,  irritability,  or  a  general  feel- 
ing of  not  being  up  to  par,  to  bilious 
headache,  rheumatism,  gout,  and  other 
serious  manifestations,  are  generally 
characterized  by  the  presence  of  excess 
of  uric  acid  in  the  blood. 

Through  the  physical  life  as  well  as 
through  the  moral  and  spiritual  there 
can  be  traced  a  curious  mixture  of 
good  and  ill.  The  healthiest  body 
carries  about  with  it  the  seeds  of  its 
own  decay,  the  wholesomest  food  pro- 
duces poisons  in  the  body.  Nitrog- 
enous food  leaves  a  waste  product, 
urea,  fancifully  called  "ashes  of  al- 
bumen," to  be  excreted  daily  by  the 
kidneys.  This  is  a  soluble  substance, 
and  about  an  ounce  is  daily  thrown 
off  in  the  urine.  It,  urea,  is  formed 
by  oxidation  of  uric  acid,  a  substance 
that  is  soluble  in  an  alkaline  medium, 
e.g.,  normal,  healthy  blood,  and  diffi- 
cultly soluble  in  urine  of  acid  reaction. 
But  all  of  the  uric  acid  formed  from 
the  breaking  down  of  proteid  food  in 
the  body,  is  not  oxidized  to  urea, 
hence,   should  the  normal  amount  of 


urea  be  excreted,  i.e.,  five  hundred 
grains,  or  about  one  ounce,  sixteen 
grains  of  uric  acid  should  also  be 
excreted.  This  small  amount  is  sol- 
uble in  the  urine.  Now,  when  this 
amount  is  not  excreted,  or  where  more 
than  this  amount  remains  unoxidized, 
some  form  of  uric  acid  disease  is  the 
result  —  either  this  substance,  remain- 
ing in  the  circulation,  the  effort  of 
nature  to  cast  it  off  will  cause  head- 
ache, depression,  or  general  malaise; 
or,  forming  insoluble  salts  which  are 
deposited  in  the  joints,  the  result  will 
be  gout  or  rheumatism. 

Dietetic  Treatment.  —  As  in  so  many 
other  cases,  the  theories  accounting 
for  the  presence  of  uric  acid  in  excess 
are  conflicting.  But  since  attention 
to  diet  is  in  most  cases  imperative, 
and  the  cure  is  proportionate  to  the 
elimination  of  certain  so-called  uric- 
acid-forming  substances  from  the  die- 
tary, it  seems  reasonable  to  agree  with 
the  school  which  maintains  that  "we 
eat  and  drink  uric  acid"  in  using  cer- 
tain forbidden  foods.  The  red  meats, 
it  is  said,  contain  fourteen  grains  of 
uric-acid-forming  substance  to  the 
pound;  liver  and  the  ordinary  visceral 
food,  seventeen  grains;  sweetbreads, 
seventy  grains;  an  ordinary  cup  of  tea 
or  coffee,  three  grains;  and  a  cup  of 
beef  tea,  eight  grains.  In  the  approved 
dietary  for  these  diseases  milk  is 
allowed  ad  lib.,  cheese  in  modera- 
tion; fresh  eggs,  fish,  or  fowl,  not  more 
than  four  ounces  daily;  vegetables, 
fruit  and  bread,  to  any  desired  ex- 
tent. 

The  discussion  of  the  last  of  the  four 
diseases  especially  influenced  by  diet 
must  be  reserved  for  the  next  paper.

FOREST  PRESERVATION 

THIS  MATTER,  now  before  our 
National  Congress,  is  of  the 
utmost  consequence  to  every 
home  in  the  land.  Within  the  past 
five  years  the  cost  of  building  has 
increased  almost  fifty  per  cent.  At 
this  rate  building,  especially  with 
wood,  will  soon  be  prohibitive.  Al- 
ready horaes  for  the  masses  of  the 
people  are  at  stake.  The  complaint 
is  common,  no  one  can  afford  to  build 
in  these  days.  Can  anyone  estimate 
the  extent  of  the  misfortune  to  a 
people,  where  the  possession  of  a  home 
is  beyond  the  reach  of  all  save  a  few? 
Shall  "Sloth  and  Greed  and  Waste, — 
the  sateless  gorgons  of  democracy,"  be 
suffered  to  deprive  us  of  the  principal 


means  of  home  making?  But  this  is 
only  one  of  the  evils  that  result  from 
forest  destruction.  The  failure  of  water 
sources,  drought  and  desolation  follow 
directly  in  those  regions,  where  moun- 
tains and  hillsides  have  been  denuded 
of  woody  growth.  From  all  these  ills 
the  way  of  salvation  lies  solely  in  the 
reservation  and  conservation  of  forest 
lands.  It  is  time  the  people  were  alive 
to  the  issue  and  ready  for  concerted 
action.  The  vital  welfare  of  New  Eng- 
land, especially,  is  dependent  upon  her 
forests. 

Of  the  widespread  destruction  of 
the  natural  resources  of  the  country, 
including,  of  course,  and  primarily,, 
the  forests,  The  Century  for  February 
says: 

"In  view  of  the  prediction  by  so 
careful  an  authority  as  Mr.  Pinchot, 
that  at  the  present  rate  of  destruction 
there  will  be  a  timber  famine  in  the 
United  States  within  twenty-five  years,, 
it  behooves  all  executives  and  legisla- 
tors charged  with  the  affairs  of  either 
Nation  or  State  to  study  this  question 
thoroughly.  To  such  we  confidently 
recommend  the  famous  and  interesting 
volume  by  the  late  George  P.  Marsh, 
formerly  American  minister  in  Rome, 
entitled  'The  Earth  as  Modified  by 
Human  Action,'  the  recent  reissue  of 
which  by  the  Scribners  is  a  public 
service.  We  challenge  anyone  to  read 
the  chapters  relating  to  the  dire  effects 
of  forest  destruction  in  the  Mediter- 
ranean countries  without  becoming  an 
ardent  champion  of  a  prompt  and 
radical  policy,  to  the  end  that  our  own 
country  may  escape  the  same  fate. 


"MODERNISM" 

IN  THE  PRESENT-DAY  modern- 
ism and  the  things  that  modernism 
represent  are  given  special  promi- 
nence. To  many  the  word  itself  may 
seem  almost  new  and  it  may  be  a 
question,  perhaps,  rather  it  refers  to 
good  or  evil  tendencies.     "Modernism 


EDITORIALS 


367 


is  a  movement,  a  process,  a  tendency, 
and  not  a  fixed  system.  Its  governing 
category  is  growth." 

In  general,  then,  with  no  specific  point 
in  view,  modernism  and  progress  seem 
to  us  one  and  identical,  and  any  conten- 
tion against  the  one  is  a  contention 
against  the  other,  and,  consequently, 
is  destined  to  be  utterly  futile.  Get  in 
the  way  of  progress  and  you  are  sure 
to  be  run  over  and  left  behind. 

The  past  is  interesting  to  us  simply 
as  it  shows  the  way  by  which  we  have 
come  to  our  present  stage  of  advance- 
ment. By  no  means  do  we  desire  to 
go  back  and  live  in  any  age  we  have 
ever  read  or  heard  of.  The  golden  age 
is  still  before  us.  Compared  with  the 
past  ours  is  the  best  of  all  the  ages. 

The  human  intellect  cannot  hark 
back  to  the  past.  Our  early  mental 
habits  are  outgrown  beyond  the  hope 
of  recovery.  In  this  age,  to  think  the 
thoughts  of  the  race  in  its  infancy  and 
regard  them  as  the  truth  is  absurd. 
"When  I  was  a  child,  I  spake  as  a 
child,  I  understood  as  a  child,  I 
thought  as  a  child;  but  when  I  became 
a  man,  I  put  away  childish  things." 
Why  should  this  not  be  true  of  the 
race  as  of  the  individual? 

We  must  confess  we  are  not  greatly 
fond  of  antiques,  in  any  sense;  save, 
perhaps,  as  historical  curios,  they  do 
not  appeal  to  us.  They  are  chiefly 
rags  and  rubbish  anyhow.  We  are 
glad  to  live  in  modern  ways  and  think 
in  modern  lines. 

In  matter  of  convenience  and  com- 
fort, for  instance,  the  modern  house 
cannot  be  compared  with  the  dwelling 
place  of  any  bygone  days.  In  sowing 
and  reaping,  in  trade  and  commerce, 
in  means  of  communication  and  travel, 
in  individual  and  social  well-being  in 
general,  the  world  has  wonderfully 
progressed.  Fear  has  been  well-nigh 
banished  from  earth,  and  man  is  rapidly 
learning  to  live  in  harmony  with  the 
laws  and  plans  of  the  universe. 

The    wheels    of   progress,    then,    are 


not  to  be  blocked.  The  spirit  of 
modernism  is  to  be  heartily  com- 
mended. The  modern  concept  of  life 
is  shown  in  the  tendency  of  the  day 
to  get  closer  and  closer  to  the  every- 
day needs  of  the  active  life  of  every- 
day men  and  women.  It  has  dropped 
the  preconceived  notion  of  what  people 
ought  to  have,  and  is  seeking  to  dis- 
cover what  people  really  need,  and 
how  to  give  it  to  them  in  usable  shape. 


PREVENTION   AND   CURE 


T 


kHERE  are  four  great  wastes 
today,"  it  is  said,  "the  more 
lamentable  because  they  are 
unnecessary.  They  are  preventable 
death,  preventable  sickness,  prevent- 
able conditions  of  low  physical  and 
mental  efficiency,  and  preventable  ig- 
norance. The  magnitude  of  these 
wastes  is  testified  to  by  experts  com- 
petent to  judge.  They  fall  like  the 
shades  of  night  over  the  whole  human 
race,  blotting  out  its  fairest  years  of 
happiness." 

Was  it  not  Dr.  Franklin  who  said, 
* '  An  ounce  of  prevention  is  better  than 
a  pound  of  cure  "  ?  The  world  is  slowly 
learning  that  most  of  the  disorders  that 
afflict  mankind  are  preventable.  Fevers 
are  now  rare  where  sanitary  rules  are 
observed.  The  spread  of  contagious 
diseases  has  come  to  be  regarded  as 
due  to  culpable  neglect.  In  matters 
social  and  economic,  as  well  as  in  the 
best  medical  practice,  the  trend  of 
modern  thought  is  toward  prophylac- 
tic rather  than  curative  methods. 

From  any  point  of  view,  it  does  not 
pay  to  disregard  hygienic  and  sanitary 
laws  and  trust  to  luck  for  the  outcome. 
It  does  not  pay  to  sow  wild  oats  in 
youth,  for  we  may  be  sure  we  shall 
reap  just  what  we  sow.  The  wonder 
is,  that  we  fail  to  learn  earlier  in  life  to 
avoid  pursuing  courses  that  lead  to  ill- 
health  and  vain  regrets  in  the  future. 

Prevention  is  safe  and  inexpensive; 
the  cure  of   deeply-seated  maladies  is 


368 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


costly  and  uncertain.  We  cannot 
transgress  natural  laws  with  impunity. 
Any  considerable  degree  of  happiness 
here  is  dependent  upon  strictly  healthy 
living.  In  no  wise  can  the  welfare  of 
a  family  be  conserved  so  unerringly  as 
in  carefully  observing  the  ways  of  san- 
itary housekeeping  and  in  providing 
wholesome,  nourishing  and  satisfying 
food  for  the  individual  members  there- 
of. In  the  long  run  it  will  cost  less  to 
live  well  than  to  recover  from  the  evil 
results  of  ill  living. 

A  writer,  in  a  religious  exchange,  re- 
cently makes  a  still  broader  applica- 
tion of  this  idea: 

"Scientific  knowledge  applied  to  the 
art  of  living  has  now  advanced  so  far 
that  those  who  obey  the  known  laws  of 
health  are  set  free  from  most  of  the 
diseases  that  afflict  mankind,  live  longer 
than  their  ancestors,  and  have  more  joy 
in  living  than  was  ever  possible  before. 
But  these  blessings  are  confined  to  a 
favored  class,  and  do  not  yet  in  full 
measure  extend  as  they  ought  to  all 
sorts  and  conditions  of  men.  Many  who 
know  what  makes  for  health,  com- 
fort and  longevity  are  unable  to 
avail  themselves  of  the  benefits  con- 
ferred by  science,  while  the  vast  ma- 
jority in  all  nations  are  as  ignorant 
as  cattle  of  the  means  by  which  disease 
may  be  prevented,  strength  increased, 
and  prosperity  made  certain.  Those 
who  represent  civilization  and  progress 
have  before  them  no  duty  more  evi- 
dent and  pressing  than  this  one  of 
compelling  the  people  to  understand 
what  concerns  their  temporal  salvation. 
The  best  chemists  and  physiologists 
now  affirm  that  all  diseases  may  be 
controlled  and  that  most  of  them  are 
preventable.  The  possibility  of  open- 
ing a  campaign  in  which  all  the  physical 
evils  which  afflict  the  race  may  be 
driven  out  suggests  for  high-minded 
young  men  and  women  a  career  second 
to  none  in  interest,  in  difficulty,  and 
the  prospect  of  great  rewards  of  the 
kind  that  heroes  appreciate." 


THE    MENTALITY   OF 
PHYSICIANS 

THE  history  of  medicine,  a  writer 
in  a  medical  journal  thinks,  of- 
fers more  than  one  example  of 
dogmatism  which  builds  a  creed  on 
some  theoretical  conception  that  may 
or  may  not  be  based  on  facts.  Medical 
instruction  is  more  and  more  strictly 
technical,  which  the  writer  considers 
a  mistake,  for  even  in  professional 
matters  the  mentality  of  the  physician 
suffers  from  lack  of  general  culture. 
He  goes  on: 

"The  time  is  past  when  encyclopedic 
knowledge  is  attainable  by  any  one 
individual,  but  even  making  allowances 
for  the  unavoidable  necessities  of 
specialization,  it  is  still  true:  'Special- 
ization is  becoming  more  and  more  of 
a  necessity  nowadays.  It  imposes  the 
necessity  for  opening  before  the  young 
mind  —  before  the  hour  of  the  inevit- 
able specialization  —  the  whole  broad 
field  of  science,  if  the  individual  is  to 
be  anything  more  than  a  mere  ma- 
chine. The  young  mind  realizes  better 
the  dignity  of  the  special  work  which 
it  takes  up  later,  if  it  comprehends  the 
relation  it  bears  to  the  whole  and  to 
general  knowledge.' " 


THERE  should  subsist  a  great 
degree  of  equality  between  the 
rank  and  fortunes  of  individuals ; 
without  which  there  cannot  exist  long 
any  equality  between  them  in  point 
of  right  and  authority.  There  should 
be  little  or  no  luxury;  for  luxury 
must  either  be  the  effect  of  wealth, 
or  it  must  make  it  necessary;  it  cor- 
rupts at  once  both  rich  and  poor; 
the  one  by  means  of  the  possession 
of  wealth,  and  the  other  by  means  of 
the  want  of  it.  —  Rousseau. 


Earth  has  nothing  more  tender  than 
a  woman's  heart  when  it  is  the  abode 
of  love  and  piety  —  and  perhaps  they 
are  the  same.  —  Luther. 


Chicken  Salad,  Spring  Style   (Page  372) 

Seasonable  Recipes 

By  Janet  M.  Hill 


IN  all  recipes  where  flour  is  used,  unless  otherwise  stated,  the  flour  is  measured  after  sifting 
once.  When  flour  is  measured  by  cups,  the  cup  is  filled  with  a  spoon,  and  a  level  cupful 
is  meant.  A  tablespoonful  or  a  teaspoonful  of  any  designated  material  is  a  level  spoonful  of 
such  material. 


Puree  of  Split  Pea  Soup  with 
Vermicelli 

PUT  one  cup  of  split  peas  over  the 
fire  in  boiling  water;  let  boil  two 
minutes,  then  drain  and  return 
to  the  fire  in  three  pints  of  cold  water; 
add  two  teaspoonfuls  of  sugar,  and  let 
boil.  Chop  fine  one-fourth  a  carrot, 
half  an  onion,  two  sprigs  of  parsley 
and  a  stalk  of  celery;  let  these  cook  in 
one  or  two  tablespoonfuls  of  melted 
butter  until  nicely  browned,  stirring 
meanwhile  to  avoid  over  cooking.  Add 
these  with  a  slice  of  bread  half  an  inch 
thick  to  the  peas  and  let  the  whole 
simmer  very  slowly  about  an  hour  and 
a  half,  or  until  the  peas  are  tender. 
Strain  the  whole  through  a  puree  sieve, 
pressing  through  the  sieve  all  that  is 
possible.  Return  to  the  fire,  let  boil 
once,  then  remove  to  a  cooler  part  of 


the  range  and  beat  in  the  beaten  yolks 
of  two  eggs  mixed  with  half  a  cup  of 
thick  cream  or  a  full  cup  of  thin 
cream.  Add  also  salt  and  pepper  as 
needed.  Have  ready  one-fourth  a 
pound  of  vermicelli  cooked  tender  in 
boiling,  salted  water;  drain  the  ver- 
micelli, rinse  in  cold  water,  drain  again 
and  turn  into  the  soup.  Let  stand  to 
become  hot,  when  it  is  ready  to  serve. 
Do  not  let  the  soup  boil  after  the  egg  is 
added.  If  the  soup  be  too  thick,  re- 
duce with  scalded  milk  or  hot  broth 
or  water. 

Cream  of  Spaghetti  Soup 

Melt  one-third  a  cup  of  butter;  in  it 
cook  one-third  a  cup  of  flour  until 
frothy,  then  add  a  quart  of  broth  of 
any  kind  and  stir  until  boiling;  add  a 
second  quart  of  broth  and  let  cook 
fifteen  minutes.     Break  half  a  pound 


369 


370 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


of  spaghetti  into  inch  lengths  and  cook 
until  tender  in  boiling,   salted  water; 


Lamb  Balls  with  Rice,  Greek  Fashion 


dram,  rinse  in  cold  water  and  drain 
again,  then  add  to  the  soup.  Beat 
the  yolks  of  four  eggs;  gradually  beat 
in  from  two  to  four  ounces  of  grated 
cheese  (Parmesan  preferred),  then  half 
a  cup  of  cream;  stir  this  mixture  into 
the  soup,  and  continue  to  stir  while 
the  cheese  melts  and  the  egg  thickens, 
but  do  not  let  the  soup  boil. 

Lamb  Balls  with  Rice, 
Greek  Fashion 

For  a  small  family  have  a  six-  or 
seven-pound  leg  of  lamb  cut  crosswise 
into  two  pieces.  Use  the  upper  and 
choicest  half  as  a  roast,  or  it  may  be 
boiled  and  served  with  a  caper  sauce. 


Veal  Pot  Pie,  Baked  Dumplings 

Remove  the  lean  meat  from  the  shank 
end  and  carefully  discard  such  tendi- 
nous portions  and  connective  tissue  as 


is  possible.  Press  the  rest  of  the  meat 
through  a  food  chopper  and  if  at  hand 
chop  with  it  a  scant  half 
a  pound  of  fresh  pork. 
Add  a  scant  teaspoonful 
of  salt,  a  chili  pepper, 
chopped  fine,  and  a  grat- 
ing of  onion,  or  a  slice  of 
onion,  chopped  fine  and 
cooked  until  softened  and 
yellowed  in  a  tablespoon- 
ful  of  butter.  Mix  thor- 
oughly and  shape  into 
balls  about  one  inch  and 
a  half  in  diameter.  Sift 
a  little  flour  onto  a  plate  or  board,  and 
in  it  roll  the  balls,  then  set  to  cook  in 
a  little  hot  fat,  shaking  the  pan,  that 
the  balls  may  be  cooked  evenly  on  all 
sides.  Remove  the  balls  to  a  sauce- 
pan; add  tomato  puree  (cooked  to- 
matoes pressed  'through  a  sieve)  and 
lamb  broth  and  let  simmer,  covered 
closely,  nearly  two  hours.  In  the 
meanwhile  blanch  three-fourths  a  cup 
of  rice,  then  set  to  cook  in  three  cups 
of  broth  or  sauce.  If  this  dish  be  pre- 
pared after  the  other  half  of  the  meat 
was  roasted,  the  sauce  made  for  the 
roast  and  left  over  is  particularly  good 
to  use  in  cooking  the  rice;  it  may  be 
diluted  with  broth  made  from  the 
cooked  and  uncooked 
bones  with  half  an  onion, 
piece  of  carrot,  sweet 
herbs,  etc.,  or  simply  with 
hot  water.  Have  ready 
also  about  a  dozen  tender 
okra  pods,  trimmed,  cut 
in  pieces  an  inch  long 
and  simmered  until  ten- 
der. (It  will  take  thirty 
minutes  or  longer.) 
Cook  until  the  liquid  is 
partly  evaporated,  then 
add  this  with  the  okra  to 
the  meat.  Serve  the  meat 
balls  on  the  rice,  shaped 
in  a  mound,  on  the  center  of  the 
dish.  Pour  the  sauce  and  okra  over 
the  whole.

Veal  Pot  Pie,  Baked  Dumplings 

Cut  two  pounds  of  veal  from  the 
shoulder  into  small  pieces,  after  first 
wiping  the  meat  carefully  with  a  damp 
cloth.  Cover  the  meat  with  boiling 
water  and  let  simmer  until  tender.  It 
will  take  at  least  three  hours.  Mix 
three  tablespoonfuls  of  flour,  half  ,a 
teaspoonful  of  salt  and  a  dash  of  pep- 
per with  enough  cold  water  to  make 
a  smooth,  thin  paste;  stir  this  into  the 
dish  of  meat,  continuing  the  stirring 
until  the  liquid  boils,  then  add  two 
tablespoonfuls  of  butter,  in  little  bits, 
and  turn  the  whole  into  a  baking  dish. 
Sift  together  two  cups  of  flour,  half  a 
teaspoonful  of  salt  and  four  level  tea- 


Baked  Beans  and  Pork, 
New  York  Style 

Let  a  pint  of  pea  beans  soak  over 
night  in  water  to  cover  generously. 
In  the  morning  drain  off  the  water; 
add  fresh  water  and  wash  and  rub  the 
beans  through  the  hands  in  the  water. 
Turn  the  beans  into  a  colander  and  let 
cold  water  run  through  them.  Then 
cover  with  cold  water  and  put  over 
the  fire  to  cook.  Dip  one-fourth  a 
pound  of  salt  pork  into  boiling  water, 
and  scrape  the  outer  surface,  includ- 
ing the  rind,  thoroughly,  then  put  the 
pork  into  the  beans  to  cook.  When 
the  skins  of  the  beans  are  easily 
pierced,   remove   them   from   the    fire, 


Baked  Beans  and  Pork,  New  York  Style 


spoonfuls  of  baking  powder.  With 
the  tips  of  the  fingers  work  one-fourth 
a  cup  of  shortening  into  the  flour,  then 
add  rich  milk  or  thin  cream  to  make 
a  soft  dough.  The  dough  should  not 
be  stiff  enough  to  knead,  but  should 
be  nearly  so.  Drop  the  dough  by 
spoonfuls  above  the  meat  in  the  dish. 
It  should  rest  on  the  meat  and  cover 
it  completely.  Let  bake  about  twenty- 
five  minutes.  In  putting  the  meat 
into  the  baking  dish  reserve  some  of 
the  sauce  to  serve  in  a  bowl  apart. 


add  a  teaspoonful  of  salt  and  turn 
them  into  a  rather  shallow  baking  dish 
(a  tin  or  agate  dish  answers  nicely). 
Score  the  rind  of  the  pork,  for  cutting 
into  slices,  and  press  it  down  into  the 
beans  in  the  middle  of  the  dish,  cover 
with  an  agate  plate  and  bake  in  a 
moderate  oven  from  four  to  six  hours. 
Add  boiling  water  as  needed  during 
the  first  of  the  cooking.  Do  not  add 
water  during  the  last  hour.  Just  be- 
fore the  last  hour,  remove  the  cover, 
to   brown   the   top   of   the   beans   and

New  York  Baked  Beans,  Moulded 
with  Sausage 

Prepare  and  bake  the  beans  as  in 
the  preceding  recipe,  substituting  three- 
fourths  a  pound  of  sausage  for  the  salt 
pork.  Keep  the  sausage  buried  in  the 
beans  during  the  cooking.  After  the 
beans  have  been  served  at  noon,  press 
the  remainder  of  them  into  a  mold, 
or  small,  tin,  bread  pan,  lined  with 
waxed  paper.  Put  the  sausage  into 
the  mold,  lengthwise  of  the  mold, 
then  it  will  be  in  good  position  for 
slicing.  Serve  with  lettuce  and  French 
or  mayonnaise  dressing. 


thoroughly  and  use  enough  dressing 
to  make  the  chicken  glossy.  Set  aside 
in  a  cool  place  till  nearly  time  to

dry  it  thoroughly  on  a  soft  cloth.  If 
canned  peas  be  used,  drain,  then  roll

of  the  February  number  of  this  maga- 
zine) to  hold  them  together.  Dispose 
the  salad  in  a  serving  dish.  Mask  or 
cover  the  outside  with  mayonnaise. 
Garnish  with  slices  of  hard-cooked  egg 
and  serve  at  once.

Chicken  Salad,  Spring  Style 

Cut  tender  chicken  into  small  pieces, 
less  than  half  an  inch  square.  Cut 
into  one-fourth  inch  shces  enough  of 
the  white,  heart  stalks  of  celery  to 
equal  one-fourth  the  bulk  of  chicken 
used.  Also  have  ready  cooked  peas 
equal  in  bulk  to  the  celery.  Over  the 
chicken  pour  oil  and  vinegar,  mixed 
in  the  proportion  of  four  tablespoon- 
fuls  of  oil  to  one  of  vinegar  with  a 
dash,   each,   of   pepper   and   salt;   mix 


Lima  Bean  Salad 

This  salad  may  be  made  of  fresh, 
canned  or  dried  beans.  At  this  season 
the  canned  or  dried  beans  will  be  avail- 
able. The  canned  beans  need  to  be 
drained  and  spread  on  a  cloth,  to  dry 
off  a  little.  Dried  beans  may  be 
soaked  in  cold  water  over  night, 
drained,  rinsed  and  set  to  cook  in 
boiling  water,  or  they  may  be  covered 
with  cold  water  and  set  to  simmer  on 
the    back    of    the    range.     When    the

Fresh  Cod  or  Haddock,  Baked  with 
Tomato  Sauce 

Have  a  fresh  cod  or  haddock  skinned 
or  boned,  to  leave  the  flesh  in  two 
fillets  or  whole  pieces.  Strain  half  a 
can  of  tomatoes.  Melt  one-fourth  a 
cup  of  butter;  in  it  cook,  stirring  con- 
stantly, a  slice  of  onion,  two  or  three 
slices  of  carrot,  a  clove  of  garlic,  in 
which  a  gash  has  been  made,  a  bit  of 
fresh  or  dried  basil  or  fennel  and  a 
sprig  of  parsley  until  well  browned, 
then  add  one-fourth  a  cup  of  flour  and

rika  with  the  strained  tomato.  In 
straining  the  tomato  press  through  the

of  the  fish  into  an  earthen  au  gratin 
dish,  well  buttered,  sprinkle  over  it 
half  a  cup  of  bread  crumbs  mixed  with
