ing three  times  with  the  tomato  sauce. 
After  basting  the  last  time  sprinkle 
the  fish  with  half  a  cup  of  bread  crumbs, 
mixed  with  two  tablespoonfuls  of 
melted  butter,  and  brown  the  crumbs. 
Serve  from  the  dish.  Keep  the  tomato 
sauce  hot  and  serve  in  a  bowl  what  is 
not  used  in  cooking  the  fish. 

Cabbage  au  Gratin 

Cut  a  solid  head  of  cabbage  into 
quarters  and  remove  the  outer  imper- 
fect  leaves   and   the    hard   portion    at 


pan  of  boiling  water;  add  a  teaspoon- 
ful or  two  of  salt  and  put  in  the  cab- 
bage. Heat  the  water  once  more  to 
the  boiling  point  and  let  boil  until  the 
cabbage  is  tender.  It  will  take  about 
an  hour.  Cut  the  cabbage  into  small 
pieces,  or  chop  it  coarsely  in  a  chop- 
ping bowl.  Butter  an  au  gratin  dish; 
put  in  a  layer  of  cabbage,  sprinkle  the 
cabbage  with  grated  or  sliced  cheese, 
then  add  a  layer  of  cream  sauce,  then 
put  in  other  layers  of  cabbage,  cheese 
and  sauce.  Have  ready  a  cup  of 
cracker  crumbs  mixed  with  one-third 
a  cup  of  melted  butter;  spread  these 
over  the  top  of  the  last  layer  of  sauce. 
Bake  until  the  crumbs  are  browned. 

Delmonico  Potatoes  with  Cheese 

Cut  cold,  boiled  potatoes  into  cubes. 
Melt  one-fourth  a  cup  of  butter;  cook 
in  it  one-fourth  a  cup  of  flour  and  half 
a  teaspoonful  of  salt;  add  two  cups  of 
milk  and  stir  and  cook  until  boiling, 
then  remove  from  the  fire  and  stir  in 
from  one  half  to  a  full  cup  of  grated 
cheese.     Anv   kind  of  cheese  may   be 


SEASONABLE  RECIPES 


37o 


used,  but  Parmesan  is  considered  the 
best.  Then  carefully  mix  in  the  po- 
tato cubes.  Use  about  two  cups  and 
a  half.  Turn  the  mixture  into  a  but- 
tered baking  dish  and  set  into  the  oven 
to  become  very  hot.  Serve  in  the 
baking  dish.  Cubes  of  cooked  turnip 
may  be  prepared  in  the  same  manner. 

Apple  Turnovers. 

Have  ready  paste,  made  and  rolled 
out  as  usual,  and  stewed  apples,  sifted, 
sweetened  and  flavored  with  grated 
nutmeg  and  a  few  grains  of  salt.  The 
apple  should  not  be  too  moist.  Ciit 
the  paste  into  squares.  Trace  a  figure 
on  one  side  of  the  square,  on  the  other 
side,  diagonally,  set  a  spoonful  of  the 
apple  mixture.  Brush  the  edge  of  the 
paste  around  the  apple  side  with  cold 
water,  turn  the  other  half  of  the  paste 
over  the  apple  and  press  the  two  edges 
closely  together.  Set  the  turnovers 
onto  a  buttered  baking  sheet,  brush 
the  tops  with  white  of  egg,  slightly 
beaten,  dredge  with  granulated  sugar, 
and  bake  in  a  moderate  oven. 


mold  or  cup  and  the  pieces  of  lady- 
fingers  around  the  cup.  The  edges  of 
the  ladyfingers  should  just  meet  the 
edge  of  the  macaroon  and  each  other. 
Pipe  rounds  or  stars  of  ornamental 
frosting  to  connect  and  hold  together 
the  macaroon  and  ladyfingers.  The 
frosting  will  harden  in  ten  or  fifteen 
minutes  (sometimes  less),  when  the 
baskets  may  be  removed.  Do  not  fill 
with  any  preparation  until  the  time  of 
serving.  The  baskets  may,  also,  be 
made  from  the  tiny  almond  macaroons. 
Use  one  macaroon  for  the  base  and 
surround  it  with  one  or  two  rows  of 
macaroons. 

Ornamental  Icing  for  Ladyfinger 
Baskets 

Beat  the  white  of  an  egg  and  two 
teaspoonfuls  of  sugar  three  or  four 
minutes,  then  add  two  teaspoonfuls  of 
sugar  every  five  minutes,  beating  con- 
stantly meanwhile,  until  the  mixture 
seems  quite  stiff,  then  add  the  sugar 
faster,  with  an  occasional  drop  or  two 
of  lemon  juice,  until  a  teaspoonful  of

of  three  eggs,  and  beat  until  the  mix-

simulate  the  petals  of  a  flower.  Bake 
about  twenty  minutes  in  a  moderate 
oven. 

Grape  Juice  Bombe  Glace 

Boil  one  quart  of  water  and  one 
pint  of  sugar  twenty  minutes;  add  one 
teaspoonful     of     granulated     gelatine, 


crushed  ice.  Line  the  mold  to  the 
depth  of  an  inch  or  inch  and  a  half 
with  the  sherbet,  make  the  sherbet 
smooth  on  the  inner  surface  and  let 
it  come  out  even  with  the  edge  of  the 
mold.  Fill  the  open  space  in  the 
center  with  a  Charlotte  Russe  filling. 
Cover  over  the  filling  with  the  grape 
mixture,  putting  it  on  by  teaspoonfuls. 
Fill  the  mold  very  full.  Spread  a 
sheet  of  waxed  paper  over  the  top  of 
the  mixture  in  the  mold  and  over 
this  press  down  the  cover.  Finish 
packing  the  mold,  using  equal  meas- 
ures of  salt  and  crushed  ice.  The 
bombe  will  be  frozen  in  about  two 
hours.  If  it  is  to  stand  longer,  drain 
off  the  water  and  add  ice,  rather 
coarsely  broken,  and  a  little  salt. 
Cover  with  a  piece  of  old  carpet  or 
burlap. 


Grape  Juice  Bombe  Glace 


softened  in  two  or  three  tablespoon- 
fuls  of  cold  water,  and  stir  until  dis- 
solved. When  the  syrup  is  cold  add 
the  juice  of  a  lemon  and  two  cups  of 
grape  juice.  Freeze  in  the  usual  way, 
using  three  measures  of  ice  to  one  of 
salt.  Have  ready  a  two-quart  mold 
neatly  lined  with  waxed  paper  and 
standing    in    a    mixture    of    salt    and 


Charlotte  Russe  Filling  for 
Grape  Bombe 

Beat  one  cup  of  double  cream  until 
thick  to  the  bottom  of  the  bowl. 
Beat  the  white  of  an  egg  until  dry, 
and  gradually  beat  in  one-third  a  cup 
of  sugar,  then  fold  the  cream  into  the 
egg  and  sugar. 


SEASONABLE  RECIPES 


377 


Grape  Juice  Sherbet  with 

Whipped  Cream 

Prepare  and  freeze  the  sherbet  as 
above.  Beat  two  cups  of  double  cream 
and  one-third  a  cup  of  sugar  until 
firm  to  the  bottom  of  the  bowl.  Chop 
fine  about  a  dozen  and  a  half  of 
blanched  pistachio  nuts.  Serve  the  sher- 
bet in  glasses.  Pipe  a  star  of  the  whipped 
cream  on  the  top  of  the  sherbet,  in 
each  glass,  and  sprinkle  the  whole  with 
the  chopped  nuts. 

Grape  Juice  Lemonade  for  Invalids 

Beat  the  white  of  an  egg  till  foamy  -^ 
add  half  a  cup  of  grape  juice,  the  juice 
of  half  a  lemon  and  one  or  two  tea- 
spoonfuls  of  sugar,  according  to  taste; 
mix  thoroughly,  then  add  half  a  cup 
of  cold  water  and  strain  into  a  glass. 
This  is  a  particularly  good  way  to  give 
nourishment,  and  particularly  eggs, 
when  eggs  are  needed,  but  the  indi- 
vidual has  become  sick  of  them. 
Taken  in  this  way  the  presence  of  the 
egg  is  not  noted. 

Baked  Indian  Pudding 

Scald  two  cups  of  milk  in  a  double 
boiler;  mix  four  level  tablespoonfuls  of 
Indian  meal  with  one  cup  of  cold  water 
and  stir  into  the  hot  milk;  continue 
stirring  until  it  thickens,  then  add  one- 
half  a  cup,  each,  of  sugar  and  molasses, 
half  a  teaspoonful  of  salt,  a  teaspoonful 
of  cinnamon,  half  a  teaspoonful  of 
ginger  and  two  beaten  eggs.  Turn  the 
mixture  into  a  buttered  pudding  dish 
and  let  bake  half  an  hour,  then  pour 
on  half  a  cup  of  cold  milk.  Do  not 
stir  in  the  milk.  Let  bake  two  hours. 
Serve  hot  with  cream. 

Savory  Cheese 

Beat  half  a  cup  of  butter  to  a  cream ; 
gradually  beat  into  it  half  a  pound  of 
grated,  American,  factory  cheese,  six 
olives,  and  a  chili  pepper,  chopped  ex- 
ceedingly fine,  half  an  onion  grated,  a 
tablespoonful  of  Worcestershire  sauce, 


a  tablespoonful  of  fine-chopped  parsley, 
two  tablespoonfuls  of  tomato  catsup 
and  salt  as  needed.  Mix  all  thor- 
oughly .together  and  press  into  a  cup 
or  mold.  If  the  mixture  be  not  soft 
enough  to  hold  together  in  good  shape, 
add  a  tablespoonful  or  more  of  thick 
cream.  Turn  out  and  let  become 
chilled.  Serve  with  toasted  crackers, 
or  pulled  bread  and  crisp  celery,  or 
lettuce  with  French  dressing. 

Macaroni  Baked  with  Cheese  and 
Cream 

Break  one-fourth  a  pound  of  maca- 
roni into  pieces  an  inch  long.  Cook 
until  very  tender  in  rapidly -boiling, 
salted  water;  it  will  take  forty  minutes 
or  longer.  Drain  the  cooked  maca- 
roni and  rinse  it  with  cold  water. 
Butter  an  au  gratin  dish,  put  in  a 
layer  of  the  macaroni,  sprinkle  it 
lightly  with  salt  and  pepper,  and  then 
with  about  half  a  cup  of  grated  cheese ; 
add  the  rest  of  the  macaroni,  season 
with  salt  and  pepper  and  sprinkle  on 
a  second  half  cup  of  cheese.  Pour 
in  a  generous  half-cup  of  cream  and 
let  bake  in  a  moderate  oven  about 
thirty  minutes.  Milk  may  be  used,  but 
cream  is  preferable.  The  broken  mac- 
aroni will  measure  one  cup ;  the  grated 
cheese  lightly  measured  will  weigh  two 
ounces.  A  mixture  of  Swiss  and  Par- 
mesan cheese  makes  the  best  flavored 
dish,  but  any  cheese  may  be  used. 

Parsnip  Fritters 

The  fritters  may  be  made  of  cooked 
parsnips  left  over  from  a  former  meal. 
Cut  off  all  the  tender  portion  from 
the  parsnips,  and  press  through  a 
puree  sieve  or  a  gravy  strainer,  set  in 
a  small  saucepan.  To  a  cup  of  this 
puree,  add  one-fourth  a  teaspoonful, 
each,  of  salt  and  pepper,  and  a  beaten 
egg,  or  simply  the  beaten  yolk  of  an 
egg;  mix  thoroughly  and  press  into 
five  or  six  small  flat  cakes.  Saute  in 
hot  butter,  bacon  or  salt  pork  fat, 
first  on  one  side  then  on  the  other.

masticated  and  digested  than  when  it  is  swallowed  whole. — Horace  Fletcher.

Cookery  for  Young  Housekeepers 

By  Janet  M.  Hill 

Lesson  IX 

Introducing  the  Cooking  of  Carbohydrates,  the  True  Heat  and  Work  Foods 

"Cooking  is  an  essential  preliminary  to  the  ingestion  of  starch-containing  foods, 
for  uncooked  starch  cannot  be  utilized  in  any  degree  by  man."  —  Chittenden. 


UP  to  this  time  we  have  been  deal- 
ing with  proteid  food  that  is 
cooked  deHcately;  but  in  the 
average  diet  of  an  adult  only  one  part 
of  proteid  is  needed  for  five  and  three- 
tenths  parts  of  carbohydrate  and  fat, 
and  of  the  carbohydrate  the  principle 
part  is  starch.  Prehistoric  and  early 
man,  endowed  with  strong  digestive 
power,  was  undoubtedly  able  to  digest, 
in  some  measure,  crude  starch.  But 
the  digestive  power  of  civilized  man 
has  been  gradually  weakened  by  his 
habits  of  life,  and  in  order  that  his 
organism  may  convert  starch  into  a 
form  that  can  be  made  use  of  in  his 
body,  it  must  first  be  changed,  by  the 
action  of  strong  heat,  into  sweet  sub- 
stances akin  to  sugar. 

In  this  connection  it  may  be  well  to 
add  that  the  mouth  is  the  great 
starch-digesting  organ  of  the  body. 
The  salivary  glands  of  the  mouth 
secrete  a  ferment,  which  continues  the 
digestive  process  begun  in  cooking. 
It  is  well  to  lighten  the  labor  of  the 
stomach  by  dividing  minutely  in  the 
mouth  meat  and  other  proteid  sub- 
stances, yet  this  is  not  obligatory. 
But  when  we  come  to  the  mastication 
of  starchy  food  we  are  dealing  with 
quite  another  matter.  Starchy  food 
must  be  chewed  and  chewed  until  it  is 


impregnated  through  and  through  with 
ptyalin,  the  starch- digesting  ferment 
of  the  mouth,  or  the  chances  are  that 
the  starch  will  be  cast  out  of  the  sys- 
tem as  waste  material. 

In  some  forms  of  cooking  more 
starch  is  changed  into  sweet  substances 
than  in  others.  Thus  in  a  baked  po- 
tato the  conversion  of  starch  has  been 
carried  further  than  it  has  in  a  boiled 
potato,  and  in  the  brown  crust  of  a 
loaf  of  bread  than  in  the  crumb  at  the 
center. 

In  general,  the  higher  the  degree  of 
heat  to  which  it  is  possible  to  subject 
the  starch,  the  greater  is  the  conver- 
sion, though  heat  at  a  lower  tempera- 
ture, long  continued,  brings  about  very 
nearly  the  same  result.  Let  us  see 
now  how  the  cooking  of  starch  may  be 
carried  out  in  the  matter  of  sauces. 

Regarding  Sauces 

Often  richness  or  moisture,  one  or 
both,  would  improve  an  article  of  food, 
and,  sometimes,  we  wish  to  add  to  the 
bulk  of  certain  articles  to  make  them 
"go  farther."  In  either  case  we  may 
have  recourse  to  sauces.  Sauces  are 
combinations  of  fat  —  usually  butter 
or  dripping  —  milk,  broth,  vegetable 
purees,  or  water  and  flavorings  with 
eggs  or  some  form  of  starch,  to  give 


381 


382 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


body  or  consistence  to  the  mixture. 
The  thing  that  interests  us  just  now  is 
the  ways  in  which  the  starch  in  our 
dish  may  be  cooked  with  the  other  in- 
gredients, to  produce  the  desired  re- 
sult. This  may  be  done  in  three 
different  ways; 

Proportions  of  the  Ingredients 
in  Sauces 

Sometimes  we  may  wish  a  very  rich 
and  buttery  sauce ;  and,  for  some  uses, 
a  very  thick  sauce,  as  in  making  cro- 
quettes; for  general  use,  however,  no 
matter  how  the  sauce  is  put  together, 
the  proportions  of  the  various  ingredi- 
ents are  the  same,  i.e.:  For  one  cup  of 
sauce,  take 

Two  tablespoonfuls  of  fat 
Two  tablespoonfuls  of  flour 
One-fourth  a  teaspoonful  of  salt 
One-fourth  a  teaspoonful  of  pepper 

1.  Melt  the  fat  in  a  small  saucepan 
set  directly  over  the  fire ;  add  the  flour, 
salt  and  pepper,  and  stir  until  frothy 
throughout,  then  add  the  cold  liquid 
"^nd  stir  constantly  and  vigorously 
until  the  mixture  is  smooth,  thick  and 
boiling.  The  liquid  may  be  added  hot, 
if  the  hot  mixture  of  butter  and  flour 
be  cooled  before  such  addition. 

2.  Heat  milk  or  cream  over  hot 
water  (in  double  boiler),  other  liquids 
directly  over  the  fire.  Beat  the  butter 
to  a  cream;  gradually  beat  into  it  the 
flour  and  seasonings;  dilute  this  with  a 
little  of  the  hot  liquid,  and  stir  until 
the  ingredients  are  evenly  blended; 
then  turn  into  the  rest  of  the  hot 
liquid  and  continue  to  stir  (over  the 
fire)  until  the  sauce  boils  and  is  smooth, 
or  (in  double  boiler)  until  the  sauce 
thickens  and  is  smooth,  then  cover  and 
let  cook  ten  minutes  or  longer,  stirring 
occasionally. 

3.  Use  a  little  of  the  cold  liquid  in 
stirring  the  flour  and  seasonings  into 
a  smooth  batter.  Scald  the  rest  of  the 
liquid,  if  it  be  cream  or  milk,  over  hot 
water,  or  heat  other  liquids  to  the  boil- 
ing point  directly  over  the  fire.     Stir 


the  batter  into  the  hot  liquid  —  dilute 
first  with  a  little  of  the  hot  liquid,  then 
when  smooth  stir  into  the  rest  of  the 
hot  liquid.  If  the  double  boiler  be 
used,  stir  and  cook  until  smooth,  then 
cover  and  let  cook  ten  or  fifteen  min- 
utes, stirring  occasionally;  if  cooked  di- 
rectly over  the  fire  stir  and  cook  until 
boiling.  Just  before  serving,  add  the 
butter  in  little  bits,  beating  in  each  bit 
thoroughly  before  the  next  is  added. 

Theory  of  the  Three  Ways  of 
Making  Sauces 

By  the  first  method  the  sauce  is 
very  quickly  made ;  thus,  when  time  is 
an  object,  this  way  should  be  employed. 
The  temperature  of  hot  fat  is  consid- 
erably higher  than  that  of  scalding 
milk  or  boiling  water  or  broth;  thus, 
when  the  flour  (starch)  is  cooked  in 
hot  fat,  it  is  cooked  more  quickly  and 
thoroughly  than  when  it  is  cooked  in 
liquid.  But  fat  that  has  been  heated 
to  a  high  temperature  is  not  very  easily 
digested,  and  thus  the  first  method  of 
making  a  sauce  should  not  be  chosen, 
when  the  food  is  designed  for  children 
or  those  of  weak  digestion.  What  is 
gained  in  the  thorough  cooking  of  the 
starch  is  more  than  lost  in  the  over- 
heating of  the  fat. 

In  cooking  starch  in  the  liquid  con- 
siderably  more  time  must  be  allowed. 
In  following  the  last  method  the  flavor 
of  the  butter  is  more  nearly  retained. 
All  sauces  made  by  these  formulas  are 
known  as  white  sauces. 

Brown  Sauce 

There  is  another  general  variety  of 
sauce  that  differs  from  the  white  sauce 
principally  in  color.  The  liquid  in 
this  sauce  may  be  vegetable  puree, 
fish,  or  meat  broth,  but  it  is  usually 
beef  broth  made  dark  by  browning  the 
ingredients  of  which  it  is  made  in  hot 
fat.  For  this  sauce,  cook  the  fat  until 
browned  but  not  burned;  add,  for  a 
high-flavored  sauce,  one  or  two  slices 
of  onion,  two  slices  of  carrot,  a  bit  of 


COOKERY  FOR  YOUNG  HOUSEKEEPERS 


383 


parsley  and  half  a  stalk  of  celery,  all 
chopped  rather  coarsely;  let  these 
brown  in  the  fat,  stirring  constantly 
that  they  may  not  burn;  now  add 
three  level  tablespoonfnls  of  flour,  and 
the  usual  quantity  of  salt  and  pepper 
and  continue  stirring  and  cooking  until 
the  flour  assumes  a  reddish  brown 
tint;  then  add  the  cold  liquid,  and  stir 
and  cook  luitil  smooth  and  boiling, 
then  strain.  You  will  note  that  the 
proportions  remain  the  same  as  for  the 
white  sauce,  save  that  more  flour  is 
used.  By  the  long  cooking  in  the  hot 
fat  the  conversion  of  the  starch  in  the 
flour  is  carried  farther  than  in  the 
white  sauce  and  the  thickening  prop- 
erty of  the  starch  is  lessened,  thus 
more  flour  is  required. 

Cream  Soups 

It  is  but  a  step  from  cream  sauce  to 
cream  soup.  Use  milk  or  thin  cream, 
in  making  the  cup  of  sauce,  then  add 
a  cup  of  any  kind  of  puree  at  hand. 
By  puree  is  meant  any  food  substance 
cooked  (usually  in  water,  though  in 
some  cases,  as  with  tomatoes,  water  is 
unnecessary)  and  pressed  through  a 
sieve.  Sometimes  more  and  sometimes 
less  of  the  article  may  be  pressed 
through  the  sieve ;  dilute  the  portion  in 
the  sieve  with  some  of  the  liquid  that 
has  already  dripped  through  so  as  to 
push  through  as  much  of  the  article  as 
is  possible.  The  larger  part  of  to- 
matoes, onions,  spinach,  peas  and 
beans  will  pass  the  sieve;  less  of  celery, 
green  corn  and  asparagus  may  be 
pressed  through,  on  account  of  the 
tough  cellular  structure  of  the  latter 
vegetables.  Cooked  fish  and  meat, 
freed  from  skin  and  bone,  then  pounded 
smooth  and  passed  through  the  sieve 
may  be  used  in  these  soups.  The  fish 
and  meat  should  be  diluted  with  fish 
or  meat  broth,  or  the  resulting  soup 
will  be  too  thick.  Salt  and  pepper,  and, 
occasionally,  a  little  more  hot  milk  or 
broth  are  needed.  A  gravy  strainer, 
set  into  one  of  the  parts  of  a  small 


double  boiler,  and  a  wooden  pestle  are 
indispensable  in  reducing  the  cooked 
material  to  the  puree  form.  With 
these  soups  serve  croutons,  toasted 
crackers,  or  pulled  bread. 

Croutons  for  Cream  Soups 

Cut  stale  bread  into  slices  from  one- 
fourth  to  one-third  an  inch  thick;  re- 
miove  the  crusts,  spread  the  surfaces 
with  butter,  and  cut  the  slices  into 
pieces  an  inch  square.  Set  these  into 
the  oven  to  brow^n,  turning  to  brown 
both  sides. 

Toasted  Crackers 

Split  the  common  Boston  or  soda 
cracker,  toast  over  the  fire  or  in  the 
oven,  spread  lightly  with  butter  and 
serve.  These  may  be  spread  with 
butter  and  then  browned  in  the  oven, 
but  they  are  more  wholesome  spread 
with  butter  after  they  are  browned. 

Pulled  Bread 

Remove  the  crust  from  a  loaf  of 
fresh-baked  bread  and  cut  and  pull  the 
loaf  into  halves,  lengthwise,  repeat  this 
cutting  and  pulling  until  the  pieces 
are  about  an  inch  thick;  they  may  be 
the  length  of  the  loaf  or  shorter.  Dry 
these  in  a  slack  oven  until  they  snap 
when  broken.  They  should  be  amber 
in  color.     Reheat  before  serving. 

Cream-of-Pea  Soup 

Press  a  cup  of  peas  and  the  liquid  in 
which  they  were  cooked  through  the 
sieve.  Melt  two  tablespoonfuls  of  but- 
ter; add  two  tablespoonfuls  of  flour 
and  one-fourth  a  teaspoonful,  each,  of 
salt  and  black  pepper  and  cook  until 
frothy  in  the  butter;  add  one  cup  of 
milk  and  cook,  stirring  constantly, 
until  smooth  and  boiling;  add  the  pea 
puree  and  stir  until  smoothly  blended 
and  again  boiling.  If  too  thick  add  a 
little  hot  milk  or  broth  with  additional 
salt  and  pepper  and  serve  at  once.  If 
canned  peas  be  used,  add  to  them  half 
a    teaspoonful    of    sugar.     Canned    or 


384 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


fresh  peas  may  be  substituted  for  the 
com  in  the  " Cream-of-Corn  Soup." 
These  soups  provide  a  nutritious 
luncheon. 

Cream-of-Corn  Soup  (to  serve  four) 

(Formula  with  variations) 

Press  enough  canned  or  fresh  corn, 
chopped  fine,  through  a  sieve  to  yield 
one-third  a  cup  of  puree.  Beat  one 
egg  and  the  yolk  of  another;  add  one- 
fourth  a  teaspoonful,  each,  of  salt  and 
black  pepper,  three  tablespoonfuls  of 
cream  and  the  puree.  Mix  thoroughly 
and  turn  into  four  buttered  timbale 
molds.  Set  these  in  a  pan  on  several 
folds  of  paper,  surround  with  water  at 
the  boiling  point,  and  let  cook  in  a 
slow  oven  until  firm.  Serve  hot,  one 
in  each  plate  of  soup.     Slice  half  an 


onion  fine;  add  a  sprig  of  parsley 
(fresh  or  dried)  and  let  cook  in  two 
tablespoonfuls  of  butter  until  the 
onion  is  softened  and  yellowed;  then 
add  one  cup  of  the  chopped  corn  and 
a  pint  of  broth  or  hot  water  (broth 
from  chicken  giblets  and  bones  is  good) 
and  let  simmer  ten  or  fifteen  minutes. 
Pour  the  liquid  through  a  sieve  and 
pound  the  corn  with  a  pestle,  then 
press  as  much  of  the  corn  as  is  possible 
through  a  sieve,  diluting  it,  meanwhile, 
with  the  hot  liquid;  then  reheat  the 
puree.  Melt  two  tablespoonfuls  of 
butter;  in  it  cook  two  tablespoonfuls 
of  flour,  half  a  teaspoonful  of  salt  and 
a  little  pepper,  then  add  one  cup  of 
milk;  stir  until  thickened  and  boiling, 
then  add  the  puree  and  one-fourth  a 
cup  of  cream. 


The  Ways  of  March 

By  Kate  Matson  Post 

What  though  the  fields  are  white  with  snow. 
And  woodland  haunts' no  flowerets  show, 
And  storm  clouds  gather  beyond  the  hill, 
Our  God  is  good,  and  the  sun  shines  still. 

What  though  the  winds  are  loud  and  bleak, 
Close  comradeship  with  spring  they  keep. 
And  they  rouse  the  buds  that  sleeping  lie. 
With  promise  of  Maytime  bye  and  bye. 

O!  March,  thou'rt  but  a  father  bluff, 
Kindness  lurks  'neath  thy  manners  rough, 
And  thy  winds,  howe'er  so  wild  they  blow. 
But  nurture  the  fairest  flowers  that  grow. 


Contributions  to  this  department  will  be  gladly  received.     Accepted  items  will  be 
paid  for  at  reasonable  rates. 

IN     serving     our     Club     of     sixteen  Aunt  Martha's  Gift 

menjbers,    I    followed    your    recipe 


for  "A  Winter  Dessert,"  and  it  was  a 
great  success.  I  used  the  white  grapes 
and  macaroons  with  whipped  cream, 
and  after  arranging  on  the  slices  of 
pineapple,  I  topped  off  with  a  spoonful 
of  whipped  cream  and  a  bit  of  currant 
jelly. 

The  next  day  I  had  a  fine  sherbet 
from  the  juice  of  the  tvv^o  cans  of  pine- 
apple with  an  equal  quantity  of  water, 
the  juice  of  one  lemon,  sugar  to  taste, 
and  the  addition  of  one  beaten  white  of 
egg,  after  the  mixture  began  to  freeze. 

Another  article  on  my  menu  went  so 
well  that  I  am  going  to  send  you  the 
recipe  in  hopes  that  it  may  be  of  use 
to  others. 

Salmon  and  Peas 

Remove  skin  and  bones  from  two 
cans  of  salmon  and  heat  thoroughly 
in  a  double  boiler.  Drain  the  juice 
into  one  pint  of  cream  and  thicken 
with  baked  flour  stirred  into  a  little 
cold  milk,  *and  season  with  salt  and 
red  pepper.  Turn  the  hot  salmon 
onto  the  center  of  a  hot  platter. 
Over  this  turn  the  hot  cream  sauce 
and  sprinkle  with  one  can  of  peas, 
heated  to  the  boiling  point  and  drained 
thoroughly.     Serve  hot. 

I  find  that  I  can  make  very  smooth 
gravies  and  cream  sauce  by  using 
baked  flour. 

I  set  a  basin  of  flour  into  the  oven 
until  thoroughly  heated  through,  then 
sift  it  into  a  can  and  set  it  away  for 
use  as  needed.  n.  h. 


I 


By  Carrie  May  Ashton 

AM  sending  you,  my  dear  child, 
a  very  common-place  and  practi- 
cal gift,"  wrote  Aunt  Martha  to  her 
grand-niece,  who  was  about  to  be 
married,  "an  emergency  box,  which 
is  not  likely  to  be  duplicated  by  any 
of  your  numerous  friends. 

"You  will  have  many  beautiful  and 
priceless  gifts,  but  into  none  has  there 
been  put  more  heartfelt  love  and  earnest 
wishes,  not  for  an  easy,  carefree  life,  but 
for  strength  and  courage,  which  will 
help  you  to  meet  the  daily  problems 
and  perplexities  of  life,  that  will  greet 
you  on  every  side,  bravely  and  wisely." 

This  emergency  box  was  fashioned 
from  an  ordinary  wooden  box  14  x  21 
inches  and  12  inches  in  height. 

The  top  was  padded  and  the  whole 
box  was  neatly  covered  with  cretonne, 
so  that  it  resembled  an  ottoman  or 
shirt-waist  box.  Casters  were  fastened 
on  the  corners,  so  that  it  could  be  rolled 
easily  from  room  to  room. 

It  contained  rolls  of  fine  old  linen, 
old  cotton,  flannel,  absorbent  cotton 
and  gauze,  safety  pins  of  assorted 
sizes,  surgeon's  plaster,  as  well  as  court- 
plaster,  mustard  plasters,  ready  for  use; 
pulverized  boracic  acid,  baking  soda  for 
burns,  vaseline,  bottle  of  witch-hazel, 
alcohol  and  brandy,  anti-phlogistine,  a 
hot- water  bag  and  fountain  syringe. 

Perhaps  the  most  useful  thing  in 
the  box  was  a  little  homemade  booklet, 
prepared  by  Aunt  Martha,  telling 
what  to  do  in  emergencies.

Only  a  young,  inexperienced  house- 
keeper in  a  strange  place,  far  away 
from  home,  could  realize  and  appreciate 
the  value  of  such  a  little  book. 


St.  Patrick's  Day  Luncheon 

By  Evelyn  Prince  Gaboon 

DECORATE  your  home  with 
streamers  and  bows  of  green. 
Have  no  flowers,  but  use  ferns  and 
palms  as  freely  as  possible.  Replace 
your  ordinary  hangings  so  far  as  you 
easily  can  by  others  of  some  cheap, 
light-green  silkoline  or  cheesecloth. 

Of  course  so  far  as  you  are  able  you 
will  use  green  dishes  and,  tied  to  each 
name  card  with  green  baby  ribbon, 
have  three  or  four  long,  white,  waxy 
potato  sprouts. 

Have  the  middle  of  the  table  orna- 
mented with  an  Irish  harp,  made  of  a 
frame  of  heavy  pasteboard,  with  strings 
of  twine,  and  all  completely  covered 
with  some  green  foliage,  smilax,  spren- 
gerii  fern,  asparagus  fern  or  even  ever- 
green. Set  the  harp  in  a  small  block 
of  wood  to  hold  it  firm. 

Of  course,  if  you  are  in  the  city, 
you  can  get  a  little  pot  of  shamrock 
to  place  near  each  end  of  the  table; 
in  the  country  and  in  a  warm  climate 
you  may  be  able  to  get  a  pot  of  clover, 
which  will  do  very  nicely. 

Serve  fried  oysters  on  a  bed  of  green 
lettuce,  potato  salad  with  a  garnishing 
of  green  parsley,  and  scalloped  pota- 
toes also  in  parsley. 

Have  olives  and  small  cucumber 
pickles  in  the  largest  potatoes  you  can 
find,  scooped  out  to  form  a  dish,  and 
set  in  a  small  plate  by  cutting  a  tiny 
slice  off  the  potato  to  make  a  flat 
surface. 

Have  tiny,  hot  biscuits  cut  out  with 
a  clover-leaf  cooky  cutter  and  finish 
off  with"  Bavarian  cream  and  nut  cake 
colored  a  very  delicate  green  with 
confectioner's  vegetable  dye. 

Write  a  choice  quotation  from  Moore 
or  Goldsmith  or  Emmet,  as  vour  taste 


may  dictate.  Write  it  in  gold  or  silver 
ink,  on  green  paper,  fold  and  enclose 
it  in  a  tiny  silk  bag,  two  inches  square, 
made  of  thin  green  ribbon,  two  inches 
wide,  and  drawn  up  with  Roman  twist 
embroidery  silk,  so  as  to  look  like  a 
miniature  work-bag  or  reticule.  Pre- 
sent one  to  each  departing  guest. 


Dust 

By  Adelaide  Goodwin 

HOW  can  housekeepers  be  awak- 
ened to  the  dangers  of  dust? 
It  is  usually  objected  to  as  a  sign  of 
poor  housekeeping,  merely.  Failure 
to  recognize  in  dust  a  possible  source 
of  infection  is  due  mainly  to  ignorance, 
and  speedy  enlightenment  on  the  sub- 
ject should  be  the  aim  of  all  house- 
keepers. When  the  possibilities  of 
dust  are  understood  generally,  as  they 
are  now  by  the  few,  surely  the  house- 
keeper will  be  first  to  guard  against 
this  source  of  danger. 

A  beginning  may  be  made  by  adopt- 
ing better  methods  of  sweeping  and 
dusting.  Sometimes  it  would  seem 
that  whatever  is  done  with  a  broom 
is  considered  a  cleaning  process. 

It  would  be  an  interesting  experi- 
ment to  hold  up  sweepers  occasionally, 
and  inquire  of  them  just  what  it  is 
they  are  doing  with  their  brooms. 
What  they  think  they  are  doing  and 
what  they  are  doing  are  two  different 
things.  Instead  of  making  for  cleanli- 
ness they  are  scattering  uncleanness. 

Improper  sweeping  and  dusting  are 
to  be  found  at  times  in  places  where 
we  should  least  expect  to  find  them. 
If  the  reader  happens  to  be  obliged 
to  trust  the  cleaning  of  her  rooms  to 
a  woman,  be  she  maid  or  mistress,  who 
is  always  referred  to  as  "neat,"  she 
deserves  sympathy,  for  such  a  neat 
woman  is  often  the  worst  offender. 
She  generally  speaks  of  the  care  of 
the  home  as  "work,"  and  goes  about 
it  with  a  sort  of  rigid  resignation  that 
bodes  ill  to  one's  personal  possessions. 


HOME  IDEAS  AND  ECONOMIES 


387 


Sweeping  should  mean  the  removal 
of  dust,  but  too  often  it  means  scatter- 
ing it  to  places  from  which  it  cannot 
be  taken  without  extra  labor.  A 
friend  once  told  me  that  she  had 
learned  many  things  about  sweeping 
and  dusting  by  being  obliged  to  do 
both  with  an  invalid  in  the  room. 
It  was  absolutely  necessary  to  raise 
the  least  dust  possible. 

Now  there  is  dust  and  dust.  Some 
of  it  is  comparatively  harmless. 
Country  dust  is  quite  different  from 
city  dust.  Some  shows  up  effectively 
and  leaves  a  smooch  when  touched. 
Of  course  various  kinds  of  handling 
must  be  employed  in  removing  the 
different  kinds  of  dust,  but  in  all 
cases  the  point  is  to  get  it  off  things 
without  soiling  them,  and  to  dispose 
of  it  in  a  sanitary  fashion. 

The  scattering  process,  however  we 
may  dignify  it  in  housekeeping,  is  at 
best  a  senseless  proceeding.  Many 
people  do  not  see  that  they  are  scatter- 
ing dust  until  attention  is  called  to  it, 
and  even  then  they  will  not  and  some- 
times apparently  cannot  adopt  better 
ways,  even  of  their  own  invention. 

The  use  of  cloths  and  brooms  that 
have  been  dampened  will  revolutionize 
sweeping  and  dusting.  By  their  use 
we  may  actually  take  away  dust,  which 
is  not  accomplished  by  the  ordinary 
method.  Sweepers  expect  to  raise  a 
certain  amount  of  dust,  and  regard 
quite  complacently  the  gray  cloud 
surrounding  them.  How  unnecessary, 
when,  by  wringing  out  a  cloth  or  mop 
in  the  hottest  water  possible  and 
going  over  a  dusty  rug  or  carpet  before 
sweeping,  one  may  secure  quietly  all 
the  loose  dust  that  otherwise  would 
fly  at  the  touch  of  a  dry  broom.  This 
should  be  repeated  after  the  mop  has 
been  rinsed  clean  in  the  hot  water. 

Or,  if  we  do  the  sweeping  with  a 
broom  that  has  been  wet  in  hot  water, 
the  broom  first  having  been  beaten 
nearly  dry,  almost  no  dust  will  rise. 
This   preliminary    dampening   of   dust 


or  lint  with  a  mop  or  broom  rolls  it 
into  wads,  which  can  be  taken  up  and 
burned.  After  this,  the  rest  of  the 
sweeping  process  is  a  pleasure,  and 
the  rug  or  carpet  not  only  looks  clean 
but  is  truly  clean. 

The  oftener  we  rinse  the  broom  in 
hot  water,  the  better  the  result,  since 
the  broom  takes  up  all  the  dust  it  can 
hold  very  quickly.  Running  hot  water 
direct  from  the  faucet  cleans  the  broom 
easily  and  quickly,  but  care  must  be 
taken  not  to  allow  threads  or  hairs 
from  the  broom  to  get  into  the  pipes. 
They  should  be  removed  before  each 
rinsing.  Where  there  is  no  running 
water,  pails  or  tubs  may  be  used,  and 
the  water  should  be  frequently  changed. 

Experiments  with  dampened  brooms 
and  with  cloths  wrung  dry  from  hot 
water  will  well  repay  us.  A  piece  of 
old,  soft  cheesecloth,  which  has  been 
sprinkled  with  hot  water  by  means  of 
a  whisk  broom,  or  better  still,  damp- 
ened with  steam,  may  be  safely  used 
for  dusting  delicate  books,  photographs, 
plaster  casts,  sofa  cushions,  couch 
covers,  wall  paper,  and,  in  fact,  most 
of  the  things  in  any  room.  It  may  be 
done  very  quickly  indeed,  as  often  as 
we  wish  and  without  upsetting  the 
room  any  more  than  we  choose. 

It  is  clear  that  the  more  dust  we 
can  take  out  without  scattering,  the 
less  will  be  left  to  settle  down  again. 
We  are  all  familiar  with  the  appear- 
ance of  rooms  after  the  sweeping  and 
consequent  dusting  by  the  dry  process. 
The  damp  method  should  be  valued, 
not  only  because  it  improves  the  ap- 
pearance, but  also  because  it  saves  labor 
for  busy  women  who  must  sometimes 
put  housecleaning  second  to  other 
occupations.  In  this  way  we  gain 
freshness  and  a  longer  respite  from  the 
untidy,  unrestful,  nagging  effect  of 
rooms  calling  for  attention. 

People  who  are  devoted  to  the  dust- 
scattering  feather  duster  will  probably 
not  take  kindly  to  these  methods;  but 
those  who  live  in  the  dusty  sections  of 


388  THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 

cities  and  who  know  what  it  means  The  "handy  man"  has  just  put  up  a 
to  allow  dust  to  remain  free  in  our  sloping  shelf  over  my  work  table  to  hold 
rooms  will  welcome  the  detaining  my  cook  books,  and  it  is  such  a  conve- 
power  of  damp  cheesecloth  and  brooms,  nience  that  I  wish  to  thank  the  Cooking- 
School  Magazine  for  suggesting  it.

The  Oatmeal  Drop  Cookies,  given  in  recipes  for  bread  of  all  kinds  that  call

"little  cakes,"  if  mixed  a  trifle  stiff  and  two  tablespoonfuls  to  a  pint  of  water,

In  the  Wood 

By  Cora  A.  Matson  Dolson 


Down  the  green  wood  in  the  spring  we  went, 

Each  with  the  joy  of  the  spring  content; 
Hand  in  hand,  and  some  secret  learned 
Whichever  way  in  the  wood  we  turned; 

While  laughter  and  bird-note  their  sweet  songs  lent. 

Maple,  and  beech,  and  elm  found  we. 

As  we  met  them  here,  good  company; 
And  all  the  little  wild  folk  of  the  wood, 
From  violet,  under  her  dark  blue  hood. 

To  the  foxfire  light  of  a  long-dead  tree. 

And  the  folk  that  almost  as  human  seem: 

The  blackbird,  winging  a  scarlet  gleam. 

The  squirrel,  now  here  —  then  whisked  from  sight, 
The  partridges,  whirring  in  sudden  flight. 

And  the  gray  owl,  dozing  a  twelve-hour  dream. 

And  he,  with  his  small  soft  hand  in  mine. 
With  his  cheeks  the  color  of  brown-red  wine, 
His  hair  that  prisoned  the  sun's  warm  rays. 
His  eyes  to  the  new  wood-sights  adaze. 
And  his  birthdays  ringing  the  round  of  nine. 

The  wood,  in  the  spring,  is  as  fair  to  see: 

The  wood,  where  we  talked  with  flower  and  with  tree  - 

But  severed,  as  sea  is  from  sea  apart, 

Seem  the  bearded  cheeks  and  the  burdened  heart  — 
From  the  boy  who  walked  in  the  wood  with  me. 


THIS  department  is  for  the  benefit  and  free  use  of  our  subscribers.  Questions  relating  to 
recipes,  and  those  pertaining  to  culinary  science  and  domestic  economics  in  general, 
will  be  cheerfully  answered  by  the  editor.  For  menus  remit  $1.00.  Communications 
for  this  department  must  reach  us  before  the  first  of  the  month  preceding  that  in  which  the 
answers  are  expected  to  appear.  In  letters  requesting  answer  by  mail,  please  enclose  postage 
stamps.  Address  queries  to  Janet  M.  Hill,  Editor  Boston  Cooking-School  Magazine,  372 
Boylston  Street,  Boston,  Mass. 


Query  1325.  —  Mrs.  E.  H.  B.,  Boston: 
"Recipe  for  preserving  cumquat  oranges." 

Cumquat  Orange  Preserves 

Cut  the  oranges  (without  peeling) 
into  halves  or  quarters  and  take  out 
the  seeds.  Weigh  the  pieces  of  orange. 
Take  one  pound  or  three-fourths  a 
pound  (according  to  richness  desired) 
of  sugar  for  each  pound  of  orange, 
also  half  the  weight  of  the  fruit  in 
water.  Let  the  fruit  simmer  in  the 
water  until  it  is  perfectly  tender  but 
not  in  the  least  broken.  Skim  out 
the  fruit.  To  the  liquid,  which  has 
lost  in  volume  by  evaporation,  add 
water  that  the  whole  may  equal  half 
the  weight  of  the  sugar;  add  also  the 
juice  of  a  lemon  for  each  pound  of 
fruit.  When  the  sugar  has  melted  let 
the  whole  boil  five  or  six  minutes,  then 
skim,  add  the  cumquat s  and  let  sim- 
mer until  transparent.  Should  the 
syrup  be  too  thin,  reduce  it  by  boiling. 
The  syrup  should  be  of  the  same  con- 
sistency as  that  in  jars  of  preserved 
ginger. 


Query  1326.  —  L.  I.,  Chihuahua,  Mexico: 
"Recipes  for  molasses  cake  and  oatmeal 
cookies." 

Oatmeal  Cookies 

Beat  one  egg  until  light.  Add  one- 
fourth  a  cup,  each,  of  sugar,  thin  cream, 
and  milk.      Add   half   a    cup    of   fine 


oatmeal,  two  cups  of  flour,  two  tea- 
spoonfuls  of  baking-powder  and  one 
teaspoonful  of  salt,  sifted  together. 
Mix  thoroughly,  then  turn  onto  a 
floured  board,  roll  out  into  a  sheet, 
cut  in  shapes  and  bake  in  a  moderate 
oven. 

Oatmeal  Macaroons 

Beat  two  eggs,  without  separating 
the  whites  and  yolks ;  gradually  beat  in 
one  cup  of  sugar,  then  a  tablespoonful 
of  softened  (not  melted)  butter,  one- 
half  a  teaspoonful  of  salt,  half  a  tea- 
spoonful  of  vanilla  and  two  cups  and 
a  half  of  rolled  oats.  Mix  all  together 
thoroughly.  Drop  from  a  teaspoon 
onto  a  buttered  baking  sheet.  Bake 
in  a  moderate  oven. 

Rochester  Gingerbread 

Beat  half  a  cup  of  butter  to  a  cream; 
gradually  beat  in  half  a  cup  of  sugar 
and  one  cup  of  molasses.  Add  two 
eggs,  beaten  very  light,  one  cup  of 
thick,  sour  milk  and  three  cups  of 
sifted  flour,  sifted  again  with  one  tea- 
spoonful and  a  half  of  soda,  one  tea- 
spoonful of  ginger  and  one  teaspoonful 
of  cinnamon.  Bake  in  a  large  shallow 
pan  or  in  two  brick-loaf  pans. 


Query  1327.  —  Miss  L.  W.,  Cambridge, 
Mass.:  "Recipe  for  white  cookies  that  are 
quite  thick  and  keep  soft,  also  dark  graham 
bread  and  Newport  cake." 


389 


390 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


Thick  White  Cookies 

Beat  half  a  cup  of  butter  to  a  cream; 
gradually  beat  in  one  cup  of  granulated 
sugar,  then  one  egg,  beaten  without 
separating,  half  a  cup  of  thick,  sour 
cream  and  two  cups  and  a  half  of 
sifted  flour,  sifted  again  with  half  a 
teaspoonful  of  soda;  drop  the  dough 
from  a  spoon  onto  buttered  baking 
tins  and  bake  in  a  moderate  oven. 

Thick  White  Cookies,  No.  2 
Beat  half  a  cup  of  butter  to  a  cream ; 
gradually  beat  in  one  cup  of  sugar,  one 
egg,  beaten  without  separating  the 
white  and  the  yolk,  then  half  a  cup  of 
milk  and  two  cups  of  sifted  flour, 
sifted  again  with  three  level  teaspoon- 
fuls  of  baking  powder.  Take  the  mix- 
ture, a  little  at  a  time,  onto  a  floured 
board,  knead  slightly,  roll  into  a  sheet 
half  an  inch  thick,  cut  into  shapes  and 
bake  in  a  moderate  oven.  Keep  the 
dough  soft,  lifting  the  dough,  when 
cut  out,  with  a  spatula  if  needed. 

Dark  Graham  Bread 

See  recipe  given  on  page  344,  of  the 
February  number  of  this  magazine. 
Increase  the  quantity  of  molasses  to 
half  a  cup  and  cut  down  the  liquid 
used  one-fourth  a  cup,  to  keep  the 
proportions  the  same  as  in  the  recipe 
referred  to. 

Newport  Cake 

We  have  not  been  successful  in 
finding  a  cake  of  this  name  and  would 
be  pleased  to  receive  an  authentic 
recipe. 


Query  1328.  —Mrs.  T.  B.  C,  New  York 
City:  "Recipe  for  sweetbreads  and  mush- 
rooms served  under  the  glass  bells  used  for 
mushrooms." 

Sweetbreads,  Alice  (Fillippini) 

Soak  six  small,  round  sweetbreads 
in  cold  water  two  hours,  then  drain 
and  set  over  the  fire  in  two  quarts  of 
boiling  water,  to  which  two  teaspoonfuls 


of  salt  have  been  added.  Let  cook 
five  minutes,  then  drain  and  trim 
neatly.  When  the  sweetbreads  are 
cool,  draw  into  each  four  lardoons  of 
fat  salt  pork.  Lay  the  sweetbreads  in 
a  saucepan,  add  a  dash  of  salt  and 
pepper,  a  grating  of  nutmeg,  a  cup  and 
a  half  of  cream  and  a  cup  of  milk. 
Cover  closely  and  let  cook  ten  minutes, 
then  add  twelve  peeled  mushroom 
heads  and  let  cook  fifteen  minutes 
longer.  Cream  a  level  tablespoonful 
of  butter  and  beat  into  a  level  table- 
spoonful  of  flour;  dilute  this  with  a 
little  of  the  hot  cream  and,  when 
evenly  blended  with  this,  stir  it  care- 
fully (to  avoid  breaking  the  mush- 
rooms, etc.)  into  the  dish  on  the 
range.  Have  ready  six  egg  shirrers- 
fitted  with  glass  bells  or  covers,  and 
six  rounds  of  toast,  the  diameter  of 
the  bells.  Place  a  slice  of  toast  in 
each  shirrer,  set  a  sweetbread  and  two^ 
mushrooms  above,  then  cover  with 
the  bells.  Pour  the  sauce  into  each 
dish  around  the  bells.  Set  on  the 
range  and  as  soon  as  the  sauce  boils- 
set  the  dishes  into  a  moderate  oven. 
Let  bake  ten  minutes.  Set  the  shirrers 
onto  dinner  plates  without  removing 
the  bells.  Remove  the  bells  after  the 
plates  have  been  set  in  place  before 
the  guests. 


Query  1329. — Subscriber:  "What  per- 
cent should  a  person  charge  above  the  cost 
of  materials  to  make  a  fair  profit  on  articles 
of  home-made  food  like  marmalade  and. 
cake?  Sunshine  cake  takes  at  least  one 
hour  and  a  quarter  of  close  attention." 

Profit  on  Home-made  Food 

At  certain  of  the  Women's  Exchanges 
the  price  of  the  various  ingredients  — 
at  retail  — ■  which  go  to  make  up  the 
article  is  determined;  this  sum  is 
doubled  and  becomes  the  selling  price 
of  the  article.  Thus,  if  the  cost  of  the 
materials  needed  for  a  sunshine  cake 
amounts  to  thirty  cents,  the  cake  should 
sell  for  sixty  cents.  Thirty  cents  can- 
not be  considered  a  high  price  for  fueL 
and  skilled  labor  for  one  hour  and  a. 


QUERIES  AND  ANSWERS 


391 


quarter.  It  would  not  be  easy  to 
procure  other  skilled  labor  at  this 
figure. 


Query  1330.  —  Mrs.  J.  T.  G.,  Colorado 
Springs,  Colo.:  "Menu  for  a  formal  dinner^ 
with  suggestions  as  to  serving, -etc. " 

Consomme  with  Vegetables,  Rasped  Rolls 
Salted  Nuts,  Olives 

Fresh  or  Canned  Salmon  Cutlets,  Peas 

Cuciraiber-and-Radish  Salad  or 

Sauce  Tartare 

Brown  Fricassee  of  Sweetbreads  in 
Patty  Shells  or  Swedish  Timbale  Cases 

Boned  Loin  of  Lamb,  Roasted,  Mint  Sauce 

Potatoes  Anna,  Individual  Shapes 

Banana  Fritters  or  Baked   Bananas,    Claret 

Sauce 

Cheese  Balls.     Lettuce-and-Tomato  Salad 
Toasted  Crackers 

Cup  St.  Jacques 
Ladyfingers.     Meringues.     Macaroons 

Black  Coffee 

Have  the  table  laid  with  service 
plates  and  let  the  soup  be  in  place, 
when  the  guests  seat  themselves.  A 
recipe  for  consomme  may  be  found  in 
any  cook  book.  The  vegetables  used 
as  a  garnish  should  be  cut  into  small, 
neat  shapes.  These  shapes  may  be 
stamped  (with  small  cutters  which 
come  for  the  purpose)  from  slices  of 
cooked  vegetables,  or  the  vegetables 
may  be  cut  into  small  cubes,  squares 
or  match-like  pieces.  Peas,  asparagus 
tips  and  flageolet  (green  kidney-shaped 
beans)  of  course  are  left  whole.  A , 
variety  of  colors,  green,  orange  "(carrot) , 
white  (turnip  or  artichoke),  are  at- 
tractive. If  fresh  salmon  be  available, 
select  a  piece  from  the  tail  end,  from 
which  cutlet  shapes  may  be  easily  cut. 
Using  canned  salmon  a  croquette  mix- 
ture with  foundation  of  thick  white 
sauce  would  be  prepared.  Fine- 
chopped  parsley,  mixed  with  the 
crumbs  used  in  the  final  breading, 
gives  a  spring  look  to  the  dish.  Serve 
peas  on  the  plate  v/ith  the  fish,  the 


salad  on  a  small,  cold  plate,  or  if  sauce 
tartare  take  the  place  of  the  salad, 
pass  this  in  a  mayonnaise  bowl,  set 
on  a  doily -covered  plate  or  small  tray. 
Pass  rolls  after  the  fish  has  been  served 
and  again  whenever  any  one's  supply 
is  exhausted,  until  time  for  serving 
the  cheese  and  salad,  but  do  not  pass 
them  with  the  entree  of  sweetbreads, 
as  the  patty  shells  or  Swedish  cases 
take  the  place  of  bread.  Serve  the 
entree  on  small  plates,  setting  them 
down  upon  the  service  plate  from 
which  the  fish  plate  has  been  taken. 
Remove  the  service  and  patty  plates 
together,  or  leave  the  service  plate 
and  substitute  for  it  the  plate  with 
lamb  and  potatoes,  when  it  is  time  to 
serve  this  course.  Serve  the  bananas 
on  small,  hot,  individual  dishes,  or 
they  may  be  brought  in  on  the  plate 
with  the  lamb  and  potatoes. 

Bring  in  the  lettuce  and  tomato 
salad  on  small,  cold  plates;  pass  the 
cheese  balls  (hot,  fried)  and  crackers 
on  a  tray.  Cup  St.  Jacques  is  a  mace- 
doine  of  fruit  (pineapple,  orange,  straw- 
berries, cherries,  two  or  more  varieties 
in  a  rich  syrup  flavored  to  taste)  in 
tall  glasses,  with  a  spoonful  of  fruit 
sherbet  above. 


Query  1331. — Miss  A.  H.,  Manhattan: 
"How  do  you  serve  celery  for  company? 
How  do  you  arrange  it  in  a  celery  dish? 
Do  you  leave  on  the  green  tops,  and  if  so 
how  much  of  them?  Do  you  add  salt  before 
or  after  cooking  rice  ? ' ' 

Serving  Celery 

The  roots  of  celery  are  much  more 
edible  than  the  outer  stalks.  Thus 
the  heads  of  celery,  when  properly 
made  ready  for  market,  are  fastened 
together  in  bunches  with  rafha  or 
similar  material,  but  the  heads  of  much 
of  the  celery  found  in  the  market  are 
held  together  by  nails  pressed  through 
the  roots,  and  when  such  celery  is  pur- 
chased the  roots  are  spoiled  and  must 
be  discarded.  If  nails  have  not  been 
used,  remove  the  green  outer  stalks 
and  trim  off  the  tops  of  others  that 


392 


THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


present  hollow  stalks.  Do  not  trim 
the  inner  stalks.  Cut  the  prepared 
heads  in  halves,  lengthwise,  down 
through  the  root;  if  the  halves  are  too 
large  for  one  service,  cut  them  again 
in  halves,  lengthwise,  through  the 
root.  Wash  these  in  cold  water,  scrap- 
ing the  root  end.  Let  them  stand  in 
clean,  cold  water  some  little  time,  to 
become  crisp,  then  wipe  dry  and  dis- 
pose in  a  glass  or  china  celery  tray. 
Celery  is  liable  to  turn  rusty,  after 
being  wet,  and  thus  it  should  not  be 
made  ready  to  serve  very  long  before- 
hand. When  wilted  and  time  to  make 
it  crisp  is  short,  add  a  tablespoonful  of 
vinegar  or  a  lemon-rind  to  the  water. 

Salt  in  Cooking  Rice 

Blanch  rice  in  unsalted  water,  then 
set  to  cook  in  boiling,  salted  liquid. 


Query  1332.  —  Mrs.  P.  M.  V.,  Chicago, 
111.:  "The  best  way  of  cooking  kidneys.  Is 
there  any  way  to  keep  milk  from  curdling 
in  macaroni  and  cheese?  Recipe  for  cook- 
ing noodles  and  cheese.  Can  most  salads 
be  masked  with  the  dressing?  Recipe  for 
cooking  mushrooms  in  some  rich,  brown 
sauce,  and  how  is  the  bread  on  which  they 
are  served  prepared?  Would  like  the  order 
of  service  for  a  luncheon  of  nine  courses. 
Does  entree  mean  an  article  served  alone  as 
a  course? " 

Cooking  Kidneys 

Kidneys  are  tender  after  five  minutes' 
cooking;  if  cooked  longer  it  must  be 
for  several  hours.  On  account  of  their 
firm,  close  texture  kidneys  are  not 
easily  digested.  As  these  organs,  from 
their  peculiar  ofhce,  are  liable  to  become 
diseased,  it  were  better  to  select  kid- 
neys from  a  young  rather  than  an  old 
creature,  and  also  to  cook  them  several 
hours.  Long  cooking  would  render  any 
disease  germs  present  unharmful. 

Kidneys  Saute  on  Toast 

Purchase  six  veal  or  lamb  kidneys. 
Remove  the  outer  skin,  then  cut  the 
kidneys  into  round  slices  about  one- 
third  an  inch  thick.  Let  the  slices 
stand  covered  with  cold,  salted  water 


about  half  an  hour,  then  drain  and 
dry  on  a  cloth.  Melt  three  table- 
spoonfuls  of  butter  in  a  small  sauce- 
pan ;  in  it  cook  a  slice  of  onion  and  two 
of  carrot;  when  the  vegetables  are 
delicately  browned,  remove  them  and 
put  in  the  pieces  of  kidney  and  stir 
constantly  while  cooking  one  minute, 
then  add  two  tablespoonfuls  of  flour 
and  stir  while  cooking  one  minute,  or 
until  the  flour  is  blended  with  the 
butter ;  then  add  one  '  cup  of  highly- 
seasoned  brown  stock  and  stir  con- 
stantly until  the  sauce  is  brought 
quickly  to  the  boiling  point.  Season 
with  salt  and  pepper.  Lemon  juice, 
Worcestershire  sauce,  wine  or  chopped 
parsley  are  often  used  to  flavor  the 
dish.  Fresh  mushrooms  are  often 
cooked  with  the  kidneys.  Serve  on 
buttered  toast. 

Kidney  Saute,  No.  2 
Remove  the  outer  skin  from  the 
kidneys,  let  stand  in  cold,  salted  water 
half  an  hour,  then  set  to  cook  in  boil- 
ing' water.  After  boiling  two  minutes 
let  simmer  six  or  eight  hours,  or  until 
very  tender.  When  cold,  cut  in  slices, 
and  add  to  a  cup  of  hot  brown  sauce. 
Reheat  without  boiling.  An  omelet 
surrounded  with  kidneys  saute  is  con- 
sidered a  choice  dish  by  many. 

Curdling  of  Milk  in  Macaroni  and 
Cheese 

Try  cooking  with  less  heat.  Milk 
and  cheese,  both,  are  articles  that  are 
injured  in  a  hot  oven.  The  macaroni  is 
cooked  before  the  cheese  and  milk  are 
added  to  it  and  there  is  no  occasion  for 
heat  enough  to  curdle  pure,  clean  milk. 

Noodles 

Noodles  are  cooked  by  any  of  the 
recipes  given  for  macaroni.  See  page 
325,  of  the  February  number  of  this 
magazine.  Like  macaroni,  noodles  are 
first  cooked  in  rapidly-boiling,  salted 
water.  They  need  cooking  about 
thirty  minutes.

HOT  BOUILLON  FOR  COLD  AFTERNOONS 

By  jVJ^ry  Jai^e  JVXcCltjre 

^  March  swirls  and  eddies  over  snow-sprinkled  hill  and  vale  in  a  mad  dance.  Somewhere  in  the  bare 
branches  a  lone  winter  bird  dreams  of  a  mate  and  breaks  out  in  a  carol  of  joy.  In  the  woods,  beneath 
dr\',  dead  leaves  and  mouldering  mast,  the  violets  are  stirring-  uneasily  and  cautiously  peering-  through 
to  catch  a  fleeting  glimpse  of  the  feeble  sun.  The  sap  is  sluggishly  starting  in  the  trees  and  swelling 
.  buds  and  crimson-threaded  hazel-blooms  prophesy  the  coming  of  the  sprmgtime.  Lent  looks  frown- 
ingly  upon  the  wild  revelry  of  the  month  of  the  four  winds.  The  season  spells  sermon  and  salmon  and 
dull,  dreary  days  to  the  devotee. 


^A.STING  forces  fashion  into  the 
background  for  the  nonce  and 
frivolity  finds  small  favor.  The 
regalia  of  society  is  laid  away 
in  lavender  and  the  afternoon 
call  is  the  only  form  of  dissipa- 
tion indulged  in.  One  ingenious 
dame  of  society,  in  order  lo 
sharpen  the  dull  edge  of  formal  calls,  has  introduced 
a  quaint  innovation  in  entertaining.  Her  little  girl, 
scarcely  more  than  a 
baby,  prepares  and 
serves  bouillon  to  the 
guests.  The  entrance 
of  the  fair  little  one 
never  fails  to  thaw  the 
ice  of  formality  and  in- 
duce a  flow  of  soul. 

The  bouillon,  of  ne- 
cessity, is  a  very  simple 
concoction.  It  would  be 
impossible  for  the  tiny 
child  to  prepare  any- 
thing complicated.  Ar- 
mour's Extract  of  Beef 
forms  the  basis  of  it. 
The  cups  are  filled  with 
boiling  water,  and  in 
each  one  a  quarter  of  a 
teaspoonf ul  of  Armour' s 
Extract  of  Beef  is  stir- 
red. Pepper  and  salt  is 
added,  and  the  resulting 
royally  rich  bouillon  is 
ready  to  serve. 

The  housewife  who  thinks  Armour's  Extract  of 
Beef  is  a  product  to  be  used  only  in  case  of  sick- 
ness, when  the  doctor  orders  "beef  tea,"  or  on 
special  occasions,  still  has  much  to  learn.  This 
delicious  concentration  of  savory  juices  of  the 
primest  beef  is  as  essential  in  every-day  cooking  as 
salt  or  pepper.  European  cooks  and  housewives 
recognize  this  fact,  and  never  are  without  it.     Be- 


sides making  a  flavoury  bouillon  when  used  alone, 
the  addition  of  the  merest  trifle  of  Armour's  Ex- 
tract of  Beef  gives  zest  to  the  soup,  meats,  vege- 
tables, sauces  and  gravies  which  tantalizes  the  taste 
and  wooes  the  appetite.  For  gravies  it  is  practi- 
cally indispensable.  It  produces  a  color  that  is  just 
right  and  at  the  same  time  gives  it  a  browned-meat 
flavor  which  cannot  be  equalled  by  the  most  elab- 
orate combinations  of  materials. 

During  the  forty  days'  fast  Armour's  Extract  of 
Beef  is  bound  to  prove 
a  blessing  in  disguise  to 
the  puzzled  cook.  It 
will  impart  a  beefy 
smack  to  dishes  without 
meat  and  make  it  pos- 
sible to  prepare  a  splen- 
did mixture  that  will 
sharpen  the  appetite 
with  cloyless  sauce  and 
tempt  a  hungry  mortal 
to  eat  the  stew  and  then 
beg  for  the  pan. 

Armour's  Extract  of 
Beef  combines  most 
happily  with  cheese  and 
may  be  introduced  with 
safety  into  almost  any 
recipe  where  cheese  is 
an  ingredient,  with  ex- 
cellent results. 

The  following  recipe 
is  a  tasty  variation  from 
the  old-fashioned,  plain 
omelet. 
Cheese  Omelet. — Break  six  eggs  into  a  dish  and 
stir  them  gently.  Add  one-half  cupful  of  grated  or 
chipped  cheese,  salt  and  pepper  to  taste  and  one- 
fourth  teaspoonful  of  Armour's  Extract  of  Beef 
dissolved  in  one  tablespoonful  of  boiling  milk. 
Melt  two  tablespoonfuls  of  butter  in  the  pan,  turn 
in  the  mixture  and  cook  slowly.  Cut  in  quarters 
and  turn  when  brown.

THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


Masking  Salads  with  Dressing 

Salads,  made  up  of  small  bits  of  one 
or  more  articles  and  dressed  with 
mayonnaise  or  a  boiled  dressing, 
may  be  "masked"  or  covered  with 
dressing. 

Mushrooms  in  Brown  Sauce 

Peel  fresh  mushrooms  and  remove 
the  stems.  Dry  the  stems  and  peel- 
ings to  use  in  another  dish,  at  some 
future  time.  If  the  caps  are  of  good 
size,  break  them  into  two  or  three 
pieces.  For  a  generous  or  heaping  cup 
of  mushrooms  melt  three  tablespoon- 
fuls  of  butter  in  a  frying-pan;  add  the 
mushrooms  and  cook  and  stir  three  or 
four  minutes,  then  add  two  tablespoon- 
fuls  of  flour  and  one-fourth  a  tea- 
spoonful  of  salt  and  stir  and  cook  until 
the  flour  is  blended  with  the  butter; 
then  add  a  cup  of  rich,  highly  seasoned, 
brown  stock  and  stir  until  the  liquid 
boils,  then  let  simmer  five  minutes. 
Have  ready  four  slices  of  toast  spread 
with  butter;  pour  the  mushrooms  and 
sauce  over  the  toast  and  serve  at  once. 
Sometimes  rounds  of  bread  are  cut 
from  slices  nearly  two  inches  thick,  and 
the  center  is  stamped  out  from  these 
rounds,  to  make  a  case,  or  bread  patty 
case;  the  case  is  dipped  into  melted 
butter  and  browned  in  the  oven.  In 
these  cases  the  mushrooms  are  served. 
When  the  cases  are  used,  three-fourths 
a  cup,  rather  than  a  full  cup,  of  broth 
should  be  used. 

Order  of  Service 
Luncheon  of  Nine  Courses 

1  Appetizer    (raw    oysters    or    fruit 

cocktail,  etc.). 

2  Soup. 

3  Fish. 

4  Entree. 

5  Entree. 

6  Main  Dish. 

7.  Game,  with  Salad,  or  Cheese  and 
Salad,  or  Handsome  Salad. 

8  Sweet  Dishes. 

9  Coffee. 


Meaning  of  Entree 

An  entree  is  a  dish,  served  between 
the  main  courses  of  a  meal  (after  the 
fish  or  the  roast),  or  in  conjunction 
with  one  of  the  courses.  It  need  not, 
necessarily,  be  served  alone  as  a  course, 
though  it  often  is — much  depends  upon 
the  character  of  the  dish  and  the  atten- 
tion the  composer  of  the  menu  wishes 
to  bring  to  bear  upon  it..  A  choice 
cauliflower  or  bunch  of  asparagus,  with 
HoUandaise  sauce,  may  be  served  en- 
tirely alone  or  with  the  main  dish  of  the 
meal;  in  either  case  it  is  an  entree. 


An  Old  Nurse 

Persuaded  Doctor  to  Drink  Postum 

An  old  faithful  nurse  and  an  experi- 
enced doctor,  are  a  pretty  strong  com- 
bination in  favor  of  Postum,  instead  of 
coffee. 

The  doctor  said:  — 

"I  began  to  drink  Postum  five  years 
ago  on  the  advice  of  an  old  nurse. 

"During  an  unusually  busy  winter, 
between  coffee,  tea  and  overwork,  I 
became  a  victim  of  insomnia.  In  a 
month  after  beginning  Postum  in  place 
of  coffee  I  could  eat  anything  and 
sleep  as  soundly  as  a  baby. 

"In  three  months  I  had  gained 
twenty  pounds  in  weight.  I  now  use 
Postum  altogether  instead  of  coffee; 
even  at  bedtime  with  a  soda  cracker 
or  some  other  tasty  biscuit. 

"Having  a  little  tendency  to  Dia- 
betes, I  use  a  small  quantity  of  sac- 
charine instead  of  sugar,  to  sweeten 
with.  I  may  add  that  today  tea  or 
coffee  are  never  present  in  our  house 
and  very  many  patients,  on  my  advice, 
have  adopted  Postum  as  their  regular 
beverage. 

"In  conclusion  I  can  assure  anyone 
that,  as  a  refreshing,  nourishing  and 
nerve-strengthening  beverage,  there  is 
nothing  equal  to  Postum."  "There's 
a  Reason."  Name  given  by  Postum 
Co.,  Battle  Creek,  Mich.  Read,  "The 
Road  to  Wellville,"  in  pkgs.

Mandalay  Sauce 

is  composed  of  choicest  fruits,  vegetables  and  spices 
of  foreign  and  domestic  origin,  skilfully  blended  after  a 
recipe  found  by  an  English  army  officer  in  the  Far  East. 

It  imparts  incomparable  goodness  to  hot  or  cold 
meats,  fish,  game,  soups  and  gravies.  Indispensable 
for  all  chafing-dish  cooking — Welsh  rarebits,  cheese 
toast,  and  so  on.  Stimulates  the  jaded  appetite;  is 
unquestionably  wholesome. 

Try  a  bottle  from  your  grocer  ;  it  is  far  removed 
from  common  sauces — unlike  any  other  in  flavor. 

0\hzt  seasonable  Heinz  delicacies  are  Sweet 
Pickles,  Preserved  Fruits,  Jellies,  Cranberry  Sauce, 
Euchred  Figs,  Tomato  Chutney,  etc.  Our  free  book- 
let, "The  Spice   of  Life,"  tells   all  about  them.

THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


Query  1333.  —  Subscriber:  "Kindly  state 
what  is  served  with  fish  in  a  dinner  or 
luncheon  of  several  courses.  When  cucumber 
salad  is  served  with  fish,  and  from  a  salad 
bowl,  are  the  cucumbers  laid  on  the  fish 
plate  and  eaten  with  the  fish  fork  ? " 

Regarding  Fish  Course  at- 

Formal  Meal 

Peas,  string  beans,  or  potatoes  in 
some  fancy  form,  as  balls  in  cream 
sauce,  or  maitre  d'hotel  style,  are 
usually  served  with  fish;  rolls  are 
passed,  when  peas  or  string  beans 
are  served,  and  may  or  may  not  be 
supplied,  with  potatoes.  Cucumbers 
with  French  dressing,  to  which  a  few 
drops  of  onion  juice  have  been  added, 
are  disposed  in  small  chilled  plates 
and  set  at  the  right  of  the  dinner 
plate,  and  are  to  be  eaten  with  the  fish 
fork. 


showing  these  high  cakes  built  up  in 
drum-like  sections  and  beautifully  orna- 
mented with  flowers,  heraldic  and 
other  appropriate  designs. 


A  Wedding  Cake 

At  a  recent  wedding,  in  Philadelphia, 
of  an  American  girl  to  an  English 
officer,  the  feature  of  the  luncheon 
was  an  English  wedding  cake  made 
at  Bolland's  in  Chester,  England,  the 
man  who  makes  the  famous  cakes  for 
royal  weddings.  It  is  said  that  his 
business  became  known  generally  after 
he  had  presented  a  cake  to  the  youth- 
ful Queen  Victoria  when  she  visited 
Chester.  She  so  liked  the  cakes  he 
sent  her  that  thereafter  he  was  always 
patronized,  and  of  course  royal  example 
was  followed. 

The  Atlantic  liner  bearing  this  great 
cake  for  the  Philadelphia  wedding  was 
three  days  late,  owing  to  heavy  storms, 
and  only  by  especial  efforts  and  a 
tremendous  overhauling  of  the  pack- 
ages was  the  cake  released  in  time 
and  rushed  to  the  house  before  the 
ceremony. 

Myrtle  and  heather  were  introduced 
into  the  bride's  flowers,  in  foreign 
style. 

An  article  appeared  in  this  magazine 
a    few    years    ago    with    illustrations 


Brain  Power 

Increased  by  Proper  Feeding 

A  lady  writer  who  not  only  has  done 
good  literary  work,  but  reared  a  family, 
found  in  Grape-Nuts  the  ideal  food  for 
brain  work  and  to  develop  healthy 
children.     She  writes:  — 

"I  am  an  enthusiastic  proclaimer  of 
Grape-Nuts  as  a  regular  diet.  I  for- 
merly had  no  appetite  in  the  morning 
and  for  eight  years  while  nursing  my 
four  children,  had  insufficient  nourish- 
ment for  them. 

"Unable  to  eat  breakfast  I  felt  faint 
later,  and  would  go  to  the  pantry  and 
eat  cold  chops,  sausage,  cookies,  dough- 
nuts or  anything  I  happened  to  find. 
Being  a  writer,  at  times  my  head  felt 
heavy  and  my  brain  asleep. 

"When  I  read  of  Grape-Nuts  I  be- 
gan eating  it  every  morning,  also  gave 
it  to  the  children,  including  my  ten- 
months  old  baby,  who  soon  grew  as 
fat  as  a  little  pig,  good  natured  and 
contented. 

"Within  a  week  I  had  plenty  of 
breast  milk,  and  felt  stronger  within 
two  weeks.  I  wrote  evenings  and,  feel- 
ing the  need  of  sustained  brain  power, 
began  eating  a  small  saucer  of  Grape- 
Nuts  with  milk  instead  of  my  usual 
indigestible  hot  pudding,  pie,  or:  cake 
for  dessert  at  night. 

"Grape-Nuts  did  wonders  for  me 
and  I  learned  to  like  it.  I  did  not 
mind  my  housework  or  mother's  cares, 
for  I  felt  strong  and  full  of  "go."  I 
grew  plump,  nerves  strong,  and  when 
I  wrote  my  brain  was  active  and  clear; 
indeed,  the  dull  head  pain  never  re- 
turned." 

"There's  a  Reason." 

Name  given  by  Postum  Co.,  Battle 
Creek,  Mich.  Read  "The  Road  to 
Wellville,"  in  pkgs.

A  Workings  Girls'  Hotel 


While  seventy-five  women's  clubs  in 
their  federated  as  well  as  their  in- 
dividual capacity  have  been  talking 
about  the  crying  need  for  a  working 
girls'  hotel,  and  racking  their  brains  for 
feasible  schemes  to  secure  the  neces- 
sary money,  the  pastor  of  a  little  church 
in  Harlem  is  busy  building  one.  About 
two  years  and  a  half  ago  it  occurred  to 
Rev.  H.  M.  Tyndall  that  the  broken 
stone  and  sand,  which  were  the  by- 
products of  building  excavations  in  the 
city,  might  be  put  to  a  better  use  than 
that  of  increasing  the  size  of  the 
various  dump-heaps.  Why  should  n't 
they  be  purchased,  he  reasoned,  for  a 
nominal  sum  by  contractors  who  are 
putting  up  concrete  buildings  and  be 
used  for  his  new  plan?  He  determined 
to  become  the  first  of  these  contractors. 
His  congregation  had  already  decided 
that  the  pastor's  project  of  a  hotel  for 
working  girls,  under  the  auspices  of  the 
People's  Tabernacle  at  52  East  102d 
Street,  was  worthy  and  practicable  and 
were  ready  to  pledge  their  support. 
So  Mr.  Tyndall  bought  the  stone  and 
sand,  and  collected  a  force  of  Italian 
laborers  to  turn  it  into  concrete.  As 
fast  as  contributions  came  in  the  work 
progressed,  and  the  building  at  58  East 
102d  Street  is  now  completed.  The 
seventy-five  rooms  as  yet  are  bare  of 
furniture. 

"Of  course  we  are  anxious  to  throw 
open  the  doors  of  the  new  house," 
said  Mr.  Tyndall,  "but  that  fact  won't 
influence  us  to  change  our  determina- 
tion about  going  in  debt.  We  have 
been  prosperous  beyond  our  hopes  in 
this  undertaking.  We  have  succeeded 
in  putting  up  a  $75,000  building  for 
$21,000,  and  I  don't  believe  we  will 
have  to  wait  very  long  for  the  $2,000 
necessary  for  furniture.  I  have  calcu- 
lated that  an  average  of  $30  a  room 
will  about  do  it." 

While  the  hotel  is  designed  primarily 
for  wage-earning  girls,   young  women 


who  are  strangers  in  the  city  will  get 
temporary  accommodation,  provided 
they  can  show  credentials  of  respecta- 
bility. Room  and  board  will  be  fur- 
nished at  from  $3  to  $5  a  week.  Wo- 
men over  thirty-five  are  reckoned  as 
undesirable  inmates.  Robert  E.  Tyn- 
dall, brother  of  the  clergyman,  is  to  be 
the  manager  of  the  house,  which  is  to 
be  for  the  benefit  of  permanent  rather 
than  of  transient  guests. 


Cooking  Schools  vs.  Saloons 

That  the  way  to  a  man's  heart  lies 
through  his  stomach  has  long  been  a 
commonplace  of  feminine  philosophy; 
but  many  .of  the  women  of  the  present 
generation  seem  to  have  ignored  it. 
The  relation  of  cookery  to  morals  has 
recently  received  attention  in  public 
addresses  of  two  men  of  widely  different 
points  of  view  —  a  judge  and  a  brewer. 
Speaking  in  praise  of  the  work  in 
domestic  science  done  by  the  Hebrew 
Technical  School  for  Girls,  in  New 
York  City,  Justice  Samuel  Greenbaum, 
of  the  State  Supreme  Court,  said, 
"Many  a  man  spends  his  nights  in  the 
gin-mill  just  because  his  body  is  badly 
nourished  by  ill-cooked  food."  Even 
more  significant  were  the  words  of 
E.  F.  Abner,  president  of  a  Washington 
brewery,  in  discussing  the  liquor  situa- 
tion in  that  city:  "It  is  a  lamentable 
fact  that  too  little  attention  is  given 
nowadays  to  cooking,  and,  as  a  conse- 
quence, when  young  women  marry  and 
preside  over  their  own  homes  they  are 
not  equipped  to  properly  conduct 
them,  whether  they  have  the  cooking 
to  do  themselves  or  merely  direct.  In 
either  case  it  is  highly  necessary  that 
the  knowledge  should  be  of  a  practical 
character.  A  man  will  not  crave  drink 
after  eating  a  good  meal.  The  prohi- 
bitionists had  better  adopt  as  a  slogan: 
"Better  the  home  table  and  keep  the 
man  from  the  free-lunch  counter."

A  Movement  for  Clean  Bread 

A  movement  is  on  foot  in  Washing- 
ton to  safeguard  the  bread  supply  of 
the  pubhc,  by  wrapping  it  in  paper  as 
soon  as  it  has  been  baked.  Some  op- 
position having  been  offered  by  the 
bakers,  who  objected  that  the  wrapping 
would  impair  its  palatability  and  digest- 
ibility, the  health  department  experi- 
mented with  various  kinds  of  paper 
and  discovered  that  the  effect  of  the 
protective  covering  was  to  preserve 
the  freshness  of  the  bread,  which  was 
superior  after  twenty-four  hours  to 
that  which  had  not  been  wrapped. 
Another  advantage  of  the  proposed 
method  is  that  the  union  label,  which 
is  now  affixed  directly  to  the  loaf  —  a 
plan  objectionable  to  many  consumers 
—  could,  with  the  adoption  of  this 
method,  be  used  to  seal  the  wrappers. 
Some  restaurants  in  New  York  have 
already  taken  to  the  use  of  similar 
wrappers  for  the  rolls  which  they  serve 
to  their  patrons. 


Bayberry  Candles  give  a  Pleasant 
Perfume 

Dear  Editor:  To  make  bayberry 
candles  will  take  one  quart  of  berries 
for  each  candle.  Into  a  six-quart 
preserve  kettle  put  three  quarts  of 
berries,  and  fill  to  the  brim  with  cold 
water.  This  allows  two  quarts  of 
water  to  one  of  berries.  Put  the  kettle 
on  the  range  and  let  it  boil  steadily 
four  hours,  and  if  the  water  gets  three 
inches  from  the  brim  fill  it  up  with  hot 
water.  When  the  four  hours  are  up, 
set  the  kettle  back  and  let  the  berries 
simmer  for  an  hour  or  two,  then  farther 
back,  where  they  will  simply  keep  hot. 
At  night  take  them  off  the  range  and 
set  away  to  cool  in  the  kettle.  The 
cooking,  down  from  the  boiling  point 
to  the  point  where  the  wax  begins  to 
form,  should  be  very  gradual  to  obtain 
the  best  results.  If  left  undisturbed 
until  morning  the  wax  will  be  hard  and 
can  be  lifted  out.     Break  it  up  and  put 


into  a  porcelain-lined  saucepan  and  set 
it  on  the  range  where  it  will  melt  slowly; 
then  strain  through  a  fine  wire  strainer 
into  a  shallow  bowl.  Let  the  wax 
become  quite  cold  and  hard.  Melt  and 
strain  again,  this  time  through  a  piece 
of  cheesecloth.  The  candles  are  made 
either  by  dipping  the  wicks  into  the 
melted  wax,  or  by  pouring  it  into  candle 
molds  after  the  wicks  are  in  place. 
We  prefer  the  molds,  as  they  burn 
more  evenly  and  give  a  brighter  light. 
The  bayberry  wax  can  also  be  bought 
from  any  wholesale  druggist. 

Mrs.  E.  L.  B.  Newton. 


The  following  advertisement,  quoted 
from  a  Boston  paper  of  a  date  early 
in  the  nineteenth  century,  shows  that 
the  domestic  problem  is  not  one  of 
modern  manufacture :  ' '  Much  Wanted : 
A  neat,  well-behaved  female  to  do 
kitchen  work  in  a  small  family  in 
Charlestown  near  Boston.  She  may 
pray  and  sing  hymns,  but  not  over 
the  dishkettle.  She  may  go  to  meet- 
ings, but  not  belong  to  the  congregation 
of  midnight  worshippers.  Inquire  at 
Repertory  Office  near  Boston."

Greatest  Lumber  Cut 

More  lumber  was  cut  in  the  United 
States  last  year  than  in  any  other  year 
in  its  history.  The  enormous  amount 
of  37,550,736  board  feet  was  produced, 
and  the  mill  value  of  ,  this  was 
$621,151,388.  In  addition,  there  were 
produced  11,858,260,000  shingles,  val- 
ued at  $24,155,555,  and  3,812,807,000 
lath,  valued  at  $11,490,570.  On  the 
whole,  it  is  safe  to  say  that  the  present 
annual  lumber  cut  of  the  United  States 
approximates  40  billion  feet,  and  that 
the  total  mill  value  of  the  lumber, 
lath  and  shingles  each  year  produced 
is  not  less  than  $700,000,000.  These 
figures  give  some  idea  of  how  vast  is 
the  lumber  industry  and  how  great  is 
the  demand  for  its  products. 

A  glance  at  the  kinds  of  lumber  pro- 
duced shows  very  clearly  the  passing 
of  white  pine  and  oak,  one  the  greatest 
softwood  and  the  other  the  greatest 
hardwood  which  the  forest  has  ever 
grown.  Since  1899  the  cut  of  white 
pine  has  fallen  off  more  than  40  per 
cent,  while  that  of  white  oak  has  fallen 
off  more  than  36  per  cent.  To-day 
yellow  pine  leads  all  other  woods  in 
amount  cut ,  while  Douglas  fir  —  and 
this  will  be  a  surprise  to  many  —  comes 
second.  Since  1899  the  cut  of  Douglas 
fir  has  increased  186  per  cent.  Louisi- 
ana is  the  foremost  yellow  pine  State, 
with  Texas,  Mississippi  and  Arkansas 
following  in  order.  Washington  pro- 
duces by  far  the  greatest  amount  of 
Douglas  fir. 

A  comparison  of  the  lumber-pro- 
ducing States  shows  that  since  1899 
there  have  been  many  changes  in  their 
relative  rank.  Washington,  which  in 
1899  stood  sixth,  now  leads,  while  Wis- 
consin, which  eight  years  ago  led  all 
others,  is  now  third.  In  the  same 
period  Oregon,  Louisiana,  Mississippi, 
Idaho  and  California  made  great 
strides  as  lumber-producing  States,, 
though,  on  the  other  hand,  the  amount 
produced  in  Michigan,  Wisconsin,  Min- 
nesota, Georgia,  Kentucky,  Tennessee,

Missouri,    Indiana    and    Ohio   fell 
anywhere  from  29  to  54  per  cent. 

The  highest-priced  native  woods  are 
walnut,  hickory  and  ash,  and  the 
cheapest  are  larch  and  white  fir.  From 
the  fact,  however,  that  since  1899  the 
average  increase  in  the  price  of  lumber 
has  been  49  per  cent,  it  will  not  be 
long  before  cheap  woods  are  few  and 
far  between. 


Health  and  Wealth  Club 

Say!     Join  the  Apple  Club  right  away. 
You  agree  to  eat  one  apple  every  day; 
Saves  doctor  bills  and  gives  good  health; 
No  fees  or  dues  to  affect  your  wealth. 

Eat  them  raw  or  cooked,  white  or  red, 

But  don't  fail  to  eat  one  when  you  go  to  bed. 

We  have  all  colors  —  red,  green,  yellow  and 

white. 
Contain  phosphorus  for  the  brain,  which  we 

need,  all  right.

Greenings,  York  Imperial  and  Pewaukee,

are  packed.

cheap  for  a  fact; 
Will  sell  you  one  or  more  boxes  at  that  rate,

freight. 

Stewed,  fried,  in  fruit  salad,  or  just  let  them 

bake.

apple  cake!

and  daughter.

Mr.  George  Nicholls,  M.P.,  told  an 
amusing  story  concerning  the  De- 
ceased Wife's  Sister  Act.  He  had  been 
shown  a  letter  which  a  man  wrote  to 
a  member  of  Parliament,  asking  with 
much  concern,  "Now  that  you  have 
the  Deceased  Wife's  Sister  Act  passed, 
is  it  compulsory?  I  mean,"  added  the 
anxious  inquirer,  "if  my  wife  dies,  am 
I  forced  to  have  her  sister?"

Preserving  Linoleum 

Good  linoleum  is,  in  its  first  cost,  an 
expensive  kitchen  floor  covering,  but  it 
can  be  made  to  last  practically  a  life- 
time by  the  very  simple  device  of  giv- 
ing it  a  coat  of  spar  varnish  every  six 
months,  at  a  cost  of  perhaps  thirty 
cents  for  a  room  10  x  12.  Apply  at 
night  and  it  will  be  dry  in  the  morning. 
An  oilcloth  or  a  linoleum  so  much  worn 
that  this  treatment  may  not  seem 
worth  while,  can  have  its  period  of 
usefulness  greatly  extended  by  going 
over  it  with  a  flannel  dipped  in  thin 
glue  water.  Before  either  application 
wash  and  thoroughly  dry  the  floor 
covering. 


Eating  Song 

In  the  shade  of  the  sheltering  bogus  palms 

The  myriad  candles  glow 
On  the  waiters  glum,  as  they  go  and  come, 

And  eke  as  they  come  and  go; 
And  it's  there  we'll  sit  as  the  moments  flit 

To  the  glug  of  the  sudsy  fizz, 
While  the  viands  rare  scent  the  ambient  air 

(If  you  know  what  a  viand  is). 

Oh,  we'll  eat,  eat,  eat, 

From  the  caviar  canap6, 
'Till  the  pont  I'eveque 
And  the  bubbling  sec 

Give  place  to  the  pousse  caf^. 
There  are  books  in  salads  and  sermons  in 
sauce, 

And  poems  in  deviled  meat, 
And  we  '11  feast  our  souls  on  the  billowy  rolls 

As  we  eat,  eat,  eat! 

The  cowboy  who  lopes  o'er  the  plains  of  the 
West, 
The  sailor  who  plows  the  main. 
Can  tuck  in  their  share  of  a  bill  of  fare, 

When  the  same  is  severely  plain; 
But  their  souls  lack  the  art  for  a  meal  a  la 
carte. 
Consumed  in  the  main  with  a  fork. 
And  they  never  would  last  till  the  punches 
were  passed 
At  a  dinner  in  old  New  York. 

So  we  '11  eat,  eat,  eat. 

Through  the  oysters  and  soup  and  fish, 
With  glad  surprise 
In  our  shining  eyes 

When  the  chef  springs  his  latest  dish. 
The  hermit  may  fancy 
It's  highly  romancy 

To  starve  m  a  dim  retreat, 
But  we  ask  of  the  Fates 
To  keep  piling  our  plates, 

While  WE  eat,  eat,  eat! 

—  New  York  American.
