Little  Qiant 
Ice  Crusher 


*^  \  1^F    /  ^^'^  ^^^  ever  stop  to  consider  the 

vX  I      I  value  of  table  napkins  or  other  linen 

^.s^^fc  li^     *  that  your  servants  were  destroying 

by  crushing  ice  by  hand  ?     Have  you 
ever  thought  of  the  valuable  pieces 
of    china    and   glassware   that  are 
broken  ?     Have  you  ever  had  to  re- 
place the  zinc  lining  in  your  ice  box 
or  chest  on  account  of  your  servants 
having  chopped  it  to  pieces  ?     Have 
you  ever  stopped   to   consider  the 
amount  of  dirt  and  dust  that  can  col- 
lect in  a  box  in  a  month  ?     Certainly 
you  could  not  expect  clean  ice  when  broken  in  such  a  box, 
in  fact,  cleanliness  is  out  of   the   question   when   ice    is 
crushed  by  hand. 

Crushing  ice  by  hand  is  a  verv  hard  and  disagreeable  job, 
besides  it  takes  lots  of  time.  With  a  LITTLE  GIANT 
MACHINE  all  you  have  to  do  is  to  drop  a  piece  of  ice  into 
the  hopper  and  turn  the  crank.  It  is  only  a  moment's 
work  and  you  have  the  ice  crushed  to  just  the  size  you  wanf 
it.  You  avoid  muss  and  s!op  and  the  ice  is  as  clean  as 
when  in  the  cake. 

The  L'.TTLE  GI 4NT  MACHINE  is  substantially 
built,  and  is  capable  of  giving  years  of  service.  Certainly 
the  saving  and  convenience  of  tliis  little  machine  is  worth 
more  than  50c.  per  year.  Size,  hopper  4  in.  x  6  in.,  height 
18  in.,  weight  30  lbs.

Faith  and  Works 

The  author  of  "Seventy  Years 
Young,"  Mrs.  Emily  P.  Bishop,  declares 
that  it  is  as  easy  to  do  as  to  wish  to  do, 
and  quotes  this  incident  in  illustration : 

A  little  girl's  brother  set  a  trap  to 
catch  birds.  She  knew  it  was  wrong, 
cruel,  against  the  laws  of  kindness, 
altogether  inexcusable  from  her  point 
of  view. 

She  wept  at  first;  then  a  little  later 
her  mother  noticed  that  she  had  be- 
come cheerful  once  more,  and  inquired 
as  to  the  cause. 

"What  did  you  do?"  asked  the 
mother. 

"I  prayed  for  my  brother  to  be 
made  a  better  boy." 

"What  else?" 

"I  prayed  that  the  trap  would  not 
catch  any  little  birds." 

"What  else?" 

' '  Then  I  went  out  and  kicked  the  old 
trap  all  to  pieces." 


A  certain  spinster  in  Indianapolis 
is  one  of  the  city's  most  notable  house- 
wives, says  the  Indianapolis  Journal. 
No  childish  fingers  ever  mar  the  bril- 
liance of  her  windows  or  play  havoc 
with  bronzes  and  vases.  At  the  home 
of  her  brother  the  same  exquisite  per- 
fection of  housekeeping  is  impossible; 
and  one  day  the  spinster's  small  niece 
returned  home  after  a  tea-party  at 
aunty's  and  in  an  awed  tone  said, 
"Mother,  I  saw  a  fly  in  Aunt  Maria's 
house,  but"  —  thoughtfully  —  "it  was 
washing  itself." 


When  the  minister,  who  was  a 
bachelor,  had  been  helped  to  Mrs. 
Porter's  biscuits  for  the  third  time,  he 
looked  across  the  table  at  Rhoda,  star- 
ing at  him  with  round,  wondering  eyes. 
"I  don't  often  have  such  a  good  supper 
as  this,  my  dear,"  he  said  in  his  most 
propitiatory  tone,  and  Rhoda's  face 
dimpled.  "We  don't,  always,"  she 
said,  in  her  clear  little  voice.  "I'm 
awful  glad  you  came." — Selected.

The  Preservation  of  Silverware 

Ordinarily  good  silverware  should 
last  a  lifetime,  and  hold  its  original 
brilliancy,  if  care  is  exercised  in  the 
use  of  a  polish  that  is  free  from  acid  or 
other  injurious  ingredients. 

Electro-Silicon  has  stood  the  test 
of  forty  years  and  demonstarted  its 
superiority  for  cleaning  and  polishing 
Gold,  Silver,  and  all  fine  metals. 

It  is  entirely  free  from  any  chemicals 
and  AT  THE  SAME  TIME  IMPARTS 
A  MARVELOUS  BRILLIANCY 
WITH  EASE  AND  RAPIDITY.  If 
you  are  not  familiar  with  the  unequaled 
qualities  of  this  celebrated  preparation, 
it  is  the  wish  of  its  proprietors,  to  send 
you  a  liberal  trial  quantity,  without 
charge,  postpaid,  and  we  strongly 
recommend  that  you  send  your  ad- 
dress without  delay  to  The  Electro 
Sihcon  Company,  30  Cliff  St.,  New 
York,  and  mention  this  publication, 
and  thereby  learn  the  proper  method 
for  preserving  your  silverware. 

Electro-Silicon  is  sold  by  the  leading 
Grocers  and  Druggists  throughout  the 
United  States,  or  is  sent  postpaid  by 
the  manufacturers  on  receipt  of  15c. 
per  box  in  stamps. 


The  New  York  World  is  a  little  con- 
cerned at  the  increasing  prices  of  food, 
and  suggests  that  we  return  to  the 
simple  life  in  the  matter  of  diet.  In 
Kennebec  County,  Maine,  1,200  people 
have  pledged  themselves  to  abstain 
from  all  meat  for  ten  days  in  protest 
against  a  demand  upon  their  pockets 
that  they  believe  to  be  due  to  greed 
rather  than  to  economic  necessity.

Cisco  Argonaut,  were  to  resort  to  such 
measures  in  San  Francisco,  we  should 
have  to  pledge  ourselves  not  to  eat 
anything  at  all. 

Evidently  it  takes  a  great  many 
different  kinds  of  people  to  make  a 
world.  We  are  told  that  in  New  York 
nobody  who  is  anybody  thinks  of 
applying  any  other  standard  of  value 
to  a  meal  than  its  price.  The  New 
Yorker  orders  a  dollar  lettuce  with  his 
dinner,  not  because  it  is  a  good  lettuce, 
but  because  it  costs  a  dollar.  If  he  could 
get  an  inferior  lettuce  for  two  dollars,  he 
would  be  glad  of  the  chance.  But  in 
Maine  they  look  at  true  values,  and 
rather  than  pay  an  extra  cent  for  their 
meat  they  will  go  without  altogether. 


An  Asset. 


The  financial  prospects  of  Mr.  Wash- 
ington Lafayette  Johnson  were  de- 
cidedly clouded,  but  he  still  preserved 
a  sanguine  spirit ,  which  his  friends  did 
not  always  appreciate. 

"I'd  like  to  borrow  jes'  a  little  money 
ob  you,"  said  Mr.  Johnson,  confiden- 
tially, to  a  friend.  "I — I  wouldn't  ax 
you  fo'  it,  but  I  ain'  got  a  single  cent 
lef  in  de  worl'." 

* '  What  secu'ity  can  you  gib  me  ? ' '  ask- 
ed his  friend,  without  any  enthusiasm. 

"  Why,  I  gib  you  my  note  ob  hand!" 
and  Mr.  Johnson  looked  pained  and 
amazed  at  such  a  question.  "I  reckon 
you  don'  know  what  a  good,  clear  hand- 
writing I  got  in  de  ebening-school. "

Veranda  Drapery 

By  Evelyn   Prince  Cahoon 


IT  is  the  exception  now-a-days  to 
find  a  veranda  wholly  undraped 
with  vines.  For  this  purpose, 
perennials  with  woody  stalks  are  best. 
Next  in  value  are  the  perennials  that 
die  to  the  roots  each  fall  and  sprout 
again  from  the  roots  in  the  spring. 
But,  after  all,  there  are  many  places 
where  it  is  not  practical  to  wait  for 
the  slow  growth  of  perennials,  and  for 
such  the  annuals  furnish  abundant 
shade  in  a  season. 

Morning-glories,  moon-flowers,  cy- 
press vines,  and  the  hearty  old  wild 
cucumber  are  the  best  annuals. 
Planted  in  the  spring,  or  dropped  in 
the  ground  the  fall  before  they  are 
wanted,  they  soon  cover  completely 
a  large  space  and  afford  abundant 
shade. 

Hops  are  among  the  best  of  those 
vines  that  die  to  the  roots  each  year 
and  sprout  up  more  strongly  every  re- 
curring spring.  And  the  Boston  ivy, 
Virginia  creeper  or  woodbine,  the  va- 
rious honeysuckles,  wistaria,  trumpet- 
vine,  the  rose,  the  grape,  both  wild 
and  cultivated,  are  all  good  strong 
perennials,  spreading  and  reaching  fur- 
ther each  year  and  in  time  covering, 
sometimes,  immense  buildings. 


The  thing  usually  wanted  above  all 
else  is  a  quick  growth  of  far-reaching, 
well-branched  stems  and  an  abundance 
of  foliage,  therefore  two  things  should 
be  provided  before  the  vine  is  planted 
—  good  drainage  and  plenty  of  rich 
earth,  for  the  foliage  will  never  be  gen- 
erous on  a  plant  of  any  kind  that  is 
starved. 

The  demands  we  are  going  to  make 
of  a  vine  should  be  pretty  definitely 
thought  out  before  a  selection  is  made. 
What  is  this  vine  to  do?  Is  it  to  fur- 
nish a  heavy,  opaque  awning  over  a 
western  entrance,  perhaps,  or  a  blos- 
soming bower  in  which  the  tea-table 
may  be  spread?  Is  it  to  provide  only 
a  dainty  screen  behind  which  one  may 
sit  and  sew,  observing  but  unobserved, 
through  the  long  afternoon,  or  is  it  to 
be  only  a  graceful  frame  for  the  pic- 
ture, made  by  the  occupants  of  the 
porch,  as  seen  from  the  street? 

For  a  dense  shade  there  is  nothing, 
on  the  whole,  very  much  better  than 
the  old  friend  of  our  mothers  and 
fathers,  the  Virginia  creeper  or  wood- 
bine, sometimes  called  the  American 
ivy.  Care  need  only  be  taken  to  keep 
the  earth  loose  around  its  feet.  It  is 
hardly  worth  while  to  plant  this  vine , 


395 


396 


THE    BOSTON    COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


unless  one  is  willing  to  give  it  plenty 
of  space  and  let  it  remain  year  after 
year;  for  it  is  sometimes  slow  about 
becoming  accustomed  to  a  new  home, 
and,  once  well-started,  is  clumpish- 
looking  and  heavy,  if  not  given  plenty 
of  room. 

Dense  shade  over  porch,  tea-house, 
arbor  or  outbuilding,  is  its  work  in  life, 
and  it  is  charming,  especially  when  the 
frost  has  colored  its  leaves  a  brilliant 
scarlet.  The  hop  and  the  grape,  espe- 
cially the  wild  variety,  are  good  for 
the  same  use.  With  all  these,  much  of 
the  beauty  of  the  vine  is  displayed  if 
it  is  trained  to  make  a  fringe  depend- 
ing from  the  roof  of  the  porch. 

But,  perhaps,  only  a  light  tracery  is 
wanted,  to  distract  attention  from  the 
occupants  of  the  veranda,  and  inter- 
pose the  merest  screen  between  them 
and  the  gaze  of  the  passer-by.  Some- 
thing with  light  but  very  prettily  out- 
lined foliage  is  needed?  The  very 
daintiest  of  all  is  the  cypress-vine;  the 
balsam  is  another,  and  the  large- 
flowered  clematis,  purple,  red,  or  white, 
is  still  another. 

For  the  plant  that  will  make  of  the 


veranda  a  fragrant  bower  the  greater 
part  of  the  summer,  where  the  hum- 
ming bird  will  hover  and  the  shade  be 
dense  enough  to  temper  the  heat,  yet 
every  leaf  so  light  that  the  slightest 
breeze  will  raise  it,  there  is  nothing 
like  the  beautiful  white  honeysuckle. 

All  the  honeysuckles  are  good,  the 
coral,  the  orange  (a  variety  not  at  all 
common,  but  very  pretty)  and  the  old 
pink  and  yellow  kind. 

Sometimes  there  is  wanted  a  vine 
that  will  climb  trimly  in  straight  lines, 
and  accommodate  itself  strictly  to  its 
supports.  The  madeira,  as  well  as 
many  of  the  annuals,  morning-glory, 
moon-flower,  wild  cucumber  and  so  on, 
are  good.  A  very  pretty  effect  is 
gotten  with  the  madeira  by  suspend- 
ing a  large  barrel-hoop  midway  be- 
tween the  roof  and  the  edge  of  the 
floor,  and  between  the  two  pillars  of 
the  veranda,  and  so  training  the  vine 
that  it  grows  closely  over  the  whole 
space  except  the  hoop. 

This  leaves  a  framed  circular  open- 
ing, which  constitutes  a  sort  of  window 
from  the  cool  green  shade  behind. 
The    shape    of    the    opening    may    be 


Roses,  Honeysuckle  and  Hops 


VERANDA  DRAPERY 


397 


Clematis  in  Bloom 

varied;  for  instance,  parts  of  two 
hoops  may  be  fastened  together  so  as 
to  make  an  elHptical  opening,  or  a 
square  frame  may  be  hung  by  the 
corner  and  make  a  diamond  opening 
set  in  the  green. 

When  the  architecture  of  a  house 
is  heavy,  it  is  wonderful  how  a  vine 
may  hghten  it.  This  result  is  never 
gotten  though,  if  the  plant  is  trained 
in  a  great  rectangular  sheet;  such  a 
thing  should  never  be  done,  except  in 
case  of  necessity,  to  provide  shade. 
Where  monotonous  lines  are  to  be 
broken,  the  vine  must  be  trained  to  an 
irregular  mass,  or  to  form  a  tracery  or 
fringe  along  the  edge  of  the  outline 
of  the  wood,  stone,  or  brick. 

A  vine,  particularly  of  some  flower- 
ing variety,  planted  beside  the  step, 
gives  the  entrance  an  air  of  hospitality 
very  desirable  in  a  private  home,  and 
it  sheds  an  informality  about  the  wel- 
come that  adds  to  the  attractiveness 
of  the  veranda. 

Where  the  vine  is  wished  for  actual 
shade,  it  is  to  be  remembered  that  it 
is  the  eyes  that  require  it.  Accord- 
ingly, the  vines  are  to  be  planted  at 
each  pillar,  and  either  carried  directly 
up  the  post  or  allowed  to  twine  around 
it,  with  most  of  the  foliage  pinched  off. 


until  the  top  of  the  porch  is  nearly 
reached.  They  may  now  be  allowed 
to  grow  out  to  the  right  and  left  as 
well  as  upward,  forming,  consequently, 
a  looped  drapery  across  the  top  of  the 
porch,  which  shades  the  eyes  but  does 
not  cut  off  the  view.  The  long 
branches  may  often  be  coaxed  to 
climb  to  the  roof  of  the  building  by 
running  a  heavy  cord  or  a  rope  from 
the  porch  to  the  roof. 

The  effect  of  this  is  pretty,  provided 
only  that  the  lines  of  the  roof  are  not 
already  so  broken  by  the  style  of 
architecture  as  to  appear  "fussy" 
when  broken  still  more  by  the  wander- 
ing vine. 

Just  as  soon  as  the  vine  begins  to 
grow  heartily,  fasten  to  it  an  honest 
and  permanent  support.  Do.  not  de- 
pend on  strings  added  from  time  to 
time  in  makeshift  style.  One  never 
gets  the  full  effect  of  the  vine  in  any 
such  way.  For  those  vines  that  twine, 
smooth  fence- wire  is  the  best.  For 
those  that  bear  tendrils,  like  the  grape 
and  woodbine,  galvanized  wire  netting 
is  best,  and  for  those  like  the  Boston 
ivy ,  that  hold  themselves  to  the  surface 
by  tiny  vacuum-cups,  strips  of  cloth 
should  be  tacked  across  the  stems  to 
the  surface  climbed  upon. 


Looped  Drapery 


398 


THE    BOSTON    COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


This  is  needed  only  while  the  vine  is 
starting,  having  once  well  begun  it 
will  hold  itself  in  position. 


Under  vine  and  fig-tree  is  the  Bibli- 
cal synonym  for  comfort,  but  much 
depends  on  the  training  of  the  vine. 


The  Balsam  Vine 


My   Lady   Dahlia 

By  Jessie  Juliet   Knox 


"To  me  the  meanest  flower  that  blows  can  bring 
Thoughts  that  do  often  He  too  deep  for  tears." 


THE  old  saying  that  "everything 
comes  to  him  who  waits  "  is  even 
true  in  the  world  of  flowers. 
The  dahlia  has  waited  long,  but  her 
turn  has  come  at  last,  for  fashion  has 
now  decreed  that  she  shall  be  queen 
regnant  in  all  the  gardens  of  flowers. 

It  was  a  bitter  moment  for  the  regal 
dahlia,  when  the  ragged  chrysanthe- 
mum was  brought  forth  from  obscurity 
and  made  much  of,  praised,  cultivated 
and  worn  upon  the  bosoms  of  famous 
beauties,  and,  when  it  came  to  her 
ears  that  the  Bohemian  queen  was 
constantly  winning  prizes  and  being 
photographed,  she  reflected,  dahlia- 
wise,  that  if  she  had  the  same  cultiva- 


tion she  might  become  even  more 
beautiful  than  the  famed  chrysanthe- 
mum, which  had  been  brought  to 
notice  by  the  wife  of  one  of  our  presi- 
dents. 

One  always  associates  the  dahlia 
with  the  old-fashioned  garden  of  child- 
hood. It  is,  indeed,  an  old  friend,  one 
who  has  been  with  us  long,  but  whose 
manner  has  always  seemed  to  have  in 
it  a  touch  of  hauteur,  and  whose  stiff 
and  dignified  bearing  seemed  to  pre- 
clude all  familiarity.  The  dahlia  al- 
ways held  her  velvety  head  as  if  in 
scorn  of  a  world  too  ignorant  to  per- 
ceive her  worth.  And  now,  at  last, 
after    ages   of   waiting,    her   turn   has 


MY  LADY  DAHLIA 


399 


come.  It  has  been  discovered  that 
she  has  a  beauty  aU  her  own,  and  so, 
we  have  crowned  her  —  the  haughty 
one  —  and  taken  her  to  our  hearts  and 
cultivated  her,  and  put  her  in  high 
places.  She  is  noble  enough  to  for- 
give us,  and  is  going  to  repay  us  for 
our  care,  and  will  even  nod  and  smile 
to  her  dethroned  rival,  since,  being 
the  queen,  she  can  afford  to  be  gen- 
erous. 

Of  course  she  has  her  faults  —  w^ho 
has  not?  —  and  she  has  her  enemies, 
the  weeds,  who  are  the  insidious  anar- 
chists in  the  floral  world,  and  hate 
beauty  of  blossom  and  perfume,  and 
are  constantly  trying  to  creep  in  and 
overthrow  them.  We  have  heard  it 
whispered  that  the  greatest  fault  of 
our  queen  is,  that  she  has  no  perfume. 
That  is  a  serious  fault,  with  a  flower. 
And  the  blossoms  say  she  is  too  dig- 
nified, in  fact,  stuck  up,  and  looks  with 
haughty  scorn  upon  her  less  fortunate 
sisters,  who  have  never  as  yet  had 
placed  upon  them  the  insignia  of  rank. 

But  then  there  are  worse  faults  than 
these,  and  one  can  be  forgiven  much, 


even  if  one  is  not  clinging,  modest  and 
perfumed,  if  only  one  has  a  rarely 
beautiful  face,  and  lips  and  cheeks  of 
velvet. 

When  a  new  queen  is  crowned,  it  is 
customary  to  find  out  all  about  her; 
from  whence  she  sprang,  the  social 
prestige  of  her  ancestors,  and  all  the 
on  dits  concerning  her.  And  so  we 
have  learned  that  our  queen  is  of 
Mexican  origin,  being  named  for  An- 
drew Dahl,  a  Swedish  botanist,  and 
pupil  of  Linnaeus,  by  whom  this  beau- 
tiful garden  plant  was  first  brought 
into  cultivation.  The  dahlia  tree  has, 
also,  been  imported  from  Mexico. 
This  attains  a  height  of  from  twelve 
to  fourteen  feet.  The  genus  was  first 
carried  over  into  Spain  about  1787. 
The  Marchioness  of  Bute,  the  same 
year,  introduced  it  into  England,  and, 
becoming  extinct,  it  was  brought  anew 
to  this  country,  in  1804. 

All  the  varieties  in  cultivation  in 
our  flower  gardens,  of  which  no  fewer 
than  two  thousand  have  been  carefully 
enumerated,  are  derived  from  two 
species,  variabilis  and  coccinea.

An  Assortment  of  Dahlias 


400 


THE    BOSTON    COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


Few  plants  manifest  such  a  desire 
to  sport  and  produce  new  varieties  as 
the  dahha,  and  florists  have,  also, 
obtained  many  by  the  artificial  fecun- 
dation of  one  with  the  pollen  of 
another. 

Among  the  most  essential  character- 
istics of  a  fine  dahlia,  according  to  the 
estimation  of  florists,  are: 

A  fullness  of  the  flower;  a  perfect 
regularity  in  the  shape  of  the  florets; 
and  the  absence  of  an  eye,  or  disc. 

Dahlias  have  tuberous  roots,  which 
contain  a  considerable  quantity  of 
inulin,  and  are  in  use  as  an  article  of 
food  in  Mexico.  It  was  at  one  time 
attempted     to     introduce     them     into 


a  long  a,  and  we  must  forego  the  old 
word,  familiar  so  long.  The  new 
dahlia,  which  is  receiving  so  much  care, 
is  a  flower  of  marvelous  beauty  and 
coloring.  Every  color  has  been  ob- 
tained except  light  blue,  and  no  doubt 
that  will  come  in  time. 

Where  could  be  found  a  more  suitable 
place  to  study  the  flower  than  under 
the  blue  and  smiling  skies  of  California  ? 
In  one  of  these  gardens  many  varieties 
are  to  be  found. 

In  Golden  Gate  Park,  in  San  Fran- 
cisco, there  is  in  one  bed  alone  two 
hundred  and  fifty  varieties,  each  having 
a  name  of  its  own.  This  is  said  to  be 
the  finest  bed  in  the  world.

The  Dahlia  in  a  California  Garden 


cultivation  in  Europe,  for  the  food 
either  of  man  or  beast,  but  the  taste 
is  nauseous  to  European  palates,  and 
even  cattle  do  not  readily  partake 
of  it. 

And  now,  the  floral  queen,  that  has 
stepped  out  of  the  old-fashioned  gar- 
dens of  the  past,  is  pronounced  with 


The  dahlia  is  the  ideal  flower  for 
decorative  purposes,  having  good  sub- 
stantial stems,  brilliant  color  and 
lasting  qualities.  If  a  piece  of  the 
stem  be  cut  off  every  day,  it  will  last 
much  longer. 

Dahlia  bulbs  should  be  planted 
during  the  winter,  one  bulb  to  a  place, 


MAIDS'    PARLORS 


401 


and,  when  the  shoots  come  up,  all  but 
one  should  be  pinched  off,  in  order  to 
insure  fine  flowers. 

They  blossom  from  June  until  the 
frost  kills  them,  requiring  a  great  deal 
of  water  and  sunshine.  There  is  really 
no  saying  what  marvelous  surprises 
this  new  queen  has  in  store  for  us. 

"At  last  I  am  great!"  she  murmurs, 
as  she  nods  gaily  in  the  breeze.     "At 


last  I  can  rest  on  the  soft  bosoms  of 
beautiful  women,  and  over  the  hearts 
of  brave  men;  I  smile  amid  the  cut- 
glass  and  silver  of  their  banquets,  and 
am  privileged  to  hear  all  the  society 
chit-chat."  Having  once  tasted  success, 
she  will  never  again  care  to  be  rele- 
gated to  her  former  place  in  an  old- 
fashioned  garden. 
Long  live  the  Queen! 


Maids'  Parlors 

By  Kate  Gannett  Wells 


I 


'T  is  so  upsetting  to  proper  family 
ideas  for  maids  to  have  parlors 
of  their  own,"  said  a  lady  the 
other  day,  exasperated  with  what  she 
termed  domestic  modernism.  "Of 
course  it  is,"  returned  her  sympathiz- 
ing friend,  "when  there  is  no  need  of 
them.  They  take  a  girl's  mind  off  her 
work  and  make  her  put  on  airs;  be- 
sides, if  maids  have  parlors  of  their 
own  they  would  always  be  having 
beaux,  —  and  that  isn't  safe." 

"It  is  just  as  safe  as  for  me  to  have 
my  young  men  in  the  library,"  inter- 
rupted the  daughter  of  the  house,  who 
had  been  growing  restive  under  the 
conservatism  of  her  elders.  "If  I 
cooked  most  of  the  day,  it  would  be 
cooling  to  have  a  place  to  go  to  where 
there  was  no  range,  and  it  is  uncanny 
to  always  be  taking  care  of  other 
people's  pretty  rooms  and  never  have 
a  pretty  place  yourself.  I  could  work 
a  great  deal  harder  if  I  knew  that  by- 
and-by  I  could  read  the  paper  in  my 
own  sitting  room,  where  the  mistress 
would  have  to  knock  if  she  wanted  me. 
So!" 

One  of  the  ladies  smiled  benevo- 
lently at  such  misplaced  exuberance  of 
democracy,  while  the  other  woman 
mildly  suggested  that  the  trouble  lay 
more  in  the  way  houses  are  built  than 


with  their  tenants  or  owners.  She  was 
right.  Most  employers  are  glad  for  all 
the  good  times  and  pleasant  surround- 
ings that  maids  can  have,  but  the 
faulty  construction  of  their  houses  bars 
their  efforts  unless  they  inconvenience 
themselves  more  than  appears  to  them 
seemly. 

The  importance  of  a  maid's  parlor 
as  against  the  inconvenience  of  the 
family  is  a  serious  matter,  and  has 
much  to  do  with  the  unrest  of  do- 
mestic service.  Maids  are,  after  all, 
just  like  other  people.  In  the  best  of 
them,  even  in  the  average  ones,  there 
lurks  a  "  sub-consciousness  "  that  craves 
the  ease,  enjoyments  and  refinements 
of  life.  If  the  signs  of  labor,  the  pots 
and  pans,  are  still  around  them  when 
they  are  off  duty,  the  spirit,  which 
wants  something  different,  is  not  really 
refreshed.  Call  the  parlor  a  sitting- 
room,  if  that  seems  a  less  pretentious 
word,  though  Society  has  given  up  the 
term  parlor  in  favor  of  library  or 
drawing-room,  for  the  gist  of  the  mat- 
ter lies  in  the  existence  of  a  room 
which  shall  be  neither  kitchen  nor 
chamber.     How  can  it  be  provided? 

Unfortunately,  architects  have  not 
yet  realized  the  psychology  that  under- 
lies the  claim  for  the  sitting-room,  and 
are  not  yet  ahead  of  the  slow  dawning 


402 


THE   BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


intuitions  of  their  clients.  When  an 
architect  builds  to  order,  the  plans 
rarely  include  provision  for  the  maids' 
sitting-  or  dining-room,  and  when  he 
builds  of  his  own  free-will,  he  fears  a 
house  will  not  sell  if  it  is  too  roomy. 
It  is  only  in  a  very  few  apartment 
houses  that  the  conscience  of  the  ar- 
chitect or  owner  seems  to  be  getting 
awakened,  and  that  at  least  better 
sleeping  quarters  are  furnished.  In 
most  Institutions,  at  present,  common 
sense  has  provided  nurses  and  attend- 
ants with  parlors,  but  not  the  maids. 
Almost  everywhere  the  building  line  is 
drawn  against  their  sitting-room.  Yet 
in  Institutions  and  in  the  homes  of  the 
wealthy  the  arrangements  for  such 
rooms  are  easily  feasible. 

It  is,  however,  in  the  average  house 
of  eight  to  twelve  rooms  that  such  a 
parlor  seems  incompatible  with  the 
family  purse  or  convenience.  The 
family  will  then  have  to  give  up  some- 
thing and  that  something  is  the 
reception-room,  usually,  for  the  kitchen 
and  laundry  are  all  that  the  basement 
accommodates.  Then  is  it  that  con- 
servatism rises  on  its  mettle  and  sets 
forth  its  necessities,  to  be  confronted 
by  the  democracy  of  humanity  that 
claims  comforts  for  workers. 

Supposing,  on  the  other  hand,  that 
the  dining-room  is  the  only  room  on 
the  street  floor,  then  it  is  that  the 
problem  of  mutual  give  and  take  is  put 
to  its  severest  test.  Must  lunch  and 
dinner  always  be  hurried  that,  meals 
ended,  the  maids  can  use  the  dining- 
room  as  their  sitting-room?  Why 
should  not  a  kindly  democracy  of  feel- 
ing insist  that  the  family  adapt  its 
meal  hours  to  the  leisure  hours  of  the 
maids  and  that  the  dining-room  be 
used  in  common  by  the  employer  and 
her  employees? 

But,  if  the  house  or  apartment  is  so 
small  that  the  dining-room  is  also  the 
only  family  sitting-room,  then  recourse 
must  be  had  to  the  conversion  of  some 
nook  in  the  kitchen  to  the  allurements 


of  a  sitting-room.  A  small  hanging 
shelf  on  the  wall,  with  novels  on  it,  a 
table  and  lamp,  a  rocker  and  mat,  can 
redeem  a  few  square  feet  of  floor  to 
the  uses  of  rest  and  prettiness. 

It  is  not  for  the  sitting-room  as  a 
sign  of  uppishness,  an  assertive  I-am- 
as-good-as-you  feeling,  that  one  pleads, 
but  for  it  as  the  assurance  of  a  rest  to 
the  body,  an  outlook  towards  beauty, 
a  recognition  that  all  work  and  no  play 
defrauds  the  soul  and  cheats  the  physi- 
cal energies. 

Still,  some  one  will  object  that  such 
a  room  will  not  be  appreciated.  It 
won't  be,  always,  but  that  is  no  reason 
why,  when  it  will  be  estimated  aright, 
it  should  not  be  provided.  Even  if 
not  appreciated,  it  yet  should  be  in 
evidence  as  an  aid  towards  refinement 
of  character,  that  ultimately  means 
improved  and  happy  domestic  service. 
As  passable  makeshift  for  a  sitting- 
room  is  the  maids'  dining-room,  which 
is  usually  just  off  the  kitchen  or  in  a 
passage-way.  It  may  be,  it  can  be, 
very  pleasant  and  supplant  the  need 
for  a  sitting-room,  but  generally  it  pre- 
sents the  forlorn  appearance  of  being  a 
storage-room  for  table  and  chairs,  with 
the  pepper  and  salt  shakers  in  the 
middle  of  the  table. 

There  is  so  much  discussion  about 
domestic  service  that  might  be  avoided 
by  the  better  construction  of  houses, 
and  thus  remedy  the  uncomfortable- 
ness  of  "living  out."  Service  by  the 
hour  can  never  wholly  do  away  with 
the  need  for  the  abode  of  at  least  one 
or  two  employees  in  the  home.  Verily, 
such  abode,  the  confinement  of  hours 
and  all  that  it  is  said  makes  domestic 
service  disagreeable,  would  not  be  so 
hard  if  there  were  the  refuge  of  a 
pretty  sitting-room  after  work  is  done. 
As  employers,  we  must  recognize  the 
principle  of  individualism  in  service 
and  soften  its  asperities  by  providing 
ease  and  beauty  for  workers.  Domes- 
tic service  then  becomes  domestic  art. 
Each  member  of  a  family,  employer 


COLLEGE   PRESIDENTS  AND   PEANUTS 


403 


and  employees,  will  continue  to  have  her 
specialty  in  occupation,  yet  there  will  be 
the  social  recognition  by  the  employer 
of  the  need  for  good  times,  card  parties 
and  candy  pulls  for  the  employees. 

Is  it  not  all  that  we  have  got  to  do, 
simply  to  treat  our  maids  as  well  as,  if 
not  better  than,  we  do  ourselves,  and 
to  insist  upon  our  recognition  of  their 
need  for  beaux  and  for  fun  ?  It  is  a 
sad  travesty  upon  the  pleasures  of 
home  when  a  church  offers  its  parlors 
for   courting  purposes.     Though   such 


a  retreat  may  be  intended  only  for 
those  who  sleep  in  hall  bedrooms  and 
go  out  working  by  the  day,  yet  there 
are  many  maids  whose  freedom  in  the 
houses  where  they  live  and  work  is  so 
restricted  that  they  might  well  wish 
for  church  advantages  other  than  re- 
ligious. A  maid's  parlor  is  an  ac- 
knowledgment of  democracy,  a  panacea 
for  the  housekeeper's  conscience,  and 
an  uplift  to  the  dormant  need  of  em- 
ployees for  beauty  and  cheerfulness  in 
their  surroundings. 


My  Minstrel 

By  Grace  Stone  Field 

Came  a  minstrel  to  my  window,  yesterday ; 
Blithely  sang  a  rondel,  lilting,  gay  — 

Piped  of  skies  that  span  the  southland  fair ; 
Prated,  soft,  of  orange  flowers  there. 

Sang,  though  northern  skies  were  dun  and  drear, 
Of  some  sweet  tomorrow,  almost  here! 

Robin  —  troubadour  in  rusty  red  — 
Fearless  of  white  Winter,  scarcely  fled; 

Bright  embodiment  of  hope  and  cheer, 
Springtime  has  no  herald  half  so  dear! 


College  Presidents  and  Peanuts 

By  Helen  Campbell 


JUST  why  college  towns  as  a  whole 
are  supposed  to  come  under  the 
head  of  "conservative"  is  a  ques- 
tion not  to  be  settled  here,  though  one 
may  add  that  a  calm  acceptance  of 
things  as  they  are  is,  by  most  of  the 
inhabitants  of  these  educational  centers, 
thought  to  be  more  conducive  to  study 
than  any  agitation  or  experiment  not 


included  in  the  college  curriculum. 
Thus  deep  amazement  filled  the  mind 
of  the  dwellers  in  the  pretty  town  of 
Aurora  when  it  became  known  that  the 
president  of  the  college,  which  has  long 
been  their  pride,  was  not  of  this  mind, 
but  proposed  definitely  to  subsist  for 
sixty  days  on  a  diet  of  ground  peanuts 
and  wheat.    .Steadily  rising  prices  and 


404 


THE   BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


the  effect  on  student  expenses  may 
have  been  the  moving  cause  or,  if 
not  that,  the  Chittenden  Report  and 
the  new  food  standard,  accepted  at 
present  as  not  only  absolutely  scientific, 
but  as  meaning  longer  life  and  better 
health  for  all  who  study  and  practice 
its  conclusions. 

As  to  the  result  we  are  not  yet  in- 
formed, but  any  grocer  knows  that  the 
demand  for  peanut -butter  is  not  only 
an  established  but  a  growing  one; 
pails  of  it  are  now  put  up  for  family 
use,  and  peanut  sandwiches  are  a 
permanent  feature  of  picnics  and  church 
festivals.  The  peanut  crop  has  doubled 
and  trebled,  and  more  and  more  land  is 
being  planted  with  this,  the  cheapest 
of  all  the  forms  of  nut  food,  while  nuts 
of  all  orders,  pecans  leading  as  fa- 
vorites, are  entering  more  and  more 
into  the  daily  menu.  Lately  a  promi- 
nent physician,  in  lecturing  before  a 
body  of  students,  told  them  that  an 
analysis  of  food  values  would  show 
them  that  ten  cents'  worth  of  peanuts, 
held  all  the  nutriment  of  a  dinner  at 
the  Waldorf,  and  various  students, 
working  their  way  through  the  medical 
college ,  have  found  his  words  true  and 
have  dropped  much  of  the  meat  they 
had  supposed  a  necessity  at  least 
twice  a  day. 

As  one  result  of  both  statement  and 
student  experiment  came  the  necessity 
for  fuller  talk,  since  peanuts  alone 
could  by  no  means  be  accepted  as  a 
chief  feature  of  the  day's  menu.  So 
it  came  to  pass  that  two  or  three  in- 
telligent women,  in  whose  houses  stu- 
dents were  boarders,  sought  the  physi- 
cian ,  to  ask  if  he  actually  believed  and 
practised  the  thing  the  students  quoted, 
and  how  a  table  could  be  made  to  look 
as  though  there  were  really  anything 
worth  while  on  it,  if  meat  and  poultry 
were  barred  out. 

"It  won't  look  worth  while,  unless 
you  can  convince  them  that  the  variety 
they  were  brought  up  to  was  an  error," 
he  said  at  last.     "You  see  I  hesitate, 


because  all  of  us,  almost  without  ex- 
ception, suppose  there  must  be  un- 
ceasing variety,  and  this  is  put  into  us 
before  we  can  talk  plain.  The  habit  of 
eating  much  the  same  things,  day  by 
day,  can  be  cultivated,  and  my  own 
children  are  entirely  content.  It  seems 
to  be  thought  I  am  preaching  peanuts 
three  times  a  day  and  little  else.  That 
would  be  simply  ridiculous.  I  bar  out 
no  good  thing  that  grows,  but  the 
desire  for  meat  passes  away  as  one 
simplifies  the  diet.  If  I  craved  it,  at 
times  I  should  take  it,  but  so  far  I  do 
not,  the  cells  that  were  built  up  on  that 
diet  not  seeming  to  desire  it.  The 
restaurants  have  found  out  that  vege- 
table hash  is  appetizing  and  popular, 
and  means  a  cleaner  form  of  food  than 
the  hash  of  all  forms  of  scraps  of  meat. 
This  hash  can  be  baked  in  a  large 
dish,  and,  well  browned  and  savory, 
makes  always  a  good  central  point. 
Soups,  of  course,  delicious  ones,  tomato, 
bean,  pea,  lentil,  pretty  much  any 
vegetable,  but  made  with  water  or 
milk  and  cooked  to  a  puree  before  the 
latter  is  added.  For  all  that  you  can 
go  to  the  cook  books." 

"But  what  do  you  do?"  the  ques- 
tioner urged.  "My  boys  swear  by 
you.     It  is  your  way  they  want." 

"My  way  means,  then,  coffee  in  the 
morning,  real  coffee,  too;  none  of 
your  substitutes,  but  with  it  the  fresh, 
unsweetened  condensed  milk,  that  is  a 
purer  form  than  anything  we  can  get 
straight  from  the  cow.  I  know,  for  I 
have  had  to  do  considerable  milk  in- 
specting. Whole  wheat  bread,  too, 
but  white  bread  also  at  will,  plenty  of 
peanut  butter  on  it;  fruit  as  in  season, 
always  falling  back  on  bananas  as  a 
standby ;  grapes  as  long  as  they  can 
be  had,  dates,  oranges  and  raisins,  the 
two  last  as  substantial  food  as  bananas. 
We  buy  the  black  Arabian  dates,  and 
soak  the  portion  for  a  meal  in  cold 
water  a  few  minutes,  then  give  a  dash 
of  boiling  water,  after  they  are  put  in 
a  colander  to  this  end,  the  process  not 


TWILIGHT 


405 


only  freeing  them  from  the  dirt  of  pack- 
ing but  making  them  better  eating. 
As  for  raisins,  I  can't  say  too  much. 
They  are  storehouses  of  energy,  and 
this  is  true  of  the  cheaper  ones,  loose 
raisins,  for  instance,  which  we  put 
through  the  same  process  as  the  dates. 
Then  there  are  countless  salads,  with 
plenty  of  olive  oil  and  lemon  juice  in- 
stead of  vinegar,  tomato  and  lettuce 
leading.  If  one  is  in  the  country, 
where  lettuce  cannot  always  be  had, 
then  cabbage,  celery,  apples,  with  nuts 
or  celery;  in  short,  any  sort  of  cold 
vegetables  that  can  be  combined  at 
taste  and  constantly  varied.  A  little 
good  cheese  is  the  best  of  foods,  and 
combining  it  with  macaroni  or  spag- 
hetti makes  another  main  dish,  which 
comes  under  the  head  of  worth  while. 
Don't  you  begin  to  get  a  glimmer  of 
what  can  be  done,  always  supposing 
your  fellows  do  not  kick,  but  honestly 
desire  to  learn  other  ways  than  the  old 
ones?  Don't  forget  baked  beans,  done 
with  either  olive  oil  or  butter  instead 
of  pork,  and  try  limas  for  a  change  in 
the  same  way.  Peanuts,  you  see,  are 
not  necessarily  always  in  the  fore- 
ground, but  hot  roasted  peanuts  taste 
as  good  to  me  as  to  my  small  boy,  and 
peanut  butter  has  come  to  stay.  Try 
it  for  a  while  and  I  think  you  will 
presently  get  even  with  your  market- 


man,  and  find  that  this  phase  of  the 
simple  life  means  less  torment  from 
the  kitchen  and  more  fun  for  the  dining- 
room.  Clean  tongues,  clear  heads  and 
better  tempers  for  all  your  crowd  are 
also  included.     Try  it  and  see." 

"But  how  did  you  happen  to  begin 
it?"  questioned  one  of  the  group. 
"Poverty  and  a  progressive  college 
president,"  said  the  Doctor  with  a 
laugh.  "A  little  Western  college 
tucked  away  where  none  of  you  would 
ever  hear  of  it,  but  a  good  many  of  its 
boys  topping  off  later  with  post-gradu- 
ate degrees  from  Harvard.  Everybody 
was  poor  together,  and  the  president 
had  gone  through  his  course  of  salera- 
tus  buscuits  and  bacon,  and  come  out 
on  the  other  side,  after  a  sojourn  in  a 
health  place,  which  was  just  beginning 
to  take  the  new  view  of  things.  He 
came  back  with  a  clear  sense  of  what 
it  might  do  for  the  boys,  and  I  am  one 
of  his  products,  and  am  passing  on  the 
gospel,  for  that  is  what  it  is  to  me. 
A  crank?  Certainly!  Everybody  is  a 
crank  who  accepts  a  new  interpretation 
and  is  willing  to  experiment,  but  we 
mind  it  less  and  less  since  the  world, 
our  little  world,  at  least,  just  about  us, 
appears  to  be  coming  over  day  by  day, 
and  soon  there  will  be  a  majority.  That 
is  what  I  work  for  and  each  convert 
means  many  more.     Trv  it  and  see." 


Twilight 

By  Alix  Thorn 


Hand  in  hand  sit  Mother  and  Nan. 

Peacefully  swaying  to  and  fro. 
Lessons  over,  a  wondrous  hour. 

Watching  the  flickering  firelights  glow. 

"Now  aren't  we  happy?"  cries  little  Nan, 
"Rocking  and  visiting,  just  us  two." 
Then  nestling  closer,  "Oh,  Mother,  dear, 
I  always  feel  so  at  home  with  you." 


The  Ethics  of  Home  Making 

By  Pauline  Carrlngton  Bouve 


THE  duty  of  home  making  has 
been  discussed  a  great  many 
times,  yet  a  careful  considera- 
tion of  much  that  has  been  written 
and  said  by  persons,  who  claim  to 
know,  a  great  deal  about  it,  reveals 
the  fact  that  a  one-sided  view  of  the 
question  is  often  taken. 

One  continually  hears  about  the 
"woman's  duty  to  make  a  home  for 
her  husband,"  while  the  reverse  side  of 
the  case  is  almost  never  touched  upon. 
Women,  themselves,  accept  the  situa- 
tion as  they  find  it,  and  scarcely  ever 
voice  a  thought  that  must  very,  very 
often  intrude  itself  into  the  mind  of 
even  the  most  patient  modern  Griselda. 

Now,  of  course,  it  is  perfectly  proper 
to  assume  that  the  responsibility  of  all 
the  domestic  details  that  make  up 
good  housekeeping  should  fall  to  the 
wife  and  mother;  that  the  adornment, 
the  refinement  of  the  home  depend, 
almost,  if  not  quite,  entirely  upon  her 
energy  and  her  taste;  that  this  inde- 
finable something  in  the  atmosphere, 
which  we  may  call  hominess,  emanates 
from  her  personality. 

Grant  all  this  and  yet  she  cannot 
accomplish  these  results  alone.  With- 
out a  certain  and  very  definite  coopera- 
tion, she  is  powerless  to  make  a  homey 
home. 

What  woman,  for  instance,  can  go 
on  indefinitely  taking  pleasure  and 
pride  in  her  well-cooked  and  daintily 
served  meals,  if  they  are  eaten  by  a 
somewhat  surly  husband,  without  the 
least  appreciation  or  even  recogni- 
tion of  her  constant  efforts? 

What  woman  continues  to  keep  up 
a  keen  interest  in  making  her  home 
cozy  and  attractive,  if  the  only  com- 
ments are:  "I  wonder  why  Mrs.  Mark- 
ham's  parlor  looks  so  much  better  than 
ours?     I've    spent    enough   money    on 


these  things  to  make  a  good  appear- 
ance!" or,  "Jim  Bradford's  wife  is  a 
corking  good  judge  of  pictures  and 
furniture;  their  house  never  looks  like 
a  crowded  junk  shop,  as  this  place;" 
or,  "Why  don't  you  get  Fanny  Bangs 
to  show  you  how  to  drape  those  cur- 
tains; they  all  swag." 

At  table,  the  same  line  of  comment 
runs  like  a  discordant  accompaniment 
to  the  conversation. 

"Don't  try  to  have  beans  baked  at 
home  again,  please;  I  can  get  them 
down  town  at  a  hotel,  when  I  want 
them.  What's  the  matter  with  them? 
Why,  of  course,  I  don't  know,  only 
they  don't  taste  like  mother's  beans; 
she  always  seemed  to  hit  it  with  beans ; " 
or,  "These  potatoes  are  cold,"  pushing 
the  dish  back;  "if  there's  anything  I 
dislike,  it's  cold  vegetables.  My  sister 
Agnes  has  her  faults,  but  she  never 
sat  the  family  down  to  a  half-cold 
dinner.  Didn't  come  in  when  the  bell 
rang?  Wait  and  let  Jane  warm  them? 
I  can't  wait  all  day  for  the  dinner  to 
be  cooked  over;  I've  got  to  get  to  the 
office." 

With  some  such  remark,  the  lord  of 
the  house  departs,  never  seeing  the 
pained  look  in  his  wife's  eyes,  as  she 
tells  the  maid  of  all  work  to  be  more 
careful  tomorrow  to  have  the  dinner 
piping  hot  when  Mr.  comes  in. 

Years  of  petty  fault-finding  will 
destroy  every  atom  of  housewifely 
pride  and  joy,  unless,  as  sometimes 
happens,  a  child's  appreciation  keeps 
it  alive. 

Little  Dora's  perfect  faith  that 
"Mamma  has  things  just  right,  no  mat- 
ter what  Papa  says,"  is  a  healing  balm 
for  a  great  many  sharp  and  deep 
wounds  that  are  bravely  hidden  from 
prying  eyes. 

With    the    intuitive    knowledge    of 


406 


THE  ETHICS  OF  HOME  MAKING 


407 


childhood,  little  Dora  knows  that 
Mamma  has  not  been  kindly  dealt  with ; 
that,  somehow,  something  is  very 
wrong  with  something! 

Where  an  element  like  this  exists, 
no  one  person  can  make  a  home. 
There  may  be  excellent  cooking,  a 
well-ordered  house,  nicely-served  meals, 
but  the  spirit  of  home,  which  is  mutual 
appreciation  as  well  as  mutual  effort, 
is  lacking. 

No  woman  alone  can  "make  a  home " 
for  a  man  who  does  not,  by  apprecia- 
tion, sympathy  and  recognition  of  lov- 
ing services,  lovingly  rendered,  help  to 
build  the  structure. 

It  not  infrequently  happens  that  it  is 
the  woman  who  does  not  do  her  part 
of  the  building.  The  home  is  always 
being  built,  and  if  the  building  process 
is  abandoned  it  soon  gets  out  of  repair, 
and,  in  such  a  case,  the  same  result 
follows. 

No  man  can  come  to  his  so-called 
home,  night  after  night,  and  find  an 
untidy  house,  untidy,  quarreling  chil- 
dren, a  badly-cooked  dinner  and  a  wife 
indifferent  to  everything  in  the  world 
except,  perhaps,  a  coming  bridge  party, 
the  weekly  matinee,  or  Marie  Corelli's 
last  novel!  There  is  no  home  atmo- 
sphere there.  He  has  simply  come 
back  to  an  uncomfortable  dwelling- 
place,  to  which  he  is  bound  by  social 
law  and  habit.  He  pays  the  grocer's 
bills,  the  servant's  wages,  the  dress- 
maker's and  milliner's  bills,  and  bills 
for  the  children's  tuition  and  music 
lessons,  but  alas!  he  is  very  far  from 
having  built  a  home. 

There  are  several  types  of  women 
who  are  not  home-makers.  There  are, 
likewise,  several  types  of  men  who  are 
not  home-makers.  Sometimes  this 
lack  of  the  home-building  capacity  is 
merely  an  inherent  infirmity,  as  much 
to  be  pitied  as  to  be  blamed.  There 
is  a  male  prototype  of  Dickens' 
"Little  Dora"  that  few  people  take 
into  account. 

He    is   the   amiable,   pleasant   man, 


whose  intentions  are  better  than  his 
performances.  He  is  perfectly  un- 
conscious of  his  failings  and  his  failures. 
He  "does  the  best  he  can,"  and  his 
best  is  not  even  a  "second"  best,  but 
a  fifth-rate  best,  or  even  farther  down 
in  the  scale.  When  he  can't  meet  his 
grocer's  bill,  he  says:  "Why,  Smith 
is  not  in  a  hole,  why  does  he  bother 
the  life  out  of  me?"  And  when  pros- 
perous Smith  (Smith  is  the  grocer) 
rides  up  to  the  front  door  in  his  auto- 
mobile to  deliver  his  bill  in  person, 
the  man  who  "  can't "  points  out  of  the 
window  and  says:  "Just  look  at  that 
turnout  of  yours,  and  you  want 
money  from  me!"  And  he  laughs. 
"Why,  man,  it's  ridiculous!  I  can't 
pay  you  now,  but  I  will  just  as  soon 
as  I  can." 

Then  Smith,  the  grocer,  goes  away 
despondent  and  shuts  down  on  further 
"credit." 

It  is  the  woman  home-builder  in 
this  case  who  suffers  most  from  the 
wavering  foundation  and  weak  walls. 
But  she  is  rarely  bitter  towards  this 
sort  of  a  hinder-mate.  She  is  sorry,  at 
first,  then  contempt  steals  into  her 
soul  before  she  knows  it  is  there,  and 
contempt  is  an  unbidden  guest  who 
takes  up  his  quarters  for  good !  ' '  Poor 
Sam! "  she  murmurs,  " I '11  just  have  to 
go  on  and  get  along  as  well  as  I  can 
without  counting  on  him  for  anything. 
The  children  will  be  able  to  help  me 
and  themselves  after  a  while.  Poor 
Sam!" 

But  it's  when  the  woman  or  the 
man  has  to  play  the  double  r61e  of 
home-provider  and  home-maker  that 
either  the  one  or  the  other  finds  the 
burden  too  heavy  to  bear.  This  hap- 
pens more  often  to  the  woman  than 
the  man,  because  the  home-making 
faculty  is  generally  born  in  her,  and, 
no  matter  what  outside  pressure  is 
put  upon  her,  that  faculty  will  find 
a  vent  for  itself. 

In  every  class  of  society,  women 
are  found  providing  the  daily  bread. 


408 


THE   BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


as  well  as  the  daily  strengthening 
spirit  in  the  home. 

The  washwoman,  who  hurries  home 
to  get  dinner  ready  for  the  children 
and  their  incompetent  father,  who 
is  perennially  "out  of  a  job,"  is  one 
of  these  unfortunate  ones.  The  restau- 
rant waitress  who  "fills  orders"  at 
some  down-town  counter  from  one  to 
four,  leaving  eight-year-old  Janie  to 
mind  the  baby  while  she  is  gone,  is 
another.  Close  questioning  will  usu- 
ally disclose  the  fact  (if  she  is  n't  a 
widow)  that  her  husband  is  "serving 
time"  for  some  drunken  row  or,  as 
she  more  delicately  puts  it,  "is  in 
trouble!" 

Then  there  is  the  educated,  refined 
gentlewoman  who  does  embroidery,  or 
typewriting,  or  anything  on  earth 
within  her  capacity,  to  be  sure  of  the 
rent  and  grocery  bills,  "because  James 
is  really  temperamentally  unfitted  to 
be  in  a  subordinate  position,  and  the 
places  he  would  find  congenial  are  so 
hard  to  find!" 

Of  course  one  makes  the  mental 
comment  that  James  is  "tempera- 
mentally" selfish  and  egotistical,  and 
one  feels  extremely  sorry  for  James' 
poor  wife,  for  neither  the  beauty  of 
an  Apollo  nor  the  complete  list  of 
masculine  charms  can  offset  a  man's 
incapacity  to  do  the  every-day  duties 
of  a  man. 

That  is  the  real  point  of  view  of  the 
woman.  She  may  try  to  deceive  her- 
self and  other  people,  but  no  matter 


how  she  may  admire  "James'  "  musical, 
poetic,  histrionic,  or  any  other  talent, 
if  he  can't  do  what  the  ordinary, 
practical,  work-a-day  man  does,  in 
her  heart  she  has  a  contempt  for  him. 
She  gently  draws  the  veil  of  pity  over 
her  own  conviction,  but  she  knows 
what  lies  under  that  veil,  though  she 
rarely  confesses  as  much.  She  chants 
praises  of  James'  rare,  but  unrecog- 
nized, gifts,  and  bravely  goes  on  in 
her  efforts  to  "make  the  home." 

The  writer  remembers  hearing  one 
man  say  of  another,  once:  "The  Cap- 
tain's a  fine  man.  He  can  make  any- 
thing in  the  world  down  here,  except 
a  dollar!'' 

The  words  made  a  lasting  impression, 
and  they  are  always  recalled  to  her 
mind  when  the  impractical  man  of 
impractical  ideas  is  being  considered. 

Certainly  the  faculty  for  "making 
a  dollar"  is  an  important  one  for  the 
family  man  to  possess,  though  he  who 
possesses  it  alone  will  never  be  a 
genuine  home-maker. 

After  all  is  said,  one  comes  back  to 
the  first  conclusion  —  that  home-mak- 
ing must  be  a  mutual  effort,  because  it 
is  a  mutual  duty,  equally  insistent, 
equally  binding  upon  the  man  and  the 
woman. 

The  happy  home,  where  high  ideals 
are  set  and  pure  influences  radiate, 
has  its  foundation  laid  upon  love  and 
patience  and  selfless  thought,  and  is, 
in  reality  and  in  truth,  "a  house  not 
builded  by  hands!" 


.ove 


Light 


Sometimes  upon  the  sumimer  hills 
A  flooding  tenderness  is  shed. 

The  low  green  intervales  it  fills 
As  fills  the  silvery  stream  its  bed. 

One  moment  past,  it  was  not  there  — 

Or  were  mine  eyes  not  yet  aware  ? 


The  Light — it  comes  with  flickering  morn, 
At  harvest  noon,  on  sunset  plains, 

And  when  the  fields  look  old  and  lorn, 
And  on  the  bough  no  leaf  remains; 

And  it  can  reach  and  overflow 

The  cruel  spirit  of  the  snow. 


A  German  "Small  and  Early" 

(An  Easter  Egg  Hunt) 
By  Kate  Hudson 


GRETCHEN'S  little  ones  have 
lately  been  favored  with  in- 
vitations to  the  houses  of 
school-mates;  one,  a  child's  party, 
from  four  till  seven,  with  two  pieces 
of  music,  dancing,  parlor-magic  by  a 
professional,  elaborate  supper  with 
souvenir  cups  and  saucers  and  bon- 
bonnieres,  etc.;  the  other,  a  luncheon 
followed  by  a  matinee  theatre-party  — 
twenty  youngsters  at  a  luxuriously- 
appointed  table  laden  with  rick-shaws, 
and  afterwards  taken  in  a  chartered 
Park  stage  to  see  some  much-discussed 
extravaganza;  at  both  of  which  func- 
tions eleven-year-old  Mariechen  and 
nine-year-old  Kurt  have  a  fine  time, 
and  of  which  they  are  never  tired  of 
telling.  Gretchen  listens  with  much 
interest  — •  not  entirely  unmixed  with 
awe  at  the  grandeur  of  the  entertain- 
ments and  the  expensive  scale  on 
which  they  have  been  conducted;  and 
also  slightly  tinged  with  amusement  at 
the  painfully  grown-up  tastes  and 
arrogances  of  modern  Young  America 
—  to  what  her  boy  and  girl  relate. 
And  feeling  in  her  hospitable  heart  that 
she  must  return  the  attentions  paid  to 
her  children  she  wonders  how  she, 
in  an  apartment,  with  serviceably 
plain  furniture,  no  maid,  and  with  the 
simplest  of  table  appointments,  can 
minister  to  the  pleasure  of  those 
pampered  little  men  and  women  of 
the  world.  But  remembering  the  plea- 
sant times  she  has  had,  in  the  homely 
surroundings  of  her  youth,  in  simple 
childlike  amusements,  and  reflecting 
that,  after  all,  deep  down  in  their  jolly 
little  hearts  all  children  are  alike,  she 
determines  to  ask  Mariechen' s  little 
school-fellows  to  a  German  egg  hunt 
on  Easter-Monday. 


To  this  end  she  buys  a  box  of  corre- 
spondence cards,  and  from  some  colored 
paper  cuts  tiny  egg-shaped  pieces, 
which  she  neatly  pastes  along  their 
border  and  lets  Mariechen,  in  her 
very  best  copy  -  book  handwriting, 
indite  her  own  invitations.  These  are 
sent  out  on  Thursday,  five  days 
before  the  day,  and  all  for  Easter- 
Monday,  from  three  till  six.  .She  sends 
out  but  ten,  as  her  dining-room  is 
small  and  her  table  will  seat  only  the 
twelve  —  counting  in  her  Mariechen 
and  Kurt  —  comfortably.  When  Sat- 
urday morning's  post  brings  only  one 
regret,  all  others  accepting,  Gretchen 
and  her  chicks  feel  correspondingly 
elated  and  immediately  begin  their 
preparations. 

First  the  eggs  are  to  be  colored,  and 
a  goodly  supply  of  them,  too;  Gretchen 
and  her  two  helpers  try  for  as  many 
different  shades  and  combinations  as 
possible.  The  twenty-four  largest  and 
whitest  eggs  are  used  for  souvenir 
purposes.  Twelve  of  these  —  one  for 
each  child  —  are  decorated,  each  with 
the  name  of  a  little  guest,  and  on  the 
reverse  side  a  rabbit,  an  Easter  lily,  or 
duckling  or  chicken;  the  drawing,  in 
plain  outline,  and  writing  should  be 
done  with  a  quill  pen  dipped  in  melted 
lard;  when  the  egg  is  subsequently 
treated  to  its  color-bath,  the  writing 
stands  out  white.  And  of  the  other 
twelve,  six  are  inscribed,  each  with  a 
short  and  easy  riddle  and  the  remaining 
six  with  the  answers.  There  are  hosts 
of  jolly  little  riddles  that  treat  of  the 
hare  —  almost  as  popular  in  German 
child-poetry  and  fairy-lore  as  the  Negro 
Brer  Rabbit  —  and  a  great  many,  on 
the  egg.  Gretchen  chooses  the  six 
funniest  that  she  can  most  easily  turn 


409 


410 


THE   BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


into  English  and  transfers  them  and 
their  answers  to  the  eggs,  and  then 
colors  the  two  dozen.  They  as  well 
as  the  plainly-dyed  eggs  make  a*  fine 
showing. 

In  the  afternoon  Gretchen  takes 
Mariechen  and  Kurt  —  who  enjoy  the 
active  preparations  for  their  first  social 
effort  as  much  as  they  are  likely  to  do 
the  afternoon  itself  —  shopping  and 
gets  twelve  small,  brightly-colored 
baskets  for  the  egg  hunt,  and  a  dozen 
of  the  smallest  penny  toys  for  the 
surprise-eggs.  Also  a  handsomely  dec- 
orated candy-box  egg,  and  a  papier- 
mache  rabbit  for  prizes  in  games. 

On  Sunday,  when  Gretchen  bakes 
her  cakes,  she  carefully  breaks  away 
from  the  eggs  she  uses  for  them  only 
just  enough  of  the  shell  to  admit  of 
the  inner  egg  to  be  shaken  out;  after 
washing  and  drying  these  thoroughly, 
the  largest  twelve  are  laid  aside  till 
afternoon.  Then  Mariechen  and  Kurt, 
with  Mother  Gretchen's  help,  insert 
into  the  little  opening  of  the  egg-shell 
fluffed-out  bits  of  pink  and  light  blue 
jeweller's  wadding,  then  a  penny  toy, 
and  then  more  bits  of  wadding,  to  stop 
any  possible  rattling.  The  broken  end 
of  the  egg  is  then  covered  with  a 
disk  of  thin  white  paper,  carefully 
pasted  on,  and  the  egg  is  placed,  paper- 
covered  end  downward,  in  an  egg-cup. 
Gretchen's  egg-cups,  with  all  the  rest 
of  her  china,  are  "made  in  Germany" 
and  just  the  size  of  an  egg.  The 
eleven  other  egg-shells  are  treated  in 
the  same  way. 

Just  before  going  to  bed  Kurt  and 
Mariechen,  under  their  mother's  di- 
rection, hide  all  the  eggs,  save  the 
egg-cup  surprise-eggs,  in  every  avail- 
able spot  in  parlors  and  hall;  under 
bookcases  and  music  stand,  behind 
chair-legs,  in  dusky  corners,  on  key- 
board of  piano,  shutting  down  the  lid, 
under  hanging  folds  of  the  window 
curtains  and  portiere  fringes,  in  the 
bric-a-brac,  on  etagere  and  mantle- 
piece.     The  Easter-egg  hunting  ground 


now  being  prepared,  the  children  retire 
to  peaceful  slumbers,  while,  with  the 
setting  of  her  Easter  bread,  Gretchen 
ends  her  busy  day's  work  also. 

On  Monday,  when  she  fills  her  two 
large  bread-pans,  she  takes  the  remain- 
ing dough  and  from  it  fashions  small 
rabbits  —  twelve  of  them  —  which  she 
bakes  with  her  loaves.  To  make  the 
"Hasen"  more  interesting,  Gretchen 
presses  into  the  soft  dough  of  the  head, 
where  Brer  Rabbit's  eye  should  twinkle, 
a  bright  new  penny  (or,  if  feeling  very 
rich  and  correspondingly  extravagant, 
a  dime) ,  which  adds  much  to  the  arch- 
ness and  amiability  of  Bunny's  ex- 
pression. But  under  the  penny  she 
lays  a  slightly  larger  round  of  thin, 
tough  writing  paper,  to  save  the  dough 
from  contact  with  the  coin. 

Gretchen  sets  her  table  for  twelve; 
on  each  plate  she  lays  a  pretty  Easter 
card,  with  the  name  of  the  guest  upon 
it,  and  the  metallic-eyed  rabbit.  In 
front  of  it  she  sets  the  egg-cup  with 
the  surprise-egg.  For  a  centerpiece 
she  takes  the  large,  old-fashioned 
"  Mai-Frank- Bowie,"  —  a  punch-bowl 
handed  down  to  her  through  mother 
from  grandmother,  —  lines  it  with  light 
green  crepe  paper  and  sets  in  the 
middle  of  it  the  dear  old  eighteen-inch- 
tall  brown  plush  Easter  rabbit,  holding 
a  nest,  which  was  given  to  Mariechen 
ten  years  ago  and  which  has  triumph- 
antly figured  at  all  subsequent 
Easter  celebrations,  between  times 
coyly  retiring  to  the  seclusion  which  a 
paper  box  high  up  on  a  dark  closet 
shelf  grants.  Gretchen  fills  his  nest 
with  egg-shaped  sugared  almonds,  but 
leaves  the  bowl  empty  and  ready  for 
the  contributions  of  the  egg-hunters. 
Then,  after  seeing  that  everything  else 
is  finished,  Gretchen  suspends  from 
the  chandelier  the  two  egg-shell  hang- 
ing baskets  given  her  on  Easter- day  by 
her  little  ones,  one  filled  with  tiny, 
sturdy,  dark- green,  flax  plants,  the 
other  with  the  light-green,  drooping 
tendrils  of  mustard  and  rape  seed,  and

leaves  the  room  to  waylay  her  boy  and 
girl  on  their  return  from  school  and 
oversee  their  prinking  for  the  company. 
The  little  guests  all  being  assembled, 
each  child  is  supplied  with  a  basket 
and  the  egg  hunt  begins.  Even  the

riddle  eggs  make  lots  of  fun  and  laugh-

the  rightful  owners  causes  much  di- 
version. The  riddle-eggs  and  those 
with  names  are  for  the  finders  to  keep ; 
the  others  all  for  the  "Bunny."  Each 
child  carries  the  filled  basket  with  it

Diet  in  Childhood,  Sickness  and  Old  Age 

By  Mary  D.   Chambers 

Tenth  and  Last  Paper 

Diseases  Especially  Influenced  by  Diet  —  Continued 


Dyspep 


sia 


WHEN  we  consider  the  length 
and  complexity  of  the  diges- 
tive processes,  and  the  fact 
that  the  slightest  deviation  from  the 
normal  will  cause  pain  and  discomfort, 
it  is  not  surprising  that  indigestion  — 
in  which  sense  "dyspepsia"  is  used  in 
this  paper  —  is  one  of  the  commonest 
complaints.  The  mouth,  the  stomach, 
the  liver,  the  pancreas,  are  all  agents 
in  the  process  of  digestion,  and  faulty 
or  defective  action  on  the  part  of  any 
one  of  these  may  give  rise  to  indiges- 
tion. 

The  Mouth.  —  One  of  the  commonest 
causes  of  indigestion  is  lack  of  sufficient 
mastication.  The  proteid  food  is  not 
divided  so  that  it  can  be  readily  acted 
on  by  the  gastric  juice;  the  starchy 
food  is  not  sufficiently  insalivated.  Be- 
sides its  action  on  the  food  itself,  the 
saliva  being  alkaline  stimulates  the  se- 
cretion of  the,  acid  gastric  juice,  thus 
aiding  the  digestion  of  food  in  the 
stomach. 

The  Stomach,  —  Many  and  various 
are  the  causes  of  indigestion  in  the 
stomach.     This  explains  why  it  is  that 


what  is  good  for  one  person  may  be 
bad  for  another  —  for  the  symptoms 
may  be  similar  while  the  cause  is  dif- 
ferent. Deficient  secretion  of  gastric 
juice  is  very  common.  A  small  cup  of 
hot  beef  tea  half  an  hour  before  dinner 
serves  as  an  excellent  stimulus  to  se- 
cretion ;  while  beginning  the  other  meals 
with  some  starchy  food  needing  masti- 
cation will  have  a  like  effect,  for  the 
reason  mentioned  in  the  last  paragraph. 
An  over-acid  secretion,  or  hyperacidity 
of  the  stomach  may  be  helped  by  the 
use  of  an  "acid-fixing"  food,  i.e.,  one 
that  can  combine  with  a  large  amount 
of  acid  in  the  process  of  gastric  diges- 
tion. Examples  of  such  foods  are  milk 
and  gelatine.  Hypopepsia,  or  deficient 
secretion  of  gastric  juice,  calls  for  easily 
digested  food,  complete  mastication, 
careful  attention  to  bowels,  and  abun- 
dance of  fresh  air.  For  sluggish  move- 
ments of  the  stomach,  resulting  in  long 
retention  of  the  food,  and  consequent 
decomposition,  the  remedies  just  stated, 
plus  endeavor  to  improve  the  circula- 
tion, will  be  found  of  value. 

The  Liver  and  Pancreas.  —  In  the 
liver,  hypersecretion  of  bile,  diminished 
secretion  of  bile,  and  alterations  in  the 


412 


THE   BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


composition  of  the  bile,  alike  provoke 
indigestion.  In  the  pancreas  the  causes 
of  indigestion  are  varied  and  obscure. 
Since  here  the  special  methods  of  treat- 
ment advocated  by  the  best  authorities 
nearly  all  contradict  one  another,  it 
may  be  inferred  that  personal  idio- 
syncracy  has  to  be  chiefly  taken  into 
account,  and  the  dietitian,  the  family 
physician  and  the  patient  must  in- 
vestigate, cautiously  experiment,  and 
cooperate  together. 

In  conclusion,  it  may  be  said  that 
the  best  remedies  for  indigestion,  and 
those  which  will  help  in  all  cases,  are 
thorough  mastication,  abundance  of 
fresh  air,  and  avoidance  alike  of  worry 
and  patent  medicine. 

The  Hygiene  of  Old  Age 

Let  me  begin  with  a  fable,  which  the 
dictionary  says  is  a  "fictitious  tale  in- 
tended to  instruct  or  amuse,"  —  only 
this  fable  is  a  really-truly  story  that 
actually  happened  to  fiesh-and-blood 
people. 

Once  upon  a  time  the  father  of  a 
small  girl  made  her  a  garden  bed  under 
the  west  windows  of  the  large  parlor, 
which  was  the  family  play-room.  And 
the  child,  taking  as  naturally  to  the 
earth  and  the  flowers  thereof  as  any 
small  daughter  of  Eve,  the  garden  grew 
with  her  growth,  until  at  last  it  became 
a  '  *  walled  garden  enclosed. ' '  But  when 
the  wind  blew  from  the  north  —  and  it 
mostly  did  —  it  was  not  tempered  to 
the  buds  and  blossoms,  but  flatted 
things  out  against  the  wet  loam  and 
most  injudiciously  caused  devastation 
in  that  portion  of  the  globe  important 
above  all  its  kingdoms,  Margaret's 
garden.  So  the  father  offered  to  plant 
a  row  of  young  trees  on  the  exposed 
side,  to  break  the  force  of  the  storms. 
Now  Margaret  had  seen  young  trees, 
and  knew  they  were  very  small  to 
begin  with,  so  she  cannily  inquired  how 
long  it  would  be  before  **they  would 
be  as  big  as  the  trees  on  the  lawn?" 

"Why,"     said    the    father,     "those 


younger  trees  on  the  lawn,  the  silver 
poplars  and  the  birches,  were  all 
planted  by  your  grandfather,  only 
twenty  years  ago." 

"Twenty  years!"  cried  Margaret, 
aghast,  "I'd  be  two  years  past  thirty 
then,  and  that  would  be  too  old  to 
enjoy  life.  No,  I'll  not  have  any 
young  trees  planted  for  me." 

In  the  fullness  of  time  life's  larger 
blessings  were  given  to  Margaret, 
—  woman's  deepest  and  richest  ex- 
periences, — •  until  one  sore  year  came 
when  she  was  bereft  of  all,  —  husband, 
and  child,  and  fortune,  and  home. 
Her  thirty-third  year  found  her  com- 
pleting her  fifth  year  of  "keeping 
books"  in  an  insurance  office,  and 
found  her  also,  much  to  her  surprise, 
finding  that  life  grew  better  the  longer 
it  was  lived.  Half  timidly,  half 
ashamed  of  her  own  singularity,  she 
mentioned  this  experience  to  the  quiet 
woman  who  worked  at  the  next  desk, 
and  related  with  a  smile  her  childish 
conviction  that  the  grown-upness  of 
thirty  meant  a  barren,  joyle§s  waste 
thenceforward.  "Now,"  she  said, 
"curiously  enough,  I  feel  happier  every 
year.     How  is  it  with  you?" 

"I  feel  just  the  same,"  said  the 
Quiet  Woman,  her  face  alight  as  Mar- 
garet had  never  seen  it  before;  "in- 
deed, my  feeling  of  happiness  increases 
so  every  year  that  I  often  think  I  must 
be  approaching  a  climax,  and  after 
that  the  'down  grade'  must  come. 
But  I'll  ask  my  sister;  she  is  six  years 
older  than  I  am,  and  she'll  know. 
Indeed,  I  often  thought  of  asking  her, 
only  it  seemed  so  silly  of  me  to  be 
feeling  happy  at  my  age,  —  and  I'm 
three  years  older  than  you." 

But  on  being  questioned,  the  Quiet 
Woman's  Sister,  at  forty -two,  confessed, 
with  shame,  to  being  still  on  the  "up 
grade,"  ridiculous  as  it  must  seem  to 
right-thinking  people.  The  spirit  of 
investigation  aroused,  the  sisters  now 
questioned  their  father,  a  man  past 
seventy,    feeble    in    body,    and    much 


DIET  IN  CHILDHOOD,  SICKNESS  AND  OLD  AGE 


413 


given  to  silence.     His  reply  was  mem- 
orable to  both  sisters. 

"If  there  is  a  climax,"  he  said,  "I 
have  not  reached  it.  Sometimes  I  feel 
that  my  happiness  is  almost  too  much, 
that  I  cannot  bear  it." 

Here  ends  the  true  story  of  Margaret 
and  her  queer  friends. 

But  was  their  experience  unusual  or 
abnormal?  The  writer  confesses  to 
having  reached  the  "uninteresting 
years,"  and  to  having  questioned  many 
other  women  as  to  how  those  years 
felt  to  them.  Five  out  of  twenty  de- 
clare them  exceeding  good,  —  a  sten- 
ographer, a  farm,  housekeeper,  the  ' 
registrar  of  a  school,  a  doctor's  wife, 
and  the  president  of  a  woman's  college, 
these  all  say  that  instead  of  "going 
down  the  vale  of  years,"  they  are 
climbing  a  mountain,  ascending  into 
sunshine  and  pure  air,  seeing  things 
behind  in  a  better  perspective,  a  truer 
proportion,  and  above  all  they  speak 
of  the  beauty  of  the  view,  the  wonder 
of  the  vision.  One  of  them  says  that 
as  she  looks  at  her  "garden  of  girls" 
she  thinks  with  joy  of  the  happy  time 
that  is  ahead  of  them,  the  abiding 
joys  of  the  years  they  now  think  so 
grey. 

To  be  sure  these  women  are  a  long 
way  from  even  the  gate  of  old  age,  but 
the  prospects  are  good  for  such  an  old 
age  as  that  of  the  Quiet  Woman's 
Father,  an  old  age  that  shall  see  the 
dross  filtered  out  and  the  permanent 
good  retained,  as  in  Rabbi  ben  Ezra, 
rather  than  the  good  drained  off  and 
the  worthless  reserved,  as  in  Matthew 
Arnold's  "Growing  Old,"  which  must 
have  been  the  fruit  of  years  of  school 
inspecting  in  England. 

The  writer  further  confesses  to  her 
belief  that  this  "joy  in  life"  is  due  not 
in  all  cases  to  righteousness,  for  she 
knows  a  great  sinner  who  possesses  it, 
but  largely  to  hygiene,  a  sense  of 
humor,  and  a  habit  of  optimism. 

The  hygiene  of  old  age  can  be  com- 
prised in  a  very  few  rules. 


In  old  age  the  stomach  is  usually 
prolapsed,  diminished  in  size,  and  its 
muscular  power  is  decreased;  hence 
the  meals  should  be  light  and  frequent, 
composed  of  easily  digested  food,  and 
free,  especially,  from  the  tough  cellu- 
lose of  the  coarser  vegetables.  Ab- 
stemiousness in  eating  is  imperative 
for  the  aged;  a  hearty  meal  is  apt  to 
introduce  too  much  chyle  into  the 
blood  for  the  enfeebled  system  to  take 
care  of. 

The  blood  of  the  old  person  is  im- 
poverished, hence  the  free  use  of  milk 
is  indicated. 

The  arteries  are  more  or  less  calcified. 
Distilled  water  should  be  used  as  a 
beverage  by  everyone  past  sixty,  and 
apple  juice  should  be  freely  used,  the 
malic  acid  being  a  solvent  for  the  lime 
salts  which  indurate  the  blood-vessels. 

The  heart  of  an  old  person  is  always 
weak,  hence  not  only  over-stimulating 
food  but  over-excitement  of  all  kinds 
should  be  avoided,  on  account  of  the 
more  or  less  harmful  reaction.  Some 
gentle  stimulant,  such  as  a  little  tea 
or  coffee,  or  a  very  small  allowance  of 
alcoholic  stimulant,  will,  unless  con- 
traindicated  by  some  pathogenic  con- 
dition, save  tissue  waste  and  give 
"tone"  to  the  circulation. 

While  strain  and  excitement  of  all 
kinds  should  be  avoided,  mental  work 
of  the  kind  most  interesting  to  the 
individual  should  be  pursued  as  far  as 
may  be  desired.  This  is  conducive  to 
health  of  mind  and  body.  In  the 
normal  old  age  the  brain  and  nerve 
centers  should  suffer  scarcely  any 
waste.  Nature,  apparently  recogniz- 
ing these  organs  as  the  most  precious, 
has  endowed  them  with  a  high  measure 
of  vitality  and  resistance  to  decay.  A 
woman  once  observed  that  not  until 
her  sight  failed  had  she  been  able  to 
see  clearly.  So  may  it  be  with  all  of 
us,  that  as  the  "veil  of  flesh"  wears 
thin  we  too  may  behold  the  wonder 
and  the  beauty,  the  joy  and  the  good- 
ness of  life..

is,  also,  an  acknowledgment  that  a  subscrip- 
tion, or  a  renewal  of  the  same,  has  been  re- 
ceived.

change  an  address,  please  give  the  old  address 
as  well  as  the  new.

know  the  name  as  it  was  formerly  given,  to- 
gether with  the  Post-office,  County,  State, 
Post-office  Box,  or  Street  Number.
