"  Manual  training  ought  to  be  a  part 
of  the  education  of  every  child,  rich  or 
poor. 

"The  training  of  the  physical  senses 
and  the  skillful  use  of  the  hands  helps 
in  the  development  and  education  of 
the  brain.  Skilled  manual  labor,  there- 
fore, in  some  form  is  good  for  every- 
body." 


"Talk  not  to  a  physician  of  music, 
nor  of  medicine  to  a  fiddler,  unless  the 
fiddler  should  be  sick,  and  the  physi- 
cian at  a  concert.  He  that  speaks  only 
of  such  subjects  as  are  familiar  to  him- 
self, treats  the  company  as  the  stork 
did  the  fox,  presenting  an  entertain- 
ment to  him  in  a  deep  pitcher,  out  of 
which  no  creature  could  feed  but  a 
long-billed  fowl." 


THE   JUDICIAL   MIND 

THERE  are  always  two  sides  to  a 
question,  and  yet  how  difficult 
it  is  to  put  oneself  in  the  other 
fellow's  place  and  look  at  matters  from 
his  point  of  view.  From  tempera- 
ment and  training,  some  have  precon- 
ceived notions  to  which  everything 
must  be  made  to  conform.  They  will 
not  listen  to  or  think  of  the  views  of 
others.  Those  who  differ  from  them 
in  any  respect  must  simply  be  smashed. 
Their  methods  are  those  of  ages  past, 
when  differences  in  opinions  were  the 
cause  of  open  persecution.  Certainly 
people  of  this  class  have  not  the 
judicial  mind. 

How  much  of  trouble  and  strife 
would  be  avoided,  if  people  could  only 
learn  to  weigh  and  consider  both  sides 
of  matters,  whether  these  be  little  or 
great,  and  to  respect  the  views  of  others. 

Toleration  has  been  a  plant  of  slow 
growth,  but  it  has  grown.  Fear  and 
hate  are  less  prevalent  in  the  conduct 
of  mankind  than  once  was  the  case. 
What  sweetness  can  be  in  a  life  of 
fear?  What  actuating  motive  can  be 
so  baneful  as  hate? 

The  judicial  mind  is  fair,  consider- 
ate and  tolerant.  It  examines  cases 
from  every  view-point  and  infers  ac- 
cordingly; it  is  the  trait  to  cultivate. 
We  are  slow  to  learn  the  fact  that  in 
case  our  theory  or  premise  be  wrong, 
our  conclusions  drawn  therefrom  are, 
also,  all  wrong.  Therefore,  guard  care- 
fully your  premises. 

"  Be  calm  in  arguing;  for  fierceness  makes: 
Error  a  fault,  and  truth  discourtesy." 


INDUSTRIAL   EDUCATION 

CONGRESSMAN  C.  R.  DAVIS  of 
Minnesota  has  introduced  into 
the  lower  branch  of  Congress  an 
industrial  high-school  bill,  and  the  hope 
is  inspired  that  Congress  will  be  in- 
duced to  consider  the  measure  during 
the  present  session.  Of  the  provisions 
of  this  bill  Judge  Shields,  of  the  Su- 


EDITORIALS 


415 


preme  Court  of  California,  in  a  private 
letter,  writes  as  follows: 

"Rightly  enforced  the  bill  is  ad- 
mirable. First  it  provides  for  tech- 
nical, industrial  and  practical  educa- 
tion as  distinguished  from  the  old 
theory  of  educating  all  alike ,  regardless 
of  his  type  or  the  life  he  was  to  lead; 
of  teaching  the  boy  or  girl  books  alone, 
that  they  might  thereby  acquire  the 
strength  and  fiber  of  mind  to  later 
learn  men  and  things.  The  character 
of  education  proposed  by  this  bill  will 
teach  the  realities  of  life  direct,  and  in 
the  process  cultivate  the  mind  as  well 
as  the  old  plan. 

"One  of  the  most  fruitful  fields  of  the 
education  of  the  future  will  be  nature, 
the  wondrous  world  we  live  in,  and 
of  this  field  agriculture  comprehends 
its  best  features.  This  bill  is  broader 
than  that,  however,  including  mechan- 
ics and  domestic  science  within  its 
purposes.  I  am  sure  that  a  vigorous 
distribution  of  learning  in  these  arts 
and  sciences  can  do  no  harm  and  will 
do  incalculable  good. 

"  I  am  not  afraid  of  vulgarizing  the 
world  or  making  it  what  is  called 
'utilitarian,'  by  teaching  all  of  the 
people  to  know  plants  and  animals; 
through  domestic  science  to  feed  the 
baby  with  wholesome  food  and  the 
soul  with  all  of  the  finer  things  which 
are  comprehended  within  the  limits  of 
a  real  home,  or  through  the  mechanic 
arts,  which  train  men's  bodies  to  their 
highest  usefulness  and  to  give  expres- 
sion to  the   mind's  best   conceptions. 

"I  am  not  afraid  of  the  fate  of  a 
country  whose  people  are  given  over  to 
breeding  perfect  animals,  rearing  crops 
of  best  excellence,  building  good  roads, 
labor-saving  machinery ,  automobiles 
and  railroads,  constructing  convenient 
houses,  furnishing  them  perfectly  and 
making  real  homes  of  them,  to  the 
building  of  bath-tubs,  arranging  plumb- 
ing, weaving  fine  cloths  and  making 
them  into  right  form.  If  the  doing  of 
such  things  does  not  make  for  peace 


and  union,  for  moral  industry  and 
wholesome  progress,  then  my  judg- 
ment is  hopelessly  at  fault. 

"Nor  can  I  doubt  that  out  of  such 
conditions  will  surely  spring  the  sound- 
est moral  and  intellectual  cu.lture  and 
impulse.  This  bill  represents  this  gen- 
eral direction  and  purpose,  and  I  have 
no  hesitation  in  pronouncing  it  sound 
and  wise." 


THE  SENSE  OF  PROPORTION 


E 


By  L.  M.  C. 

VERYTHING  goes  by  compari- 
son," cried  a  wise  man  long 
ago.  But  he  might  have  added 
that  it  taxes  the  world's  brain  to  make 
these  comparisons  accurately ,  and  that 
the  resultant  selections  we  m_ake,  from 
our  principles  and  matrimonial  part- 
nerships down  to  the  veriest  details  of 
our  daily  living,  these  determine  what 
we  are  and  what  we  shall  be. 

The  Indian  who  traded  a  tract  of 
land  for  a  string  of  beads  was  simply 
a  child  in  his  realization  of  values. 
He  made  no  comparisons.  The  fancy 
of  the  moment  was  his  motive  of 
action. 

The  question, "  What  is  worth  while  ?" 
is  a  supreme  one,  the  question  most 
worth  asking  ourselves;  and  it  is  con- 
stantly recurring  as  to  details,  although 
our  aim  in  life  may  be  definitely 
determined. 

For  so  much  is  worth  while  in 
twentieth  century  days  that  many 
minds  are  overwhelmed.  People  want 
to  be  and  do  and  have  all  the  good 
things.  As  a  result,  their  lives  are  a 
conglomeration,  perplexing,  complex, 
contradictory,  unsatisfying.  Success 
and  satisfaction  belong  to  the  sim- 
plified life. 

Men  in  business  are  obliged  to  nar- 
row their  attention  to  one  chosen  line, 
in  order  to  make  of  it  anything  Hke 
success.  Women,  however,  are  usually 
left  freer,  and  they  flit  from  this  to 
that,    butterfly    fashion;    hence    their 


416 


THE   BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


lack  of  stability  of  character,  their 
whimsical  living,  their  passing  fads, 
and  their  ultimate  failure,  too  often, 
to  achieve  anything  that  is  worthy. 

They  not  only  fail  to  settle  upon  a 
definite  line  of  action,  but  there  is  a 
lost  sense  of  proportion,  a  failure  akin 
to  the  Indian's  to  realize  true  values. 
It  is  shown  in  little  things  rather  than 
larger  ones.  For  instance,  a  woman 
will  go  without  an  umbrella  in  order 
to  buy  a  pair  of  white  shoes;  she  will 
spend  money  for  ice-cream  soda,  when 
there  are  holes  in  the  heels  of  her 
stockings;  she  will  furnish  her  parlor 
elaborately,  and  tack  up  ragged  scrim 
for  bedroom  curtains.  These  are  ex- 
treme cases,  yet,  alas!  not  few. 

With  a  lost  sense  of  the  value  of 
time  and  the  inestimable  value  of  eye- 
sight ,  she  will  labor  over  lace  or  drawn- 
thread  work,  making  articles  that  are 
neither  artistic  nor  durable.  Think  of 
the  time,  eyesight,  nerve  force,  and 
good  calico  wasted  by  our  grand- 
mothers in  their  elaborately-pieced 
quilts!  Yet  a  dollar  and  a  half  bed- 
spread is  a  hundred  times  prettier  and 
more  serviceable.  If  they  had  only 
spent  that  time  and  energy  in  cultivat- 
ing their  minds  and  educating  our 
fathers  and  mothers,  America  would  be 
a  long  step  ahead  of  what  it  is  today. 

It  was  a  mistake  in  their  compari- 
sons. They  thought  they  were  thrifty, 
that  they  were  performing  a  real  duty. 
Just  so  today;  women  are  toiling  con- 
scientiously over  things  that  are  not 
worth  the  doing,  or  not  worth  nearly 
so  much  as  other  things  at  their  very 
hands.  Pendulum-like,  they  have 
thrown  down  the  quilt  blocks  and 
swung  to  the  other  extreme,  going 
here  and  there,  doing  a  thousand 
things  outside  their  homes,  some  of 
which  are  grand  and  noble,  but  others 
not  worth  what  they  are  putting  into 
them.  How  sadly  they  need  to  pause 
and  ask,  "Is  this  important?  Is  it 
as  important  as ?" 

To  know  beyond  a  peradventure,  let 


her  ask  herself,  "Which  will  have 
counted  most  a  year  hence?"  The 
time  test  is  the  best  we  have. 

Weigh  your  child's  disposition 
against  its  ability  to  play  the  piano; 
its  health  against  the  family  reputa- 
tion for  being  fashionable.  (Teddy- 
bear  coats  and  bare  legs  sound  har- 
monious enough,  but  common  sense 
weeps  at  the  sight.)  What  are  social 
achievements  to  your  own  health  and 
the  comfort  of  your  home? 

A  just  sense  of  proportion  in  matters 
of  dress,  in  household  expenditures,  in 
time  values,  and  in  affairs  of  the  heart 
—  this  is  the  secret  of  happiness.  By 
means  of  it  we  eliminate  the  unim- 
portant ;  small  worries  drop  away  from 
us  or  fail  to  excite  as  they  once  did, 
and  the  really  good  things  of  life  loom 
large  to  us.  The  way  of  duty  becomes 
straight  and  clear,  so  that  we  walk 
forward  with  hope  and  cheer  and  per- 
fect serenity,  knowing  we  are  in  the 
right. 

But  it  often  takes  a  great  sorrow  to 
open  the  eyes;  tears  to  clear  the  vision, 
and  show  that  the  baubles  we  fought 
over  are  but  ashes  of  roses  when 
grasped.  A  child  cries  because  her 
dollie's  wig  is  loose;  a  woman  weeps 
because  another  woman  unintentionally 
snubbed  her;  but,  by  and  by,  both, 
having  suffered  greater  hurts,  learn  to 
smile  at  the  pin-pricks. 

We  cannot  "gain  the  whole  world" 
however  hard  we  try,  but  each  of  us 
can  "save  her  own  soul"  (and  several 
besides)  by  living  sensibly,  earnestly, 
and  for  that  which  is  most  worth  while. 
Therefore,  make  your  comparisons  care- 
fully, decide  with  definiteness  and  de- 
termination, and  enter  upon  the  Foot- 
path of  Peace. 


A  man  sent  this  answer  to  a  book- 
seller, who  sent  in  his  account  for  a 
book  some  time  before  delivered:  "I 
never  ordered  the  book.  If  I  did,  you 
did  not  send  it.  If  I  got  it,  I  paid  for 
it.     If'l  didn't,  I  won't." 


Asparagus,  with  Hollandaise  Sauce 

Seasonable  Recipes 

By  Janet  M.  Hill 

IN  all  recipes  where  flour  is  used,  unless  otherwise  stated,  the  flour  is  measured  after  sifting 
once.  When  flour  is  measured  by  cups,  the  cup  is  filled  with  a  spoon,  and  a  level  cupfiil 
is  meant.  A  tablespoonful  or  a  teaspoonful  of  any  designated  material  is  a  level  spoonful  of 
such  material. 


Orange  and  Strawberry  Cocktail 

PEEL  the  oranges  and  remove  the 
pulp  neatly  and  without  having 
any  of  the  white  membrane  left 
upon  it;  divide  it  into  half -inch  pieces, 
saving  all  the  juice.  Hull  the  berries, 
wash  and  dry  them,  if  necessary,  then 
cut  into  halves.  Set  the  whole  aside 
to  become  chilled.  When  ready  to 
serve,  mix  the  pieces  of  berry  and 
orange  with  a  very  little  sugar.  Divide 
the  fruit  among  the  glasses  and  pour 
over  it  the  juice.  A  little  champagne 
or  maraschino  may  be  added  with  the 
juice. 

Cream  of  Asparagus  and  Tomato 

Cook  a  small  bunch  of  asparagus  in 
boiHng  salted  water.  When  tender 
press  as  much  of  the  asparagus  as  pos- 
sible through  a  sieve;  add  the  water  in 
which  it  was  cooked,  half  an  onion  in 
which  two  cloves  have  been  pressed, 


two  sprigs  of  parsley,  a  cup  and  a  half 
of  tomato  puree  (cooked  tomatoes 
pressed  through  a  sieve)  and  a  pint  of 
broth  or  water,  and  let  simmer  very 
gently  twenty  minutes.  In  the  mean- 
time, make  a  white  sauce  with  one- 
third  a  cup,  each,  of  butter  and  flour, 
half  a  teaspoonful  of  salt  and  three 
cups  of  milk.  Remove  the  onion, 
cloves  and  parsley  from  the  vegetable 
mixture  and  add  the  sauce;  pass  the 
whole  through  a  fine  sieve,  reheat, 
adding  seasoning  if  needed,  and  serve. 
The  beaten  yolks  of  two  eggs,  muxed 
with  half  or  a  whole  cup  of  cream  or 
beaten  into  three  tablespoonfuls  of 
creamed  butter,  gives  a  richer  soup. 
Do  not  let  the  soup  boil  after  the  yolks 
are  added. 

Salt  Mackerel  Cooked  in  Milk 

Let  a  mackerel  lie,  flesh  side  down, 
overnight  in  cold  water.  In  the  morn- 
ing drain  and  dispose  in  an  agate  pan

where  it  will  lie  flat ;  cover  to  the  depth 
of  one-fourth  an  inch  with  fresh,  sweet 
milk  and  let   simmer  very   gently,  on 


Truffled  Fish  Mousse 

the  back  of  the  range  or  in  the  oven, 
about  twenty  minutes.  Serve  with 
plain  boiled  or  baked  potatoes. 

Shad  Roes  Baked  in  Tomato  Sauce 

Cut  fine  a  small  onion  and  a  green 
or  red  pepper  and  cook  in  two  table- 
spoonfuls  of  butter  until  softened  and 
yellowed,  then  skim  from  the  butter 
and  sprinkle  over  the  bottom  of  an 
au  gratin  dish.  Above  the  vegetables 
set  two  fresh  shad  roes.  Have  ready 
a  cup  of  hot  tomato  sauce;  pour  this 
over  the  roes  and  set  the  dish  into  the 


Crab-Meat  Flakes  for  Patties  and 
Swedish  Timbale  Cases 

Melt  three  table- 
spoonfuls  of  butter,  and 
in  it  cook  three  table- 
spoonfuls  of  flour  and 
half  a  teaspoonful,  each, 
of  salt  and  paprika  until 
frothy,  then  add  a  cup 
and  a  half  of  thin  cream ; 
stir  constantly  until 
boiling,  then  lightly 
mix  in  two  cups  of  crab- 
meat  flakes;  let  stand 
on  the  range  to  become  very  hot,  then 
mix  in  the  beaten  yolks  of  two  eggs 
mixed  with  one-fourth  a  cup  of  creamed 
butter  or  thin  cream  and  stir  gently 
until  the  egg  is  set.  A  fine-chopped 
truffle  may  be  added  to  the  dish,  if 
desired. 

Truffled  Fish  Mousse 

Cut  truffles  into  thin  slices  and  from 
these  stamp  out  rounds,  crescents,  etc., 
to  decorate  individual  moulds.  For 
four  moulds  one  or  two  truffles  will  be 
needed.    Butter  the  moulds  thoroughly, 


Lamb  Cutlets  a  la  Soubise 


oven.  Let  bake  about  half  an  hour, 
basting  several  times  with  the  tomato 
sauce,  unless  the  sauce  covers  the  roes, 
when  basting  is  not  needed.  Serve  in 
the  baking  dish. 


press  the  decorations  into  the  butter, 
and  set  aside  to  become  chilled.  Make 
a  sauce  of  three  level  tablespoonfuls, 
each,  of  butter  and  flour,  one-fourth  a 
teaspoonful,  each,  of  salt  and  pepper,

419 


and  one  cup  and  a  half  of  water  or 
fish  broth  (remnants,  skin  and  bone  of 
fish  cooked  with  water,  onion  and 
carrot).  Cool  one-fourth  a  cup  of  the 
sauce  to  use  in  the  mousse  and  keep 
the  rest  hot  to  serve  with  the  dish. 
Pound  enough  clear  fish  to  give  half  a 
cup;  add  the  white  of  one  egg  and 
pound  again;  add  the  cold  sauce  and 
continue  pounding  until  the  mixture  is 
very  smooth,  then  press  through  a  sieve. 
Then  fold  in  the  white  of  one  egg, 
beaten  dry,  and  half  a  cup  of  double 
cream,  beaten  solid,  also  half  a  tea- 
spoonful  of  salt  and  pepper  as  desired. 
Turn  into  the  prepared  moulds  and  let 
cook  on  several  folds  of  paper  in  a  dish 


of  such  as  contain  this  bone.  Broil  on 
a  hot,  well-oiled  broiler  from  six  to  ten 
minutes,  according  to  thickness.  Have 
ready  a  ring  or  mound  of  hot,  mashed 
potato,  and  dispose  the  chops  on  the 
mound;  pour  a  soubise  sauce  around 
the  mound  and  dispose  in  the  sauce  as 
many  browned  onions  as  persons  to 
serve.  Cover  the  rib  bones  with  paper 
frills. 

Soubise  Sauce 

Slice  four  onions  of  medium  size; 
add  four  sprigs  of  parsley  and  boiling 
water  to  cover,  and  let  simmer  until 
the  onion  is  tender  and  the  water  re- 
duced   somewhat,    then    remove    the 


Entire -Wheat  Biscuit 


of  hot  water  until  firm  in  the  center. 
Serve  turned  from  the  moulds.  To 
the  sauce  kept  hot  for  the  purpose  add 
three  or  four  tablespoonfuls  of  butter 
and  the  trimmings  from  the  truffles, 
chopped  fine,  and  pour  around  the 
moulds  of  ■  fish ;  a  little  lemon  juice 
may  be  added  to  the  sauce.  Serve 
cucumber  salad  on  chilled  plates. 

Lamb  Cutlets  a  la  Soubise 

Wipe  the  chops  with  a  damp  cloth, 
to  remove  any  bits  of  bone  clinging  to 
them.     Scrape  the  flesh  from  the  ribs 


parsley  and  press  the  rest  through  a 
sieve.  Prepare  a  cup  of  sauce,  using 
milk,  cream  or  broth  as  the  liquid;  add 
the  onion  puree  and  beat  in  two  table- 
spoonfuls  of  butter  with  salt  as  needed. 

Browned  Onions 

Butter  a  baking  dish.  Set  the  re- 
quired number  of  peeled  onions  in  the 
dish  and  pour  around  them  a  cup  of 
beef  broth  (dark  in  color) ;  put  in  some 
left-over  bits  of  cooked  bacon  or  a 
tablespoonful  of  fine-chopped,  raw, 
lean   ham,   sprinkle   a   little   sugar   on

the  top  of  each  onion,  and  above  this 
set  a  bit  of  butter;  cover  the  whole 
with   a   buttered   paper   and   let   cook 


Cucumber-and-Radish  Salad 

slowly  until  it  is  tender,  and  the  liquid 
is  reduced.  It  will  take  from  one  to 
two  hours.  This  liquid  (strained)  may 
be  used  in  making  the  soubise  sauce. 

Cucumber-and-Radish  Salad 

Let  the  cucumber  and  radishes  stand 
in  cold  or  iced  water  until  chilled. 
Pare  the  cucumber  and  cut  it  into  thin, 
smooth  slices.  Slice  the  radishes  with- 
out paring  them.  Dispose  the  slices 
of  cucumber  in  a  salad  bowl  in  a  circle, 
with   two   radish   slices   between   each 


General  Rule  for  French  Dressing 

Mix  half  a  teaspoonful  of  salt  and 
one-fourth  a  teaspoon- 
ful of  pepper,  then  add 
six  tablespoonfuls  of  oil 
and  gradually  beat  in 
three  tablespoonfuls  of 
vinegar. 

Asparagus,  with 
Hollandaise  Sauce 

Tie  the  trimmed  as- 
paragus into  as  many 
bunches  as  persons  to 
serve.  Cook  the  aspar- 
agus in  boiling,  salted 
water  until  tender  (about 
twenty  minutes) .  Have 
ready  a  slice  of  toast  for  each  bunch 
of  asparagus,  also  some  Hollandaise 
sauce.  Set  the  asparagus  on  the  toast 
and  pour  the  sauce  over  the  tips. 

Hollandaise  Sauce 

Cream  half  a  cup  of  butter;  add  the 
yolks  of  four  eggs,  one  at  a  time,  also 
one-fourth  a  teaspoonful,  each,  of  salt 
and  paprika  and  half  a  cup  of  boiling 
water.  Cook  over  hot  water,  stirring 
constantly  until  the  sauce  thickens; 
add  two  tablespoonfuls  of  lemon  juice 


Asparagus  Omelet 


slice  of  cucumber.  Pour  over  a  French 
dressing  to  which  half  a  teaspoonful  of 
onion  juice  has  been  added. 


and  it  is  ready  to  serve.  This  may  be 
made  with  two  instead  of  four  yolks. 
With  four  yolks  the  sauce  will  be  of 


SEASONABLE    RECIPES 


421 


the  consistency  of  mayonnaise  dressing. 
This  sauce  cannot  be  kept  hot  over  hot 
water,  for  it  will  curdle.  If  the  sauce 
should  curdle,  make  one-fourth  a  cup  of 
white  sauce  (one-half  a  tablespoonful, 
each,  of  butter  and  flour,  one-fourth 
a  cup  of  the  asparagus  water)  and 
gradually  beat  the  curdled  sauce  in- 
to it. 

Asparagus  Omelet 

Cut  all  but  three  stalks  of  a  bunch 
of  asparagus  into  pieces  about  an  inch 
long  and  let  all  simmer  in  boiling, 
salted  water  until  tender.  Melt  three 
tablespoonfuls  of  butter  and  cook  in 
it  three  tablespoonfuls  of  flour,  a  scant 
half-teaspoonful  of  salt  and  a  dash  of 
black  pepper;  when  frothy  add  one 
cup  and  a  half  of  the  water  in  which 


the  bottom,  put  the  pan  into  the  oven 
and  let  cook  until  no  uncooked  egg 
clings  to  a  knife  thrust  into  the  center 
of  the  omelet.  Score  at  right  angles 
to  the  handle  of  the  pan.  Put  some 
of  the  asparagus  on  half  of  the  omelet; 
fold  and  turn  out  onto  a  hot  platter. 
Dispose  the  three  stalks  of  asparagus 
on  top  of  the  omelet  and  the  rest  of 
the  asparagus  and  sauce  around  it. 
Serve  at  once. 

Baltimore  Samp 

Pour  a  quart  of  boiling  water  over 
a  cup  of  Baltimore  samp  and  heat 
quickly  to  the  boiling  point;  let  boil 
two  or  three  minutes,  then  drain  and 
add  a  second  quart  of  boiling  water. 
Let  boil  rapidly  about  half  an  hour, 
then  cover  and  let  set  on  the  back  of 


Baltimore  Samp,  with  Cheese 


the  asparagus  was  cooked  (cooled  for 
this  purpose)  and  stir  and  cook  until 
boiling;  beat  in  a  tablespoonful  of 
butter  and  add  the  asparagus.  Have 
ready  the  yolks  of  four  eggs,  beaten 
light,  and  the  whites,  beaten  dry.  To 
the  yolks  add  one-fourth  a  teaspoon- 
ful,  each,  of  salt  and  pepper,  then  turn 
them  over  the  whites  and  cut  and  fold 
the  two  together.  Melt  a  tablespoonful 
of  butter  in  a  hot  omelet  pan;  turn  in 
the  egg  mixture,  and,  when  "set"  on 


the  range  to  simmer  for  the  rest  of  the 
day.  Add  boiling  water  as  needed, 
and,  after  cooking  about  six  hours,  a 
teaspoonful  and  a  half  of  salt.  During 
the  last  of  the  cooking  the  water  should 
evaporate.  The  samp  is  now  ready  to 
serve,  reheated,  as  a  breakfast  cereal, 
or  may  be  further  seasoned  to  serve  as 
a  vegetable  at  luncheon  or  dinner.  To 
serve  as  a  cereal,  add  a  little  hot  water 
and  reheat.  It  is  improved  by  re- 
cooking. 


422 


THE    BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


Baltimore  Samp,  with  Cheese 

Melt  two  tablespoonf uls  of  butter ;  in 
it    cook   two    tablespoonf  uls    of    flour, 


Peach  Tarts 

one-fourth  a  teaspoonful  of  salt,  also 
one-fourth  a  teaspoonful  of  paprika,  if 
desired,  and  when  well  blended  stir  in 
one  cup  of  cream  or  rich  milk;  con- 
tinue stirring  until  the  sauce  boils, 
then  stir  in  from  two  tablespoonfuls 
to  half  a  cup  of  grated  cheese  and  one 
cup  and  a  fourth  of  the  cooked  samp. 
Let  the  samp  stand  over  hot  water  or 
in  the  oven  until  very  hot.  Fine- 
chopped  parsley  and  a  little  onion  juice 
may  be  used  with  the  cheese  or  with- 
out it.

Steamed  Custard 

Steamed  Custard  (for  Two) 

Beat  one  egg  and  the  yolk  of  another; 
add  three  level  tablespoonfuls  of  sugar 


and   one-fourth   a   teaspoonful   of   salt 
and  beat  again,  then  stir  in  one  cup  of 
milk.     Turn  into  two  custard  cups;  set 
these    in    a    steamer    or    over    boiling 
water,  cover    and    let 
cook  until  firm.     The 
water  should  not  boil 
during  the  cooking  or 
the    custard    will    be 
spoiled.     A  double 
boiler    may    be    used, 
or  the   cups    may    be 
set  on  several  folds  of 
paper,  in  a  dish  of  hot 
water,  and  be  cooked 
either  on  the  back  of 
the    range    or   in   the 
oven.     Cover  during 
cooking  with  a  butter- 
ed paper.    When  firm, 
remove   from   the   water   and   grate   a 
little    nutmeg    over    the    top    of    the 
custards. 

Peach  Tarts 

Sift  together  two  cups  of  flour  and 

half  a  teaspoonful,  each,  of  salt  and 

baking  powder.     With  the  tips  of  the 

fingers  work  into  the  flour  half  a  cup- 

of   shortening,   then    add    gradually   a 

little  cold  water  and  mix  the  whole  to 

a  paste.     Use  a  knife  in  mixing.   When 

the  paste  worked  with  the  knife  comes. 

from  the   sides    of   the 

bowl,   leaving  it  clean, 

turn  the   paste  onto   a. 

floured     board,     knead. 

slightly,   and  then  with. 

the  rolling  pin  pat  and 

roll  into  a  sheet.     Have 

ready  one-fourth  a  cup- 

of    butter,    washed    ini 

cold  water  until  pliable, 

patted   on    a    cloth,  to* 

remove   water,  and 

chilled  a  little.   Cut  the- 

butter  into  shavings  and 

spread  them  evenly  over  the  paste ;  fold 

the  paste,  three  times,  turn  it  around  (tO' 

roll  in  a  direction  opposite  from  that  first 

employed)  and  roll  into  a  sheet,  keep- 


SEASONABLE    RECIPES 


423 


ing  the  edges  even ;  fold  to  make  three 
layers,  turn  it  around  and  again  roll 
into  a  sheet.  Fold,  turn  and  roll  again, 
leaving  the  paste  in  a  sheet  one-fourth 
an  inch  thick.  Use  this  to  cover  in- 
verted individual  tins.  Prick  the  paste 
all  over  with  a  fork  (to  let  out  the  air 
and  keep  the  paste  close  to  the  tin) 
and  bake  in  a  moderate  oven.  Put  a 
canned  peach  in  each  piece  of  pastry. 
Pipe  whipped  cream  above  and  sprinkle 
the  cream  with  fine-chopped  pistachio 
nuts.  Or,  fill  the  hollow  spaces  in  the 
center  and  around  the  peach  with 
jelly  or  marmalade  and  put  a  blanched 
almond  in  the  center  of  the  peach. 
Serve  with  the  syrup  from  the  peaches 
as  a  sauce.  Preserved  or  brandied 
peaches  may  be  used  in  the  same  way. 


close-covered,  well-buttered  mould.  An 
empty  baking-powder  box  makes  a 
good  can.  Leave  plenty  of  room  for 
the  pudding  to  swell  as  it  becomes  very 
light.     Serve  with  hard  or  liquid  sauce. 

Honey  Cakes 

(Emma  C.  Matern) 

Beat  four  eggs  very  light  without 
separating  the  whites  and  yolks;  then 
gradually  beat  in  one  cup  of  granu- 
lated sugar  and  half  a  cup  of  honey. 
Add  also  two  or  three  ounces,  each,  of 
blanched  almonds  and  preserved  citron 
that  have  been  run  through  a  food 
chopper  together  and  made  exceedingly 
fine.  Sift  together  two  cups  of  sifted 
flour,  two  level  teaspoonfuls  of  baking 
powder,  half  a  teaspoonful  of  ground

Honey  Cakes 


Steamed  Prune  Pudding  (for  Two) 

Mix  together  one-third  a  cup,  each, 
of  stale  bread  crumbs,  flour  sifted  with 
a  level  teaspoonful  of  baking  powder, 
fine-chopped  suet  and  sugar.  Beat 
one  egg  very  fight;  add  one-third  a 
cup  of  prune  puree  (about  four  cooked 
prunes,  stoned,  chopped  and  mixed 
with  prune  liquid),  one-fourth  a  tea- 
spoonful of  salt  and  one-third  a  cup 
of  milk.  Stir  the  liquid  into  the  dry 
ingredients.     Steam    two    hours    in    a 


ginger  and  one-fourth  a  teaspoonful  of 
ground  cloves.  Beat  the  flour  mixture 
into  the  honey  mixture.  Have  ready 
little  drum-shaped  tins  nicely  buttered. 
Fill  them  a  little  more  than  half.  Bake 
in  an  oven  of  good  heat.  Turn  from 
the  molds  as  soon  as  baked.  Boil 
together  a  cup  of  sugar  and  half  a  cup 
of  water  without  stirring  after  the 
sugar  is  melted.  The  sugar  should 
melt  before  boiling  begins.  After  the 
syrup  has  boiled  five  or  six  minutes, 
remove  from. the  fire  and  beat  with  a 


424 


THE    BOSTON    COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


spoon  until  the  syrup  thickens  and 
begins  to  look  less  transparent,  then 
at  once  dip  the  cakes  into  it,  one  by 
one,  or  with  a  spoon  spread  the  syrup 
over  the  sides  and  tops  of  the  cakes. 
These  little  cakes,  set  into  fancy  drums, 
are  often  used  at  Christmas  time  as 
souvenirs.  The  cake  may  be  baked 
in  a  loaf.    This  cake  improves  with  age. 

Entire  Wheat  Rolls  or  Biscuit 

Soften  one  or  two  cakes  of  com- 
pressed yeast  in  half  a  cup  of  boiled 
water,  cooled  to  a  lukewarm  tempera- 
ture; mix  together  thoroughly,  then 
add  to  a  cup  of  scalded  milk  and  half 
a  cup  of  boiled  water,  cooled  to  a  luke- 
warm temperature;  add  also  a  tea- 
spoonful  of  salt,  half  a  cup  of  melted 
shortening  and  half  a  cup  of  molasses; 
then  stir  in  three  cups  of  sifted  entire 
wheat  flour  and  one  cup  and  a  half  of 
white  flour.  Stir  the  whole  together 
thoroughly,  cutting  through  the  dough 
again  and  again.  It  is  too  soft  to 
knead.  Cover  and  set  aside  to  become 
very  light.     Melt    a   little    shortening; 


dip  the  fingers  into  it,  then  take  up 
bits  of  the  dough  and  shape  it  into 
rounds.  If  the  fingers  are  kept  well- 
buttered  the  dough  will  not  stick  to 
them.  Dispose  the  rounds  close  to- 
gether in  buttered  baking  pans.  When 
very  light  bake  about  fifty  minutes. 
Brush  over  the  tops  of  the  rolls  with 
thin  starch  (cornstarch  cooked  in  water) , 
dredge  with  granulated  sugar  and  re- 
turn to  the  oven  to  set  the  glaze. 

Pecan-Nut  Wafers 

Beat  two  eggs  without  separating 
the  whites  and  yolks;  beat  in  one  cup 
of  sifted  brown  sugar,  a  teaspoonful  of 
vanilla  extract  and  three  level  table- 
spoonfuls  of  flour,  sifted  with  one- 
fourth  a  teaspoonful,  each,  of  salt  and 
baking  powder.  Lastly,  beat  in  a  cup 
of  pecan-nut  meats  broken  into  small 
pieces.  Spread  the  mixture  on  but- 
tered baking  sheets,  decorate  with 
whole  nut  meats  and  bake  in  a  mod- 
erate oven,  five  or  six  minutes.  These 
may  also  be  baked  in  lady-finger  or 
very  shallow  brownie  tins.

Cookery  for  Young  Housekeepers 

By  Janet  M.  Hill 
Lesson  X 

The  Cooking  of  Vegetables 

Few  things  show  the  difference ^Detween  comfortable  and  slovenly  house- 
keeping more  quickly  than  the  dressing   of  vegetables." — Mrs.   Rorer. 


IN  cooking  vegetables  the  questions 
most  often  asked  refer  to: 
1.   The  temperature  of  the  water 
in  which  the  vegetables  are  set  to  cook. 

2.  The  temperature  of  the  water 
during  cooking,  i.e,,  Shall  the  water 
simmer,  simply  boil,  or  boil  rapidly? 

3.  The  quantity  of  water  (much  or 
little). 

4.  The  time  to  add  salt,  also  the 
quantity. 

5.  Is  soda  ever  added  to  the  water, 
and,  if  so,  for  what  purpose? 

Save  for  the  first  item,  no  rule  can  be 
made  that  will  apply  to  all  vegetables. 

Temperature    of    Water    in    which 
Vegetables  are  Set  to  Cook 

All  vegetables  are  set  to  cook  in 
water  that  is  boiling  at  the  time  they 
are  put  into  it. 

All  wilted  vegetables  should  be  re- 
vived in  cold  water  before  cooking. 

All  dried  vegetables  should  be  soaked 
in  cold  water  several  hours,  or  over 
night,  before  cooking. 

A  knowledge  of  the  composition  of  a 
vegetable  gives  the  key  to  the  way  in 
which  it  is  to  be  treated  during  cooking. 

Compounds  in  Vegetables 

Starch  is  the  dominant  principle  in 
most  vegetables,  though  protein,  sugar. 


fat,  mineral  matter  and  water  (one  or 
all)  are  combined  with  the  starch.  In 
some  vegetables  the  starch  is  largely 
in  the  form  of  cellulose  or  woody  tissue, 
but ,  in  whatever  form  it  may  be ,  starch 
must  be  thoroughly  cooked  or  it  is 
unwholesome. 

Vegetables  with  only  Slight   Trace 
of  Starch 

Vegetables,  like  lettuce,  endive,  cel- 
ery (inner  blanched  stalks),  tomatoes, 
cucumbers,  and  small,  quickly-grown 
radishes  contain  but  a  slight  trace  of 
starch.  They  are  composed  largely  of 
water  and  mineral  salts,  both  of  which 
would  be  lost  during  cooking,  unless  the 
cooking  be  done  at  a  gentle  simmer, 
and  the  water  be  retained  as  food. 
These  vegetables,  then,  might  be  ex- 
empt from  cooking,  save  for  variety. 
When  cooked,  no  more  water  should  be 
used  than  can  be  served  with  them, 
and  the  cooking  should  be  at  a  gentle 
simmer. 

Vegetables  containing  Protein,  Sugar 
and  Some  Starch 

Green  peas  and  asparagus  contain 
so  much  starch  that  cooking  is  a 
necessity,  but,  to  retain  the  sugar 
and  other  compounds,  the  cooking 
should   be   at   a   gentle   simmer,   in   a 


427 


428 


THE   BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


small  measure  of  water,  and  the  water 
should  form  a  part  of  the  finished  dish. 
The  same  is  true  of  spinach;  the  water 
that  clings  to  the  leaves  in  washing 
being  sufficient  for  the  cooking. 

Vegetables  with  Starch  as  Cellulose 

Parsnips,  salsify,  carrots  and  turnips 
contain  but  little  starch,  other  than 
that  found  in  their  cellular  structure; 
this  fiber,  like  animal  fiber,  is  hardened 
by  high  heat,  and  cooking  should  not 
be  carried  on  at  a  temperature  higher 
than  the  boiling  point  of  water.  The 
cooking  should  be  prolonged  until  the 
fiber  is  tender,  but  no  longer.  All 
vegetables  should  be  removed  from 
the  fire  as  soon  as  they  are  cooked. 

Vegetables  With  Much  Starch 

Potatoes,  breakfast  cereals,  rice, 
samp,  macaroni,  noodles  and  other 
pastes,  used  as  vegetables,  are  rich  in 
starch.  A  good  potato,  properly 
cooked,  is  mealy.  A  potato,  no  matter 
how  good  it  may  be,  cooked  in  sim- 
mering water  is  water-soaked  and 
soggy.  If  potatoes  be  cooked  in  furi- 
ously-boiling water,  the  outside  be- 
comes softened  and  washed  away, 
while  there  is  "a  bone  in  the  center." 
To  cook  in  perfection,  keep  the  water 
just  at  the  boiling  point  until  the 
process  is  finished.  The  same  is  true, 
practically ,  in  respect  to  the  cooking  of 
other  starchy  vegetable  products,  but 
in  cooking  rice  and  macaroni,  rapidly- 
boiling  water  is  necessary ,  in  order  that 
the  grains  or  pieces  be  kept  moving  and 
separate  from  each  other.  A  light, 
flaky  dish  of  rice  cannot  be  secured  in 
a  double  boiler,  where  the  water  is  of 
necessity  below  the  boiling  point. 

When  to  Use  a  Large  Quantity  of 
Water 

Some  varieties  of  onions  are  strong 
flavored;  when  such,  and  also  members 
of  the  cabbage  family,  are  to  be  cooked, 
the  use  of  a  large  quantity  of  water 
will  insure  a  more  delicate  flavor. 


Use  of  Salt  and  Soda 

Hard  water  has  a  tendency  to  harden 
cellulose  or  woody  fiber,  and  thus  keeps 
sweet  juices  or  other  valuable  com- 
pounds within  the  article  cooked;  soft 
water  acts  in  the  opposite  way.  Salt 
added  to  water  makes  it  hard,  raises 
the  boiling  point  a  little,  and  intensi- 
fies the  color  of  green  vegetables.  Soda 
softens  water  and  causes  green  vege- 
tables to  assume  a  faded  look.  As  the 
appearance  of  food  has  much  to  do 
with  our  taste  or  distaste  thereof, 
certainly,  for  aesthetic  reasons,  the  use 
of  salt  in  the  cooking  of  green  vege- 
tables would  be  a  gain.  Again,  as 
green  vegetables  contain  little  woody 
tissue,  but  often  sugar  that  we  wish 
to  retain  in  the  article,  the  cooking  of 
these  in  salted  water  would  seem  ad- 
visable. In  general,  salt  should  be 
added  to  the  water  in  which  all  vege- 
tables, except  those  containing  much 
cellulose,  are  to  be  cooked.  Potatoes 
and  onions  never  taste  just  right  unless 
the  water  in  which  they  are  cooked  be 
salted.  A  teaspoonful  of  salt  to 
each  generous  quart  of  water  will  be 
found  about  right.  Soft  water  is 
called  for  when  the  cellular  structure 
of  dried  peas,  beans  and  lentils  is  to 
be  made  tender.  If  such  water  be  not 
available,  a  teaspoonful  of  bicarbonate 
of  soda,  added  to  each  two  quarts  of 
water  in  which  the  vegetable  is  to  be 
cooked,  will  soften  the  water. 

Preparation  of  Potatoes  for  Boiling 

New  potatoes:  Scrape  off  the  skin, 
scrub  with  a  vegetable  brush,  then 
wash  thoroughly.  Old  potatoes:  Pare 
the  potatoes,  remove  the  eyes  and,  if 
they  be  large,  cut  them  in  halves, 
lengthwise ;  let  lie  in  cold  water  —  no 
salt  —  an  hour  or  longer  before  cook- 
ing. 

Boiled  Potatoes 

Add  a  teaspoonful  of  salt  to  a  gen- 
erous quart  of  boiling  water;  put  in 


COOKERY  FOR  YOUNG  HOUSEKEEPERS 


429 


the  potatoes  and  let  cook  until  tender. 
It  will  take  from  twenty  to  thirty 
minutes.  Drain  thoroughly,  sprinkle 
lightly  with  salt  and  shake  the  pan 
back  and  forth  on  the  hot  stove  lid. 
If  they  cannot  be  served  at  once,  move 
to  the  back  of  the  range  and  cover  with 
a  cloth.  Salt  sprinkled  on  the  hot, 
moist  potato,  on  account  of  its  affinity 
for  water,  tends  to  draw  it  out  and 
make  the  potato  mealy. 

Mashed  Potatoes 
Boil  the  potatoes  as  above  and  pass 
through  a  vegetable  press  into  a  hot 
saucepan.  For  about  a  pint,  or  four 
or  five  potatoes,  add  two  level  table- 
spoonfuls  of  butter,  a  little  hot  milk 
(one-fourth  a  cup)  and  half  a  tea- 
spoonful  of  salt.  Beat  with  a  per- 
forated wooden  spoon  until  very  light 
and  fluffy,  then  turn  into  a  hot  vege- 
table dish  and  serve  at  once. 

Mashed  Potato  Cakes 

Roll  mashed  potato,  left  from  a 
meal,  in  the  hands  into  balls  and 
flatten  into  cakes.  Set  to  cook  in  a 
frying  pan  containing  hot  bacon  fat 
or  dripping;  let  brown  on  one  side, 
then  turn  and  brown  the  other  side. 
The  cakes  may  be  patted  down  in  a 
little  flour  before  cooking.  For  baked 
cakes,  set  the  cakes  on  a  buttered  dish, 
put  a  small  piece  of  butter  on  the  top 
of  each  and  let  cook  in  the  oven  until 
hot  and  lightly  browned. 


Baked  Potatoes 

Scrub  the  potatoes  with  a  brush. 
Bake  in  a  hot  oven.  The  oven  should 
be  at  a  temperature  that  will  render 
potatoes  of  medium  size  soft  in  about 
forty-five  minutes.  The  skins  should  not 
be  blackened.  If  the  potatoes  are  to  be 
eaten  at  once,  take  them  from  the  oven 
to  the  dining-room.  If  there  is  to  be  a 
delay  of  five  minutes,  make  two  gashes 
in  the  top  of  each  potato,  one  at  right 
angles  to  the  other.  Gently  squeeze  the 
potato  to  let  out  the  steam,  partially 
wrap  in  a  napkin  and  take  to  the  table. 

Potatoes  Anna 
Butter  the  inside  of  two  or  three 
English  mufifin  rings  and  set  them  on 
a  baking  sheet,  also  buttered.  Fill 
the  rings  compactly  with  pared,  raw 
potatoes,  sliced  very  thin  and  dipped 
in  melted  butter,  bacon  fat,  or  drip- 
ping. Also  season  the  potatoes  with 
salt  and  pepper,  as  they  are  set  in 
place.  Put  half  a  teaspoonful  of  but- 
ter on  the  top  of  each  ring  of  potatoes. 
Let  cook  on  the  floor  of  a  hot  oven 
about  ten  minutes,  then  with  a  spat- 
ula turn  ring  and  potatoes  together, 
and  let  cook  ten  minutes  longer.  With 
the  oven  at  a  proper  temperature,  the 
potatoes  will  be  browned  on  both  sides 
and  well  cooked  throughout  in  twenty 
minutes.  Remove  the  potatoes  and 
rings  (with  a  spatula  or  broad  knife)  to 
the  serving  dish,  then  discard  the  rings. 


Anonymous 


A  small  ship  launched  upon  an  unknown  sea, 
A  small  seed  planted  from  an  unknown  tree, — 
Such  is  this  strange  New  Year  to  you  and  me. 

Whither  the  vessel  goeth, 

And  how  the  seed  upgroweth, 

God  only  knoweth. 
But  sail  the  ship  and  plant  the  seed: 
What's  done  in  faith  is  done  in  deed. 


Ways  of  Entertaining 

A  Party  for  Easter  Vacation 
By  Evelyn  Prince  Cahoon 


AT    Easter  the   boy   and    the   girl 

l\  come  home  from  school  for  a 
JL  JL  week  of  fun  with  the  other  boys 
and  girls,  a  week  of  ''telling  things"  to 
the  dear  home  folks;  and  a  good  taste 
of  home  cooking  counts  not  least  among 
the  minor  joys  of  the  spring  vacation. 

Give  the  home-loving  boy  a  party 
to  tell  of,  when  he  returns  to  school. 
Include  both  girls  and  boys  in  the 
invitation  list ,  and  try.  to  avoid  mak- 
ing enemies  for  the  young  person, 
by  either  including  every  one  with 
whom  your  boy  has  any  social  affilia- 
tions at  all,  or  else  confining  the  list  to 
only  such  as  are  known  to  be  imme- 
diate friends.  Remember  the  young 
folks  are  going  to  have  months  in 
which  to  nurse  slights  before  your  boy 
comes  back  again  to  set  the  matter 
right  by  another  party. 

Since  the  time  is  Easter,  it  is  inter- 
esting to  make  this  distinctly  an 
Easter  party.  Yet  there  was  never 
a  boy  who  didn't  want  his  home 
decorated  in  his  school  colors,  so  I 
suggest  that  the  entrance  hall  be  quite 
elaborately  decorated  in  the  school 
colors,  also  that  the  table  from  which 
the  refreshments  are  served  be  deli- 
cately treated  in  the  same  colors,  but 
that  the  other  rooms  be  beautified 
only  with  palms,  ferns  and  a  few 
flowers;  for  class  colors  are  seldom 
becoming  combinations  when  made  a 
background  for  human  faces  and  for 
young  ladies'  party  gowns,  while  flow- 
ers and  greenery  always  are. 

I  have  in  mind  a  party  for  a  young 
man  in  his  spruce  cadet  uniform,  just 
home  from  a  military  academy.  The 
hall  was  draped  with  the  national 
colors,  because  along  with  his  fine 
bearing  our  young  man  had  acquired 


a  fine  reverence  for  that  same  red, 
white  and  blue. 

Unfortunately  the  hall  was  of  the 
old-fashioned,  long,  narrow  variety, 
but  the  colors  were  so  arranged  that 
the  stripes  of  the  flags  ran  horizontally, 
which  made  the  room  appear  much 
wider  than  it  really  was.  On  the  walls 
were  hung  such  curios,  in  the  shape  of 
army  guns,  an  uncle's  sabre  and  so  on, 
as  the  family  possessed.  The  stair- 
rail  was  draped  beautifully  with  the 
red,  white  and  blue,  —  even  the  floor 
was  covered  with  red  Navajo  rugs,  — 
and  altogether  it  impressed  one,  on 
entering,  as  being  one  of  the  rooms  in 
an  officer's  quarters,  ready  for  a  ball, 
at  some  army  post. 

A  sister  of  the  host  was  ready  at  the 
door  to  meet  the  guests,  while  the 
young  host  assisted  his  mother  in  re- 
ceiving them  in  the  parlor  and  in 
seeing  that  each  guest  was  made 
happy. 

A  tiny  midget  of  a  boy,  cousin  of 
the  host,  stood  at  the  entrance,  dressed 
in  a  cadet  uniform,  the  exact  minia- 
ture of  the  big  cousin's,  and  fitting 
his  Lilliputian  body  like  a  glove.  He 
held  a  small  drum,  and  as  each  guest 
approached  the  house  this  tiny  mili- 
tary boy  beat  a  rat-tat-tat  announce- 
ment on  his  drum. 

At  once  the  sister,  before  mentioned, 
greeted  the  new-comer  and  directed  her 
or  him  to  the  room  where  the  wraps 
were  to  be  laid  aside. 

The  guest,  having  passed  through 
the  hall  and  entered  the  parlor,  left 
the  military  features  all  behind,  for 
the  drawing-room  was  arranged  as  for 
an  ordinary  party.  The  library  carpet 
was  spread  with  a  canvas  that  those 
so  inclined  might  dance ;  the  piano  had 


430 


A  PARTY  FOR  EASTER  VACATION 


431 


been  moved  to  a  position  just  outside 
that  room,  and  the  other  musicians, 
the  vioHnist  and  celhst,  were  placed 
beside  it.  In  the  drawing-room  and 
in  a  Httle  den,  opening  from  it,  a 
number  of  games  had  been  arranged 
for. 

But  the  invitations  had  read,  "  Please 
wear  or  bring  something  suggestive  of 
a  particular  hero  in  United  States 
history"  (on  the  girls'  invitations,  of 
course,  the  word  "heroine"  was  used), 
and  so  fully  three-quarters  of  an  hour 
was  pleasantly  spent  in  guessing  as  to 
the  hero  or  heroine  represented  by 
each  comer,  and  many  a  hearty  laugh 
at  the  symbols  used  effectually  broke 
the  ice  for  the  timid  ones. 

Each  was  provided  with  paper  and 
pencil,  and  was  asked  to  write  the 
names  of  the  guests  with  a  guess  as 
to  the  character  represented  by 
each. 

As  each  made  a  decision  and  filled 
out  the  names  of  the  characters  rep- 
resented, he  handed  his  paper  to  some 
one  appointed,  and  a  committee  went 
over  the  lists  to  ascertain  who  guessed 
rightly  in  the  largest  number  of  cases, 
also  a  vote  was  taken  as  to  the  best 
representation.  Two  very  nice  statu- 
ettes of  soldier  heroes  were  given,  as 
prizes  to  these  two  winners. 

A  laugh  was  raised,  when  a  young 
man  entered  carrying  a  cold  boiled 
potato  and  a  bag  of  salt.  He  was 
undoubtedly  Francis  Marion,  the  party 
thought. 

One  wore  a  small,  light-colored  tin 
horse  pinned  to  his  coat. 

Another,  w4th  a  jet  black  horse 
pinned  to  his  coat,  was  thought  to  be 
Sheridan. 

One,  with  a  necktie  inscribed  in  out- 
line stitch  "We  have  met  the  enemy 
and  they  are  ours,"  was  a  good  deal 
laughed  at,  but  was  voted  to  be  Com- 
modore Perry. 

One  boy  w^as  laughed  at  for  his 
coon-skin  cap,  which  he  persisted  in 
wearing,  with  its  long  fur  tail  hanging 


down  his  neck  at  the  back,  until  the 
winner  was  declared.  He  was  un- 
doubtedly Daniel  Boone. 

A  little  miss  who  appeared  with 
thimble  on  finger  and  an  unfinished, 
tiny  silk  flag,  at  which  she  sewed  in- 
dustriously for  a  time,  was,  of  course, 
Betty  Ross. 

Another,  who  made  everyone  laugh 
by  carrying  gravely  around  an  old 
ramrod  and  a  milk  pitcher,  without 
once  laughing,  although  all  the  others 
were  convulsed,  was  finalty  found  to 
be  Molly  Pitcher. 

William  Penn  was  there  and  Peter 
Stuyvesant,  though  he  had  to  carry 
his  peg-leg  in  his  hand  in  miniature, 
and  Dolly  Madison,  with  her  basket  of 
wet,  newly- washed  (doll's)  clothes, 
ready  to  hang  in  the  east  room  of  the 
White  House. 

Barbara  Freitche  was  there,  and 
Martha  Washington,  with  her  white 
pompadour,  and  so  on. 

In  the  dining-room  the  thought  was 
directed  to  Easter.  For  convenience 
in  serving,  one  large  table  and  four 
small  ones  were  used.  In  each  case 
the  table  took  the  form  of  an  outspread 
butterfly  (an  Easter  emblem).  This 
was  done  by  having  a  carpenter  make 
false  tops  to  slide  over  the  tops  of  the 
family  dining-table,  and  four  stands. 
This  false  top  in  outline  resembled  a 
butterfly  with  outspread  wings,  as  we 
see  them  when  mounted  in  collections. 
In  the  middle  of  each  table  was  an 
arrangement  of  bright  red  carnations; 
on  the  smaller  ones,  simple  groups  of 
the  flowers  in  slender,  cut-glass  vases, 
but  on  the  large  one,  quite  an  elaborate 
arrangement  in  a  low,  large  fern- 
dish. 

Apparently  rising  from  each  table 
and  soaring  toward  the  ceiling  was  a 
perfect  cloud  of  butterflies,  with  no 
visible  means  of  suspension.  These 
butterflies  were  really  crudely  made, 
being  cut  from  white  crepe  paper,  with 
large  round  and  oval  spots  painted  on 
the  wings  with  silver  and  other  deli- 


432 


THE   BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


cate  colors.  Each  butterfly  was  then 
fastened  to  the  lower  end  of  a  delicate 
wire,  such  as  is  used  for  the  stamens 
of  artificial  flowers.  These  wires  were 
all  joined  together  at  a  point  about 
four  feet  above  the  table  and  carried 
to  the  ceiling,  and  the  different  butter- 
flies were  drawn  out  into  position,  so  as 
to  look  as  if  poised  for  flight,  vibrating 
with  every  little  current  of  air;  per- 
haps two  dozen  were  above  each  table. 
From  some  of  them  depended  dainty, 
narrow,  white  ribbons  (of  the  lightest 
texture,  so  as  not  to  be  weighty  for 
the  butterfly) ,  which  carried  one  ribbon 
to  each  plate,  and  to  which  were 
affixed  the  name-cards,  which  also  were 
butterflies,  but  very  finely  painted 
ones,  on  water-color  paper,  in  delicate 
shades  of  pale  green,  yellow,  blue  and 
pink. 

This  hostess  had  a  fancy  for  serving 
food  that  was  red  and  blue  —  and 
white,  of  course,  in  color.  But  it 
seems  to  me  in  better  taste  to  have, 


as  some  one  else  did  for  an  Easter 
lunch,  the  serving  dishes  bordered 
with  violets,  or  if  the  really  bluish 
violets  cannot  be  obtained,  then  with 
blue  hyacinths,  which  at  this  season 
can  always  be  had. 

So  let  slices  of  white  cake  alternate 
with  those  of  cake,  colored  red  on 
plates  bordered  with  blue  hyacinths. 
The  cream,  in  the  case  mentioned,  was 
of  red  and  white  bricks,  with  a  can- 
died violet  or  two  laid  on  each  serving. 
Nut  sandwiches  were  served  and  bril- 
liant red  fruit  punch. 

The  host  sat  at  the  larger  table  and 
some  member  of  the  family  sat  at 
each  of  the  smaller  ones,  with  three 
others  who  could  be  counted  on  to 
have  a  good  time  together. 

At  parting  the  host  presented  each 
guest  "with  his  portrait,"  in  a  very 
dainty,  little,  fancy,  water-colored  box, 
as  a  souvenir.  On  opening  the  boxes, 
each  guest  found  a  little  tin  soldier 
about  three  inches  tall. 


A  GERMAN  "SMALL  AND  EARLY"

when  the  supper  march  is  sung  in 
chorus ;  arrived  at  the  table  the  baskets 
are  emptied  and  handed  back  to  the 
children;  the  eggs  are  all  put  into  the 
big  bowl  at  Bunny's  feet.  Refresh- 
ments consist  of  cups  of  cocoa  and 
glasses  of  milk,  thin  slices  of  buttered 
Easter  bread,  eggs  —  but  in  great 
moderation,  they  being  nightmare  food 
—  from  Bunny's  store,  homemade  cake, 
and  ice  cream  in  the  shape  of  Easter 
eggs.  At  the  end  of  the  collation  the 
surprise-eggs  are  all  broken  at  a  signal 
and  the  uproarious  delight  at  finding 
a  tiny  dog,  cat,  mouse,  camel,  doll, 
flat-iron,  padlock,  bedstead,  donkey, 
lantern  and  mug  (all  procurable  at 
a    cent    each)    makes    a    fitting    close 


to  the  five -o'clock  tea,  or  juvenile 
Kaflee-Klatsch, — which  shall  it  be? 
Gretchen  starts  the  youngsters  in  some 
competition  game  appropriate  to  the 
day,  egg  rolling,  egg  chipping,  or  egg 
football,  with  a  prize  (the  egg  candy 
box  or  the  papier-mache  bunny)  to 
strive  for,  and  the  happy  afternoon 
ends  with  an  impromptu  Virginia  reel 
with  Gretchen  at  the  piano.  If  any 
mother  of  children  under  fourteen  feels 
it  necessary  to  have  a  child's  party,  let 
her  try  a  simple,  old-fashioned,  whole- 
some entertainment  of  this  sort  for  her 
olive  branches  and  their  young  friends, 
and  she  will  find  it  to  be  a  welcome 
change  from  the  more  ambitious  and 
extravagant  juvenile  hop. 


Contributions  to  this  department  will  be  gladly  received, 
paid  for  at  reasonable  rates.

One  Woman's  Work 

BEING  interested  in  ways  and  means 
for  the  self-support  of  women, 
brought  up  to  expect  that  any  such 
effort  would  not  fall  to  their  lot,  I 
should  like  to  relate  how  one  woman, 
so  situated,  succeeded. 

When  financial  losses   came  to  my 

friend  Mrs. ,  she  found  herself  with 

three  young  children  and  the  rem- 
nants of  an  income  that,  when  gathered 
together,  promised  only  a  small  part 
towards  the  maintenance  of  her  little 
family.  She  could  think  of  no  one 
thing  she  could  do  really  well,  yet 
something  had  to  be  done  at  once,  to 
make  both  ends  meet,  and  that  some- 
thing must  be  done  at  home,  so  she 
could  care  for  her  children. 

After  many  hours  of  anxious  thought, 
a  possible  plan  came  into  her  mind. 

That  very  afternoon  she  visited 
several  prominent  physicians  and  told 
them  that  she  would  like  orders  from 
their  patients  for  homemade  delica- 
cies ;  she  would  hold  herself  in  readiness 
to  prepare  expressed  beef  juice,  broths 
and  meat  jellies  of  all  kinds,  to  be 
delivered  at  hotels,  or  at  the  homes  of 
the  patients.  The  doctors  promised 
to  give  the  matter  their  attention, 
provided  the  materials  used  were 
strictly  fresh,  and  that  no  "cold 
storage"  poultry  should  be  used. 

Of  course  my  friend  had  discourage- 
ments —  orders  came  in  slowly ,  but 
within  two  years  she  built  up  a  nice 
little  business;  by  carefully  attending 
to  all  details  and  following  the  doctors' 


orders  implicitly,  and  selecting  all  the 
meat  and  poultry  herself,  she  steadily 
increased  her  list  of  customers.  She 
bought  the  best  materials  the  market 
afforded  and  set  her  prices  accordingly. 
She  put  up  her  broths  and  jellies  in 
neat  and  attractive  jars. 

Each  order  was  freshly  prepared,  as 
she  felt  that  new  customers  might  have 
doubts  as  to  the  absolute  freshness  of 
the  article,  were  the  delicacies  kept  on 
hand.  Finding  it  unsatisfactory  to 
send  out  her  hotel  orders  by  messen- 
gers, she  personally  delivered  the  first 
order  of  a  new  customer  at  the  door  of 
the  patient's  room;  in  this  way  she 
could  see  the  trained  nurse  or  person 
in  charge  and  more  satisfactorily  make 
arrangements  for  further  orders.  Her 
work  gave  perfect  satisfaction,  and  she 
could  command  the  highest  prices. 

It  was  hard  work  certainly,  but  she 
was  able  to  keep  a  home  for  her  little 
girls,  and  at  the  same  time  feel  that 
she  was  not  only  earning  her  living, 
but  that  her  efforts  were  appreciated 
by  a  large  circle  of  customers,  many  of 
whom  felt  that  to  her  conscientious 
work  was  due  the  recovery  of  some 
loved  one. 


M.   A.   w. 


M^ 


Answer  to  Inquiry 

ISS  A.  M.  F.,  Dear  Madam:  You 
have  asked  me  to  solve  a  some- 
what difficult  problem,  when  you  wish 
me  to  decide  whether  your  picture  called 
"Queen  of  the  Roses"  is  worthy  to  be 
placed  with  the  other  works  of  art. 
which  you  mention. 


433 


434 


THE   BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


There  are  so  many  things  involved, 
besides  the  simple  question  itself,  that 
the  situation  is  quite  complicated. 
But  I  will  endeavor  to  give  a  few 
suggestions  that  may  help  you.  From 
your  description  of  the  picture  I  should 
say  that  it  was  by  a  modern  artist 
and  that  your  copy  is  painted,  either 
in  water-colors,  or  oils,  and  is  very 
beautiful.  Now,  if  it  is  painted  well 
and  by  a  good  artist,  I  do  not  see  why 
it  is  not  worthy  to  be  placed  with 
the  others  on  your  list. 

One  cannot,  always,  tell  from  the 
name  of  a  picture  who  the  artist  may 
be,  because  there  are  hundreds  of 
artists;  and,  if  you  have  ever  gone 
through  the  European  galleries,  you 
know  that  there  are  many  repetitions 
of  the  same  subject  by  the  different 
masters. 

Greuze  has  painted  many  lovely 
pictures  of  young  girls  with  decollete 
gowns  and  carrying  roses,  but  I  do 
not  recall  one  with  a  lingerie  hat. 
However,  if  your  copy  were  a  Greuze, 
it  would,  certainly  be  worthy,  as  far 
as  subject  is  concerned,  to  go  with 
"Madame  Le  Brun  and  her  daughter." 

I  do  not  know  that  I  can  help  you 
much  without  more  definite  knowledge 
of  your  picture,  but  it  is  the  same  in 
art  as  it  is  in  music,  you  must  think  of 
the  harmony  of  the  whole ;  for  a  picture 
that  is  good  in  itself  may  be  placed  in 
a  very  .inappropriate  position,  and,  if 
such  a  picture  clashes  with  its  sur- 
roundings, in  either  subject,  color,  size, 
or  in  any  other  way,  the  best  thing  to 
do  is  to  remove  it;  even  if  you  have 
to  hang  it  in  the  attic !  An  inexpensive 
print,  well  placed,  is  far  better  than  a 
costly  painting  that  is  unadapted  to 
its  position. 

A  good  rule  to  follow  is  to  group 
photographs  together,  water-colors  to- 
gether, and  oil  paintings  by  themselves: 
it  is  still  better  to  have  oil  paint- 
ings in  one  room,  photographs  in  an- 
other, and  water-colors  in  still  another. 
Thus   arranged,    the   effect   is   always 


good,  and  if,  at  the  same  time,  you 
can  have  the  subjects  of  your  pictures 
harmonize  with  their  environment,  you 
will  be  a  very  fortunate  person  —  to 
say  nothing  of  having  a  scheme  of 
decoration  that  would  be  very  at- 
tractive. 

In  the  living-room  which  is  less 
formal  than  the  other  rooms  of  a 
house,  more  freedom  can  be  used,  and, 
if  your  group  of  pictures  is  in  the 
living-room,  I  should  say  that,  being  of 
a  miscellaneous  character,  that  room 
would  be  the  appropriate  place  for 
them. 

As  I  said  in  the  first  place,  it  is  quite 
difficult  to  decide  the  matter  definitely, 
there  are  so  many  things  to  think 
about,  but  I  hope  the  few  suggestions 
offered  may  help  you  to  come  to  the 
right  decision  about  your  picture,  and 
I  wish  you  good  luck  in  your  efforts. 
Sincerely  yours, 

Margaret  L.  Sears. 


ON  my  kitchen  sink  I  keep  a 
small,  stiff  scrubbing  brush,  that 
I  bought  at  the  five-and-ten  cent 
store,  and  it  is  the  handiest  thing 
to  clean  all  vegetables,  like  radishes, 
turnips,  potatoes,  beets  and  carrots. 
I  have  even  used  it  on  coarse  stalks  of 
celery,  when  the  dirt  between  the 
fibers  simply  would  not  wash  out. 
But  last  summer  I  found  still  another 
use  for  it  —  it  will,  if  used  dry,  take 
the  silks  off  green  corn,  thus  trans- 
forming a  tedious  task  into  a  light  one. 


When  peeling  tomatoes  for  salad, 
and  not  wishing  to  scald  them,  first 
rub  the  tomato  'all  over  firmly  with  the 
dull,  back  edge  of  the  knife.  The  skin 
may  then  be  readily  removed. 


A  "company"  dessert  that  is  uni- 
versally liked  with  us  is  made  this 
way:  To  serve  six,  whip  very  stiff  and 
sweeten  a  half -pint  of  cream,  and  set 
away  to  chill.  Cut  up  fine  a  small 
banana,   a   small   orange,   and   twelve 


HOME  IDEAS  AND  ECONOMIES 


435 


marshmallows.  Add  two  tablespoon- 
fuls  of  preserved  pineapple,  and  a  few 
broken  nut  meats.  Just  before  serv- 
ing beat  the  fruit  through  the  cream, 
and  put  in  sherbet  glasses,  with  a 
cherry  on  top.  This  makes  a  very 
agreeable  change  from  ice-cream  for  an 
afternoon  tea  or  "At  Home."  It  also 
has  the  merit  of  being  something  the 
most  inexperienced  cook  cannot  fail  on. 


A  delicious  peach  pie  that  an  old 
Pennsylvania  Aunt  of  mine  makes  has 
an  under  crust  of  regular  pie  dough, 
then  halves  of  fresh  or  canned  peaches 
laid  all  over  it,  a  little  of  their  juice 
poured  in,  and  then  the  pie  is  filled 
up  with  sour  cream,  and  sugar  is 
sprinkled  in  generously.  Tart  strips 
are  laid  across  the  face;  it  is  baked 
slowly  and  is  simply  delicious. 

J.   D.   u. 


When  in  search  of  paper  to  use  in 
the  bottom  of  bureau  drawers,  try  a 
roll  of  pure  white  moire  ceiling  paper, 
which  can  be  had  at  ten  cents  a  roll 
and  is  the  width  of  the  average  bureau 
drawer. 

Not  long  ago  I  banished  from  the 
kitchen  wardrobe  all  the  gingham 
aprons  and  replaced  them  with  a  smaller 
number  of  oilcloth  aprons,  long- skirted, 
bibbed,  and  pocketed.  No  form  of 
work  can  spoil  or  stain  them;  they  call 
for  no  tedious  washing  or  ironing,  but 
they  can  be  cleaned  like  a  slate  with  a 
moist  cloth. 

'  H.    L.    p. 


Suggestions  for  the  Housekeeper 

No  matter  how  long  folks  live,  or 
how  long  they  have  kept  house,  there 
seems  always  to  be  something  of  value 
to  learn,  if  they  will,  and  a  few  helpful 
suggestions  may  be  appreciated  by  some. 
Among  them  may  be  mentioned: 

Every  two  weeks,  fill  the  tea  and 
coffee  pots  with  cold  water,  and  drop 
in  a  piece  of  washing  soda  the  size  of 


a  walnut;  bring  slowly  to  the  boiling 
point,  and  let  simmer  half  an  hour, 
then  wash  and  drain  them,  and  they 
will  look  almost  like  new  inside,  and 
the  strong  odors  will  be  gone. 


When  making  paste  in  large  quanti- 
ties, if  washing  soda  be  added,  it  will 
stick  better,  especially  if  it  is  to  be 
used  for  wall  papering. 


When  boiling  fish,  or  crabs,  add  a 
large  sprig  of  parsley,  a  small  white 
onion,  and  half  a  cup  of  vinegar  to  the 
water;  it  improves  the  flavor  greatly. 


Try  a  small  green  cucumber,  a 
pinch  of  ground  cinnamon  and  a 
pinch  of  ground  cloves  as  seasoning 
in  the  tomato  soup. 


Sweet  pickles,  olives,  capers  and 
celery,  chopped  very  fine,  make  an  ex- 
cellent salad  seasoning. 


Meat  without  fat  is  usually  tough; 
mutton  fat  is  almost  white;  veal  and 
pork  the  same;  while  beef  fat  must  be 
firm  and  of  a  pale  yellow  shade. 


Chickens  with  yellow  skin  and  feet 
make  the  richest  stews. 


Ham  bones  have  been  used  in  in- 
numerable ways,  and  the  next  time 
you  have  one  on  hand  try  boiling  it 
with  macaroni;  it  makes  a  very 
pleasant  change;  the  bone  and  fat  are 
simply  put  in  the  boiling  water  and 
cooked  (with  the  macaroni)  three- 
fourths  of  an  hour,  and  allowed  to 
cool  (in  the  water) ;  then  drain  and 
finish  with  cheese  and  milk,  or  tomato 
sauce,  as  preferred. 


Horseradish  is  delicious  and  health- 
ful, but  most  people  are  affected  dis- 
agreeably by  the  fumes  while  grating 
it;  the  unpleasantness  can  be  almost 
entirely  overcome  by  putting  it  through 
the  meat  grjnder.  s.h.r. 


A  Glimpse  of  Southern  Life 

By  Julia  Davis  Chandler 


A  LTHOUGH  many  books  of  lo- 
l\  calities,  like  "  Cape  Cod  Folks," 
JL  A-  "Tilly,  the  Mennonite  Maid," 
"The  Tallahassee  Girl,"  and  others, 
have  described  local  customs  and  pe- 
culiar dialects,  perhaps  none  have 
dwelt  more  upon  food  than  Owen 
Wister  has  done,  in  "Lady  Baltimore." 
Though  the  book  appeared  some  time 
ago,  Charleston  still  continues  to  re- 
ceive an  influx  of  interested  visitors 
who  are  not  of  the  "Replacer  type," 
while  the  Lady  Baltimore  cake  is  fre- 
quently ordered  by  express  from  the 
Woman's  Exchange,  where  the  story 
is  centred. 

The  cake  is  said  to  have  received  its 
name  because  a  lady  in  Baltimore 
sent  the  recipe,  or  contributed  some  of 
the  cake.  It  became  known  as  "that 
Baltimore  lady's  cake."  In  time,  the 
word,  lady,  preceded  the  Baltimore, and 
in  due  time  became  capitalized;  as  the 
cake  proved  "capital,"  no  one  minds 
the  title,  or  appears  to  have  sought 
out  the  originator. 

A  letter  from  a  Northern  woman, 
sojourning  for  health  and  pleasure  in 
the  vicinity  of  Charleston,  says: 
"Of  course  we  went  not  only  to  the 
Huguenot  Church,  but  to  the  Ex- 
change, and  enjoyed  some  of  the 
cake.  It  is  served  very  fresh,  so  fresh 
it  will  fall  all  to  pieces,  and  the  filling 
contains  both  fruit  and  nuts.  We 
have  it  here  for  dessert ,  sometimes  with 
wine  jelly  and  syllabub.  I  never  had 
better  partridge  than  when  I  dined  at 
a  private  house  in  Charleston.  We  do 
not  have  distinctively  Southern  dishes 
here  on  this  fine  old  place,  though 
managed  by  Southern  ladies  of  the  old 
regime,  who  receive  us  and  a  few  others 
as  'paying  guests.'  Did  you  ever  hear 
deviled  crabs  called  *  crabs  in  the  back '  ? 
'Hopping  John'  is  a  Southern  dish  of 


rice  and  beans,  cooked  together  as  a 
vegetable.  Of  course,  we  have  plenty 
of  different  kinds  of  corn  bread,  made 
thin  and  with  a  nice  brown  crust,  — 
hominy,  too,  and  sweet  potato  pone. 
This  sweet  potato  pone  is  made  by 
grating  raw  potatoes  into  a  baking 
dish  and  adding  brown  sugar,  molasses, 
ginger,  eggs  and  a  little  butter.  The 
colored  cooks  here  make  excellent  mo- 
lasses cake,  flavored  with  lemon  peel 
and  peanuts,  chopped  and  mixed  in. 
Dried  orange  peel,  powdered  fine,  is 
more  commonly  used  throughout  the 
South  than  lemon  in  such  cakes. 

' '  One  day  we  had  wild  turkey  stuffed 
with  chopped  peanuts.  Another  meat 
is  leg  of  fresh  pork,  soaked  in  brine  for 
about  ten  days,  which  is  very  good. 

"We  all  feel  we  are  adding  years 
to  our  earthly  existence  and,  alas!  — 
pounds  to  our  weight.  We  stroll  under 
the  great,  green  live  oaks  and  among 
large  japonica  bushes,  and  often  walk  a 
mile  to  the  fine  hotel  or  postoffice,  play 
tennis  or  golf,  and  sit  in  the  sun;  we 
read,  or  sew,  and  then  it  is  time  to  go 
in  and  eat  again.  In  the  evenings  and 
on  cold  days  we  sit  by  the  blazing 
wood  fires,  for  furnaces  are  not  in  use, 
and  we  are  glad  to  escape  over-heated, 
shut-up  houses  for  this  out-of-door  ex- 
istence; the  invalid  of  the  party  even 
has  a  cot  in  the  garden,  for  reclining 
there  as  much  as  possible. 

"The  fashion  of  collecting  old  recipes 
and  making  the  most  of  the  old  South- 
em  cuisine  has  started  here,  and  recipes 
are  being  collected  for  a  little  book  to 
be  sold  for  the  benefit  of  the  Civic 
League.  So,  please  send  South  again 
any  recipes  you  have  collected  in  former 
years  from  old  Southern  sources." 

In  response  to  this  appeal  a  Northern 
woman,  who  had  found  real  Southern 
hospitality  in  time  of  homelessness  and

reverses,  sent  to  them  recipes  for  many  North    and    South    before    the    '60's, 

a    dish    of    Southern    origin  —  tomato  as  they   now   are ,   and    had    the   two
