Aboard  for  Shut-eye  Town 

By  Mrs.  Charles  Norman 

Who  is  this  climbing  on  my  lap  and  saying,  "Story!  Story! "  ? 

Why,  't  is  a  tiny,  little  girl,  with  hair  a  golden  glory! 

She  spoils  my  collar,  blinds  my  eyes  and  plays  "chin-chopper-chin," 

And  before  I've  thought  a  single  thought,  she  cries,  "Begin!  Begin!'! 

Well,  settle  down,  my  precious  one,  be  very  good  and  still; 

I  '11  surrender,  as  I  always  do,  to  your  imperious  will. 

Once  I  heard  a  pretty  story  of  a  place  called  Shut-eye  Town, 
Where  children,  of  all  ages,  spend  their  nights  the  whole  year  roiind. 
Now  listen  sharp  to  every  word,  for  you  really  ought  to  know  — 
If  you  should  wish  to  take  a  trip  —  just  where  't  was  best  to  go. 
If  you  searched  the  wide  world  over,  in  all  sorts  of  homes  and  places, 
You'd  not  find  so  many  children  with  sweet,  contented  faces. 

For  no  matter  who  it  is,  who  goes  to  visit  Shut-eye  Town, 

He  picks  up  smiles  along  the  way,  and  drops  his  ugly  frown. 

He  may  be  so  very  weary,  but  there  his  rest  is  sweet ; 

Something  cures  all  bumps  and  bruises,  sightless  eyes  and  crippled  feet. 

Even  hungry  little  beggars  all  their  sorrows  soon  forget, 

And  there 's  not  a  child  about  the  place  who  likes  to  tease  or  fret. 

They  say,  the  thing  that  cures  them  is  a  blessed  kiss  that 's  given 
Upon  each  little  forehead,  by  an  angel,  fresh  from  heaven ; 
And  then,  upon  her  snow-white  wings,  the  angel  goes  away 
And  fairies  come  to  take  her  place,  until  the  dawn  of  day. 
They  fill  the  air  with  music,  sweet,  to  capture  and  surprise, 
And  as  they  sing  they  dance  about  upon  your  very  eyes ; 
And  all  the  while  the  moonbeams,  fair,  spin  webs  of  silver  threads, 
And  splendid  stars  shine  softly  down,  on  lovely  lily  beds. 

You  want  to  go  this  minute?     Well,  the  train's  entirely  ready; 
I  hear  the  old  conductor  now;  his  tones  are  strong  and  steady. 
Oh,  hurrah!     It  is  the  sandman,  who  takes  you  to  the  place! 
You  know  his  silken,  silvery  hair;  you  love  his  smiling  face. 
He's  calling  to  you!     Listen,  child,  I  know  that  you  can  hear! 
His  voice,  so  full  and  deep  and  kind,  comes  plainly  to  my  ear: 

"All  aboard  for  Shut-eye  Town!     Wear  your  long,  white  muslin  gown! 
Kiss  your  Daddy,  kiss  your  mother,  kiss  your  sister  and  your  brother! 
Do  it  quick!     Don't  be  too  late,  for  you  know  the  train  can't  wait. 
Come,  dear  children,  near  and  far,  get  aboard  the  sleeping  car! " 


THIS  department  is  for  the  benefit  and  free  use  of  our  subscribers.  Questions  relating  to 
recipes,  and  those  pertaining  to  culinary  science  and  domestic  economics  in  general, 
will  be  cheerfully  answered  by  the  editor.  For  menus  remit  $1.00.  Communications 
for  this  department  must  reach  us  before  the  first  of  the  month  preceding  that  in  which  the 
answers  are  expected  to  appear.  In  letters  requesting  answer  by  mail,  please  enclose  postage 
stamps.  Address  queries  to  Janet  M.  Hill,  Editor  Boston  Cooking-School  Magazine, 
372  Boylston  Street,  Boston,  Mass. 


Query  1334.  —  M.  R.  S.:  "Recipe  for 
Apricot  Tarts,  which  was  given  in  one  of 
the  magazines  last  year." 

Apricot  Tartlets 

Cover  six  fluted  "patty-pans"  with 
pastry.  Beat  one-fourth  a  cup  of 
butter  to  a  cream;  gradually  beat  in 
one-fourth  a  cup  of  sugar  and  the 
grated  rind  of  an  orange  or  lemon; 
add  the  yolks  of  two  eggs,  beating  in 
one  and  then  the  other;  then  beat  in 
one  tablespoonful  of  syrup  from  a  can 
of  apricots,  also  one-third  a  cup  of 
sifted  flour,  sifted  again  with  a  scant 
fourth  a  teaspoonful  of  baking-powder. 
Put  the  mixture  into  the  tins,  lined 
with  the  pastry,  and  bake  about  twenty 
minutes,  or  until  the  filling  is  well 
rounded  and  of  good  color.  If  the 
cake  mixture  falls,  on  removal  from 
the  oven,  more  flour  or  more  baking 
was  needed.  Put  half  a  canned  apri- 
cot, with  skin  removed  (half  a  brandied 
peach  may  be  substituted) ,  on  the  top 
of  each,  cover  with  meringue,  and 
return  to  the  oven  for  ten  minutes,  or 
until  the  meringue  is  cooked  and 
lightly  browned.  Serve  hot  or  cold 
for  dessert. 

Meringue  for  Tartlets 

Beat  the  whites  of  two  eggs  until 
dry,  then  gradually  beat  in  two  level 
tablespoonfuls   of    sugar   and,    finally, 


fold  in  two  tablespoonfuls  of  sugar 
and  half  a  teaspoonful  of  vanilla  ex- 
tract. 


Query  1335.  —  Mrs.  E.  T.  S.,  Norris- 
town,  Pa.:  "What  shape  and  size  of  moulds 
can  be  used  to  the  best  advantage  in  mold- 
ing desserts  for  a  family  of  two  ? ' ' 

Moulds  for  Family  of  Two 

The  individual  aluminum  moulds 
given  by  the  publishers  of  this  magazine 
as  premiums  for  new  subscriptions,  and 
similar  moulds  shown  in  our  advertis- 
ing pages,  are  of  convenient  size  for 
a  family  of  two.  Charlotte  russe 
moulds,  oval  in  shape,  holding  half- 
pint  and  pint,  are  also  serviceable. 


Query      1336.  —  Mrs. ,      Calumet, 

Mich.:  "Give  method  of  making  a  creamy, 
evenly-cooked  Omelet;  recipes  for  cooking 
eggs  with  mushrooms,  etc.  Recipe  for 
French  pancakes;  doughnut  recipe,  the 
one  given  not  long  ago  in  the  magazine 
seems  too  stiff  and  hard;  White  Muffins 
without  yeast,  and  light  Graham  Gems; 
Lobster  Cocktail. 

Creamy,  Evenly-cooked  Omelet 
To  cook  an  omelet  properly  the  pan 
must  be  of  just  the  right  temperature 
when  the  eggs  are  poured  into  it.  The 
pan  should  not  be  hot  enough  to  burn 
the  butter  put  into  it,  but  should  have 
been  gradually  heated  throughout. 
Then,  when  the  mixture  touches  the 


438 


QUERIES  AND  ANSWERS 


439 


pan,  it  coagulates  to  some  extent  at 
once  and,  when  the  pan  is  shaken, 
the  cooked  egg  sHps  easily  on  the  pan 
and,  when  the  pan  is  tipped,  the  un- 
cooked portion  runs  down  onto  the 
bottom  of  the  pan.  If  the  pan  be 
properly  heated,  a  fork  is  not  needed; 
a  spatula,  to  separate  the  egg  from  the 
sides  of  the  pan,  may  be  required 
occasionally. 

Omelet,  with  Brown  Mushroom 
Sauce 

Remove  the  stems  and  peel  the  caps 
of  six  or  eight  mushrooms;  cook  the 
caps,  broken  in  pieces,  in  two  table- 
spoonfuls  of  melted  butter,  three  or 
four  minutes,  then  drain  off  the  butter 
into  a  small  saucepan;  add  a  cup  and 
a  fourth  of  rich,  well-flavored  brown 
stock  to  the  mushrooms  and  let  them 
simmer  about  ten  minutes.  To  the 
butter  add  enough  more  butter  to 
make  two  tablespoonfuls  in  all;  when 
hot,  add  two  tablespoonfuls  of  flour 
and  let  cook  until  brown;  let  cool,  then 
add  the  broth  from  the  mushrooms 
and  stir  and  cook  until  smooth  and 
boiling;  add  the  mushrooms,  and  salt 
and  pepper  as  desired,  and  keep  hot 
over  boiling  water  while  a  French  or  a 
puffy  omelet  is  made  with  four  eggs. 
Put  part  of  the  sauce  over  the  omelet, 
before  it  is  folded,  and  turn  the  rest 
around  it,  after  it  is  turned  onto  the 
serving  dish. 

Stuffed  Eggs,  Italian  Fashion 

Cut  hard-cooked  eggs  in  halves, 
lengthwise.  Have  ready  mushroom 
stems  and  peelings,  chopped  very  fine, 
with  a  shallot  and  two  sprigs  of  pars- 
ley; cook  these  in  butter  four  or  five 
minutes,  theil  add  stock  to  cover  and 
let  simmer  fifteen  minutes,  when  the 
mixture  should  be  quite  dry;  add  the 
cooked  yolks,  chopped  fine  or  sifted, 
and  salt  and  pepper  to  season.  Put 
corresponding  halves  of  eggs  together, 
dip  in  beaten  egg,  diluted  with  two 
tablespoonfuls  of  water,  roll  in  sifted 


bread    crumbs    and   fry    in    deep    fat. 
Serve  with  tomato  sauce. 

Hard  Eggs  a  la  Dreux 

Prepare  a  pint  of  cream  sauce  and 
four  "hard-boiled"  eggs.  Peel  half  a 
pound  of  mushroom  caps,  and  cut  or 
break  them  into  small  pieces;  let  cook 
in  three  tablespoonfuls  of  hot  butter 
till  the  butter  is  absorbed,  then  pour 
on  the  cream  sauce  and  let  cook  over 
hot  water  fifteen  minutes;  add  half  a 
cup  of  cooked  ham,  cut  in  small  cubes, 
and  the  cooked  eggs,  cut  in  slices;  mix 
and  turn  into  buttered  shells  or 
cocottes,  cover  with  a  cap  of  cracker 
crumbs,  mixed  with  one-third  a  cup 
of  melted  butter,  and  let  stand  in  a 
hot  oven  to  brown  the  crumbs. 

Eggs  in  Cocottes  a  la  Reine 

Have  ready  one-fourth  a  cup,  each, 
of  cooked  chicken,  mushrooms  and 
truffles,  all  cut  into  tiny  squares  and 
stirred  into  a  cup  of  cream  saucF.  Put 
from  one  to  two  tablespoonfuls  of  the 
mixture  into  well-buttered,  individual 
cocottes,  and  over  the  mixture  break 
an  egg.  Set  the  dishes  into  the  oven 
and  let  cook  six  or  seven  minutes. 
Serve  at  once. 

Doughnuts 

The  flour  in  the  recipe  referred  to 
is  measured  after  once  sifting;  a  cook- 
ing-school cup  is  used  and  level  cupfuls 
are  taken.  Pastry  flour  is  used.  We 
do  not  see  how  either  the  dough  or 
the  doughnuts  made  from  the  recipe 
with  flour  thus  measured,  can  be  "stiff 
and  hard."  Usually  the  complaint  is 
too  soft  a  dough.  The  following  recipe 
is  very  similar  to  the  one  given  before, 
but  is  preferred  sometimes,  because  the 
doughnuts  are  a  little  sweeter. 

Sift  together  five  cups  of  sifted  pastry 
flour,  half  a  teaspoonful  of  mace,  one 
teaspoonful  of  salt,  three  and  one-half 
level  teaspoonfuls  of  cream  of  tartar 
and  one  level  teaspoonful  of  soda. 
Beat  two  eggs  and  the  yolk  of  another; 


440 


THE   BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


add  a  generous  cup  of  granulated  sugar 
and,  when  well  mixed,  a  scant  cup  of 
milk.  Stir  the  liquid  into  the  dry  in- 
gredients, adding  a  little  more  flour,  if 
necessary,  to  make  a  soft  dough.  Take 
a  little  of  the  dough  onto  the  board, 
pat  into  a  sheet,  cut  into  rounds  and 
fry  in  deep  fat. 

French  Pancakes 

Sift  together  one  cup  of  flour,  one- 
fourth  a  cup  of  sugar  and  one-fourth 
a  teaspoonful  of  salt.  Beat  three  eggs 
and  three  yolks  of  eggs;  add  one-fourth 
a  cup  of  melted  butter  and  stir  into 
the  flour;  add  milk  as  needed  (about 
one-fourth  a  cup),  and  beat  the  whole 
with  an  egg  beater  until  very  light. 
Bake  on  a  hot  griddle,  or  in  a  frying 
pan.  The  cakes  should  be  very  small 
and  thin  and  browned  very  delicately. 
Put  one  above  another  on  the  dish, 
sprinkling  each  with  sifted  powdered 
sugar  as  set  in  place.  Serve  very  hot. 
Preserves  or  jelly  is  used  in  place  of 
the  sugar. 

White  Muffins 

Cream  one-third  a  cup  of  butter; 
beat  in  one-fourth  a  cup  of  sugar; 
alternately,  add  one  egg,  beaten  and 
mixed  with  three-fourths  a  cup  of 
milk,  and  two  cups  of  sifted  pastry 
flour,  sifted  again  with  four  level  tea- 
spoonfuls  of  baking  powder  and  one- 
fourth  a  teaspoonful  of  salt.  Bake  in 
a  hot,  iron  muffin  pan  about  twenty- 
five  minutes. 

Lobster  Cocktail 

Mix  together  one  cup  of  chili  sauce, 
half  a  cup  of  tomato  catsup,  half  a 
teaspoonful  of  salt,  fifteen  drops  of 
tabasco  sauce,  half  a  tablespoonful  of 
horseradish,  and  the  juice  of  a  lemon. 
Turn  into  six  or  eight  cocktail  glasses. 
Set  these  in  the  center  of  small  plates, 
and  dispose  on  the  plates  around  the 
glasses  six  or  eight  small  bits  of  lobster 
meat.  The  lobster,  taken  up  on  an 
oyster  fork,  is  dipped  into  the  mixture 


before  being  eaten.  If  the  lobster  be 
put  into  the  cups  with  the  sauce,  less 
sauce  should  be  used. 

Graham  Muffins 

Sift  together  one  cup,  each,  of  white 
flour  and  graham  flour,  from  one  table- 
spoonful  to  one-fourth  a  cup  of  sugar, 
four  level  teaspoonfuls  of  baking  pow- 
der and  half  a  teaspoonful  of  salt. 
Beat  one  egg;  add  about  one  cup  and 
a  fourth  of  milk  and  stir  into  the  dry 
ingredients;  beat  in  from  two  to  four 
tablespoonfuls  of  melted  butter.  Bake 
in  a  hot,  well-buttered,  iron  gem  pan. 
Muffins  of  this  sort  are  best  when 
baked  in  an  iron  pan. 


Query  1337.  —  Mrs.  R.  B.,  Fort  Scott, 
Kans. :  "Kindly  repeat  the  recipe  for 
Pecan  Wafers  or  Pecan  Sticks,  given  some 
time  ago  in  the  magazine.  Also  give 
recipes  for  the  Canned  Deviled  Crabs,  and 
for  a  Yellow  Cake  in  which  soda  is  used, 
with  butter  Caramel  Frosting. 

Pecan  Sticks 

Beat  two  eggs  very  light,  without 
separating  the  whites  and  yolks.  Then 
gradually  beat  in  half  a  cup  of  sugar,  a 
tablespoonful  of  melted  butter,  half  a 
cup  of  sifted  flour,  and  half  a  cup  of 
pecan  meats,  broken  in  pieces.  Bake 
in  a  quick  oven,  in  buttered  pans, 
similar  to  those  for  lady  fingers,  but 
square  on  the  ends  and  of  the  same 
width  throughout.  Put  but  a  small 
portion  of  the  mixture  in  each  section 
of  the  pan,  as  the  cakes  should  be  very 
thin  when  baked.  These  may  be 
baked  as  wafers  on  any  baking  sheet. 
Drop  by  small  spoonfuls  onto  a 
buttered  sheet  and  at  some  distance 
apart,  as  they  spread  in  baking. 

A  similar  recipe  is  giveji  in  this  issue 
of  the  magazine  under  the  name, 
"Pecan  Nut  Wafers."  The  brown 
sugar  seems  an  improvement  in  flavor, 
but  possibly  a  larger  quantity  of  flour, 
as  in  the  pecan  sticks,  might  not  be 
amiss.  The  wafers  or  sticks  are  at 
their  best  when  thin  and  crisp.

Meatless  Dishes  with  the  Meat  Flavor 
for  Lenten  Season  Days 


By:  MARY  JANE  McCLURE 


1 


P  R I  L,   all  mud  and  slush 
underfoot  and 
frowning,  weeping 
skies  overhead,  pre- 
sent^  a  somber  set- 
ting for  the  season 
of  sackcloth  and 
ashes.     Fasting  and 
penance  would  not 
be  bugbears  if  the 
forty  days  of  Xent 
fell  in  Midsummer, 
when  skies  are  bright  and  gush  of 
bird-song  and  fragrance  of  flowers 
distract  the  mind  from  such  a  ma- 
terial thing  as  longing  for  a  good 
square  meal— and  meat.    But,  fall- 
ing as  it  does  in  the  spongy  month,  the  time  of 
retirement  from  frills  and  frivolity  and  roast  beef 
is  doubly  hard  to  bear. 

It  requires  a  stronger  character  than  is  pos- 
'  sessed  by  the  average  mortal  to  deliberately 
put  aside  meat  for  forty  days,  without  com- 
promising in  some  way.  Nature  and  training  force  the  stom- 
ach to  cry  out  in  an  insistent  demand  for  '  the  fleshpots  of 
Egypt."  Some  sort  of  a  crutch  is  required  upon  which  to 
lean  in  moments  of  weakness.  Put  something  into  the  stom- 
ach that  tastes  like  the  food  it  is  crav- 
ing, and  it  quits  crying  and  goes  to 
sleep  like  a  satisfied  baby.  By  taking 
advantage  of  this  propensity  it  will  be 
possible  to  slip  through  the  Lenten 
season  without  much  discomfort  or 
effort  at  self-denial. 

No  better  compromise  could  be  im-  Trout  with  ; 

agined  at  this  juncture  than  Armour's 

Extract  of  Beef.  With  its  help,  substantial  meatless  dishes 
may  be  prepared  with  the  rich  flavor  of  roast  beef,  as  well  as 
tasty  tit -bits  that  may  be  eaten  without  the  slightest  qualm  of 
conscience. 

The  possibilities  contained  in  the  tiny  jar  of  Armour's  Ex- 
tract of  Beef  are  limited  only  by  the  Ingenuity,  originality  and 
daring  of  the  cook,  and  the  ingredients  at  hand.      All  the 

delicious  juices  of 
rich,  red,  lean  beef 
arelconcentrated  In  an 
aromatic,  savory  es- 
sence, delicately  sea- 
soned and  full  of  pal- 
ate- captivating 
properties. 

In  buying  beef  extract  it  Is  necessary  to  specify  "Armour's" 
If  you  want  the  best.  Most  other  brands  not  only  are  lacking 
in  quality,  but  they  contain  a  certain  percentage  of  water, 
which  makes  It  necessary  to  use,  in  some  cases,  four  times  as 


Meatless  Beef  Loaf 


much  as  would  be  required  if  Armour's  was  employed.  For 
this  reason,  while  other  brands  at  first  sight  may  appear  to  be 
less  expensive,  Armour's,  in  the  long  run,  is  the  cheapest,  be- 
cause only  about  a  quarter  as  much  of  it  will  be  needed  to 
produce  the  same  results,  and  thus  it  will  last  four  times  as 
long  as  the  "  bargain  "   brand. 

IMeatless  Beef  Loaf.      Soak  3^  pound  of  stale 

bread  in  warm  water  and  squeeze  it  dry.  Put  a  piece  of 
butter  the  size  of  an 
egg  in  a  stew-pan  and 
when  hot,  mix  In  It  a 
large  onion,  finely 
chopped.  As  soon 
as  the  onion  be- 
comes a  golden  color, 
put  in  the  bread 
and  a  tablespoonful 
of  chopped  parsley,  and  salt  to  taste.  Stir  It  until  it  leaves 
the  sides  of  the  pan,  then  add  two  eggs  In  which  has  been 
mixed  3^  teaspoonful  of  Armour's  Extract  of  Beef  dissolved 
in  1  tablespoonful  of  boiling  water.  Put  in  a  baking  dish  and 
bake  for  ten  minutes.  Serve  with  brown  sauce  made  as  follows: 

Bro^wn  Sauce.  Melt  1  heaping  teaspoonful  of  butter. 
Into  this  stir  1  tablespoonful  of  flour.  Add  boiling  water  to 
make  the  gravy  the  proper  consistency.  Season  with  pepper 
and  salt  and  stir  in  1  teaspoonful  of  Armour's  ELxtract  of  Beef. 
Macaroni  Loaf.  Cook  the 
macaroni  in  salted  water  until  it  is  ten- 
der ;  then  drain  free  from  the  water 
and  blanch  In  cold  water,  and  place 
in  a  basin  In  alternate  layers  of  grated 
cheese,  bits  of  butter,  salt  and  pepper 
until  the  basin  is  filled ;  then  cover 
with  milk  and  bake  in  a  moderate 
oven  for  half  an  hour.  Slip  a  knife 
around  the  edge  and  unmold  onto  a  dish,  covering  with  a 
sauce  made  as  follows: 

Devil  Sauce.  Press  through  a  sieve  the  hard-boiled 
yolks  of  two  eggs.  Have  the  whites  chopped  fine  and  add 
to  them  a  teaspoonful  of  minced  parsley,  3^  teaspoonful  of 
paprika,  1  chopped  Spanish  sweet  pepper,  and  a  teaspoonful 
of  grated  onion.  Add  all  these  Ingredients  to  a  sauce  made 
from  I  pint  of  hot  water  to  which  has  been  added  2  tea- 
spoonfuls  of  Armour's  ELxtract  of  Beef,  I  tablespoonful  of 
flour  mixed  in  a  paste  with  I  rounding  tablespoonful  of  but- 
ter, then  [stirring  it  into  the  hot  water  and  cooking  until 
creamy.  Sprinkle  the  egg  yolk  over  the  top,  and  serve  hot 
for  luncheon  or  as  an  entree  for  dinner.

QUERIES  AND  ANSWERS 


Scalloped  Crabs 

Pick  fine  the  crab  meat  in  a  can; 
add  a  grating  of  nutmeg  (this  may  be 
omitted),  two  level  tablespoonfuls  of 
sifted  bread  crumbs,  two  eggs,  beaten 
light,  and  two  tablespoonfuls  of  melted 
butter;  mix  thoroughly  and  use  to  fill 
crab  shells,  well-buttered  (china  dishes 
or  paper  cases  may  be  used).  Cover 
the  preparation  in  each  dish  with 
cracker  crumbs,  mixed  with  melted 
butter,  and  let  cook  in  a  hot  oven 
until  the  crumbs  are  browned.  Three- 
fourths  a  cup  of  crumbs  and  one-fourth 
a  cup  of  butter  will  suffice. 

Creamed  Crabs  au  Gratin 

Prepare  a  cup  of  cream  sauce;  into 
this  stir  the  crab  meat,  turn  into  an 
au  gratin  dish,  cover  with  buttered 
cracker  crumbs  and  finish  as  above. 

Crab  Meat,  Creole  Style 
Cook  half  an  onion  and  half  a  pepper 
pod,  cut  fine,  in  two  tablespoonfuls  of 
butter,  until  softened  and  yellowed; 
add  three-fourths  a  cup  of  cooked 
tomato  and  half  a  cup  of  chicken  broth 
and  let  simmer  ten  minutes,  then  strain 
and  cool.  Cook  two  tablespoonfuls  of 
flour  in  two  tablespoonfuls  of  butter; 
add  one-fourth  a  teaspoonful  of  salt 
and  the  strained  liquid;  stir  until  boil- 
ing, then  add  a  generous  cup  of  flaked 
crab  meat.     Serve  when  hot. 

Butter  Caramel  Frosting 

Boil  three  cups  of  brown  sugar,  half 
a  cup  of  cream  and  two  tablespoonfuls 
of  butter  about  five  minutes ;  after  boil- 
ing actually  begins,  add  a  teaspoonful 
of  vanilla  and  beat  until  the  mixture 
begins  to  thicken. 

Yellow  Cake 
Cream  one  cup  of  butter;  beat  in 
one  cup  and  a  half  of  granulated  sugar, 
the  well-beaten  yolks  of  four  eggs,  half 
a  cup  of  sweet  milk,  alternately,  with 
two  cups  and  one-fourth  of  sifted 
pastry  flour,  sifted  again  with  half  a 


teaspoonful  of  soda  and  two  level 
teaspoonfuls  of  cream  of  tartar.  Flavor 
with  a  teaspoonful  of  vanilla  extract; 
add  the  whites  of  four  eggs,  beaten  dry. 
Bake  in  layers. 


Happy  Old  Age 

Most  Likely  to  Follow  Proper  Eating 

As  old  age  advances,  we  require  less 
food  to  replace  waste,  and  food  that 
will  not  overtax  the  digestive  organs, 
while  supplying  true  nourishment. 

Such  an  ideal  food  is  found  in  Grape- 
Nuts,  made  of  whole  wheat  and  barley 
by  long  baking  and  action  of  diastase 
in  the  barley  which  changes  the  starch 
into  sugar. 

The  phosphates  also,  placed  up  under 
the  bran-coat  of  the  wheat,  are  included 
in  Grape-Nuts,  but  left  out  of  white 
flour.  They  are  necessary  to  the  build- 
ing of  brain  and  nerve  cells. 

"I  have  used  Grape-Nuts,"  writes 
an  Iowa  man,  "for  8  years  and  feel 
as  good  and  am  stronger  than  I 
was  ten  years  ago.  I  am  over  74  years 
old  and  attend  to  my  business  every 
day. 

"Among  my  customers  I  meet  a  man 
every  day  who  is  92  years  old,  and  at- 
tributes his  good  health  to  the  use  of 
Grape-Nuts  and  Postum  which  he  has 
used  for  the  last  5  years.  He  mixes 
Grape-Nuts  with  Postum  and  says  they 
go  fine  together. 

"For  many  years  before  I  began  to 
eat  Grape-Nuts,  I  could  not  say  that 
I  enjoyed  life  or  knew  what  it  was  to  be 
able  to  say,  'I  am  well.'  I  suffered 
greatly  with  constipation,  now  my 
habits  are  as  regular  as  ever  in  my 
life. 

"Whenever  I  make  extra  effort  I 
depend  on  Grape-Nuts  food  and  it  just 
fills  the  bill.  I  can  think  and  write  a 
great  deal  easier." 

"There's  a  Reason."  Name  given 
by  Postum  Co.,  Battle  Creek,  Mich. 
Read  "The  Road  to  Wellville,"  in 
pkgs.

Mandalay  Sauce 

is  composed  of  choicest  fruits,  vegetables  and  spices 
of  foreign  and  domestic  origin,  skilfully  blended  after  a 
recipe  found  by  an  English  army  officer  in  the  Far  East. 

It  imparts  incomparable  goodness  to  hot  or  cold 
meats,  fish,  game,  soups  and  gravies.  Indispensable 
for  all  chafing-dish  cooking — W^elsh  rarebits,  cheese 
toast,  and  so  on.  Stimulates  the  jaded  appetite  ;  is 
unquestionably  wholesome. 

Try  a  bottle  from  your  grocer;  it  is  far  removed 
from  common  sauces — unlike  any  other  in  flavor. 

Other  seasonable  Heinz  delicacies  are  Sweet 
Pickles,  Preserved  Fruits,  Jellies,  Cranberry  Sauce, 
Euchred  Figs,  Tomato  Chutney,  etc.  Our  free  book^ 
let,  "The   Spice   of  Life,"  tells   all  about   them.

Query  1338.  —  Mrs.  H.  H.  R.,  Middle- 
town,  Pa.:  "Recipe  for  Chocolate  Sauce 
for  Ice  Cream." 

Chocolate  Sauce  for  Ice  Cream 

Cook  one  cup  of  sugar  to  caramel; 
add  a  cup  of  boiling  water  and  let 
cook  to  a  syrup,  then  add  a  cup  of 
sugar,  an  ounce  of  chocolate,  half  a 
cup  of  boiling  water  and  two  inches  of 
stick  cinnamon;  stir  until  the  sugar 
and  chocolate  are  dissolved,  then  let 
boil  until  of  a  suitable  consistency; 
strain  and  use  hot  or  cold. 

Chocolate  Sauce  for  Ice  Cream  No.  2 

Melt  an  ounce  of  chocolate  over  hot 
water;  add  one  cup  of  sugar,  one-fourth 
a  teaspoonful  of  salt  and  one  cup  and 
a  half  of  boiling  water ;  stir  and  let  boil 
five  minutes;  add  half  a  teaspoonful  of 
gelatine ,  softened  in  cold  water  to  cover; 
strain,  and  when  cold  add  a  teaspoonful 
of  vanilla  extract. 


Query  1339. 
for  Raisin  Pie." 


A    Subscriber:      "Recipe 


R 


aism 


Pie 


Line  a  pie  plate  with  pastry;  mix 
together  one  cup  of  seeded  raisins,  the 
yolks  of  two  eggs,  beaten  and  mixed 
with  half  a  cup  of  sugar  and  the  grated 
rind  and  juice  of  half  a  lemon,  and  a 
cup  of  water;  turn  into  the  pastry - 
lined  plate,  sprinkle  on  a  little  salt 
and  add  a  tablespoonful  of  butter,  in 
little  bits,  here  and  there;  brush  over 
the  edge  of  the  paste  with  cold  water, 
cover  with  pastry,  putting  it  on  loosely, 
brush  the  edges  together  with  water 
and  bake  about  thirty-five  minutes. 


Query  1340.  —  F.  La  S.,  Rock  City,  111.: 
"Recipe  for  Hickory  Nut  Cake.  Can  black 
walnuts  be  used  in  cake  as  other  nuts?" 

Hickory  Nut  Cake 

One-half  a  cup  of  butter,  one  cup  of 
sugar,  yolks  of  three  eggs,  beaten  light; 
one-half  a  cup  of  milk,  one  and  three- 
fourths  cups  of  sifted  pastry  flour,  four 
level  teaspoonfuls  of  baking  powder, 
whites  of  three  eggs,  beaten  dry;  one 


teaspoonful  of  vanilla  extract,  three- 
fourths  a  cup  of  walnut  meats,  broken 
in  pieces. 

Cream  the  butter;  add  the  sugar 
gradually,  then  the  yolks,  alternately, 
the  milk  and  flour,  sifted  with  the 
baking  powder,  the  egg  whites,  extract 
and  nuts.  Bake  in  small  tins  or  in 
layers.     Put   together   with    chocolate 


Growing  Stronger 

Apparently,  with  Advancing  Age 

"In  1896  at  the  age  of  50  years,  I 
collapsed  from  excessive  coffee  drink- 
ing," writes  a  man  in  Mo.  "For  four 
years  I  shambled  about  with  the  aid  of 
crutches  or  cane,  most  of  the  time  un- 
able to  dress  myself  without  help. 

"My  feet  were  greatly  swollen,  my 
right  arm  was  shrunken  and  twisted 
inward,  the  fingers  of  my  right 
hand  were  clenched  and  could  not  be 
extended  except  with  great  effort  and 
pain.  Nothing  seemed  to  give  me  more 
than  temporary  relief. 

"Now,  during  all  this  time  and  for 
about  30  years  previously,  I  drank  daily 
an  average  of  6  cups  of  strong  coffee  — 
rarely  missing  a  meal. 

"My  wife  at  last  took  my  case  into 
her  own  hands  and  bought  some  Pos- 
tum.  She  made  it  according  to  direc- 
tions and  I  liked  it  fully  as  well  as  the 
best  high  grade  coffee. 

"Improvement  set  in  at  once.  In 
about  6  months  I  began  to  work  a  little, 
and  in  less  than  a  year  I  was  very 
much  better,  improving  rapidly  from 
day  to  day.  I  am  now  in  far  better 
health  than  most  men  of  my  age  and 
apparently  growing  stonger  with  ad- 
vancing age. 

"I  am  busy  every  day  at  some  kind 
of  work  and  am  able  to  keep  up  with 
the  procession  without  a  cane.  The  arm 
and  hand  that  were  once  almost  useless, 
now  keep  far  ahead  in  rapidity  of  move- 
ment and  beauty  of  penmanship."

Postum  Co.,  Battle  Creek,  Mich.  Read 
"The  Road  to  Wellville,"  in  pkgs.

THE    BOSTON    COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


or  maple  frosting.  Decorate  with 
halves  of  nut  meats.  We  see  no

Query  1341.  —  Mrs.  R.  E.  L.  C,  Bir- 
mingham, Ala.:  "Recipe  for  a  White 
Butter  Cake,  rich  and  fine-grained,  made 
with  six  or  seven  egg  whites;  also  recipe 
for  Gold  Layer  Cake,  to  use  the  yolks. 
Can  yolks  of  eggs  be  used  with  good  results 
in  any  cake  recipe  calling  for  whole  eggs?" 

Elegant  Cake 

One  cup  of  butter,  two  cups  of  sugar, 
one-half  a  cup  of  milk,  three  and  one- 
half  cups  of  sifted  pastry  flour,  four 
level  teaspoonfuls  of  baking  powder, 
seven  whites  of  eggs,  beaten  dry. 

Mix  in  the  same  manner  as  indicated 
in  the  recipe  for  Hickory  Nut  Cake  given 
in  answer  to  query  1340.  Do  not  at- 
tempt to  cream  the  butter  and  sugar  to- 
gether at  the  start.  First  cream  the 
butter,  then  gradually  beat  the  sugar  in- 
to the  butter.  Beat  a  little  after  the 
egg  whites  are  added.  Flavor  to  taste. 
Yolks  of  eggs  cannot,  in  all  cases,  be 
used  in  cake  where  whole  eggs  are  called 
for.  More  baking  powder  is  usually 
needed,  when  egg  whites  are  omitted. 
Omelets  and  custards  are  vastly  better 
when  a  preponderance  of  yolks  are 
taken.  And  there  are  so  many  other 
uses  for  egg  yolks,  that  they  can 
always  be  used.  The  following  cake 
is  particularly  good,  and  may  be 
baked  in  loaf,  layer,  or  as  little  cakes. 
In  baking  in  a  loaf  use  a  tube  pan. 

Golden  Cake 

One-half  a  cup  of  butter,  one  cup  of 
sugar,  eight  yolks  of  eggs,  beaten  light, 
one-half  a  cup  of  milk,  one  and  three- 
fourths  cups  of  flour,  four  level  tea- 
spoonfuls  of  baking  powder,  one  tea- 
spoonful  of  orange  extract. 

Mix  as  in  the  recipe  for  Hickory  Nut 
Cake. 


Brown  Betty 

One-half  a  cup  of  melted  butter,  one 
pint  of  sifted  bread  crumbs,  one-half  a 
teaspoonful  of  cinnamon,  one  quart  of 
sliced  apples,  one-half  a  cup  of  cold 
water,  one-half  a  cup  of  sugar.  . 

Stir  the  butter  into  the  crumbs. 
Into  a  buttered  baking  dish  put  a 
layer  of  the  buttered  crumbs,  then  a 
layer  of  sliced  apples,  sprinkle  with  the 
cinnamon  and  sugar,  and  continue 
until  the  materials  are  used,  having 
the  last  layer  of  crumbs;  pour  in  the 
water  before  the  last  layer  of  crumbs. 
If  the  apples  are  juicy,  use  less  water. 
Cover  the  dish  and  let  bake  about  an 
hour  in  a  moderate  oven.  Remove 
the  cover,  at  the  last,  to  brown  the  top. 
Serve  with  cream. 

Boston  Fudge  Cake 

The  following  fudge  cake  may  not 
be  the  one  our  New  York  subscriber 
has  in  mind. 

One-half  a  cup  of  butter,  two  cups  of 
brown  sugar,  two  yolks  of  eggs,  beaten 
light;  two  ounces  of  chocolate,  melted; 
one-half  a  cup  of  sour  milk,  one-half  a 
cup  of  water,  two  and  one-half  cups 
of  flour,  one  teaspoonful  of  soda,  two 
whites  of  eggs,  beaten  dry.  Filling:  two 
cups  of  brown  sugar,  one-fourth  a  cup 
of  butter,  one-half  a  cup  of  milk,  one- 
half  a  cup  of  nut  meats.  Frosting:  six 
tablespoonfuls  of  grated  chocolate,  six 
tablespoonfuls  of  granulated  sugar, 
one  tablespoonful  of  sweet  cream, 
one   tablespoonful  of  butter. 

Prepare  the  cake  in  the  usual 
manner.  The  ingredients  are  given  in 
the  order  of  use.  Boil  the  sugar,  butter 
and  milk  to  a  soft  ball,  for  the  filling; 
add  the  nut  meats  and  beat  until  thick 
enough  to  spread.  Melt  the  chocolate 
for  the  frosting,  add  the  sugar,  cream 
and  butter;  let  cook  a  few  minutes, 
then  spread  over  the  cake.

filling  for  oyster  patties  (how  many  quarts 
of  oysters);  how  much  ice  cream,  and  how 
many  potatoes  and  how  much  cream  for 
creamed  potatoes  to  serve  the  same 
number?" 


Quantity  of  Salad,  Sandwiches,  etc., 
to  Serve  One  Hundred 

Allowing  a  quart  of  salad  to  serve 
a  dozen  guests,  about  eight  and  a  half 
quarts  would  be  needed.  A  chicken 
weighing  three  pounds  and  a  half 
usually  gives  a  pint  of  meat;  add  half 
the  measure  of  celery  and  about  a  cup 
of  mayonnaise  dressing.  This  will  give 
a  scant  quart  of  salad.  The  above  may 
be  a  scant  allowance,  if  no  other  meat 
dish  is  to  be  served.  Attention  should 
be  given  to  this  matter.  A  quart  of 
ice  cream  will  serve  ten  or  twelve. 
Plan  for  one  hundred  and  fifty  or  one 
hundred  and  seventy-five  sandwiches. 
A  loaf  of  bread  will  make  twelve 
or  fourteen  sandwiches.  Ten  or  twelve 
quarts  of  oysters,  a  generous  half -peck 
of  potatoes  and  five  or  six  quarts  of 
rich  milk  (or  part  thin  cream)  will  be 
needed  of  these  several  articles,  for  the 
dishes  mentioned,  to  serve  one  hundred 
plates. 


Query  1344.  —  Mrs.  J.  C.  B.,  Minneapo- 
lis, Minn.:  "Recipes  for  Frozen  Piinches, 
such  as  are  used  after  the  roast  at  a  course 
dinner." 


Roman  Punch 

Mix  a  quart  of  water,  a  pint  of 
sugar,  the  white  of  an  egg,  and  a 
generous  cup  of  lemon  juice.  When 
the  sugar  is  dissolved,  freeze  to  a  mush. 
Take  out  the  dasher,  pour  in  three 
tablespoonfuls  of  Jamaica  rum,  and 
mix  thoroughly  with  a  paddle.  When 
ready  to  serve,  put  the  ice  in  glasses, 
make  an  open  space  in  the  center  of 
each,  put  in  a  teaspoonful  of  rum,  and 
serve  at  once.  The  usual  lemon 
sherbet,  made  by  boiling  a  quart  of 
water  and  pint  of  sugar  twenty  minutes, 
and  adding  the  lemon  juice  when  cold, 
is  often  used  for  a  punch  foundation, 
though  its  smoothness  is  not  desirable 
in  a  punch. 

Elizabeth  Punch 

Boil  a  pint  of  water  and  a  cup  of 
sugar  ten  minutes;  let  cool,  add  a  cup 
of  strawberry  juice  and  the  juice  of 
half  a  lemon;  freeze  as  ice  cream. 
When  ready  to  serve,  beat  in  half  a 
cup  of  kirsch  and  two  or  three  table- 
spoonfuls  of  brandy. 

Siberian  Punch,  or  Lalla  Rookh 

To  a  quart  of  vanilla  ice  cream, 
packed  in  a  freezer,  add  three  table- 
spoonfuls  of  Italian  meringue  and  half 
a  cup  of  rum. 


Grape-Fruit  Punch 

Boil  half  a  cup  of  granulated  sugar 
and  one-third  a  cup  of  water  to  a 
thick  syrup  —  or  until  it  will  spin  a 
very  short  thread.  It  should  not  be 
boiled  as  much  as  for  boiled  frosting. 
Pour  the  syrup  onto  the  white  of  an 
egg,  beaten  dry,  proceeding  as  for 
boiled  frosting.  This  gives  an  Italian 
meringue.  When  the  meringue  is  cold, 
work  it  into  a  freezer  of  grape-fruit 
sherbet,  prepared  as  above.  When 
ready  to  serve,  make  a  depression  in 
the  center,  of  the  sherbet  in  the  glasses 
and  put  into  each  about  a  teaspoonful 
of  maraschino.

The  Mother  as  Citizen 

An  International  Congress  on  the 
welfare  of  the  child  was  held  in  Wash- 
ington last  month.  This  meeting  was 
held  under  the  auspices  of  the  Na- 
tional Mothers'  Congress,  and  the  sub- 
jects discussed  concern  every  house- 
hold. 

In  his  address  of  welcome,  delivered 
at  the  White  House,  President  Roose- 
velt spoke  in  part  as  follows: 

"The  successful  mother,  the  mother 
who  does  her  part  in  rearing  and  train- 
ing aright  the  boys  and  girls  who  are 
to  be  the  men  and  women  of  the  next 
generation,  is  of  greater  use  to  the 
community  and  occupies,  if  she  only 
would  realize  it,  a  more  honorable  as 
well  as  a  more  important  position  than 
any  successful  man  in  it. 

"No  scheme  of  education,  no  social 
attitude  can  be  right  unless  it  is  "based 
fundamentall}^  upon  the  recognition  of 
seeing  that  the  girl  is  trained  to  un- 
derstand the  supreme  dignity,  the  su- 
preme usefulness  of  motherhood. 

"Unless  the  average  woman  is  a 
good  wife  and  good  mother,  unless 
she  bears  a  sufficient  number  of  chil- 
dren, so  that  the  race  shall  increase, 
and  not  decrease,  unless  she  brings  up 
these  children  sound  in  soul  and  mind 
and  body  —  unless  this  is  true  of 
the  average  woman,  no  brilliancy  of 
genius,  no  material  prosperity,  no 
triumphs  of  science  and  industry  will 
avail  to  save  the  race  from  ruin  and 
death. 

"The  mother  is  the  one  supreme 
asset  of  national  life;  she  is  more  im- 
portant by  far  than  the  successful 
statesman,  or  business  man,  or  artist, 
or  scientist. 

"A  good  woman  who  does  full  duty 
is  sacred  in  our  eyes,  exactly  as  the 
brave  and  patriotic  soldier  is  to  be 
honored  above  all  other  men.  But  the 
woman  who,  whether  from  cowardice, 
from  selfishness,  from  having  a  false 
and  vacuous  ideal,  shirks  her  duty  as 
wife  and  mother,  earns  the  right  to 
our   contempt  just   as   does   the   man

who,  from  any  motive,  fears  to  do  his 
duty  in  battle,  when  the  country  calls 
him. 

"I  want  to  ask  your  assistance  for 
two  or  three  matters  that  are  not  im- 
mediately connected  with  the  life  in 
the  family  itself,  but  that  are  of  vital 
consequence  to  the  children.  In  the 
first  place,  see  that  the  school  work  is 
made  as  practical  as  possible. 

"For  boys  I  want  to  see  training 
provided  that  shall  train  them  toward, 
and  not  away  from,  their  life  work, 
that  will  train  them  toward  the  farm 
or  the  shop,  and  not  away  from  it. 
With  the  girl,  see  that  it  is  not  made  a 
matter  of  mirth  that  the  girl  who  goes 
to  college  comes  out  unprepared  to  do 
any  of  the  ordinary  duties  of  woman- 
hood. 

"So,  in  other  words,  that  with  the 
higher  education  which  she  should 
have  —  for  she  should  have  a  right  to 
just  as  much  education,  to  just  as 
high  an  education  as  any  man  —  see 
that  with  that  goes  the  education  that 
will  fit  her  to  do  her  fundamental  work 
in  the  world. 

"As  regards  our  public  schools,  espe- 
cially, I  want  to  put  in  a  special  word 
in  behalf  of  the  right  kind  of  play- 
grounds. No  school  is  a  good  school 
if  it  has  not  a  good  playground. 

"Help  the  children  to  play,  and  re- 
member that  you  can  often  help  them 
most  by  leaving  them  entirely  alone. 
I  misread  them  if  they  themselves  do 
not  often  know  better  how  to  play 
better  than  we  old  folks  can  teach  them. 
It  is  the  gravest  kind  of  wrong,  not 
only  to  the  children  but  to  the  whole 
community,  to  turn  out  the  boys  and 
girls,  especially  in  the  congested  part 
of  the  city,  with  no  place  to  play  in 
but  the  streets. 

"You  cannot  have  good  citizens, 
good  men  and  women  of  the  next  gen- 
eration, if  the  boys  and  girls  are  worked 
in  factories  to  the  stunting  of  their 
moral,  mental  and  physical  growth. 
Wherever  the  national  government  can 
reach,  it  should  do  away  with  the  evils 
of  child  labor."

College 


The  Summer  school  at  Simmons 
College,  Boston,  will  include  this  year 
courses  in  Household  Economics  as 
well  as  courses  in  Kindergarten 
Methods,  Secretarial  Studies  and  Li- 
brary Training.  These  new  courses 
are  designed  especially  for  women  who 
have  had  experience  in  teaching,  and 
the  conferences  will  be  adapted  so  far 
as  possible  to  the  immediate  needs  of 
the  students  constituting  the  classes. 
The  courses  are  to  be  under  the  direc- 
tion of  Dean  Arnold,  who  will  herself 
give  a  series  of  lectures  on  the  principles 
of  teaching  as  applied  to  Household 
Economics.  Professor  Howard  will 
give  instruction  in  Advanced  Cooking 
and  on  Normal  Methods  in  Cooking. 
Miss  Dike  will  lecture  on  Dietaries, 
Miss  Hanna  and  Miss  Spooner,  on 
Normal  Methods  in  Sewing,  with  dis- 
cussions and  practice,  and  Professor 
Mark  will  give  an  elementary  course  in 
Chemistry,  with  special  reference  to  its 
applications  in  Household  Economics. 
Some  of  the  College  dormitories  will 
be  open  for  the  use  of  the  students 
attending  this  session. 


There  is  great  genius  displayed  in 
advertising.  In  the  window  of  a  little  ^ 
book-store  in  Eighth  Avenue,  New 
York,  was  recently  heaped  a  great  pile 
of  Bibles,  marked  very  low  —  never 
before  were  Bibles  offered  at  such  a 
bargain;  and  above  them  all,  in  big 
letters,  was  the  inscription, 

"  Satan  trembles  when  he  sees 
Bibles  sold  as  low  as  these." 


"Is  the  master  of  the  house  in?" 
inquired  the  smooth-tongued  book- 
agent  of  the  little  boy  who  had  an- 
swered his  ring.  "Nope,"  said  the 
boy.  "Little  boys  should  not  tell 
falsehoods,"  said  the  book-agent. 
"Isn't  that  your  father  reading  the 
newspaper  there  by  the  window?" 
"Yep,"  was  the  answer,  "that's  pa 
all   right,   but   ma   is  out."

IDEAL 
EASTER 
HAMS 


Squire's  Arlington  Hams  are  the  choicest  of  the  choice  hams  which 
bear  the  Squire  name. 

From  the  thousands  of  superior  hams  produced  every  week  the  best 
are  chosen  for  the  Arlington  brand.  The  selection  is  along  rigid  lines.  Each 
ham  must  have  a  certain  amount  of  fat  to  provide  for  sweet,  tender,  juicy 
and  delicious  meat — but  not  such  an  amount  of  fat  as  to  be  wasteful. 

Then,  since  these  hams  are  prepared  for  the  most  discriminating  taste, 
a  light,  mild  cure  is  essential.  Arlington  Hams  are  light  smoked,  sugar 
cured.  They  have  an  unusually  mild,  sweet  flavor  and  are  the  IDEAL 
EASTER  HAMS.  If  you  are  not  already  acquainted  with  the  Arlington 
brand,  give  it  a  trial  in  your  next  order.    It  is  easy  to  identify  the  real  thing. 

Each  ham  is  carefully  wrapped,  first  in  a  heavy  paper  and  then  in  an 
outside  wrapper  of  vegetable  parchment  paper  and  tied  with  a  red  cord.  In 
keeping  with  the  quality  of  the  goods  a  handsome  red  and  gold  label  is  used 
to  form  a  distinguishing  mark. 

A  similar  label  appears  on  the  World's  Best 
Bacon.  This  is  Arlington  Brand  Bacon.  Squire's 
products  are  all  of  the  highest  grade,  but  the  very 
best  of  all  bear  not  only  the  name  John  P.  Squire 
but  A  rlington  Brand  as  well.   Readers  of  the  Boston 

Cooking-School  Magazine  want  the  best  and  that  is      Jjji,,    /^fOf^         '^^ 
why  we  tell  them  about  the  Arlington  Brand. 

d. Remember  when  ordering  hams  and  bacon   to 
ask  for  the  package  with    the   Arlington    label

npHE  MODEL  HUB  RANGE  is  just 
the  same  every  day — year  in  and 
year  out.  You  can  run  it  light  to  cook 
for  two,  or  run  it  strong  to  cook  for 
twenty.  You  can  start  a  new  fire 
every  morning,  or  you  can  keep  the 
fire  running  night  and  day  contin- 
uously, and  the  oven  will  work  equally 
well  in  either  case.  A  turn  of  the  grate 
cuts  out  the  old  part  of  the  fire  and 
leaves  the  live  part.  You  can  bake 
biscuit  morning  noon  or  night  with 
the  same  fire. 

Isn*t  this  a  range  worth  investigat- 
ing ?  Isn't  it  the  Range  you  have  been 
looking  for?

Not  Uninteresting 

The  editor  of  a  periodical  that  pays 
only  on  publication  sent  the  following 
letter  to  the  ancient  address  of  a  con- 
tributor: "If  the  author  of  'The  Cave 
in  the  Sea'  is  still  living,  he  is  hereby 
notified  that  his  stor}^  has  just  been 
published,  and  that  we  have  a  check 
to  his  credit."  Shortly  afterward  the 
editor  received  the  following  reply: 
"Dear  Sir,  —  He  died  twenty  years 
ago;  but  his  greatgrandchildren  will  be 
pleased  to  receive  the  check,  if  for- 
warded during  the  present  century."" 
—  Atlanta  Constitution. 


Questions  are  often  raised  as  to  the 
value  of  expert  evidence  in  courts  of 
law;  but  the  difficulty  of  finding  an 
expert  who  may  be  accepted  as  a  final 
authority  upon  any  subject  is  not  con- 
fined to  the  courts.  In  astronomy,  for 
instance,  we  are  dependent  upon  the 
testimony  of  learned  men  who,  be- 
cause they  have  knowledge,  are  sup- 
posed to  speak  with  authority.  But 
here  is  Dr.  Lowell  assuring  us  that  on 
the  planet  Mars  are  signs  of  intelligent 
handiwork,  making  it  probable  that 
the  planet  is  inhabited.  On  the  other 
hand,  Prof.  Pickering,  of  the  Har- 
vard Observatory,  says,  with  great 
positiveness,  that  nothing  of  the  sort 
has  been  proved.  Mr.  Horace  Fletcher 
has  won  an  enviable  reputation  as  an 
authority  in  regard  to  eating  and  diet. 
He  has  convinced  learned  men  of  two 
continents  that  his  theories  are  valid 
and  his  practices  to  be  commended. 
Now  comes  Metchnikofi,  who  says  that 
there  are  diseases  of  the  digestive  or- 
gans caused  by  the  new  method  of 
slow  mastication.  Bradyfagy  (or  slow 
eating)  is  the  name  given  to  the  dis- 
ease which  Fletcher  is  considered  to 
have  evoked.  —  Christian  Register.

weighty  words,  that  will  appeal  strongly 
to  the  majority:  "An  exclusive  diet  of

any  sort,  whether  it  be  meat,  fish, 
vegetables,  fruits,  nuts  or  mud  (as  in 
some  remote  parts  of  the  earth),  is 
foohsh  and  against  nature.  A  judi- 
cious choice,  mixture,  progression  from 
one  to  the  other  is  surely  the  obvious 
and  healthiest  course  (or  courses), 
maintaining  always  that  strict  modera- 
tion which  sensible  men  and  women 
know  to  be  the  best." 


Capital  advice,  this,  from  Dr.  Sam- 
uel G.  Dixon:  "Wash  your  hands,  and 
prevent  disease."  People  who  live  in 
sooty  regions,  like  Boston  and  Pitts- 
burg, are  simply  forced  to  wash  their 
hands;  they  can't  help  themselves, 
whether  they  would  like  to  be  unclean 
or  not.  But ,  probably ,  when  they  real- 
ize their  or  somebody  else's  hands  are 
conveyers  of  many  afflictions,  scrub- 
bing and  manicuring  will  become  more 
popular.  Mme.  Waddington,  in  her 
delightful  descriptions  of  French  coun- 
try life,  speaks  of  a  young  man  from  a 
neighboring  chateau  who  spent  the  day 
at  her  house,  went  out  shooting,  played 
with  the  dogs  and  did  various  other 
things,  and  when  asked  before  dinner 
if  he  would  like  to  "wash  up  "in  a 
guest  room,  sweetly  replied,  "No,  he 
was  quite  clean!"  The  state  of  his 
hands  Mme.  Waddington  leaves  to  the 
reader's  imagination.  Some  one  should 
send  this  young  man,  if  he  is  alive,  a 
report  of  what  Dr.  Dixon,  a  trained 
sanitarian,  says  on  the  necessity  of 
frequent  washings.  Imitate  the  Mus- 
sulman, who  presents  his  guests  with  a 
basin  of  water  and  a  napkin  at  the 
beginning  of  each  feast ! 


Call  upon  yourself  first  when  you 
need  help,  for  no  one  else  is  so  anxious 
to  aid  you. 


Eminent  Teachers  of  Cookery.

Your  first  interest  is  that  it  shall  be  durable,  next  that  it 
shall  be  beautiful  in  design  and  faultless  in  finish.

r«TXItIlITTTIIIIIIIIlIIITl 


In  the  matter  of  diet  one  is  inclined 
to  believe  in  the  axiom  ''vox  populi, 
vox  Dei."  The  commonsense  and  in- 
stinct of  every  community  inclines  it 
to  the  choice  of  foods  best  suited  to 
its  environment;  in  hot  countries  veg- 
etables and  fruits  preponderate  in  the 
national  bills  of  fare.  Nature  confirms 
the  choice  by  making  them  plentiful 
and  cheap;  in  cold  climes  meat  is  the 
staple  article,  and  a  more  equally  bal- 
anced mixed  diet  suits  best  in  the 
temperate  zone.  Instinct,  too,  has  led 
to  more  scientific  food  combinations 
than  the  professedly  scientific  have 
ever  compassed.  The  world  has  never 
learned  the  name  of  the  genius  who 
conceived  the  idea  of  spreading  butter 
upon  his  bread,  a  combination  of 
food  elements  more  palatable,  more 
wholesome  and  more  universally  pop- 
ular than  any  that  the  combined 
wisdom  of  all  the  professors  who 
have  ever  lived  has  been  able  to  de- 
vise.

Picnicking  on  Hurricane  Mountain 


I  'HIS  school  is  unique  in  its  location, 
•■•  in  the  personal  attention  given  each 
pupil,  and  in  the  combination  of  recre- 
ation and  practical  work. 

^The  kitchens  and  dining  rooms  are 
new,  light,  airy  and  spacious. 

^  The  climate  is  bracing. 

fl  The  special  needs  of  each  pupil  are 
carefully  considered. 


Classes  for  Graduates  of  Schools  and  Colleges  of  Domestic  Science,  Housekeepers  and  Caterers,  Beginners  in  Cookery. 

"Topo  Pino"  overlooks  two  large  ponds,  which  afford  good  opportunity  for  boating  and  bathing,  while  Mountains 
"Kearsarge"  and  "Hurricane"  in  the  background,  and  "Baldface"  and  "Royce"  in  the  distance,  challenge  aspirants  to 
mountain  climbing.     For  circular  of  terms  and  other  information  apply,  in  person  or  by  mail,  to 

THE  BOSTON  COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE  CO. 

372  Boylston  Street,  Boston,  Mass.
