A  Unique  and  Formal  Plan 

A  Garden  Study 

By  Mary  H.  Northend 


ONE  cloudless  afternoon,  in  the 
month  of  July,  I  stepped  from 
the  veranda  of  the  farmhouse 
where  I  was  stopping  and,  crossing  the 
green  sward,  passed  under  a  rustic  arch, 
covered  with  a  tangled  mass  of  morn- 
ing glories,  clematis  and  cinnamon 
roses,  and  entered  the  little  box- 
bordered  garden,  brilliant  in  color  and 
fragrant  with  the  perfume  of  helio- 
trope and  mignonette.  It  was  a  quaint, 
little,  old-fashioned  garden,  one  of  the 


type  beloved  and  chenshed  in  colonial 
days,  but  rarely  met  with  now.  As  I 
stood  in  the  midst  of  its  quiet  beauty, 
I  could  not  help  contrasting  it  with 
the  elaborately  designed  gardens  of  the 
favorites  of  fortune,  where,  under  the 
direct  supervision  of  a  landscape  ar- 
chitect, they  assume  larger  and  more 
symmetrical  proportions.  The  old- 
time  garden  border  of  box  has  been 
replaced  by  borders  of  sward  and 
flowers   and,   cleverly   introduced,   are 


443 


444 


THE    BOSTON    COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


fragments  of  marble,  sun  dials  and 
fountains.  The  simple  gardens  of  our 
grandmothers'  time  have  been  super- 
seded by  old  world  creations. 

Fifty  years  ago  there  were  practically 
no  summer  homes,  for  people,  whose 
lives  had  been  spent  in  the  confines  of 
the  city,  did  not,  as  they  do  today, 
realize  the  health-giving  effects  of  life 
in  the  open.  The  growth  of  this  idea, 
while  it  has  been  gradual,  has  been 
steady,  so  that  the  present  generation, 
with  interests  centered  in  tennis,  boat- 
ing, golf  and  gardening,  has  obtained 
a  broader  conception  of  the  beauties 
of  nature.  Some  special  feature  of  life 
in  the  open  is  each  year  being  devel- 
oped, and  the  constantly  growing  de- 
sire for  out-of-door  exercises  has  caused 
the  summer  home  to  be  an  all-year- 
round  one.  The  country  has  its  own 
individual  charm,  at  each  season  of  the 
year,  which  fact  is  fast  leaving  its  im- 
pression on  the  mind  of  the  summer 
guest. 

The  large  estates,  once  farm  land, 
meadow,  swamp  or  forest,  have   been 


reclaimed;  trees  and  shrubs  have  been 
set  out,  and  fences,  walls,  paths  and 
rocks  have  been  beautified  by  border- 
ings  of  flowers.  Each  owner  has  his 
own  ideas  as  to  how  his  grounds  shall 
be  laid  out.  On  some  estates,  the 
garden  is  the  principal  feature,  while, 
on  others,  the  woodland  has  received 
first  consideration. 

Many  pretty  ideas  are  noted  on  the 
now  famous  North  Shore  estates.  On 
one,  the  wood-paths  are  defined  by 
flowers,  which  give  to  them  their 
names.  On  another,  Scotch  heather  has 
been  allowed  to  thrive  unmolested. 
This  heather  was  transported  to  this 
country  from  Scotland,  many  years  ago. 
It  took  kindly  to  its  new  home,  spread 
rapidly,  and  now  covers  a  large  area 
of  the  woodland  portion  of  the  estate. 
English  ivy  is  very  hardy,  resists  the 
cold  of  the  New  England  winter,  and 
has  been  employed  as  a  decorative 
feature  on  many  estates.  The  wild 
garden,  too,  is  a  prominent  feature. 
Here  are  found  groups  of  wild  flowers 
and  rockeries,  and  often  a  vine-covered 


From  a  Garden  at  Beverly  Cove 


A  GARDEN  STUDY 


445 


A  Walk  in  a  Garden  at  Danvers 


arbor;  the  aim  of  the  owner  being  to 
have  it  appear  natural,  and  not  ar- 
tificiah  Rhododendrons  and  azahas, 
during  the  season  of  their  blossoming, 
make  fine  bits  of  landscape  harmony. 
Tall  yew  hedges,  after  the  fashion  of 
English  estates,  are  often  introduced 
as  features,  and  grow  to  a  height  of 
from  fifteen  to  twenty  feet.  In  some 
cases,  these  outline  the  four  sides  of 
the  garden,  and  serve  as  protection 
walls  for  the  tender  plants  within. 

At  Dreamwold,  Thomas  W.  Lawson's 
estate  at  Cohasset,  Massachusetts,  is  a 
most  interesting  garden,  one  part  of 
which  has  been  set  aside  for  the  pleas- 
ure of  his  youngest  daughter,  the  pet 
of  the  household.  So  interested  has 
she  become  in  this  little  plot  that  dur- 
ing the  summer  season  the  principal 
part  of  her  time  is  devoted  to  its  culti- 
vation. Every  seed  is  planted  by  her 
own  hand,  and  she  can  tell  you  glibly 
the  names  and  varieties  of  every  flower 
or  plant  to  be  found,  not  only  in  her 


own  garden,  but  in  her  father's.  Many 
gardens  have  the  summer  house  or 
pergola  as  a  central  feature,  but  in  the 
case  of  the  Lawson  garden,  it  has  taken 
the  form  of  a  rustic  bungalow,  built  on 
the  crest  of  the  hill,  overlooking  the 
garden.  Across  the  front  is  a  rustic 
veranda,  shaded  with  a  covering  of 
vines  and  rambler  roses,  and  inside  this 
effective  setting  are  many  charming 
pieces  of  colonial  furniture.  This  spot 
is  reserved  for  Mr.  Lawson's  own  en- 
joyment, and,  during  the  season  at 
Dreamwold,  not  a  day  passes  but  that 
some  of  his  time  is  devoted  to  work  in 
the  garden.  Visitors  may  wander  as 
they  will  over  the  rest  of  the  estate, 
but  this  small  area  is  held  sacred  for 
the  members  of  the  family. 

American  gardens  exclude,  as  a  rule, 
the  great  wealth  of  hardy  plants,  con- 
fining the  number  to  a  few  only,  while 
the  more  tender  ones  are  to  be  seen 
everywhere.  They  are  set  out  in  stere- 
otype plans  and  in  ribbon  beds.     There 


446 


THE    BOSTON    COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


is  nothing  more  charming  than  the 
early  garden,  when,  with  the  disap- 
pearing of  the  snow,  the  snow-drop, the 


are  in  blossom,  the  grounds  are  thrown 
open  to  friends  and  visitors,  and  the 
display  is  unequalled  in  America. 


General  View  of  a  Garden  at  Prides  Crossing 


crocus  and  the  hyacinth  burst  forth 
from  their  winter  beds  and  delight  the 
eye  with  their  wealth  of  exquisite 
coloring. 

One  of  the  most  beautiful  early  gar- 
dens in  New  England  is  to  be  found  at 
Brookline,  Mass.,  at  the  home  of  Pro- 
fessor Sargent,  Director  of  the  Arnold 
Arboretum.  Here,  at  Holm  Lea,  as 
the  estate  is  known,  he  devotes  his  at- 
tention principally  to  the  earlier  va- 
rieties. His  grounds,  during  the  en- 
tire season,  are  crowded  with  visitors, 
who  delight  in  the  wealth  of  blossoms 
found  around  the  large  pond  at  the 
foot  of  the  hill,  over  whose  surface 
droop  Chinese  lilacs  and  other  flower- 
ing trees  and  shrubs.  At  one  side  is  a 
large  and  extensive  rockery,  with  its 
many  wood  flowers,  while  Chinese  and 
Japanese  iris  are  found  in  great  pro- 
fusion. The  lawn  mower  rolls  relent- 
lessly over  the  dainty  marguerite,  in 
cutting  the  sward.  During  the  month 
of  June,  roses  are  found  everywhere. 
While  the  rhododendrons  and  azalias 


Within  the  last  few  years  rambler 
roses  have  come  much  into  evidence, 
and  do  a  great  deal  in  forming  the 
decorative  scheme  in  every  well  ap- 
pointed garden.  The  iris,  too,  is  a 
flower  that  is  much  in  favor.  We  can 
not  wonder  at  this,  for  surely  there  is 
nothing  more  beautiful  than  the  ex- 
quisite coloring  of  its  blossoms.  There 
is  the  Japanese  and  the  German  iris; 
the  former  differs  from  the  latter  in 
that  it  has  a  broader,  blade-like  leaf, 
while  its  flowers  have  three  strongly 
recurved  and  handsomely  marked 
petals,  and  three  upright  standards. 
These  are  not  so  impressively  magnifi- 
cent as  are  those  of  the  Japanese  iris, 
but  they  are  very  beautiful,  and  their 
colors  include  white,  yellow,  lilac  and 
purple,  with  some  very  queer  browns, 
and  rich  velvety  maroons,  combined 
with  yellows,  which  give  to  them  a  very 
unusual  and  harmonious  effect. 

The  flowers  of  the  present  time  can 
be  divided  into  two  great  classes  —  the 
annuals,  which  last  only  for  the  season, 


A  GARDEN  STUDY 


447 


and  the  perennials,  which  come  up 
anew  every  year.  Around  every  old 
homestead  we  find  the  latter,  unsur- 
passed in  luxuriance  and  wealth  of 
coloring.  These  are  intermingled  with 
annuals,  which  are  in  many  cases  pur- 
chased through  catalogs,  and  when  in 
blossom  make  an  effect  not  always 
harmonious. 

One  of  the  most  attractive  of  displays 
comes  from  the  wild  garden  seeds.  I 
planted  a  triangle  full  of  these  seeds 
one  year,  and  the  whole  season  it  was 
a  perfect  delight,  for  each  day  new 
and  unexpected  flowers  opened.  There 
were  poppies  of  every  kind,  from  the 
Shirley  poppy  to  the  double,  snapdrag- 
ons of  every  shade,  and  multitudinous 
other  flowering  plants.  Our  summer 
home  was  on  the  rocky  shores  of  Mar- 
blehead,  and  from  the  lawn  great  rocks 
were  taken  to  make  it  smooth.  These 
rocks  were  piled  at  one  side  into  a 
great  rockery,  and  around  them  was 
planted  a  circle  of  scarlet-and-white 
double  poppies.  The  next  season,  from 
the  seeds  dropped,  came  up  quantities 
of  new  plants  which,  when  in  bloom. 


The  stately  gardens  of  the  present 
period  are  of  the  type  known  as  formal. 
The  garden  plat  is  laid  out  in  formal 
paths,  with  a  sun-dial  or  fountain  as 
its  central  feature.  The  ingenuity  of 
the  gardener  is  taxed  to  the  utmost  to 
introduce  new  and  rare  plants,  which, 
when  combined  with  the  old-fashioned 
favorites,  will  make  an  artistic  and 
pleasing  effect. 

Italian  gardens  are  becoming  more 
and  more  a  feature  of  large  estates. 
Here  may  be  found  fragments  of  Ve- 
netian marble,  old  Italian  vases,  foun- 
tains, surmounted  with  rare  bits  of 
sculpture,  and  old-time  relics,  brought 
from  foreign  lands. 

There  is  generally  more  sward  than 
flowers  in  the  Italian  garden,  but  an 
exception  to  this  rule  is  found  at  Weld. 
Here  Capt.  Larz  Anderson,  of  Brook- 
line,  has  made  flow^ers  the  dominant 
part,  instead  of  a  mere  incident.  This 
garden  is  undoubtedly  the  finest  in  the 
United  States,  and  was  designed  by  the 
well-known  architect,  Mr.  Charles  A. 
Piatt,  who  has  given  the  most  careful 
attention  to  every  portion  of  its  con- 


Near  View  of  a  Garden  at  Manchester 


showed  all  the  shades  of  purple,  from 
the  almost  black  to  the  lightest  laven- 
der, and  often,  from  actual  count, 
there  were  a  thousand  in  bloom  at  once. 


struction.  Here  are  found  fountains, 
pergolas,  ludovisi  jars,  wells  and  ever- 
greens. The  arrangement  of  the  flow- 
ers is  such  that  thev  follow  themselves 


448 


THE    BOSTON    COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


in  unbroken  succession.  Even  in  the 
month  of  October  there  is  no  lack  of 
flowering  plants. 

Japanese  gardens  are  always  of  in- 
terest with  their  quaint  little  stone 
lanterns,  rising,  about  three  feet  in 
height,  their  ponds  and  their  Japanese 
flowers. 

A  good  result  is  obtained  in  every 
garden  by  the  introduction  of  water, 
either  in  the  form  of  a  brook,  a  pond, 
or  a  fountain.  A  small  garden  need 
not  lack  this  accompaniment,  for  it  is 
not  a  hard  matter  to  excavate  a  hollow 
and  form  a  small  pond  in  which  can 
be  planted  lily  roots  and  aquatic 
plants.  The  owner  of  a  small  estate 
can  arrange  his  flower  bed  with  just 
as   much    symmetry,    and    can    obtain 


fully  as  good  results  as  can  his  neigh- 
bor across  the  way  from  his  magnifi- 
cent formal  or  Italian  garden.  There 
is  no  reason  why  everybody  should  not 
be  able  to  have  a  little  flower  bed,  and 
the  making  of  a  garden  is  full  of  inter- 
est, and  at  the  same  time  it  is  most 
healthful  work.  It  is  a  fact  well  known 
that  many  a  tired  feeling  of  worry  has 
been  buried  deep  in  Mother  Earth. 

It  is  a  mistaken  idea  to  believe  that 
only  to  the  rich  is  the  privilege  of  a 
garden  accorded.  In  these  days,  flower 
seeds  can  be  purchased  very  cheap, 
and  no  summer  home  or  country  farm- 
house, without  a  garden  plat,  is  com- 
plete. It  gives  the  needed  touch  of 
beauty,  and  well  repays  the  amount  of 
time  and  money  expended  upon  it. 


The  Plan 

By  Pauline  Carrington  Bouve 

Oh,  little  star,  so  very  high. 

Oh,  little  flower,  so  low. 
From  the  great  loving  source  of  life 

You  each  of  you  doth  flow. 
The  star  burns  from  the  sun's  great  heart 
Up  from  the  soil  the  flower  doth  start ; 
Each  hath  its  place  on  Nature's  chart, 

An  order  rules  that  all  things  show. 


Oh,  magnate,  in  your  high  estate. 

Oh,  humble  life,  bereft  of  grace, 
In  the  long  lines  of  battle  rank 

Each  of  you  has  some  place. 
Each  wins  or  loses  in  that  span 
Set  ere  the  fight  for  every  man  — 
Both  gain  and  loss  are  in  the  plan. 

But  God's  love  shines  throughout  all  space. 


The  Soup  Bed  and  the  Flower  Hedge 


By  Evelyn  Prince  Cahoon 


CLOSE  to  the  back'  porch,  just 
under  the  kitchen  window,  is  the 
"soup  bed." 

A  space  hardly  two  yards  long  and 
not  quite  a  yard  wide  is  dug  up  and 
enriched  each  year.  A  few  plants  grow 
there  perennially,  but  the  larger  part 
of  the  space  is  left  for  the  planting  of 
those  vegetables  needed  frequently, 
and  in  variety,  for  soups  and  for 
flavoring. 

"I  don't  want  to  buy  a  pound  or 
two,  each,  of  turnips,  carrots,  parsnips, 
onions  and  what  not,  in  order  to  get 
just  the  one  vegetable  of  each  kind 
that  I  need  for  my  soup.  Nor  do  I 
wish  to  see  the  new  clerk  at  the  grocery 
stare,  when  I  say  I  want  a  bunch  of 
'soup  vegetables.'  They  wouldn't  do 
so  abroad,  but  they  do  here,  for  they 
don't  know  what  I  mean." 

She  says  she  can  easily  raise  what 
she  wants  for  such  things,  and  so  have 
them  fresh,  in  greater  variety,  and 
within  reach,  no  matter  what  sudden 
change  of  plan  may  be  made,  — -  "and 
then  it's  a  pleasure  to  raise  them,  too." 

So,  each  year,  long  before  any  one 
else  has  thought  of  making  a  garden, 
the  mistress  is  poking  and  jabbing  at 
the  soil,  planting  onion,  carrot,  parsnip 
and  radish  seed  in  very  tiny  drills,  only 
six  inches  apart,  across  the  bed,  and 
then  scattering  rape  seed  broadcast 
over  the  whole  thing. 

I  ought  to  have  mentioned  that  she 
plants  Tom  Thumb  peas  lengthwise 
along  the  back  edge  of  the  bed.  They 
never  grow  more  than  eight  inches 
high  and,  being  at  the  back  of  the  bed, 
shade  nothing  else.  Peas  must  have 
the  cold  weather  of  early  spring,  and  as 
this  variety  fruits  in  about  six  weeks, 
the  plants  are  safely  out  of  the  way 
before  the  space  is  needed  for  any- 
thing else. 


The  rape  seed  she  scatters  simply 
because  it  is  a  vigorous  grower  that 
likes  the  cold  and  comes  up  early 
and,  being  of  the  cabbage  family,  is 
very  nice  in  the  first  few  weeks  of  its 
existence,  pulled,  like  spinach,  for  greens, 
and  it  has  all  been  used  by  the  time  the 
regular  garden  making  is  on  in  full 
force.  A  very  little  of  it  is  planted 
from  time  to  time  right  along  until 
fall,  so  there  may  always  be  a  little  for 
soup  flavoring,  instead  of  cabbage,  if 
that  is  not  at  hand. 

The  plan  is  to  plant  the  vegetables 
in  rows  close  together  in  such  order 
that,  as  one  row  is  used  up,  it  may 
give  needed  space  to  plants  in  the  row 
next  it.  But  this  applies  only  to  the 
later  part,  of  the  summer,  for  while  the 
plants  are  all  small  and  need  only 
narrow  fee  ding- ground,  as  fast  as  a 
vegetable  is  pulled  for  soup  or  salad, 
another  seed  is  slily  poked  into  the 
ground  in  the  place  of  its  roots,  and 
so  a  succession  of  growth  is  maintained. 

The  very  young  roots  are  the  ones 
wanted.  Carrots  two  inches  and  a 
half  long,  radishes  half  an  inch  in  dia- 
meter (they  are  a  substitute  for  tur- 
nips in  flavor  for  soup  in  the  spring), 
and  onions  no  larger  than  respectable 
hat-pins  are  more  delicate  in  texture 
and  flavor  than  full  grown  ones. 

Lettuce  (of  the  head  variety)  she 
plants  —  not  for  soup,  but  because  it 
is  so  useful  for  garnishing  and  easily 
made  salads. 

One  plant  (or  possibly  two)  of  sage, 
thyme,  sweet  marjoram,  summer  sa- 
vory, parsley  and  caraway  take  up 
some  room  at  one  end  of  the  bed,  the 
rest  is  planted  in  rows  in  the  following 
order: 

First  Row.  Radish,  followed  by  rad- 
ish until  the  next  row  overshadows. 

Second  Row.     Carrots,  as  each  root 


449 


450 


THE   BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


is  withdrawn,  it  is  replaced  by  a 
carrot  seed  until  early  summer. 

Third  Row.  Head  lettuce.  Row 
left  vacant  after  lettuce  is  grown  and 
used. 

Fourth  Row.  Turnips,  replaced  by 
turnips,  all  season. 

Fifth  Row.  Seed  onion,  used  while 
very  young,  replaced  early  by  radish. 

Sixth  Row.  Head  lettuce,  leaving 
room  when  used  for  the  next  row, 
parsnips. 

Seventh  Row.  Pepper  grass  or  cress, 
eaten  soon  and  giving  room  for  the 
overshadowing  parsnips. 

Eighth  Row.  Onion  sets,  each  place, 
as  vacated,  filled  with  brussels  sprouts. 

Ninth  Row.  Celery,  alternating  with 
kohl-rabi. 

This  arrangement  repeated,  fills  the 
bed,  and  provides  the  desirable  vege- 
tables, from  spring  to  fall,  for  the  daily 
soup  in  endless  variety  of  flavoring. 

The  Flower  Hedge 

There  are  places  where  it  is  not 
advisable  to  have  a  permanent  hedge 
of  trees  or  shrubs,  but  where,  never- 
theless, a  dividing  line  of  some  sort  is 
desirable  and,  when  this  is  so,  nothing 
can  be  much  prettier  than  a  hedge  of 
flowers. 

This  may  be  made  of  perennial 
plants,  dying  down  to  the  root  each 
fall,  but  shooting  forth  each  spring 
raore  vigorously  than  ever,  or  it  may 
be  made  of  those  flowers  which  reach 
their  perfection,  drop  their  seeds  and 
die  in  a  year  —  the  annuals. 

Of  the  perennials,  perhaps,  the  very 
best  for  this  use  are  the  perennial 
phloxes.  They  have  been  improved 
greatly  of  late  years  and  now  every 
color,  even  to  a  pretty  good  blue,  is 
represented.  The  method  of  prepar- 
ing the  ground  for  a  hedge  of  phlox  is 
exactly  the  same  as  for  any  other  plant. 
The  ground  is  spaded,  worked,  ferti- 
lized and,  as  far  as  possible,  powdered 
for  a  width  of  two  feet,  a  depth  of  one, 
and  as  long  as  the  hedge  is  to  be.     It  is 


best  to  set  the  plants  in  the  exact 
middle,  and  then  they  should  be  con- 
fined sternly  between  parallel  wires, 
tightly  strung  on  pegs  driven  into  the 
ground. 

The  characteristic  beauty  of  a  hedge 
is  in  its  exact  symmetry  of  outline. 

The  perennial  phlox  grows  to  about 
three  feet  in  height,  and,  when  the 
hedge  is  made  of  one  variety  alone,  it 
is  most  beautiful. 

The  next  perennial  is  the  Rud- 
beckia  (Golden  Glow),  but  it  must 
never  be  used  unless  height  is  wished, 
as  it  grows  at  least  five  feet,  and  may 
reach  six  or  seven.  It  especially  needs 
close  confining  with  wires,  as  the  blos- 
soms are  very  heavy  and  overweigh 
the  stalks  so  that  in  blossoming  time 
they  droop  badly.  They  continue  in 
great  splendor  of  vivid  yellow  bloom 
for  a  long  time,  but  must  be  cut  away 
immediately  after,  for  their  stalks  are 
unsightly. 

Peonies,  iris  and  yucca,  all  make 
very  beautiful  low  hedges  during  their 
blossoming  time,  but  they  are  not 
attractiA^e  at  other  times.  The  yucca 
has  one  advantage,  though,  in  that  it 
is  an  evergreen. 

Perhaps  the  hardy  chrysanthemum 
is  the  most  generally  attractive  and 
easiest  to  get  and  to  manage.  They 
grow  easily  from  the  merest  slip,  and 
will  stand  cheerfully  almost  any  amount 
of  neglect.  They,  like  the  golden  glow, 
need  close  reining  in. 

The  difficulty  with  chrysanthemums 
is  that  the  more  vigorous  ones  crowd 
out  those  not  so  strong,  or  in  some 
cases  mix  badly.  This  results  some- 
times in  very  pretty  new  varieties,  but 
equally  often  in  the  losing  of  fine  ones. 
The  hollyhock  is  about  the  only 
biennial  worth  anything  as  a  hedge, 
and  it  is  useful  rather  as  a  screen,  it  is 
so  tall.  The  effect  is  always  prettier,  if  all 
colors  are  together.  It  must  be  planted 
during  the  summer  previous  to  the 
one  in  which  it  is  expected  to  blossom. 
August  is  a  good  month  for  the  planting.. 


COOK-BOOKS  AND  INTUITIONS 


451 


Among  the  annuals  the  zinnia  is  the 
favorite.  Its  blossom  is  so  coarse  and 
stiff  that  it  seems  to  be  entirely  unfit 
for  anything  else,  but  when  grown  in 
a  mass,  as  in  a  hedge  and  in  rich  soil, 
it  is  surprising  how  decorative  it  is 
seen  to  be.  Its  firm,  strong  stalks 
make  it  unnecessary  to  wire  up  the 
zinnia  hedge. 

The  annual  chrysanthemum  is  an- 
other handsome  hedge  plant,  especially 
because  of  its  finely  cut,  handsome 
foliage.  It  grows  nearly  three  feet 
high,  and  its  blossoms  are  always 
some  shade  of  yellow  or  cream. 

The     old-fashioned    four-o'clock    is 


another  pretty  flower,  and  is  sturdy 
and  strong  stalked.  Its  colors  are 
red,  pink,  white  and  yellow.  Its  fra- 
grance is  sweet  and  musky  in  the  after- 
noon and  evening^  when  it  opens. 
Uniformity  of  growth  is  one  of  its 
good  points.  The  tobacco  plant  and 
the  canna  make  similar  effect,  both 
being  broad  leafed  and  stately. 

And  then  there  are  the  coarse,  strong, 
but  healthy  and  decorative  old  mari- 
golds; yellow  and  velvety  of  texture 
and  dark  green  of  foliage,  with  almost 
infinite  variety  of  marking  in  the 
flowers.  There  is  no  .yellow  hedge  to 
compare  with  these. 


Cook-books  and  Intuitions 

By  Kate  Gannett  Wells 


JUST  because  housekeeping  has 
never  been  considered  a  science 
until  lately,  has  it  suffered  from 
the  inexperience  of  conscientious 
women,  whose  morals  were  stronger 
than  their  culinary  achievements.  Now, 
per  contra,  housekeeping  is  becoming 
so  organized  and  trained  that  there  is 
fear  lest  home  life  become  engulfed  in 
State  nurseries  and  caravansaries,  and 
that  what  is  called  cooperative  house- 
keeping may  take  the  place  of  indi- 
vidual homes. 

As  against  such  a  dilemma  is  the 
remedy  of  being  in  love  all  the  time 
with  one's  husband,  which  acts  as  a 
continuous,  effective  aversion  to  shar- 
ing home  life  with  any  cooperative 
housings  or  persons.  Yet  if  one  is 
husbandless  and  wants  people  around, 
then  take  to  cooperation.  Only  it  is 
a  sad  fact  that,  if  a  woman  is  eighty, 
or  even  seventy  years  of  age,  nobody 
wants  to  cooperate  with  her,  when  it 
is  just  the  time  she  most  needs  to  be 
cooperated  with  and  to  lead  at  least 
a  makeshift  for  a  dual  family  life. 


Whichever  way  one  adopts,  indi- 
vidual housekeeping  as  a  pas  seul,  or 
a  cooperative  establishment  as  a  kind 
of  square  dance,  one  must  largely  rely 
on  cook-books  for  the  successful  mak- 
ing of  dishes.  Thus  do  recipes  be- 
come an  important  element  in  preserv- 
ing the  singleness-of-heart  point  of 
view.  Cook-books  for  mothers,  public 
school  domestic  science  for  children, 
and  love  stories  that  turn  on  some 
culinary  success  for  jaded,  heart-sore 
magazine  readers,  are  creating  a  wide 
circle  of  home  efficiency  as  offset  to 
primaeval  and  hereditary  intuitions 
about  bread  making  and  pies.  Any 
woman  today  can  make  and  keep  a 
home,  even  in  a  closet,  if  she  applies 
herself  but  cursorily  to  domestic 
science. 

Of  course,  there  always  will  be  women 
who  scorn  printed  directions,  preferring 
to  rely  on  their  intuitions,  memory, 
and  muscular  arm  power.  But  in  re- 
futation of  such  women,  who  here  and 
there  sniff  at  the  cook-books  in  travel- 
ing libraries,  is  the  record  of  the  John 


452 


THE   BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


Crerar  Library  in  Chicago,  There, 
under  the  head  of  Apphed  Sciences, 
are  1,031  volumes  on  "domestic  econ- 
omy," that  last  year  were  called  for 
1,137  times,  which  is  about  double  the 
average  that  other  scientific  books  were 
taken  out.  Scientific  training  against 
intuitive  housekeeping! 

As  further  encouragement  to  the 
student  of  housekeeping  is  the  num- 
ber of  hours,  forty-seven  per  cent, 
which  is  given  to  domestic  art  and 
science  courses  in  the  Normal  schools 
of  the  Middle  West  and  in  Ontario. 
The  East,  however,  leads  in  the  train- 
ing of  pupils  as  teachers  in  Household 
Arts,  the  Normal  School  at  Framing- 
ham,  Mass.,  probably  exceeding  all 
other  schools  in  the  amount  of  time 
required,  three  years,  for  such  course. 
None  too  long  a  period,  however,  if 
one  has  relied  on  intuition  only,  since 
the  future  health  of  our  population 
depends  largely  on  the  spread  of  do- 
mestic science.  Not  candies  but  Ham- 
burg steaks  give  strength,  candies 
being  an  artificial  solace. 

It  was,  not  is,  the  condition  of  bake 
shops  in  many  cities,  which  gave  an 
impetus  to  cookery  in  public  schools. 
If  "consumptive  cooks"  were  not 
wanted  in  private  homes,  still  less  were 
they  desired  in  shops.  So  bakeries  are 
gradually  becoming  licensed,  while  the 
health  of  the  employees  is  being  re- 
garded as  much  as  the  cleanliness  of 
the  shops. 

Yet,  in  statistics  on  this  subject  for 
the  whole  country,  it  is  computed  that 
only  in  fifty-five  schools  is  cookery 
taught  as  a  "Branch,"  the  word 
schools  probably  applying  to  groups 
of  schools,  which  would  increase  the 
number  indefinitely.  In  one  school 
"Baking,"  and  in  another  "House- 
keeping," is  reckoned  as  a  Branch. 
At  least  recognition  to  be  considered 
as  a  "Branch"  is  better  than  being 
totally  ignored.  But  then  it  takes  a 
long  time  for  any  kind  of  domestic 
work  to  be  ranked  as  scientific ;  witness 


the  Nurses  Training  Schools.  In  1880 
there  were  but  fifteen  in  the  country; 
in  1906  there  were  974.  After  all  it 
depends  upon  how  it  is  done,  whether 
or  not  domestic  knowledge  can  ever  be 
scientific. 

To  say  that  public  school  instruction 
dignifies  such  training  is  mere  accep- 
tance of  a  truism,  and  that  it  will 
eventually  supplant  intuitigns  as  to 
the  whys  and  wherefores  of  cookery  is 
taken  as  a  matter  of  course.  Yet  now 
that  fashionable,  private  schools  are 
adding  cookery  to  their  subjects,  there 
is  fresh  hope  for  the  continuance  of  in- 
dividual homes,  as  "society"  girls  will 
know  something  about  the  art  of  keep- 
ing them. 

Still  no  domestic  science  training  and 
no  cook-book  recipes  can  supplant 
kindly  intuitions  in  the  art  of  making 
maids  happy,  intelligent  or  serviceable. 
We  are  either  too  easy  or  too  strict 
with  them  and  they  themselves  have 
not  the  knack,  which,  it  is  said,  French 
cooks,  maids  and  butlers  possess,  of 
appearing  to  take  an  intimate  interest 
in  their  employers'  affairs.  It  is  re- 
lated that,  when  the  first  Ladies'  Club 
was  opened  on  the  banks  of  the  Seine, 
its  houseporter  was  filled  with  amaze- 
ment, almost  with  incredulity,  at  the 
freedom  of  Anglo-Saxon  manners,  but 
never  for  a  moment  did  he  allow  his 
astonishment  to  be  seen  or  felt. 

Just  how  domestic  problems  are  to 
be  adjusted  between  intuitions,  of  all 
sorts,  and  arbitrary  scientific  training, 
is  a  matter  to  be  settled  by  tempera- 
ment as  much  as  by  social  research, 
which  by  its  very  name  suggests  prying 
into  other  people's  affairs.  A  central 
house,  where  maids  lived,  going  out  to 
their  work  for  long  or  short  day  periods, 
did  not  prove  an  effective  solution  in 
Boston,  though  in  theory  or  principle 
it  was  excellent. 

Unfortunately  some  private  homes 
call  to  mind  the  "living-in"  system  of 
English  shop  girls,  who  are  compelled 
in  many  places  to  live  in  houses  pro- 


A  COMMITTEE  OF  INQUIRY 


463 


vided  for  them,  which  may  or  may  not 
have  "recreation  rooms,"  and  where 
usually  girls  are  not  permitted  to  or- 
nament their  half  or  third  of  a  bed- 
room with  even  their  mother's  photo- 
graph. Under  the  annoying  chaper- 
onage  of  such  minute  regulations,  they 
envy  the  sociability  of  the  men  and 
boys,  who,  as  soon  as  their  day's  shop 
work  is  ended,  live  and  do  as  they 
please. 

Even  more  than  in  cookery  does  in- 
tuition in  the  employer  need  to  be  sup- 
planted by  scientific  training  in  giving 
employees  their  rights.  The  best  part 
of  any  training  for  housekeeping  in- 
cludes domestic  art,  with  domestic 
science  cookery,  furnishings,  sanitation 
and  millinery,  a  kind  of  union  of  craft 
(cookery)  and  art  (the  house  beauti- 
ful), which  will  make  intuitions  sane 
and  progressive.  At  the  Horological 
Department  of  the  Bradley  Polytech- 
nic Institute  at  Peoria,  111.,  no  credit 
is  given  a  man  for  knowing  how  to 
make  a  piece  until  he  has  actually 
made  it  unaided.  Should  it  not  be 
the  same  with  the  housekeeper  and 
her  employee  ?  so  that  each  should  be 
able  to  do  the  work,  and  that  one 
should  keep  house,  not  solely  on  the 
"living-in"  system  nor  on  the  hour 
basis,  but  that  theoretically  one  should 


constantly  be  able  to  put  herself  in 
another's  place,  the  ability  to  do  it 
not  always  necessitating  the  doing. 

The  weaker  are  intuitions,  the  more 
do  older  women  need  cook-books  and 
their  children  school  training  in  do- 
mestic science.  The  daintier  are  the 
cook-books,  the  more  are  kindly  in- 
tuitions needed  to  make  home  cheerful, 
when  expensive  dishes  cannot  be  af- 
forded. And  all  the  time  is  it  true 
that  love,  without  good  cooking,  lacks 
a  physical  basis,  and  that  nourishing 
food ,  without  the  art  of  making  it  look 
attractive,  is  somewhat  devitalizing. 
The  photographic  illustrations  of  rec- 
ipes are  better  helps  towards  the  ar- 
tistic setting  forth  of  foods  than  the 
elaborate  toilets  of  those  who  preside 
at  a  dinner,  especially  if  they  have  not 
the  charm  of  sympathetic  intuitions. 

Just  as  long  as  temperament  aids 
nutrition,  as  it  always  does,  will  the 
secret  of  effective  housekeeping  be 
found  in  knowing  how  to  manage  one's 
temper  and  to  meet  emergencies;  in 
keeping  up  repair  work  (left  overs  for 
lunches),  rather  than  by  new  inven- 
tions (receipts) ,  and  in  finding  use  for 
the  "waste"  in  our  foods  and  in  our 
emotions,  a  "waste"  that  is  always 
accumulating  from  the  high  tension  in 
which  we  live,  —  so  needlessly. 


A  Committee  of  Inquiry 

By  Helen  Campbell

F  it  were  anything  but  Spanish," 
the  girl  said.  "We've  tried 
French,  of  course,  and  Italian 
that  I  like  better,  and  Hungarian, 
Russian, — in  short,  everything  there 
is  but  Spanish,  but  somehow  I  don't 
feel  drawn  to  that  kind." 

"That's  a  singular  conclusion  for 
one  who  announced  she  meant  to  get 
at  the  bottom  facts  about  the  special 


dishes  of  every  nation.  Why  do  you 
draw  the  line  at  Spain  ? ' ' 

"Oh,  I  don't  know,  exactty.  It 
must  be  because  of  Fox's  Book  of 
Martyrs.  I  can't  think  of  any  other 
reason. " 

"What  can  you  mean?"  and,  "Yes, 
what  can  you  mean,  sure  enough?" 
came  from  one  and  another  of  the  little 
group    in  '  the    studio    ready    for    the 


454 


THE   BOSTON    COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


weekly  trip  to  foreign  restaurants 
which  served  the  greatest  number  of 
dishes  unknown  to  the  average  Amer- 
ican." 

"Why,  it's  this,"  the  charming  girl 
went  on,  flushing  a  little  as  she  found 
each  pair  of  eyes  focused  upon  her. 
"I  used  to  read  the  terrible  old  book 
long  ago  at  an  old  house  in  the  country 
where  we  staid  one  summer,  and  some- 
how it  was  the  Spanish  Inquisition  and 
the  things  they  did  to  poor  martyrs 
that  got  into  my  head,  so  that  I  had 
horrible  nightmares,  and  acquired  what 
I  suppose  you  will  consider  a  prejudice. 
Of  course  I  am  going  with  you.  We 
settled  to  do  the  thing  thoroughly. 
I  only  felt  so  for  a  minute,  you  know," 
and  now  she  settled  her  hat  and  fol- 
lowed the  leader  down  the  long  flights 
of  stairs,  the  climbing  of  which  the 
young  doctor  in  the  party  declared  to 
be  one  of  the  best  exercises  that  could 
be  taken,  provided  they  were  mounted 
by  holding  the  body  perfectly  erect 
and  letting  the  foot  be  quite  flat  on 
each  stair. 

"It's  just  as  the  Italian  officers  teach 
the  soldiers  to  climb  mountains,"  he 
instructed  a  newcomer.  "Eyes  right, 
head  up,  chest  free  and  easy  breaths, 
and  the  foot  set  down  flat  and  firm  yet 
light,  and  up  you  go!  I'll  show  you 
when  we  come  back,  that  is,  if  you  do 
come  back,  and  if  not  today,  why  then 
any  day  that  we  happen  to  think  of  it 
again.  In  the  meantime,  if  we  do  not 
go  faster  there  will  not  be  a  place  left." 
And  now  he  hurried  them  down  a  side 
street,  and  through  a  long  and  narrow 
passage-way,  to  a  large  room  at  the 
back,  a  long  table  at  one  side  and  other 
smaller  ones  taking  the  remainder  of 
the  space,  a  counter  at  the  end  on 
which  were  cold  dishes  of  various  or- 
ders, while  from  the  kitchen  beyond 
came  savory  odors. 

"This  is  almuerzo,  otherwise,  de- 
jeuner a  la  fourchette,  otherwise  lunch- 
eon," said  their  guide.  "All  the  na- 
tives   have    had    desayuno,    which    is 


coffee  and  rolls,  early  this  morning, 
but  you  will  see  now  that  numbers  of 
them  will  order  what  we  are  going  to 
try,  —  Huevos  Malaguena,  which  is 
Cuban  as  well  as  old  Spanish.  You 
see  it  is  en  casserole,  served  in  the  dish 
they  are  cooked  in,  and  the  other  day 
they  let  me  see  just  how  they  were 
done  so  that  I  went  home  and  tried  it 
in  the  chafing  dish  with  distinguished 
success.  But  the  thick  casserole  is 
the  real  thing,  after  all.  Here  comes 
ours." 

"Tell  us  how  it  is  done,"  one  of  the 
party  said  after  a  critical  tasting  of 
the  compound  and  a  nod  of  approval, 
at  which  the  serious-faced  waiter  smiled 
happily,  as  if  he  himself  were  the 
author. 

"First,  the  best  of  olive  oil,  not  less 
than  half  an  inch  or  so  in  the  casserole, 
and  it  must  be  bubbling  hot.  Then 
into  that  go  your  eggs,  dropped  gently, 
to  which  you  add  at  once,  say  for  half 
a  dozen  eggs,  a  pint  of  shrimps,  a  quart 
of  French  peas,  a  heaped  tablespoon- 
ful  of  capers,  and  keep  it  all  on  the 
fire  just  long  enough  for  the  eggs  to  be 
poached  to  a  turn,  soft  or  harder  as 
your  taste  may  be.  Season,  then,  with 
salt,  pepper  —  cayenne,  preferably,  or 
white  pepper  and  paprika.  Simple, 
you  see,  but  good,  isn't  it?"  and  all 
the  heads  nodded  approval. 

"The  next  specialty  here,"  their 
guide  said  as  they  took  up  the  bill  of 
fare,  "is  polio;  that's  chicken,  you 
know,  —  polio  catalana,  —  another  cas- 
serole thing,  really  a  kind  of  brown 
fricassee  of  chicken  and  a  rich  brown 
sauce  in  which  they  put  mushrooms, 
peas  and  pimentos,  with  chopped 
parsley  sprinkled  over  it.  That  and 
a  sweet  omelet  ought  to  do  today, 
though  you've  had  eggs  already,  and 
perhaps  you'd  better  try  chocolate  and 
their  delicious  little  cakes.  There  are 
ices,  too." 

The  waiter  who  had  disappeared  as 
the  doctor  said  polio  catalana,  appeared 
now,  with  another  casserole,  from  which 


A  COMMITTEE  OF  INQUIRY 


455 


lie  proceeded  to  serve  beautifully 
browned  portions  of  chicken,  smothered 
in  the  rich  brown  sauce  and,  quite  on 
his  own  account,  with  a  conviction  that 
these  young  people  should  be  taught 
how  to  order  properly,  he  had  added  a 
dish  of  arroz  amarillo,  which  is  merely 
boiled  rice  with  a  bit  of  saffron  in  the 
water  in  which  it  is  boiled,  and  which 
colors  it  a  charming  yellow. 

"Why  doesn't  our  chicken  ever  taste 
like  this? "  the  prettiest  girl  complained. 

"Because* we  don't  understand  the 
virtues  of  the  casserole,"  said  the  doc- 
tor. "Slow  cooking,  only  a  suspicion 
of  water  to  start  things  and  the  juice 
of  the  meat  doing  duty  instead.  They 
do  veal  in  the  same  way,  so  it  can 
hardly  be  told  from  chicken,  and  their 
famous  olla  podrida  simmers  over  just 
a  few  bits  of  charcoal  and  is  as  much 
ahead  of  even  the  best  New  England 
baked  beans  as  it's  possible  to  imagine. 
I've  eaten  it  in  South  America,  and 
know.  And  then  there's  bacalao.  If 
it  were  Friday  now,  we'd  have  bacalao." 

"It  sounds  like  a  musical  dish, — 
those  long-drawn  vowels.  It  ought  to 
be  good,  but  what  is  it?" 

"Turn  around  and  you  will  see." 

The  girl  turned  to  discover,  in  the 
restaurant  window,  something  like  a 
m^ammoth  dried  codfish. 

"That's  a  Norway  cod.  The  Span- 
ish won't  have  any  other,  if  they  can 
help  it,  and  I  have  found  out  how  to 
handle  them  or  it,  for  you  can  buy  a 
pound,  if  you  like.  That's  what  I  did, 
after  instructions.  First  it  is  soaked 
some  hours,  then  you  boil  it  twenty 
minutes,  and  break  it  into  flakes.  Then 
you  fry  an  onion  minced  fine  in  olive 
oil,  put  in  a  mere  dash  of  cinnamon 
and  some  cayenne  to  taste,  and  a  quart 
of  well-stewed  tomatoes  already  sea- 
soned;, stir  your  flakes  into  this  and 
put  all  into  a  thick  earthen  baking 
dish,  such  as  you  see  over  there  on 
the  long  table.  Cover  the  top  with 
slices  of  buttered  bread,  and  bake 
about    twenty    minutes;    long    enough 


for  all  to  blend  well.  That's  bacalao," 
and  the  speaker  smacked  his  lips  and 
went  on  with  his  chicken,  but  paused. 

"There  goes  a  sweet  omelet,  now," 
he  said  as  a  waiter  hurried  by  and  de- 
posited it  on  a  table  near  them.  "See ? 
He  has  lighted  the  rum,  just  enough 
of  it  for  a  little  blaze.  It's  peach;  just 
good  canned  peaches  broken  up  and 
heated  and  a  touch  of  sherry  or  rum. 
Sometimes  there  are  four  fruits  added 
in  bits;  pineapple,  bananas  and  figs. 
It's  fine!  But  the  swell  thing,  the  real 
Spanish  thing  is  guayaba  paste,  a  form 
of  guava  jelly  or  marmalade,  for  it's 
thick  and  firm,  and  you  take  it  as 
crema  de  guayaba,  which  means  that  it 
is  served  on  a  thin  slice  or  a  little  block 
of  cream  cheese,  and  over  it  is  poured 
a  thick  syrup  of  sugar  and  water  boiled 
to  just  the  right  consistency.  And 
then  there  is  turron  hijon,  three  or  four 
sorts  of  it,  but  all  made  of  almonds, 
nuts  and, honey,  so  rich  that  you  eat 
only  a  bit  of  it.  They  know  dozens  of 
pastes  and  nougats  that  Spanish  nuns 
make  in  the  convents,  but  I  haven't 
got  any  forms  for  them,  though  I  mean 
to  if  they  will  tell.  I  think  the  owner 
here  eyes  me  with  suspicion,  as  one 
who  may  set  up  a  rival  establishment. 
And  now  I  have  to  run.  Do  you  want 
to  come  again?" 

"We  don't  want  to  go  anywhere  else 
till  we  have  tried  every  dish  that  isn't 
like  anything  we  have  ever  had  before. 
Even  the  chicken  was  a  touch  beyond, 
and,  tomorrow  being  Friday,  let's  come 
back  and  take  bacalao  and  that  starry 
kind  of  a  dish  over  there.     See?" 

It  was  a  dish  of  arroz  amarillo,  but 
bordered  with  yellow  circles  of  fried 
plantain;  and  other  mysteries  were  ar- 
riving with  no  time  to  consider  them. 

"I've  buried  the  prejudice  and  will 
not  mention  Fox's  Book  of  Martyrs 
again,"  said  the  objector  as  they  passed 
out,  making  note  of  the  number  and 
street  as  they  went. 

"It's  delightful,"  she  said.  "Of 
course  we  are  all  coming  again! " 


How  Lady  Betty  Was  Captured 

A  Story  of  Colonial  Days 
By  Pauline  Carrington  Bouve 


N 


'AY,  Nurse  Bevis,  I  would  see 
the  Queen  go  by!" 

' '  Queen ! ' '  sniffed  Dame 
Bevis  contemptuously  as  she  made 
room  for  Lady  Betty's  small  person  in 
the  window  niche. 

"A  sorry  piece  of  royalty,  an  it 
please  you  to  look  at  the  savage. 
Mistress  Betty." 

"Nay,  but  nurse,  she  hath  a  brave 
front  and  walks  with  a  majestic  tread. 
I  wonder  what  hath  caused  her  dis- 
pleasure. I  heard  Lady  Frances  tell 
Lady  Janet  Boyd  that  the  Governor 
was  sorely  in  need  of  her  warriors. 
But  look  at  the  fashion  of  her  hair 
with  the  shells  upon  it  and  the  long- 
fringed  mantle  from  shoulder  to  foot! 
Saw  you  ever  the  like?"  and  Lady 
Betty  tittered  as  she  thrust  her  head 
out  between  the  hangings  of  the 
window,  to  get  a  better  view. 

It  was  indeed  a  remarkable  spectacle 
upon  which  the  young  girl  and  Dame 
Bevis  looked  from  their  perch  in  one 
of  the  windows  of  the  Governor's 
palace  in  Jamestown,  Virginia,  on  a 
certain  June  morning  in  the  year  1676. 

Queen  Pannunky,  accompanied  by 
her  son,  several  warriors  and  an  inter- 
preter, had  come  to  Jamestown,  at  the 
bidding  of  Sir  William  Berkeley,  on  a 
matter  of  importance  to  the  colonists 
of  the  Old  Dominion,  as  Virginia  was 
then  called. 

The  troubles  between  the  Indians 
and  the  exposed  Virginians,  dwelling 
along  James  River,  had  assumed  an 
alarming  aspect.  Young  Nathaniel 
Bacon,  who  had  suffered  severely  from 
their  attacks,  after  repeated  refusals 
from  the  Governor  to  his  petitions  for  a 
Commission,  had  imprudently  put  him- 
self at  the  head  of  a  band  of  colonists 


and  had  fought  a  pitched  battle  with 
one  of  the  neighboring  tribes,  in  which 
the  dusky  enemy  was  completely 
routed.  Sir  William  Berkeley,  who 
considered  his  authority  endangered 
by  such  a  bold  proceeding,  promptly 
proclaimed  Bacon  a  rebel.  To  the 
chagrin  of  many  of  the  planters,  who 
had  suffered  much  from  the  Indians 
and  who  regarded  their  non-com- 
missioned leader  as  a  hero,  young 
Bacon  was  arrested  and  brought  to 
trial  in  the  Council  Chamber  at  James- 
town. Moved  by  motives  of  policy, 
Sir  William  had  finally  pardoned  Bacon 
on  parole,  and  reinstated  him  in  his 
seat  in  the  Council,  but  those  who 
knew  Sir  William  best,  shook  their 
heads  and  whispered  it  was  wiser  for 
the  young  man  to  go  aboard  the  first 
sloop  whose  sails  should  be  set  for 
England. 

It  was  on  account  of  the  late  troubles 
that  Sir  William  had  bethought  him 
that  it  might  be  wise  to  join  forces  with 
Queen  Pannunky,  whose  tribe  was  at 
enmity  with  the  Susquehannas.  The 
Queen,  however,  was  in  no  mood  to 
send  her  warriors  forth  to  fight  for  the 
white  men,  even  though  it  be  against 
a  common  enemy.  Deep  in  her  heart 
she  felt,  perhaps,  that  no  enemy  was 
so  much  to  be  feared  as  the  strangers 
who  were  building  towns  and,  day  by 
day,  growing  more  powerful  in  the 
land  of  her  ancestors.  She  felt,  too, 
with  all  the  pride  of  primitive  royalty, 
that  she  had  not  been  received  with 
the  pomp  and  ceremony  her  rank  de- 
served. So  when  the  clerk  put  the 
question:  "How  many  men  will  you 
pledge  us  to  fight  the  Susquehannas?" 
she  shook  her  shell-crowned  head 
gravely,  and  in  a  low  voice  answered, 


456 


HOW  LADY  BETTY  WAS  CAPTURED 


457 


"Twelve,"  and  then  with  a  gesture  to 
her  attendants,  rose  and  walked  out 
of  the  Council  Chamber,  the  long 
thick  fringe  of  her  deerskin  mantle 
softly  trailing  over  the  flagstones  of 
the  floor,  her  dark  eyes  gazing  straight 
before  her,  her  shapely  brown  hands 
clasping  the  long  wand  of  brightly- 
colored  feathers,  which  was  the  en- 
sign of  her  royalty. 

Sir  William  Berkeley  flushed  angrily 
as  he  watched  the  proud  figure  vanish 
through  the  doorway. 

"On  my  word,  gentlemen,"  he  cried 
hotly,  "this  piece  of  royal  baggage 
grows  somewhat  insolent.  But  mark 
ye,  I  well  know  whom  to  thank  for 
this  sullen  response  to  a  civilly-put 
request." 

"Whom  means  your  Excellency?" 
The  questioner  was  Mr.  Geoffrey  Page. 

"Mean?  Why,  'tis  plain  enough 
that  this  is  the  upshot  of  Nathaniel 
Bacon's  meddling  in  the  King's  affairs. 
Am  I  not  right,  gentlemen?" 

There  was  a  moment's  silence. 

"Mr.  Bacon  did  but  defend  himself 
and  his  neighbors,"  said  one. 

"Aye,  and  taught  the  savages  a 
lesson  they  needed,"  said  another. 

."He  was  over  hasty,  maybe,  but 
gallant  withal,"  remarked  a  third. 
"Your  Excellency's  clemency  "was 
Christian  and  wise." 

The  Governor  turned  sharply  at 
these  words. 

"Yes,  I  was  clement  when  I  should 
have  been  severe.  Another  time  — 
but  fare  ye  well,  gentlemen,  my  Lady 
is  in  haste  to  set  out  for  Green  Spring," 
and  Sir  William  passed  out  with  a 
courtly  bow,  and  was  joined  by  Lady 
Frances. 

While  this  was  happening  in  the 
Council  Chamber,  Lady  Betty  and 
Dame  Bevis  were  watching  in  the  great 
Hall.  The  folds  of  the  curtains  had 
so  fallen  about  the  two  watchers  en- 
sconced in  the  wide  window  niche  that, 
when  the  Governor  and  Lady  Frances 
entered   the    Hall,  they  were   entirely 


screened  from  view.  Lady  Betty 
would  have  stepped  forth,  but  Dame 
Bevis,  who  stood  in  great  awe  of  Sir 
William,  held  her  back  whispering, 
"Whist,  he  is  angry!" 

"Twelve  men!  It  is  an  outrage,  I 
say!" 

Sir  William's  face  was  puffed  with 
passion,  and  he  grasped  the  hilt  of  his 
sword  beneath  his  velvet  doublet  with 
nervous  fingers.  "The  traitor!  the 
rebel !  he  shall  answer  for  it ! " 

Lady  Frances  tiirned  a  puzzled  face 
up  to  his. 

"Nay,  but  Mr.  Bacon  suffered  the 
loss  of  some  of  his  household;  and 
besides  —  thou  hast  pardoned  him." 

A  flush  spread  over  Sir  William's 
face. 

"He  hath  many  followers,"  con- 
tinued Lady  Frances.  "It  were  best 
perhaps  to  be  lenient  with  these 
Baconians  — " 

"Baconians!"  repeated  Sir  William 
furiously,  "and  has  it  come  to  pass 
that  I  must  hear  mine  own  household 
prate  of  Baconians?" 

' '  But  — •  but ,  he  was  pardoned  on  —  " 

"Parole,"  finished  Sir  William,  his 
eyes  narrowing  to  a  slit,  a  crafty  smile 
on  his  mouth.  ' '  Yet  after  my  clemency 
the  fellow  keeps  to  his  rebellious  course 
and  is  holding  treasonable  correspond- 
ence with  that  traitor  Drummond.  But 
they  shall,  one  and  all,  feel  my  power 
—  I  have  not  yet  done  with  this  Na- 
thaniel Bacon  and  his  business." 

' '  I  only  thought  —  they  might  make 
a  grievance  against  thee  with  his 
majesty,"  urged  Lady  Frances. 

At  mention  of  the  King,  Sir  William 
doffed  his  hat  till  its  plumes  swept  the 
floor. 

"For  thirty  years  I  have  served  the 
King,"  he  said  with  glistening  eyes; 
"when  our  martyred  King  Charles  I. 
was  murdered,  was  it  not  at  my  bidding 
that  Richard  Lee  did  go  to  Breda  to 
offer  Virginia's  allegiance  and  proft'er 
the  loyal  Colony  as  a  refuge  and  a 
kingdom?    .Let    him    reward    me    ac- 


458 


THE    BOSTON    COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


cording  to  his  will ! ' '  With  these  words 
the  GoA'ernor  and  his  Lady  passed  on 
out  of  the  Hall  and  down  Stuart  Street, 
which  ran  between  the  State  House 
and  the  Governor's  palace  in  old 
Jamestown. 

"Alas,"  munnured  Dame  Bevis,  "I 
fear  that  evil  will  come  to  young  Mr. 
Bacon,  and  well  do  I  remember,  when 
war  raged  in  England  'twixt  Royalist 
and  Roundhead,  how  his  father  saved 
the  life  of  old  Sir  Berkeley." 

"Mr.  Bacon's  father  saved  my  grand- 
sire?  I  did  not  know  there  was  this 
debt  between  the  houses  of  Bacon  and 
AVyckoff."  Lady  Betty's  eyes  were 
very  wide  and  there  was  a  note  of 
eager  interest  in  her  voice. 

"Yes,  and  at  his  own  life's  risk  at 
Marston  Moor,  when  all  was  lost  to 
our  cause." 

Lady  Betty  was  evidently  puzzling 
over  a  hard  problem. 

"Yet,  nurse,  was  it  not  wrong  to 
fight  the  Indians  without  Sir  William's 
leave?"  she  asked. 

"Alack!  alack!"  said  the  old  woman. 
"It  is  not  safe  for  women  folk  to 
speak  opinions  about  such  matters  — - 
and  yet  folks  cannot  sit  down  and  be 
scalped,  to  please  Sir  William." 

"No,  they  cannot.  If  King  Charles 
were  here  he  would  put  an  end  to  the 
savages'  outrages.  I  am  sure  of  it,  no 
matter  what  Sir  William  says;  and 
nurse,  I  am  sure  Mr.  Bacon  did  right.'' 

"Hush,  dearie,  hush!  You  w411  get 
us  into  trouble  with  such  talk." 

"  I  am  Lady  Betty  Wyckoff ,  of  Wyck- 
off  Manor,"  said  the  little  girl  drawing 
herself  up  to  her  full  height. 

"Though  thou  be  Lady  Betty  Wyck- 
off, of  Wyckoff  Manor,  a  hundred  times 
over,  thou  art  but  a  little  maid  of 
fourteen  summers,  who  is  often  an 
addlepate,  and  will  be  for  full  five 
years  to  come  the  ward  of  her  kinsman, 
the  Governor,  Sir  William  Berkeley," 
said  the  old  nurse. 

"I'll  be  fifteen  in  three  days," 
laughed  Lady  Betty,  "and  what  you 


said  sounds  like  a  piece  out  of  the 
lesson  book,"  and  Lady  Betty  ran  out 
of  the  hall  singing: 

"  Oh!  years  go  ye  lightly, 
Oh!  years  go  ye  brightly  — 
Till  I  hie  rae  home,  heigh  ho!  " 

Several  days  had  passed  since  the 
Indian  Queen's  visit  to  Jamestown, 
and  the  Governor  and  his  family  were 
at  Green  Spring,  the  estate  some  miles 
from  Jamestown,  where  Sir  William 
Berkeley  lavished  princely  hospitality 
and  held  a  sort  of  miniature  court. 

There  was  more  than  the  ordinary 
bustle  at  Green  Spring  this  particular 
day,  for  Lady  Frances  was  preparing  a 
birthday  party  for  her  husband's  ward. 
Lady  Betty  Wyckoff. 

As  the  day  wore  on,  and  coaches 
from  the  neighboring  plantations  ar- 
rived with  old  and  young  guests,  the 
excitement  of  the  occasion  stirred  Lady 
Betty's  young  heart  with  new  emotions. 

As  the  family  coaches  rolled  up  the 
great  avenue,  the  guests  alighted  and 
paid  their  respects  first  to  Lady  Frances 
and  Sir  William,  and  then  to  the  young 
girl  in  whose  honor  they  had  been 
bidden.  Lady  Betty,  shy  and  blush- 
ing, in  her  white  satin  petticoat  and 
flowered  bodice,  made  a  pretty  picture 
standing  under  the  green  garland,  with 
roses  as  red  as  her  lips  wreathing  her 
chestnut  hair. 

"I  greet  you.  Lady  Betty,  and  wish 
you  as  many  bright  years  as  you  may 
count  on  this,"  said  a  tall,  stately 
gentleman,  throwing  over  her  head,  as 
he  spoke,  a  necklace  of  gold  beads  that 
fell  below  her  waist. 

"Oh,  how  beautiful!  how  good  of 
you  to  think  of  me,  Colonel  Bacon. 
How  can  I  thank  you,  sir." 

"By  treading  a  measure  with  a 
childless  old  man  like  me,"  he  answered 
with  a  smile,  "for  my  stay  here  is 
brief;  matters  of  importance  require 
my  presence  in  Jamestown  tonight, 
and  I  must  soon  be  off." 


HOW  LADY  BETTY  WAS  CAPTURED 


459 


"I  will  be  proud  to  be  your  partner 
in  the  dance.  See,  they  are  forming 
now." 

As  the  hours  wore  away  with  games 
and  pastimes  of  the  period,' Sir  William 
showed  signs  of  nervousness  and  de- 
pression. It  was  during  a  game  of 
''Hide  and  go  seek,"  and  Lady  Betty 
had  hidden  from  her  young  companions 
^behind  the  thick  jasmine  vines  in  the 
summer  arbor,  that  something  hap- 
pened that  changed  for  her  the  game 
into  a  momentous  responsibility.  In 
the  darkness  she  distinguished  two  per- 
sons approaching  her  hiding  place,  and 
she  crouched  further  down  among  the 
vines  and  shrubs,  laughing  softly  to 
herself.  They  came  so  near  that  she 
could  almost  have  touched  their  doubt- 
lets. 


"I  tell  you,  there  is  no  other  way. 
He  must  be  taken." 

"But  how?  'Twere  an  ugly  thing 
to  arrest  a  pardoned  man  without 
good  grounds." 

"There  are  grounds  enough  to  hang 
him!"  said  a  voice,  which,  though 
almost  a  whisper.  Lady  Betty  recog- 
nized with  a  blush  of  shame.  She  saw 
the  other  figure  start  at  the  words. 

"  But  the  Colonel  —  his  followers  — " 

"Will  be  helpless  when  once  I  have 
him.  Here  is  the  writ  for  the  arrest. 
My  sloop  lies  in  the  river.  Board  her; 
you  can  reach  the  middle  plantation  by 
cock-crow.  Be  secret  in  your  move- 
ments. Nathaniel  Bacon  is  wary  as  a 
fox,  and  there  be  traitors  behind  every 
bush  these  perilous  days."

Arbutus 

By  A.  T.  Frost 

Oh,  miracle  of  breezy  upland  meadows, 
And  joy  of  piny  woods  in  April  days! 

First  darlings  of  the  year,  the  early  blossoms. 
That  shyly  spring  along  untrodden  ways. 

Flushed  faces  pressed  to  rugged,  mossy  boulders. 
Or  nestling  'mid  the  leaves  all  sere  and  brown ; 

They  bud  and  bloom,  alike  in  sun  and  shadow. 
While  wandering  snowflakes  idly  flutter  down. 


The.  birds'  glad  twitter,  in  the  leafless  branches, 
A  brook  sings  softly  as  it  hurries  by. 

And,  over  all,  as  if  to  guard  her  treasures, 

Low  bending,  see  the  cloud-flecked,  azure  sky. 


P^ggy  and  I  go  South 

By  Phoebe  D.  Rulon 


TIRED  of  looking  at  the  heaps  of 
snow  in  our  back  yard,  and  the 
ice-bound  leaders,  hanging  like 
huge  icicles  from  the  roof,  Peggy  and  I 
determined  to  make  a  spring  pilgrim- 
age to  the  Southland,  not  to  be  fashion- 
able, but  to  be  comfortable,  and  to  get 
one  long  breath  of  soft,  warm  air  after 
a  fast  of  four  months. 

The  Ashevilles  and  Aikens  were  al- 
luring, but  they  demanded  more  money 
than  we  had  to  proffer. 

Peggy  is  always  philosophical,  and 
declared  that  bright  sunshine  and 
warm  air  are  as  lavish  in  modest  places 
as  in  fashionable  ones.  With  this  fact 
uppermost  we  began  to  plan. 

We  had  exactly  one  hundred  and 
twenty  dollars  between  us.  We 
wanted  to  stay  a  month.  We  must  have 
comforts,  and  we  yearned  for  a  bit  of 
interesting  and  intelligent  sight-seeing. 

We  had  heard  good  things  said  about 
the  Blue  Ridge  section  of  Virginia,  as 
to  climate,  and  we  remembered  that 
Thomas  Jefferson  made  his  home  there, 
so  we  decided  to  try  C ,  a  charm- 
ing educational  town,  in  full  view  of 
the  blue- veiled  mountains. 

It  had  come  to  us  from  an  authori- 
tative source  that  the  standards  of  liv- 
ing in  the  South  were  quite  different 
from  our  own.  Peggy  had  a  mental 
tussle  with  this  fact,  with  the  result  that 
we  decided  to  set  up  a  housekeeping  es- 
tablishment of  our  own,  since  we  could 
not  hope  to  change  the  customs  of  a 
community  in  four  weeks.  We  packed 
our  trunks  accordingly,  allowing  two 
good-sized  ones.  In  the  bottom  of  one 
went  a  chafing-dish,  an  alcohol  lamp 
stove,  two  granite  saucepans,  one  hold- 
ing a  pint  the  other  three  pints,  an 
egg  beater,  wooden  spoon,  can  opener, 
toasting  fork,  small  frying  pan,  sifter, 
and  one  small  granite  stewpan. 


In  the  other  trunk  went  a  few  simple 
dishes  for  civilized  table  setting,  a 
small  tablecloth  and  napkins,  and  the 
pet  books  that  we  enjoyed,  at  odd 
moments,  aloud. 

This  left  ample  room  for  light  and 
heavy  clothing,  which  we  found  an 
early  spring  necessity  in  Virginia. 

The  eventful  day  arrived,  and  Peggy 
went  straight  to  the  ticket  office  and 
bought  our  tickets  for  C ,  with  un- 
wavering resolution.  The  Pennsylva- 
nia Railroad  took  us  safely  and  speed- 
ily to  Washington,  and  the  Baltimore 
and  Ohio  the  rest  of  the  way.  We 
found  excellent  a  la  carte  service  in  the 
dining  car,  so  no  disproportionate  in- 
roads were  made  on  our  one  hundred 
and  twenty  precious  dollars.  I  men- 
tion this  as  a  decided  improvement  on 
the  dollar  meal  so  long  prevailing. 

Once  off  the  train  and  in  full  view 
of   the   mountains,  we  felt    sure   that 

C was  the  place  of  places  for  us. 

We  forthwith  proceeded  to  "take 
lodgings"  as  the  English  say,  that  is, 
we  hired  two  furnished  rooms,  includ- 
ing heat,  light  and  service.  For  these 
we  paid  twenty  dollars  for  the  month. 
We  were  located  in  the  suburbs,  mid- 
way between  a  broad  stretch  of  coun- 
try on  the  one  side  and  the  center  of 
town  on  the  other,  with  a  trolley  pass- 
ing our  door  to  take  us  to  either,  as  our 
mood  dictated.  These  lodgings  were 
with  a  typical  Virginia  family  and  we 
proved  anew  the  Virginian  hospitality. 
Each  room  was  provided  with  an  open 
grate,  in  which  soft  coal  is  burned. 
The  furniture  was  of  the  simplest,  but 
it  included  a  trustworthy  table  for 
dining  purposes,  a  stand  for  our  cook- 
ing utensils,  rocking  chairs  for  com- 
fort, with  plenty  of  sunshine  and  air 
stealing  in  through  four  large  windows. 
We  transformed  the  rooms  to  fit  our 


460 


PEGGY  AND  I  GO  SOUTH 


461 


needs,  one  into  a  living-room  by  day, 
the  other  into  a  kitchen,  and  both  into 
sleeping-rooms  at  night. 

A  large,  round  tin  box  with  tight- 
fitting  cover,  costing  sixty  cents,  pro- 
tected all  of  our  perishable'food  supply, 
while  a  donated  wooden  crate  from  the 
grocer  stored  our  fruit  and  vegetables. 
One  bureau  became  a  sideboard  and 
china  closet,  and  one  trunk  top  a 
kitchen  table. 

The  little  colored  maid,  called  Lily 
by  way  of  contrast,  I  presume,  came 
to  us  each  morning  with  almost  the 
regularity  of  the  sunrise  and  "did  up" 
our  rooms,  and  left  us  with  a  bright 
fire  in  each  grate.  Now  Peggy  had  a 
weakness  for  an  open  fire,  because  of 
its  eventide  glow  and  poetic  suggest- 
iveness,  but  she  openly  confessed  that 
never  until  this  trip  did  she  have  any 
idea  of  its  possibilities  as  a  cooking 
apparatus.  All  the  odors  of  broiling 
meat  were  carried  up  chimney,  toast- 
ing was  a  delight,  and  boiling  became 
a  matter  of  a  minute.  The  portable 
iron  hearth  was  an  excellent  warming 
shelf,  with  heat  enough  to  keep  things 
simmering. 

Over  and  above  all,  the  fine  black 
dust  from  the  fire  made  one  feel  sure 
she  was  camping,  yes,  actually  camping 
out  of  doors.  We  found  it  advisable 
to  substitute  a  small  kerosene  stove 
for  the  alcohol  lamp,  and  this,  together 
with  the  chafing-dish  and  grate  fire, 
gave  us  a  feeling  of  delightful  inde- 
pendence, forming  an  equipment,  Peggy 
declared,  sufficient  to  go  forth  and 
convert  the  world  to  right  living. 

We  found  the  stores  and  markets  of 

C good,  so  we  had  no  difficulty  in 

getting  ample  supplies  and  of  excellent 
quality.  Country  honey,  fresh  eggs 
and  excellent  milk  from  an  inspected 
dairy,  made  us  thankful  at  the  very 
outset. 

Nourishing  and  tasty   cream   soups 


lent  dignity  to  many  of  our  dinners, 
while  a  twist  of  Peggy's  spoon  elabo- 
rated sour  milk  into  the  nicest  sort  of 
griddle  cakes  for  many  a  breakfast. 

We  avoided  any  approach  to  a  "feast 
or  famine"  condition,  being  careful  to 
average  up  every  day ,  but  the  occasions 
that  brought  forth  veal  potpie  with 
egg  dumplings,  or  salmon  with  creamed 
macaroni,  were  hailed  with  special 
votes  of  thanks. 

Porterhouse  steak  was  our  standby 
as  to  meat,  varying  it  with  veal  cutlets, 
pot  roast,  stews,  ham  and  chicken. 
These  were  either  broiled,  fried,  stewed, 
or  boiled.  On  one  particularly  cheer- 
less day  Peggy  brightened  our  experi- 
ment by  producing  a  plate  of  most  de- 
licious cream  of  tartar  biscuit  that  she 
had  baked  in  the  frying  pan,  and  the 
"family"  shouted  "what  next!"  The 
next  turned  out  to  be  escalloped  to- 
matoes done  before  the  open  grate  on 
the  hot  hearth.  Cheese  souffie  was 
balanced  by  Irish  stew,  and  French 
pancakes  by  hard  tack  and  milk.  We 
resorted  seldom  to  canned  goods  or 
other  makeshifts,  relying  upon  fresh 
fruit  and  vegetables. 

Peggy  declared  that  our  experiment 
was  in  no  sense  light  housekeeping,  for 
not  one  can  of  baked  beans  came  on 
our  table  in  the  four  weeks.  We 
planned  our  meals  so  that  they  were 
expeditiously  prepared,  giving  us  ample 
time  for  sight-seeing  and  reading. 
Furthermore,  we  proved  the  truth  that, 
if  you  would  learn  to  know  people,  you 
must  live  among  them,  and  so,  when 
we  turned  our  faces  homeward,  it  was 
with  a  feeling  that  we  were  leaving 
friends  behind.  With  one  accord  we 
declared  the  trip  had  been  a  great 
success,  and,  wonderful  to  relate,  we 
have  a  small  balance  on  hand  of  the 
one  hundred  and  twenty  dollars,  which 
Peggy  suggests  we  keep  as  a  souvenir, 
or  as  a  sinking  fund  for  our  next  trip.

EFFICIENT  LIVING 

THEORIES  and  doctrines  and 
dogmas  are  all  man-made.  They 
are  likely  to  be  narrow,  errone- 
ous and  misleading.  In  the  course  of 
history  abstract  formulas  have  been  the 
source  of  serious  and  infinite  troubles. 
The  race  has  ever  been  struggling  to  rid 
itself  of  one  set  of  formularies  and 
adopt  what  has  seemed,  at  the  time 
being,  wiser  and  better  ways. 

Plato,  the  disciple  and  exponent  of 
Socrates,  represents  the  high- water 
mark  of  intellectuality  in  the  ancient 
world.  *'No  writer  in  that  flowering 
time  of  beauty  and  reason,  which  we 
call  The  Age  of  Pericles,  has  exerted 


so  profound  an  influence  as  Plato." 
And  yet  Plato  was  a  dreamer.  Most 
of  his  ideas  and  speculations  have 
long  since  been  outgrown.  Perhaps, 
the  best  known  of  Plato's  works  is 
"The  Republic."  In  this  he  "outlines 
an  ideal  form  of  government,  where 
everybody  would  be  healthy,  happy 
and  prosperous."  Certainly  no  fault 
can  be  found  with  this  as  an  ideal 
proposition .  "In  his  division  of  labor , ' ' 
for  instance,  "each  individual  was 
to  do  the  thing  he  was  best  fitted  to  do. 
It  was  assumed  that  each  person  had 
a  gift  and  that  to  use  this  gift  all  that 
was  necessary  was  to  give  him  an 
opportunity.  So  here,  that  very  mod- 
ern cry  of  'equality  of  opportunity' 
harks  back  to  Plato."  Plato  was 
great  in  speculation;  Aristotle,  favorite 
and  most  eminent  pupil  of  Plato,  was 
great  in  observation.  Well  has  it 
been  said,  that  it  was  Aristotle  who 
discovered  the  world. 

Over  against  all  human  theories  and 
speculations  are  the  laws  of  the  Uni- 
verse. These  we  feel  are  uniform, 
comprehensive  and  far-reaching.  In 
application  they  are  universal  and 
regardless  of  individuals.  Though  ever 
trying,  man  has  never  been  able  to 
grasp  the  full  significance  of  Nature's 
plans.  He  must  simply  and  without 
cavil  conform  to  conditions  as  he  finds 
them,  and  act  accordingly. 

We  cannot  live  on  ideas  alone,, 
however  valuable  and  uplifting  these 
may  be.  Nature,  it  seems,  demands 
that  we  should  put  forth  some  practi- 
cal effort  to  get  a  living.  All  must 
work,  or  they  who  do  toil  must  support 
themselves  and  those  who  do  not  work. 
"Nature  knows  nothing  of  bank  bal- 
ances. In  order  to  have  an  appetite 
for  dinner,  one  must  first  earn  his 
dinner.  If  you  would  sleep  at  night, 
you  must  first  pay  for  sweet  sleep  by 
physical  labor."  The  practical  affairs, 
then,  of  life  must  claim  a  goodly  share 
of  our  attention.  We  prepare  to  live 
not  by  thought  alone,  but  by  living. 


EDITORIALS 


463 


Does  it  not  follow  that  a  young 
woman,  for  instance,  may  be  versed 
in  music  and  art;  she  may  be  ac- 
quainted with  biology  and  bacteriol- 
ogy, and  yet,  unless  she  has  learned 
how  to  cook  and  serve  food  properly, 
she  can  not  be  a  genuine  and  successful 
homemaker  ? 

In  an  address  to  young  people, 
President  Eliot  said,  recently: 

"Americans  believe  in  education 
more  than  anything  else  in  the  world; 
the  lifting  up  of  the  individual  which 
is  the  making  of  the  nation." 

In  education  "attention  should  be 
given  to  the  subject  that  most  interests 
the  individual,  which  is  sure  to  be  the 
one  upon  which  he  will  work  the  hard- 
est and  which  is  most  likely  to  result  in 
achievement.  Concentration  is  the 
secret  of  success  in  the  work  of  life ;  the 
earning  of  a  livelihood,  a  family  life 
and  a  happy  life.  Learn  to  do  some- 
thing skilfully,  as  an  individual,  which 
will  help  to  give  pleasure  to  others." 

Efficient  living,  therefore,  is  not  to 
be  attained  through  fond  desire  and 
idle  dreaming,  but  by  that  more  fruitful 
form  of  effort  which  is  inspired  by  a 
lofty  purpose. 

HEALING  BY  SUGGESTION 


r 


kHE  influence  of  mind  on  mind 
has  never  been  denied.  Like- 
wise, the  direction  and  control  of 
mind  over  bodily  functions  is  pretty 
well  understood.  But  cure  by  sugges- 
tion is  a  comparatively  modern  method 
of  treatment.  It  seems  to  have  been 
left  to  these  latter  days  to  call  atten- 
tion anew,  and  in  a  somewhat  unusual 
manner,  to  the  familiar  saying  of  the 
Great  Teacher,  "Thy  faith  hath  made 
thee  whole."  Evidently  the  science  of 
therapeutics  is  destined  to  be  subjected 
to  certain  and  sure  modifications. 

In  the  truest  sense,  the  chief  source 
of  our  help  and  guidance  in  the  con- 
cerns of  life  is  in  ourselves.  To  each 
individual    the    admonition    has    been 


given  to.  work  out  his  own  salvation. 
The  best  books  are  those  that  incite 
us  to  conscientious  and  independent 
thought.  Our  best  teachers  are  those 
who  inspire  in  us  the  greatest  enthusi- 
asm and  activity.  Others  may  suggest 
and  encourage,  but  the  salutary  life- 
giving  effort  must  be  put  forth  by  our 
own  selves. 

Most  of  the  nostrums,  it  is  said,  for 
the  cure  of  disease  are  put  forth  in 
advertisements  intended  to  w^ork  di- 
rectly upon  the  minds  of  the  people 
who  buy  and  use  the  medicines ;  and  the 
good  they  seem  to  do,  in  many  cases, 
is  often  the  direct  result  of  the  confi- 
dence created  by  the  advertisement. 

On  this  subject  a  writer,  in  an  ablv 
conducted  religious  weekly,  well  puts 
our  thought: 

"Out  of  a  medley  of  incoherent  and 
conflicting  forces   an   ancient  truth  is 
emerging  in  rational  form,  and  w^hen 
the  sects  and  cults  and  rival  societies 
and  practitioners  have  lost  their  nov- 
elty, and  with  that  their  influence,  and 
when  the  practice  of  healing  by  these 
methods  is  no  longer  especially  lucra- 
tive, we  shall  come  to  the  root  of  the 
matter,  and  do  what  we  have  done  in 
the  case  of  every  popular  mania  and 
movement  —  save  the  good  and  let  the 
rest  go.     Out  of  alchemy  came  chem- 
istry, out  of  astrology  came  astronomy, 
out    of    phrenology    came    a    scientific 
study  of  the  brain,  out  of  homoeopathy 
came  a  distinct  revolution  in  the  ad- 
ministration of  drugs,  out  of  mesmerism 
and  magnetism  came  scientific  hvpno- 
tism,  and  out  of  Christian  Science  and 
allied,  but  hostile,  systems  will  come 
a  new  and  more  rational  trust  in  hu- 
man nature,  and  especially  in  that  mys- 
terious force  which  the  ancients  called 
the  vis  medicatrix  natures  (the  healing 
powder   of   nature).     Pure    food,    fresh 
air,    clean   water,    fewer    drugs,    more 
courage,  and  the  will  to  beheve  that 
good  health  is  hkely  to  be  had  for  the 
asking,  will  help  mightily  in  the  battle 
with  disease',  whether  we  do  or  do  not 


464 


THE   BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


believe  that  the  age  of  miracles  is  to 
return,  as  is  now  held  by  some  religious 
healers  who  are  adopting  the  new 
methods." 


A  GREAT  scientist,  Alfred  Russell 
Wallace,  in  some  late  reflections 
on  human  progress ,  predicts  that 
the  two  great  influences  which  will 
operate  in  the  future  more  powerfully 
than  ever  before  to  uplift  humanity, 
are  education  and  selection  by  mar- 
riage.    To  quote  from  his  words: 

"As  yet  we  have  no  true  and  effective 
education.  The  very  first  essential  in 
the  teacher  —  true  love  of,  or  any  sym- 
pathy with,  the  children  —  is  not  made 
one  of  the  conditions  of  entering  that 
gi*eat  profession.  Till  this  is  made  the 
primary  qualification  (as  it  was  by 
Robert  Owen  at  his  schools  in  New 
Lanark)  no  real  improvement  in  social 
and  moral  character  can  be  effected. 
Mere  intellectual  instruction  —  which 
is  all  now  given  —  is  not  a  complete 
education,  is  really  the  least  important 
half  of  it. 

"The  other  and  more  permanently 
effective  agency,  selection  through  mar- 
riage, will  come'  into  operation  only 
when  a  greatly  improved  social  system 
renders  all  our  women  economically  and 
socially  free  to  choose ;  while  a  rational 
and  complete  education  will  have 
taught  them  the  importance  of  their 
choice  both  to  themselves  and  to  hu- 
manity. It  will  act  through  the  agency 
of  well-known  facts  and  principles  of 
human  nature,  leading  to  a  continuous 
reduction  of  the  lower  types  in  each 
successive  generation,  and  it  is  the 
only  mode  yet  suggested  which  will 
automatically  and  naturally  effect  this. 

"When  we  consider  the  enormous 
importance  of  such  a  continuous  im- 
provement in  the  average  character, 
and  that  our  widespread  and  costly 
religious  and  educational  agencies  have, 
so  far,  made  not  the  slightest  advance 
towards  it,  we  shall,  perhaps,  realize, 
before  it  is  too  late,  that  we  have  be- 


gun at  the  wrong  end.  Improvement 
of  social  conditions  must  precede  im- 
provement of  character;  and  only  when 
we  have  so  reorganized  society  as  to 
abolish  the  cruel  and  debasing  struggle 
for  existence  and  for  wealth  that  now 
prevails,  shall  we  be  enabled  to  liberate 
those  beneficent  natural  forces  which 
alone  can  elevate  character." 


Even  the  physicians  are  awakening 
to  the  meaning  and  value  of  dietetics. 
According  to  a  writer  in  a  leading 
medical  journal : 

"There  are  few  subjects  connected 
with  the  practice  of  medicine  so  little 
understood  by  the  profession  as  the 
food  factor  in  health  and  in  disease. 
And  yet  when  we  consider  that  the 
number  of  diseases  attributed  to  a  dis- 
ordered nutrition  is  constantly  increas- 
ing, it  is  to  be  commented  upon  that 
our  medical  institutions  should  devote 
so  little  attention  to  the  science  of 
dietetics,  and  that  the  vast  majority 
of  them  should  furnish  no  facilities  for 
acquiring  practical  instruction  in  this 
branch  of  therapeutics.  To  prescribe 
the  right  food  at  the  right  time  is  an 
art  in  itself." 


Have  you  not  taken  pleasure  in 
handling  the  last  two  or  three  numbers 
of  the  Cooking-School  Magazine?  In 
the  character  of  the  printing,  binding 
and  general  make-up,  the  Magazine 
has  been  greatly  improved.  It  is  our 
purpose  not  only  to  sustain  the  high 
standard  of  excellence  with  which  we 
have  been  so  widely  credited,  but  to 
make  steady,  wholesome  progress  in 
our  special  line  of  effort.  Would  that 
we  could  place  before  our  readers  the 
choice  and  useful  matter  we  are  con- 
stantly desirous  of  reaching. 

We  believe  a  growing  sentiment  is 
abroad  in  the  land  in  favor  of  the 
study  of  foods  and  feeding.  And  the 
subject  is  in  no  wise  one  of  least  im- 
portance; for  it  concerns  cheerful, 
healthy,  prosperous  living  everywhere.
