Fresh,  Dried  and  Canned  Mushrooms 


Seasonable  Recipes 

By  Janet  M.  Hill 


IN  all  recipes  where  flour  is  used,  unless  otherwise  stated,  the  flour  is  measured  after  sifting 
once.  When  flour  is  measured  by  cups,  the  cup  is  filled  with  a  spoon,  and  a  level  cupful 
is  meant.  A  tablespoonful  or  a  teaspoonful  of  any  designated  material  is  a  level  spoonful  of 
such  material. 


Chicken-and -Tomato  Soup 

MELT  a  tablespoonful  of  butter 
in  a  saucepan  and  add  half  an 
onion  and  two  sprigs  of  pars- 
ley, chopped  fine;  stir  and  cook  until 
yellowed,  but  not  browned,  then  add 
a  can  of  tomatoes  and  about  three 
pints  of  chicken  broth,  and  let  simmer 
twenty  minutes;  press  through  a  sieve 
and  reheat  to  the  boiling  point ;  stir  two 
tablespoonfuls  of  corn  starch  with  water 
to  pour  and  cook  in  the  hot  soup  ten 
minutes;  skim,  if  needed,  season  with 
salt  and  pepper  and  serve. 

Cream-of-Dried  Mushroom  Soup 

Cover  a  fourth  of  a  pound  of  dried 
mushrooms  with  cold  water  and  let 
stand  overnight.  In  the  morning  set 
to  cook  in  the  same  water,  adding  more, 
if  needed,  and  let  simmer  ten  minutes, 


then  pound  with  a  pestle.  Melt  two 
tablespoonfuls  of  butter;  in  it  cook 
half  an  onion,  sliced  fine,  till  softened 
and  yellowed,  then  add  the  pounded 
mushrooms,  two  sprigs  of  parsley,  a 
tablespoonful  of  salt,  a  quart  of  broth 
or  water  and  six  or  eight  peppercorns, 
and  let  simmer  very  gently  twenty 
minutes.  Melt  one-fourth  a  cup  of 
butter  and  in  it  cook  one-fourth  a  cup 
of  flour;  add  three  cups  of  milk  and 
stir  until  boiling;  strain  in  the  mush- 
room mixture;  let  boil  once,  then  re- 
move to  a  cooler  part  of  the  range  and 
stir  in  the  beaten  yolks  of  two  eggs, 
diluted  witli  half  a  cup  of  cream. 

Salmon  Boiled  with  Vegetables 

Put  a  solid  piece  of  fresh  salmon 
(weighing  about  three  pounds)  into  an 
earthen  dish,  and  lay  around  it  a 
sliced  onion,   three   sprigs   of   parsley, 


465 


466 


THE    BOSTON    COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


half  a  lemon,  cut  into  thin  slices, 
three  cloves,  a  dozen  peppercorns  and 
half    a    small    carrot,    cut    in    slices. 


Broiled  Mushrooms  ox  Toast 


Pour  over  one-fourth  a  cup  of  vinegar, 
a  teaspoonful  of  salt,  and  a  quart  of 
cold  water;  cover  closely  and  let  stand 
in  a  cool  place  five  or  six  hours.  When 
time  to  cook  set  the  dish  over  the  fire, 
and  slowly  heat  the  water  to  the  boil- 
ing point  (add  water,  if  needed,  that 
the  fish  be  covered) ;  let  simmer  two  or 
three  minutes,  then  move  to  the  back 
of  the  range,  to  stand  five  minutes. 
Have  ready  a  folded  napkin  on  a  hot 
platter;  with  a  skimmer  remove  the 
fish    to    the    napkin,    letting    it    drain 


Fresh  Mushrooms,  Broiled 

Remove  the  stems  close  to  the  caps 
and  peel  the  caps;  put 
the  caps  in  a  well-oiied 
or  buttered  broiler,  set 
gill  side  down  over  the 
fire  and  let  cook  about 
five  minutes,  then  turn 
and  broil  the  skin  side 
five  minutes.  Have 
ready  broiled  steak  or 
rounds  of  toast,  nicely 
buttered ;  set  the  mush- 
rooms on  the  steak  or 
toast,  gill  side  up,  and 
put  about  a  fourth  a 
teaspoonful  of  creamed  butter  in  the 
center  of  each  mushroom.  Set  the  dish 
into  the  oven  half  a  minute,  no  longer, 
and  serve  at  once. 

Salmon  Steaks  Baked  with 
Mushrooms 

Have  four  salmon  steaks  cut  just 
below  the  head  of  the  fish.  Put  some 
bits  of  salt  pork  on  a  fish  sheet  (the 
cover  of  a  tin  cracker  box  with  edge 
pounded  flat  may  be  used  for  this 
purpose) ;  on  the  pork  lay  two  of  the 


Salmon  Steaks  Baked  with  Mushrooms 


well  before  setting  it  in  place.  Serve 
with  drawn  butter  or  Hollandaise 
sauce.  The  fish  left  from  the  meal  may 
be  used  in  a  salad  as  creamed  or  cur- 
ried fish,  croquettes  or  scalloped  fish. 


steaks,  the  open  ends  towards  each 
other.  Remove  the  stems  from  half 
a  pound  of  fresh  mushrooms  and  peel 
the  caps.  Melt  three  or  four  table- 
spoonfuls  of  butter  in  a  frying  pan;

grate  in  about  a  tablespoonful  of

hour,  basting  four  or  five  times  with 
melted  butter.  In  the  meanwhile,  chop 
the  mushroom  stems  and  peelings 
very  fine;  melt  two  tablespoonfuls  of

it  cook  in  three  tablespoonfuls  of 
butter,  until  yellowed;  add  three  level 
tablespoonfuls  of  flour,  one  level  table- 
spoonful  of  curry  powder  and  half  a 
teaspoonful  of  salt;  let  cook  until 
frothy,  then  add  one  cup  and  a  half  of 
broth,  two  tablespoonfuls  of  currant 
jelly,  a  tablespoonful  of  lemon  juice, 
and  stir  until  boiling;  add  the  meat  and 
set  the  dish  over  hot  water.  Serve 
when  very  hot  inside  a  border  of  boiled 
rice.  The  dish  may  be  served  at  once, 
but  is  at  its  best  if  allowed  to  stand 
some  time,  that  the  meat  may  absorb 
the  flavors  in  the  sauce.  Chicken  or 
veal  may  take  the  place  of  the  lamb.

Curry  of  Lamb  in  Rice  Border 


chopped  material  until  the  butter  is 
absorbed,  then  add  a  cup  and  a  half 
of  cream  and  let  simmer  very  gently 
until  the  fish  is  cooked.  Season  with

Curry  of  Lamb  in  Rice  Border 

Remove  all  fat  and  gristle  from  cold, 
cooked  lamb,  cutting  the  meat  into 
thin  slices  or  pieces.  For  a  pint  of 
the  pieces,  chop  half  an  onion  and  let 


Boiled  Rice 

Pour  three  pints  or  more  of  cold 
water  over  a  cup  of  rice  and  heat 
quickly  to  the  boiling  point,  stirring 
meanwhile;  let  boil  rapidly  three  or 
four  minutes,  then  drain  through  a 
sieve;  add  cold  water  to  rinse  the  rice. 
Set  over  the  fire  to  cook  in  a  quart  of 
boiling  water,  to  which  a  teaspoonful  of 
salt  has  been  added;  cover  closely  and 
let  boil  rapidly,  or  cook  in  the  un- 
covered kettle,  adding  water  as  needed, 
to  replace  that  lost  by  the  rapid  evap- 
oration. When  the  rice  is  tender,  let 
dry  off  in  the  oven,  or  at  the  back  of 
the  range,  and  use  as  indicated  with  the 
curry  or  rechaufee  of  lamb. 


468 


THE    BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


Rechaufee  of  Lamb,  Creole  Style 

Melt  three  tablespoonfuls  of  butter; 
in  it  cook  one  tablespoonful,  each,  of 
onion,  and  green  pepper,  chopped  fine; 
cook  until  softened  and  yellowed,  but 
not  browned;  add  three  level  table- 
spoonfuls  of  flour  and  cook  until 
frothy,  then  add  a  cup  of  broth  and 
half  a  cup  of  tomato  puree  and  stir 
until  boiling;  add  half  a  teaspoonful  of 
salt,  a  dash  of  paprika,  half  a  teaspoon- 
ful of  grated  horseradish,  a  teaspoonful 
of  lemon  juice  and  two  cups  of  cooked 
meat,  cut  in  small  pieces  and  neatly 
trimmed.  Serve  in  a  border  of  plain, 
boiled  rice. 

Chicken  Loaf  with  Asparagus 

Take  the  flesh  from  an  uncooked 
chicken,  free  it  from  tendons,  then 
pass  through  a  food  chopper.  Cook 
half  a  cup  of  fine  bread  crumbs  (taken 


Set  the  pan  in  a  dish  on  many  folds  of 
paper,  pour  in  boiling  water  to  half 
the  height  of  the  mould  and  let  cook, 
without  the  water  boiling,  from  three- 
quarters  of  an  hour  to  two  hours, 
according  to  the  age.  Turn  from  the 
mould  onto  a  hot  platter,  and  surround 
with  cooked  asparagus  tips  and  Bech- 
amel sauce. 

Asparagus  Tips  and  Bechamel  Sauce 

If  the  asparagus  stalks  be  of  various 
lengths,  cut  them  into  inch  lengths  and 
stew  till  tender  in  a  little  salted  water. 
Melt  three  tablespoonfuls  of  butter; 
in  it  cook  three  tablespoonfuls  of  flour, 
then  add  a  cup  of  chicken  broth  or 
asparagus  liquid  and  half  a  cup  of 
cream;  stir  until  boiling,  then  add  the 
tips  with  salt  and  pepper,  as  needed. 
If  the  tips  are  of  the  same  length, 
serve  in  bunches  around  the  chicken, 
with  a  little  of  the  sauce  poured  over 


Chicken  Loaf,  with  Asparagus

then  strain  off  the  broth. 

Hash,  Creole  Style 

Chop  fine  a  small  onion  and  a  green 
or  red  pepper  from  which  the  seeds 
have    been    taken.     In    a    frying    pan

Filling  for  Chicken  Patties, 
French  Style 

Melt  two  tablespoonfuls  of  butter 
and  cook  in  it  two  tablespoonfuls  of 
flour,  one-fourth  a  teaspoonful,  each, 
of  salt  and  pepper  and  a  grating  of 
nutmeg  (one-fourth  a  teaspoonful). 
When  the  mixture  becomes  frothy ,  add 
one  cup  of  chicken  broth  and  stir  con- 
stantly until  smooth  and  boiling.  Add 
six  canned  mushrooms  and  one  truffle, 
cut  in  small  pieces,  and  one  cup  and  a 
fourth  of  tender,  cooked  chicken,  cut 
in  one-third  an  inch  cubes.  Stir  until 
very  hot  but  not  boiling,  then  beat  in 
the  yolk  of  an  egg,  beaten 
and  mixed  with  one 
tablespoonful  of  thick, 
rich  cream.  Stir  until 
the  egg  thickens,  then 
use  to  fill  hot  patties. 


of  cold  water.     Add  a  little  of  the  gel- 
atine   mixture    to    the    chicken.       Set 


Chopped  Chicken  Molded  with  Aspic 


moulds  in  ice  and  water,  and  put  in  the 
chicken  and  broth  in  alternate  layers. 
Let  each  layer  harden  before  another 
is  poured  in.  Serve,  turned  from  the 
moulds,with  lettuce  and  French  dressing. 

Corn  Meal  Griddle  Cakes 

Sift  together  three-fourths  a  cup  of 
corn  meal,  one  cup  and  a  fourth  of 
flour,  four  level  teaspoonfuls  of  baking 
powder,  half  a  teaspoonful  of  salt  and 
two  level  tablespoonfuls  of  sugar.  Beat 
an  egg;  add  a  cup  and  one-fourth  of 
milk  and  stir  into  the  dry  ingredients, 
with  two  tablespoonfuls  of  melted 
butter.  Bake  on  a  hot  griddle;  turn 
when  full  of  bubbles;  the  under  side 
should  be  a  good  brown.

Orange  Sections  in  Jelly 

When  the  sponge  is  light,  add  a  table- 
spoonful  of  sugar,  half  a  teaspoonful  of 
salt,  two  tablespoonfuls  of  melted 
butter,  the  white  of  one  egg,  beaten 
light,  and  flour  to  make  a  soft  dough. 
Knead  until  elastic,  cover  and  let  rise 
until  the  bulk  is  doubled.  Pull  off  the 
dough  in  small  pieces  and  knead  it  into 
balls;  cover  these  on  a  floured  board; 
when  light  roll  them  under  the  hands 
on  the  board  into  lengths  to  fit  a 
bread-stick  pan.  Bake  when  again 
light.  Brush  over  with  slightly  beaten 
white  of  egg  and  return  to  the  oven, 
to  set  the  glaze. 


may  be  used)  and  half  a  lemon  (there

with  the  fruit  mixture.  Serve,  when 
set  and  cold,  turned  from  the  moulds. 
To  unmold  dip  the  moulds  to  the 
top,  one  at  a  time,  in  warm  water, 
loosen  the  edges,  if  needed,  and  turn 
onto  a  plate.

and  gradually  beat  into  it  one  cup  and 
a  half  of  sifted  flour,  sifted  again  with

Newport  Cake,  Nut  Caramel  Frosting 


Orange  Sections  in  Jelly 

Remove  the  juice  from  three  or  four 
blood  oranges  (other  small  tart  oranges 


a  level  teaspoonful  of  baking  powder. 
Beat  the  yolks  of  five  eggs  until  lemon- 
colored  and  thick,  then  gradually  beat 
into  them  one  cup  and  a  half  of  sifted „ 


SEASONABLE  RECIPES

powdered  sugar.  Now,  gradually  beat 
the  yolk  and  sugar  mixture  into  the 
butter  and  flour  mixture.  Lastly,  beat 
in  the  whites  of  five  eggs,  beaten  dry, 
and  one  tablespoonful  of  brandy. 
Bake,  in  a  loaf,  one  hour;  in  a  sheet, 
about  forty-five  minutes.  The  heat  of 
the  oven  should  be  moderate,  especially 
when  the  cake  is  first  put  into  it.  The 
cake  is  very  fine  grained,  tender  and 
delicate. 

Nut  Caramel  Frosting  for  Cake 
Cook  half  a  cup  of  granulated  sugar 
to  caramel;  add  half  a  cup  of  water 
and  let  simmer  to  form  a  thick  syrup; 
turn  this  into  a  bottle  for  use,  as  re- 
quired. Boil  one  cup  of  sugar,  two 
tablespoonfuls  of  caramel  syrup  and 
one-fourth  a  cup    of   water  until   the 


Blood  Orange  Bombe  Glace 

Boil  one  quart  of  water  and  two  cups 
of  sugar  twenty  minutes  (count  the 
time  after  boiling  actually  begins) ; 
add  half  a  teaspoonful  of  granulated 
gelatine,  softened  in  two  or  three 
tablespoonfuls  of  cold  water.  When 
the  syrup  is  cold,  add  the  juice  of  one 
lemon  and  two  cups  of  "blood"  orange 
juice.  Usually  seven  oranges  will  yield 
this  quantity  of  juice.  Pack  the  can  of 
the  freezer  in  salt  and  crushed  ice, 
using  one  part  of  salt  to  three  of  ice, 
and  freeze  as  usual.  Have  ready  a 
two-quart  melon  mould,  partially  lined 
with  two  strips  of  tough  paper.  Such 
paper  as  is  used  for  wrapping  butter 
and  meat  is  the  kind  used.  (See  illus- 
tration.)    Set  the  lined  mould  into  a 


Blood  Orange  Bombe  Glace,  molded  in  Melon  Mould  lined  with  Paper 


syrup,  dripping  from  a  spoon,  spins  a 
thread  two  inches  long.  Pour  the 
syrup  in  a  fine  stream  onto  the  white 
of  an  egg,  beaten  dry,  beating  vigor- 
ously, meanwhile.  Continue  beating 
until  the  frosting  cools  a  little,  then  set 
into  a  dish  of  boiling  water  and  beat 
until  the  frosting  begins  to  sugar  a 
little  on  the  sides  of  the  dish;  beat  in 
half  a  cup  of  pecan  nut  meats  and 
spread  upon  the  cake,  leaving  the 
surface  a  little  rough  and  uneven. 


pail  of  salt  and  crushed  ice,  to  chill, 
then  line  with  the  frozen  mixture, 
leaving  an  open  space  in  the  center; 
in  this  space  dispose  a  Charlotte  Russe 
mixture;  carefully  cover  the  mixture 
with  sherbet,  filling  the  mould  to  over- 
flow. Spread  a  sheet  of  paper  over 
the  filled  mould  and  over  it  press  the 
cover.  Let  stand  nearly  two  hours, 
packed  in  equal  measures  of  ice  and 
salt.  Rinse  the  salt  from  the  mould 
with   cold   water,    and   unmold    on    a

THE    BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


serving  dish,  spread  with  a  lace  paper. 
By  pulhng  gently  on  the  ends  of  the 
strips  of  paper  used  to  -line  the  mould, 
the  ice  can  be  unmolded  at  once  and 
without  trouble. 

Charlotte  Russe  Filling  for  Bombe 

.  Beat  the  white  of  one  egg  dry;  beat 
into  it  one-third  a  cup  of  sugar  and  a 
teaspoonful  of  vanilla  or  other  flavor- 
ing, then  fold  in  one  cup  of  double 
cream,  beaten  solid,  to  the  bottom  of 
the  bowl. 

Fruit  Sponge 

.  This  may  be,  made  of  any  variety  of 
cooked  fruit,  as  apples,  pears,  peaches, 
apricots  or  quinces.  In  the  dish  shown 
in  the  illustration,  preserved  quinces 
were  used.  Press  the  cooked  fruit 
through  a  sieve;  to  one  cup  and  a  half 
of  fruit  puree,  add  the  juice  of  an 
orange,  the  juice  of  half  a  lemon,  four 
tablespoonfuls  of  sherry  wine  and,  with 
unsweetened  fruit,  as  apple,  one  cup 
of  sugar.  A  scant  fourth  a  cup  of 
sugar  was  used  with  the  quinces.  Add 
also  one  level  tablespoonful  and  a  half 
of  granulated  gelatine,  softened  in  one- 
third  a  cup  of  cold  water  and  dissolved 
over  hot  water.     Set  these  ingredients 


into  a  dish  of  water  and  crushed  ice; 
stir  occasionally  until  beginning  to  set, 
then  add  the  whites  of  three  eggs, 
beaten  dry,  and  beat  the  whole  with 
an  egg  beater  until  it  will  hold  its 
shape,  then  turn  into  a  mould.  Serve 
with  whipped  cream;  decorate  with 
fruit.  This  dish  may  also  be  made 
with  bananas.  Peel  the  bananas  and 
press  the  pulp  through  a  ricer  or  sieve ; 
add  the  cup  of  sugar  and  cook  in  a 
double  boiler  until  scalded  through- 
out; add  the  softened  gelatine  and 
when  cold  the  other  ingredients,  and 
finish  as  above. 

Newport  Tea  Cake 

Sift  together,  three  times,  four  cups 
of  sifted  flour,  half  a  teaspoonful  of 
salt,  six  level  tablespoonfuls  of  granu- 
lated sugar,  one  teaspoonful  of  soda 
and  two  slightly  rounding  teaspoonfuls 
of  cream  of  tartar.  Beat  three  eggs; 
add  a  cup  of  milk  and  stir  into  the  dry 
ingredients,  with  one-third  a  cup  of 
melted  butter.  Turn  into  a  buttered 
baking  sheet  and  let  bake  about 
twenty-five  minutes  in  a  quick  oven. 
Cut  into  squares  and  serve,  hot  or  cold, 
with  butter  and  cocoa,  for  supper  or 
luncheon. 


Fruit  Sponge 


Menus  for  a  Week  in  May 

One  man  may  require  vegetable  food  with  little  meat;  another  may  fare  best  on  meat  with 
little  vegetable  food;  still  another  may  live  on  animal  food  (milk  or  eggs)  alone,  btU  no  race  exists 
on  exclusive  vegetable  diet. — N.  B.  Jenkins,  M,D.

about  one-fiph  of  the  proteid  requirements  of  the  body,  they  promptly  produce  flatulence  and  failure 
of  nutrition.

Cookery  for  Young  Housekeepers 

By  Janet  M.  Hill 

Lesson  XI. 

Introducing  the  Great  Fuel  Foods:  Fats 


FATS  form  the  third  great  class 
of  food  stuffs;  these  are  rep- 
resented in  our  bodies  in  the 
form  of  fatty  tissue.  Fatty  tissue 
is  made  up  of  a  mass  of  albuminous 
cells,  containing  oil  and  held  together 
by  a  slight  framework  of  connective 
tissue.  The  oil  in  nuts,  olives  and 
seeds  is,  in  the  same  way,  held  in  cells 
connected  together  by  a  framework 
of  connective  tissue.  Fat  and  oil  are 
practically  the  same  thing,  fat  being 
oil  in  a  solid  state  and  oil  being 
fat   in   a   liquid   state. 

The  Uses  of  Fats  in  the  Body 

As  fats  contain  about  eighty  per 
cent  of  carbon,  largely  available  as 
a  source  of  heat,  fats  would  seem 
to  be  indicated  for  use  in  cold  rather 
than  in  warm  weather,  and  in  a 
cold  climate  rather  than  in  the 
reverse.  Still  this  compound  is  a  neces- 
sity for  people  even  in  hot  climates; 
for  growth  and  proper  nutrition  are 
dependent  upon  the  ingestion  of 
a  due  proportion  of  all  the  food 
principles.  Fats  lubricate  the  human 
machine  and  aid  in  building  up 
fatty  tissue,  which  serves  as  a 
cushion  for  the  nerves,  and  provides 
a  reserve  store  of  material  to  fur- 
nish heat  and  energy  in  time  of  need. 
During     convalescence     from     severe. 


acute  disorders,  and  while  suffering: 
from  chronic  wasting  diseases,  fats  in 
larger  measure  are  a  necessity.  In 
both  cases  an  easily  digested  form  of 
fat    is    called    for. 

Forms  of  Fats  Used  as  Food 

1.  Fatty  tissue  in  the  form  of 
bacon,  the  exterior  of  roasts,  steaks 
and  chops,  or  of  boiled  ham,  corned 
beef  or  mutton. 

2.  The  fat  that  is  cooked  from 
bacon  and  meats  (drippings),  the 
kidney  fat  of  beef  (suet)  and  pork, 
removed  by  heat  from  the  fatty 
tissues  and  clarified. 

3.  Oil  expressed  from  vegetables 
and  seeds,  as  olive  and  cotton-seed  oil. 

4.  Combinations  of  suet  and  vege- 
table  oil,  as  cottolene  and  cotosuet. 

5.  Butter    and    cream. 

Digestion  of  Fats 

Starch  is  digested,  largely,  in  the 
mouth,  proteid  in  the  stomach,  but 
fats  are  changed  but  little  until 
they  reach  the  small  intestine.  Here 
the  pancreatic  juice,  bile  and  intestinal 
juice  divide  the  fat  into  smaller 
and  still  smaller  droplets;  these  minute 
droplets  are  then  taken  up  by  the 
cellular  lining  of  the  intestine  and 
carried  to  the  blood.  Anything  that 
aids    in     the     division     of      the      oil. 


475 


476 


THE   BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


is  helpful  to  its  digestion  and  assimi- 
lation. Thus,  with  fat  and  oil,  we 
masticate  food  that  is  deficient  in 
these  compounds;  as  lean  meat,  bread, 
potato  and  other  vegetables,  which 
tend  to  separate  the  masses  of 
fatty  tissue  (bacon  and  the  like). 
Or,  we  spread  butter  upon  steak 
or  bread,  or  toss  hot  vegetables 
in  butter,  to  coat  them  with  it,  or 
turn  lettuce  and  other  green  vege- 
tables over  and  over  in  oil,  that 
each  leaf  may  glisten  with  it. 

Why  Pastry  and  Doughnuts  are 
Not  Easily  Digested. 

From  what  has  been  said  above, 
it  would  seem  that  pastry,  in  which 
starch  is  surrounded  by  fat,  and  dough- 
nuts, which  are  largely  starch,  cooked 
in  fat,  would  form  ideal  ways  of  pre- 
senting fat  to  the  system.  But  fats 
are  not  affected  by  either  saliva  or 
gastric  juice,  and  if  starch  be  per- 
meated, through  and  through,  with  fat, 
how  is  the  saliva  to  get  at  the  starch 
to  digest  it?  If  the  doughnuts  be  so 
made  and  fried  that  the  fat  does  not 
penetrate  the  dough,  the  principal  ob- 
jection to  this  form  of  food  is  re- 
moved, but  pastry  cannot  be  called  a 
hygienic  form  of  food.  It  should  be 
reserved  for  occasional  use  and  not 
presented  in  daily  bills  of  fare. 

DigestibiHty  of  Fats  and  Oils 

In  health  all  forms  of  fat,  in  moder- 
ation, are  usually  well  digested,  but 
they  produce  satiety  earlier  in  a 
meal  than  do  other  food  compounds. 
Good  butter,  cream  and  cod  liver 
oil  are  the  forms  of  fat  most  easily 
digested.  All  fats  heated  to  a  certain 
temperature  {i.  e.,  to  the  smoking 
point),  which  varies  with  the  kind 
of  fat,  become  decomposed,  and  some 
of  the  products  evolved,  when  the 
fat  is  ingested,  occasion  irritation 
of  the  mucous  membrane  throughout 
the  digestive  tract,  and,  consequently, 
indigestion.     Hence,  the  greatest  care 


must  be  taken  that  fats  be  not  over- 
cooked. This  is  one  reason  why  fried 
foods  may  be  harmful,  and  why 
butter,  which  is  quickly  affected  by 
heat,  is  more  wholesome  when  un- 
cooked than  when  cooked.  Used  with 
steak,  vegetables,  etc.,  the  heat  of  the 
article,  upon  which  it  is  spread,  is  all 
that  should  be  applied  to  it. 

Effect  of  Heat  on  Fats 

Melt  butter  or  drippings  over  the 
fire  and  they  will  foam  and  bubble, 
as  if  boiling;  continue  the  heating, 
and  the  bubbling  ceases,  the  fat  becom- 
ing still.  If  we  tested  the  fat  with 
a  thermometer  we  should  find  that, 
when  it  was  still,  the  temperature 
was  considerably  above  the  boiling 
point  of  water  (212°  F.),  and  that, 
when  it  was  bubbling,  the  tempera- 
ture was  about  that  of  boiling  water. 
When  fat  bubbles,  as  if  boiling,  it 
is  because  there  is  boiling  water  in 
it;  when  the  boiling  ceases,  water 
is  no  longer  present.  Fat  or  oil 
will  burn  before  the  boiling  point  is 
reached.  Where  all  the  water  present 
in  fat  or  oil  has  evaporated  and 
the  fat  is  still,  it  grows  hot  very 
fast,  rising  to  300°  or  400°  F.  Olive 
oil  may  be  heated  even  to  600°  F. 
If  we  put  a  piece  of  dough  into  fat 
that  is  bubbling,  we  find  that  it  absorbs 
fat  and  does  not  take  on  any  color; 
but,  if  we  put  the  dough  into  fat 
that  is  still,  it  becomes  brown  in  a 
few  moments,  and,  if  properly  mixed, 
does  not  absorb  fat.  In  reality,  the 
higher  the  temperature  to  which  we 
can  raise  the  fat  without  its  burning, 
the  more  quickly  will  the  exterior 
of  the -article  to  be  cooked  be  browned 
and  hardened  so  as  to  exclude  the 
fat.  Olive  oil  can  be  heated  to  a 
temperature  of  about  600°  F.  before 
it  burns,  and  thus  it  is  the  ideal  fat 
for  frying  purposes.  On  account  of 
the  cost  it  is  not  generally  used  for 
this  purpose.  Lard  is  the  fat  that 
is   next   best   for   frying,   then   comes 


COOKERY  FOR  YOUNG  HOUSEKEEPERS 


477 


drippings  from  meat,  then  suet,  while 
butter  is  the  least  desirable  medium 
for  frying  that  we  have. 

How  to  Test  the  Temperature  of 
Fat  for  Frying 

We  have  noticed  that  we  do  not 
speak  of  boiling  fat,  nor  do  we  wish 
to  speak  of  smoking  hot  fat;  for, 
when  fat  smokes,  it  is  burning  and 
is  too  hot  for  use;  nor  can  such  fat 
be  restored  to  proper  condition  for 
future  use.  Food  cooked  in  it,  espe- 
cially if  it  has  been  overheated  a  long 
time,  or  several  times,  will  not  take 
on  appropriate  color,  and  whatever 
fat  is  absorbed  by  the  article  will 
not  be  wholesome.  When  the  fat 
becomes  still,  have  ready  some  inch 
cubes  of  stale  bread  and  drop  one  of 
these  into  the  fat.  If  the  cube  of 
bread  becomes  a  golden  brown,  while 
you  count  forty,  as  the  clock  ticks, 
the  fat  is  at  the  right  temperature 
for  croquettes,  fish-balls,  small  fish, 
like  brook  trout  and  smelts,  or  small 
fillets  of  fish  and  oysters.  If  it  takes 
sixty  seconds  to  brown  the  cube  of 
bread,  the  fat  is  at  the  proper  temper- 
ature for  frying  fritters,  doughnuts 
and  other  similar  mixtures  of  uncooked 
ingredients.  You  may  think  the  fat 
should  not  be  as  hot  for  frying  fillets 
of  raw  fish  and  oysters  as  for  cro- 
quettes, made  of  cooked  ingredients, 
which  are  simply  to  be  reheated. 
But  fish  and  oysters  being  cold,  will 
cool  the  fat  so  that  longer  cooking 
will  be  needed  to  secure  the  right 
color  for  the  exterior,  and  the  proper 
cooking  of  the   article. 

Preparation  of  Food  for  Frying 

Heat,  as  we  know,  coagulates  and 
hardens  albuminous  substances;  thus, 
if  we  cover  an  article  to  be  fried  with 
a  substance  rich  in  albumen,  as  eggs, 
or  even  less  rich,  as  milk,  and  plunge 
it  into  hot  fat,,  a  covering  will  be 
produced  that  will  keep  out  the  fat 
perfectly.     With  the  egg,  we  use  sifted 


bread  crumbs;  with  the  milk,  we  use 
flour  or  meal.  The  starch  in  the  flour, 
meal  or  crumbs,  is  changed  by  the 
heat  to  caramel,  and  a  well-tasting 
crust  is  formed.  A  thin  coating  is 
all  that  is  essential.  The  juices  of 
chops  and  oysters,  thus  protected, 
can  not  pass  out  into  the  fat  any 
more  than  the  fat  can  enter,  thus 
such  articles  when  properly  fried  are 
particularly  juicy  and  well-flavored. 
It  should  be  needless  to  add,  that 
the  covering,  through  them,  must 
be  complete  at  every  point,  and 
that  the  fat  must  be  deep  enough 
to  cover  the  articles  to  be  cooked 
in  it. 

The  dough  for  fritters,  doughnuts 
and  the  like,  must  contain  enough 
egg  to  keep  out  fat  heated  to  the  proper 
temperature  for  frying.  Croquettes, 
oysters  and  the  like,  are  often  placed 
in  a  basket,  so  that  three  or  four 
may  be  lowered  into  the  fat  and 
removed  from  it  at  the  same  time. 
The  articles  must  not  touch  each 
other,  or  steam  will  be  produced, 
which  will  moisten  the  crisp  crust. 
Drain  carefully,  in  the  basket,  over 
the  kettle  of  fat,  then  remove  to 
soft  paper  for  the  final  draining. 

Trying  Out  and  Clarifying  Fats 

Cut  the  fat  in  small  pieces,  cover 
with  cold  water  and  let  stand  over 
night;  pour  off  this  water,  add  fresh 
water  or  milk  —  a  cup  to  each  two 
pounds  of  fat  —  and  let  cook  very 
slowly  until  the  pieces  are  light  brown 
in  color,  and  the  fat  is  clear  and 
still  (no  sound  of  bubbling  or  cooking) . 
Strain  through  a  cloth  and  press 
the  fat  from  the  pieces  for  a  second 
quality  of  fat. 

Fat  from  Cooked  Meat,  the  Soup 
Kettle,  etc. 

When  the  liquid  is  cold,  remove 
the  fat  to  a  saucepan,  add  part  or  a 
whole  cup  of  cold  water  and  let  cook 
slowly,  until  the  water  has  evaporated 


478 


THE   BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


and  the  sound  of  cooking  has  ceased, 
then  strain  through  a  cloth.  Much 
of  the  flavor  and  odor  of  the  fat 
passes  off  in  the  water,  during  its  evapo- 
ration. Slices  of  raw  potato  may  be 
used  instead  of  water  to  clarify  fat. 
It  is  probably  the  evaporation  of 
the  water  in  the  potato  that  is  respon- 
sible for  the  clarification. 

How  to  Treat  Fat  After  Frying 

After  the  frying  is  finished,  remove 
the  fat  to  a  cool  place  and  let  it  settle. 
Crumbs  or  flour  left  in  fat  and  reheated 
with  it  will  burn  long  before  the  fat 
is  hot  enough  for  frying,  and  spoil 
the  fat.  When  the  fat  is  cool,  pour 
it  carefully  from  the  sediment  in 
the  bottom,  through  one  or  two 
folds  of  cheese  cloth  laid  over  a  colander 
or  sieve,  add  cold  water  or  slices  of 
potato  and  clarify  as  above.  Wipe 
out  the  kettle  with  soft  paper,  wash 
and  dry,  and  when  the  fat  is  again 
cool,  return  it  to  the  kettle.  A  round- 
bottomed  Scotch  bowl  (iron)  is  the 
best  utensil  for  frying. 

Fats  and  Oils  Used  in  the  Preserva- 
tion of  Food 

Fats  and  oils  may  be  used  to  advan- 
tage in  the  preservation  of  many 
kinds  of  food;  these  by  excluding  air 
prevent  the  drying  or  decomposition 
of  the  food  that  is  covered  with  them. 
After  a  bottle  of  olives  or  pimentos 
has  been  opened  and  a  part  of  the  con- 
tents used,  the  remainder  may  be 
kept  as  long  as  the  oil  remains  in 
good  condition  —  often  six  months  or 
longer  —  by  simply  putting  into  the 
bottle  or  can  a  few  spoonfuls  of  oil 
that  may  float  —  a  thin  layer  of  oil  — 
on  the  top  of  the  liquid.  So  a  layer 
of  oil  over  fish — as  sardines  —  preserves 
them  for  a  long  time .  Potted  meats  and 
pate-de-foie-gras  are  kept  by  means  of 
lard  or  butter,  which  is  melted  and 
poured  over  the  articles,  when  they  are 
put  up.  Of  course  all  articles  preserved  in 
this  way  should  be  stored  in  a  cool  place. 


Use  of  Olive  Oil 

Our  largest  use  for  olive  oil  is  in 
the  form  of  salad  dressings.  Occa- 
sionally butter  or  cream  is  taken 
for  this  purpose.  Cream  is  well 
adapted  for  use  in  salad  dressings, 
but  as  butter  is  too  solid  to  coat 
cold  vegetables,  it  is  usually  made 
into  a  dressing  by  cooking ;  this  detracts 
from  its  digestibility,  and  thus  it 
were  preferable  not  to  consider  it 
in  this  connection.  The  dressings  that 
a  family  of  two  will  oftenest  elect 
are  called  French  and  mayonnaise 
dressings.  French  dressing  is  prefer- 
able with  fruit  and  green  vegetables, 
and,  also,  to  season  fish  and  meats, 
that  are  afterwards  to  be  served  with 
mayonnaise  dressing.  A  mayonnaise 
dressing  is  rich,  and  thus  is  used  prin- 
cipally at  luncheons  or  banquets,  while 
the  lighter  French  dressing  is  selected 
for  the  more  hearty  meal  of  dinner. 

Cream,  beaten  solid,  is  added  to  a 
mayonnaise  dressing,  or,  with  season- 
ings and  a  little  lemon  juice,  is  used  by 
itself  as  a  salad  dressing. 

French  Dressing 

One-fourth  a  teaspoonful  of  salt,  one- 
eighth  a  teaspoonful  of  pepper,  three 
tablespoonfuls  of  oil,  one  to  three  table- 
spoonfuls  of  lemon  juice  or  vinegar. 

The  ingredients  for  the  dressing 
may  be  mixed  and  poured  at  once 
over  the  salad  materials,  which  are 
then  turned  over  and  over  until 
the  dressing  has  been  taken  up  by 
them,  or,  the  condiments,  mixed  with 
the  oil,  may  be  first  used,  then,  after 
each  leaf  or  separate  piece  has  been 
thoroughly  coated  with  the  oil,  the 
acid  may  be  poured  on  and  the  salad 
turned  over  and  over  until  the  acid 
is  evenly  mixed  throughout. 

Mayonnaise  Dressing 

A  few  grains  of  cayenne  or  paprika, 
one-fourth  a  teaspoonful  salt,  the  yolk  of 
one  raw  egg,  one  cup  of  olive  oil,  one 


FAMOUS  RECIPES  OF  PIONEER  SPANISH  SETTLERS 


479 


tablespoonful  of  lemon  juice,  one  table- 
spoonful  of  vinegar. 

Mix  the  salt  and  pepper,  add  the 
yolk  of  egg,  and  beat  until  thickened 
a  little;  add  the  lemon  juice  and 
vinegar  gradually.  Use  a  Dover  egg 
beater,  and  beat  in  the  oil,  a  teaspoon- 
ful  at   a  time.     After  a  time  the   oil 


may  be  added  by  the  tablespoonful. 
Beat  thoroughly  after  each  addition 
of  oil,  as  also  during  the  time  the 
oil  is  being  taken  up  by  the  mix- 
ture. 

Cover  the  dressing  with  an  earthen 
or  glass  dish,  and  let  stand  in  a  cool 
place  until  ready  to  use. 


Famous  Recipes  of  Pioneer  Spanish  Settlers 

ByS.  S.  H. 


SPANISH  BEEFSTEAK  (from  a 
caterer's  note-book) .  Lay  a  slice  of 
upper  round  steak,  about  two  or 
two  and  one-half  pounds,  rather  more 
than  two  inches  thick,  on  a  deep  pie 
plate,  leaving  on  the  rim  of  suet;  add 
a  small  teacup  of  water  and  baste 
every  ten  minutes  for  thirty  minutes, 
baking  in  a  moderate  oven;  remove 
from  the  oven,  sprinkle  with  a  tea- 
spoonful  of  salt  and  half  a  saltspoonful 
of  pepper,  and  cover  with  a  layer  of 
sliced  onions  (dust  top  with  salt  and 
bake  fifteen  minutes  longer) .  Sprinkle 
with  a  saltspoonful  of  salt,  a  dash  of 
pepper,  and  cover  with  a  layer  of 
chopped  tomatoes,  or  the  solid  meat 
from  a  can  of  tomatoes.  Dust  with 
salt  and  pepper  and  bake  again  fifteen 
minutes.  Sprinkle  over  it,  then,  two 
tablespoonfuls  of  grated  cheese,  salted, 
and  place  in  oven  long  enough  for 
cheese  to  melt  nicely.  There  will  be 
a  thick,  rich  gravy  on  it,  deliciously 
flavored  with  onions  and  tomatoes,  and 
the  steak  should  be  very  tender. 

Chili  Sauce  (won  Gold  Medal  at 
Centennial).  Boil  two  gallons  of  to- 
matoes one  hour;  add  two  quarts  of 
chopped  onions,  two  cups  of  sugar,  one- 
half  a  cup  of  mustard  seed.  Put  into 
a  bag  or  tie  in  cloth  one  tablespoonful 
of  whole  black  pepper,  one  tablespoon- 
ful, each,  of  allspice  berries  and  cinna- 


mon bark,  and  add  to  the  tomatoes ;  also 
add  one  teaspoonful  of  cayenne  pepper, 
salt  to  taste,  and  three  green  and 
three  ripe  peppers,  chopped.  Boil 
four  hours  and  then  add  two  quarts  of 
vinegar  and  boil  one  hour  longer. 
This  sauce  put  through  a  sieve  makes 
a  delicious  Spanish  catsup.  Bottle  as 
usual. 

Spanish  Sweet  Potato  Pone  {re- 
cipe originally  came  from  the  island  of 
Barhadoes) .  Wash,  peel  and  grate  the 
best  quality  of  sweet  potatoes.  Meas- 
ure five  cups  into  a  large  bowl.  Into 
this  stir  three  cups  of  best  West  India 
molasses,  two  cups  of  butter  (melt  the 
butter  carefully  and  do  not  let  it  get 
oily),  one  cup,  each,  of  preserved 
ginger  and  preserved  orange  peel,  cut 
fine,  one  teaspoonful  of  salt,  same  of 
ground  ginger,  and  two  tablespoonfuls 
of  mixed  spices,  cinnamon,  mace, 
cloves,  and  a  little  allspice.  Mix  all 
these  ingredients  thoroughly,  then  pour 
the  pone  into  a  well-greased  pan  and 
bake  in  a  moderately  hot  oven.  Try 
it  with  a  thin  knife,  and  when  the  blade 
comes  out  clean  take  it  from  the 
oven.  Let  cool  before  taking  from 
the  pan.  It  should  look  dark,  like  a 
rich  preserve,  when  properly  baked. 

Alligator  Pear  Salad  (jrom 
Mexico).     Take     two     large     alligator 


480 


THE   BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


pears,  peel  and  remove  stone;  cut  in 
one-half  inch  cubes,  sprinkle  with  salt, 
add  two  tablespoonfuls  or  more  of  best 
olive  oil  (slowly)  with  or  without  a  very 
small  piece  of  onion,  minced  fine,  to 
flavor.  Place  in  a  salad  dish  already  pre- 
pared with  crisp  lettuce  leaves.     Serve. 

Frijoles.  Put  one  pint  of  pink 
beans  to  soak  over  night,  then  put 
them  on  to  boil  in  salted  water  until 
tender.  While  they  are  cooking,  fry 
two  good-sized  onions  in  bacon  fat 
and  add  half  a  can  of  tomatoes.  Take 
six  or  eight  red  chili  peppers  that  have 
been  cleaned,  and  part  of  the  veins 
removed,  unless  one  likes  them  very 
"hot,"  put  them  in  the  water  and  let 
boil  a  few  minutes,  then  scrape  off 
the  red  jelly-like  chili  from  the  tough 
skin  and  add  this  with  the  onions  and 
tomatoes  to  the  beans.  Let  all  boil 
together  several  hours  until  very  soft, 
stirring  frequently  to  prevent  burning. 
(For  American  use  cone-shaped  cro- 
quettes can  be  made  and  fried,  and 
they  are  fine.) 

Asparagus  a  l'Espagnol.  Wash 
two  bunches  of  asparagus  and  tie  each 
bunch  in  three  bundles,  having  the 
tips  all  one  way.  Cover  with  boiling 
water  and  cook  about  twenty-five 
minutes.  Place  in  a  saucepan  two 
level  tablespoonfuls  of  butter,  one  small, 
fine-chopped  onion,  a  bay  leaf  and 
six  pepper  corns;  cook  without  brown- 
ing, then  add  three  level  tablespoon- 
fuls of  flour  and  two  cups  of  white 
stock  (either  chicken  or  veal),  season, 
add  a  pinch  of  nutmeg  and  let  the 
sauce  simmer  fifteen  minutes.  Beat 
the  yolks  of  two  eggs  very  light,  and 
mix  with  one  tablespoonful  of  lemon 
juice.  Strain  the  sauce,  return  it  to 
the  saucepan  and  place  where  it  will 
keep  warm,  but  not  boil.  Add  very 
gradually  the  eggs  and  lemon  juice. 
Stir  well,  and  add  a  scant  tablespoonful 
of  butter.  Serve  at  once  in  a  bowl, 
with  the  asparagus  on  toast. 


Genuine  Spanish  Chicken  Tam- 
ALES  (Madame  Yglesias).  For  one 
dozen  good-sized  tamales:  Use  one 
chicken,  one  pound  of  lard,  half  a 
pound  of  red  chilis,  two  quarts  of  white, 
dry  corn,  one  pint  of  olives  and  one 
dozen  com  husks.  Prepare  the  com 
the  night  before,  by  putting  to  boil  in 
enough  water  to  cover,  with  half  a  cup 
of  lime,  dissolved  in  a  little  water. 
Do  not  put  in  the  hard  pieces  of  lime 
that  settle  in  the  cup.  Boil  about 
fifteen  minutes,  take  from  the  fire  and 
let  stand  that  way  until  next  morning. 
Then  put  in  clean,  cold  water  and  rub 
the  corn  with  the  hands  until  it  is 
cleaned  well.  Wash  two  or  three 
times  more  in  cold  water  and  it  is 
ready  to  be  ground.  Grind  the  corn 
in  any  grinder,  the  old  way  being  on  a 
flat  stone,  caljed  a  met  ate.  Cut  up 
the  dressed  chicken  and  boil  until 
quite  tender;  season  with  salt.  In  the 
meantime  remove  seeds  from  chilis, 
and  put  them  in  deep  stewing  pan 
with  hot  water;  cook  until  tender, 
cool,  drain  off  water  and  grind  to  a 
pulp.  Put  a  clove  of  garlic  in  chili, 
if  desired.  To  prepare  the  chili,  after 
it  is  ground,  place  in  a  stew  pan  with 
a  large  spoonful  of  flour  and  a  salt- 
spoonful  of  salt,  stirring  so  it  will  not 
bum ;  put  in  chili  and  cook  five  minutes ; 
then  put  in  boned  and  cut  chicken  and 
stoned  olives.  Let  all  come  to  a  boil; 
take  from  fire  and  cool.  To  prepare 
dough,  take  a  large  pan  or  basin,  put 
in  two  cups  of  lard  and  beat  it  with 
the  hands.  When  real  light  put  in 
corn,  beat  a  few  minutes,  drop  some 
of  the  broth  in  which  the  chicken  was 
boiled,  a  little  at  a  time,  beating 
constantly  until  the  dough  is  soft  and 
smooth.  Season  with  salt  to  taste. 
Drop  a  bit  of  the  dough  into  a  glass  of 
water;  if  it  rises  to  the  top,  it  is  done, 
if  not,  beat  it  a  little  longer.  Have 
some  dry  com  husks  well  soaked  for 
several  hours  in  cold  water;  shake 
them  well  and  apply  a  thin  layer  of 
the  dough  on  the  half  of  each  leaf ;  then 


DANDELION  BLOSSOMS 


481 


put  a  spoonful  of  prepared  stew  on  the 
dough  and  roll  with  more  prepared 
leaves.  Tie  the  ends  with  strings 
made  of  leaves.  Then  place  in  a  large 
pot  with  two  pints  of  boiling  water; 
steam  gently  for  two  or  three  hours. 
Soak  in  warm  water  a  clean  dish  towel, 
spread  on  top  of  the  tamales  just 
before  putting  on  the  cover.  To  pre- 
vent burning,  place  a  few  hard  ends 
of  corn  husks  on  bottom  of  pot  before 
putting  in  the  tamales.  Flank  of  beef 
or  other  meat  may  be  substituted  for 
chicken. 

Baked  Green  Peppers,  with  Span- 
ish Rice.  Wash  and  cook  one  cup  of 
rice  until  tender;  chop  one  onion,  one 


red  pepper  and  two  green  peppers  very 
fine,  and  fry  slowly  in  frying  pan,  with 
two  small  slices  of  bacon.  When 
onion  is  tender,  add  three  tomatoes  or 
same  amount  of  canned  tomatoes,  and 
let  cook  five  minutes;  add  the  cooked 
rice,  and  enough  more  salt  to  make 
the  seasoning  right,  and  cook  for  five 
minutes  again.  Take  half  a  dozen  of 
bell  or  bull-nosed  peppers,  wash  and 
cut  stem  end  off  and  remove  seeds. 
Place  in  baking  pan  and  stuff  with  the 
Spanish  rice.  Put  a  small  lump  of 
butter  on  top  of  each  pepper,  cover 
with  crumbs  and  bake  until  the 
peppers  are  tender.  Serve  hot.  Fresh 
tomatoes  may  be  stuffed  and  baked  in 
the  same  manner. 


Dandelion    Blossoms 

By  Agnes  Lockhart  Hughes 

Little  buttons  of  burnished  gold, 

Clasping  a  mantle  of  green. 
Over  the  russet  gown  of  earth, 

With  jewels  sown  between. 

Dandelion  blossoms  kissed  by  the  sun, 

Stealing  his  rays  of  light. 
And  hiding  them  'midst  your  yellow  curls. 

While  daisies  pale  in  affright. 

Nothing  daunted  by  wind  or  storm, 

Bravely  you  face  the  gale. 
Till  winter  chills  your  pulsing  hearts. 

And  your  tiny  suns  grow  pale. 

Then  away  drifts  your  gold  in  a  fluffy  down, 
And  in  silver  you  gleam  on  the  spray, 

Till  the  Frost  King,  blowing  his  icy  breath, 
Scatters  your  white  forms  away. 

Away  o'er  the  waves  of  the  laughing  brook 

That  sweetly  to  me  doth  sing, 
And  I  know  that  again,  in  your  yellow  frocks. 

You  will  dance  with  the  blossoms  of  spring. 


Contributions  to  this  department  will  be  gladly  received.     Accepted  items  will  be 
paid  for  at  reasonable  rates. 


Pie  Kinklets  Worth  Knowing 

FIRST  of  all,  to  be  successful  in 
pie  making,  one  must  have  good 
materials,  and  manipulate  them  judi- 
ciously ;  no  matter  how  much  shorten- 
ing, is  used,  if  too  much  moisture  is 
added,  the  crust  will  be  hard  and 
tough. 

Put  water  in  by  the  spoonful  rather 
than  risk  having  it  wet;  pie  crust 
should  be  so  dry  that  it  is  really  diffi- 
cult to  roll,  then  it  will  be  light  and 
flaky  when  baked,  and  it  also  browns 
prettier. 

Next  time  you  make  crust,  add  one- 
half  a  teaspoonful  of  baking  powder, 
and  one  tablespoonful  of  sugar  to  the 
flour  for  each  pie. 

Before  baking  fruit  pies  with  a  cover 
on,  put  a  tiny  funnel  made  of  white 
writing  paper  in  the  opening;  the 
juice  cooks  up  through  it,  and  does 
not  run  out  over  the  pie  or  oven. 

Another  good  idea  is  to  sprinkle 
over  the  fruit  one  tablespoonful  of 
sago,  or  manioca,  before  the  cover  is 
put  on;  it  absorbs  the  liquid,  and 
makes  the  pie  quite  rich. 

The  next  time  you  make  apple  pie, 
put  bits  of  butter  on  the  lower  crust 
before  the  apples  are  put  in,  instead 
of  on  top  of  the  apples;  pour  over  the 
apples  half  a  cup  of  very  weak  tea 
instead  of  plain  water,  then  a  thin 
coating  of  brown  sugar  and  a  slight 
grating  of  nutmeg,  then  the  upper 
crust,  and  you  will  never  make  it  any 
other  way. 

Seedless  or  seeded  raisins  are  a  great 


addition  to  cocoanut  pies ;  sprinkle  one- 
half  a  cup  of  them  over  the  crust,  then 
pour  in  the  cocoanut  mixture;  they 
impart  a  delicious  flavor. 

To  Test  Eggs 

An  interesting  experiment  may  be 
tried  to  see  if  eggs  are  fresh.  Fill  a 
finger  bowl  with  cold  water  and  put 
an  egg  in  it. 

A  strictly  fresh  egg  feels  quite 
heavy  in  the  hand,  and  immediately 
goes  to  the  bottom  of  the  bowl,  and 
stays  there  on  its  side. 

When  three  weeks  old,  it  lies  almost 
on  the  pointed  end,  but  still  keeps  under 
the  water. 

If  the  egg  is  three  months  old,  it 
stands  directly -on  the  point,  not  quite 
touching  the  bottom  of  the  vessel,  and 
with  the  round  end  slightly  above  the 
water. 

When  older  than  that  it  bobs  up 
and  down,  and  nearly  half  of  the  egg 
comes  above  the  water,  point  down, 
round  end  up. 

City  folks  will  hardly  credit  it  that 
farmers  and  storekeepers  sell  such 
old  eggs,,  but  they  do,  and  frequently. 

Stale  eggs  feel  very  light,  held  in  the 
hand,  and  the  shells  present  a  dull 
appearance,  very  easy  to  detect  when 
once  observed. 


An  easy  way  to  make  icing,  that  never 
disappoints,  is  as  follows:  Put  the 
white  of  one  large  egg  into  a  bowl  and 
a  pinch  of  salt;  add  gradually  half  a 
pint  of  pulverized  sugar,  beating  it 
until  smooth  and  shiny ;  then  beat  into 


482 


HOME  IDEAS  AND  ECONOMIES 


483 


it  half  a  teaspoonful  of  vanilla  and 
two  teaspoonfuls  of  cold  water,  and 
it  is  ready  to  spread  on  the  cake  with 
a  silver  knife.  If  the  flavor  of  orange 
or  lemon  is  enjoyed,  grate  the  yellow 
rind  into  the  beaten  egg  and  sugar, 
and  use  three  teaspoonfuls  of  the 
juice,  then  beat  well  again.  When 
three  teaspoonfuls  of  juice  are  used, 
the  water  is  to  be  omitted,  of  course. 


Brush  the  lower  crust  of  rhubarb' 
and  berry  pies  with  unbeaten  white  of 
egg,  and  it  will  not  get  so  wet  and 
"soggy,"  and  thus  be  much  more 
palatable  and  digestible.         s.  h.  r. 


Food  from  Queer  Sources 

FOOD  for  man  has  often-  been  ob- 
tained from  queer  sources. 

In  Central  America,  for  instance, 
there  is  the  bread  tree  and  the  milk 
tree,  the  former  having  a  solid  fruit 
a  little  larger  than  a  cocoanut,  which 
when  cut  into  slices  and  cooked  can 
hardly  be  distinguished  from  excellent 
bread.  The  milk  tree  has  a  thick, 
tough  skin  that  can  be  used  for  half- 
soling  shoes,  and  the  tree  is  milked 
by  boring  into  the  trunk,  when  it  will 
let  down  sap  as  white  and  sweet  as 
any  milk  from  a  :ow. 

In  Africa  there  is  a  tree  that  yields 
butter.  While  not  as  good  as  that 
churned  from  cream  it  can  be  made 
somewhat  similar  in  taste  by  salting. 

In  South  America  there  is  a  bird 
known  as  "the  oil  bird,"  from  which 
a  species  of  butter  can  be  obtained. 
One  of  its  favorite  haunts  is  the  island 
of  Trinidad.  It  breeds  in  rocky  caves 
on  the  mainland,  laying  its  eggs  in  a 
nest  constructed  of  mud.  The  young 
birds  are  extraordinarily  fat,  and  the 
fat,  having  been  melted  down  in  clay 
pots,  produces  a  kind  of  butter. 

A  few  years  ago  Professor  Berthelot, 
a  French  savant,  announced  at  a  dinner 
in  Paris  that  a  large  portion  of  our  food 
products   could   be    directly   manufac- 


tured from  the  constituent  elements, 
carbon,  oxygen,  nitrogen  and  hydrogen. 
Our  milk,  eggs  and  flour  will  eventu- 
ally be  made  in  factories.  It  is  now 
over  fifteen  years  since  a  Paterson, 
N.J.,  man  was  able  to  manufacture 
eggs  by  artificial  means  so  nearly  like 
the  real  thing  that  it  is  said  even  the 
hens  themselves  were  deceived. 

J.    A.    M. 


KEEPERS  of  boarding-house  tables 
make  the  mistake  of  serving  chicken 
soup  and  stewed  chicken  for  Sunday 
dinner.  It  is  too  much  of  a  good  thing. 
The  chicken  stock  will  keep  until 
another  day.  It  can  be  skimmed  free 
of  fat  when  cold.  This  is  good  fat 
when  clarified  for  many  purposes,  and 
saves  butter  and  lard.  Serve  the 
chicken  soup  with  some  other  kind  of 
meat  the  next  day.  Make  an  inex- 
pensive cream  of  potato  or  vegetable 
soup  on  Sunday,  to  offset  the  cost  of 
the  chicken. 


One  can  hardly  grumble  at  the  cost 
of    living    in    Philadelphia    when,    at 
tables  that  make  the  modest  charge 
of   twenty-five   cents   a   meal,  such  a 
Sunday  dinner  as  follows  is  to  be  had: 
Soup  with  egg-balls 
Stewed  Chicken.     Potatoes 
Greens.    Cucumbers.     Dressed  Lettuce 
Asparagus  on  Toast 
Whole  Wheat  and  White  Bread 
Coffee  and  Vanilla  Ice  Cream.     Home- 
made Cake.     Tea 


Here  are  two  ways  to  keep  the  juice 
in  pies  from  running  out  into  the 
oven,  while  baking. 

First  is  one  that  we  have  used  many 
years  with  success.  When  the  pie  is 
all  finished,  fasten  the  two  crusts  at 
the  edge  securely  together,  then  take 
a  strip  of  white  cloth,  one  inch  wide 
and  long  enough  to  reach  around  the 
pie  and  lap  a  little,  dip  in  cold  water 
and  bind  the  edge  of  the  pie.  Place  the 
pie  in  the  oven,  and  when  baked  re- 


484 


THE   BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL  MAGAZINE 


move  the  strip  of  cloth.  Your  pie  will 
come  out  good  and  whole,  and  no  one 
will  ever  suspect  that  it  had  been 
bandaged. 

The  second  is  a  very  satisfactory 
way,  which  we  have  only  recently 
learned.  Place  the  top  crust  on  the 
pie  and  do  not  fasten  it  down  at  all. 
The  steam  will  escape  from  between 
the  crusts,  and  the  juice  will  not  run 
out.  p.  c.  H. 


after  washing.     Wipe  dry  with  a  soft 
cloth  and  it  is  ready  to  serve,  g.  m.s. 


To  Freshen  Lettuce 

IN  our  magazines,  I  often  read  topics 
on  '*How  to  freshen  lettuce,"  but 
I  have  found  a  method  which  has 
proven  better  than  any  other  I  ever 
tried.  It  is  this:  Wash  the  lettuce  in 
cold  water,  and  if  you  can  do  it  under 
running  water  so  much  the  better,  as 
that  tends  to  cleanse  it  more  easily. 
After  it  is  thoroughly  cleaned,  put  it 
in  a  large  earthen  dish,  and  cover  it 
with  another  earthen  dish  as  tightly 
as  possible.  Let  stand  until  you  are 
ready  to  use  it,  and  you  will  find  the 
lettuce  even  more  crisp  than  when 
first  picked  from  the  garden.  Do  not 
put  any  water  into  the  dishes  aside 
from  that  which  clings  to  the  leaves 


THE  long  drudgery  of  much  dish- 
washing may  be  greatly  lightened 
by  the  following  method: 

Have  the  water  very  hot  and  soapy. 
After  washing  the  glasses  and  silver 
and  polishing  them,  place  a  large  pan 
of  cold  water  in  the  sink  between  the 
dish-pan  and  the  drainer.  Using  the  mop, 
quickly  cleanse  each  earthen  dish  in  the 
hot  suds,  dip  rapidly  in  the  cold  water  and 
set  in  drainer.  The  water  in  the  pan  will 
still  be  hot  for  the  tin- ware,  when  the 
table  dishes  are  all  in  the  drainer. 

Turn  out  the  cold  water  from  the 
middle  pan,  and,  leaving  the  crockery- 
still  in  the  drainer,  wash  the  tins,  drain- 
ing them  in  the  cold-water  pan.  They 
may  be  wiped  first  on  their  own  cloth. 
The  other  dishes  will  now  be  all  dry 
and  shining  and  will  need  only  a  hasty 
inspection,  a  rub  here  and  there,  before 
being  put  away. 

The  cold  water  rinses  away  all  the 
suds,  thus  preventing  streaks  on  the 
dishes,  and  the  sudden  change  in  tem- 
perature dries  them.  They  will  be 
found  beautifully  clean  and  bright. 

G.    D.    B, 


My  Choice 

By  James  Whitcomb  Riley 

It  ain't  no  use  to  grumble  and  complain; 

It's  jest  as  cheap  and  easy  to  rejoice; 
When  God  sorts  out  the  weather  and  sends  rain, 

W'y,  rain's  my  choice. 

In  this  existence,  dry  and  wet 

Will  overtake  the  best  of  men  — 
Some  little  skift  o'  clouds  '11  shet 
The  sun  off  now  and  then; 

They  ain't  no  sense  as  I  can  see, 
Immortals  sich  as  you  and  me, 
A-faultin'  Nature's  wise  intents, 
And  lockin'  horns  with  Providence. 

It  ain't  no  use  to  grumble  and  complain; 

It's  jest  as  cheap  and  easy  to  rejoice; 
AVhen  God  sorts  out  the  weather  and  sends  rain, 

W'y  rain's  my  choice. 


THIS  department  is  for  the  benefit  and  free  use  of  our  subscribers.  Questions  relating  to 
recipes,  and  those  pertaining  to  culinary  science  and  domestic  economics  in  general, 
will  be  cheerfully  answered  by  the  editor.  For  menus  remit  $1.00.  Communications 
for  this  department  must  reach  us  before  the  first  of  the  month  preceding  that  in  which  the 
answers  are  expected  to  appear.  In  letters  requesting  answer  by  mail,  please  enclose  postage 
stamps.  Address  queries  to  Janet  M.  Hill,  Editor  Boston  Cooking-School  Magazine, 
372  Boylston  Street,  Boston,  Mass. 


Query  1344. —  Continued. 

Roman  Punch  No.  2 

Boil  one  quart  of  water  and  one  pint 
of  sugar  fifteen  minutes.  Count  the 
time  after  boiling  begins  and  let  boil 
rapidly.  When  cold,  add  one  cup  of 
lemon  juice  and  freeze  as  ice  cream. 
Just  before  serving  beat  in  thoroughly 
half  a  cup  of  Jamaica  rum,  and,  if  con- 
venient, one  cup  of  Italian  meringue. 
This  meringue  is  the  same  as  boiled 
frosting  and  is  usually  added  to  all 
frozen  punches,  but  may  be  omitted. 


Query  1345.  —  New  Haven:  "Recipes 
for  Tomato  Rabbit,  Curry  of  Chicken  and 
Celery,  and  Pineapple  Sponge." 

Tomato  Rabbit 

Melt  two  tablespoonfuls  of  butter 
in  a  blazer  or  double  boiler;  add  one 
pound  of  cheese,  grated  or  cut  fine, 
and  stir  until  the  cheese  is  melted, 
adding  meanwhile  half  a  teaspoonful, 
each,  of  salt,  paprika  and  soda.  Beat 
the  yolks  of  four  eggs  (two  whole  eggs 
may  be  substituted) ;  add  a  cup  of 
tomato  puree  (cooked  tomatoes  pressed 
through  a  sieve  to  exclude  skin  and 
seeds)  and  stir  into  the  cheese  mix- 
ture. Stir  constantly  until  the  mix- 
ture thickens.  The  mixture  should 
not  boil  from   start  to   finish;    when 


boiling  seems  imminent,  set  the  hot 
water  pan  in  place.  Serve  on  the  un- 
toasted  side  of  bread  toasted  upon  but 
one  side. 

Curry-of-Chicken  and  Celery 
Have  ready  a  cup  of  cooked  celery 
(cut  in  half-inch  slices  and  simmered 
till  tender  in  water  to  cover)  and  a 
cup  and  a  half  of  cooked  chicken,  cut 
in  small  pieces.  Melt  four  table- 
spoonfuls  of  butter  and  in  it  cook  two 
slices  of  onion  until  yellowed  and  soft- 
ened, but  not  browned;  take  out  the 
onion,  add  one  level  tablespoonful  of 
curry  powder,  four  level  tablespoonfuls 
of  flour,  half  a  teaspoonful  of  salt  and 
a  slight  dash  of  paprika;  stir  until 
frothy  and  bubbling,  then  add  one 
cup  of  chicken  broth,  three-fourths  a 
cup  of  nut  milk  (or  plain  milk  with- 
out the  admixture  of  nuts)  and  two 
rounding  tablespoonfuls  of  currant 
jelly;  stir  and  cook  until  smooth  and 
boiling,  then  add  the  chicken,  celery, 
and  a  tablespoonful  of  lemon  juice. 
When  the  mixture  boils  it  is  ready  to 
serve.  Plain  boiled  rice  should  accom- 
pany the  dish,  but  crackers  or  toast 
may  be  used.  For  the  nut  milk 
grind  one-half  a  cup  of  blanched 
almonds  or  a  full  cup  of  fresh  cocoanut, 
as  fine  as  possible,  then  add  to  a  gen- 
erous   cup    of   milk  scalded   over   hot 


485 


486 


THE    BOSTON    COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


water;  let  stand  an  hour  or  longer, 
then  press  through  a  piece  of  cheese 
cloth.  The  milk  found  in  the  cocoanut 
may  be  used. 

Pineapple  Sponge 

Put  a  pound  can  of  grated  pine- 
apple and  a  cup  of  boiling  water  over 
the  fire,  and  when  boiling  stir  in  half 
a  cup  of  a  quick-cooking  variety  of 
tapioca;  continue  stirring  until  the 
mixture  boils  vigorously',  then  cover 
and  let  cook  over  boiling  water  until  the 
tapioca  is  transparent,  adding  a  second 
cup  of  boiling  water,  if  needed.  Add 
three-fourths  a  cup  of  sugar,  half  a  tea- 
spoonful  of  salt,  the  juice  of  a  lemon,  and 
when  well  mixed  fold  in  the  whites  of 
from  two  to  four  eggs  beaten  dry ;  cover 
and  let  cook  over  the  the  hot  water  a  few 
minutes  to  "set"  the  egg.  Serve  with 
whipped  cream  or  a  cold,  boiled  custard. 


Query  1346.  —  L.  Z.,  Chicago:  "Direc- 
tions for  making  a  Chocolate  Roll.  The 
roll  has  a  cream  filling  and  is  decorated  with 
chocolate." 

Chocolate  Roll 

Roll  puff  or  flaky  pastry  into  a  rec- 
tangular sheet  about  ten  inches  wide. 
Some  hollow  metal  or  wooden  tubes, 
ten  inches  in  length  and  between  two 
and  three  inches  in  diameter,  are 
needed  (solid  pieces  of  wood  may  be 
used).  Cut  the  paste  into  strips  of  a 
width  just  to  fit  the  tubes;  press  the 
paste  together  where  it  meets  on  the 
tubes,  set  onto  a  baking  sheet  and 
bake  in  a  moderate  oven,  then  slip  at 
once  from  the  tubes.  Boil  a  cup  of 
sugar  and  half  a  cup  of  water  to  a 
syrup,  and  in  it  melt  an  ounce  of  choco- 
late; add  a  teaspoonful  of  vanilla  and 
stir  in  confectioner's  sugar  to  make 
an  icing  that  will  remain  in  place. 
When  the  pastry  cylinders  are  cold, 
cover  the  outside  with  the  chocolate 
icing  and  let  stand  to  dry.  Beat  a 
cup  of  cream  solid;  beat  in  one-fourth 
a  cup  of  sugar  and  a  teaspoonful  of 
vanilla  and  use  to  fill  the  cvlinders. 


Query     1347.  —  Subscriber,  Cleveland, 

Ohio:    "Recipe    for    Scalloped  Fish  and  a 

relish  to  be  served  with  it  as  a  fish  course, 
also  for  Maple  Syrup  Fudge." 

Scalloped  Fish  as  Fish  Course 

Free  the  fish  from  skin  and  bones 
and  separate  it  into  flakes  (with  a 
silver  fork)  while  it  is  hot.  Into  a 
buttered  dish,  or  individual  dishes, 
put  a  layer  of  the  fish;  above  this  a 
layer  of  left-over  dressing  or  sauce; 
or,  if  these  be  lacking,  use  the  liquid  in 
which  the  fish  was  cooked;  or,  cook 
the  head  and  trimmings  of  the  fish  with 
a  sliced  onion  and  pieces  of  carrot  in 
water  to  cover,  and  set  aside  until 
needed  for  the  scalloped  dish.  Over 
the  fish  and  liquid  sprinkle  a  very  thin 
layer  of  buttered  bread  crumbs  (from 
center  of  a  loaf,  not  dry  crumbs). 
Repeat  the  layers,  having  buttered 
crumbs  on  top.  Bake  till  the  crumbs 
are  brown.  Serve  at  the  same  time 
rolls,  peas,  cuctimbers,  with  French 
dressing,  seasoned  with  a  few  drops 
of  onion  juice  or  chopped  chives.  Or, 
substitute  for  the  cucumbers,  Philadel- 
phia relish,  cole  slaw,  or  sour  pickles. 

Maple  Syrup  Fudge 

Boil  three  cups  of  maple  syrup  and 
one  cup  of  sweet  cream,  or  half  a  cup 
of  butter  until,  when  tested  in  cold 
water,  a  soft' ball  may  be  found.  Beat 
with  a  silver  fork  or  perforated  wooden 
spoon  until  the  mixture  begins  to 
thicken,  then  turn  into  a  buttered 
pan.     A  cup  of  nuts  may  be  added. 


Query  1348.  — Mrs.  C.  M.  D.,  Waterbury, 
Ct.:  "Recipe  for  Bouch^es,  a  delicate,  crisp 
muffin  served  with  afternoon  tea." 

Bouchees 

Bouchees  are  tiny  puff-paste  patties. 
They  are  .about  an  inch  and  a  half  in 
diameter.  A  recipe  for  puff  paste  was 
given  on  page  194,  Vol.  X,  of  this 
magazine.  The  recipe  is  too  long  to 
repeat  here.  Minute  directions  for 
making  puff  paste  are  also  given  in 
** Practical  Cooking  and  Serving."

Query  1349.  —  T.  R.  F.,  Clifton,  Arizona: 
"Recipe  for  Lady  Baltimore  Cake,  also  a 
rough  frosting  of  caramel  color  and  con- 
taining nuts," 

Lady  Baltimore  Cake 
For  the  cake,  take  one  cup  of  butter, 
two  cups  of  granulated  sugar,  three 
and  one-half  cups  of  sifted  flour,  two 
level  teaspoonfuls  of  baking  powder, 
one  cup  of  milk,  one  teaspoonful  of  rose 
water  and  the  whites  of  six  eggs.  For 
the  frosting,  take  three  cups  of  granu- 
lated sugar,  one  cup  of  boiling  water, 
the  whites  of  three  eggs,  one  cup  of 
chopped  raisins,  one  cup  of  chopped  nut 
meats  and  five  figs,  cut  into  bits.  Mix 
the  cake  in  the  usual  manner ;  the  ingre- 
dients are  enumerated  in  order.  For 
the  frosting,  cook  the  sugar  and  water 
until  when  tested  the  syrup  will  spin 
a  thread  two  inches  long;  pour  in  a  fine 
stream  onto  the  whites  of  the  eggs 
beaten  dry;  add  the  fruit  and  use 
when  cold  as  filling  and  frosting. 

Rough  Frosting  of  Caramel  Cake 

The  frosting  desired  is  given  among 
the  seasonable  recipes.  It  is  shown 
in  the  illustration  of  Newport  Cake, 
with  caramel  nut  frosting. 


Query  1350.  —  Mrs.  C.  E.  D.,  St.  Johns, 
Canada:  "Are  Mayonnaise  and  French  Dress- 
ing one  and  the  same  thing  and  must  they 
be  made  with  oil.  Is  the  recipe  (enclosed) 
a  good  one  and  can  it  be  called  mayonnaise 
dressing  and  used  in  all  recipes  calhng  for 
mayonnaise  or  French  dressing? 

Regarding  Salad  Dressings 

French  and  mayonnaise  dressings  are 
two  distinct  dressings,  both,  however, 
have  olive  oil  as  their  basis.  We  think 
the  recipe  sent  may  make  a  good  dress- 
ing, if  it  be  put  together  properly.  It 
would  be  called  a  "boiled  dressing"  or 
a  mock  mayonnaise.  Mayonnaise  is  an 
emulsion  of  oil.  The  boiled  dressing 
is  used  in  the  place  of  mayonnaise 
and  not  in  place  of  French  dressing. 
In  making  the  boiled  dressing  do  not 
add  the  eggs  until  after  the  starch  has 


been  cooked  ten  or  fifteen  minutes  in 
the  other  ingredients.  Cooked  butter 
is  not  particularly  wholesome,  while 
olive  oil,  used  as  a  dressing,  is  exceed- 
ingly wholesome ;  the  prices  of  the 
butter  and  oil  are  about  the  same, 
especially  if  the  oil  be  bought  in  bulk 
rather  than  in  small  bottles.  Why 
not  learn  to  like  the  oil?  For  further 
particulars  on  this  subject,  see  article 
on  another  page,  "Cookery  for  Young 
Housekeepers." 


Query  1351.  —  C.  V.  P.,  Cincinnati,  Ohio: 
"Recipes  for  Cucumber  and  Grape-fruit 
Jellies.  Where  may  tomato-shaped  moulds 
for  tomato  jelly  be  purchased?  " 

Grape-Fruit  Jelly 

Soften  a  generous  tablespoonful  of 
granulated  gelatine,  or  half  an  ounce 
of  shredded  gelatine,  in  half  a  cup  of 
cold  water,  and  dissolve  by  pouring  on 
one-fourth  a  cup  of  boiling  water.  Add 
one-fourth  a  cup  of  sugar  and  one  cup 
and  a  half  of  grape-fruit  pulp  and 
juice.  If  desired,  two  or  three  table- 
spoonfuls  of  sherry  or  maraschino  and 
a  teaspoonful  of  lemon  juice  may  also 
be  added.  Soften  six  or  eight  candied 
cherries  in  boiling  water;  then  cut 
them  in  slices  to  form  rings.  Set  five 
or  six  moulds  in  a  pan  of  ice  and  water 
(aluminoid  moulds  chill  very  quickly). 
With  a  steel  skewer  dip  the  rings  of 
cherry  in  the  grape-fruit  mixture,  and 
set  them  on  the  chilled  sides  and  bot- 
toms of  the  moulds,  to  which  they  will 
adhere.  Then  fill  the  moulds  with 
the  grape-fruit  mixture.  Put  the  mix- 
ture into  the  moulds  a  little  at  a  time, 
to  avoid  having  the  pieces  of  fruit 
settle  to   the   bottom   of  the  moulds. 

Cucumber  Jelly 

Pare  two  cucumbers  and  cut  in 
slices.  Add  a  slice  of  onion,  a  stalk 
of  celery,  half  a  tablespoonful  of 
nasturtium  seeds,  a  piece  of  green 
pepper-pod,  and  one-fourth  a  teaspoon- 
ful of  sweet  herbs,  with  water  to  cover. 
Let    simmer    until    the    cucumber    is 


488 


THE    BOSTON   COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


tender,  then  press  through  a  very  fine 
sieve.  Season  with  salt,  pepper,  and 
tarragon  vinegar  or  lemon  juice  as 
needed.  Then  add,  for  each  pint  of 
liquid,  one-third  a  package  of  gelatine, 
softened  in  one-third  a  cup  of  cold 
water  and  dissolved  over  hot  water. 
Tint  delicately  with  green,  vegetable 
color-paste,  and  turn  into  moulds,  to 
harden.  Serve  with  any  salad  dress- 
ing, in  the  same  ways  that  tomato 
jelly  is  used. 

Tomato -Shaped  Moulds 

Tomato-shaped  moulds  can  proba- 
bly be  procured  from  a  kitchen  fur- 
nishing store  in  any  of  the  larger 
cities.  For  a  store  in  Boston  see  our 
advertising  pages. 


Query  1352.  —  Subscriber,  Fall  River, 
Mass.:  "How  may  French  Fried  Potatoes 
be  cooked  so  as  to  be  crisp  and  tender?  " 

Crisp,  Tender  French  Fried  Potatoes 

Pare  the  potatoes  and  cut  them 
into  lengthwise  pieces,  similar  in  shape 
and  thickness  to  the  sections  of  an 
orange.  Let  the  prepared  potato  stand 
in  ice  cold  water  an  hour  or  more. 
Have  ready  a  kettle  of  fat  that  has  not 
been  used  for  frying  (or  used  but  once 
or  twice) ;  wipe  the  potatoes  on  a  soft 
cloth,  a  few  at  a  time,  and  drop  at  once 
into  the  hot  fat.  Let  cook  about  five 
minutes,  then  drain  on  soft  paper,  sprin- 
kle with  salt  and  serve.  The  fat  should 
not  be  too  hot,  as  the  pieces  of  potato 
should  not  brown  until  cooked  tender. 
Do  not  select  too  large  potatoes.  Fry 
at  one  time  as  many  pieces  as  will  float 
on  the  top  of  the  fat  without  inter- 
fering, one  with  another. 

Query  1353.  —  C.  B.  P.,  Allston,  Mass. : 
"Should  there  be  cream  of  tartar  and  soda 
or  baking  powder,  in  the  flour  cakes  given 
in  March  number  of  the  magazine?  " 

Leavening  in  Flower  Cakes 

No  leavening  agent  is  used  in  the 
flour  cakes;  the  recipe  is  correct  as 
published. 


Query  1354. —M.  J.  K.,  Bangor,  Me.: 
"Recipe  for  cooking  Artichokes." 

Jerusalem  Artichokes 

Jerusalem  artichokes  resemble  pota- 
toes in  appearance,  but  in  composition 
they  are  more  like  turnips.  Pare  and 
throw  into  cold  water,  to  keep  them 
from  turning  black.  Set  to  cook  in 
boiling,  saltfed  water.  When  nearly 
tender  (twenty  or  twenty-five  minutes) 
prepare  a  white  sauce  —  a  cup  is 
enough  for  four  or  five  potatoes  — 
using  half  cream  and  half  water,  in 
which  the  artichokes  were  cooked,  as 
the  liquid.  Drain  the  cooked  arti- 
chokes, shake  over  a  hot  stove-lid  to 
dry  off,  then  turn  into  a  hot  dish  and 
pour  the  sauce  over'  them.  A  little 
onion  or  lemon  juice,  or  both,  may  be 
added  to  the  sauce. 

Jerusalem  Artichokes  with  Meat 

Pare  and  trim  the  artichokes  and 
cut  them  into  halves;  bring  to  the 
boiling  point  in  boiling,  salted  water, 
and  let  boil  two  or  three  minutes, 
drain  and  dispose  around  a  roast  of 
beef  or  mutton.  Cook  about  thirty- 
five  minutes,  basting  with  the  liquid 
in  the  pan  each  ten  minutes. 

Jerusalem  Artichokes  au  Gratin 

Boil  the  artichokes  as  usual,  then 
cut  into  cubes.  Dispose  the  cubes  in 
an  earthen  dish  in  layers,  alternately, 
with  cream  or  Bechamel  sauce;  sprin- 
kle each  layer  with  grated  cheese; 
cover  the  last  layer  with  bread  or 
cracker  crumbs  mixed  with  melted 
butter,  and  set  the  dish  into  the  oven, 
to  brown  the  crumbs. 


Query  1355.  —  Subscriber,  Havana,  111. : 
' '  When  turkey  or  fowl  of  any  kind  is  served 
on  the  plate,  from  the  kitchen,  by  a  caterer, 
is  a  knife  placed  with  the  other  silver  at  the 
right  of  the  plate.  Recipe  for  Gherkin 
Pickles." 

Knife  in  a  Course  Dinner 

Presumably  the  turkey  or  other 
fowl  would  be  tender  enough  to  be  cut

HONEST  GOODS 
HONEST  LABELS 
—THEN  QUALITY 

Mother's  pastry  was  always  good  because 
her  shortening  was  always  good  and  the 
only  shortening  she  knew  was  leaf  lard— 
genuine  leaf  lard.  That's  exactly  what 
"Simon  Pure"  Lard  is,  only  it  really  is 
better  than  home-made  lard  can  be  be- 
cause of  the  uniform  excellence  found 
on  A?  in  "Simon  Pure."  Besides,  mother 
could  not  get  as  good  raw  leaf  as  we  do. 
We  have  the  pick  of  thousands  of  pieces 
daily — she  had  only  what  her  butcher 
could  give  her.  It's  due  to  these  spe- 
cially selected,  crinkly,  edible  leaves  — 
refined  by  honest.  Quality-producing 
methods — that  "Simon  Pure'*  is  the  fin- 
est lard  on  earth — the  "Cream  of  Short- 


ening." Sold  only  in  Goverrmient  Inspected 
and  Sealed  Pails,  three's,  five's  and  ten's.  If 
you  wish  to  better  mother's  pastry  you  must 
better  her  shortening.  You  can  by  using  Simon 
Pure  —  many  a  mother  says  so  herself,  and 
she  ought  to  know.    So  will  you  if  you  try  it. 


FRIED  food  is  as  nutritious  as  that 
which  is  baked  or  boiled,  in  spite 
of  the  solemn  protests  of  faddists 
who  declare  that  it  is  most  in- 
digestible. If  it  proves  unhealthful,  the 
cause  may  be  traced  to  one  of  two  things 
—the  frying  process  has  been  unskill- 
fully  performed,  or  more  often,  the  lard 
used  was  of  an  inferior  quality. 

"Frying"  means  immersing  the  article 
to  be  cooked  in  fat  that  covers  it.  In 
the  last  analysis  it  is  steaming.  The 
moment  the  article  to  be  cooked  touches 


Golden  Ball  Fritters 


"Simon  Pure"  Pop-Overs 
the  fat,  its  surface  becomes  coagulated, 
making  it  impossible  for  the  natural 
juices  to  get  out.  These  are  turned  into 
steam,  which  cooks  the  food.  The  fat 
merely  browns  the  outer  surface.  About 
three  pounds  of  Armour's  Simon  Pure 
Leaf  Lard  will  be  required  for  use  in  a 
kettle  eight  inches  in  diameter.  This 
may  appear  to  be  an  extravagance  to  a 
housekeeper  whose  idea  of  frying  con- 
sists in  greasing  the  bottom  of  a  frying 
pan  with  a  tiny  bit  of  fat.  As  a  mat- 
ter of  fact,  frying  in  deep  fat  is  an  econ- 


omy as  well  as  an  absolute  necessity 
from  the  standpoint  of  heahh. 

The  fat  should  not  be  boiling,  but 
"smoking"  hot.  A  good  general  rule 
regarding  temperature  is  this:  If  a  slice 
of  raw  potato  browns  in  it  in  from  40  to 
60  seconds,  the  fat  is  ready  for  use.  Gen- 
erally speciking,  doughnuts  and  batters 
require  a  lower  heat  than  breaded  meats, 
and  the  latter  do  not  require  as  high  a 
temperature  as  potatoes,  fish  and  all  wat- 
ery articles,  v/hich  must  be  fried  at  the 
highest  possible  temperature.

with  the  fork,  but  the  knife  is  usually 
set  in  place  for  all  roasts  of  meat. 
A  knife  is  not,  however,  usually  pro- 
vided for  fish. 

Sour  Gherkin  Pickles 

Wash  the  cucumbers  (select  those 
of  small  size)  and  throw  them  into 
salted  water  to  remain  over  night. 
If  two  cups  of  salt  be  used  to  six 
quarts  of  water,  the  pickles  may  be 
kept  in  the  brine  some  time.  A  cup 
of  salt  to  a  gallon  of  water  is  a  good 
proportion,  when  the  pickles  are  to 
stand  in  it  not  more  than  thirty-six 
hours.  Scrub  and  wipe  the  pickles, 
throw  them  into  an  earthen  jar  with 
cloves,  small  red  peppers,  peppercorns, 
mace,  nasturtium  and  white  mustard 
seeds.  Half  a  cup  of  mixed  seeds  and 
spices  is  enough  for  a  gallon  of  pickles. 
Scald  vinegar  and  pour  over  the  cuctmi- 
bers;  cover  and  let  stand  a  week  or 
longer;  much  depends  on  the  strength 
of  the  vinegar.  When  ready  to  store, 
drain  off  the  vinegar,  and,  if  strong,  use 
to  scald  other  pickles.  Pack  the  pickles 
in  glass  fruit  jars;  add  fresh  spices 
and  seeds  and  scald  a  fresh  supply 
of  vinegar  and  pour  over  the  pickles 
in  the  jars.  Let  the  jars  stand  on  a 
cloth  wrung  out  of  boiling  water,  while 
the  vinegar  is  being  poured  into  them. 
Fill  the  jars  to  overflow  and  cover  as 
in  canning  fruit.  The  pickles  will 
keep  indefinitely.

be  pleased  to  receive  information  on 
this  subject. 


Dandelion  Wine 

Take  three  quarts  of  dandelion 
blossoms,  four  quarts  of  boiling  water, 
three  and  a  half  pounds  of  sugar,  and 
three  or  four  sliced  lemons.  Pour  the 
boiling  water  over  the  blossoms,  cover 
and  let  stand  over  night.  In  the 
morning  strain  off  the  water,  add  the 
sugar  and  lemon  and  set  over  the  fire;

Col.:  "Recipes  for  Dandelion  Wine  and

like  recipe  for  the  peppers  in  which  olive  oil

A  Food  Drink 

Which  Brings  Daily  Enjoyment 

A  lady  doctor  writes: 

"Though  busy  hourly  with  my  own 
affairs,  I  will  not  deny  myself  the  pleas- 
ure of  taking  a  few  minutes  to  tell  of 
my  enjoyment  daily  obtained  from  my 
morning  cup  of  Postum.  It  is  a  food 
beverage,  not  a  stimulant  like  coffee. 

"I  began  to  use  Postum  eight  years 
ago,  not  because  I  wanted  to,  but  be- 
cause coffee,  which  I  dearly  loved, 
made  my  nights  long  weary  periods  to 
be  dreaded  and  unfitting  me  for  busi- 
ness dtiring  the  day. 

"On  advice  of  a  friend,  I  first  tried 
Postum,  making  it  carefully  as  sug- 
gested on  the  package.  As  I  had 
always  used  'cream  and  no  sugar,'  I 
mixed  my  Postum  so.  It  looked  good, 
was  clear  and  fragrant,  and  it  was  a 
pleasvire  to  see  the  cream  color  it  as 
my  Kentucky  friend  always  wanted  her 
coffee  to  look  —  'like  a  new  saddle.* 

"Then  I  tasted  it  critically,  for  I 
had  tried  many  *  substitutes '  for  coffee. 
I  was  pleased,  yes,  satisfied  with  my 
Postum  in  taste  and  effect,  and  am 
yet,  being  a  constant  user  of  it  all 
these  years.  I  continually  assure  my 
friends  and  acquaintances  that  they 
will  like  it  in  place  of  coffee,  and  re- 
ceive benefit  from  its  use.  I  have 
gained  weight,  can  sleep  and  am  not 
nervous."  "There's  a  Reason."  Name 
given  by  Postum  Co.,  Battle  Creek 
Mich.  Read  "The  Road  to  Wellville,' 
in  pkgs.

heat  the  mixture  just  enough  to  dis- 
solve the  sugar,  pour  into  a  jar,  cover 
with  cheese  cloth  and  let  stand  in  a 
warm  place.  In  four  or  five  days 
it  will  begin  to  ferment.  After  about 
two  weeks  fermentation  will  cease, 
then  strain  into  bottles  and  cork 
securely. 

Canned  Red  or  Green  Peppers 

(Mrs.  Gibson.) 

Cut  off  a  piece  around  the  stem,  and 
with  scissors  remove  the  veins  and 
seeds;  then  cut  round  and  round  the 
pepper  to  form  ribbon-like  strips.  The 
peppers  may  be  put  up  whole,  but  a 
smaller  number  will  fill  a  jar.  Cover 
the  peppers  with  boiling  water ;  imme- 
diately drain,  and  repeat  the  scalding 
process,  if  a  very  mild  flavor  be  desired. 
Cover  the  peppers  with  ice  water,  leav- 
ing a  large  piece  of  ice  in  the  dish. 
Boil  one  cup  and  a  half  of  cider  vinegar 
and  three-fourths  a  cup  of  sugar  to  a 
thick  syrup.  Drain  the  peppers  and 
pack  them  closely  in  glass  jars;  over 
them  pour  the  hot  syrup  to  fill  the  jars 
to  overflow.  Put  on  the  covers  as  in 
canning  fruit.  The  jars  may  be  opened 
as  often  as  wished  and  the  peppers  will 
keep  until  used. 

Canned  Peppers 

We  think  the  peppers  may  be  canned 
without  sugar,  as  any  fruit  or  fruit- 
juice  is  canned,  then  when  a  can  is 
opened  for  use,  pour  in  olive  oil  to 
cover  the  liquid  in  the  jar;  the  oil 
excludes  air,  thus  preserving  the  pep- 
pers. Olives  and  pim-olas  may  be 
kept  for  an  indefinte  time  in  this  man- 
ner, and  we  see  no  reason  why  the 
peppers  may  not  be  kept  in  the  same 
way.

Built  Right 

Brain  and  Nerves  Restored  by  Grape-Nuts  Food 

The  number  of  persons  whose  ail- 
ments were  such  that  no  other  food 
could  be  retained  at  all  is  large,  and 
reports  are  on  the  increase. 

"For  twelve  years  I  suffered  from 
dyspepsia,  finding  no  food  that  did 
not  distress  me,"  writes  a  Wisconsin 
lady.  "I  was  reduced  from  145  to 
90  pounds,  gradually  growing  weaker, 
until  I  could  leave  my  bed  only  a  short 
while  at  a  time,  and  became  unable  to 
speak  aloud. 

' '  Three  years  ago  I  was  attracted  by 
an  article  on  Grape-Nuts  and  decided 
to  try  it. 

"My  stomach  was  so  weak  I  could 
not  take  cream,  but  I  used  Grape-Nuts 
with  milk  and  lime  water.  It  helped 
me  from  the  first,  building  up  my  sys- 
tem in  a  manner  most  astonishing  to 
the  friends  who  had  thought  my  recov- 
ery impossible. 

"  Soon  I  was  able  to  take  Grape-Nuts 
and  cream  for  breakfast,  and  lunch  at 
night,  with  an  egg  and  Grape-Nuts  for 
dinner. 

"I  am  now  able  to  eat  fruit,  meat 
and  nearly  all  vegetables  for  dinner, 
but  fondly  continue  Grape-Nuts  for 
breakfast  and  supper. 

"At  the  time  of  beginning  Grape- 
Nuts  I  could  scarcely  speak  a  sentence 
without  changing  words  around  or 
'talking  crooked'  in  some  way,  but  my 
brain  and  nerves  have  become  so 
strengthened  that  I  no  longer  have 
that  trouble."  "There's  a  Reason." 
Name  given  by  Postum  Co.,  Battle 
Creek,  Mich.  Read  "The  Road  to 
Wellville,"  in  pkgs.
