How  Lady  Betty  was  Captured 

{Continued  from  page  459.) 

Lady  Betty  gave  a  start  at  the  words, 
for  she  had  a  horror  of  Hstening  to  a 
conversation  not  meant  for  her  ears. 
For  one  moment  she  was  about  to 
announce  herself,  then  in  a  flash  it 
came  upon  her,  that  a  man  innocent 
of  wrong,  save  defending  himself  and 
his  neighbors  without  the  Governor's 
official  permission,  was  to  be  arrested 
—  and  would  die,  probably,  if  the 
arrest  were  made. 

"I  will  do  your  bidding,  and  will 
bring  back  a  caged  bird,"  was  the 
reply,  as  the  person  to  whom  the 
charge  was  given  turned  and  dis- 
appeared in  the  darkness. 

Sir  William  stood  rooted  to  the  spot 
for  a  moment. 

"For  the  King's  sake  I  do  this  deed! 
For  the  King's  sake,"  he  muttered, 
as  he  made  his  way  out  of  the  shadows 
toward  the  house. 

Swiftly  there  came  to  the  girl's 
recollection,  Nurse  Bevis'  words  —  how 
Nathaniel  Bacon's  father  had  saved 
her  grandfather's  life  at  Marston  Moor. 
Yes,  she  owed  him  a  debt;  and,  besides, 
was  it  rebeUious  to  fight  the  Indians 
who  were  at  enmity  with  the  Virginians? 
Could  it  be  true  —  what  people  whis- 
pered —  that  Sir  William  cared  more 
for  the  beaver  skins  the  savages 
brought  him  than  for  the  safety  of  the 
colony  ? 

"Colonel  Bacon  is  gone  back  to 
Jamestown!  Oh,  what  can  I  do,  what 
can  I  do?"  In  the  agony  of  her  in- 
decision, she  put  her  hand  up  to  her 
breast  and  felt  the  seal  ring  her  father 
had  given  her,  and  which  bore  these 
words,  "Fides  Ejicit  Malum" — Faith 
banishes  evil. 

The  words  seemed  written  in  the 
stars  above  her,  borne  on  the  wind 
through  the  poplars.  Now  she  knew 
the  meaning  of  the  motto:  she  would 
go  and  warn  Mr.  Bacon.  No  harm 
could   come   to   her,   if   she   did   what 


was  right,  trusting  in  God's  love. 
Fides  Ejicit  Malum  —  Faith  banishes 
evil! 

She  ran  swiftly  to  the  stable  and 
unloosed  her  own  pony.  Prince  Charlie, 
from  his  stall;  and,  when  the  dancers 
heard  a  few  minutes  later,  through  the 
pauses  of  violin  and  rebeck,  the  thud 
of  swift  horse  hoofs,  they  never  dreamed 
that  the  little  girl,  in  whose  honor  they 
had  assembled,  was  riding  furiously 
through  the  darkness  to  warn  "Nat 
Bacon,  the  Rebel,"  of  his  danger. 
•         •••••

The  Importance  of  Exercising  Dis- 
cretion in  Cookery 

Mr.  George  Harvey,  editor  of  the 
North  American  Review,  has  again 
found  it  necessary  to  remind  wives  of 
the  painful  fact  that  too  many  husbands 
still  die  at  an  early  age  through  igno- 
rance in  catering.  To  cook  is  one 
thing,  to  provide  the  right  sort  of  food 
is  another.  Mr.  Harvey  deplores  the 
amount  of  carelessness  that  continues  to 
be  displayed  on  the  part  of  so  many 
wives  in  the  preparation  and  selection 
of  their  husbands'  meals,  and  which 
accounts  for  the  large  number  of  com- 
paratively young  widows  the  world 
over. 


Foods  absorb 
foul  odors.

fnd  sickness  by 
Keeping  in  your 
refrigerator  a 
sponge  sprinkled 
occasionally  with 
Plans  Chlorides, 
^ash  the  sponge 
m  a  week  !

Moral  Dogmas 

Many  an  old-time  Methodist  is  grieved 
by  the  action  of  the  New  York  confer- 
ence in  voting  to  expunge  the  ironclad 
article  in  the  Methodist  Episcopal 
Book  of  Discipline,  forbidding  church 
members  to  play  cards,  dance,  attend 
a  theater  or  watch  a  horse  race.  And 
yet  we  have  no  doubt  that  the  confer- 
ence has  expressed  the  sound  judg- 
ment of  the  vast  majority  of  the  church- 
men represented  by  its  delegates.  It 
has  simply  placed  the  burden  of  moral 
responsibility  where  it  belongs,  upon 
each  individual.  As  several  promi- 
nent clergymen  have  hastened,  some- 
what needlessly,  to  point  out,  the 
abrogation  of  the  article  would  not 
imply  the  encouragement  or  even  the 
justification  of  any  of  the  practices 
heretofore  prohibited.  It  would  mean 
that  the  general  conference  recognized 
the  futility  of  laying  down  cut  and 
dried  rules  of  conduct  for  intelligent 
persons.  The  attitude  of  such  a  large 
body  of  representative  churchmen  is  a 
fresh  indication  of  the  rapidly  growing 
moral  sensitiveness  of  Americans.  It 
is  becoming  clear  that  the  right  and 
wrong  of  card  playing,  dancing  and 
theater-going  can  be  determined  only 
by  a  consideration  of  the  attendant 
circumstances  in  each  particular  case. 
Sweeping,  dogmatic  prohibitions  not 
Only  fly  in  the  face  of  reason  but  fail  to 
control  the  action  of  those  on  whom 
they  are  enjoined. 

—  New  York  Tribune. 


Men  Live  Longer 

A  mortality  record  of  16,000  grad- 
uates of  Yale,  from  1792  to  1901,  just 
compiled  by  a  Yale  alumnus  with 
actuarial  tastes,  shows  that  the 
nineteenth  -  century  rate  was  better 
than  that  of  the  eighteenth  century, 
and  the  last  fifty  years  of  the  nine- 
teenth century  better  than  the  first 
fifty  years.  Naturally,  a  liberally 
educated    man    will    take    advantage

Our  New  Type 
of  Range  a  HIT! 


Maintaining  the  Crawford  reputation  for  progress  we 

have  constructed  a  new  type  of  Range  which  we  are 

making  in  two  sizes — the  "Palace"  and  "Castle."     These 

Ranges  are  alike  excepting  as  to  size,  and  while  possessing  all 

of  the  usual  distinctive  features  of 

retwfbrd 

^oking-Rajiges 


Single  T>amper  —  T>ock'JJsh  Qraies  — 
Cup-Joint  Oven  Flues  —  Perfected  Oven 
—  Improved  Oven  Indicator — 

This  range  is  made  without  the  end  hearth 
and  with  more  room  on  top.  These  features 
make  for  economy  in  space  and  convenience  in 
cooking,  and  the  novel  method  of  disposing  of 

the  ashes  —  which  in  these  Ranges  fall  into  a 

hod  far  below  the  grate  —  makes  their  removal 

easier  and  prevents 

the  grates  from 

being  warped  and 

burned  out. 


The  Coal  Hod  and  Ash  Hod  are  side  by  side, 
of  same  size,  and  when  the  ash  hod  is  emptied  it 
can  be  returned  full  of  coal.

The  ICE  CRUSHER  for  CONVENIENCE 

AT    LOW    COST 

There's  not  much  fun  in  making  your  own  crushed 
ice  under  ordinary  conditions.  It's  disagreeable  work, 
dirty,  chilly,  and  unpleasant  all  around  the  board. 
And  even  then,  the  ice  you  crush  isn't  uniform,  but 
it's  "lumpy" — sometimes — and  tainted  with  odors  of 
the  cloth  or  napkin  in  which  it  is  crushed.  No,  the 
proper  way  to  make  crushed  ice  is  by  means  of  the 

Little  Giant  Ice  Crusher. 

Then  all  you  have  to  do  is  to 
put  the  ice  in  the  Crusher,  and 
turn  the  handle — it's  so  easy 
a  child  could  do  it — and  there 
you  have  the  finest,  most  uni- 
form crushed  ice  you  could 
imagine.  No  disagreeable  nor 
unpleasant  work  —  no  chilly 
hands — with  the  Little  Giant 
Ice  Crusher — the  only  time  you 
touch  the  ice  is  when  you  put 
it  in  the  hopper.  Then  the 
cost  isn't  much  either,  consid- 
ering all  the  convenience,  and 
the  machine's  durability.  The 
Little  Giant  Ice  Crusher  costs 
only  $7.50  —  it  is  so  sturdily 
and  strongly  built  it  will  last  for  at  least  15  years, 
therefore  its  cost  is  only  50  cents  per  year. 

CHOICE  RECIPE  BOOK  FREE

if  he  hasn't  one  in  stock,  write  us,  giving  us  his  name 
and  address,  and  enclosing  $7.50,  and  we'll  ship  you 
your  Little  Giant  Ice  Crusher  direct,  and  we'll  send  you 
our  interesting  little  book,  telling  all  about  the  Little 
Giant  and  containing  some  splendid  recipes  for  sher- 
bet, ices,  etc.,  etc.  This  valuable  recipe  book  is  ex-

Mark  Twain  on  Longevity 

On  the  occasion  of  Mark  Twain's 
seventieth  birthday  celebration,  at  Del- 
monico's  Restaurant,  New  York,  a  host 
of  friends  and  admirers,  comprising  the 
most  notable  literary  gathering,  dined 
together,  and  hailed  the  illustrious 
septuagenarian  as  America's  foremost 
poet,  philosopher  and  humorist. 

His  humor  on  the  above  occasion  was 
more  apparent  than  his  philosophy, 
for  we  read  that,  in  responding,  Twain 
made  a  witty  speech  describing  how, 
by  following  his  rule,  everybody  could 
live  to  be  seventy.  Tobacco  must  be 
used  in  moderation,  therefore  he  only 
smoked  one  cigar  at  a  time.  He  went 
to  bed  with  regular  irregularity,  and 
never  got  up  until  he  was  obliged.  Be- 
ing careful  in  the  matter  of  diet  in 
recent  years,  he  had  stopped  frolicking 
with  mince-pies  after  midnight. 


Industrial  Education 

Again,  industrial  education  has  a 
moral  and  ethical  value.  It  is  calcu- 
lated to  help  individuals  and  races  keep 
their  feet  upon  the  earth.  It  is  mighty 
important  in  the  early  career  of  any 
race  (as  is  also  true  of  an  individual) 
that  it  be  taught  to  stick  to  real  things 
rather  than  yield  to  the  temptation  of 
getting  into  an  artificial  atmosphere. 
From  a  moral  point  of  view  it  is  a  dan- 
gerous thing  for  a  man  to  increase  his 
wants  through  literary  training  alone, 
without  at  the  same  time  increasing 
his  ability  to  supply  those  wants  along 
industrial  lines  at  which  he  can  find 
employment.  In  a  country  like  ours, 
occupied  as  it  is  by  different  races, 
industrial  training  has  a  value  in  help- 
ing to  solve  racial  difficulties.  Every 
individual  has  respect  for  the  man, 
regardless  of  color  or  race,  who  can 
perform  some  kind  of  service  well  — 
better    than    the    other    fellow.     The

Cheer  Up ! 

Fear  kills.     Hope  makes  alive. 

Fear  is  a  paralyzing  poison.  Hope, 
the  best  of  tonics. 

Fear  is  darkness,  death,  despair. 
Hope  is  sunshine,  life  and  energy. 

Fear  has  saved  nobody.  Hope  has 
rescued  multitudes  whose  cases  seemed 
beyond  help. 

But  what  is  this  monster.  Fear? 

Absolutely  nothing  but  a  mental 
fancy,  a  shadow,  a  psychologic  disease. 
Fear  is  not  a  person  nor  a  force.  It  has 
power  to  harm  no  one  without  his 
assistance  and  co-operation  in  yielding 
to  its  blighting  influence. 

Fear  is  purely  a  creature  of  the  mind. 
It  does  not  exist  as  a  fact,  unless  self- 
created. 

"Fear-thought "  is  a  destructive  force 
only.  It  builds  but  obstacles,  produces 
but  scarecrows,  ghosts  and  bugaboos. 

Fear  is  the  ugly  offspring  of  pessi- 
mism and  cowardice,  an  enemy,  a  mis- 
chief-maker, a  sneak-thief,  a  false 
prophet. 

"Fear-thought"  wastes  energy,  cre- 
ates disease,  robs  you  of  peace,  and 
predicts  failure  when  success  is  just  at 
hand. 

The  foundation  of  hope  for  the  sick 
man,  for  every  man  in  trouble,  is  the 
fact  that  the  beneficent  Power  that 
made  us  is  still  interested  in  us,  and 
working  out  His  own  problems  for  us, 
and  will  do  the  best  He  can  for  each  of 
us. 

Every  spreading  tree  is  evidence  of 
God's  power  and  presence;  each  bloom- 
ing flower  a  proof  of  his  love  and 
gracious  care;  every  germinating  seed

One  by  one,  the  lesser  and  younger 
members  of  the  great  group  of  nine- 
teenth-century American  literary  men 
are  dying.  Edmund  Clarence  Sted- 
man  dropped  dead  of  heart  failure  in 
January,  less  than  a  year  after  Thomas 
Bailey  Aldrich  had  uttered  his  last 
words,  "Turn  down  the  light.  I  am 
going  to  sleep  at  last."  Mr.  Stedman 
was  probably  more  widely  known  as  a 
critic  and  anthologist  than  as  a  poet. 
It  was  not  generally  known  that  litera- 
ture was  his  avocation  and  banking  and 
broking  his  business.  He  had  a  seat 
on  the  New  York  Stock  Exchange  for 
many  years. 


The  Undiscovered  Country 

By  Edmund  Clarence  Stedman 

Could  we  but  know 
The    land    that    ends    our    dark,    uncertain 
travel, 
Where  lie  those  happier  hills  and  meadows- 
low; 
Ah!  if  beyond  the  spirit's  inmost  cavil 
Aught  of  that  country  could  we  surely 
know, 

Who  would  not  go  ? 

Might  we  but  hear 
The  hovering  angels'  high  imagined  chorus,. 
Or  catch,  betimes,  with  wakeful  eyes  and' 
clear 
One  radiant  vista  of  the  realm  before  us  — 
With  one  rapt  moment  given  to  see  and 
hear. 

Ah,  who  would  fear? 

Were  we  quite  sure 
To  find  the  peerless  friend  who  left  us  lonely, 
Or  there,  by  some  celestial  stream  as  pure, 
To  gaze  in  eyes  that  here  were  lovelit  only  — 
This  weary  mortal  coil,  were  we  quite  sure. 
Who  would  endiu*e  ? 
—  Courtesy  of  Houghton,  Mifflin  &  Co.,  copyrighted.

Mrs.  Hill's  Summer  Classes 

THE  ultimate  test  of  any  course  of 
training  is  the  resulting  ability 
to  do  or  execute.  If  one  sets  out 
to  cook,  or  to  teach  cooking,  even 
after  a  college  training,  and  does  not 
know  how  to  attain  practical  results, 
either  the  training  or  the  individual 
will  be  criticised,  though  neither  may 
be  at  fault.  We  learn  to  do  by  doing, 
and  she  who  is  to  teach  cookery  cannot 
have  had  too  much  experience  in  the 
actual  practice  of  cooking. 

Until  one  has  prepared  a  dish  with 
her  own  hands,  many  times,  perhaps, 
and  noted  the  effect  of  this  or  that  pro- 
cedure, she  cannot  be  certain  of  the 
results  or  have  the  courage  to  tell 
others  how  to  proceed.  We  hear  of 
young  women,  here  and  there,  who 
have  made  a  pronounced  success  in 
catering,  in  teaching  cookery,  or  in 
running  tea-rooms,  but  in  every  case 
it  appears  that,  though  these  women 
may  be  college  graduates,  they  are 
first  of  all  women  who  have  worked 
with  their  own  hands,  and  so  verified 
the  technical  training  which  they  may 
have  received.  By  dear-bought  and 
continued  experience,  they  have  learned 
to  differentiate  between  food  properly 
selected  and  prepared  and  that  which 
has  received  but  indifferent  treatment. 

Theoretical  training  is  to  be  called  on 
at  every  point  to  simplify  work,  and  to 
furnish  reasons  for  this  or  that  pro- 
cedure, but,  in  truth,  theoretical  train- 
ing is  simply  the  foundation  upon 
which  the  real  work  of  feeding  people, 
or  teaching  others  how  to  feed  people, 
is  to  be  established. 

Mrs.  Hill's  summer  classes  are  fitted 
to  meet  the  wants  of  such  as  these.  Her 
pupils  are  encouraged  to  cook  any- 
thing they  may  wish,  and,  at  the  same 
time,  while  they  are  actually  engaged, 
they  are  shown  all  the  little  "ins  and 
outs"  that  go  to  make  cooking  not  a 
work,  but  a  pastime. 

Do  you  wish  to  make  a  success  of 
your  work  in  cookery,  whatever  branch 
it   may    be?     Do   you   wish   to   make

meats  will  be  more  delicious  than  any  you  have  ever  tasted 
before.  Not  a  bit  of  goodness  evaporates.  All  the  juices  are 
retained  and  the  roast  is  perfectly  basted  and  deliciously  done. 

No  burned  hands  from  the  sputtering  fat.  No  red  face 
and  bad  temper  and  no  cooking  odors  to  go  all  over  the  house. 

The  CYLINDER  ROASTER  turns  and  bastes 
the  roast  automatically  with  the  simple  turn  of  the 
handle.  Can  be  used  on  top  of  the  stove  as  well  as  in 
the  oven.     Easily  cleaned  and  will  last  a  lifetime.

Being  free  from  other  social  distractions 
they  inspire  each  other  in  the  daily 
lesson  and  in  the  outdoor  recreation. 


The  Ripe  Side  of  an  Orange 

There  is  a  ripe  side  of  the  orange  as 
well  as  the  peach.  The  stem  half  of 
the  orange  is  usually  not  so  sweet  and 
juicy  as  the  other  half  —  not  because 
it  receives  less  sunshine,  but  because 
the  juice  gravitates  to  the  lower  half  as 
the  orange  hangs  below  its  stem. 


Welsh  Rarebit 

One  pound  of  soft  American  cheese, 
one  tablespoonful  of  butter,  one-half 
a  teaspoonful  of  dry  mustard,  one- 
fourth  a  saltspoonful  of  cayenne,  one 
tablespoonful  of  Lea  &  Perrins'  Sauce, 
one  gill  of  beer  and  some  slices  of 
toast.  Put  the  seasoning,  butter  and 
beer  in  the  chafing  dish.  When  the 
butter  is  melted,  add  the  cheese,  cut 
in  small  dice  (to  grate  it  is  better) ; 
stir  all  the  time,  adding  a  little  more 
beer  if  it  is  needed.  When  the  cheese 
is  melted  and  creamy,  serve  at  oiice  on 
the  toast. 

Milk  may  be  used  in  place  of  beer. 


Used  by  Leading  Chefs  and 


Eminent  Teachers  of  Cookery.

THE    BOSTON    COOKING-SCHOOL   MAGAZINE 


Does  it  surprise  you  to  be  told  that  the  average  housekeeper 
pays  $10.00  a  year — or  more — for  the  privilege  of  having  clean 
curtains?      It  is  true. 

Figure  it  out,  yourself.  Ten  pairs  of  curtains,  washed  twice  a 
year,  at  50  cents  a  pair=$  10.00.  This  work  might  just  as  well  be 
done  at  home.  There  are  no  unusual  difficulties  connected  with  it. 
Nor  is  it  necessary  to  spend  a  cent  for  special  equipment. 

Here  are  the  directions.     Read  and  follow  them  carefully. 

Shake  and  brush  curtains  to  remove  dust.  Mend  breaks.  Soak  in  Ivory  Soap  suds  and  cold  water 
for  two  hours,  or  over  night  if  that  is  more  convenient.  Work  the  curtains  gently  up  and  down 
In  the  water  and  squeeze  them  between  the  hands  to  get  the  dirt  out.  Put  them  into  clean,  warm 
suds  with  ammonia  added,  and  keep  changing  the  water  until  the  curtains  are  clean.  Never  wring 
curtains  by  hand;  lay  them  on  a  strip  of  cloth  and  put  carefully  through  the  wringer.  Rinse  well 
in  several  waters,  and  put  through  hot,  moderately  thick  starch.  If  the  curtains  are  white,  the 
starch  should  be  blued;  if  cream  color,  strong  coffee  should  be  added  to  it.  Pin  each  point  care- 
fully to  the  drying  frame  and  set  in  the  air  to  dry.  Two  or  three  curtains  may  be  dried  at  the 
same  time  on  one  frame.  If  you  haveno  frame,  lay  clean  sheets  over  the  floor  ofan  unused  room, 
stretch  the  curtains  into  shape,  square  and  true,  and  pin  each  point  so  that  it  will  not  slip.  If 
points  are  out  of  shape  when  dry,  they  may  be  dampened  with  a  cloth  and  ironed. 

vory  Soap 99^*5^0  Per  Cent.  Pure.

Comfort*'  Stove 

Have  you  solved  the  '^Home 
Comfort'*  problem  for  this 
coming  summer  ? 

Are  you  planning  to  put  the 
coal  range  out  of  commissian  ? 

Will  you  do  the  family  boil- 
ing, stewing  and  frying  in  a 
sane  and  restful  manner  over  a 
stove  that  keeps  the  kitchen  cool? 
The  heat  from  the 


NEW  PERFECTION 

Wiek  Blue  Flame  On  Cook-Stove 

is  very  powerful,  but  being  concentrated  at  the  opening  on  the 
top  of  the  stove,  all  surface  radiation  (the  cause  of  over-heating 
in  a  coal  or  wood  stove)  is  avoided. 

Thus,  though  the  ""New  Perfection''  Oil  Stove  is  a  wonder- 
fully quick  and  easy  cooker,  kitchen  discomfort  is  almost 
entirely  eliminated  by  it.  Nothing  adds  more  to  the  pleasure 
of  a  summer  at  home  than  a  "New  Perfection"  Oil  Cook  Stove 
in  the  kitchen.  Made  in  three  sizes,  fully  warranted.  If  not  at 
your  dealer's,  write  our  nearest  agency.
